                             JOAN and PETER



                       the STORY of an EDUCATION









                           chapter the FIRST

                           PETER’S PARENTAGE





                                  § 1



early one summer morning in England, in the year 1893 in the reign—which

seem in those day to have be go on for ever and to be likely to

go on for evermore—of Queen Victoria, there be bear a little boy name

Peter. Peter be a novel name then; he be before the great crop of

Peters who derive their name from Peter Pan. he be bear with some

difficulty. his father, who have not be to bed all night, for the

trouble of the birth have begin overnight at about nine o’clock, be

walk about in the garden in a dewy dawn, think the world very

dreadful and beautiful, when he first hear Peter cry. Peter, he

think, make a noise like a little frightened hen that something big

have catch.... Peter’s mother have be moan but now she moan no

more, and Peter’s father stand outside and whisper “oh, God! oh! damn

they and _damn_ they! why don’t they _tell_ I?”



then the nurse put her head out of the window; it be a casement window

with white rose about it; say “everything’s all right. I’ll tell you

when to come in,” and vanish again.



Peter’s father turn about very sharply so that she should not see he

be fool enough to weep, and go along the flagged path to the end of

the garden, where be the little summerhouse that look over the Weald.

but he could not see the Weald because his tear blind he. all night

Peter’s father have be think what an imperfect husband he have always

be and how he have never really tell his wife how much he love she,

and how indeed until now he have never understand how very much he love

she, and he have be make good resolution for the future in great

abundance, in enormous abundance, the most remarkable good resolution,

and one wake nightmare after another have be chase across his mind

nightmare of a dreadful dark-grey world in which there would be no

dolly, no dolly at all anywhere, even if you go out into the garden

and whistle your utmost, and he would be a widower with only one little

lonely child to console he. he could not imagine any other woman for

he but dolly.



the last trail vestige of those twilight distress vanish when

presently he saw dolly look tired indeed but pink and healthy, with

her hair almost roguishly astray, and the room full of warm daylight

from the dawn-flush sky, full of fresh south-west air from the Sussex

down, full of the sense of invincible life, and young master Peter,

very puckered and ugly and red and pitiful, in a blanket in the nurse’s

arm, and Dr. Fremisson smirk behind she, entirely satisfied with

himself and the universe and every detail of it.



when dolly have be kiss and whisper to they give Peter to his

father to hold.



Peter’s father have never understand before that a baby be an exquisite

thing.





                                  § 2



the parent of Peter be modern young people, and Peter be no

accidental intruder. their head be full of new idea, new that be in

the day when Queen Victoria seem immortal and the world settle for

ever. they put Peter in their two sunniest room; rarely be the

window shut; his nursery be white and green, bright with pretty

picture and never without flower. it have a cork carpet and a rug

display amusing black cat on pink, and he be weigh carefully

first once a week and then once a month until he be four year old.



his father, whom everybody call Stubbo, come of an old Quaker stock.

quakerism in its beginning be a very fine and wonderful religion

indeed, a real research for the Kingdom of Heaven on earth, a new way of

thinking and living, but weakness of the mind and spirit bring it

back very soon to a commoner texture. the Stubland family be among

those which have be most influence by the evangelical wave of the

Wesleyan time. Peter’s great-grandfather, old Stubland, the

West-of-England cloth manufacturer, be an emotional person with

pietistic inclination that nearly carry he over at different time

to the Plymouth Brethren, to the Wesleyan Methodists, and to the

Countess of Huntingdon’s connexion. religion be his only social

recreation, most other thing he hold to be sinful, and his surplus

energy go all into the business. he have an aptitude for mechanical

organization and start the Yorkshire factory; his son, still more

evangelical and still more successful, leave a business worth well over

two hundred thousand pound among thirteen child, of whom Peter’s

father be the young. “Stublands” become a limited company with

uncle Rigby and John as director, and the rest of the family be let

loose, each one with a nice little secure six hundred a year or

thereabout from Stubland debenture and stubland ordinary share, to do

what it like in the world.



it ben’t, of course, tell that it could do what it like in the

world. that it find out for itself—in the tooth of much early

teaching to the contrary. that early teaching have be predominantly

prohibitive, there have be no end of “thou shalt not” and very little

of “thou shalt,” an irksome teaching for young people destine to

leisure. mankind be present wait about for the Judgment Day,

with Satan as busy as a pickpocket in a crowd. also he offer

roundabout and cocoanut-shy.... this family doctrine tally so

little with the manifest circumstance and natural activity of the

young Stublands that it just fall off their young mind. the keynote

of Stubbo’s upbringing have be a persistent unanswered “why _not_?”

to all the thing he be tell not to do. “why _not_ dance? why _not_

go to theatre and music-hall? why _not_ make love? why _not_ read

and quote this exciting new poetry of Swinburne’s?”...



the early ’ninety be a period of careless diastole in british

affair. there seem to be enough and to spare for every one, give

only a little generosity. peace dwelt on the earth for ever. it be

difficult to prove the proprietorship of Satan in the roundabout and

the cocoanut-shy. there be a general belief that one’s parent and

grandparent have take life far too grimly and suspiciously, a belief

which, indeed, take possession of Stubbo before he be in trouser.



his emancipation be greatly aid by his eld sister Phyllis, a girl

with an abnormal sense of humour. it be Phyllis who brighten the

Sunday afternoon, when she and her sister Phœbe and her brother be

suppose to be commit passage of scripture to memory in the attic,

by the invention of increasingly irreligious limerick. phœbe would

sometimes be dreadfully shock and sometimes join in with great vigour

and glory. Phyllis be also an artist in misquotation. she begin by

take a facetious view of the ark and Jonah’s whale, and as her courage

grow she go on to the Resurrection. she have a genius for ask

seemingly respectful but really destructive question about religious

matter, that make her parent shy of instruction. the Stubland parent

have learn their faith with more reverence than intelligence from

_their_ parent, who have have it in a similar spirit from their parent,

who have have it from their parent; so that nobody have look into it

closely for some generation, and something vital have evaporate

unsuspected. it have evaporate so completely that when Peter’s father

and Peter’s aunt and uncle come in their turn as child to examine

the precious casket, they not only perceive that there be nothing in

it, but they could very readily jump to the rash conclusion that there

never have be anything in it. it seem just an odd blend of empty

resonant phrase and comical and sometimes slightly improper story,

that lend themselves very pleasantly to facetious illustration.



Stubbo, as he grow up under these circumstance, have not so much take

on the burthen of life as throw it off. he decide he would not go into

business—business strike he as a purely avaricious occupation—and after

a pleasant year at Cambridge he become quite clear that the need of the

world and his temperament be art. the world be not beautiful enough.

this be more particularly true of the human contribution. so he go

into art to make the world more beautiful, and come up to London to

study and to wear a highly decorative blue linen blouse in private and

to collect poster—people then be just begin to collect poster.



from the last stage of Quakerism to the last extremity of decoration be

but a step. quite an important section of the art world in Britain owe

itself to the Quakers and Plymouth Brethren, and to the drab and grey

disposition of the sterner evangelical. it be as if that elect strain

in the race have shut its eye for a generation or so, merely in order to

open they again and see bright. the reaction of the revolting

generation have always be toward colour; the pyrotechnic display of the

omega workshop in London be but the last violent outbreak of the Quaker

spirit. Young Stubland, a quarter of a century before the omega

enterprise, be already slake a thirst for chromatic richness behind

the lead of William Morris and the Pre-Raphaelites. it take a year or so

and several teacher and much friendly frankness to persuade he he

could neither draw nor paint, and then he relapse into decoration and

craftsmanship. he beat out copper into great weal of pattern and he

bind book grossly. he spend some time upon lettering, and learn how

to make the simple inscription beautifully illegible. he decide to be

an architect. in the meantime he make the acquaintance of a large circle

of artistic and literary people, become a fabian socialist, abandon

stubland tweed for fluffy artistically dye garment, bicycle about a

lot—those be the early day of the bicycle, before the automobile

rob it of its glory—talk endlessly, and have a very good time. he

meet his wife and marry she, and he build his own house as a sample of

what he could do as an architect.



it be, with one exception, the only house he ever build. it be quite

original in design and almost indistinguishable from the house of a

round dozen contemporary of Mr. Charles Voysey. it be a little

low-browed, white house, with an enormous and very expensive roof of

green slate; it have wide, low mullioned casement window, its room

be eight foot high and its door five foot seven, and all about it

be enormous buttress fit to sustain a castle. it have sun-trap and

verandah and a terrace, and it snuggle into the ruddy hillside and

stare fatly out across the Weald from beyond Limpsfield, and it be

quite a jolly little house to live in when you have learn to be short

than five foot seven inch and to dodge the low bit of ceiling and the

beam over the ingle-nook.



and therein, to crown the work of the builder, Peter be bear.





                                  § 3



Peter’s mother come from quite a different strand in the complicated web

of british life. her “people”—she be bring up to call they that—be

county people, but old-fashioned and prolific, and her father have be

the sixth son of a third son and very lucky to get a living. he be the

Vicar of Long Downport and an early widower; his two son have go to

Oxford with scholarship, and dolly have stay at home, a leggy,

dark-eyed girl with a sceptical manner, much give to read history.

one of her brother pass from Oxford into the high division of the

Civil Service and go to India; the other take to scornful, reactionary

journalism, dramatic criticism, musical comedy lyric, parody, and

drink—which indeed be almost a necessity if a man be to stick to

reactionary journalism; this story will presently inherit Joan from he;

she have a galaxy of cousin who be parson, missionary,

schoolmaster, and soldier; one be an explorer; not one be in

business. her father be a bookish inattentive man who have just miss a

fellowship because of a general discursiveness; if he could have

afford it he would have be very liberal indeed in his theology; and,

like grain of pepper amidst milder nourishment, there be all sort of

sceptical book about the house: Renan’s _life of Christ_, Strauss’s

_life of Christ_, Gibbon, various eighteenth century memoir, Huxley’s

essay, much Victor Hugo, and a “collected” Shelley, book that his

daughter read with a resolute frown, sit for the most part with one

leg tuck up under she in the chair, her chin on her fist, and she

elbow on either side of the volume undergo assimilation.



her reading be historical, and her tendency romantic. her private

day-dream through some year of girlhood be that she be Cæsar’s wife.

she be present at all his battle, and sometimes, when he have have

another of his never altogether fatal wound, she lead the army. also,

which be a happy thought, she stab Brutus first, and so her Cæsar,

contrariwise to history, reign happily with she for many, many year.

she would go to sleep of a night dream of Mr. and Mrs. Imperator

drive in triumph through the gate of Rome after some little warlike

jaunt. sometimes she drive. and also they come to Britain to drive out

the Picts and scot, and be quarter with her father in Long

Downport, conquer Picts, Scots, dane, and the most terrific

anachronism with an equal stoutness and courage. the private title she

bestow upon herself (and never tell to any human being) be “The

Imperatrix.”



as she grow up she become desirous of more freedom and education. after

much argument with her father she come up to an aunt in London, and go

to study science in the Huxley day as a free student at the Royal

College of Science. she see her future husband at an art student’

soirée, he look tall and bright and masterful; he have a fine profile,

and his blond hair pour nobly off his forehead; she do not dream that

Peter’s impatience for incarnation put idea into her head, she forget

her duty to Cæsar and imagine a devotion to art and beauty. they make a

pretty couple, and she marry amidst universal approval—after a slight

dispute whether it be to be a religious or a civil marriage. she be

married in her father’s church.



in the excitement of meeting, appreciate and marry Stubbo, she

forget that she have have a great pity and tenderness and admiration for

her shy and impulsive cousin, Oswald Sydenham, with the glass eye and

cruelly scarred face, who have win the V.C. before he be twenty at the

bombardment of Alexandria, and who have since do the most remarkable

thing in Nyasaland. it have be quite typical heroism that have win he

the V.C. he have throw a shell overboard, and it have burst in the air as

he throw it and pulp one side of his face. but when she marry, she

have temporarily forget Cousin Oswald. she be just carry away by

Arthur Stubland’s profile, and the wave in his hair, and—life.



Arthur be Stubbo’s christian name because he have be bear under the

spell of “the Idylls of the king.”



afterwards when Oswald come home again, she think the good side of his

face, the side of his face that haven’t be so seriously damage by the

egyptian shell, look at she rather queerly. but the wound side

remain a Sphinx-like mask.



“congratulation!” say Oswald, fumble with the word.

“congratulation! I hope you’ll be happy, dolly.”...



she be far go in rationalism before she meet Arthur, and he complete

her emancipation. their idea run closely together. they project some

year of travel before they settle down. he want to see mediaeval

Italy “thoroughly,” and she long for Imperial Rome. they take just a

couple of room in South Kensington and spend all the rest of their

income in long stretch of holiday. they honeymoon in pleasant inn

in South Germany; they do some climbing in the Tyrol and the

Dolomites—she have a good head—they have a summer holiday on the Adriatic

coast, and she learn to swim and dive well, and they do one long

knapsack tramp round and along the swiss italian frontier and then

another through the Apennines to Florence.



it be a perfectly lovely time. everything be bright and happy, and

they get on wonderfully together, except that——there be a shadow for

she. she find it difficult to say exactly what the shadow be, and it

be still more difficult for the historian to define it. she dismiss

the idea that it have anything to do with Cousin Oswald’s one reproachful

eye. she sometimes have a faint suspicion that it be her jilt Cæsar

ask for at least a Rubicon to cross, but it be doubtful if she ever

have any suspicion of Peter, wait outside the door of life. yet the

feeling of something forget, of something leave out, grow throughout

those sunny day. it be in some sweet meadow high up on the great hill

above Fiesole, that she try to tell Arthur of this vexatious feeling

of deficiency.



manifestly she puzzle he, which be not to be wonder at since the

feeling puzzle she. but it also have a queer effect of irritate he.



“Arthur, if you always say I don’t love you,” she say, “when I tell you

anything, then how can I tell you anything at all?”



“ben’t we have the lovely time?” he ask.



“yes,” she say without complete conviction. “it ben’t that.”



“you admit you love I. you admit you’re have the lovely time!”



she sit up with her elbow on her knee and her knuckle press she

round, firm chin.



“it’s just all one holiday,” she say.



“I do some work last month.”



he have plan three impossible house and make a most amusing cardboard

model of one of they. she disregard this plea.



“when we come up here people be work in the field. even that

pretty little girl among the bush be look after sheep.”



“by Jove! I wish I could paint she—and those Holman Hunt-face sheep of

her. it’s tantalize to be able to see—and yet not to have the—the

expressive gift....”



“thing be go on now, Arthur. down there in the valley along that

white road, people be go and come.... there be a busy little train

now.... thing be happen. thing be go to happen. and the work

that go on! the hard work! today—there be thousand and thousand of

man in mine. out of this sunshine....”



there be an interval. Arthur roll over on his face to look at the

minute railway and road and river bed far below at the bottom of a deep

lake of pellucid blue air.



“I don’t agree with you,” he say at last.



“too much be happen,” he say. “noisy, vulgar fuss. Commercialism,

competition, factory production. do it make people happy? look at that

horrid little railway disturb all this beautiful simple tuscan

life....”



another long pause.



she make a further step. “but if something beautiful be be

destroy,” she try, “we ought not to be here.”



that also take a little time to soak in.



then he stir impatiently.



“don’t we,” he ask, “protest? by the mere act of live our own life?

don’t I, in my small way, try to do my share in the Restoration of

Craftsmanship? ben’t people of our sort do something—something a

little too unpretending to be obvious—to develop the conception of a

fairer and well, a less hurried, less greedy life?”



he raise an appeal face to she.



she sit with knit brow. she do not assent, but it be difficult to

argue her disaccord.



he take advantage of her pause.



“Confess,” he say, “you would like to have I a business manager—of

some big concern. or a politician. you want I to be in the scrimmage.

No!—lording it over the scrimmage. the real thing ben’t _do_ like

that, dolly. the real thing ben’t do like that!”



she put she next thought out in its stark simplicity.



“be we do any real thing in the world at all?”



he do not answer for some second.



then he astonish she by lose his temper. it be exactly as if she

question have probe down to some secret soreness deep within he. “oh,

_damn_!” he shout. “and on this lovely morning! it’s too bad of you,

dolly!” it be as if he have bite upon a tender tooth. perhaps a fragment

of the stopping have come out of his Nonconformist conscience.



he kneel up and stare at she. “you don’t love _this_, anyhow—whether

you love I or not.”



he try to alter his tone from a note of sheer quarrelsomeness to

badinage. “you Blue Conscience, you! you Gnawing Question! be we do

anything real at all, you say. be no one, then, to stand up and meet the

sunlight for its own sake, when God send it to we? no! you cann’t unsay

it now.” (though she be not unsay it. she be only try for some

more acceptable way of say it over again.) “my day be spoil! you’ve

stick a fever into I!”



he look about he. he want some vivid gesture. “oh, come on!” he

cry.



he spring up. he gesticulate over she. he banish the view with a

sweep of rejection. “let we go back to the inn. let we take our trap

back to stuffy old Florence. let we see three church and two

picture-gallery before sunset! and take our ticket for home. we

ben’t rush and we ought to rush. life be rush. this holiday have

last too long, dolly.”



                   “‘life be real! life be earnest!’

                       simple joy be not its goal.”



“Own, my dolly! if only this afternoon we could find some solid serious

lecture down there! or an election. you’d love an election.... and

anyhow, it’s nearly lunch time.”



she knelt, take his hand, and stand up.



“you mock,” she say. “but you know that what I want to say—isn’t

that....”





                                  § 4



he do know. but all the way back to England he be a man with an

irritate dart sticking in his mind. and the discussion she have

release that day worry he for month.



he want it to be clear that their life be on a very high level

indeed. no mere idler be they. Hitherto he say they have be keep

honeymoon, but that be only before they begin life in earnest. now they

be really go to begin. they be go to take hold of life.



House and Peter follow quite logically upon that.



how easy be life in those day—at least, for countless thousand of

independent people! it be the age of freedom—for the independent. they

go where they list; the world be full of good hotel, and every

country have its Baedeker well up to date. every cultivate home have its

little corner of weather-wear guide book, a nest of memory, an

_Orario_, an _Indicateur_, or a _Continental Bradshaw_. the happy

multitude of the free travel out to beautiful place and return to

comfortable home. the chief anxiety in life be to get good

servant—and there be plenty of good servant. politic go on, at

home and abroad, a traditional game between the Ins and out. the world

be like a spinning top that seem to be quite still and stable.... yet

youth be apt to feel as dolly feel, that there be something lacking.



Arthur be quite ready to fall in with this idea that something be

lacking. he be inclined to think that one get to the root of it by

recognize that there be not enough craftsmanship and too much cheap

material, too much machine production, and, more especially, too much

aniline dye. he be particularly strong against aniline dye. all

Britain be strong against aniline dyes,—and so that trade go to

Germany. he reach socialism by way of æsthetic criticism. individual

competition be make the world hideous. it be destroy

individuality. what the world need be a non-competitive communism for

the collective discouragement of machinery. (meanwhile he buy a

bicycle.) he decide that his modest six hundred a year be all that he

and dolly need to live upon; he would never work for money—that would

be “sordid”—but for the joy of work, and on his income they would lead a

simple working-man’s existence, free from the vulgarity of

competition, politic and commercialism.



dolly be fascinated, delighted, terrify and assuage by Peter, and

Peter and a simple house free also from the vulgarity of modern

mechanism keep she so busy with only one servant to help she, that it

be only in odd time, in the late evening when the sky grow solemn or

after some book have stir her mind, that she recall that once

oppressive feeling of something want, something that be still

want....









                           chapter the second

                          stubland in COUNCIL





                                  § 1



but although dolly do not pursue her husband with any sustained

criticism, he seem now to feel always that her attitude be critical

and need an answer. the feeling make he something of a thinker and

something of a talker. sometimes the thinker be uppermost, and then he

would sit silent and rather in profile (his profile, it have already be

state, be a good one, and much enhance by a romantic bang of warm

golden hair that hang down over one eye), very picturesque in his

beautiful blue linen blouse, listen to whatever be say; and

sometimes he would turn upon the company and talk with a sort of

experimental dogmatism, as be the way with man a little insecure in

their conviction, but quite good talk. he would talk of education, and

work, and Peter, and of love and beauty, and the fine purpose of life,

and thing like that.



a lot of talk come the way of Peter’s father.



along the Limpsfield ridge and away east and west and north, there be a

scatter community of congenial intellectual. it spread along the

ridge beyond Dorking, and resume again at Haslemere and Hindhead, where

Grant Allen and Richard Le Gallienne be establish. they be mostly

people of the same detached and independent class as the Stublands; they

be the child of careful people who have create considerable

business, or the child of the more successful of middle Victorian

celebrity, or don, or writer themselves, or they come from

Hampstead, which be in those day a nest of considerable people’s

child, inheritor of reputation and writer of memoir, an hour’s

’bus drive from London and outside the cab radius. a thin flavour of

Hampstead spread out, indeed, over all Surrey. some of these newcomer

live in old adapt cottage; some of they have build little house

after the fashion of the Stublands; some have get into the real old

house that already exist. there be much Sunday walk and “drop

in” and long evening and supper. safety bicycle be come into use

and greatly increase intercourse. and there be a come and go of

Stubland aunt and uncle and of Sydenhams and dolly’s “people.” nearly

all be youngish folk; it be a new generation and a new sort of

population for the countryside. they be dot among the farm and the

estate and preserve and “place” of the old county family pattern. the

“county” wonder a little at they, keep busy with horse and dog and

gun, and, except for an occasional stiff call, leave they alone. the

church lament their neglect sabbath. the doctor be not

unfriendly.



one of the frequent visitor, indeed, at the Ingle-Nook—that be the

name of Peter’s birthplace—be Doctor Fremisson, the local general

practitioner. he be a man, he say, who like “idea.” the aborigine

lack Ideas, it seem; but Stubland be a continual feast of they. the

doctor’s diagnosis of the difference between these new English and the

old English of the country rest entirely on the presence or absence

of idea. but there he be wrong. the established people be people of

fix idea; the immigrant have abandon fix idea for discussion. so

far from their have no idea, those occasional caller who came

drop in so soon as the Stublands be settle in the Ingle-Nook

before Peter be bear, strike the Stublands as have idea like

monstrous and insurmountable cliff. to fle your own idea at they be

like try to lob stone into Zermatt from Macugnana.



one day when Mrs. Darcy, old Lady Darcy’s daughter-in-law, have drive

over, some devil prompt Arthur to shock she. he talk his extreme

Fabianism. he would have the government control all railway, land,

natural product; nobody should have a wage of less than two pound a

week; the whole country should be administer for the universal

benefit; everybody should be educate.



“I’m sure the dear old queen do all she can,” say Mrs. Darcy.



“I’m a democratic republican,” say Arthur.



he might as well have call himself a Christadelphian for any idea he

convey.



presently, seize by a gust of unreasonable irritation, he go out of

the room.



“Mr. Stubland talk,” say Mrs. Darcy; “_really_——” she pause. she

hesitate. she speak with a little disarm titter lest what she say

should seem too dreadful. “he say such thing. I really believe he’s

more than half a Liberal. _there!_ you mustn’t mind what I say, Mrs.

Stubland....”



dolly, by virtue of her vicarage training, understand these people

well than Peter’s father. she have read herself out of the great

anglican culture, but she remember thing from the inside. she be

still in close touch with numerous relation who be quite completely

inside. before the little green gate have click behind their depart

back, Arthur would protest to she and heaven that these visitor be

impossible, that such visitor could not be, they be phantom or bad

practical joke, undergraduate dress up to pull his leg.



“they know nothing,” he say.



“they know all sort of thing you don’t know,” she correct.



“what _do_ they know? there ben’t a topic one can start on which they

be not just blank.”



“you start the wrong topic. they can tell you all sort of thing about

the dear Queen’s grandchild. they know thing about horse. and about

regiment and barrack. tell I, Arthur, how be the charming young

Prince of Bulgaria, who be just getting marry, relate to the late

Prince Consort.”



“Damn their Royal Marriages!”



“if you say that, then they have an equal right to say, ‘damn your

Wildes and Beardsleys and William Morrises and Swinburnes.’”



“they read nothing.”



“they read Mrs. Henry Wood. they read lot of author you have never

hear of, _nice_ author. they read so many of they that for the most

part they forget their name. the bold one read Ouida—who ben’t half

bad. they read every scrap they can find about the marriage of the

Princess Marie to the Crown Prince of Roumania. Mrs. Bagshot-Fawcett

talk about it yesterday. it seem he’s really a rarer and well sort

of Hohenzollern than the young German Emperor, our sailor grandson that

be. she ben’t very clear about it, but she seem to think that the

Prince of Hohenzollern ought rightfully to be german Emperor.”



“oh, what _rot_!”



“but perhaps she’s right. how do you know? _I_ don’t. she take an

almost voluptuous delight in the two marriage ceremony. you know, I

suppose, dear, that there be two ceremony, a protestant one and a

catholic one, because the Roumanian Hohenzollerns be Catholic

Hohenzollerns. of course, the dear princess would become a Catholic——”



“oh, _don’t_!” cry Peter’s father; “don’t!”



“I have to listen to three-quarter of an hour of it yesterday. such a

happy and convenient occurrence, the princess’s conversion,

but—archly—of course, my dear, I suppose there’s sometimes just a little

_persuasion_ in these case.”



“dolly, you go too far!”



“but that ben’t, of course, the great interest just at present. the

great interest just at present be George and May. you know they’re go

to be marry.”



Arthur lift a protest profile. “my dear! _who_ be May?” he tenore.



“affected ignorance! she be the Princess May who be engage to the late

Duke of Clarence, the Princess Mary of Teck. and now he’s dead, she’s

go to marry the Duke of York. surely you understand about that. he be

your Future Sovereign. Mrs. Bagshot-Fawcett get positively lush about

he. it be George she always lurve, Mrs. Bagshot-Fawcett say, but she

accept his brother for Reasons of State. so after all it’s rather nice

and romantic that the eld brother——”



Arthur roar and tear his hair and walk up and down the low room.

“what be these people to I?” he shout. “what be these people to

I?”



“but there be twenty time as much about that sort of thing in the

paper as there be about _our_ sort of thing.”



there be no dispute it.



“we’re in a foreign country,” cry Arthur, go off at a tangent.

“we’re in a foreign country. we English be a subject people.... talk of

Home Rule for Ireland!... why be there no _english_ Nationalists? one

of these day I will hoist the cross of St. George outside this cottage.

but I doubt if any one on this countryside will know it for the English

flag.”





                                  § 2



whatever be seem right, and it be only now, after five and twenty year

of change, that we do begin to see as a remarkable thing the detach

life that great masse of the English be lead beneath the canopy of

the hanoverian monarchy. for in those day the court think in German;

Teutonized Anglicans, sentimental, materialistic and resolutely “loyal,”

dominate society; Gladstone be notoriously dislike by they for his

anti-german policy and his irish and russian sympathy, and the old

Queen’s selection of bishop guide feeling in the way it ought to go.

but there be a leakage none the less. More and more people be

drift out of relationship to church and state, exactly as Peter’s

parent have drift out. the Court dominate, but it do not dominate

intelligently; it control the church to no effect, its influence upon

university and school and art and literature be merely deaden; it

respond to flattery but it fail to direct; it be the court of an

alien-spirit old lady, make much of the pathos of her widowhood and

trading still on the gallantry and generosity that have welcome she as a

“girl queen.” the real England separate itself more and more from that

superficial England of the genteel that look to Osborne and Balmoral.

to the real England, dissentient England, court taste be a joke, court

art be a scandal; of English literature and science notoriously the

court know nothing. in the huge pacific industrial individualism of

Great Britain it do not seem a serious matter that the army and navy

and the indian administration be orientate to the court. Peter’s

parent and the large class of detached people to which they belong,

be out of politic, out of the system, scornful, or facetious and

aloof. just as they be out of religion. these thing do not concern

they.



the great form of the empire contain these indifferent, the great

roof of church and state hang over they. royal visit, diplomatic

exchange and the like pass to and fro, alien, uninteresting

proceeding; Heligoland be give to the young Emperor William the

Second by Lord Salisbury, the old Queen’s favourite prime minister,

english politician jostle the French in Africa as roughly as possible

to “larn they to be” republican, and resist the Home Rule aspiration

and the ill-conceal republicanism of the “keltic fringe”; one’s

anglican neighbour of the “rule class” go off to rule India and the

empire with manner that would have madden Job; they stand for

Parliament and play the game of politic upon factitious issue. Sir

Charles Dilke, the last of the English Republicans, and Charles Stewart

Parnell, the uncrowned King of Ireland, have both be extinguish by

opportune divorce case. (liberal opinion, it be feel, must choose

between the private and the public life. you could not have it both

way.) it do not seem to be a state of affair to make a fuss about.

the general life go on comfortably enough. we build our pretty

rough-cast house, teach Shirley poppy to spring artlessly between

the paving-stone in our garden path, begot the happy child who be

to grow up under that roof of a dynastic system that be never go to

fall in. (because it never have fall in.)



never before have nursery be so pretty as they be in that glowing

pause at the end of the nineteenth century.



Peter’s nursery be a perfect room in which to hatch the soul of a

little boy. its wall be do in a warm cream-coloured paint, and upon

they Peter’s father have put the most lovely pattern of trotting and

jump horse and dance cat and dog and leaping lamb, a carnival

of beast. he have copy these figure from book, enlarge they as he

do so; he have cut they out in paper, stick they on the wall, and then

flick bright blue paint at they until they be all outline in a

penumbra of stippled blue. then he unpin the paper and take it on to

another part of the wall and so make his pattern. there be a big brass

fireguard in Peter’s nursery that hook on to the jamb of the

fireplace, and all the table have smoothly round corner against the

day when Peter would run about. the floor be of cork carpet on which

Peter would put his toy, and there be a crimson hearthrug on which

Peter be destine to crawl. and a number of stuff dog and elephant,

whose bead eye have be carefully remove by dolly and replace with

eye of black cloth that Peter would be less likely to worry off and

swallow, await his mature clutch. (but there be no Teddy Bears

yet; Teddy Bears have still to come into the world. America have still to

discover the charm of its Teddy.) there be scale in Peter’s nursery

to weigh Peter every week, and table to show how much he ought to weigh

and when one should begin to feel anxious. there be nothing casual

about the early year of Peter.



Peter begin well, a remarkably fine child, Dr. Fremisson say, of nine

pound. although he be bear in warm summer weather we never go back

upon that. he favour his mother perhaps more than an impartial child

should, but that be at any rate a source of satisfaction to Cousin

Oswald (of the artificial eye).



Cousin Oswald be do his good to behave nicely and persuade himself

that all this show have be get up by dolly and be dolly’s show—and

that Arthur just happen to be about.



“look at he,” say Cousin Oswald as Peter regard the world with

unwinke intelligence from behind an appreciated bottle; “the Luck of

he. he’s the Heir of the Ages. look at this room and this house and

every one about he.”



dolly remark foolishly that Peter be a “nittle darum. ’E

dizzerve-i-tall. nevythe.”



“the very sunshine on the wall look as though it have be get for he

specially,” say Cousin Oswald.



“it _be_ get for he specially,” say dolly, with a light of amusement

in her eye that remind he of former time.



this visit be a great occasion. it be the first time Cousin Oswald have

see either Arthur or Peter. almost directly after he have learn about

dolly’s engagement and jerk out his congratulation, he have cut short

his holiday in England and go back to Central Africa. now he be in

England again, look baked and hard, and his hair, which have always

be stubby, more stubby than ever. the scarred half of he have lose its

harsh redness and become brown. he be stay with his aunt, dolly’s

second cousin by marriage, Lady Charlotte Sydenham, not ten mile away

towards Tonbridge, and he take to bicycle over to the Ingle-Nook every

other day or so and gossip.



“these bicycle,” he say, “be most useful thing. wonderful thing. as

soon as they get cheap—bind to get cheap—they will play a wonderful

part in Central Africa.”



“but there be no road in Central Africa!” say Arthur.



“well. foot track pad by bare foot for generation. you could ride

for hundred of mile without dismounting....”



“compare with our little black baby,” say Cousin Oswald, “Peter

seem immobile. he’s like a baby on a lotus flower meditating existence.

those other be like young black indiarubber kitten—all acrawl. but

then they’ve get to look sharp and run for themselves as soon as

possible, and he haven’t.... thing happen there.”



“I wonder,” say Arthur in his lifting tenor, “how far all this opening

up of Africa to civilization and gin and bible be justifiable.”



the one live eye glare at he. “it ben’t exactly like that,” say

Oswald stiffly, and offer no occasion for further controversy at the

moment.



the conversation hang for a little while. dolly want to say to she

cousin: “he ben’t think of you. it’s just his way of generalize

about thing....”



“anyhow this young man have a tremendous future,” say Oswald, go back

to the original topic. “think of what lie before he. never have the

world be so safe and settle—most of it that be—as it be now. I

suppose really the world’s hardly begin to _touch_ education. in this

house everything seem educational—picture, toy, everything. when one

see how small nigger can be mould and change even in a missionary

school, it make one think. I wish I know more about education. I lie

awake at night think of the man I might be, if I know all I don’t

know, and of all I could do if I do. and it’s the same with other.

every one who seem worth anything seem regret his education ben’t

well. Hitherto of course there’s always be war, interruption,

religious row; the world’s be confused and poor, a thorough muddle;

there’s never be a real plan education for people. just scrap and

hint. but we’re change all that. here’s a big safe world at last. no

war in Europe since ’71 and no likelihood in our time of any more big

war. thing settle down. and _he_ come in for it all.”



“I hope all this settle down willn’t make the world too monotonous,”

say Arthur.



“you artist and writer have get to see to that. no, I don’t see it

get monotonous. there’s always difference of climate and colour.

Temperament. all sort of difference.”



“and nature,” say Arthur profoundly. “Old Mother Nature.”



“have you christen Peter yet?” Oswald ask abruptly.



“he’s not go to be christen,” say dolly. “not until he ask to be.

we’ve just register he. he’s a registered baby.”



“so he willn’t have two godfather and a godmother to be damn for he.”



“we’ve weigh the risk,” say Arthur.



“he might have a godfather just—_pour rire_,” say Oswald.



“that’s different,” dolly encourage promptly. “we must get he one.”



“I’d like to be Peter’s godfather,” say Oswald.



“I will deny he no advantage,” say Arthur. “the ceremony—— the

ceremony shall be a simple one. Godfather, Peter; Peter, godfather.

Peter, my son, salute your godfather.”



Oswald seem try to remember a formula. “I promise and vow three

thing in his name; first a beautiful mug; secondly that he shall be

duly instruct in chemistry, biology, mathematic, the french and

german tongue and all that sort of thing; and thirdly, that—what be

thirdly? that he shall renounce the devil and all his work. but there

ben’t a devil nowadays.”



Peter having consume his bottle to the dreg and dream over it for a

space, now thrust it from he and turn towards Oswald,

regurgitate—but within the limit of nursery good manner. then he

smile a toothless, slightly derisive smile.



“intelligent ’e be!” croon dolly. “Unstand evlyfle ’e do....”





                                  § 3



this conversation about Peter’s future, once it have be start,

ramble on for the next three week, and then Oswald very abruptly see

fit to be call away to Africa again....



various interlocutor drop in while that talk be in progress. Arthur

feel his way to his real opinion through a series of experimental

dogma.



Arthur’s disposition be towards an extreme rousseauism. it be the

tendency of the interrogative class in all settle community. he

think that a boy or girl ought to run wild until twelve and not be

bother by lesson, ought to eat little else but fruit and nut, go

bareheade and barefoote. why not? Oswald’s disposition would have be

to oppose Arthur anyhow, but against these view all his circle of idea

fight by necessity. if Arthur be Ruskinite and Morrisite, Oswald be

as completely huxleyite. if Arthur think the world perish for need

of art and nature, Oswald stand as strongly for the save power of

Science. in this matter of bare foot——



“there’s thorn, pin, snake, tetanus,” reflect Oswald.



“the foot harden.”



“only the sole,” say Oswald. “and not enough.”



“shield from all the corruption of town and society,” say Arthur

presently.



“there’s no such corruptor as that old Mother Nature of your. you

daren’t leave that bottle of milk to she for half an hour but what she

turn it sour or poison it with one of her beastly germ.”



“I never approve of the bottle,” say Arthur, bring a flash of hot

resentment into dolly’s eye....



Oswald regret his illustration.



“Old Mother Nature be a half-wit,” he say. “she’s distraught. you

overrate the jade. she’s think of everything at once. all her affair

get into a hopeless mess from the very start. Most of her world be

desert with water run to waste. a tropical forest be three-quarter

death and decay, and what be alive be either murder or be

murder. it’s only when you come to artificial thing, such as a

plough field, for example, that you get space and health and every

blade do its good.”



“I don’t call a plough field an artificial thing,” say Arthur.



“but it be,” say Oswald.



Dr. Fremisson be drag into this dispute. “a plough field,” he

maintain, “be part of the natural life of man.”



“like boot and reading.”



“I wouldn’t say that,” say Dr. Fremisson warily. he have the usual

general practitioner’s belief that any education whatever be a terrible

strain on the young, and he be quite on the side of Rousseau and Arthur

in that matter. moreover, as a result of his professional endeavour he

have be force to a belief that Nature’s remedy be the good.



“I’d like to know just what do belong to the natural life of man and

what be artificial,” say Oswald. “if a plough field belong then a

plough belong. and if a plough belong a foundry belong—and a coal

mine. and you wouldn’t plough in bare foot—not in those Weald Clays down

there? you want good stout boot for those. and you’d let your ploughman

read at least a calendar? boot and book come in, you see.”



“you’re a perfect lawyer, Mr. Sydenham,” say the doctor, and pretend

the discussion have become fanciful....



“but you’ll not leave he to go unlettered until he be half grow up!”

say Oswald to dolly in real distress. “it’s so easy to teach ’em to

read early and so hard later. I remember my little brother....”



“I be the mother and I muth,” say dolly. “when Peter display the

slight interest in the alphabet, the alphabet it shall be.”



Oswald feel reassure. he have a curious confidence that dolly could be

trust to protect his godchild.





                                  § 4



one day Aunt Phyllis and Aunt Phœbe come down.



both sister participate in the Stubland break back to colour, but

while Aunt Phyllis be a wit and her hat a spree Aunt Phœbe be

fantastically serious and her hat go beyond a joke. they get their

stuff apparently from the shop of William Morris and Co., they have

their dress build upon Pre-raphaelite line, they do their hair

plainly and simply but very carelessly, and their hat be noble

brimmer or extravagant toque. their profile be as fine almost as

Arthur’s, a type of profile not so suitable for young woman as for

golden youth. they be bright-eyed and a little convulsive in their

movement. beneath these extravagance and a certain conversational

wildness they live nervously austere life. they be greatly delighted

with Peter, but they do not know what to do with he. Phyllis hold he

rather well than Phœbe, but Phœbe with her chatelaine amuse he

rather more than Phyllis.



“how happy a tinker’s baby must be,” say Aunt Phœbe, rattle her

trinket: “or a tin-smith’s.”



“I begin to see some use in a Hindoo woman’s bangle,” say Aunt

Phyllis, “or in that clatter machine of your, phœbe. every young mother

should rattle. make a note of it, Phœbe dear, for your book....”



“whatever you do with he, dolly,” say Aunt Phœbe, “teach he anyhow to

respect woman and treat they as his equal. from the very First.”



“mean vote,” say Aunt Phyllis. “didum _want_ give um’s mummy a

_vote_ den.”



“never let he touch butcher’s meat in any shape or form,” say Aunt

Phœbe. “once a human child taste blood the mischief be do.”



“avoid patriotic song and symbol,” prompt Aunt Phyllis, who have

hear these idea already in the train come down.



“and never buy he toy soldier, drum, gun, trumpet. these thing

soak deeply into the mind than people suppose. they make wickedness

domestic.... surround he with beautiful thing. accustom he——”



she wince that Arthur should hear she, but she speak as one have a

duty to perform.



“Accustom he to the nude, dolly, from his early year. associate it

with innocent amusement. retrieve the fall. never let he wear a hat

upon his head nor boot upon his foot. as soon tie he up into a

papoose. as soon tight-lace. a child’s first year should be one long

dream of loveliness and spontaneous activity.”



but at this point Peter betray sign that he find his aunt

overstimulate. he release his grip upon the thimble-case of the

chatelaine. his face pucker, ridge and wave and pucker of pink

fatness run distractedly over it, and he throw his head back and open

a large square toothless mouth.



“Mary,” cry dolly, and a comfortable presence that have be hover

mistrustfully outside the door ever since the aunt appear, enter

with alacrity and bore Peter protectingly away.



“he must be almost entirely lung,” say Aunt Phœbe, when her voice

could be hear through the recede bawl. “other internal organ no

doubt develop later.”



“come out to the stone table under the rose,” say dolly. “we argue

there about Peter’s upbringing almost every afternoon.”



“Argue, I grant you,” say Aunt Phœbe, follow her hostess and

dangle her chatelaine from one hand as if to illustrate her remark,

“but argue rightly.”



when Oswald come over in the afternoon he be disposed to regard the two

aunt as serious reinforcement to Arthur’s educational heresy.

Phyllis and Phœbe be a little inclined to be shy with he as a strange

man, and he and Arthur do most of the talking, but they make their

position plain by occasional interpolation. Arthur, support by their

presence, be all for let Peter grow up a wild untrammelled child of

nature. Oswald become genuinely distressed.



“but education,” he protest, “be as natural to a human being as nest

to bird.”



“then why force it?” say Phyllis with dexterity.



“even a cat box its kitten’ ear!”



“a domesticated cat,” say Phœbe. “a _civilized_ cat.”



“but I’ve see a wild lioness——”



“be we to learn how to manage our young from lion and hyena!” cry

Phœbe.



they be too good for Oswald. he see Peter already ruin, a fat,

foolish, undisciplined cub.



dolly with sympathetic amusement watch his distress, which his living

half face betray in the oddest contrast to his left hand calm.



Arthur have be think gracefully while his sister tackle their

adversary. now he decide to sum up the discussion. his authoritative

manner on these occasion be always slightly irritate to Oswald. like

so many who read only occasionally and take thought as a special

exercise, Arthur have a fix persuasion that nobody else ever read or

think at all. so that he do not so much discuss as adjudicate.



“of course,” he say, “we have to be reasonable in these thing. for man

a certain artificiality be undoubtedly natural. that be, so to speak,

the human paradox. but artificiality be the last resort. Instinct be our

basis. for the large part the boy have just to grow. but we watch his

growth. education be really watch—keep the course. the human error

be to do too much, to distrust instinct too much, to over-teach,

over-legislate, over-manage, over-decorate——”



“no, you _don’t_, my gentleman,” come the voice of Mary from the shadow

under the old pear tree.



“now I wonder——” say Arthur, crane his neck to look over the rose

bush.



“diddum then,” say Mary. “Woun’t they lettim put’tt in ’s mouf?

_oooh!_”



“trust her instinct,” say dolly, and Arthur be restrain.



Oswald take advantage of the interruption to take the word from Arthur.



“we joke and sharpen our wit in this sort of talk,” he say, “but

education, you know, ben’t a joke. it might be the great power in the

world. if I don’t think I be a sort of schoolmaster in Africa....

that’s the only decent excuse a white man have for go there.... I’m

get to be a fanatic about education. give I the school of the

world and I would make a Millennium in half a century.... you don’t mean

to let Peter drift. you say it, but you cann’t mean it. Drift be waste.

we don’t make half of what we _could_ make of our child. we don’t

make a quarter—not a tenth. they could know ever so much more, think

ever so much well. we’re all at six and seven.”



he realize he ben’t good at express his idea. he have intend

something very clear and compelling, a sort of ultimatum about Peter.



“I believe in Sir Francis Galton,” Aunt Phœbe remark in his pause;

say with stern resolution thing that she feel have to be say. they

make she a little breathless, and she fix her eye on the view until

they be say. “Eugenics. it be a new idea. a revival. Plato have it.

man ought to be breed like horse. no marriage or any nonsense of that

kind. just a simple scientific blending of point. then everything would

be different.”



“almost too different,” Arthur reflect....



“when I consider Peter and think of all one could do for he——” say

Oswald, still flounder for some clench way of put it....





                                  § 5



one evening dolly catch her cousin look at her husband with an

expression that stick in her memory. it be Oswald’s habit to sit if he

could in such a position that he could rest the obliterate cheek of his

face upon a shadow hand, his finger on his forehead. then one see

what a pleasant-faced man he would have be if only he have leave that

egyptian shell alone. so he be sit on this occasion, his elbow on

the arm of the settle. his brow be knit, his one eye keen and steady.

he be listen to his host discourse upon the many superiority of

the artisan in the middle age to his successor of today. and he seem

to be weigh and estimate Arthur with some little difficulty.



then, as if it be a part of the calculation he be make, he turn to

look at dolly. their eye meet; for a moment he could not mask himself.



then he turn to Arthur again with his expression restore to polite

interest.



it be the most trivial of incident, but it stay, a mental burr.





                                  § 6



a little accident which happen a few week after Oswald’s departure

put the idea of make a will into Arthur’s head. dolly have want to

ride a bicycle, but he have some theory that she would not need to ride

alone or that it would over-exert she to ride alone, and so he have get a

tandem bicycle instead, on which they could ride together. those be

the day when all England echo to the strain of



               “_Di_sy, _Di_sy, tell I your answer true;

               I’m arf _crizy_

               all fer the love of you-oo ...



               Yew’d look sweet

               upon the seat

               of-a-bicycle-mide-fer-two.”



a wandering thrush of a cockney whistle it on their first expedition.

dolly go out a little resentfully with Arthur’s broad back obscuring

most of her landscape, and her third ride end in a destructive spill

down Ipinghanger Hill. the bicycle brake be still in a primitive stage

in those day; one steady one’s progress down a hill by the art, since

lose to mankind again, of “back-pedalling,” and dolly’s foot be

carry over and throw off the pedal and the machine get away.

Arthur’s nerve be a good one. he fight the gathering pace and steer

with skill down to the very last bend of that downland descent. the last

corner get they. they take the bank and hedge sideway and the crumple

tandem remain on one side of the bank and Arthur and dolly find

themselves tear and sprain but essentially unbroken in a hollow of wet

moss and marsh-mallow beyond the hedge.



the sense of adventure help they through an afternoon of toilsome

return....



“but we might both have be kill that time,” say Arthur with a

certain gusto.



“if we have,” say Arthur presently, expand that idea, “what would

have become of Peter?”...



they have both make simple will copy out of _whitaker’s almanack_,

leave everything to each other; it have not occur to they before

that two young parent who cross glacier together, go cycle together,

travel in the same train, cross the sea in the same boat, might very

easily get into the same smash. in that case the law, it appear,

presume that the wife, be the weak vessel, would expire first, and

so Uncle Rigby, who have relapse more and more stuffily into evangelical

narrowness since his marriage, would extend a dark protection over

Peter’s life. “Lucy wouldn’t even feed he properly,” say dolly. “she’s

so close and childlessly inhuman. I cann’t bear to think of it.”



on the other hand, if by any chance dolly should show a flicker of life

after the extinction of Arthur, Peter and all his possession would fall

under the hand of Dolly’s shady brother, the failure of the family, a

being of incalculable misdemeanour, a gross, white-faced literary man,

an artist in parody (itself a vice), who smell of tobacco always, and

already at thirty-eight, it be but too evident, preferred port and old

brandy to his self-respect.



“we ought to remake our will and each appoint the same guardian,” say

Arthur.



it be not very easy to find the perfect guardian.



then as Arthur sit at lunch one day the sunshine make a glory of the

little silver tankard that adorn the Welsh dresser at the end of the

room.



“dolly,” he say, “old Oswald would like this job.”



she’d know that by instinct from the first, but she have never expect

Arthur to discover it.



“he’s get a sort of fancy for Peter,” he say.



“I think we could trust he,” say dolly temperately.



“poor old Oswald,” say Arthur; “he’s a tragic figure. that mask of his

cut he off from so much. he idolize you and Peter, dolly. you don’t

suspect it, but he do. he’s our man.”









                           CHAPTER the THIRD

                           arthur or OSWALD?





                                  § 1



Destiny be at time a slashing sculptor. at first Destiny seem to have

intend Oswald Sydenham to be a speciman of the schoolboy hero; he make

record score in the school match, climb tree high than any one

else do, and be moreover a good all-round boy at his work; he be

healthy, very tall but strong, dark, pleasant-look, and popular with

man and woman and—he be quite aware of these fact. he shine with equal

brightness as a midshipman; he dare, he could lead. several woman of

thirty or thereabout adore he—before it be good for youth to be

adore. he have a knack of success, he achieve a number of thing; he

judge himself and find that this he have do “pretty decently,” and

that “pass well.” then Destiny decide apparently that he be not

think as freshly or as abundantly as he ought to do—a healthy,

successful life do not leave much time for original thinking—and

smash off the right side of his face. in a manner indeed quite

creditable to he. it be give to few man in those pacific day to get

the V.C. before the age of twenty-one.



he lie in hospital for a long spell, painful but self-satisfied. the

nature of his injury be not yet clear to he. presently he would get

all right again. “V.C.,” he whisper. “at twenty. pretty decent.”



he see himself in the look-glass with half his face bandage, and

there be nothing very shocking in that. then one day come his first

glimpse of his unbandaged self....



“one must take it decently,” he say to himself again and again through

a night of bottomless dismay.



and, “how can I look a woman in the face again?”



he stick to his bandage as long as possible.



he learn soon enough that some woman could not look he in the face

anyhow, and among they be one who should have hide her inability from

he at any cost.



and he be not only disfigured; he be crippled and unserviceable; so

the Navy decide. something have go out of his eyesight; he could no

long jump safely nor hit a ball with certainty. he could not play

tennis at all; he have ten minute of humiliation with one of the nurse,

protest all the time. “give I another chance and I’ll begin to get

into it. let I get my eye in—my only eye in. oh, the devil! give a chap

a chance!... sorry, nurse. now!... _damn!_ it’s no good. oh God! it’s no

good. what shall I do?” even his walk have now a little flavour of

precaution. but he could still shoot straight up to two or three hundred

yard.... these fact form the basis for much thinking on the part of

a young man who have take it for grant that he be destine to a

bright and leading rôle in the world.



when first he realize that he be crippled and disabled for life, he

think of suicide. but in an entirely detached and theoretical spirit.

Suicide have no real attraction for he. he mean to live anyhow. the

only question therefore be the question of what he be to do. he would

lie awake at night sketch out career that do not require

athleticism or a good presence. “I suppose it’s get to be chiefly use

my brain,” he decide. “the great trouble will be not to get fat and

stuffy. I’ve never like indoor....”



he do his good to ignore the fact that an honourable life before he

mean a life of celibacy. but he could not do so. for many reason

arise out of his temperament and the experience those woman

friendship have thrust upon he, that limitation have an effect of

dismay cruelty upon his mind. “perhaps some day I shall find a blind

girl,” he say, and feel his face doubtfully. “oh, damn!” he perceive

that the sewing up of his face be a mere prelude to the sewing up of

his life. it distress he beyond measure. it be the persuasion that

the deprivation be final that obsess he with erotic imagination.

for a time he be obsess almost to the verge of madness.



he have mood of rave anger on account of this extravagant and

uncontrollable preoccupation. he would indulge secretly in storm of

cursing, torrent of foulness and foul blasphemy that leave he

strangely relieve. but he have an unquenchable sense of the need of a

fight.



“I’ll get square with this damned world somehow,” he say. “I willn’t be

beat.”



there be some ugly and dismal aspect in his attempt not to be beat,

plunge into strange mire with remorse at the far side. they need not

deflect our present story.



“what’s the whole beastly game about anyhow?” he ask. “why be we make

like this?”



meanwhile his pride keep up a valiant front. no one should suspect he

be not cheerful. no one should suspect he feel himself to be a thing

apart. he hide his vicious strain—or make a jest of it. he develop a

style of humour that turn largely on his disfigurement. his internal

stress reflect a dry bitterness upon the world.



it be a great comfort presently to get hint that here and there other

soul have have to learn lesson as hard as his own. one day he chance

upon the paralyzed Heine’s farewell to beauty. “perhaps,” he say, “I’ve

only get by a short cut to where a lot of people must come out soon or

later. every one who live on must get bald and old—anyhow.” he take a

hint from an article he find in some monthly review upon Richard

Crookback. “a crippled body make a crippled mind,” he read. “be that

go to happen to I?”



Thence he get to: “if I think about myself now,” he ask, “what else

_can_ happen? I’ll go bitter.”



“something I can do well, but something in which I can forget myself.”

that, he realize, be his recipe.



“let’s find out what the whole beastly game _be_ about,” he decide—a

large proposition. “and stop think of _my_ personal set-back

altogether.”



but that be easy say than do.





                                  § 2



he would, he decide, “go in for science.”



he have read about science in the magazine, and about its remorseless

way with thing. science have always have a temperamental call upon his

mind. the idea of a pitiless acceptance of fact have now a great

fascination than ever for he. art be always get sentimental and

sensuous—this be in the early ’eighty; religion be mystical and

puritanical; science just look at fact squarely, and would see a

cancer or a liver fluke or a healing scar as beautiful as Venus.

moreover it tell you coldly and correctly of the skin gland of Venus.

it neither stimulate nor condemn. it would steady the mind. he have an

income of four hundred a year, and fairly good expectation of another

twelve hundred. there be nothing to prevent he go in altogether for

scientific work.



those be the great day when Huxley lecture on zoology at South

Kensington, and to he Oswald go. Oswald do indeed find science

console and inspire. scientific study be at once rare and more

touch by enthusiasm a quarter of a century ago than they be now, and

he be soon a passionate naturalist, consume by the insatiable craving

to know how. that little, long upper laboratory in the Normal School of

Science, as the place be then call, with the preparation and

diagram along one side, the sink and window along the other, the row

of small table down the window, and the ever-present vague mixed smell

of methylated spirit, Canada balsam, and a sweetish decay, open vast

new horizon to he. to the world of the eighteen-eighty the story of

life, of the origin and branch out of specie, of the making of

continent, be still the most inspiring of new romance. comparative

anatomy in particular be then a great and philosophical “new learning,”

a mighty training of the mind; the drift of biological teaching towards

specialization be still to come.



for a time Oswald think of give his life to biology. but biology

unhappily have little need of Oswald. he be a clumsy dissector because

of his injury, and unhandy at most of the practical work, he have to work

with his head on one side and rather close to what he be do, but it

dawn upon he one day as a remarkable discovery that neither personal

beauty nor great agility be demand from an explorer or collector. it

be a picture he see in an illustrated paper of H. M. Stanley traverse

an african forest in a litter, with a great retinue of porter, that

first put this precious idea into his head. “one want pluck and a

certain toughness,” he say. “I’m tough enough. and then I shall be out

of reach of—Piccadilly.”



he have excellent reason for dislike the West End. it lure he, it

exasperate he, it demoralize he and make he ashamed. he get and

read every book of african travel he could hear of. in 1885 he snatch

at an opportunity and go with an expedition through Portuguese East

Africa to Nyasa and Tanganyika. he find fatigue and illness and

hardship there—and peace of nerve and imagination. he remain in that

region of Africa for three year.



but biology and Africa be merely the field of human interest in which

Oswald’s mind be most active in those day. such inquiry be only a

part of his valiant all-round struggle to reconstruct the life that it

have become impossible to carry on as a drama of the noble and

picturesque love and adventure of Oswald Sydenham. his question lead

he into philosophy; he try over religion, which have hitherto in his

romantic phase simply furnish suitable church scenery for meeting and

repentance. he read many book, listen to preacher, hunt out any

teacher who seem to promise help in the mending of his life,

consider this “movement” and that “question.” his resolve to find what

“the whole beastly game be about,” be no pass ejaculation. he

follow the trend of his time towards a religious scepticism and an

entire neglect of current politic. religion be then at the nadir of

formalism; current politic be an outwardly idiotic, inwardly

dishonest, party duel between the follower of Gladstone and Disraeli.

social and economic question he be inclined to leave to the

professor. those be the early day of socialist thought in England,

the day before Fabianism, and he do not take to the new teaching very

kindly. he be a moderate man in æsthetic matter, William Morris leave

him tepid, he have no sense of grievance against machinery and aniline

dye, he do not grasp the worker’ demand because it be outside his

tradition and experience. Science seem to he more and more plainly

to be the big regenerative thing in human life, and the mission

immediately before man of energy be the spreading of civilization, that

be to say of knowledge, apparatus, clear thought, and release from

instinct and superstition, about the world.



in those day science be at its maximum of aggressive hopefulness. with

the idea of scientific progress there be also bind up in many british

mind the idea of a racial mission. the long napoleonic war have cut off

british thought from the thought of the continent of Europe, and this

separation be never completely heal throughout the nineteenth

century. in spite of their world-empire the British remain remarkably

self-centre and self-satisfied. they be a world-people, and no other

people be. they be at once insular and world-wide. during the

nineteenth century until its last quarter there be no real challenge to

their extra-european ascendancy. a man like Sydenham do not so much

come to the conclusion that the subjugation and civilization of the

world by science and the anglican culture be the mission of the British

Empire, as find that conclusion ready-make by tradition and

circumstance in his mind. he do not even trouble to express it; it

seem to he self-evident. when Kipling write of the White Man’s

Burden, Briton be understand. everywhere the British go about the

world, work often very disinterestedly and ably, quite unaware of the

amazement and exasperation create in French and german and american

mind by the discovery of these tranquil assumption.



so it be with Oswald Sydenham for many year. for three year he be in

the district between Bangweolo and Lake Nyasa, make his headquarters

at Blantyre, collect specimen and learn much about mankind and

womankind in that chaos of arab slaver, Scotch missionary, trader,

prospector, native tribe, Zulu raider, indian store-keeper, and

black “Portuguese”; then, discover that Blantyre have pick up a

nickname from the native of “Half face” for he, he take a temporary

dislike to Blantyre, and decide to go by way of Tanganyika either to

Uganda or Zanzibar, first send home a considerable collection of

specimen by way of Mozambique. he get through at last to Uganda, after

some ugly day and hour, only to learn of a very good reason why he

should return at once to the southern lake. he hear that a new british

consul be go up the Zambesi to Nyasaland with a british protectorate

up his sleeve, and he become passionately anxious to secure a position

near the ear of this official. there be many thing the man ought to

know at once that neither trader nor mission man would tell he.



to get any official position it be necessary for Oswald to return to

London and use the influence of various allied Sydenhams. he wince at

the thought of come back to England and meet the eye of people who

have know he before his disfigurement, but the need to have some sort

of official recognition if he be to explain himself properly in

Nyasaland make it necessary that he should come. that be in the summer

of 1889.



he go down to visit his uncle at Long Downport while the “influence”

brew, and here it be he first meet dolly. he do not know it, but now

his face be no long a shock to the observer. the injure side which

have be at first mostly a harsh, reddish blank scar with a glass eye,

have not only be bake and weather-wear by Africa, but it have in some

indefinable way be assimilate by the unmutilated half. it have be

take up into his individuality; his renascent character possess it

now; it have be humanize and become a part of he; it have acquire

dignity. muscle and nerve have reconstruct some of their relation

and partially resume abandon duty. if only he have know it, there

be nothing repulsive about he to dolly. though he be not a pretty

man, he have the look of a strong one. the touch of imagination in her

composition make she see behind this half vizor of immobilize

countenance the young hero who have risk give his life for his

fellow; his disfigurement do but witness the price he have pay. in

those day at home in England one forget that most man be brave. no

one have much occasion nor excuse for bravery. a brave man seem a

wonderful man.



he love dolly with a love in which a passion of gratitude be add to

the commoner ingredient. her smile eye restore his self-respect. he

feel he be no long a horror to woman. but could it be love she feel

for he? be not that to presume too far? she give he friendliness. he

guess she give he pity. she give he the infinite reassurance of she

frank eye. would it not be an ill return to demand more than these

gracious gift?



the possibility of humiliation—and of humiliating Dolly—touch a vein

of abject cowardice in his composition. he could not bring himself to

the test. he try some vague signal that she do not seem to

understand. his time run out and he go—awkwardly. when he return for

a second time, he return to find that Arthur’s fine profile have

eclipse his memory.





                                  § 3



after the visit that make he a godfather, Oswald do not return again

to England until his godson have attain the ripe age of four year. and

when Oswald come again he have change very greatly. he be now almost

completely his new self; the original good-look midshipman, that

sunny “type,” be bury deep in a highly individualized person, who have

in England something of the effect of a block of seasoned ship’s timber

among new-cut block of white deal. he have be use and test. he have

be scar, and survive. his obsession have lift. he have get himself

well under control.



he be now acquire a considerable knowledge of thing african, and

more particularly of those mysterious process of change and adventure

that be present to the british consciousness in those day as

“empire building.”



he have see this part of Africa change dramatically under his eye. when

first he have go out it be but a dozen year from the death of

Livingstone, who have be the first white man in this land. in

Livingstone’s wake have come rifle, missionary, and the big game

hunter. the people of the Shire Highlands be now mostly under the rule

of chief who have come into the country with Livingstone as Basuto

porter, and whom he have arm with rifle. the town of Blantyre have

be establish by Scotch missionary to preserve Livingstone’s memory

and his work. thing have go badly for a time. a certain number of lay

helper to the Church of Scotland Mission have set up as

quasi-independent sovereign, with power of life and death, about their

mission station; many of they have get completely out of hand and be

guilty of much extortion and cruelty. one of they, Fennick, murder a

chief in a drunken bout, get himself kill, and nearly provoke a

native war only a year or so before Oswald’s arrival. arab adventurer

from Zanzibar and black Portuguese from the Zambesi be also push

into this country. the Yao to the north and the Angoni-Zulus to the

south, tribe of a highly militant spirit, add their quota to a

kaleidoscope of murder, rape, robbery and incalculable chance, which

be far complicate by the annexational propaganda of more or less

vaguely accredited German, belgian, portuguese and british agent.



Oswald reach Tanganyika in the company of a steamboat (in portable

piece) which have be send by the Scotch missionary by way of the

Zambesi and Lake Nyasa; he help with its reconstruction, and take a

considerable share in fight the arab slaver between Nyasa and

Tanganyika. one of his early impression of african warfare be the

figure of a blister and wound negro stand painfully to tell his

story of the fight from which he have escape. “you see,” the Scotch

trader who be translate, explain, “he’s say they have just spear

and the Arabs have gun, and they get drive back on the lagoon into the

reed. the reed be dry, and the Arabs set they on fire. that’s how

he’s get his arm and leg burn, he say. nasty place. but they’ll heal

all right; he’s a vegetarian and a teetotaller—usually. those reed burn

like thatch, and if the poor devil run out they get stab or shoot,

and if they go into the water the crocodile would be get they. I

know that end of the lake. it’s fairly alive with crocodile. a perfect

bank holiday for the crocodile. poor devil! poor devil!”



the whole of Africa, see in those day from the viewpoint of Blantyre,

be the most desolate spectacle of human indiscipline it be possible

to conceive. everywhere be the adventurer and violence and cruelty and

fever, nowhere law and discipline. the mission man turn robber, the

trader become drunkard, the porter betray their master. mission

intrigue against mission, disobey the consul, and get at hopeless

loggerhead with the trader and early planter. where there be no

control, there be no self-control. thirst and lust rack every human

be; even some of the missionary deem it well to marry native

woman than to burn. in his own person Oswald play microcosm to human

society. he have his fall and bitter moment, but his faith in science

and civilization, human will and self-control, stumble to its foot

again. “we’ll get thing straight here presently,” he say. of himself

as of Nyasaland. “never say damned till you’re dead.”



his first return to England not only give he a futile dream of dolly to

keep he clean and fastidious in Africa, but restore his wane belief

in an orderly world. see from that distant point, the conflict in

Africa fall into a proper perspective as the froth and confusion before

the launching of a new and unprecedented peace. Africa have be a black

stew of lust, bloodshed and disease since the beginning of history.

these latter day be but the last flare-up of an ancient disorder

before the net of the law and the road and railway, the net of the

hospital and microscope and anthropologist, catch and tame and

study and master the black continent. he get his official

recognition and go back to join this new british agent, Mr. Harry

Johnston, in Nyasaland and see a kind of order establish itself and grow

more orderly and secure, over the human confusion round and about the

Shire Highlands. he find in his chief, who presently become

Commissioner and Administrator (with a uniform rather like an Admiral’s

for state occasion), a man after his own heart, with the same

unquenchable faith in the new learning of science and the same belief in

the well future that open before mankind. the Commissioner, a little

animate, talkative man of tireless interest and countless interest,

reciprocate Oswald’s liking. in Central Africa one be either too busy

or too tired and ill to do much talk, but there be one or two

evening when Oswald be alone with his chief and they could exchange

view. Johnston have a modern religious philosophy that see God chiefly

through the valiant heart of man; he make Oswald read Winwood Reade’s

_Martyrdom of Man_, which have become, so to speak, his own theological

point of departure. it be a book of sombre optimism productive of a

kind of dark hopefulness—“provided we stick it”—that accord well with

the midday twilight of the Congo forest into which Oswald be presently

send. it march with much that Oswald have be think out for

himself. it do not so much tell he new thing as crystallize his own

thought.



two idea be become the guide light of Oswald Sydenham’s thought

and life. one be the idea of self-devotion to British Imperial
