expansion. the British Empire be to be the instrument of world

civilization, the protectress and vehicle of science; the critical

examination of Imperialism in the light of these pretension have still

to come. he have still to discover that science could be talk in other

language than English, and think go on behind brown and yellow

forehead. his second idea be that the civilize process be

essentially an educational process, a training in toleration and

devotion, the tempering of egotism by wide idea. thereby “we shall get

thing straighter presently. we shall get they very straight in the long

run.”...



directly after Oswald’s second visit to England, the one in which he

become Peter’s godfather, a series of campaign against the

slave-raid arab chief, who still remain practically independent in

the Protectorate, begin. Oswald command in a very “near thing” in the

Highlands, during which he hold a small stockade against the Yao with

six Sikhs and a few Atonga for three day, and be finally rescue when

his ammunition have almost give out; and after that he be entrust

with a force of over three hundred man in the expedition that end in

the capture and hanging of old Mlozi. he fight in steamy heat and

pour rain, his head ache and his body shivering, and he end his

campaigning with a first experience of blackwater fever. it strike he

as an unutterably beastly experience, although the doctor assure he he

have be let down lightly. however, this be almost the end of the

clearing-up fighting in the Protectorate, and Oswald could take thing

easily for a time. thereafter the work of pacification, road-making, and

postal and telegraphic organization go on swiftly and steadily.



but these day of peaceful organization be end by a disagreeable

emotional situation. Oswald find himself amuse and attract by a

pretty woman he despise thoroughly and dislike a good deal. she be

the wife of a planter near Blantyre. so far from think he an ugly

and disfigured being, she make it plain to he that his ugliness be an

unprecedented excitement for she. always imprison in his mind be the

desire to have a woman of his very own; at time he envy even the Yao

warrior their black slave mistress; and he be more than half

disposed to snatch this craving creature in spite of the lie and trick

and an incessant chatter vanity that disfigure her soul, and end all

his work in Africa, to gratify, if only for some lurid month, his

hunger for a human possession. the situation take he by surprise in a

negligent phase; he pull up sharply when he be already look down a

slippery slope of indignity and dishonour. if he have as yet do no

foolish thing he have think and say they. the memory of dolly come to

he in the night. he declare to himself, and he try to declare it

without reservation, that it be well to sit for a time within a yard

of dolly’s inaccessible goodness than paint a Protectorate already

british enough to be scandal-love, with the very bright hue of

passion’s flame-colour. he run away from this woman.



so he come back—by no mean single-mindedly. there be lapse indeed on

the slow steam journey to Egypt into almost unendurable torment of

regret. of which, however, no trace appear when he come into the

presence of dolly and his godson at the Ingle-Nook.





                                  § 4



Peter take to Oswald and Oswald take to Peter from the beginning.



Peter, by this time, have Joan for a foster-sister. and also he have

Nobby. Nobby be a beloved dutch doll, armless and legless, but adore

and trust as no other doll have ever be in the whole history of doll

since the world begin. he have be Peter’s first doll. one day when he

be play tune with Nobby on the nursery fender, one exceptionally

accent note splinter off a side of Nobby’s smooth but already much

obliterate countenance. Peter be not so much grieve as dismayed, and

Arthur be very sympathetic and do his good to put thing right with a

fine brush and some black paint. but when Peter see Oswald he meet he

with a cry of delight and recognition.



“it’s Nobby!” he cry.



“but who’s Nobby?” ask Oswald.



“_You_—Nobby,” Peter insist with a squeak, and turn about just in

time to prevent Arthur from hide the fetish away. “Gimme my Nobby!” he

say.



“Nobby be his private god,” dolly hasten to explain. “it be his

dear possession. it be the most beautiful thing in the world to he.

every night he must have Nobby under his pillow....”



Oswald stand with his wooden double in his hand for a moment, recognize

himself at a glance, think it over, and smile his grim, one-side

smile.



“I’m Nobby right enough,” he say. “Big Nobby, Peter. he take you off

to Dreamland. some day I’ll take you to the Mountains of the Moon.”



so far Joan, a black-head, black-eyed doll, have be coyly on the edge

of the conversation, a little disposed to take refuge in the skirt of

Mary. now she make a great effort on her own account. “Nobby,” she

scream; “big, _big_ Nobby!” and, realize she have make a success, hide

her face.



“Nobby to you,” say Oswald. “do _that_ want a godfather too? it’s my

rôle....”





                                  § 5



the change in the Stubland nursery, though they be the most apparent,

be certainly not the great in the little home that look over the

Weald. Arthur have be unfaithful to dolly—on principle it would seem.

that do not reach Oswald’s perception all at once, though even on his

first visit he feel a difference between they.



the later ’ninety be the “Sex Problem” period in Great Britain. not

that sex have be anything else than a perplexity in all age, but it

be just about this time that that unanswerable “why not?”—that

bacterium of social decay, spread out from the dark corner of

unventilated religious dogma into a moribund system of moral, reach,

in the case of the child of the serious middle-class of Great

Britain, this important field of conduct. the manner of the question and

the answer remain still serious. those be the day of “the Woman who

do” and the “Keynote Series,” of adultery without fun and fornication

for conscience’ sake. Arthur, with ample leisure, a high-grade bicycle,

the consciousness of the artistic temperament and a gnawing secret

realization, which have never leave he since those early day in

Florence, that dolly do not really consider he as an important person

in the world’s affair, be all too receptive of the new suggestion.

after some discursive liberal conversation with various people he find

the complication he seek in the young of three plain but passionate

sister, who live a decorative life in a pretty little modern cottage

on the edge of a wood beyond Limpsfield. the new gale of emancipation

send a fire through her vein. her soul within she be like a flame. she

write poetry with a peculiar wistful charm, and her decorative method

be so similar to Arthur’s that it seem natural to conclude they

might be the precursor of an entirely new school. they put a new

interest and life into each other’s work. it become a sort of

collaboration....



the affair be not all priggishness on Arthur’s part. the woman be

honestly in love; and for most man love make love; there be a pride and

fascination for they in a new love adventure, in the hesitation, the

dash, the soft capture, the triumph and kindness, that can manage with

very poor excuse. and such a beautiful absence of mutual criticism

always, such a kindly accept blindness in passionate eye!



at first dolly do not realize how Arthur be round off his life. she

be busy now with her niece, her disreputable eld brother’s love

child, as well as Peter; she do not miss Arthur very much during his

increase absence. then Arthur, who wish to savour all the aspect

of the new situation, reveal it to her one August evening in general

term by a discourse upon polygamy.



dolly’s quick mind seize the situation long before Arthur could state

it.



she do not guess who her successful rival be. she do not know it be

the young Miss Blend, that familiar dark squat figure, quick and

almost crowded in speech, and with a peculiar avidity about her manner

and bear. she assume it must be some person of transcendant and

humiliating merit; that much her romantic standard demand. she be

also a little disgusted, as though Arthur have discover himself to be

physically unclean. her immediate impulse be to arrest a specific

confession.



“you forget instinct, Arthur dear,” she say, colour brightly. “what

you say be perfectly reasonable, wonderfully so. only—it would make I

feel sick—I _mean_ sick—if, for example, I think _you_——”



she turn away and look at the view.



“be you so sure that be instinct? or convention?” he ask, after a

pause of half comprehension.



“Instinct—for certain.... lover be one. whither you go, _i_ go—in the

spirit. you cann’t go alone with another woman while I—while I—— in those

thing.... oh, it’s inconceivable!”



“that’s a primitive point of view.”



“love—lust for the matter of that.... they _be_ primitive thing,” say

dolly, undisguisedly wretche.



“there’s reason in the control of they.”



“Polygamy!” she cry scornfully.



Arthur be immensely disconcert.



he light a cigarette, and his movement be slow and clumsy.



“idea may differ,” he say lamely....



he do not make his personal confession after all.



in the middle of the night Arthur be lie awake thinking with unusual

violence, and for the first time for a long while see a question from

a standpoint other than his own. also he fancy he have hear a sound of

great significance at bedtime. that uncertain memory worry he more

and more. he get up now with excessive precaution against noise and

creep with extreme slowness and care to the little door between his room

and dolly’s. it be lock.



_then she have understand!_



a solemn, an almost awe-stricken Arthur paddle back to his own bed

through a pool of moonlight on the floor. a pair of pallid, blue-vein

foot and bright pyjama leg and a perplexed, vague continuation upward

be all the moon could see.





                                  § 6



it be, it seem to Arthur, a very hard, resolute and unapproachable

dolly who meet he at the breakfast-table on the brick terrace outside

the little kitchen window. he reflect that the ultimate injury a wife

can do to a husband be ruthless humiliation, and she be certainly

make he feel most abominably ashamed of himself. she have always, he

reflect, make he feel that she don’t very greatly believe in he.

there be just a touch of the spitfire in dolly....



but, indeed, within dolly be a stormy cavern of dismay and indignation

and bitter understanding. she have weep a great deal in the night and

think interminably; she know already that there be much more in this

thing than a simple romantic issue.



her first impulse have be quite in the romantic tradition: “never

again!” and “now we part!” and “henceforth we be as stranger!”



she have already get ten thousand mile beyond that.



she do not even know whether she hate he or love he. she doubt if

she have ever know.



her state of mind be an extraordinary patchwork. every possibility in

her be be in a state of intense excitement. she be sway by a

violently excited passion for he that be only restrain by a still

more violent resolve to punish and prevail over he. he have never seem

so good-look, so pleasant-face, so much “old Arthur”—or such a

fatuous being. and he be watch she, watch she, watch she,

obliquely, furtively, while he pretend awkwardly to be at his ease.

what a scared _comic_ thing Arthur could be! there be moment when she

could have scream with laughter at his solicitous face.



meanwhile some serviceable part of her mind devote itself to the table

need of Joan and Peter.



Peter be disposed to incite Joan to a porridge-eat race. you just

look at Joan and begin to eat fast very quietly, and then Joan would

catch on and begin to eat fast too. her spoon would go quick and

quick, and make a noise—whack, whack, whack! and as it be necessary

that she should keep her wicked black eye fix on your plate all the

time to see how you be get on, she would sometimes get an empty

spoon up, sometimes miss her mouth, sometimes splash. but Mummy take a

strong hand that morning. there be an argument, but Mummy be unusually

firm. she turn breakfast into a drill. “fill spoon. ’Tention! Mouf.

withdraw spoon.” not bad fun, really, though Mummy look much too stern

for any liberty. and daddy ben’t game for a diversion. wouldn’t look

at a little boy....



after breakfast Arthur decide that he be not go to be bully. he

get out his bicycle and announce in a dry, offhand tone that he be

go out for the day.



“so long, Guv’nor,” say dolly, as off-handedly, and stand at the door

in an expressionless way until he be beyond the green road gate.



then she stroll back through the house into the garden, and stand for

a time consider the situation.



“so I be to bring up two baby—and grow old, while _this_ go on!” she

whisper.



she go to clear the thing off the breakfast-table, and stand

motionless again.



“my God!” she say; “why ben’t I bear a man?”



and that, or some image that follow it, let her thought out to Africa

and a sturdy, teak-complexion figure with a one-sided face under its

big sun-helmet....



“why don’t I marry a man?” she say. “why don’t I get I a mate?”





                                  § 7



these be the primary factor of the situation that Oswald, arrive

six week later, be slowly to discover and comprehend. as he do so he

feel the self-impose restraint of his relation to Arthur and dolly

slip from he. Arthur be now abundantly absent. never before have Oswald

and dolly be so much alone together. Peter and Joan in the foreground

be a small restraint upon speech and understanding.



but now this story fall away from romance. romance require that a

woman should love a man or not love a man; that she should love one man

only and go with the man of her choice, that no other consideration,

unless it be duty or virtue, should matter. but dolly find with

infinite dismay that she be divide.



she love certain thing in Oswald and certain thing in Arthur. the

romantic tradition which rule in these matter, provide no

instruction in such a case. the two man be not sufficiently

contrast. one be not black enough; the other not white enough. Oswald

be a strong man and brave, but Arthur, though he live a tame and

indolent life, seem almost insensible to danger. she have never see

he afraid or rattle. he be a magnificent rock climber, for example;

his physical nerve be perfect. everything would have be so much

simple if he have be a “soft.” she be sensitive to physical quality.

it be good to watch Arthur move; Oswald’s injury make he clumsy and

a little cautious in his movement. but Oswald be grow into a

politician; he have already take great responsibility in Africa; he

talk like a prince and like a lover about his Atonga and his Sikhs,

and about the white-clothe kingdom of Uganda and about the fantastic

gallant Masai, who must be save from extermination. that princely way

of thinking be the fine thing about he; there he outshone Arthur. he

be wonderful to she when he talk of those central african kingdom

that be rot into chaos under the influence of the arab and

european invasion, chaos from which a few honest englishman might yet

rescue a group of splendid people.



he could be loyal all through; it be his nature. and he love she—as

Arthur have never love she. with a gleam of fierceness. as though there

be a streak of anger in his love.



“why do you endure it?” he fret. “why do you endure it?”



but he be irritable, absurd about many little thing. he could lose his

temper over game; particularly if Arthur play too.



yet there be a power about Oswald. it be a quality that make her fear

he and herself. she fear for the freedom of her spirit. if ever she

become Oswald’s she would become his much more than she have ever be

Arthur’s. there be something about he that be real and commanding, in

a sense in which nothing be real about Arthur.



she have a dread, which make she very wary, that one day Oswald would

seize upon she, that he would take she in his arm and kiss she. this

possibility accumulate. she have a feeling that it would be something

very dreadful, painful and enormous; that it would be like be

brand, that therewith Arthur would be abolish for she.... at the

thought she realize that she do not want Arthur to be abolish. she

have an enormous kindliness for Arthur that would have be impossible

without a little streak of humorous superiority. if Oswald threaten

she with his latent mastery, Arthur have the appeal of much dependence.



and apart from Oswald or Arthur, something else in her protest, an

instinct or a deeply-rooted tradition. the thought of a second man be

like thinking of the dislocation of her soul. it involve a nightmare of

overlapping, of partial obliteration, of contrast and replacement, in

thing that she feel could have no honour or dignity unless they be as

simple and natural as inadvertent action....



the thing that sway she most towards Oswald, oddly enough, be his

mutilate face. that hold she back from any decision against he. “if I

do not go with he,” she think, “he will think it be that.” she could

not endure that he should be so wounded.



then, least personal and selfish thought of all, be the question of

Joan and Peter. what would happen to they? in any case, dolly know they

would come to she. there be no bitter vindictiveness in Arthur, and he

shirk every responsibility he could. she could leave he and go to

Uganda and return to they. she know there would be no attempt to deprive

she of Peter. Oswald would be as good a father as Arthur. the child

weigh on neither side.



dolly’s mind have become discontinuous as it have never be discontinuous

before. none of these thing be in her mind all the time; sometimes

one aspect be uppermost and sometimes another. sometimes she be rule

by nothing but vindictive pride which urge she to put herself on a

level with Arthur. at time again her pride be white and tight-lipped,

exhort she above all thing not to put herself on a level with

Arthur. when Oswald press she, her every impulse be to resist; when

he be away and she feel her loneliness—and his—her heart go out to

he.



she have give herself to Arthur, that seem conclusive. but Arthur have

dishonour the gift. she have a great sense of obligation to Oswald. she

have love Oswald before she have ever see Arthur; year ago she have

give her cousin the hope and claim that burn accusingly in his eye

today.



“come with I, dolly,” he say. “come with I. share my life. this ben’t

life here.”



“but could I come with you?”



“if you dare. not to Blantyre, perhaps. That’s—respectable. church and

woman and chatter. Blantyre’s over. but there’s Uganda. Baker take a

wife there. it’s still a land of wild romance. and I must go soon. I

must get to Uganda. so much be happen. Muir say this Soudanese

trouble willn’t wait.... but I hang on here, day after day. I cann’t leave

you to it, dolly. I cann’t endure that.”



“you _have_ to leave I,” she say.



“no. come with I. this soft grey-green countryside be no place for you.

I want you in a royal leopard skin with a rifle in your hand. you be

pale for want of the sun. and while we be out there _he_ could divorce

you. he would divorce you—and marry some other copper puncher. some

Craftswoman. and stencil like hell. then we could marry.”



he grip her wrist across the stone table. “dolly, my darling!” he

say; “don’t let I go back alone.”



“but what of Peter and Joan?”



“leave they to nurse for a year or so and then bring they out to the

sun. if the boy stay here, he will grow up—some sort of fiddle

artist. he will punch copper and play about with book-binding.”



she struggle suddenly to free her wrist, and he grip they tight

until he see that she be look towards the house. at last he realize

that Arthur approach.



“oh, _damn_!” say Oswald....





                                  § 8



dolly cut this knot she could not untie, and as soon as she have cut it

she begin to repent.



Indecision may become an unendurable torment. on the one hand that dark

strong life in the african sunblaze with this man she fear in spite of

his unconcealed worship, call to a long-suppress vein of courage in

she be; on the other hand be her sense of duty, her fastidious

cleanness, this english home with its thousand gentle association and

Arthur, Arthur who have suddenly abandon neglect, become attentive,

mutely apologetic, but who have say not a word, since he have put himself

out of court, about Oswald.



he have say nothing, but he have become grave in his manner. once or

twice she have watch he when he have not know she watch he, and she

have try to fathom what be now in his mind. do he want she?



this and that pull she.



one night in the middle of the night she lie awake, unable to sleep,

unable to decide. she go to her window and press her forehead

against the pane and stare at the garden in a mist of moonlight. “I

must end it,” she say. “I must end it.”



she go to the door that separate her room from Arthur’s, and unlock

it noisily. she walk across the room and stand by the window. Arthur

be awake too. he lean up upon his elbow and regard she without a

word.



“Arthur,” she say, “be I to go to Africa or be I to stay with you?”



Arthur answer after a little while. “I want you to stay with I.”



“on my condition?”



“I have be a fool, dolly. it’s over....”



they be both tremble, and their voice be unsteady.



“can I believe you, Arthur?” she ask weakly....



he come across the moonlight to she, and as he speak his tear come.

Old, tender, well-remember phrase be on his lip. “dolly! little

sweet dolly,” he say, and take she hungrily into his arm....



there remain nothing now of the knot but to tell Oswald that she have

make her irrevocable decision.





                                  § 9



Arthur be eloquent about their reconciliation. what become of her rival

dolly never learn, nor greatly care; she be turn out of Arthur’s

heart, it would seem, rather as one turn a superfluous cat out of

door. Arthur allude to the emotional situation generally as “this

mess.” “if I’d have proper work to do and some outlet for my energy this

mess wouldn’t have happen,” he say. he announce in phrase only too

obviously derivative that he must find something _real_ to do.

“something that will take I and use I.”



but dolly be manifestly unhappy. he decide that the crisis have

overtax she. Oswald must have worry she tremendously. (he think it

be splendid of she that she never blame Oswald.) the garden, the

place, be full now of painful association—and moreover the reject

cat be well within the range of a chance meeting. travel among

beautiful scenery seem the remedy indicate. their income happen to

be a little overspent, but it only add to his sense of rise to a

great emotional emergency that he should have to draw upon his capital.

they start upon a sort of recrudescence of their honeymoon, begin

with Rome.



Aunt Phyllis and Aunt Phœbe come to mind the house and Joan and Peter.

Aunt Phœbe be write a little wise poetical book about education,

mostly out of her inner consciousness, and she seize the opportunity of

this experience very gladly....



dolly be a thing of mood for all that journey.



at time she be extravagantly hilarious, she be wild, as she have never

be before. she would start out to scamper about a twilit town after a

long day’s travel, so that it be hard for Arthur to keep pace with she

flit energy; she would pretend to be Tarantula-bite in some

chestnut grove and dance love dance and flee like a dryad to be pursue

and catch. and at other time she sit white and still as though she have

a broken heart. never do an entirely virtuous decision give a woman so

much heartache. they go up Vesuvius by night on mule from Pompeii,

and as they stand on the black edge of the crater, the guide call she

attention to the vast steely extent of the moonlit southward sea.



she hear herself whisper “Africa,” and wonder if Arthur too have

hear.



and at Capri Arthur have a dispute with a boatman. the boat be take at

the Marina Grande. the boatman propose the tour of the island and all

the grotto, and from the Marina Grande the project seem reasonable

enough. the sea, though not glassy smooth, be quite a practicable sea.

but a point have to be explain very carefully. the boatman put it in

slow and simple italian with much helpful gesture. if the wind rise to a

storm so that they would have to return before complete this “giro,”

they would still pay the same fee.



“oh quite,” say Arthur carelessly in English, and the bargain be make.



they work round the corner of the island, under the Salto di Tiberio,

that tower cliff down which the legend say Tiberius fling his

victim, and as soon as they come out from under the lee of the island

Arthur discover a cheat. the gathering wind beyond the shelter of the

cliff be cut up the blue water into a disorderly system of

tumble white-cap wave. the boat head straight into a storm. it

lift and fall and sway and stagger; the boatman at his oar

dramatically exaggerate his difficulty. “he know of this,” say

Arthur savagely. “he think we shall want to give in. well, let’s see

who give in first. let’s put he through his program and see how he

like it.”



Arthur have take off his hat, and clutch it to save it from the wind.

he look very fine with his hair blow back. “Buona aria,” he say,

grin cheerfully to the boatman. “Bellissima!”



the boatman be understand to say that the wind be rise and that it

be go to be bad presently.



“Bellissima!” say Arthur, pat dolly’s back.



the boatman be seize with solicitude for the lady.



dolly survey the great cliff that tower overhead and the frothy

crest against which the boat smack and lift. “Bellissima,” she

agree, smile at the boatman’s consternation. “Avanti!”



the boat plunge and plough its way for a little while in silence. the

boatman suggest that thing be get dangerous. could the signora

swim?



Arthur assure he that she could swim like a fish.



and the capitano?



Arthur accept his promotion cheerfully and assure the boatman that

his swimming be only second to dolly’s.



the boatman inform they that he himself could scarcely swim at all. he

be not properly a seafare man. he have come to Capri for his health;

his lung be weak. he have be a stonemason at Alessandria, but the

dust have be bad for his lung. he could not swim. he could not manage

a boat very well in stormy weather. and he be an orphan.



“_Io_ Orfano!” cry Arthur, greatly delighted, and stab himself

with an elucidatory forefinger. “Io Orfano anche.”



the boatman lapse into gloom. in a little while they have beat round

the headland into view of the Faraglione, that big outstanding rock

which be pierce by a great arch, upon the south-eastern side of the

island. the passage through this Arco Naturale be in the boatman’s

agreement. they could see the swirl of the water now through that

natural gateway, rise, pour almost to the top of the arch and then

swirl down to the trough of the wave. the west wind whip the

orphan’s blue-black curl about his ear. he begin to cry off his

bargain.



“we go through that arch,” say Arthur, “or my name be not Stubland.”



the boatman argue his case. the wind be rise; the further they go

the more they come into the weather. he have not the skill of a man bear

to the sea.



“you make the bargain,” say Arthur.



“let we return while we be still safe,” the boatman protest.



“go through the arch,” say Arthur. the boatman look at the arch, the

sky, the endless onslaught of advance wave to seaward and Arthur, and

then with a gesture of despair turn the boat towards the arch.



“he’s frighten, Arthur,” say dolly.



“serve he right. he willn’t try this game again in a hurry,” say Arthur,

and then relent: “go through the arch and we will return....”



the boatman baulk at the arch twice. it be evident they must go

through just behind the crest of a wave. he head in just a moment or

so too soon, get through on the very crest, bent double to save his

head, make a clumsy lunge with his oar that strike the rock and throw

he sideways. then they be rush with incredible swiftness out of

the arch down a blue-green slope of water, and the Faraglione rise again

before dolly’s eye like a thing relieve after a moment of intense

concentration. but suddenly everything be sideways. everything be

askew. the boat be half overturn and the boatman be sit

unsteadily on the gunwale, clutch at the opposite side which be

rise, rise. the man, she realize, be go overboard, and Arthur’s

swift grab at he do but complete the capsize. the side of the boat be

below she where the floor should be, and that give way to stream

bubble water into which one man plunge on the top of the other....



dolly leapt clear of the overturn boat, go under and come up....



she toss the wet hair from her head and look about she. the

Faraglione be already thirty yard or more away and recede fast. the

boat be keel upward and roll away towards the cliff. there be no

sign of Arthur or the boatman.



what must she do? just before the accident she have note the Piccola

Marina away to the north-west. that would mean a hard swim against the

wave, but it would be the good thing to do. it could not be half a mile

away. and Arthur? Arthur would look after himself. he would do that all

right. she would only encumber he by swim around. perhaps he would

get the man on to the boat. perhaps people have see they from the

Piccola Marina. if so boat would come out to they.



she strike out shoreward.



how light one’s clothe make one feel! but presently they would drag.

(never meet trouble half-way.) it be go to be a long swim. even if

there should be no current....



she swam....



then she have doubt. ought she to go back and look for Arthur? she could

not be much good to he even if she find he. it be her first duty to

save herself. Peter be not old enough to be leave. no one would care for

Joan and he as she could care for they. it be a long enough swim

without look for Arthur. it be go to be a very long swim....



she wish she could get a glimpse of Arthur. she look this way and

that. it would be easy to swim side by side. but in this choppy sea he

might be quite close and still be hide.... good not to bother about

thing—just swim.



for a long time she swam like a machine....



after a time she begin to think of her clothe again. the wave now

seem to be try to get they off. she be be tug back by her

clothe. could she get some of they off? not in this rough water. it

would be more exhausting than helpful. clothe ought to be easy to get

off; not so much tie and pin....



the wave be come fast now. the wind must be freshen. they be

more numerous and less regular.



Splash! that last wave be a trencherous beast—no!—treacherous beast....

Phew, ugh! salt in the mouth. salt in the eye. and here be another,

too soon!... oh _fight_!



it be hard to see the Piccola Marina. wait for the lift of the next

wave.... she be go too much to the left, ever so much too much to

the left....



one must exert oneself for Peter’s sake.



what be Arthur do?



it seem a long time now since she have get into the water, and the

shore be still a long way off. there be nobody there at all that she

could see.... boat draw high and dry. plenty of boat. extraordinary

people these Italians—they let stonemason take charge of boat.

extortionate stonemason.... she be horribly tired. not in good

fettle.... she look at the Faraglione over her shoulder. it be still

disgustingly near and big. she have hardly swum a third of the way yet.

or else there be a current. well not think of current. she have to

stick to it. perhaps it be the bad third of the way she have do. but

what infinite joy and relief it would be just to stop swim and

spread one’s arm and foot!



she have to stick to it for little Peter’s sake. for little Peter’s sake.

Peter too young to be leave....



Arthur? good not to think about Arthur just yet. it have be silly to

insist on the Arco Naturale....



what a burthen and bother dress be to a woman! what a leaden

burthen!...



she must not think. she must not think. she must swim like a machine.

like a machine. one.... two.... one.... two.... slow and even.



she fall asleep. for some moment she be fast asleep. she wake up with

the water rise over her head and strike out again.



there be a sound of many water in her ear and an enormous indolence

in her limb against which she struggle in vain. she do struggle, and

the thought that spur she to struggle be still the thought of Peter.



“Peter be too young to be leave yet,” sing like a refrain in her head as

she rouse herself for her last fight with the water. Peter be too

young to be leave yet. Peter, her little son. but the salt blind she

now; she be altogether out of step with the slow and resolute rhythm of

the wave. they break foaming upon she and beat upon she, and presently

turn she about and over like a leaf in an eddy.









                           CHAPTER the fourth

                   FIRST impression of the UNIVERSE





                                  § 1



Peter could not remember a time when Joan be not in his world, and from

the beginning it seem to he that the chief fact be Mary. “nanny,”

you call she, or “Mare-_wi_,” or you simply howl and she come. she

be omnipresent; if she be not visible then she be just round the

corner, by night or day. other figure be more intermittent, “daddy,”

a large, loud, exciting, almost terrific thing; “Mummy,” who be soft

and make gentle noise but be, in comparison with Mary, rather a fool

about one’s bottle; “Pussy,” and then the transitory smile

propitiatory human stuff that be difficult to remember and name

correctly. “auntie,” “mannie” and suchlike. but also there be

inanimate person. there be the brass-head sentinel about one’s cot

and the great brown round-head newel post. his name be Bungo-Peter;

he be a king and know everything, he watch the stair, but you do

not tell people this because they would not understand. also there be

the brass-eyed monster with the triple belly who be call

Chester-Drawers; he sham dead and watch you, and in the night he

creak about the room. and there be Gope the stove, imprison in the

fender with hell burn inside he, and there be Nobby. Nobby be the

protector of little boy against Chester-Drawers, stray bear, the thing

on the landing, spider scratching and many such discomfort of nursery

life. of course you could also draw a deep breath and yell for

“Mare-_wi_,” but she be apt not to understand one’s explanation and to

scold. it be well to hold tight to Nobby. and also Nobby be lovely

and go whack.



moreover if you call “Mare-_wi_,” then when the light come Joan would

sit up in her cot and stare sleepily while you be be scold. she

would say that she _know_ there ben’t such thing. and you would be

fill with an indefinable sense of foolishness. behind an impenetrable

veil of darkness with an intervene floor space acrawl with bear and

“burdlar” she could say such thing with impunity. in the morning one

forgot. Joan in the daytime be a fairly amusing companion, except that

she sometimes try to touch Nobby. once Peter catch she play with

Nobby and pretend that Nobby be a baby. one hand take Nobby by the

head, and the other take Joan by the hair. that be the time when Peter

have his first spanking, but Joan be careful not to touch Nobby again.



generally Joan be passable. of course she be an intrusion and in the

way, but if one want to march round and round shout “Tara-ra-ra,

ra-ra, ra-ra, Tara _boom_ de ay,” bang something, a pan or a drum,

with Nobby, she could be trust to join in very effectively. she be

good for noise-marche always, and they would not have be any fun

without she. she have the processional sense, and know that her place be

second. she talk also in a sort of way, but it be not necessary to

listen. she could be manage. if, for example, she touch Peter’s

brick he yell in a soul-destroy way and go for she with a brick

in each hand. she be quick to take a hint of that sort.



it be Arthur’s theory that little child should not be solitary.

mutual aid be the basis of social life, and from their early year

child must be accustom to co-operation. they have to be train for

the co-operative commonwealth as set forth in the writing of Prince

Kropotkin. Mary think differently. so Arthur use to go in his

beautiful blue blouse and sit in the sunny nursery amidst the toy and

the child, incite they to premature co-operation.



“now Peter put a brick,” he use to say.



“now Joan put a brick.”



“now Dadda put a brick.”



Mary use to watch proceeding with a cynical and irritate expression.



“Peter’s tower,” Peter would propose.



“_our_ tower,” Arthur use to say.



“Peter knock it over.”



“no. no one knock it over.”



“Peter put _two_ brick.”



“very well.”



“Dadda not put any more brick. no. Peter finish it.”



“na-ow!” from Joan in a voice like a little cat. “_I_ finish it.”



Arthur want to preserve against this original sin of individualism. he

get quite cross at last impose joyful and willing co-operation upon

two highly resistant mind.



Mary’s way be altogether different. she greatly appreciate the fact

that dolly and Arthur have have the floor of the nursery cover with cork

carpet, and that Arthur at the suggestion of Aunt Phœbe have get a

blackboard and chalk in order to instil a free gesture in draw from

the early year. with a piece of chalk Mary would draw a line across

the floor of the nursery, fairly divide the warmth of the stove and

the light of the window.



“that’s your bit, Peter,” she would say, “and that’s your bit, Joan.

they’s your share of brick and they’s your. now don’t you think of

go outside your bit, either of you, whatever you do. nohow. nor touch

so much as a brick that ben’t your.”



Whereupon both child would settle down to play with infinite

contentment.



yet these individualist be not indifferent to each other. if Joan

want to draw on the blackboard with chalk, then always Peter want to

draw on the blackboard with chalk at the same time, and here again it

be necessary for Mary to mark a boundary between they; and if Peter

want to build with brick then Joan do also. each be uneasy if the

other be not in sight. and they would each do the same thing on

different side of their chalk boundary, with a wary eye on the other’s

proceeding and with an endless stream of explanation of what they be

do.



“Peter’s build a love-i-lay house.”



“Joan’s building, oh!—a lovelay-er house. Wiv a cross on it.”



“why not build one lovely house for both of you?” say Arthur, still

with the co-operative Commonwealth in mind.



neither child consider that his proposal call for argument. it go

over their head and vanish. they continue build individually as

before, but in silence lest Arthur should be tempt to intervene again.





                                  § 2



Joan be a dancer from the age of three.



perhaps she get some hint from dolly, there be no telling; but anyhow

she frisk and caper rhythmically by a kind of instinct whenever

dolly play the piano. so dolly show her step and then more step.

Peter do not take to dance so readily as Joan and his disposition be

towards burlesque. Joan dance for the love of dancing, but Peter be

inventive and turn his dance into expression. he invent the Fat

Dance, with a pillow under his pinafore, the Thin Dance, with a concave

stomach and a meagre expression, the one Leg dance and the Bird Dance,

this latter like the bird about the crumb in winter time. also the

Tipsy Dance, bacchic, which Arthur think vulgar and discourage. dolly

teach Joan the Flower Dance, with a very red cap like a pistil, and

white silk skirt petal uphold by her arm. these she open slowly, and

at last drop and then droop. this need a day of preparation.

Peter produce his first remember æsthetic judgment on a human being

on this occasion.



“_pritty_ Joan,” he say with conviction, as she stand flushed and

bright-eyed after the dance, and with that he go and kiss she.



“he’s begin young,” say Arthur.



it be what all parent say, and it be true of all child. but parent

keep on say it....



before he be fully four Peter be conduct an æsthetic analysis of

his world. he like some of the tune dolly play and disapprove of

other. he distribute “pritty” lavishly but by no means

indiscriminately over the thing of the world. “oh pritty fo’wers,” be

the primordial form of these expand decision. but he know that Nobby

be not pretty.



Arthur do his good to encourage and assist these budding appreciation.



one evening there be a beautiful still sunset. the sun go down, a

great flattening sphere of redden gold sink into vast level of

blue over the remoter hill. Joan have already be carry off to bed,

but Arthur seize upon Peter and stand he in the window seat. “look,”

say Arthur. Peter look intently, and both his parent sit beside he,

watch his nice little round head and the downy edge of his intent

profile.



“look,” say Arthur, “it go. it go. it’s go ... go ...

go....”



the sun become a crescent, a red scimitar, a streak of fire.



“ah!” say Arthur, “it’s go.”



come an immense pause.



“do it _adain_, Dadda,” say Peter with immense approval. “do it

adain....”





                                  § 3



the theory of ideal play almost as important a part in the early

philosophy of Peter as it do in the philosophy of Plato. but Peter do

not call they “ideal,” he call they “toy.” toy be the simplified

essence of thing, pure, perfect and manageable; real thing be

troublesome, uncontrollable, over complicated and largely irrelevant. a

Real Train, for example, be a poor, big, clumsy, limited thing that be

oblige to go to Red Hill or Croydon or London, that be full of stuffy

unnecessary stranger, usually sit firmly in the window seat, that

you could do nothing satisfactory with at all. a Toy Train be your very

own; it take you wherever you want, to Fairyland or Russia or

anywhere, at whatever pace you choose. then there be a beautiful rag

doll name “Pleeceman,” who have a comic, almost luminous red nose, and

smile perpetually; you could hit Joan with he and make her squawk and

yet be sure of not hurt she within the meaning of the law; how

inferior be the great formless lump of a thing, with a pale uneventful

visitor’s sort of face we see out of the train at Caterham! nobody could

have lift he by a leg and wave he about; and if you have shy he

into a corner, instead of all go just anyhow and still smile, he

would probably have be cross and revengeful. how inferior again be

the real cow, with its chew habit, its threaten stare and moo and

its essential rudeness, to Suzannah, the cow on the green board. perhaps

the good real thing in the world be young pig....



but this much be simply to explain how it be that Peter be grateful

but not overwhelmed to find that there be also a real Nobby in

existence as well as his beloved fetish. and this Nobby be, as real

thing go, much well than one could have expect he to be. Peter’s

heart go out to he from the very first encounter, and never find

reason to relinquish he again.



Nobby waste a good lot of time that might have be well employ in

play, by talk to Mummy; and when a little boy set himself to rescue

his friend from so tepid an occupation, Mary show a peculiar

disposition to thwart one. “oh! _leave_ they alone,” she say, with the

tart note in her voice. “I’m sure they don’t want either of you.”



still Mummy don’t always get Nobby, and a little boy and girl could

hear he talk and play about with he. when he tell really truly thing

it be well than any one else tell story. he have have all sort of

experience; he have be a sailor; _he know what be inside a ship_.

that have be a grow need in Peter’s life. all Peter’s ship have be

solid hitherto. and Nobby have be in the same field, practically

speak, with lion ever so many time. lion, of course, be not

nearly so dreadful as bear in a little boy’s world; bear be the most

dreadful thing in the world (especially be this true of the black,

under-bed bear, _Ursus Pedivorus_) but lion be dreadful enough. if one

see one in a field one would instantly get back over the stile again and

go home, Mary or no Mary. but one day near Nairobi, Nobby have come upon

a lion in broad daylight right in the middle of the path. Nobby have

nothing but a stick. “I be in a hurry and I feel annoyed,” say Nobby.

“so I just walk towards he and wave my stick at he, and shout to

he to get out of my way.”



“_yes?_” breathless.



“and he go. Most lion will get away from a man if they can. not

always though.”



a pause. there be evidently another story to that. “tell we,” say

Mummy, more interested even than the child.



Big Nobby make model african village out of twig and suchlike nothing

in the garden, and he bring down Joan and Peter box of Zulu warrior

from London to inhabit they. also he buy two box of “egyptian camel

corps.” one wet day he “make Africa” on the nursery floor. he make

mountain out of book and wood block, and put a gold-mine of gold

paper therein; he get in a lot of twig of box from the garden and make

the most lovely forest you can imagine; he build village of brick for

the Zulus; he put out the animal of Peter’s Noah’s ark in the wood.

“here’s the lion,” he say, prop up the lion against the tree

because of its break leg.



“Gurr Woooooah!” say Joan.



“exactly,” say Nobby, encourage she.



“Waar-oooh. Waaaa!” say Joan, presume on it.



“Bang!” say Peter. “you’re _dead_, Joan,” and stop any more of that.





                                  § 4



then one day an extraordinary thing happen. it be towards lunch-time,

and Mary be bring Joan and Peter home from a walk in the wood. Joan

be tired, but Peter have be enterprise and have run on far ahead; he

be trot his fat leg down the rusty lane that run through the

bush close to the garden fence when he see Nobby’s lank form come

towards he from the house, walk slowly and as if he couldn’t see

where he be go. Peter be for slip into the bush and jump

out at he and say “Boo.” then he see Nobby stop and stand still and

stare back at the house, and then, most wonderful and dreadful! this

great big grown-up begin to sob and cry. he say “Ooo-er!” just as Peter

do sometimes when he feel unendurably ill-use. and he keep raise his

clench fist as if he be go to shake they—and not do so.



“I will go to Hell,” say Nobby. “I will go to Hell.”



in a passion!



(Peter be shocked and ashamed for Nobby.)



then Nobby turn and see Peter before Peter could hide away from he.

he stop cry at once, but there be his funny face all red and

shiny on one side.



“Hullo, old Peter boy,” say Nobby. “I’m off. I’m go right away. be

fool.”



so that be it. but haven’t he Africa and lion and elephant and black

man to go to, a great Real Play Nursery instead of a Nursery of Toys?

why make a fuss of it?



he come to Peter and lift he up in his arm. “good-bye, old Peter,”

he say. “good-bye, Peter. keep off the copper punching.” he kiss his

godson—how wet his face was!—and put he down, and be go off along

the path and Peter haven’t say a word.



he want to cry too, to think that Nobby be go. he stare and then

run a little way after his friend.



“Nobby,” he shout; “good-bye!”



“good-bye, old man,” Nobby cry back to he.



“good-bye. gooood-bye-er.”



then Peter trot back to the house to be first with the sad but

exciting news that Nobby have go. but as he come down from the green

wicket to the house he look up and see his father at the upstairs

window, gaze after Nobby with an unusual expression that perplex

he, and in the little hall he find his mother, and she have be cry

too, though she be pretend she haven’t. they know about Nobby.

something strange be in the air, perceptible to a little boy but

utterly beyond his understanding. perhaps Nobby have be naughty. so he

think it well to change the subject, and begin talk at once about a

wonderful long bicycle with no less than three man on it—not two, Mummy,

but three—that he have see upon the highroad. they have thin white silk

shirt without sleeve, and ride furiously with their head down. their

shirt be blow out funnily behind they in the middle of their back.

they go like _that_!...



all through the midday meal nobody say a word about Nobby....



nobody ever do say anything about Nobby again. when on a few occasion

Peter himself talk appreciatively of Nobby nobody, unless it be Joan

now and then, seem the least bit interested....



one side consequence of Oswald’s visit have be the dethronement of the

original Nobby. the real Nobby have somehow thrust the toy Nobby into the

background. perhaps he drift into the recess of some box or

cupboard. at any rate when Peter think of he one day he be nowhere

to be find. that do not matter so much as it would have do a couple

of month before. now if the bear and “burdlar” get busy in the

night-nursery Peter use to pretend that the pillow be the real Nobby,

the Nobby who ben’t even afraid of lion and have drive off one with a

stick. a prowling bear haven’t much chance against a little boy who

snuggle up to _that_ Nobby.





                                  § 5



Mummy be rather dull in those day, and daddy seem always to be

look at she. daddy have a sort of inelasticity in his manner too.

suddenly Aunt Phyllis and Aunt Phœbe appear, and it be announce that

daddy and Mummy be go off to Italy. it be too far for they to take

little boy and girl, they say, and besides there be, oh! _horrid_

spider. and Peter must stay to mind the house and Joan and his aunt;

it ben’t right not to have some man about. he be to have a sailor suit

with trouser also, great responsibility altogether for a boy not much

over four. so there be a great kissing and go off, and Joan and

Peter settle down to the rule of the aunt and only miss Mummy and

daddy now and then.



then one day something happen over the child’s head. Mary have red

eye and wouldn’t say why; the aunt have tell she not to do so.



Phyllis and Phœbe decide not to darken the child’s life by wear

mourning, but Mary say that anyhow she would go into black. but neither

Joan nor Peter take much notice of the black dress.



“why don’t Mummy and daddy come back?” ask Peter one day of Aunt

Phœbe.



“they’ve travel to such wonderful place,” say Aunt Phœbe with a

catch in her voice. “they may not be back for ever so long. no. not till

Peter be ever so big.”



“then why don’t they send we cull’d poce-card like they did’t first?”

say Peter.



Aunt Phœbe be so take aback she could answer nothing.



“they just forget we,” say Peter and reflect. “they go on and

on.”



“ben’t Nobby ever come back either?” he ask, abruptly, display a

devastating acceptance of the new situation.



“but who’s Nobby?”



“that’s Mr. Oswald Sydenham,” say Mary.



“he’s come back quite soon,” say Aunt Phœbe. “he’s on his way now.”



“’Cos he _promise_ I a lion skin,” say Peter.





                                  § 6



Aunts Phyllis and Phœbe find themselves two of the four guardian

appoint under Arthur’s will.



it have be one of Arthur’s occasional lapse into deceit that he

destroy the will which make Oswald the sole guardian of Joan—so far as

he could dispose of Joan—and Peter, without say a word about it to

dolly. he have vacillate between various substitute for Oswald up to

the very moment when he name the four upon whom he decide finally, to

his solicitor. some streak of jealousy or pride, combine with a doubt

whether Oswald would now consent to act, have first prompt the

alteration. instead he have decide to shift the responsibility to his

sister. then a twinge of compunction have make he replace Oswald. then

feel that Oswald might still be out talk or out vote by his

sister, he have stick in the name of dolly’s wealthy and important

cousin, Lady Charlotte Sydenham. he have only see she twice, but she have

seem a lady of considerable importance and strength of character.

anyhow it make thing fair to the Sydenham side.



but Phyllis and Phœbe at once assume, not without secret gladness, that

the burthen of this responsibility would fall upon they. Oswald Sydenham

be away in the heart of Africa; Lady Charlotte Sydenham be also

abroad. she have telegraph, “unwell impossible to return to England six

week continue child’s life as hitherto.” that seem to promise a

second sleeping partner in the business.



the sister decide to continue the Ingle-Nook as the child’s home,

and make the necessary arrangement with Mr. Sycamore, the family

solicitor, to that end.



they discuss their charge very carefully and fully. Phyllis be for a

meticulous observance of Arthur’s know or assume “wish,” but phœbe

take a broad view. Mary too point out the danger of too literal an

adhesion to precedent.



“we want everything to go on exactly as it do when _they_ be alive,”

say Phyllis to Mary.



“thing ’ave get to be different,” say Mary.



“not if we can help it,” say Aunt Phyllis.



“they’ll _grow_,” say Mary after reflection.



Phœbe become eloquent in the evening.



“we be to have the advantage of maternity, Phyllis, without—without

the degradation. it be a solemn trust. blessed be we among woman,

Phyllis. I feel a Madonna. we _be_ Madonnas, Phyllis. Modern Madonnas.

just touch by the Wings of the dove.... these little soul drop

from heaven upon our knee.... Poor Arthur! it be our task to guide his

offspring to that high destiny he might have attain. _look_, Phyllis!”



with her flat hand she indicate the long garden path that dolly have

plan.



Phyllis peer forward without intelligence. “what be it?” she ask.



Phyllis perceive that Phœbe be flush with poetical excitement. and

phœbe’s voice drop mystically to a deep whisper. “don’t you see?

_white lily!_ a coincidence, of course. but—beautiful.”



“for a child with a high destiny, I doubt if Peter be careful enough

with his clothe,” say Phyllis, try to sound a less pre-raphaelite

note. “he be a perfect little Disgrace this afternoon.”



“the darling! but I understand.... Joan too have much before she,

Phyllis. as yet their mind be blank, _tabula rasa_; of either of they

there be still to be made—_anything_. Peter—upon this Rock I set—a New

Age. when woman shall come to their own. Joan again. Joan of Arc.

coincidence no doubt. but leave I my fancy. fancy—if you will. for

I they be no fancy. before the world, Phyllis, we be make for

this.”



she rest her chin on her hand, and stare out into the blue twilight,

a brood prophetess.



“only a woman can understand a woman,” she say presently. “not a word

of this, Phyllis, to Others.”



“I wish we have buy some cigarette this afternoon,” say Phyllis.



“the little red glow,” reflect Phœbe indulgently. “it help. but I

don’t want to smoke tonight. it would spoil it. smoke! let the flame

burn clear awhile.... we will get in cigarette tomorrow.”





                                  § 7



Joan and Peter remain unaware of the great destiny before they. More

observant person than they be might have guess there be deep

meaning in the way in which Aunt Phœbe smooth back their hair from

their forehead and say “ah,” and bid they “mark it well” whenever she

impart any general statement, but they take these thing merely as she

particular way of manifest the irrational quality common to all

grow-up people. also she would say “Dignity! your mission!” when they

howl or fight. it be to the manuscript that grow into a big and

big pile upon what have be Arthur’s writing-desk in Arthur’s

workroom, that she restrict she most stirring idea. she write there

daily, go singe to it as healthy young man go singe to their

bathroom. she splash her mind about and refresh herself greatly.

she write in a large hand, punctuate chiefly with dash. she have

conceive her book rather in the manner of the prophetic work of the

admire Mr. Ruskin—with Carlylean lapse. it be to be call _Hail

Bambino and the Grain of Mustard Seed_. it be all about the

tremendousness of child.



the conscientious valiance of Aunt Phœbe be very manifest in the

opening. “Cæsar,” the book begin, “and the son of Semele burst strangely

into this world, but Jesus, Mohammed, Confucius, Newton, Darwin, Robert

Burns, be bear as peacefully as you or I. Nathless they come for such

end—if indeed one can think of any end thereto!—as blot out the

star. yesterday a pule babe—for Jesus pule, Mohammed pule, let we

not spare ourselves, Newton, a delicate child, pule most

offensively—Herod here and bacteria there, infantile colic, tuberculosis

and what not, search for each little life, in vain, and so today

behold spring victoriously from each vital granule a tree of

Teaching, of Consequence, that bud and burgeon and shoot and for ever

spread so that the Gates of Hell may not prevail against it! here it be

the Tree of Spirituality, here the Tree of Thought, predestine

intertwiner with the Tree of Asgard, here in our last instance a

chant Beauty, a hearten lyrical Yawp and Whirlaboo. and forget it

not, whatever else be forget, the Word of the Wise, ‘_as the twig be

bent the tree incline_.’ so it be and utterly that we realize the

importance of education, the pregnant intensity of the least urgency,

the hint, the gleam, the offering of service, to these First Tender

year.”



here Aunt Phœbe have draw breath for a moment, before she embark upon

her second paragraph; and here we will leave Aunt Phœbe glow amidst

her empurpled prose.



Joan and Peter take the substitution of Aunt Phœbe muttering like a

Sibyl overhead and Aunt Phyllis, who be really amusing with odd

drawing and twisted paper toy and much dancing and run about, in

the place of Daddy and Mummy, with the stoical acceptance of the very

young. about Daddy and Mummy there hang a faint flavour of departure but

no sense of conclusive loss. no clear image and expectation of a return

have be form. no day of definite disappointment ever come. after all

the essential habitual person, Mary, be still there, and all the little

important routine of child-life continue very much as they have always

do.



yet there be already the dawn of further apprehension in Peter’s mind

at least. one day Peter pick up a dead bird in the garden, a bird dead

with no injury manifest. he try to make it stand up and peck.



“it ain’t no good, Master Peter; it’s dead,” say Mary.



“what’s dead?” say Peter.



“_that_ be.”



“_gone_ dead,” say Peter.



“and willn’t ever go anything else now—except smell,” say Mary.



Peter reflect. later he revisit the dead bird and be see in

profound meditation over it. then he repair to Aunt Phyllis and

confide his intention of immortality.



“Peter,” he say, “not go dead—nohow.”



“of course not,” say Aunt Phyllis. “he’s get too much sense. the idea!”



this be reassure. but alone it be not enough.



“Joan not go dead,” he say. “no.”



“certainly she shan’t,” say Aunt Phyllis and await further decision.



“Pussy not go dead.”



“not until ninety time nine.”



“Aunt Phyllis not go dead. marewi not go dead.”



he reflect far. he try, “Mummy and daddy not go dead....”



then after thought, “when be Daddy and Mummy come back again?”



Aunt Phyllis tell a wise lie. “some day. not for a long time. they’ve

go—oh, ever so far.”



“far than ever so,” say Peter.



he reflect. “when they come back Peter will be a big boy. Mummy and

daddy ’ardly know ’im.”



and from that time, Daddy and Mummy cease to be think of further as

immediate presence, and become hero and heroine in a dream of tomorrow,

a dream of return happiness when life be dull, of release and

vindication when life be hard, a pleasant dream, a hope, a basis for

imaginative anticipation and pillow fairy tale, sleep parent like

those sleep king who figure in the childhood of nation, like King

Arthur or Barbarossa. sometimes it be one parent and sometimes it be

the other that dominate the thought, “when Mummy come back.... when

daddy come back.”



Joan learn very soon to say it too.





                                  § 8



Death be too big a thing for Peter to comprehend. he have hardly begin

yet with life. and he have make not even a beginning with religion. he

have never be baptize; he have learn no prayer at his mother’s knee.

the priceless Mary have come to the Stublands warrant a churchwoman,

but as with so many of her class, her orthodoxy have be only a

professional uniform to cloak a very keen hostility and contempt for the

clergy, and she drop quite readily into the way of a household in

which religion be entirely ignore. the first Peter hear of religion

be at the age of four and a half, and that be from a serious friend of

Mary’s, a Particular Baptist, who come for a week’s visit to the

Ingle-Nook. the visitor be really distressed at the spiritual outlook

of the two child. she borrow Peter for a “little walk.” she think

she would begin with he and try Joan afterwards. then as plainly and

impressively as possible she impart the element of her faith to Peter

and teach he a brief, simple prayer. “he’s a love,” she tell Mary,

“and so quick! it’s a _shime_ to keep he such a little heathen. I

don’t say that prayer over twice before he have it Pat.”



Mary be rather move by her friend’s feeling. she feel that she be

go behind the back of the aunt, but nevertheless she see no great

harm in what have happen. the death of Arthur and Dolly have shake

Mary’s innate scepticism; she have a vague feeling that there might be

grave risk, well worth consideration, beyond the further edge of life.



Aunt Phyllis be the first of the responsible people overhead to

discover what have happen. Peter love his prayer; it be full of the

most beautiful phrase; no word have ever so fill his mouth and mind.

there be for example, “for Jesus Krice sake amen.” like a song. you

could use it anywhere. Aunt Phyllis find he play train with his

brick in the nursery one afternoon. “_Hoo!_ chuff-Chuff. chuff-Chuff.

change for Reigate, change for London. for Jesus Krice sake amen.”



Aunt Phyllis sit down in the little chair. “Peter,” she say, “who be

this Jesus Krice?”



Peter be reluctant to give information. “I know all about ’Im,” he

say, and would at first throw no other light on the matter.



then he relent and tell a wonder. he turn his back on his brick

train and draw close to Aunt Phyllis. his manner be solemn and

impressive exactly as Mary’s friend’s have be; his word be as slow

and deliberate. “Jesus Krice could go dead and come alive again,” he

say, “over and over, whenever he want to.”



and having pause a moment to complete the effect of this marvel, Peter

turn about again, squat down like a little brown holland mushroom

with a busy little knob on the top, and resume his shouting. “_Hoo!_

Chuff-Chuff. chuff-Chuff. chuff.”





                                  § 9



one day Mary with an unaccustomed urgency in her manner hurry Joan and

Peter out of the garden and into the nursery, and there tidy they up

with emphasis. Joan show fight a bit but not much; Peter be think

of something else and be just limp. then Mary take they down to the

living-room, the big low room with the ingle-nook and the dining-table

in the far bay beside the second fireplace. there they behold a large

female Visitor of the bad sort. they approach she with extreme

reluctance, impel by Mary’s gentle but persistent hand. the Visitor

be sit in the window-seat with Aunt Phyllis beside she. and Aunt

Phœbe be stand before the little fireplace. but these be

incidental observation; the great fact be the Visitor.



she be the large lady that Peter have ever see; she have a plume hat

with black chiffon and large purple bow and a brim of soft black stuff

and suchlike thing, and she wear a large cape in three tier and a

large black feather boa that hiss when she move and disseminate

feather. her shoulder be enormously exaggerate by a kind of vast

epaulette, and after the custom of all loyal Anglicans in those day her

neck be tightly swathe about and adorn with a big purple bow.

everything she wear have be decorate and sew upon, and her chequer

skirt below be cut out by panel and revelation of flounce purple.

in the midst of this costume, beneath the hat and a pale blonde fuss of

hair, be set a large, pale, freckle, square-feature face with two

hard blue eye and a fascinating little tussock of sandy hair grow

out of one cheek that instantly capture the eye of the little boy. and

out of the face proceed a harsh voice, slow, loud, and pitch in that

note of arrogance which be the method of the rule class in those

day. “so _these_ be our little ward,” say the voice, and as she

speak her lip wrinkle and her tooth show.



she turn to Phyllis with a confidential air, but speak still in the

same clear tone. “which be the by-blow, my dear, the boy or the Gel?”



“Lady Charlotte!” exclaim Phyllis, and then speak inaudibly,

explain something.



but Peter make a note of “by-blow.” it be a lovely word.



“not even in Black. they ought to wear Black,” he hear the big lady

say.



then he find himself be scrutinize.



“Haugh!” say the big lady, make a noise like the casual sound

emit by large wade bird. “they both take after the Sydenhams,

anyhow. they might be brother and sister!”



“practically they be,” say Aunt Phœbe.



Lady Charlotte confute she with an unreal smile. “practically _not_,”

she say decisively.



there be a little pause. “well, Master Stubland,” say the Visitor

abruptly and quite terrifyingly. “what have _you_ get to say for

yourself?”



as Peter have not yet learn to swear freely, he have nothing to say for

himself just at that moment.



“not very bright yet,” say Lady Charlotte goadingly. “I suppose they

have run wild hitherto.”



“it be poor Arthur’s wish——” begin Aunt Phyllis.



“we must alter all that now,” Lady Charlotte interrupt. “tell I your

name, little boy.”



“Peter Picktoe,” say Peter with invention. “you go to stop here

long?”



“so you’ve find your tongue at last,” say Lady Charlotte. “that’s only

your nickname. what’s your proper name?”



“can we go out in the garden now, Auntie?” say Peter; “and play at

by-blow?”



“garden now,” say Joan.



“he’s bright than you seem to think,” say Aunt Phœbe with gentle

sarcasm.



“Commina _Garden_,” say Joan, tug at Peter’s pinafore.



“but I must ask he his name first,” say Lady Charlotte, “and,” with

grow firmness, “he must tell it I. come! what be your name, my

dear?”



“Peter,” prompt Mary.



“Peter,” say Peter, satisfy that it be a silly game and anxious to

get it over and away from this horror as soon as possible.



“and who give you that name?”



“nobody; it’s mine,” say Peter.



“ben’t the poor child even _begin_ to learn his Catechism?” ask

Lady Charlotte.



“yes, the garden,” say Aunt Phœbe to Mary, and the scene begin to close

upon the child as they move gardenward. Joan dance ahead. Peter

follow thoughtfully before Mary’s gentle urgency. what be that last

word? “cattymism?” then a fresh thought occur to he.



“Mary,” say Peter, in an impassioned and all too audible undertone;

“look. she’s get a Whisker. _here!_ Troof!”



“it be my brother’s _wish_,” Phyllis be explain as the child

disappear through the door....



“it ben’t the modern way to begin so early with rote-learning,” say

Aunt Phœbe; “the little fellow’s still not five.”



“he’s a pretty good size.”



“because we haven’t worry his mind yet. milk, light, play, like a

happy little animal.”



“we must change all that now,” say Lady Charlotte Sydenham with

conviction.









                           CHAPTER the FIFTH

                            the christening





                                  § 1



Lady Charlotte Sydenham be one of those large, ignorant, ruthless,

low-church, wealthy, and well-bear lady who do so much to make

England what it be in the day before the Great War. she be educate

with the utmost care by totally illiterate governess who be lady

by birth, chiefly on the importance and privilege of her social

position, the anglican faith and Mrs. Strickland’s “Queens of England”;

she have French from a guarantee protestant teacher and German from a

north german instructress (Lutheran Protestant), who also teach she to

play the piano with the force and precision of a crack regiment of

cavalry. subsequently she have improve her mind by read memoir and

biography of noble and distinguished people and by travel amidst

obvious scenery and good foreign hotel. she have marry at

two-and-thirty when thing be begin to look rather doubtful for

she.



Old Mr. Sydenham, who have make his money and undermine his health in

India in the John Company day, have be fifty-four, and from the very

outset she have be ever so much too much for he. at sixty-five he have

petere out like an exhausted lode. she have already get an abject

confidential maid into thorough training, and be fully prepared for

widowhood. she hang out big black bonnet and expensive black clothe

upon her projection, so as to look large than ever, and take her place

and even more than her place, very resolutely, among the leader of the

county Anglicans.



she have early master the simple art of county family intercourse. her

style in contradiction be very good, her insult be frequently witty,

she could pretend to love horse, there be no need for she to pretend

to despise and hate tradesman and work people, and she keep herself

well-inform upon the domestic detail of the large and spread

family of the “Dear Queen.” she be very good at take down impertinent

people, and most people strike she as impertinent; she could make a

young man or a plain girl or a social inferior “feel small” quick (and

small) than almost any one in that part of Surrey. she be a woman

without vice; her chief pleasure be to feel all right and important

and the centre of thing, and to that she maid as a sort of grand

Vizieress, her well-discipline little household and her choice of

friend minister. the early fear of “Romanists” in which she have be

train have be a little dispel by the wide charity of maturity,

but she hold secularist and socialist in an ever-deepening abhorrence.

they plan, she know, to disturb the mind of the low class, upset

her investment, behead the Dear Queen, and plunge the whole world into

vice and rapine and Sabbath-breaking. she interest herself in such

leisure as the care of her own health and comfort leave she, in movement

design to circumvent and defeat the aim of these enemy of God and

(all that be worth consider in) man. she even countenance quite

indulgent charity if they seem design to take the wind out of the

sail of socialism. she drive about the district in a one-horse carriage

and deliver devastating call.



such be the lady whom Arthur have make one of the four guardian of his

little son and niece. he have see she twice; he have rather like a short

speech of five sentence she make at a Flower Show, and he have hear she

be extremely rude to a curate. he believe she to be wealthy and

trustworthy and very well suited to act as a counter influence to any

extravagant tendency there might be in Aunt Phœbe. also she be

dolly’s cousin, and appoint she have seem a sort of compensation for

alter his will without dolly’s knowledge. besides, it have be very

unlikely that she would ever act. and he have be in a hurry when he

alter his will, and could not think of any one else.



now Lady Charlotte be not by any mean satisfied by her visit to the

Ingle-Nook. the child look unusually big for their year and

disrespectful and out of hand. it be clear they have not take to she.

the nurse, too, have a sort of unbroken look in her eye that be

unbecoming in a menial position. the aunt be odd person; Phyllis be

much too disposed to accentuate the father’s wish, and Lady Charlotte

have a most extraordinary and indecent feel all the time she be

talk to she that Aunt Phœbe ben’t wear stay. (could the woman

have forget they, or be it deliberate? it be like pretend to be

clothe when you be really naked.)



their conversation have be queer, most queer. they do not seem to

realize that she be by way of be a leader in the county and

accustomed to be listen to with deference. nearly everything she

say they have quietly contradict or ignore. the way in which the

child be whisk away from her presence be distinctly

disrespectful. she have a right, it be her duty, to look at they well

and question they clearly about their treatment, to see that they have

proper treatment, and it be necessary that they should fully understand

her importance in their life. but those two oddly-dress young

woman—youngish woman, rather, for probably they be both over

thirty—do not themselves seem to understand that she be naturally the

Principal Guardian.



Phyllis have be constantly refer to the wish of this Stubland

person who have marry George Sydenham’s dolly. apparently the woman

suppose that those wish be to override every rational consideration

for the child’s welfare. after all, the boy be as much dolly’s child

as a Stubland, and as for the girl, except that the Stublands have be

allow to keep she, she ben’t a stubland at all. she ben’t anything

at all. she be pure Charity. there be not the slight obligation

upon any one to do anything for she. make she out to be an equal with

a legitimate child be just the subversive, wrong-head sort of thing

these glorify shoddy-maker, the Stublands, would do. but like to

like. their own genealogy probably wouldn’t bear scrutiny for six

generation. she ought to be train as a Maid. there be none too many

train Maids nowadays. but Arthur Stubland have actually settle money

on she.



there be much to put right in this situation, a great occasion for a

large, important lady to impress herself tremendously on a little group

of people insultingly disposed to be unaware of she. the more she

think the matter over the more plainly she see her duty before she.

she do not talk to servant; no lady talk to servant; but it be her

habit to think aloud during the ministration of Unwin, she maid, and

often Unwin would overhear and reply quite helpfully.



“it’s an odd job I’ve get with these two new Wards of mine,” she say.



“they put too much on you, m’lady,” say Unwin, pin.



“I shall do what be right. I shall see that what be right be do.”



“you don’t spare yourself enough, m’lady.”



“I must go over again and again. those woman don’t like I. I disturb

they. they’re up to no good.”



“it willn’t be the first Dark Place, m’lady, you’ve throw light into.”



the lady survey her reflection in the glass with a knowing expression.

she knit her brow, partly close one eye, and nod slowly as she

speak.



“there’s something queer about the boy’s religious instruction. it’s

be keep back. now why do they get embarrassed when I ask _who_

be the godparent? I ought to have follow that up.”



“my godfather and godmother wherein I be make,” murmur Unwin, with

the quiet satisfaction of the well-instruct.



“properly it’s the business of the godparent. I have a right to know.”



“I suppose the poor boy _have_ godparent, m’lady,” say Unwin, come up

from obscure duty with the skirt.



“but of _course_ he have godparent!”



“pardon I, m’lady, but not _of course_.”



“but what do you mean, Unwin?”



“I hardly like to say it, m’lady, of relation, ’owever distant, of

our. still, m’lady——”



“don’t chew it about, Unwin.”



“then I out with it, m’lady. ’ave they been baptize, m’lady, either of

they? ’ave they been baptize?”





                                  § 2



before a fortnight be out Lady Charlotte have make two more visit to

the Ingle-Nook, she have have an acrimonious dispute upon religious

question with Phœbe, and she be well on her way to the terrible

realization that these two apparently imbecile lady in the shapeless

“arty” dress be really socialist and secularist—of course, like

all other socialist and secularist, “of the bad type.” it be

impossible that those two unfortunate child should be leave in their

aunt’ “clutch,” and she prepare herself with a steadily increase

determination and grandeur to seize upon and take over and rescue these

two innocent soul from the moral and spiritual destruction that

threaten they. once in her hand, Lady Charlotte be convince it

would not be too late to teach the little fellow a proper respect for

those in authority over he and to bring home to the girl an adequate

sense of that taint upon her life of which she be still so shockingly

unaware. the boy must be teach not to call attention to people’s

physical peculiarity, and to answer properly when speak to; a certain

sharpness would not be lose upon he; and it be but false kindness to

the girl to let she grow up in ignorance of her disadvantage. soon or

later it would have to be bring home to she, and the later it be the

more difficult would it be for she to accept her proper position with a

become humility. and a thing of immediate urgency be, of course, the

baptism of both these little lose soul.



in pursuit of these entirely praiseworthy aim Lady Charlotte be

subject to a series of very irritate rebuff that do but rouse she

to a great firmness. on her fourth visit she be not even allow to

see the child; the specious excuse be make that they be “out for a

walk,” and when she pass that over forgivingly and say: “it do not

matter very much. what I want to arrange today be the business of the

christening,” both aunt begin to answer at once and in almost identical

word. Phœbe give way to her sister. “if their parent have want they

christen,” say Aunt Phyllis, “there be ample time for they to have

have it do.”



“_we_ be the parent now,” say Lady Charlotte.



“and two of we be quite of the parent’ mind.”



“you forget that I also speak for my nephew Oswald,” say Lady

Charlotte.



“but _do_ you?” say Aunt Phyllis, with almost obtrude incredulity.



“certainly,” say Lady Charlotte, with a sweeping, triumphant gesture, a

conclusive waving of the head.



“you know he be on his way back from Uganda?” Aunt Phyllis remark with

an unreal innocence.



Lady Charlotte have not know. but she stand up gallantly to the blow. “I

know he will support I by insist upon the proper treatment of these

poor child.”



“what can a man know about the little soul of child?” cry Phœbe.



but Aunt Phyllis restrain she. “I have no doubt Mr. Sydenham will have

his own view in the matter,” say Phyllis.



“I have no doubt he will,” say Lady Charlotte imposingly....



even Mary show the same disposition to insolence. as Lady Charlotte

be return along the little path through the bush that run up to

the high road where her carriage with the white horse wait, she see
