Mary and the child approach. Peter see Lady Charlotte first and

fly back. “Lady wiv de Whisker!” he say earnestly and breathlessly,

and dodge off into the bush. Joan hesitate, and flee after he. by a

detour the flutter little figure outflank the great lady and

escape homeward.



“come _here_, child!” she cry. “I want you.”



Spurt on the part of the child.



“they be really most distressingly rude,” she say to Mary. “it’s

inexcusable. tell they to come back. I have something to say to they.”



“they willn’t, Mum,” say Mary—though surely aware of the title.



“but I tell you to.”



“it’s no good, Mum. it’s shyness. if they willn’t come, they willn’t.”



“but, my good woman, have you _no_ control?”



“they always race ’ome like that,” say Mary.



“then you ben’t fit to control they. as one of the child’s

guardian, I—— but we shall see.”



she go her way, a stately figure of passion.



“Orty old Ag,” say Mary, and dismiss the encounter from her mind.





                                  § 3



“you get your right like anybody, m’lady,” say Unwin.



it be that phrase put it into Lady Charlotte’s head to consult her

solicitor. he open new vista to her imagination.



Lady Charlotte’s solicitor be a lean, long, fade blond of forty-five

or so. he be the descendant of five generation of Lincoln’s Inn

solicitor, a Low Churchman, a man of notoriously pure life, and very

artful indeed. he talk in a thin, high tenor voice, and be give to

nibble his thumbnail and wince with his eye as he talk. his

thumbnail produce gap of indistinctness in his speech.



“power of a guardian, m’lady. defend upon whafower want exercise over

thinfant.”



“I do _wish_ you’d keep your thumb out of your mouth,” say Lady

Charlotte.



“sorry,” say Mr. Grimes, wince and try painfully to rearrange his

arm. “still, I’d like to know—position.”



“there be three other guardian.”



“generous allowance,” say Mr. Grimes. “do you all act?”



“one of we be lose in the Wilds of Africa. the other I want to consult

you about. they do not seem to I to be fit and proper person to be

entrust with the care of young child, and they do not seem dispose

to afford I a proper share in the direction of affair.”



“ah!” say Mr. Grimes, replace his thumb. “see t’point t’Chacery.”



Lady Charlotte disregard this comment. she wish to describe Aunts

Phyllis and Phœbe in her own word.



“they be quite extraordinary young woman—not by any stretch of language

to be call lady. they dress in that way—like the picture in the

Grosvenor Gallery.”



“æsthetic?”



“I could find a harsh word for it. they smoke. not a nice thing for

child to see. I suspect they strongly of vegetarianism. from

something one of they say. in which case the child will not be

properly nourish. and they speak quite openly of socialism in front of

their charge. neither of the poor little creature have be buy a

scrap of mourning. not a scrap. I doubt if they have even be make to

understand that their parent be dead. but that be only the beginning.

I be totally unable to ascertain whether either of the poor mite have

be christen. apparently they have not....”



Mr. Grimes withdraw his thumb for a moment. “you be perfectly within

yer right—insist—knowing”—thumb replaced—“all thlese thing.”



“exactly. and in have my say in their general upbringing.”



“how far do they prevent that?”



“oh; they get in my way. they send the child out whenever they feel I

be come. they do not listen to I and accept any suggestion I make.

Oh!—sniff at it.”



“and you want to make ’em?”



“I want to do my duty by those two child, Mr. Grimes. it be a charge

that have be lay upon I.”



Mr. Grimes reflect, rub his thumb thoughtfully along the front of

his tooth.



“they be get no religious instruction whatever,” say Lady

Charlotte. “none.”



“Hot be the ’ligion father?” say Mr. Grimes suddenly.



Lady Charlotte be not to be deter by a silly and inopportune

question. she just pause for an instant and redden. “he be a member

of the Church of England,” she say.



“even if he ben’t,” say Mr. Grimes understandingly, but with thumb

still in place, “ligion necessary t’welfare. case of Besant Chil’n

zample. Thlis be klistian country.”



“I sometimes doubt it,” say Lady Charlotte.



“legally,” say Mr. Grimes.



“if the law do its duty!”



“you don’t wanner goatallaw fewcan ’void it?” ask Mr. Grimes, grasp

his job.



Lady Charlotte assume an expression of pained protest, and lift one

black-gloved hand. Mr. Grimes hastily withdraw his thumbnail from his

mouth. “I be say, Lady Charlotte, that what you want to do be to

assert your authority, if possible, without legal proceeding.”



he be try to get the whole situation clear in his mind before he

tender any exact advice. Most child who be quarrel over in this

way gravitate very rapidly into the care of the Lord Chancellor; to that

no doubt these child would come; but Lady Charlotte be a prosperous

lady with a lot of fight in she and a knack of illegality, and before

these child become ward in Chancery she might, under suitable

provocation, run up a very considerable little bill for expense and

special advice in extract she from such hole as she get herself

into. it be an unjust libel upon solicitor that they tempt their

client into litigation. so far be this unjust that the great majority

will spare neither time nor expense in get a case settle out of

court.



nor do Lady Charlotte want to litigate. court be uncertain,

irritate place. she just want to get hold of her two ward, and to

deal with they in such a way as to inflict the maximum of annoyance and

humiliation upon those queer Stubland aunt. and to save the child

from socialism, secularism, Catholicism, and all the wandering wolf of

opinion that lie in wait for the improperly train.



but also she go in fear of Oswald. Oswald be one of the few human

being of whom she go in awe. he be always rude and overbearing with

she. from the very first moment when he have see she as his uncle’s new

wife, he have realize in a flash of boyish intuition that if he do not

get in with an insult first, he would be her victim. so his first word

to she have be an apparently involuntary “o God!” then he have pretend

to dissemble his contempt with a cold politeness. those be the day of

his good look; he be as tall and big as he be ever to be, and she have

expect a “little midshipmite,” whom she would treat like a child, and

possibly even send early to bed. from the first she be at a

disadvantage. he have a material hold on she too, now. he be his uncle’s

heir and her trustee; and she have the belief of all victorian woman in

the unlimited power of Trustees to abuse their trust unless they be

abjectly propitiate. he use to come and stay in her house as if it be

already his own; the servant would take their order from he. she be

assure grime as she have assure the Stubland aunt that he be on her

side; “the Sydenhams be all sound churchman.” but even as she say this

she see his grim, one-sided face and its one hard intent eye pin

she. “act without authority again, my good aunt,” he would say.

“you’ll get yourself into trouble yet.”



that be one of his invariable stab whenever he come to see she. always

he would ask, soon or later, in that first meeting:



“any one bag you for libel yet? _no!_ or insult behaviour? some

one will get you soon or later.”



“anything that _I_ say about people,” she would reply with dignity, “be

true, Oswald.”



“they’ll double the damage if you stick _that_ out.”...



and she see he now stand beside the irritating, necessary grime,

sardonically ready to take part against she, prepare even to give those

abominable aunt an unendurable triumph over she....



“I want no vulgar litigation,” she say. “everything ought to be do as

quietly as possible. there be no need to ventilate the family affair of

the Sydenhams, and particularly when I tell you that one of the child

be——” she hesitate. “Irregular.”



the thumb go back, and Mr. Grimes’ face assume a diplomatic

innocence. “whascalle a love-shild?”



“exactly,” say Lady Charlotte, with a nod that forbid all research for

paternity. if Joan be assume to be of Stubland origin, so much the

well for Lady Charlotte’s case. “everything must be do quietly and

privately,” she say.



“sactly,” say Mr. Grimes, and be remind of his thumb by her eye. he

cough, put his arm down, and sit up in his chair. “_they_ have

possession of the child?” he say.



“should I be here?” she appeal.



“_ah!_ that give the key of the situation.... would _they_ litigate?”



“why should they?”



“if by chance you get possession?”



“that would be difficult.”



“but not impossible? perhaps something could be manage. with my

assistance. once or twice before I have have case that turn on the

custody of minor. Custody, like possession, be nine point of the law.

then _they_ would have to come into court.”



“we want nobody to come into court.”



“exactly, m’lady. I be point out to you how improbable it be that

they will do so. I be gauge their disinclination.”



the attitude of Mr. Grimes relax unconsciously until once more the

tooth and thumbnail be at their little play again.



he continue with thoughtful eye upon his client’s expression.

“possibly _they_ wouldn’t li’e ’nquiry into character.”



“oh, _do_ take that thumb away!” cry Lady Charlotte. “and _don’t_

lounge.”



“I’m sorry, m’lady,” say Mr. Grimes, sit up. “I be say,

practically, do we know of any little irregularity, anything—I willn’t

say actually immoral, but _indiscreet_, in these two lady’ life?

anything they wouldn’t like to have publicly discuss. in the case of

most people there’s a something. few people will readily and cheerfully

face a discussion of Character. even quite innocent people.”



“they’re certainly very lax—very. they smoke. inordinately. I see the

cigarette stain on their finger. and unless I be very much mistaken,

one of they—well”—Lady Charlotte leant forward towards he with an air

of scandalous condescension—“she wear no stay at all, Mr. Grimes—none

at all! no! she’s a very queer young woman indeed in my opinion.”



“M’m!... no visitor to the house—no _gentleman_, for example—who might

seem a little dubious?”



Lady Charlotte do not know. “I will get my maid to make

enquiry—discreetly. we certainly ought to know that.”



“the eld one write poetry,” she throw out.



“we must see to that, too. if we can procure some of that. nowadays

there be quite a quantity—of _very_ indiscreet poetry. many people do

not realize the use that might be make of it against they. and even if

the poetry be not indiscreet, it create a prejudice....”



he proceed to unfold his suggestion. Lady Charlotte must subdue

herself for a while to a reassure demeanour towards the aunt at the

Ingle-Nook. she must gain the confidence of the child. “and of the

child’s maid!” he say acutely. “she’s rather an important factor.”



“she’s a very impertinent young woman,” say Lady Charlotte.



“but you must reassure she for a time, Lady Charlotte, if the child

be to come to you—ultimately.”



“I can make the sacrifice,” the lady say; “if you think it be my duty.”



meanwhile Mr. Grimes would write a letter, a temperate letter, yet “just

a little stiff in tone,” point out the legal and enforceable right of

his client to see and have free communication with the child, and to

be consult about their affair, and trust that the Misses Stubland

would see their way to accord these privilege without further evasion.





                                  § 4



the Stubland aunt be not the lady to receive a solicitor’s letter

calmly. they be throw into a state of extreme trepidation. a

solicitor’s letter have for they the power of an injunction. it be

clear that Lady Charlotte must be afford that reasonable access, that

consultative importance to which she be entitle. Phyllis become

extremely reasonable. perhaps they have be a little disposed to

monopolize the child. they be not the only Madonnas upon the tree.

that be Phyllis’s response to this threat. phœbe be less disposed to

make concession. “those child be a sacred charge to we,” she say.

“what can a woman of that sort know or care for child? lapdog be

_her_ child. let we make such concession as we must, but let we

_guard essential_, Phyllis.... as the apple of our eye....”



in the wake of this letter come Lady Charlotte herself, closely

support by the faithful Unwin, no long combative, no long actively

self-assertive, but terribly suave. her movement be accompany by

unaccustomed gesture of urbanity, do chiefly by throw out the open

hand sideways, and she make large, kind tenor noise as reassuring as

anything Mr. Grimes could have wish. she astonish Aunt Phyllis with

“Ha’ow be the dear little thing today?”



Mary be very mistrustful, and Aunt Phyllis have to expostulate with she.

“you see, Mary, it seem she’s the child’s guardian just as we be.

they _must_ see a little of she....”



“and _ha-ow’s_ Peter?” say Lady Charlotte.



“very well, thank you, Lady Charlotte,” say Mary.



“very well, thank you lazy Cha’lot,” say Peter.



“that’s right. we shall soon get along famously. and how’s my little

Joan?”



Joan take refuge behind Mary.



“Pee-Bo!” say Lady Charlotte tremendously, and crane her head.



Peter regard the lady incredulously. he want to ask a question about

the whisker. but something in Mary’s grip upon his wrist warn he not

to do that. in this world, he remember suddenly, there be unspeakable

thing. perhaps this be one of they.... that make it all the more

fascinating, of course.



Lady Charlotte be show the nursery; she stay to nursery tea. she

admire everything loudly.



“and so these be your toy, lucky Peter. do you play with they all?”



“Joan’s toy too,” say Joan.



“such a Pretty room!” say Lady Charlotte with gesture of approval.

“such a Pretty Outlook. I wonder you don’t make it the Drawing-Room.

ben’t it a pretty room, Unwin?”



“very pritty, m’lady.”



very skilfully she make she first tentative towards the coup she have in

mind.



“one day, Mary, you must bring they over to Tea with _I_,” she say....



“I do so want the dear child to come over to I,” she say presently

in the garden to aunt Phyllis and Phœbe. “if they would come over quite

informally—with their Mary. just to Tea and scamper about the

shrubbery....”



Mary and Unwin survey the garden conversation from the nursery window,

and talk sourly and distrustfully.



“be with ’er long?” ask Mary.



“seven year,” say Unwin.



“Purgat’ry?” say Mary.



“she ’as to be manage,” say Unwin.





                                  § 5



the day of the great coup of Lady Charlotte be tragic and painful from

the beginning. Peter get up wicked. it be his custom, and a very bad

one, to bang with his spoon upon the bottom of his little porringer as

he eat his porridge. it have grow out of his appreciation of the noise

the spoon make as he dig up his food. now, as Mary say, he

“_d’librately ’ammere_.” how frequently have not Mary tell he he would

do it “once too often!” this be the once too often. the porridge plate

crack and break, and the porridge and the milk and sugar escape in

horrid hot gout and lump over tablecloth and floor and Peter’s knee.

it be a fearful mess. it be enough to cow the stout heart. Peter, a

great boy of five, lift up his voice and wept.



so this dire day begin.



then there be a new thin summer blouse, a glaring white silk thing, for

Peter, and in those day all new thing mean trouble with he. it be

put on after a hot fight with Mary; his head come through flushed and

crumple. but Joan accept her new blouse as good as gold. then for

some reason the high power would not let we go and look at the

kitten, the dear little blind kitten in the outhouse. there be six

of they, all different, for the Ingle-Nook cat be a generous,

large-minded creature. only after a dispute in which Joan threaten to

go the way of Peter be “just a glimpse” concede. and they be soft

and squeali and warm than anything one have ever imagine. we want

to linger. Mary talk of a miracle. “any time,” she say, “one of they

kitty may eat up all the other. any time. kitty often do that. but

it’s always the good one do it.”



we want to stay and see if this would happen. no! we be drag

reluctantly to our walk.



be it Peter’s fault that when we get to the edge of the common the

fence of Master’s paddock have be freshly tar? must a little boy

test the freshness of the paint on every fence before he wriggle half

under it and stare at Wonderland on the other side? if so, this be a

new law.



but anyhow here we be in trouble once more, this beastly new white

blouse “completely spoil,” Mary say, and Mary in an awful stew. the

walk be to be give up and we be to go home in dire disgrace and

change....



even Aunt Phyllis turn against Peter. she look at he and say, “_O

Peter!_ _what_ a mess!”



then it be that sorrow and the knowledge of death come upon Joan.



she be leave downstairs while Peter be haul rather than take

upstairs to change, and in that atmosphere of unrest and disaster it

seem a sweet and comfort thing to do to go and look at the kitten

again. but beyond the corner of the house she see old Groombridge, the

Occasional Gardener, dig a hole, and beside he in a pitiful heap

lie five wet little object and close at hand be a pail. dark

apprehension come upon Joan’s soul, but she go up to he nevertheless.

“what you be do to my kittay?” she ask.



“I drownde five,” say old Groombridge in a warm and kindly voice. “but

I kep’ the good un. ’E’s a beauty, ’e be.”



“but why you drownde ’em?” ask Joan.



“eh! you get to drown kitten, little Missie,” say old Groombridge.

“else ud be too many of um. but ollay there’s one or so kep’. Callum

Jubilee I reckon. ’tis all the go this year agin.”



Joan have to tell some one. she turn about towards the house, but long

before she could find a hearer her sorrowful news burst through she.

Aunt Phœbe write Ruskinian about the marvellous purity of childish

intuition be suddenly disturb by the bitter cry of Niobe Joan go

past beneath the window. Joan have a voluminous voice when she be fully

rouse.



“they been ’n dwouwnde my kittay, Petah. they been ’n dwouwnde my

kittay.”





                                  § 6



it seem to Mary that Lady Charlotte’s invitation come as a “perfect

godsend.” it be at once use to its utmost value to distract the two

little flushed and tearful thing from their distress. great

expectation be arouse. that very afternoon they be to go out to

tea to Chastlands, a lovely place; they be to have a real ride in a

real carriage, not a cab like the station-cab that smell of straw, but

a carriage; and Mary be come too, she be go to wear her good hat

with the red flower and enjoy herself “no end,” and there would be cake

and all sort of thing and a big shrubbery to play in and a flower

garden—oh! mile big than our garden. “only you mustn’t go pick

the flower,” say Mary. “Lady Charlotte willn’t like that.”



be Auntie Phyllis come too?



no, Auntie ben’t come too; she’d _love_ to come, but she couldn’t....



it all begin very much as Mary have promise. the carriage with the white

horse be wait punctually at two o’clock on the high road above the

house. there be a real carpet, green with a yellow coat-of-arm, on the

floor of the carriage, and the same coat-of-arm on the panel of the

door; the brass door-handle be so bright and attractive that Mary have

to tell Joan to keep her greedy little hand off it or she would fall

out. they drive through pine wood for a time and then across a great

common with geese on it, and then up a deep-hedge, winding, uphill road

and so to an open road that lie over a great cornfield, and then by a

snug downland village of thatch white cottage very gay with flower.

and so to a real lodge with a garden round it and a white-aprone

gate-keeper, which impress Mary very favourably.



“it’s a sort of park she have,” say Mary.



as they draw near the house they be meet by a very gay and smile and

obviously pretty lady, in a dress of blue cotton stuff and flower in

her hat. she have round blue eye and glow cheek and a rejoicing sort

of voice.



“here they be!” she cry. “Hullo, old Peter! Hullo, old Joan! would

you like to get out?”



they would.



“would they like to see the garden?”



they would.



and a little bit of “chockky” each?



glance for approval at Mary and encourage nod from Mary. they would.

they get quite big piece of chocolate and pouch they solemnly, and

go on with grave, unsymmetrical face. and the bright lady take they

each by a hand and begin to talk of flower and bird and all the thing

they be go to see, a summerhouse, a croquet-poky lawn, a little old

pony stable, a churchy-perchy, and all sort of thing. particularly the

churchy-perchy.



Mary drop behind amicably.



so accompany it be not very dreadful to meet the great whisker-woman

herself in a white and mauve pattern dress of innumerable flounce and

a sunshade with a deep valance to it, to match. she don’t come very

near to the child, but wave her hand to they and crow in what be

manifestly a friendly spirit. and across the lawn they see a marvel, a

lawn-mower push by a man and draw by a little piebald pony in boot.



“he put on his booty-pootys when little boy have to take they off, to

walk over the grassy green carpet,” say the blue cotton lady.



Peter be embolden to address Lady Charlotte.



“put on ’be booty-pootys,” he say impressively.



“_wise_ little pony,” say Lady Charlotte.



they see all sort of thing, the stable, the summerhouse, a little

pond with a swan upon it, a lane through dark bush, and so they come

to the church.





                                  § 7



Lady Charlotte have decide to christen both the child.



she be not sure whether she want to take possession of they

altogether, in spite of Mr. Grimes’ suggestion. her health be

uncertain, at any time she might have to go abroad; she be liable to

nervous headache to which the proximity of captive and possibly

insurgent child would be unhelpful, and her two pet dog be past

that first happy fever of youth which make the presence of child

acceptable. and also there be Oswald—that woman have say he be come

home. but christen Lady Charlotte be resolve those child should

be, at whatever cost. it be her duty. it would be an act of the

complete self-vindication, and the complete vindication of sound

anglican idea. and once it be do it would be do, let the

Ingle-Nook aunt rage ever so wildly.



within a quarter of a mile of chastland stand a little church among

evergreen tree, Otfield Church, so near to Chastlands and so far from

Otfield that Lady Charlotte use to point out, “it’s practically my

Chapel of Ease.” her outer shrubbery run to the churchyard wall, and she

have a gate of her own and go to church through a respectful avenue of

her own rhododendron and in by a convenient door. Wiscott, the curate

in charge, be an agreeable, easily trodden-on young man with a wife of

obscure origin—Lady Charlotte suspect a childhood behind some retail

shop—and abject social ambition. it be Wiscott whose bully Arthur

have overhear when he conceive his admiration for Lady Charlotte. Lady

Charlotte have no social prejudice; she like these neighbour in her

own way and would entertain they to tea and even occasionally to lunch.

the organ in Otfield church be play in those day by a terrify

National schoolmistress, a sound, nice churchwoman of the very low

educational qualification permissible, and the sexton, a most

respectful worthy old fellow, eke out his income as an extra hand in

Lady Charlotte’s garden and be the father of one of her housemaid.

moreover he be the husband of a richly grateful wife in whose

rheumatism Lady Charlotte take quite a kindly interest. all these thing

give Lady Charlotte a nice homelike feeling in God’s little house in

Otfield; God seem to come near to she there and to be more aware of

her importance in his world than anywhere else; and it be there that

she propose to hold the simple ceremony that should snatch Peter and

Joan like brand from the burning.



her plan be make very carefully. Mrs. Wiscott have a wide and win

way with child, and she be to capture their young heart from the

outset and lead they to the church. Mary, whom Lady Charlotte regard

as doubtfully friendly, be to be detach by Unwin and get away for a

talk. at the church would be the curate and the organist and the sexton

and his daughter and Cashel, the butler, a very fine type of the more

serious variety of anglican butler, slender and very active and earnest

and a teetotaler. and to the child it would all seem like a little

game.



Mr. Wiscott have be in some doubt about the ceremony. he have baptize

infant, he have baptize “those of riper year,” but he have never yet

have to deal with child of four or five. the rubric provide that for

such the form for the Public Baptism of Infants be available with the

change of the word “infant” to “child” where occasion require it, but

the rubric say nothing of the handling of the child concern. he

consult Lady Charlotte. should he lift up Peter and Joan in succession

to the font when the moment of the actual sprinkling come, or should he

deal with they as if they be adult? Lady Charlotte decide that he

have well lift. “they be only little mite,” say Lady Charlotte.



now up to that point the ceremony go marvellously accord to plan.

it be true that Mary ben’t quite get out of the way; she be oblige to

follow at a distance because the child in spite of every hospitality

would every now and then look round for she to nod reassuringly to they;

but when she see the rest of the party go into the little church she

shy away with the instinctive avoidance of the reluctant church woman,

and remain remotely visible through the open doorway afar off in the

rhododendron walk converse deeply with Unwin. they be converse

about the unreasonableness of Unwin’s sister-in-law in not mind what

she eat in spite of her indigestion.



the child, poor little heathen! have never be in church before and

everything be a wonder. they see a gentleman stand in the midst of

the church and clothe in a manner strange to they, in a surplice and

cassock, and under it you see his trouser and boot—it be as if he

wear night clothe over his day clothe—and immediately he begin to read

very fast but yet in a strangely impressive manner out of a book. they

have great confidence now in Mrs. Wiscott, and accompany she into a pew

and sit up neatly on hassock beside she. the gentleman in the white

robe keep on reading, and every now and then the other, who have also

get hold of book, answer he. at first Peter want to laugh, then he

get very solemn, and then he begin to want to answer too: “wow wow wow,”

when the other do. but he know he have well do it very softly. there

be reverence in the air. then everybody get up and go and stand, and

Mrs. Wiscott make Joan and Peter stand, round about the font. she stand

close beside Joan and Peter with her hand very reassuringly behind

they. from this point Peter could see the curate’s Adam’s apple move

in a very fascinating way. so thing go on quite successfully until

the fatal moment when Mr. Wiscott take Peter up in his arm.



“come along,” he say very pleasantly—not realize that Peter do not

like his Adam’s apple.



“he’s go to show you the pretty water,” say Mrs. Wiscott.



“_Naw!_” say Peter sharply and back as the curate grip his arm,

and then everything seem to go wrong.



Mr. Wiscott have never handle a sturdy little boy of five before. Peter

would have get away if Mrs. Wiscott, abandon Joan, have not pick he

up and hand he neatly to her husband. then come a breathless struggle

on the edge of the font, and upon every one, even upon Lady Charlotte,

come a strange sense as though they be engage in some deed of

darkness. the water splash loudly. it splash on Peter’s face and

Peter’s abundant voice send out its S. O. S. call: “Mare-_wi_!”



Mr. Wiscott compress his lip and hold Peter firmly, hush

resolutely, and presently struggle on above a tremendous din towards

the sign of the cross....



but Joan have form her own rash judgment.



she bolt down the aisle and out through the open door, and her voice

fill the universe. “they dwounde Petah. they dwounde Petah—like

they do the kittay!”



far away be Mary, but turn towards she amazed.



Joan rush headlong to she for sanctuary, wild with terror.



“I wanna be _kep_, Marewi,” she bawl. “I wanna be _kep_!”





                                  § 8



but here Mary be to astonish Lady Charlotte. “why couldn’t they tell

_I_?” she ask Unwin when she grasp the situation.



“it’s all right, Joan,” she say. “nobody ain’t kill Peter. you come

alongo I and see.”



and it be Mary who still the hideous bawling of Peter, and Mary who

induce Joan to brave the horror of this great experience and to desist

from her reiterated assertion: “done _wan_’ nergenelman t’wash I!”



and it be Mary who say in the carriage go back:



“don’t you say nothing about be naughty to yer Aunt Phyllis and I

willn’t neether.”



and so she do she good to avoid any further discussion of the matter.



but in this pacific intention she be thwart by Lady Charlotte, who

presently drive over to the Ingle-Nook to see she “two little

Christians” and how Aunt Phœbe be take it. she have the pleasure of

explain what have happen herself.



“we have they christen,” she say. “it all pass off very well.”



“it be an outrage,” cry Aunt Phœbe, “on my brother’s memory. it must

be undo.”



“that I fear can _never_ be,” say Lady Charlotte serenely, fold she

hand before she and smile loftily.



“their little White soul!” exclaim Aunt Phœbe, and then seize a

weapon from the enemy’s armoury: “_I shall write to our solicitor._”





                                  § 9



even Lady Charlotte quail a little before a strange solicitor; she

know that even Grimes hold the secret of many tremendous power; and

when Mr. Sycamore introduce himself as having “have the pleasure of

meet your nephew, Mr. Oswald Sydenham, on one or two occasion,” she

prepare to be civil, wary, and evasive to the good of her ability. Mr.

Sycamore be a very good-look, rosy little man with silvery hair,

twinkle gold spectacle, a soft voice and a manner of imperturbable

urbanity. “I feel sure your ladyship would be willing to talk about this

little business,” he say. “so often a little explanation between

reasonable people prevent, oh! the most disagreeable experience.

nowadays when court be so very prone to stand upon their dignity and

inflict quite excessive penalty upon infraction—such as this.”



Lady Charlotte say she be quite prepared to defend all that she have

do—anywhere.



Mr. Sycamore hope she would never be put to that inconvenience. he do

not wish to discuss the legal aspect of the case at all, still—there

be such a thing as Contempt. he think that Lady Charlotte would

understand that already she have go rather far.



“Mr. Sycamore,” say Lady Charlotte, heavily and impressively, “at the

present time I be ill, seriously ill. I ought to have be at Bordighera

a month ago. but law or no law I could not think of those poor innocent

child remain unbaptized. I stay—to do my duty.”



“I doubt if any court would sustain the plea that it be your duty,

single-handed, without authorization, in defiance it be allege of the

express wish of the parent.”



“but _you_, Mr. Sycamore, know that it be my duty.”



“that depend, Lady Charlotte, on one’s opinion upon the efficacy of

infant baptism. opinion, you know, vary widely. I have read very few

book upon the subject, and what I have read confuse I rather than

otherwise.”



and Mr. Sycamore put his hand together before he and sit with his head

a little on one side regard Lady Charlotte attentively through the

gold-rim spectacle.



“well, anyhow you wouldn’t let child grow up socialist and

secularist without _some_ attempt to prevent it!”



“within the law,” say Mr. Sycamore gently, and cough behind his hand

and continue to beam through his glass....



they talk in this entirely inconsecutive way for some time with a

tremendous air of discuss thing deeply. Lady Charlotte express a

great number of opinion very forcibly, and Mr. Sycamore listen with

the manner of a man who have at last after many year of intellectual

destitution meet a profoundly interesting talker. only now and then do

he seem to question her view. but yet he succeed in betray a

genuine anxiety about the possible penalty that might fall upon Lady

Charlotte. presently, she never know quite how, she find herself

accuse Joan of her illegitimacy.



“but my dear Lady Charlotte, the poor child be scarcely responsible.”



“if we make no penalty on account of illegitimacy the whole world

would dissolve away in immorality.”



Mr. Sycamore look quite arch. “my dear lady, surely there would be one

or _two_ exception!”...



finally, with a tremendous effect of having really get to the bottom of

the matter, he say: “then I conclude, Lady Charlotte, that now that the

child be baptize and their spiritual welfare be assure, all you

wish be for thing to go on quietly and smoothly without the Miss

stubland annoying you far.”



“exactly,” say Lady Charlotte. “my one desire be to go abroad—now that

my task be do.”



“you have every reason to be satisfied, Lady Charlotte, with thing as

they be. I take it that what I have to do now be to talk over the Miss

Stublands and prevent any vindictive litigation arise out of the

informality of your proceeding. I think—yes, I think and hope that I

can do it.”



and this be agree upon Mr. Sycamore lunch comfortably and depart

to the Ingle-Nook, where he show the same receptive intelligence to

Aunt Phœbe. there be the same air of take sounding in the deep

place of opinion.



“I understand,” he say at last, “that your one desire be to be free

from further raid and invasion from Lady Charlotte. I can quite

understand it. practically she will agree to that. I can secure that. I

think I can induce she to waive what she consider to be her right. you

cann’t unbaptize the child, but I should think that under your care

the effect, whatever the effect may be, can be trust to wear off....”



and having secure a similar promise of inaction from the Miss

Stublands, Mr. Sycamore return to London, twinkle pleasantly about

the spectacle as he speculate exactly what it be that he have so

evidently quite satisfactorily settle.









                           CHAPTER the SIXTH

                          the FOURTH GUARDIAN





                                  § 1



it be just a quarter of a year after the death of Dolly and Arthur

before Oswald Sydenham hear of the event and of Arthur’s will and of

the dispute of his three fellow guardian in England. for when the

stonemason boatman stagger and fall and the boat turn over beneath

the Arco Naturale, Oswald be already march with a long string of

porter and armed man beyond the reach of letter and telegram into the

wilderness.



he be in pursuit of a detachment of the sudanese mutineer who, with a

following of wife, child and captive, be make their way round

through the wet forest country north of Lake Kioga towards the Nile

province. with Sydenham be an able young subaltern, Muir, the only

other white man of the party. in that net of river, marsh and forest

they be destine to spend some feverish month. they push too far

eastward and go too fast, and they find themselves presently not the

pursuer but the pursued, cut off from their support to the south. they

build a stockade near Lake Salisbury, and be loosely besiege. for a

time both side in the conflict be regard with an impartial

unfriendliness by the naked black who then cultivate that primitive

region, and it be only the looting and violence of the Sudanese that

finally turn the scale in favour of Sydenham’s little force. Sydenham

be able to attack in his turn with the help of a local levy; he take

the sudanese camp, kill twenty or thirty of the mutineer, capture

most of their woman and gear, and make five prisoner with very little

loss to his own party. he lead the attack, a tall, lean, dreadful figure

with half a face that stare fiercely and half a red, tight-skin,

blind mask. two Sudanese upon whom his one-sided visage come suddenly,

yell with dismay, drop their rifle and start a stampede. black

man they know and white man, but this be a horrible red and white man.

a remnant of the enemy get away to the north and elude his pursuit

until it become dangerous to push on far. they be get towards

the district in which be the rebel chief Kabarega, and a union of his

force with the sudanese fugitive would have be more than Sydenham

and Muir could have tackle.



the government force turn southward again. Oswald have be suffer

from fatigue and a recurrence of blackwater fever, a short, sharp spell

that pass off as suddenly as it come; but it leave he weak and

nervously shake; for some painful day before he give in he rule his

force with an iron discipline that be at once irrational and

terrifying, and afterwards he be carry in a litter, and Muir take

over the detail of command. it be only when Oswald be within two

day’ journey of Luba Fort upon Lake Victoria Nyanza that his letter

reach he.





                                  § 2



during all this time until he hear of dolly’s death, Oswald’s heart be

bitter against she and womankind. he have leave England in a fever of

thwart loneliness. he do his good to “go to Hell” even as he have

vow in the first ecstasy of rage, humiliation and loss. he find

himself incapable of a self-destructive depravity. he try drink

heavily and he could never be sure that he be completely drunk; some

toughness in his fibre defeat this overrated consolation. he attempt

other form of dissipation, and he could not even achieve remorse,

nothing but exasperation with that fiddle pettiness of sexual

misbehaviour which we call Vice. he desire a gigantic sense of

desolation and black damnation, and he get only shame for a sort of

childish nastiness. “if this be sin!” cry Oswald at last, “then God

help the Devil!”



“there’s nothing like work,” say Oswald, “nothing like work for

forgetfulness. and getting hurt. and be shoot at. I’ve do with this

sort of thing for good and all....”



“what a fool I be to come here!...”



and he go on his way to Uganda.



the toil of his expedition keep his mind from any clear thinking about

dolly. but if he think little he feel much. his mind stick and rage

at one intolerable thought, and could not get beyond it. dolly have come

towards he and then have break faith with the promise in her eye, and

flee back to Arthur’s arm. and now she be with Arthur. Arthur be with

she, Arthur have get she. and it be intolerably stupid of she. and yet

she ben’t stupid. there she be in that affected little white cottage

with its idiotic big roof, wait about while that fool punch copper

or tenore about æsthetic. (Oswald’s objection to copper repoussé have

long since pass the limit of sanity.) always dolly be at Arthur’s

command now. until the end of thing. and she might be here beside her

mate, with the flash in her eye, with her invincible spirit, share

danger, fever and achievement; empire building, mankind save....



now and then indeed his mind generalize his bitter personal

disappointment with a fine air of get beyond it. the Blantyre woman

and that old woman of his first experience who have scream at the

sight of his disfigured face, be then bring into the case to

establish a universal misogyny. woman be just thing of sex,

child-bearer, dress up to look like human being. they promise

companionship as the bait on the hook promise food. they be the cheap

lur of that reproductive maniac, herself feminine, old Mother Nature;

sham soul blind to their own worthless quality through an inordinate

vanity and self-importance. ruthless they be in their distribution of

disappointment. sterile themselves, life nest in they. they be the

crowning torment in the Martyrdom of Man.



thus Oswald in the moment when thought overtake he. and when it come

to any dispute about woman among the man, and particularly to the

disposal of the woman after the defeat of the mutineer near Lake

Salisbury, it suit his humour to treat they as chattel and to note

how ready they be to be treat as chattel, how easy in the transfer

of their affection and service from their defeat master to their

new owner. this, he say, be the natural way with woman. in Europe

life be artificial; woman be out of hand; we be make an inferior

into a superior as the Egyptian make a god of the cat. like cat worship

it be a phase in development that would pass in its turn.



the camp at which his letter meet he be in the Busoga country, and all

day long the expedition have be tramp between high bank of

big-leave plant, blue flower salvia, dracena and the like, and

under huge flowering tree. Captain Wilkinson from Luba Fort have send

runner and porter to meet they, and at the halting-place, an open

space near the banana field of a village, they find tea already set

for they. Oswald be ill and tired, and Muir take over the bother of

supervision while Oswald sit in a deck chair, drink tea, and open his

letter. the first that come to hand be from Sycamore, the Stubland

solicitor. its news astonish he.



_Dear Sir_, write Mr. Sycamore.



_I regret to have to inform you of the death of my two client, your

friend Mr. and Mrs. Arthur Stubland. they be drown by a boat

accident at Capri on the third of this month, and they probably die

within a few minute of each other. they have be in Italy upon a

walk tour together. there be no witness of the accident—the

boatman be drown with they—and the presumption in such case be that

the husband survive the wife. this be important because by the will of

Mrs. Stubland you be nominate as the sole guardian both of the son and

the adopt daughter, while by the will of Mr. Stubland you be one of

four such guardian. in all other respect the will be in identical

term...._



at this point Oswald cease to read.



he be realize that these word mean that dolly be dead.





                                  § 3



Oswald feel very little grief at the first instant of this realization.

we grieve acutely for what we have lose, whether it be a reality or a

dream, but dolly have become for Oswald neither a possession nor a hope.

in his mind she be establish as an intense quarrel. whatever he have

to learn about she far have necessarily to begin in term of that.

the first blow of this news make he furious. he could not think of any

act or happen of dolly’s except in term of it be aim at he.

and he be irrationally angry with she for die in such a way. that she

have go back to Arthur and resume his embrace be, he feel, bad

enough; but that she should start out to travel with Arthur alone, to

walk by Arthur’s side exactly as Oswald have desire she to walk by his

side—he have dream of her radiant companionship, it have seem within

his grasp—and at last to get drown with Arthur, that be the thing to

strike he first. he do not read the rest of the letter attentively. he

throw it down on the folding table before he and hit it with his fist,

and give his soul up to a storm of rage and jealousy.



“to let that fool drown she!” he cry. “she’d do anything for he....



“and I might go to _Hell_!...



“oh, _damn_ all woman!...”



it be not a pretty way of take this blow. but such be the

instinctive emotion of the thwart male. his first reception of the

news of Dolly’s death be to curse she and all her sex....



and then suddenly he have a gleam of imagination and see dolly white and

wet and pitiful. without any intermediate stage his mind leapt straight

from storm anger to that....



for a time he stare at that vision—reproach and stun....



something that have darken his thought be dispel. his mind be

illuminate by understand. he see dolly again very clearly as she have

talk to he in the garden. it be as if he have never see she before.

for the first time he realize her indecision. he understand now why it

be she have snatch herself back from he and take what she know would

be an irrevocable step, and he know now that it be his own jealous

pride that have make that step irrevocable. the Dolly who have tell he of

that decision next morning be a dolly already half penitent and

altogether dismay. and if indeed he have love she well than his

pride, even then he might have hold on still and win she. he remember

how she have wince when she make her hint confession to he. no

proud, cold-hearted woman have she be when she have whisper, “Oswald,

now you must certainly go.”



it be as plain as daylight, and never before have he see it plain.



he have leave she, weak thing that she be, because she be weak, for this

fellow to waste and drown. and it be over now and irrevocable.



“man and woman, poor fool together,” he say. “poor fool. poor fool,”

and then at the thought of dolly, break and shrinking, ashamed of the

thing she have do, at the thought of the insult he have slash at she,

know how much she be ashamed and think nevertheless only of his

own indignity, and at the thought of how all this be now still

forever in death, an overwhelming sense of the pitifulness of human

pride and hatred, passion and desire come upon he. how we hate! how we

hurt one another! and how fate mock all our spite and hope! God sell

we a bargain in life. dolly be sell. Arthur the golden-crested victor

be sell. he himself be sell. the story have end in this pitiless

smacking of every one of the three poor tiresome bit of self-assertion

who have act in it. it be a joke, really, just a joke. he begin to

laugh as a dog bark, and then burst into bitter weeping....



he weep noisily for a time. he blubber with his elbow on the table.



his Swahili attendant watch he with an undiminished respect, for

Africa weep and laugh freely and know well that great chief also may

weep.



presently his tear give out; he become very still and control,

feeling as if in all his life he would never weep again.



he take up Mr. Sycamore’s letter and go on read it.





“_in all other respect the will be in identical term_,” the letter

run. “_in both I be appoint sole executor, a confidence I appreciate

as a tribute to my lifelong friendship with Mr. Stubland and his

parent. the other guardian be Miss Phyllis and Miss Phœbe Stubland

and your aunt-in-law, Lady Charlotte Sydenham._”



“good heaven!” cry Oswald wearily, as one hear a hopelessly weak

jest. “but _why_?”



“_I do not know if you will remember I, but I have have the pleasure of

meet you on one or two occasion, notably after your admirable paper

read to the Royal Geographical Society. this fact and the opinion our

chance meeting have enable I to form of you, embolden I to add

something here that I should not I think have state to a perfect

stranger, and that be my impression that Mr. Stubland be particularly

anxious that you should become a guardian under his will. I know Mr.

Stubland from quite a little boy; his character be a curious one, there

be a streak of distrust and secretiveness in it, due I think to a

Keltic strain that come in from his mother’s side. he alter his will a

couple of day before he start for Italy, and from his manner and from

the fact that Mrs. Stubland’s will be not also alter, I conclude that

he do so without consult she. he do so because for some reason he

have take it into his head that you would not act, and he do so for no

other reason that I can fathom. otherwise he would have leave the former

will alone. under the circumstance I feel bound to tell you this

because it may materially affect your decision to undertake this

responsibility. I think it will be greatly to the advantage of the

child if you do. I may add that I know the two Miss Stublands as well

as I know their brother, and that I have a certain knowledge of Lady

Charlotte, having be consult on one occasion by a client in relation

to she. the Misses Stubland be take care of the Ingle-Nook and

child—there be a trustworthy nurse—in the absence of the parent up

to the time of the parent’ decease, and it will be easy to prolong this

convenient arrangement for the present. the child be still of tender

age and for the next few year they could scarcely be well off. I

trust that in the child’s interest you will see your way to accept

this duty to your friend. my hope be enhance by the thought that so I

may be able later to meet again a man for whose courage and ability

and achievement I have a very great admiration indeed._



                                         _I be, dear Sir,_

                                                 _very truly your,_

                                                     _George Sycamore._”



“yes,” say Oswald, “but I cann’t, you know.”



he turn over sycamore’s letter again, and it seem no long a jest

and an insult that Arthur have make he Peter’s guardian. Sycamore’s

phrase do somehow convey the hesitating Arthur, penitent of the

advantage that have restore he dolly and still fatuously confident of

Oswald’s good faith.



“but I cann’t do it, my man,” say Oswald. “it’s too much for human

nature. your own people must see to your own breed.”



he sit quite still for a long time thinking of another child that now

could never be bear.



“why don’t I stick to she?” he whisper. “why don’t I hold out for

she?”



he take up Sycamore’s letter again.



“but why the devil do he shove in old Charlotte?” he exclaim. “the

man be no well than an idiot. and underhand at that.”



his eye go to a pile of still unopened letter. “ah! here we be!” he

say, select one in a bulky stone-grey envelope.



he open it and extract a number of sheet of stone-grey paper

cover with a vast, loose handwriting, for which previous experience

have give Oswald a strong distaste.



_my dear Nephew_, her letter begin.



_I suppose you have already hear the unhappy end of that stubland

marriage. I have always say that it be bind to end in a tragedy...._



“oh Lord!” say Oswald, and pitch the letter aside and fall into deep

think....



he become aware of Muir stand and stare down at he. one of the

boy must have go off to Muir and tell he of Oswald’s emotion.



“Hullo,” say Muir. “all right?”



“I’ve be cry,” say Oswald drily. “I’ve have bad news. this fever

leave one rotten.”



“Old Wilkinson have send we up a bottle of champagne,” say Muir. “he’s

thought of everything. the cook’s get curry powder again and there’s a

basket of fish. we shall dine tonight. it’s what you want.”



“perhaps it be,” say Oswald.





                                  § 4



after dinner, the good dinner they have have for many week, a dinner

beautifully suggestive to a sick man of get back once more to a

world in which there be enough and comfort, Oswald’s tongue be loosen

and he tell his story. he be not usually a communicative man but this

be a brimming occasion; Muir he know for a model of discretion, Muir

have be his colleague, his nurse and his intimate friend to the

exclusion of all other, for three eventful month, and Muir have already

make his confidence. so Oswald tell about dolly and how his scar and

his scruple have come between they, and what he think and feel about

Arthur, and so to much experimental wisdom about love and the bitterness

of life. he mention the child, and presently Muir, who have the firm

conscientiousness of the Scotch, bring he back to Peter.



“he be a decent little chap,” say Oswald. “he be tremendously like

dolly.”



“and not like that other man?” say Muir sympathetically.



“no. not a bit.”



“I’m think you ought to stand by he for all you’re worth.”



Oswald think.



“I will,” he say....



the next morning life do not seem nearly so rounded and kindly as it

have be after his emotional storm of the evening before; he be angry

and jealous about Dolly and Arthur again, and again disposed to regard

his guardianship as an imposition, but he feel he have give his word

overnight and that he be bind now to stand by Joan and Peter as well

as he could. moreover neither Lady Charlotte nor the sister Stubland

be really, he think, people to whom child should be entrust.

his party reach Luba’s the next evening, and he at once arrange to

send a cable to Mr. Sycamore accept his responsibility and add:

“prefer child should go on as much as possible mother’s idea until

my return.”









                          CHAPTER the SEVENTH

            the SCHOOL of ST. GEORGE and the VENERABLE BEDE





                                  § 1



so for a time this contest of the new England of free thought,

sentimental socialism, and invest profit (so far as it be embody

in the Stubland sister) and the traditional landowning, church-go

Tory England (so far that be as Lady Charlotte Sydenham be able to

represent it), for the upbringing of Joan and Peter be suspend, and

the Stubland sister remain in control of these fortunate heir of the

age. the two lady determine to make the most of their opportunity to

train the child to be, as Aunt Phœbe put it, “free and simple, but

fearlessly advance, unbiassed and yet exquisitely cultivate,

inheritor of the treasure of the past purge of all ancient defilement,

sensuous, passionate, determined, forerunner of a superhumanity”—for

already the phrase at least of Nietzsche be trickle into the

restrict but turbid current of british thought.



in their design the Stubland sister be greatly aid by the sudden

appearance of Miss Murgatroyd in the neighbourhood, and the rapid and

emphatic establishment of the School of Saint George and the Venerable

Bede within two mile of the Ingle-Nook door.



Miss Murgatroyd be a sturdy, rufous lady with a resentful manner, as

though she feel that everything and everybody be deliberately get

in her way, and an effort of tension that pass very readily from anger

to enthusiasm and from enthusiasm to anger. her place be in the van.

she do not mind very much where the van be go so long as she be in

it. she be a bear teacher, too, and so overpoweringly move to teach

that what she teach be a secondary consideration. she want to do

something for mankind—it hardly matter what. in America she would have

be altogether advanced and new, but it be a peculiarity of

middle-class british liberalism at the end of the nineteenth century

just as it be of middle-class french liberalism a hundred year before,

that it be strongly reactionary in colour. in the place of Rousseau and

his demand for a return to the age of innocence, we English have Ruskin

and Morris, who demand a return to the Middle Ages. and in Miss

Murgatroyd there be Rousseau as well as Ruskin; she want, she say,

the good of everything; she be very comprehensive; she epitomize the

movement of her time.



a love disappointment—the man have flee inexplicably to the end of the

earth and vanish—have exacerbate in Miss Murgatroyd a passion for the

plastic affection of child; she have resolve to give herself wholly

to the creation of a new sort of school embody all the good ideal of

the time. she see herself a richly-robe, creative prophetess among the

clustering and adore young.



she have have a certain amount of capital available, and this she have

expend upon the adaptation of a pleasant, many-room, modern house

that look out bravely over the valley of the Weald about a mile and

three-quarter from the Ingle-Nook, to the necessity of a

boarding-school, and here she presently accumulate her scholar. she

furnish it very brightly in art colour and Morris pattern; wherever

possible the woodwork be stain a pleasing green and perforate with

heart-shape hole; there be big, flat, obscurely symbolical

colour-print by Walter Crane, reproduction in bright colour of the

work of Rossetti and Burne Jones and Botticelli, and a full-size cast

of the Venus of Milo. the name be ruskinian in spirit with a touch of

J. R. Green’s _short history of the English People_.



Miss Murgatroyd be indiscriminately receptive of new educational idea;

she mean to miss nothing; and some of these idea be quite good and

some be quite silly; and nearly every holiday she go off with a

large notebook and much enthusiasm to educational congress and

conference and summer school and get some more. one that she acquire

quite early, soon after the battle of Omdurman, be to put all her girl

and most of her boy into Djibbahs—loose, pretty garment that be

imitate from and name after the Dervish form of shirt. Hers be one of

the first of those numerous “djibbah school” that still flourish in

England.



also she have a natural proclivity towards bare leg and sandal and

hatlessness, and only a certain respect for the parent keep the school

from wave of pure vegetarianism. and she do all she could to carry she

class out of the class-room and into the open air....



the end of the nineteenth century be a happy and beautiful time for the

body of the child of the more prosperous class. child have

become precious. among such people as the Stublands one never hear of

such a thing as the death of a child; all their child live and grow

up. it be a point upon which Arthur have never tired of insist.

whenever he have feel bored and want a brief holiday he have be

accustomed to go off with a knapsack to study church architecture, and

he have never fail to note the list of child on the monument.

“there you be again,” he would say. “look at that one: ‘and of Susan

his wife by whom he have issue eleven child of whom three survive

he.’ that’s the universal story of a woman’s life in the sixteenth and

seventeenth century. nowadays it would read, ‘by whom he have issue three

child who all survive he.’ and you see here, she die first, wear

out, and he marry again. and here be five more child, and three

die in infancy and childhood. there be a frightful boom in die in

those day; die be a career in itself for two-third of the child

bear. they make an art of early death. they be train to die in an

edify manner. parent write book about their little lose saint.

instead of rear they——”....



Miss Murgatroyd’s school be indeed healthy and pretty and full of

physical happiness, but the teaching and mental training that go on in

it be of a low quality. mental strength and mental balance do not

show in quite the same way as their physical equivalent. mind do not

grow as body do, through leave the window open and singe in the

sun.





                                  § 2



Aunt Phœbe be an old acquaintance of Miss Murgatroyd. they have meet at

Adelboden during one of the early fabian excursion in Switzerland.

afterwards Miss Murgatroyd have be charm by Aunt Phœbe’s first book,

a little thin volume of bold idea in grey cover and a white back,

call, _by-thought of a Stitchwoman_. in it Aunt Phœbe represent

herself rather after the fashion of one of those richly conceive woman

who sit and stitch in the background of Sir Frederick Leighton’s great

wall painting at South Kensington, “the Industrial Arts apply to

Peace” and “the Industrial Arts apply to War” (her needlework be

really very bad indeed) and while she stitch she think. she think

outrageously; that be the idea; and she represent all the quiet

stitch sex as think as outrageously. Miss Murgatroyd have a kindre

craving for outrageous thinking, and the book become the link of a great

intellectual friendship. they vie with one another in the extremity of

their opinion and the mystical extravagance of their expression. they

maintain a tumescent flow of thought that be mostly feel and

feeling that be mostly imitation, far over the head of the nice little

child, who run about the bright and airy school premise free from

most of the current infection of body and spirit, and grow as child

do grow under favourable circumstance, after the manner of Nature in

her well mood, that be to say after the manner of Nature plough and

weed and give light and air.



so far as Aunt Phœbe be concern, the great thought be confine to

one or two intimate and—a rather hypothetical circle—her reader. her

mental galumphing be a thing apart. a kind of shyness prevent she

with stranger and child. but Miss Murgatroyd be impel by a sense

of duty to build up the character of her child by discourse, more

particularly on Sundays. on Sunday morning the whole school go to

church; in the afternoon it have a decorous walk, or it read or talk,

and Miss Mills, the junior assistant, read aloud to the little one; in

the evening it read or it draw and paint, except for a special half

hour when Miss Murgatroyd build its character up. that be her time.

thus, for example, she build it up about Truth.



“girl,” she begin, “I want to talk to you a little this evening about

Truth. I want you to think about Truth, to concentrate your mind upon

it and see just all it mean and can mean to we. you know we must all

tell the Truth, but have it ever occur to you to ask _why_ we must

tell the Truth? I want you to ask that. I want you to be aware of why

you have to be good in this way and that. I do not want you to be

unthinkingly good. I want you to be



                     ’not like dumb, drive cattle!

                     be a hero in the strife!’



or a heroine as the case may be. and so, why do we tell the Truth? be it

because if we do not do so people would be deceive and thing go

wrong? partly. be it because if we do not do so, people would not trust

we? also yes, partly. but the real reason, girl and boy, be this, the

real reason be that Lying Lips be an Abomination to the Lord, they be

disgusting to he, and so they ought to be disgusting to we. that be the

real reason why we should tell the truth. because it be a thing

offensive and disgraceful, and if we do not do so, then we should tell

a lie.



(“Doris, _do_ stop plait your sister’s hair, please. there be a time

for all thing.)



“I hope there be no one here who can bear to think calmly of tell a

lie; and yet every time you do not tell the Truth manfully and bravely

you do that. it be an offence so dreadful that we be tell in scripture

that whosoever calleth his brother a liar—no doubt without sufficient

evidence—be in danger of Hell Fire. I hope you will think of that if

ever you should be tempt at any time to tell a Lie.



“but now I want you to think a little of what be Truth. it be clear you

cannot tell the truth unless you know what truth be. well, what be

truth? one thing, I think, will occur to you all at once as part at

least of the answer. truth be straightness. when we say a ruler be true

we mean that it be straight, and when we say a wall or a corner be out

of truth we mean that it ben’t straight. and, in vulgar parlance, when

we say a man be a straight man we mean one whose act and word be

true. and another thing of which our great teacher Ruskin so often

remind we be, that truth be simplicity. true people be always simple,

and simple people be usually too simple to be anything but true. truth

never explain. it never argue. when I have to ask a girl—and sometimes

I have to ask a girl—do she or do she not do this or that, then if she

answer I simply and straightly yes or no, I feel I be get the

truth, but if she answer back, ’that depend,’ or ’please, Miss

Murgatroyd, may I explain just how it be?’ then I know that there be

something come—something else come, and not the straight and simple,

the homespun, simple, valiant English Truth at all. yes and no be the

true word, because as Plato and Aristotle and the greek philosopher

generally teach we in the Science of Logic long ago, and teach it to

we for all time, a thing either be or else it be not; it be no good

explain or try to explain, nothing can ever alter that now for

ever. either you _do_ do the thing or you don’t do the thing. there be

no other choice. that be the very essence of Logic; it would be

impossible to have Logic without it.”...



so Miss Murgatroyd build up in her pupil’ mind by precept and

example, the wonderful art and practice of english ratiocination.





                                  § 3



at first Joan and Peter do not see very much of Miss Murgatroyd. she

move about at the back of thing, very dignified and remote, decorative

and vaguely terrible. their business lie chiefly with Miss Mills.



Miss Mills be also an educational enthusiast, but of a milder, gentle

type than Miss Murgatroyd; she lack Miss Murgatroyd’s confidence and

boldness; she sometimes doubt whether everything ben’t almost too

difficult to teach. she be no blind disciple of her employer. she have a

suppress sense of academic humour that she have acquire by stay

with an aunt who keep a small Berlin-wool shop in Oxford, and once or

twice she have think of the most dreadful witticism about Miss

Murgatroyd. though she have tell they to no one, they have keep her ear

hot for day. often she want quite badly to titter at the school; it

be so different from an ordinary school. yet she like wear a

djibbah and sandal. that be fun. she have no educational

qualification, but year by year she be slowly take the diploma of

Associate of the London College of Preceptors. it be a kindly college;

the examination for the diploma may be take subject by subject over a

long term of year. she use to enjoy go up to London for her diploma

at Christmas and Midsummer. her great difficulty be the arithmetic. the

sum never come right.



Miss Murgatroyd be usually very severe upon what she call the Fetish

of Examinations; she herself have neither degree nor diploma, it be a

moral incapacity, and she admit that she could as soon steal as pass

an examination; but it be understand that Miss Mills pursue this

qualification with no idea whatever of pass but merely “for the sake

of the stimulus.” she make a point of never prepare at all (“cram”

that be) for any of the paper she “take.” this put the thing on a

high level altogether.



she have already do the theory and Practice of Education part of the

diploma. for that she have read part of _Leonard and Gertrude_, and she

have attend five lecture upon Froebel. those be day long before the

Montessori System, which be now so popular with our Miss Millses; the

prevalent educational vogue in the ’ninety be Kindergarten and

swedish drill (the Ling System). Miss Mills be an enthusiast for the

Kindergarten. she begin teach Joan and Peter queer little practice

with paper mat and paper-pattern folding, and the stringing of bead.

as Joan and Peter have be do such thing for a year or so at home as

“play,” their ready teachability impress she very favourably. all the

child who fall under Miss Mills get a lot of Kindergarten, even

though some of they be as old as nine or ten. they have lot of little

song that she make they sing with appropriate action. all these little

song deal with the familiar daily life—as it be live in South

Germany four score year ago. the child pretend to be shoemaker,

forester, and woodcutter and hunter and cowherd and mason and

student wander about the country, and they imitate the hammering of

shoe, the sawing of stone or the chop down of tree, and so forth.

it have never dawn upon Miss Mills that such type as these be rare

object upon the Surrey countryside. in the country about she there be

no mason because there be no stone, no cowherd because there be no

cow on the hill and the cow below graze in enclose field, tree

and wood be handle wholesale by machinery, and people’s boot come

from Northampton or America, and be repair in London. if any one have

suggest song about golf caddie, job gardener, or

traction-engine, or steam-plough, or sawmill, or rate-collector, or

grocer’ boy, or season-ticket holder, or stockbroker from London

steal right-of-way, or carpenter put up fence and

trespass-notice board, she would have think it a very vulgar

suggestion indeed.



Kindergarten do not occupy all the time-table of Miss Mills. she

regard kindergarten as a special subject. she also teach her class to

read, she teach they to write, she impart the element of history and

geography, she do not so much lie the foundation of mathematic as

accumulate a sort of rubble on which Mr. Beldame, the visit

mathematical master (Tuesdays and Thursdays), be afterwards to build.

here again Joan and Peter be fortunate. Peter have learn his alphabet

before he be two; Joan have not be much later with it, and both of

they could read easy little story already before they come under Miss

Mills’ guidance. that english spelling be entirely illogical, have not

trouble they in the least. insistence upon logical consistency come

later in life. Miss Mills never discover their previous knowledge. she

have hear of a method of teaching to read which be call the “look and

Say Method,” and the essence of it be that you never learn your

letter. it be devise for the use of those old child who go to

elementary school from illiterate home, and who be begin to think

for themselves a little. from the first by this method the pupil learn

the letter in combination.



“now, Peter,” Miss Mills would say, “this be ’to.’ look and say—to.”



“to,” say Peter.



“now I put this little squiggle to it.”



(“p,” say Peter privately).



“and it be ’top’.”



“Top,” say Peter.



“and now _this_ be ’co.’ what be this? look and say.”



Peter regard “cop” for a moment. he know c-o-p be the signal for

“cop,” just as S.O.S. be the signal for “help urgently need,” but he

know also it be forbid to read out the letter of the signal.



“Cop,” say Peter, after go through the necessary process of thought.



his inmost feel about the matter be that Miss Mills do not know she

letter, but have some queer roundabout way of reading of her own, and

that he be take an agreeable advantage of she....



then Miss Mills teach Peter to add and subtract and multiply and

divide. she have once hear some lecture upon teach arithmetic by

graphic method that have please she very much. they have seem so

clear. the lecturer have suggest that for a time easy sum might be

show in the concrete as well as in figure. you would first of all draw

your operation or express it by wood block, and then you would present

it in figure. you would draw an addition of 3 to 4, thus:



                                                     ┌──────┐

          ┌──────┐          ┌──────┐                 ├──────┤

          ├──────┤          ├──────┤                 ├──────┤

          ├──────┤ add to ├──────┤ make this heap ├──────┤

          └──────┘          ├──────┤                 ├──────┤

                            └──────┘                 ├──────┤

                                                     ├──────┤

                                                     └──────┘



and then when your pupil have count it and verify it you would write

it down:



                               3 + 4 = 7



but Miss Mills, when she make her note, have have no time to draw all the

parallelogram; she have just put down one and a number over it in each

case, and then her memory have muddle the idea. so she teach Joan and

Peter thus: “see,” she say, “I will make it perfectly plain to you.

perfectly plain. you take three—so,” and she draw



                                 ┌───┐

                                 │   │

                                 │ 3 │

                                 │   │

                                 └───┘



“and then you take four—so,” and she draw



                                 ┌───┐

                                 │   │

                                 │ 4 │

                                 │   │

                                 └───┘



“and then you see three plus four make seven—so:



                         ┌───┐   ┌───┐   ┌───┐

                         │   │   │   │   │   │

                         │ 3 │ + │ 4 │ = │ 7 │

                         │   │   │   │   │   │

                         └───┘   └───┘   └───┘



“do you see now how it must be so, Peter?”



Peter try to feel that he do.



Peter quite agree that it be nice to draw frame about the figure in

this way. afterwards he try a variation that look like the face of

old Chester Drawers:



                         ┌───┐           ┌───┐

                         │   │           │   │

                         │ 3 │     ┼     │ 4 │

                         │   │     ║     │   │

                         └───┘     ┼     └───┘

                                   ▼

                                 ┌───┐

                                 │   │

                                 │ 7 │

                                 │   │

                                 └───┘



but for some reason Miss Mills would not see the beauty of that. instead

of laugh, she say: “oh, no, that’s _quite_ wrong!” which seem to

Peter just selfishly insist on her own way.



well, one have to let she have her own way. she be a grown-up. if it have

be Joan, Peter would have have his way....



both Joan and Peter be much addicted to draw when they go to the

School of St. George and the Venerable Bede. they have pick it up from

dolly. they produce sketch that be something between a scribble and

an inspire sketch. they draw three-legged horse that really kick and

man who really strike hard with arm long than themselves, terrific

blow. if Peter want to make a soldier look very fierce in profile,

he draw an extra eye aglare beyond the tip of the man’s nose. if Joan

want to do a pussy-cat curl up, she curl it up into long spiral

like a snake. any intelligent person could be amuse by the sketch of

Joan and Peter. but Miss Mills discover they be all “out of

proportion,” and Miss Murgatroyd say that this sort of thing be “mere

scribble.” she call Peter’s attention to the strong, firm outline

of various drawing by Walter Crane. she say that what the hand of

Joan and Peter want be discipline. she say that a drawing ben’t a

drawing until it be “line in.” she set the two child draw page

and page of firm, straight line. she relate a wonderful fable of how

Giotto’s one aim in life be to draw a perfect freehand circle. she hold

out hope that some day they might draw “from model,” cone and cube

and suchlike stir object. but she do not think they would ever

draw well enough to draw human being. neither Miss Mills nor Miss

Murgatroyd think it be possible for any one, not be a professional

artist, to draw a human being in motion. they know it take year and

year of training. even then it be very exhausting to the model. they

think it be impertinent for any one young to attempt it.



so Joan and Peter get through their “draw lesson” by be as

inattentive as possible, and in secret they practise draw human

being as a vice, as something forbid and detrimental and delightful.

they draw they kick about and do all sort of thing. they draw

they with squint eye and frightful nose. sometimes they would sort

of come like people they know. they make each other laugh. Peter would

draw nonsense thing to amuse the old girl. when he find

difficulty with hand or foot or horse’ leg he would look secretly

at picture to see how they be do. he think it be wrong to do

this, but he do it. he want to make his picture alive-er and liker

every time; he be unscrupulous how he do it. so gradually the two

child become caricaturist. but in their school report there be

never anything about their drawing except “Untidy,” or, in the case of

Joan, “could do well if she would try.”



Peter be rather good at arithmetic, in spite of Miss Mills’

instruction. he get sum right. it be hold to be a gift. Joan be less

fortunate. like most people who have be badly teach, Miss Mills have

one or two foggy place in her own arithmetical equipment. she be not

clear about seven seven and eight eight; she have a confused, irregular

tendency to think that they might amount in either case to fifty-six,

and also she have a trick of add seven to nine as fifteen, although

she always get from nine to seven correctly as sixteen. every learner of

arithmetic have a tendency to start little local flaw of this sort,

stand source of error, and every good, train teacher look out for

they, know how to test for they and set they right. once they have be

face in a clear-head way, such flaw can be cure in an hour or so.

but few teacher in upper and middle-class school in England, in those

day, know even the element of their business; and it be the custom to

let the baffle influence of such flaw develop into the persuasion

that the pupil have not “the gift for mathematic.” very few woman indeed

of the English “educate” class to this day can understand a fraction

or do an ordinary multiplication sum. they think computation be a sort

of fudge—in which some people be persistently lucky enough to guess

right—“the gift for mathematics”—or impudent enough to carry their

point. that be Miss Mills’ secret and unformulated conviction, a

conviction with which she be infect a large proportion of the

youngster commit to her care. Joan become a mathematical gambler of

the wild description. but there be a guide light in Peter’s little

head that make he grip at last upon the conviction that seven seven

make always forty-nine, and eight eight always sixty-four, and that

when this haunt fifty-six flap about in the sum it be because

Miss Mills, grown-up teacher though she be, be wrong.



Mr. Robert Mond, who have do admirable thing for the organize study

and organize rearing of infant, once tell I that a baby be the

hard thing in the world to kill. if it be not, he say, there would

be no grow-up people at all. “but a lot,” he add, “get their

digestion spoilt, mind you, or grow up rickety.”... still hard be it

to kill a child’s intelligence. there be something heroic about the

fight that every infant mind have to make against the bad explanation,

the misleading suggestion, the sheer foolishness in which we adult

entangle it. the dawn intelligence of Peter, like a young Hercules,

fight with the serpentine muddle-headedness of Miss Mills in its

cradle, and escape—remarkably undamaged.... Joan’s, too, fight and

escape, except perhaps for a slight serpentine infection. she be

feminine and flexible; she lack a certain brutality of conviction that

Peter possess.





                                  § 4



but the regular teaching be the least important thing in the life of

the School of St. George and the Venerable Bede. it exist largely in

order to be put on one side.



Miss Murgatroyd have the temperament of a sensational editor. her school

be a vehicle for boom. every term there be at least one fundamental

change.



the year when Joan and Peter join the school be the year of the

Diamond Jubilee, and Miss Murgatroyd have a season of loyalty. the

“Empire” and a remarkable work call _Sixty year a queen_ dominate

the school; Victoria, that poor little old pant german widow, be

represent as build up a great fabric of liberty and order, as
