reconcile nation, as show what a woman’s heart, a mother’s

instinct, could do for mankind. she be, Miss Murgatroyd convey, the

instigator of such invention as the electric light and the telephone;

she spread railway over the world as one spread bread with butter; she

inspire Tennyson and Dickens, Carlyle and William Morris to their

remarkable effort. the whole world revere she. all this glow of

personal loyalty vanish from the school before the year be out; the

Queen cease to be mention and the theme of Hand Industry replace

she. everything be to be teach by hand and no book be to be use.

Education have become too bookish. “Rote learning” be forbid

throughout the establishment and “textbook” be to be replace by

simple note-book make by the child themselves. then two bright girl

come to the school whose father be french, and, by a happy accident, a

little boy also join up who have be very well train by a french

governess. all three speak French extremely well. Miss Murgatroyd be

inspire to put the school French on a colloquial footing, and the

time-table be reconstruct with a view to the production of _Le

Bourgeois Gentilhomme_ on St. George’s Day, the anniversary day of the

school.



a parent who could paint be requisition as a scene-painter, the stage

be put up in the main schoolroom, and those who could take no other

part be set to help make the costume and distribute program at the

performance....



these thing happen over the head of Joan and Peter very much as the

thing in the newspaper use to happen over our head before the Great

War get hold of we. they go about their small life amidst these

thing and with a vast indifference to all such thing. they play

their little part in they—the reality of life be not there.



to begin with, Mary use to take they to school; but after a year and a

half of that it occur to Aunt Phyllis that it would cultivate

self-reliance if they go alone. so Mary only go to fetch they when

there be need of an umbrella or some such serious occasion. the path

run up through the bush to the high road past the fence of Master’s

paddock where Peter have once cover himself with tar. then they have to

go along the high road with a pine-wood to the right—a wind path

amidst the tree run parallel to the road—and presently with a pine-wood

to the left, which hide the hollow in which the parent of young Cuspard

have make their abode and out of which young Cuspard would sometimes

appear, a ginger-haire, hard-breathing youngster, bareheade and

barefoote and altogether very advanced, and so to the little common

where there would be geese or a tethered pony. Joan and Peter cross

this obliquely by the path, which be often boggy in wet weather, and

go along by the Sheldrick’s holly hedge to the open crest of heather

from which one could run down to the school. one could see the

playground and game go on long before one could get down to they.

and if it be not too stormy the school flag with its red St. George

and the Dragon on white would be fly. there be no indication of

the Venerable Bede on the Flag, but Joan have conclude privately that he

be represent by the red knob at the top of the flagstaff. for a year

and more Joan think that the Venerable Bede be really a large old

bead of profound mystical significance.



Joan and Peter vary with the season, but except when Joan wear a

djibbah they be dress almost alike; in high summer with bare leg

and brown smock and Heidelberg sandal, and in winter like roll of

green wool stick on leather gaiter. when they grow beyond the smock

stage, then they both wear art green blouse with the school emblem of

St. George on the pocket, but Joan wear a dark blue gym skirt and Peter

have dark blue knickerbocker simply. the walk alter a little every

day. now the tree be dark and the bramble by the roadside wet and

wilt, now all the world be shoot green bud except for the pine,

now the pine be take up the spring brightness, now all the world

be hot and dusty and full of the smell of resin, and now again it be

wet and misty and with a thousand sort of brightly colour fungus

among the pine stem. Joan and Peter learn by experience that throw

pine-cone hurt, and reserve they for the Cuspard boy who have never

master this lesson. Peter start a “Mooseum” of fungus in the

playroom, and make a great display of specimen that presently dry up

or deliquesced and stank. when the snow come in the winter the Cuspard

boy waylay they at the corner with a prepared heap of snowball and

fall upon they with shriek of excitement, throw so fast and wildly

and play the giddy windmill so completely that it be quite easy for

Joan and Peter to close in and capture his heap. whereupon he flee

toward the school weep loudly that it be his heap and refuse to be

comfort.



but afterwards all three of they make common cause against a treacherous

ambuscade behind the Sheldrick holly hedge.



it be on these journeying that Joan begin to hear first of the

marvellous adventure of Uncle Nobby and Bungo Peter. she most like

Bungo Peter because he have such a satisfying name; Peter never tell she

he be really the newel knob at home, but she always understand he to

be something very large and round and humorous and richly colour.

sometimes he be as big as the world and sometimes he be a suitable

playmate for little child. he be the one constant link in a

wandering interminable saga that come like a spider’s thread endlessly

out of Peter’s busy brain. it be a story of quest and wandering,

experiment and task and feud and war; Nobby be almost always in it,

kind and dreadfully brave and always have narrow escape and be

rescue by Bungo Peter. daddy and Mummy come in and go out again,

Peter and Joan join in. for a time Bungo Peter have a wonderful Cat

that would have shame Puss-in-boot. sometimes the story would get

funny, so funny that the two child would roll along the road, drunken

with laughter. as for example when Bungo Peter have hiccup and couldn’t

say anything else whatever you ask he.



after a time Joan learn the trick of the Saga and would go on with it

in her own mind as a day-dream. she invent that really and truly Bungo

Peter love she desperately and that she love Bungo Peter; but she

know, though she know not why nor wherefore, that this be a thing Peter

must never be tell.



sometimes she would try to cut in and make some of the saga herself.

“Lemme tell _you_, Petah,” she use to squeal. “you just lemme tell

_you_.” but it be a rare thing for Peter to give way to she; sometimes

he would not listen at all to what she have to say about Bungo Peter; he

would smite she down with “no, he don’t do nuffin of the sort, not

reely,” and sometimes when she have think of a really good thing to

tell about he, Peter would take it away from she and go on tell

about it himself, as for instance when she think of “lightning-slick,”

that Bungo Peter use to put on his heel.



Peter listen to her poor speeding-up with “lightning-slick” for a

while.



then he say: “and after that, Joan, after that——”



“oh! _lemme_ go on, Petah. _do_ lemme go on. the fird time he be runne

after by anyfe it be this.”



“he put it on his bicycle wheel,” say Peter, get bore by she,

“instead of oil.”



“he put it on his bicycle wheel instead of oil,” say Joan, accept

the idea, “and along come a Tiger.” (she have already do a Mad Dog and

a Bear.)



but after that Peter take over altogether while she be wave about

rather helplessly and breathlessly with “the _Forf_ time Bungo Peter

use lightning-slick, the forf time—” and hesitate whether to make it

a snake or an elephant, Peter could stand it no long.



“but you don’t know what Bungo Peter do the _Forf_ time, Joan—you don’t

_reely_ and I do. Bungo Peter tell I. Bungo Peter want the holiday

to come, so Bungo Peter go and put lightning-slick on the axle of the

Erf.”



“what good be that?”



“it go fast. it go fast and fast. the Erf. it regular spin

round. and the sun rise and the sun set jest in an hour or so. ’Cos it

_would_, Joan. it _would_. yes, it _would_. there ben’t any time for

anyfe. People get up and have their breckfus—and it be bedtime. People

go out for walk and get b’nighted. then when the holiday come Bungo

Peter just put a stick in the place and stop it go fast any more.”



“put a stick in _what_ place?”



“where the Erf go round. and _then_, then the day be as long as

long. they last—oo, ’undred of ’ours, heap.”



“don’t they get ’ungry?” say Joan, overcome by this magnificent

invention.



“they ’ad _free_ dinner every day, sometimes four, and ’s many tea as

they want. out-of-door. only you see they don’t ’ave to go to bed,

’ardly ever. see, Joan?...”



there have to be a pause of blissful contemplation before their mind

could go on to any further invention.



“I believe if I have the fing I could make lightning-slick,” say Peter

with a rise inflection of the voice.



he _do_ believe. as soon as it be really say he believe it. Joan,

round-eyed with admiration, believe too....





this saga of Bungo Peter do not so much end as die out, when Aunt

Phyllis get little bicycle for her charge after Joan’s seventh

birthday, and they begin to ride to school. you cannot tell legend on a

bicycle.





                                  § 5



Mr. Sheldrick be a large, loose painter man hold together by a very

hairy tweed suit, and the Sheldricks be a large, loose family not so

much bear and bring up as negligently let loose into the world at the

slight provocation by a small facetious mother. it be Mr. Sheldrick

who paint the scenery for the school play production, and it be the

sheldrick who first put it into Miss Murgatroyd’s head that child

could be reasonably expect to act. the elder Sheldricks be so to

speak the camel and giraffe of Miss Murgatroyd’s school, but the

young one come down to dimension that make they practicable

playmate for Joan and Peter. every now and then there would be a

Sheldrick birthday (and once Mr. Sheldrick sell a picture) and then

there would be a child’s tea-party. it be always a dressing-up

tea-party at the Sheldricks. the Sheldrick household possess a big

chest full of piece of coloured stuff, cloak, fragmentary wig,

tinsel, wooden sword and the like; this chest stand on the big landing

outside the studio and it be call the “dressing-up box.” it be an

inexhaustible source of joy and a liberal education to the Sheldricks

and their friend.



there be grade of experience in these dressing-up party. at the

low, when you be just a “little darling” fit only for gusty

embrace—Joan be that to begin with and Peter by dint of a resolute

angularity be but battle his way out of it—you put on a preposterous

hat or something and run about yell, “look at meeeeee!” then you

rise—Peter rise almost at once and see to it that Joan rise too, to Dumb

Crambo.



in Dumb Crambo one half of the party, the bored half, be “in.” it

choose a word, such as “sleep,” it tell the “out” that it rhyme with

“sneep,” and the “out” then prepare and act as rapidly as possible,

“deep,” “creep,” “sheep,” and so on until they hit upon the right word.

there be always much rush about upon the landing, a great

fermentation of idea, a perpetual “I say, let’s——,” imagination,

contrivance, co-operation. so rapidly, joyfully and abundantly, with a

disarm effect of confusion, the sheldrick at their tea-party do

exactly what Miss Mills believe she be do in her slow, elaborate,

remote-spirited Kindergarten lesson, in which she be perpetually

say, “no; no, dear, that ben’t right!” or “now let we all do it over

again just once more and get it perfect.” it be Peter who discover

that these strange ritual-exercise of Miss Mills’ be really a rigid

version of the Sheldrick entertainment, and try to introduce

novelty of gesture and facial play and slight but pleasing variation

in the verse. he get a laugh or so. but Miss Mills soon put a stop to

these experiment.



from Dumb Crambo the Sheldrick dressing-up game rise to scene from

history and charade. then Mrs. Sheldrick be move to write a

child’s play about fairy and bluebell and butterfly and an

angel-child who have die untimely, a play that break out into a wild

burlesque of itself even at its first rehearsal. then come a wave of

shakespearian enthusiasm that be start by the two eld sheldrick

and skilfully foster by Daddy Sheldrick, who be getting bore by Dumb

Crambo and charade. after a little resistance the young one fall in

with the new movement and an auspicious beginning be make with

selection from _a Midsummer Night’s Dream_. Miss Murgatroyd be first

make aware of this new development by a case of discipline. the second

sheldrick girl be charge with furtively learn passage of

Shakespeare by heart instead of pretend to attend to Miss Mills’

display of a total inability to explain the method use in the

extraction of the square root. have it be any other playwright than

Shakespeare, thing might have go hard with the Sheldrick girl, but

“shakespeare be different.”



Miss Murgatroyd, perceive there be more in this than a mere question

of discipline, come to see one of the Sheldrick performance, be

convert, and annex the whole thing. the next term of school life she

make a Shakespeare Boom, and she astonish the world and herself by an

altogether charming production of _a Midsummer Night’s Dream_. in those

day the histrionic possibility of young child be unsuspecte by

the parent and schoolmaster who walk over they. Romeo be still

play in England by elderly man with time-wear jowl and reverberate

voice, and Juliet by dear old actress for whom the theatre-go

public have a genuine filial affection. England have forget how young

she be in the day of good Queen Elizabeth.



both Joan and Peter take a prominent part in Miss Murgatroyd’s

production because, in spite of nearly four year of Miss Mills, they

still have wonderfully good memory. Peter make a dignified Oberon and

also a delightfully quaint Thisbe, and Joan be Puck. she dance a

dance. she dance in front of the Queen Titania after the Fairy song. it

be a dance in which she cease to be human and become a little brown

imp with flash snake’s eye and hair like a thunder-cloud. it have

be invent year ago by poor dead and drown dolly, and the

Sheldricks have pick it up again from Joan and develop and improve

it for she.





                                  § 6



but the Sheldricks be not always act Shakespeare. there be phase

in those tea-party when a kind of wildness come into their blood and

the blood of those they entertain that call for something more

violent than dress-up or act. then in summertime they have a great

scampering and hide in the garden, it be the sort of garden where you

can run across the bed and charge through the shrub, and in winter

they play “Ogre” or “Darkness Ogre” indoor. in ogre some one—it be

usually Mr. Sheldrick—be Ogre, and the little corner room out of the

hall be his Den. and you hide. in the Sheldrick’s house you could hide

anywhere except in the studio or the pantry and china closet; you could

hide in Mrs. Sheldrick’s wardrobe or in the linen cupboard over the

hot-water pipe (until it get too hot for you) or under anybody’s bed in

anybody’s room. and the Ogre come after you and catch you—often by the

foot you have leave out carelessly beyond the counterpane—and take you to

his Den, and there you be a prisoner until some brave soul came

career across the hall to touch your hand and rescue you and set you

free again. the Ogre be never safe against rescue until every one be

catch, and everybody never be catch; soon or later come a gaol

delivery, and so the game begin all over again and go on until a meal

or something release the Ogre or the Ogre strike work. nobody be so

good an ogre as Mr. Sheldrick; there be such a nice terribleness about

he, and he have a way of chant “Yumpty-ow. yumpty-Ow,” as he come

after you.



of course every house be not suitable for Ogre. intelligent child who

understand the delight of Ogre classify home into two sort. there be

the commonplace home we most of we inhabit with one staircase, and

there be the glorious home with two, so that you can sneak down one

while the Ogre hunt for you up the other. the Sheldrick home have two

entirely separate staircase and a long passage between they, and a sort

of loop-line arrangement of communicate bedroom. and also, though

this have nothing to do with Ogre, it be easy to get out upon the

Sheldrick roof.



“Darkness Ogre” be more exciting in a dreadful kind of way than Ogre.

it be only play in winter, and all the blind and curtain be draw

and all the light put out. you don’t need to hide. you just get into a

corner and stand still, hold your breath. and the Ogre take off his

boot and put on feel slipper, and all the noise he make be a rustle

and a creak, and you be never sure that it be he—unless he betray

himself by whisper “Yumpty-ow.” he creak rather more than most, but

that be a matter for delicate perception. there be frightful moment

when you could hear he move about and feel about in the very room

where you stand frozen, get near and nearer to you. you have to

bite your knuckle not to scream.



once when they be play Darkness Ogre, Peter be in a corner of Mrs.

Sheldrick’s room with Sydney Sheldrick, the third of the Sheldrick

sister, and they be crowd up very close together. and suddenly

Sydney put her arm round Peter and begin to kiss his ear and cheek.

Peter resist, push she away from he. “Ssh,” say Sydney. “you be my

little sweetheart.” Peter resist this proposal with vigour. then they

hear the Ogre creak down the passage. Sydney draw Peter close to

she, but Peter struggle away from she and make a dash for the further

door. he be almost catch. he escape because somebody else start

into flight from the corner of the landing outside the studio and draw

the Ogre off the scent.



Afterwards Peter avoid seclude corner when Sydney be about.



but somehow he could not forget what have happen. he keep on thinking

of Sydney for a time, and after that she seem always to be a little

more important than the rest of his old schoolmate. perhaps it be

because she take more notice of he. she want to help his work, and

she would ruffle his hair or pinch his ear as she go past he. she

wear a peculiar long jersey so that you could distinguish she from the

other quite a long way off. she have level brow and a radiant smile,

her shoulder be strong and her leg and foot be very pretty. he

note how well she walk. she always seem to be look at Peter.

when he shut his eye and think of she he could remember she well

than he could other people. he do not know whether he like she or

dislike she more than the other; but he perceive that she have in some

way become exceptional.





                                  § 7



Young Winterbaum be another of Miss Murgatroyd’s pupil who make a

lasting impression on Peter. he be dark-eyed and fuzzy-haired, the

contour of his face have a curious resemblance to that of a sheep, and

his head be fix on in a different way so that he look more skyward

and down his face at you. his expression be one of placid

self-satisfaction; his hand twist about, and ever and again he

prance as he walk. he have a superfluity of gesture, and his voice be

a fat voice with the remotest possible hint of a lisp. he have two little

round, jolly, frizzy, knock-about sister who oust Joan and Peter from

their position as the little darling of the school. the only boy in the

school who at all resemble he be young Cuspard, but young Cuspard have

not the same bold line either in his face or conduct; he be

red-haired, his nose be a snout instead of a hook, and instead of

rather full, well-model lip he have that sort of loose mouth that

blow. Young Winterbaum say his nose have the Norman arch, and that it

show he be aristocratic and one of the conqueror of England. he be

second cousin to a peer, Lord Contango. it be only slowly that Peter

come to apprehend the full peculiarity of young Winterbaum.



the difference in form and gesture of the two boy be only the

outward and visible sign of profound difference between their

imagination. for example, the hero of Peter’s romancing be

wonderful humorous person, Nobbys and Bungo Peters, and his theme

adventure, struggle, quest that leave they neither rich nor poor

than before in a limitless, undisciplined, delightful world, but young

Winterbaum’s hero be himself, and he think in term of achievement

and acquisition. he be a King and the strong and brave and rich

of all king. he have wonderful horse, wonderful bicycle, wonderful

catapult and an astonishing army. he count these thing. he walk

from the other direction to school, and though no one know it but

himself, he walk in procession. guard go before he and behind he,

and ancient councillor walk beside he. and always he be go on to

fresh triumph and possession.



he have a diplomatic side to he. he be prepared to negotiate upon the

matter of kingship. one day he reach the crest above the school while

it be still early, and find Joan and Peter sit and survey the

playground, wait for the first bell before they run down. he stand

beside Peter.



“all this be my Kingdom,” he say, wave both his arm about over the

Weald. “I be King of all this, I have a great army.”



“not over this part,” say Peter modestly but firmly.



“you be King up to here,” say young Winterbaum. “you have an army too.”



“_i_ want a kingdom too,” say Joan.



Young Winterbaum propose a fair division of Peter’s kingdom between

Joan and Peter.



Peter let Joan have what young Winterbaum give she. it take some moment

to grasp this new situation. “my kingdom,” he say suddenly, “go right

over to those pond there and up to the church.”



“you cann’t,” say young Winterbaum. “_I’ve_ claim that.”



Peter grunt. it do not seem worth while to have a kingdom unless

those pond be include.



“but if you like I’ll give your people permission to go over all that

country whenever they like.”



Peter still feel there be a catch in it somewhere.



“I’ve get a hundred and seven soldier,” say young Winterbaum. “and six

gun that shoot.”



Joan be surprised and shock to hear that Peter have five hundred

soldier.



“each of my soldier, each one, count as a thousand man,” say young

Winterbaum, get ahead again.



then the first bell rang and suspend the dispute. but Peter go down

to the school with a worried feeling. he wish he have think of

claim all Surrey as his kingdom first. it be a lamentable oversight.

he be disposed to ask the eld Sheldrick girl whether young

Winterbaum really have a _right_ to claim all the Weald. there be a

reason in these thing....



Young Winterbaum have an extraordinary knack of accentuate possession.

Joan and Peter be very pleased and proud to have bicycle; the first

time they arrive upon they at the school young Winterbaum take

possession of they and examine they thoroughly. they be really good

bicycle, excellent bicycle, he explain, and new, not second-hand;

but they be not absolutely the good sort. the good sort nowadays have

wood rim. he be go to have a bicycle with wood rim. and there

ought to be a Bowden brake in front as well as behind; the one in front

be only a spoon brake. it be a pity to have a spoon brake; it would

injure the tyre. he doubt if the tubing be helical tubing. and the

bell ben’t a “king of the Road.” it be no good for Peter to pretend it

have a good sound, “the King of the Road” have a well sound. when young

Winterbaum get his bicycle _his_ bell be go to be a “king of the

Road, 1902 pattern.”...



Young Winterbaum be always do this with thing, bring they up

into the foreground of life, grade they, make they competitive and

irritate. there be no getting ahead of he. he make Peter feel that

the very dust in the Winterbaum dustbin be Grade A. Standard I. while

the Ingle-Nook be satisfied with any old makeshift stuff.



Young Winterbaum’s clothe be make by Samuelson’s, the good boy’

tailor in London; there be no dispute it because there be an

advertisement in _the Daily Telegraph_ that say as much; he be in

trouser and Peter have knickerbocker; he wear sock suspender, and he

have his name in gold letter inside his straw hat. also he have a

pencil-case like no other pencil-case in the school. he be always

propose a comparison of pencil-case.



his imagination turn precociously and easily to romance and love and

the beauty of woman. he read a number of novelette that he have borrow

from his sister’s nurse. he impart to Peter the idea of a selective

pair off of the specie, an idea for which _a Midsummer Night’s

Dream_ have already prepare a favourable soil. it be after he have see

Joan dance her dance when that play be perform and hear the

unstinted applause that greet she, that he decide to honour she above

all the school with his affection. previously he have waver between

the eld Sheldrick girl because she be the big, tall and

heavy girl in the school (though a formidable person to approach) and

little Minnie Restharrow who be top in so many class. but now he know

that Joan be “it,” and that he be in love with she.



but some instinct tell he that Peter have to be deal with.



he approach Peter in this manner.



“who’s your girl, Peter?” say young Winterbaum. “who be your own true

love? you’ve get to have some one.”



Peter draw a bow at a venture, and subconscious process guide the

answer. “Sydney Sheldrick,” he say.



Young Winterbaum seem to snatch even before Peter have do speak.

“I’m go to have Joan,” he say. “she dance well than any one.

she’s go to be, oh!—a lovely woman.”



Peter be dimly aware of an error. he have forget Joan. “I’m go to

have Joan too,” he say.



“you cann’t have two sweetheart,” say young Winterbaum.



“I _can_. I’m go to. I’m different.”



“but Joan’s mine already.”



“get out,” say Peter indignantly. “you cann’t have she.”



“but she’s mine.”



“shut it,” say Peter vulgarly.



“I’ll fight you a duel for she. we will fight a real duel for she.”



“you haven’t well begin,” say Peter.



“but I mean—you know—a duel, Peter.”



“let’s fight one now,” say Peter, “’f you think you’re go to have

Joan for _your_ girl.”



“we will fight with sword.”



“stick.”



“yes, but _call_ they sword. and we shall have to have second and a

doctor.”



“Joan’s my second.”



“you cann’t have Joan. _my_ second’s the Grand Duke of Surrey-Sussex.”



“then mine’s Bungo-Peter.”



“but we’ve get no stick.”



“I know where there’s two stick,” say Peter. “under the stair. and we

can fight in the shrubbery over by the fence.”



the stick be convenient little cane. “they ought to have hilt,”

say young Winterbaum. “you ever fence?”



“not much,” say Peter guardedly.



“I’ve often fence with my cousin, the honourable Ralph—you know. like

this—guard. one. two. you’ve get to have a wrist.”



they repair to the field of battle. “we stand aside while the second

pace out the ground,” explain young Winterbaum. “now we shake hand.

now we take our place.”



they proceed to strike fencer-like attitude. Young Winterbaum

suddenly become one of the master swordsman of the world, but Peter be

chiefly intent on where he should hit young Winterbaum. he have get to

hit he and hurt he a lot, or else he would get Joan. they cross

sword. then young Winterbaum feint and Peter hit he hard on the arm.

then young Winterbaum thrust Peter in the chest, and begin to explain at

once volubly that Peter be now defeat and dead and everything

conclusively settle.



but nobody be go to take away Peter’s Joan on such easy term.

Peter, give his antagonist no time to complete his explanation,

slash he painfully on the knuckle. “I’m _not_ dead,” say Peter,

slash again. “I’m not dead. see? come on!”



Whereupon young Winterbaum cry out, as it be with a trumpet, in a

loud and grief-stricken voice. “now I shall _hurt_ you. that’s too

much,” and swipe viciously at Peter’s face and raise a weal on Peter’s

cheek. Whereupon Peter, feel that Joan be slip from he, begin

to rain blow upon young Winterbaum wherever young Winterbaum might be

suppose to be tender, and young Winterbaum begin to dance about

obliquely and cry out, “mustn’t hit my leg. mustn’t hit my leg. not

fair. oo-oh! my knuckle!” and after one or two revengeful slash at

Peter’s head which Peter—who have have his experience with Joan in a

rage—parry with an uplifted arm, young Winterbaum turn and run—run

into the arm of Miss Murgatroyd, who have be attract to the

shrubbery by his cry....



it be the first fight that have ever happen in the school of St.

George and the Venerable Bede since its foundation.



“he say I couldn’t fight he,” say Peter.



“he go on fighting after I’d pink he,” say young Winterbaum.



neither of they say a word about Joan.



so Miss Murgatroyd make a great session of the school, and the two

combatant, flush and a little heroic, sit on either side of she

discourse. she say that this be the first time she have ever have to

reprove any of her pupil for fight. she hope that never again would

it be necessary for she to do so. she say that nothing we could do be

quite so wicked as fight because nothing be so flatly contradictory

to our Lord’s commandment that we should love one another. the only

fight we might fight with a good conscience be the good fight. in that

sense we be all warrior. we be fighter for righteousness. in a

sense every one be a knight and a fighter, every girl as well as every

boy. because there be no more reason why girl should not fight as well

as boy. some day she hope this would be recognize, and girl would be

give knighthood and wear their spur as proudly as the opposite sex.

Earth be a battlefield, and none of we must be dumb drive cattle or

submit to injustice or cruelty. we must not think that life be make for

silken ease or self-indulgence. let we think rather of the Red Indian

perpetually in training for conflict, lean and vigorous and breathe

only through his nose. no one who breathe through his or her open mouth

would ever be a fighter.



at this point Miss Murgatroyd seem to hesitate for a time. breathing

be a very attractive topic to she, and it be draw she away from her

main theme. she be, so to speak, dredge for her lose thread in the

swift undertow of hygienic doctrine as one might dredge for a lost

cable. she get it presently, and conclude by hope that this would be

a lesson to Philip and Peter and that henceforth they would learn that

great lesson of Prince Kropotkin’s that co-operation be well than

conflict.



neither of the two combatant listen very closely to this discourse.

Peter be wrestle with the question whether a hot red weal across

one’s cheek be compatible with victory, and young Winterbaum with the

still more subtle difficulty of whether he have be actually run

away or merely step back when he have collide with Miss Murgatroyd,

and what impression this apparently retrograde movement have make on her

mind and upon the mind of Peter. do they understand that sometimes a

swordsman _have_ to go back and could go back without the slight

discredit?...





                                  § 8



after this incident the disposal of Joan cease to be a topic for

conversation between young Winterbaum and Peter, and presently young

Winterbaum convey to Peter in an offhand manner that he adore Minnie

Restharrow as the clever and most charming girl in the school. she

be indeed absolutely the good thing to be get in that way. she be, he

opine, cleverer even than Miss Murgatroyd. he be therefore, he

intimate, in love with Minnie Restharrow. it be a great passion.



so far as Peter be concern, he gather, it might be.



all the canon of romance require that Peter, having fight for and win

Joan, should thereupon love Joan and she only until he be of an age to

marry she. as a matter of fact, having dispose of this invader of his

private ascendancy over Joan, he think no more of she in that

relationship. he decide, however, that if young Winterbaum be go to

have a sweetheart he must have one too, and mysterious process of his

mind indicate Sydney Sheldrick as the only possible person. it be not

that Peter particularly want a sweetheart, but he be not go to let

young Winterbaum come it over he—any more than he be go to let

young Winterbaum be king of more than half of Surrey. he be profoundly

bore by all this competitiveness, but obscure instinct urge he to

keep his end up.



one day Miss Murgatroyd be expatiate to the mother of a prospective

pupil upon the wonderful effect of coeducation in calm the passion.

“the boy and girl grow up together, get use to each other, and

there’s never any nonsense between they.”



“and don’t they—well, take an interest in each other?”



“not in that way. not in any _undesirable_ way. such as they would if

they have be morbidly separate.”



“but it seem almost unnatural for they not to take an interest.”



“experience, I can assure you, show otherwise,” say Miss Murgatroyd

conclusively.



at that moment two figure, gravely converse together, pass across

the lawn in the middle distance; one be a well-grow girl of thirteen

in a short-skirt gymnasium dress, the other a nice-look boy of ten,

knickerbockere, bare-legged, sandalle, and wear the art green

blouse of the school. they look the most open-air and unsophisticated

child of modernity it be possible to conceive. this be what they

be say:



“Sydney, when I grow up I’m go to marry you. you got to be my

sweetheart. see?”



“you darling! be that what you have to tell I? I don’t think you love

I a little bit.”



“I’m go to marry you,” say Peter, stick to the fact of the case.



“I’d hug you. only old Muggy be look out of the window. but the very

first chance I get I’ll kiss you. and you’ll have to kiss I back, mind,

Peter.”



“where some one cann’t see we,” Peter stipulate.



“oh! I _love_ spoon,” say the ardent Sydney. “’member when I kiss

you before?...”



“the girl refine the boy and the whole atmosphere be just a _family_

atmosphere,” Miss Murgatroyd be explain at the window.









                           CHAPTER the EIGHTH

                   the HIGH cross PREPARATORY SCHOOL





                                  § 1



from the time when he be christen until he be ten, Lady Charlotte

Sydenham remain only a figure in the remotest background of Peter’s

life. once or twice he see she in the downstairs room at the Ingle-Nook

with his aunt bristle defensively beside she, and once she come to

the school, and each time she look at he with a large, hard, hostile

smile and say: “and ha-ow’s Peter?” and then with a deepening

disapproval: “ha-ow’s Joan?” but that do not mean that Lady Charlotte

have do with Joan and Peter, nor that she have relinquish in the

slight degree her claim to dominate their upbringing. she be just

let they grow up a little “accord to their mother’s idea, poor

woman,” and bide her time. she write every now and then to Aunts

Phyllis and Phœbe, just to remind they of her authority, and she write

two long and serious letter to Oswald about what be to be do. he

answer she briefly in such term as: “let well alone. religion come

later.” Oswald have never return to England. he have be in Uganda now

for five long year, and her fear of he be die down. she be

begin to think that perhaps he do not care very much for Joan and

Peter. he have have blackwater fever again. perhaps he would never come

home any more.



then in the year 1901 and 1902 she have be much occupy by a special

campaign against various London socialist that have end in a libel

case. she be quite convinced that all socialist be extremely immoral

people, she be greatly alarmed at the spread of socialism, and so she

write and employ a secretary to write letter to a number of people

mark “private and confidential,” warn they against this or that

prominent socialist. in these she make various definite statement

which, as her counsel vainly try to argue, be not to be regard as

statement of fact so much as illustration of the tendency of socialist

teaching. she be tackle by a gentleman in a red necktie name Bamshot,

of impregnable virtue, in whom her free gift of “numerous illegitimate

child” have evoke no gratitude. her effort to have he “thoroughly

cross-examined” produce no sympathy in either judge or jury. all man,

she realize, be wicked and anxious to shield each other. she leave the

court with a passionate and almost uncontrollable desire to write more

letter about Bamshot and more, bad than ever, and with much nasty

charge. and it be perhaps a subconscious effort to shift the pressure

of this dangerous impulse that turn her mind to the state of spiritual

neglect in which Joan and Peter be grow out of childhood.



a number of other minor cause move she in the same direction. she have

have a violent quarrel about the bill with the widow of an anglican

clergyman who keep her favourite pension at Bordighera; and she could

still not forgive the establishment at Pallanza that, two year before,

have refuse to dismiss its head-waiter for say “Vivent les boer!” in

her hearing. she have be take advice about a suitable and thoroughly

comfortable substitute for these resort, and meanwhile she have stay

on in England—until there be oyster on the table. Lady Charlotte

Sydenham have an unrefined appetite for oyster, and with oyster come a

still less refined craving for Dublin stout. it be an odd secret

weakness understand only by her domestic, and note only by a small

circle of intimate friend.



“I don’t seem to fancy anything very much today, Unwin,” Lady Charlotte

use to say.



“I don’t know if you’d be tempt by a nice oyster or two, m’lady.

they’re very pick-me-up thing,” the faithful attendant would suggest.

“it’s September now, and there’s an r in the month, so it’s safe to

venture.”



“Mm.”



“and if I might make so bold as to add a ’arf bottle of good Guinness,

m’lady. it’s a tonic. run down as you be.”



without oyster neither Lady Charlotte nor Unwin would have consider

stout a proper drink for a lady. and indeed it be not a proper drink

for Lady Charlotte. a very little stout suffice to derange she

naturally delicate internal chemistry. upon the internal chemistry of

Lady Charlotte her equanimity ultimately depend. there be wrath in

stout....



then Mr. Grimes, who have never cease to hope that considerable

out-of-court activity might still be develop around these two little

ward, have take great pain to bring Aunt Phœbe’s _Collected Papers of

a Stitchwoman (Second Series)_ and her little precious volume _Carmen

Naturæ_ before his client’s notice.



these book certainly make startling reading for Lady Charlotte. she have

never see the first “Stitchwoman” paper, she know nothing of

Swinburne, Ruskin, Carlyle, the decadent, nothing of the rich

inspiration of the later victorian period, and so the almost luscious

richness of Aunt Phœbe’s imagination, her florid verbiage, her note of

sensuous defiance, burst almost devastatingly upon a mind that be

habituate to the order passion and pearly grey of Mrs. Henry Wood’s

novel _More leave, good word_, and _the Quiver_.



“’with what measure ye mete,’” she read, “’so shall it be mete unto you

again,’ and the Standard that Man have fix for woman recoil now upon

his head. which standard be it to be,—His or Hers? no long can we

fight under two flag. wild oat, or the Immaculate Banner? question to

be answer shrewdly, and accord to whether we deem it be experience

or Escape we live for, now that we be out of Eden foot it among the

sturdy, exhilarate thistle. what will ye, my masters?—pallid man

unstaine, or seasoned woman? Judgment hesitate. Judgment may indeed

hesitate. I, who sit here stitch, mark her hesitation,

myself—observant. be it too bold a speculation that presently golden

lassie as well as golden lad will sow their wild oat bravely on the

slope of life? be it too much to dream of that grave mother of a

great world, the Woman of the Future, glance back from the glowing

harvest of her life to some tall premonition by the wayside?—her one

Wild Oat! the crown and seal of her education!”



“either she mean nothing by that,” say Lady Charlotte, “or she mean

just sheer depravity. Wild Oat, indeed! really! to call it _that!_ with

Joan on her hand already!”



and here again be a little poem from _Carmen Naturæ_, which also

impress Lady Charlotte very unfavourably:



                      the MATERIALIST SINGS



                  Put by your tangled trinity

                    and let the atom swing,

                  the merry magic atom

                    that trace out everything.



                  these ancient god be fantasy,

                    Mere Metaphors and Names;

                  but I can feel the Vortex Ring

                    go singe through my vein.



                  no casket of a pallid ghost,

                    but all compact of thrill,

                  my body beat and throb and life,

                    my Mighty Atom will.



“I _don’t_ know what the world be come to,” say Lady Charlotte. “in

other time a woman who venture to write such blasphemy would have be

Struck Dead....”



“thrill again!” say Lady Charlotte, turn over the offend page.

“in a book that any one may read. expose her thrill to any Bagman who

choose to put down three and sixpence for the pleasure. imagine it,

Unwin!”



Unwin do she good, assume an earnest expression....



other contributory influence upon Lady Charlotte’s state of mind be

her secret anxiety for the moral welfare of the realm now that Queen

Victoria have give place to the notoriously lax Edward VII., and the

renascence of sectarian controversy in connexion with Mr. Balfour’s

Education Act. anglicanism be rouse itself for a new struggle to keep

hold of the nation’s child, the Cecils and Lord Halifax be range

wide and free with the educational dragnet, and Lady Charlotte be a

part of the great system of Anglicanism. the gale that blow the ship

home, lift the leave.... but far more powerful than any of these

cause be the death of a certain Mr. Pybus, who be Unwin’s

brother-in-law; he die through an operation undertake by a plucky

rather than highly educate general practitioner, to remove a neglected

tumour. this leave Unwin’s sister in want of subsidy, and while Unwin

lie in bed one night puzzle over this family problem, it occur to

she that if her sister could get some little girl to mind——...





                                  § 2



Mr. Grimes be very helpful and sympathetic when Lady Charlotte

consult he. he repeat the advice he have give five year ago, that

Lady Charlotte should not litigate but act, and so thrust upon the other

party the onus of litigation. she should obtain possession of the two

child, put they into suitable schools—“I don’t see how we can put

that by-blow into a school,” Lady Charlotte interpolate—and refuse to

let the aunt know where they be until they consent to reasonable

term, to the proper religious education of the child, to their

proper clothing, and to their separation. “directly we have the

engagement of the Misses Stubland not to disturb the new arrangement,”

say Mr. Grimes, “we shall have gain our point. I see no harm in

let the child rejoin their aunt for their holiday.”



“that woman may corrupt they at any time,” say Lady Charlotte.



“on that point we can watch and enquire. of course, the boy might stay

at the school for the holiday time. there be a class of school which

cater for that sort of thing. that we can see to later.”...



Mr. Grimes arrange all the detail of the abduction of Joan and Peter

with much tact and imagination. as a preliminary step he make Lady

Charlotte write to Aunt Phœbe express her opinion that the time be

now ripe to put the education of the child upon a rational footing.

they be no long little child, and it be no long possible for

they to go on as they be go. Peter be bear an english gentleman,

and he ought to go to a good preparatory school for boy forthwith;

Joan’s destiny in life be different, but they be certainly

destiny for which play-act, run about with bare foot, and

dress like a little savage could be no sort of training. Lady

Charlotte (Mr. Grimes make she say) have be hope against hope that

some suggestion for a change would come from the Misses Stubland. she

could not hope against hope for ever. she must therefore request a

conference, at which Mr. Grimes could be present, for a discussion of

the new arrangement that be now urgently necessary. to this the

Misses Stubland reply evasively and carelessly. in their reply Mr.

Grimes, without resentment, detect the hand of Mr. Sycamore. they be

willing to take part in a conference as soon as Mr. Oswald Sydenham

return. they have reason to believe he be on his way to England now.



Lady Charlotte, still guide by Mr. Grimes, then assume a more

peremptory tone. she declare that in the interest of both child it

be impossible for thing to go on any long as they have be go.

already the boy be ten. the plea that nothing could be do until Mr.

Sydenham return be a mere delaying device. the boy ought to go to

school forthwith. Lady Charlotte be extremely sorry that the Misses

Stubland would not come to any agreement upon this urgent matter. she

could not rest content with thing in this state, and she would be

oblige to consider what her course of action—for the time have come for

she to take action—must be.



with the way thus clear, Mr. Grimes set his force in motion. “leave

it to I, Lady Charlotte,” he say. “leave it to I.” a polite young man

appear one morning seat in a chariot of fire outside the road gate

of the School of St. George and the Venerable Bede. he be in one of

those strange and novel portent, a “motor-car.” this alone make he

interesting and attractive, and it greatly impress young Winterbaum to

discover that the visitor have come about Joan and Peter. Young

Winterbaum go out to scrutinize the motor-car and its driver, and see

if there be anything wrong about it. but it be difficult to

underestimate.



“it’s a petrol car,” he say. “Belsize.... those be fine lamp.”



Miss Murgatroyd gather that the guardian of Joan and Peter find it

necessary to interview the child, and have send the car to fetch they.



“Miss Stubland say nothing of this when I see she the day before

yesterday,” say Miss Murgatroyd. “we do not care for interruption in

the child’s work.”



the young man explain that the case be urgent. “Lady Charlotte have

be call away. and she must see the child before she go out of

England.”



there be something very reassuring about the motor-car. they depart

cheerfully to the ill-conceal envy and admiration of young Winterbaum.



the young man have red hair, a white, freckled face, and a costly and

remarkable make-up necktie of green plush. the expression of his pale

blue eye be apprehensive, and ever and again he blow. his effort at

conversation be fragmentary and unilluminating. “I get to take you for

a long ride,” he say, seat himself between Peter and Joan. “a lovely

long ride.”



“where?” say Joan.



“you’ll see in a bit,” say the young man.



“we go to Chastlands?” ask Peter.



“no,” say the young man.



“then where be we go?” say Peter.



“these here cars’ll do forty—fifty mile an hour,” say the young man,

change the subject.



in a little while they have pass beyond the limit of Peter’s knowledge

altogether, and be upon an unknown road. it be astonish how the

car devour the road. you see a corner a long way off and then

immediately you be turn this corner. the car go as swiftly up the

hill as down. it say “honk.” the tree and hedge fly by as if one

be in a train, and behind we trail a marvellous cloud of dust. the

driver sit before we with his head sink between his hunch-up

shoulder; he never seem to move; he be quite different from the

swaying, noble coachman with the sun-red face, wear a top hat with a

waist and a broad brim, who sit erect and poise his whip and drive Lady

Charlotte’s white horse.





                                  § 3



for a time the road run undulating between high hedge and tall tree

and through village, and all along to the right of it be the steep,

round-head down. then come a little town, and the automobile turn

off into a valley that cut the Downs across and open out more and

more, and then come heathery common and a town, and then lane and many

village, flat meadow and flat, poplar, and then another town with

a bridge, and then across long level of green a glimpse of the big

tower of Windsor Castle. “this be Runnymede, where Magna Carta be

sign,” say the young man suddenly. “and that’s Windsor, where the

King live—when he ben’t live somewhere else, as he usually do....

he’s a _’ot_ un be the king.... see the chap there sail a boat?”



they go right into Windsor and have a glimpse of the great gate of the

Castle and the round tower very near to they, and then they turn down

a steep, narrow, paved street and so come into a district of little mean

villa in row and row. and outside one of these the car stop.



“here we be,” say the young man.



“where be we?” ask Peter.



“where we get out,” say the young man. “time we have a feed.”



“Dinnah,” say Joan, with a bright expression, and prepare to descend.



a small, white-faced, anxious woman appear at the door. she be

wear amiability as one wear a Sabbath garment. moreover, she have a

greyish-black dress that end in a dingy, stiff buff frille at the

neck and wrist.



“you Mrs. Pybus?” ask the young man.



“I been expect you a nour,” say Mrs. Pybus, acquiesce in the name.

“be this the young lady and gentleman?”



that again be a question that need no answer. the group halt

awkwardly on the doorstep for a few second. “and this be Miss Joan?”

say Mrs. Pybus, with a joyless smile. “I don’t expect you to be ’arf

yr’ size. and what a short dress they put you in! you must ’ave regular

shoot up. make you what I call leggy....”



this again be poor as a conversational opening.



“’ow old might you be, dearie?” ask Mrs. Pybus.



“I’m eight,” say Joan. “but I’ll be nine soon.”



the young man for inscrutable reason find this funny. he guffaw.

“she’s eight,” he say to the world at large; “but she’ll be nine soon.

that’s good, that be!”



“if you’re spare, you shud say,” say Mrs. Pybus. “you’re a big eight,

any’ow. ’ow old be _you_, dear?”



Peter be dislike she quietly with his hand in his pocket. he pause

for a moment, doubt whether he would answer to the name of “dear.”

“Ten,” he say.



“just ten?” ask the young man as if alert for humour.



Peter nod, and the young man be thwart.



“I suppose you’ll be ready for something to eat,” say Mrs. Pybus.

“’Adn’t you well come in?”



they go in.



the room they enter be, perhaps, the most ordinary sort of room in

England at that time, but it strike upon the observant mind of Joan and

Peter as be strange and remarkable. they have never be before in an

ordinary english living-room. it be a small, oblong room with a faint

projection towards the street, as if it have attempt to develop a bow

window and have lack the strength to do so. on one side be a fireplace

surmount by a mantelshelf and an “overmantel,” an affair of

walnut-wood with a number of patch of look-glass and small bracket

and niche on which be display an array of worthless object make to

suggest ornament, small sham bronze, shepherdess, sham japanese

fan, a disjointed german pipe and the like. in the midst of the

mantelshelf stand a black marble clock insist fixedly that the time

be half-past seven, and the mantelshelf itself and the fireplace be

“drape” with a very cheap figure muslin that one might well have

suppose have never be to the wash except for the fact that its pattern

be so manifestly wash out. the wall be paper with a florid pink

wallpaper, and all the woodwork be paint a dirty brownish-yellow

colour and “grain” so as to render the detection of dirt impossible.

small as this room be there have be a strenuous and successful attempt

to obliterate such floor space as it contain by an accumulation of

useless furniture; there be flimsy thing call whatnot in two of

its corner, there be a bulky veneered mahogany chiffoni opposite the

fireplace, and in the window two fern and a rubber plant in

wool-adorn pot die slowly upon a rickety table of bamboo. the wall

have be a basis for much decorative activity, partly it would seem to

conceal or minimize a mysterious skin disease that affect the

wallpaper, but partly also for a mere perverse impulse towards litter.

there be weak fret work bracket stick up for their own sake and more

or less askew, and stouter bracket entrust with the support of more

“ornament,” small bowl and a tea-pot that valiantly pretend they

be thing of beauty; there be cross palm fan, there be a steel

engraving of Queen Victoria give the Bible to a dusky potentate as the

secret of England’s greatness; there be “the Soul’s Awakening,” two

portrait of George and May, and a large but faded photograph of the sea

front at Scarborough in an Oxford frame. a gas “chandelier” descend

into the midst of this apartment, betray a confused ornate

disposition in its line, and the obliteration of the floor space be

complete by a number of black horsehair chair and a large table, now

“lay” with a worn and greyish-white cloth for a meal. such be the

home that the victorian age have evolve by the million in England, and

to such nest do the common mind of the british resort when it wish

to meditate upon the problem of its imperial destiny. Joan and Peter

survey it open-mouthed.



the table be lay about a cruet as its central fact, a large, metallic

edifice surmount by a ring and bear weary mustard, spiritless

pepper, fade cayenne pepper, vinegar and mystery in bottle. Joan and

Peter be interested in this strange object and at the same time

vaguely aware of something miss. what they miss be flower; on

this table there be no flower. there be a cold joint, a white jug of

beer and a glass jug of water, and pickle. “I get cold meat,” say Mrs.

Pybus, “not be sure when you be come.” she arrange her guest.

but she do not immediately begin. she have have an idea. she regard

Peter.



“now, Peter,” she say, “let I ’ear you say Grice.”



Peter wonder.



“say Grice, dearie.”



“Grice,” say Peter.



the young man with the red hair be convulse with merriment. “that’s

good,” he say. “that’s reely good. kid _be_ amusing.”



“but I tole you to say Grice,” say Mrs. Pybus, ruffle.



“I say it.”



the young man’s voice squeak as he explain. “he don’t know _’ow_

to say Grace,” he say. “never ’eard of it.”



“be it a catch?” ask Peter.



the young man catch and restrain a fresh outburst of merriment with

the back of his hand, and then explain again to Mrs. Pybus.



“’E’s a perfec’ little ’eathen,” say Mrs. Pybus. “I _never_ do.

they’ll teach you to say grice all right, my boy, before you’re very

much old. mark my word.” and with a sort of businesslike reverence

Mrs. Pybus gabble her formula. then she proceed to carve. as she

carve she purse her lip and frown.



the cold meat be not bad, but the child eat fastidiously, and Joan,

after her fashion, leave all her fat. this attract the attention of

Mrs. Pybus. “eat it up, dearie,” say Mrs. Pybus. “Wiste not, want not.”



“I don’t eat fat.”



“but you _must_ eat fat,” say Mrs. Pybus.



Joan shake her head.



“we’ll ’ave to teach you to eat fat,” say Mrs. Pybus with a dangerous

gentleness. for the time, however, the teaching be not insist upon.

“lovely bit! enough to feed a little dog,” say Mrs. Pybus, as she

remove Joan’s plate to make way for apple tart.



the conversation be intermittent. it be as if they wait for some

further event. the young man with the red hair speak of the great world

of London and the funeral of Lord Salisbury.



“’e be a great statesman, say what you like,” say the young man with

red hair.



he also speak of Holbein’s attempt to swim the channel.



“they say ’e oil ’imself all over,” say the young man.



“Lor’!” say Mrs. Pybus.



“it cann’t be comfortable,” say the young man; “say what you like.”



presently the young man break a silence by say: “these here balkan

seem to be give trouble again.”



“troublesome lot they be,” say Mrs. Pybus.



“Greeks and Macedonians and Turks and Bulgarians and such. it fair make

my head spin, the lot of they. servian there be too, and montenegroe.

too many of ’em altogether. cat and dog.”



“be they the same Greeks that use to be so clever?” ask Mrs. Pybus.



“_use_ to be,” say the young man with a kind of dark scorn, and

suddenly begin to pick his tooth with a pin.



“they cann’t even speak their own language now—not properly. fair

rotten,” the young man add.



he fascinate Joan. she have never watch anything like he. but Peter

just hate he.





                                  § 4



upon this scene there presently appear a new actor. he be prelude by

a knocking at the door, he be usher in by Mrs. Pybus who be open

and shut her mouth in a state of breathless respect; he be receive

with the utmost deference by the young man with red hair. indeed, from

the moment when his knocking be hear without, the manner and bearing

of the red-haired young man undergo the most marvellous change. an

agitated alacrity appear in his manner; he stand up and move

indicate he now regret wear such a bright green tie. the newcomer

appear in the doorway. he be a tall, grey-clothe, fair gentleman, with

a face that twitch and a hand that dandle in front of he. he grin

his tooth at the room. “so thassem,” he say, touch his tooth with

his thumbnail.



he nod confidentially to the red-haired young man without remove

his eye from Joan and Peter. he show still more of his tooth and

rattle his thumbnail along they. then he wave his hand over the table.

“clear all this away,” he say, and sit down in the young man’s chair.

Mrs. Pybus clear away rapidly, assist abjectly by the young man.



Mr. Grimes seem to check off the two child. “you’re Joan,” he say.

“I needn’t bother about you. you’re provide for. Peter. Peter’s our

business.”



he get out a pocket-book and pencil. “let’s look at you, Peter. just

come out here, will you?”



Peter obey reluctantly and suspiciously.



“no stocking. don’t they wear stocking at that school of your?”



“not when we don’t want they,” say Peter. “no.”



“’Mazes I you wear anything,” say Mr. Grimes. “S’pose it’ll come to

that. let’s see your hat.”



“haven’t get a hat,” say Peter. “wouldn’t wear it if I have.”



“_wouldn’t_ you!” say Mr. Grimes. “H’m!”



“nice little handful,” say Mr. Grimes, and hum. he produce a paper

from the pocket-book and read it, rub his tooth with the point of

his pencil.



“lersee whassor outfit we wan’,” say Mr. Grimes. “H’m.... H’m....

H’m....”



he stand up briskly. “well, young man, we must go out and get you some

clothe and thing. what’s call a school outfit. we’ll have to go in

that motor-car again. quickest way. get your hat. but you haven’t get a

hat.”



“I come too,” say Joan.



“no. you cann’t come to a tailor’s, and that’s where we’re go. little

girl cann’t come to tailor, you know,” say Mr. Grimes.



Peter think privately that Mr. Grimes be just the sort of beast who

would take you to a tailor’s. well, he would stick it out. this couldn’t

go on for ever. he allow himself to be guide by Mr. Grimes to the

door. he restrain an impulse to ask to be allow to sit beside the

driver. one don’t ask favour of beast like Grimes.



Joan go to the window to watch the car and Mr. Grimes’ proceeding

mistrustfully.



“I get a nice picture-book for you to look at,” say Mrs. Pybus, come

behind she. “don’t go stand and stare out of the window, dearie.

it’s an idle thing to stare out of window.”



Joan have an unpleasant feeling that she have to comply with this. under

the initiative of Mrs. Pybus she sit up to the table and permit a

large book to be open in front of she, feign attention. she keep

her eye as much as possible on the window. she be aware of Peter

get into the car with Mr. Grimes. there be a sudden buzzing of

machinery, the slam of a door, and the automobile move and vanish.



she give a divided attention to the picture-book before she, which be

really not properly a picture-book at all but an old bind volume of the

_Illustrated London News_ full of wood engraving of royal procession

and suchlike desiccated matter. it be a dusty, frowsty volume,

damp-stain at the edge. she try to be amuse. but it be very grey

and dull, and she feel strangely uneasy. every few minute she would

look up expect to see the car back outside, but it do not return....



she hear the red-haired young man in the passage say he think he’d

have to be get round to the railway-station, and there be some

point explain by Mrs. Pybus at great length and over and over again

about the difference between the Great Western and the South Western

Railway. the front door slam after he at last, and Mrs. Pybus be

audible return to her kitchen.



presently she come and look at Joan with a thin, unreal smile on her

white face.



“get on all right with the pretty picture, dearie?” she ask.



“when’s Peter come back?” ask Joan.



“oh, not for a longish bit,” say Mrs. Pybus. “you see, he’s go to

school.”



“can I go to school?”



“not _’be_ school. he’s go to a boy school.”



“oh!” say Joan, learn for the first time that school have sex.

“can I go out in the garden?”



“it ben’t much of a garden,” say Mrs. Pybus. “but what there be you’re

welcome.”



it ben’t much of a garden. rather it be a yard, into which a lean-to

scullery, a coal shed, and a dustbin bit deeply. along one side be a

high fence cut it off from a similar yard, and against this high

fence a few nasturtium ginger the colour scheme. a clothe-line

stretch diagonally across this space and bear a depressed pair of

black stocking, and in the corner at the far end a lilac bush be

slowly but steadily and successfully wish itself dead. the opposite

corner be devoted to a collection of bottle, the rib of an umbrella,

and a dust-pan that have lose its handle. from beneath this curious

rather than pleasing accumulation peep the skeleton of a “rockery”

build of brick clinker and free from vegetation of any sort. an unseen

baby a garden or two away deplore its existence loudly. at interval a

voice that sound like the voice of an embitter little girl cut

across these lamentation:



“well, you shouldn’t ’ave _break_ yer bottle,” say the voice, with a

note of moral demonstration....



Joan stay in this garden for exactly three minute. then she return

to Mrs. Pybus, who be engage in some dim operation with a kettle in

the kitchen. “drat this old kitchener!” say Mrs. Pybus, rattle at a

damper.



“want to go ’ome,” Joan say, in a voice that betray emotion.



Mrs. Pybus turn her meagre face and survey Joan without excessive

tenderness.



“this _be_ your ’ome, dearie,” she say.



“I live at Ingle-Nook,” say Joan.



Mrs. Pybus shake her head. “all that’s be do away with,” she say.

“your aunt ’ave give you up, and you’re go to live ’ere for

good—’long o’ I.”





                                  § 5



meanwhile Mr. Grimes, with a cheerful kindliness that Peter perceive to

be assume, convey that young gentleman first to an outfitter, where

he be subject to nameless indignity with a tape, and finally send

behind a screen and tell to change out of his nice, comfortable old

clothe and Heidelberg sandal into a shirt and a collar and a grey

flannel suit, and hard black shoe. all of which he do in a mute,

helpless rage, because he do not consider himself equal to Mr. Grimes

and the outfitter and his staff (with possibly the chauffeur throw in)

in open combat. he be then take to a hairdresser and severely clip,

which strike he as a more sensible proceeding; the stuff they put on

his head be indeed pleasingly aromatic; and then he be buy some

foolery of towel and thing, and finally a Bible and a prayer-book and

a box. with this box he return to the outfitter’s, and be quite

interested in discover that a pile of thing have accumulate on the

counter, tie, collar and thing, and be to be pack in the box for

he forthwith. a junior assistant be do up his Limpsfield clothe in

a separate parcel. so do we put off childish thing. that parcel be to

go via Mr. Grimes to the Ingle-Nook.



a memory of certain beloved sea story come into Peter’s head. “this my

kit?” he ask Mr. Grimes abruptly.



“you might call it your kit,” say Mr. Grimes.



“be I go on a battleship?” ask Peter.



Mr. Grimes—and the two outfit assistant in sympathy—be loudly

amuse.



“you’re go to High Cross School,” say Mr. Grimes, emerge from his

mirth. “firm treatment. Sound Church training. unruly boy not object

to.”



“I don’t know,” say Peter.



they return to the automobile, and after a mile or so of road and

turning stop outside a gaunt brace of drab-colour semi-detach

villa stand back behind a patch of lawn, and have a wall

enclosure to the left and an overgrown laurel shrubbery to the right.

“here’s High Cross School,” say Mr. Grimes, a statement that be

render unnecessary by a conspicuous black and gold board that rise

above the walled enclosure. they descend.



“Wonther which ithe houth,” muse Mr. Grimes, consult his tooth, and

then suddenly decide and lead Peter towards the right hand of the two

associate door. “this,” say Mr. Grimes, as they wait on the

doorstep, “be a _real_ school.... no nonsense about it,” say Mr.

Grimes.



Peter nod with affected intelligence.



they be usher by a slatternly maid-servant into the presence of a

baldish man with a white, puffy face and pale grey eye, who be wear

a university gown and seem to be expect they. he be stand

before the fireplace in the front parlour, which have a general air of

be a study. there be an untidy desk face the window and

bookshelf in the recess on either side of the fireplace. over the

mantel be a tobacco-jar bear the arm of some college, and reminder

of Mr. Mainwearing’s university achievement in the form of a college

shield and Cambridge photograph.



“well,” say Mr. Grimes, “here’s your young man,” and thrust Peter

forward.



“so you’ve come to join we?” say Mr. Mainwearing with a sort of clouded

amiability.



“join what?” say Peter.



Mr. Mainwearing raise his eyebrow. “High Cross School,” he say.



“I’m at the School of St. George and the Venerable Bede,” say Peter.

“so how can I?”



“no,” say Mr. Grimes; “you’re join here now.”



“but I cann’t go to _two_ school.”



“consequently you’re come to _this_ one,” say Mr. Grimes.



“it’s very sudden,” say Peter.



“what’s this about the School of Saint What’s-his-name?” ask Mr.

Mainwearing of Mr. Grimes.



“it’s just a sort of fad school they’ve be send he to,” Mr. Grimes

explain. “we’re alter all that. it’s a girl’ school, and he’s a

grow boy. it’s a school where socialism and play-act be school

subject, and everybody run about with next to nothing on. so his

proper guardian have decide that’s get to stop. and here we be.”



Mr. Mainwearing regard Peter heavily while this be go on.



“done any square root yet?” he ask suddenly.



Peter have not.



“know the date of Magna Carta?”



Peter do not. “it be under John,” he say.



“I want the date,” say Mr. Mainwearing. “what’s the capital of

Bulgaria?”



Peter do not know.



“know any french irregular verb?”



Peter say he don’t.



“got to begin at the beginning,” say Mr. Mainwearing. “got your

outfit?”



“we’ve just see to that,” say Mr. Grimes. “there’s one or two thing

I’d like to say to you—”



he glance at Peter.



Mr. Mainwearing comprehend. he come and lay one hand on Peter. “time

you see some of your schoolfellow,” he say.



under his guiding pressure Peter be impel along a passage, through

an archway, across an empty but frowsty schoolroom in which one solitary

small boy sit and sob grievously, and so by way of another passage to

a kind of glass back-door from which step go down to a large

gravelled space, behind the high wall that carry the black and gold

board. in the corner be parallel bar. a group of nine or ten boy

be stand round these bar; they be all clothe in the same sort of

grey flannel that Peter be wear, and they have all start round at

the sound of the opening of the door. one shock-head boy, perhaps a

head tall than any of the rest, have a great red mouth beneath a red

nose.



“Boys!” shout Mr. Mainwearing; “here’s a new chum. see that he learn

his way about a bit, Probyn.”



“Yessir!” say the shock-head boy in a loud adult kind of voice.



Mr. Mainwearing give Peter a shove that start he down the step

towards the playground, and slam the door behind he.



Most of these boy be big than any boy that Peter have ever know

before. they look enormous. he reckon some must be fifteen or

sixteen—quite. they be as big as the big Sheldrick girl. Probyn

seem indeed as big as a man; Peter could see right across the

playground that he have a black smear of moustache. his neck and wrist

and elbow stick out of his clothe.



Peter with his hand in his new-find pocket walk slowly towards

these formidable creature across the stony playground. they regard

he enigmatically. so explorer must feel, who land on a strange beach

in the presence of an unknown race of man.





                                  § 6



“come on, fathead!” say Probyn as he draw near.



Peter have expect that tone. he affect indifference.



“what’s your name?” ask Probyn.



“stubland,” say Peter. “you Probyn?”



“stubland,” say Probyn. “stubland. what’s your christian name?”



“Peter. what’s your?”



Probyn disregard this counter question markedly. “Simon Peter, eh!

your father get you out of the Bible, I expect. know anything of

cricket, Simon Peter?”



“not much,” say Simon Peter.



“can you swim?”



“no.”



“can you fight?”



“I don’t know.”



“what’s your father?”



Peter don’t answer. instead, he fix his attention upon a fair-haired

boy of about his own size who be stand at the end of the parallel

bar. “what’s _your_ name?” he ask.



the fair boy look at Probyn.



“damn it!” say Probyn. “I ask _you_ a question, Mr. Simon Peter.”



Peter continue disregardful. “haven’t this school get a flagstaff?” he

ask generally.



Probyn come close to he and grip he by the shoulder. “I ask you

a question, Mr. Simon Peter. what be your father?”



it be a question Peter could not answer because for some obscure reason

he could not bring himself to say that his father be dead. if ever he

say that, he know his father would be dead. but what else could he say

of his father? so he seem to shrink a little and remain mute. “we’ll

have to cross-examine you,” say Probyn, and shake he.



the fair boy come in front of Peter. it be clear he have great

confidence in Probyn. he have a fat, smooth, round face that Peter

dislike.



“Simon Peter,” he say. “answer up.”



“what be your father?” say Probyn.



“what’s your father?” repeat the fair boy, and then suddenly flick

Peter under the nose with his finger.



but this do at least enable Peter to change the subject. he smite at

the fat-faced boy with great vigour and miss he. the fat-face boy

dodge back quickly.



“Hullo!” say Probyn. “ginger!”



“that chap’s not go to touch my nose,” say Peter. “anyhow.”



“touch it when I like,” say the fat-face boy.



“you willn’t.”



“you want to _fight?_” ask the fat-faced boy, conscious of popular

support.



Peter say he ben’t go to have his nose flicked anyhow.



“flick it again, Newton,” say Probyn, “and see.”



“I’ll show you in no time,” say Newton.



“Why!—I’d lick you with one hand,” continue Newton.



Peter say nothing. but he regard his antagonist very intently.



“skinny little snipe,” say Newton. “Whaddyou think you’d do to I?”



“hit he, Newton,” say a cadaverous boy with freckle.



“hit he, Newton. he’s too cocky,” say another. “flick his silly nose

again and see.”



“I’ll hit he ’f’e want it,” say Newton, and button up his jacket in

a preparatory way.



“hit he, Newton,” other voice urge.



“let he put up his fist,” say Newton.



“do that when I please,” say Peter rather faintly.



Newton have seem at first just about Peter’s size. now he seem very

much large. all the boy seem to have grow large. they be

gather in a vast circle of doom round a minute and friendless Peter.

Probyn loom over he like a figure of fate. Peter wonder whether he

need have hit at Newton. it seem now a very unwise thing indeed to

have do. Newton be alternately sway towards he and sway away

from he, and repeat his demand for Peter to put his hand up. he

seem on the verge of flick again. he be go to flick. Probyn

watch they both critically. then with a rapid movement of the mind

Peter realize that Newton’s face be sway now well within his range;

the moment have come, and desperately, with a great effort and a wide and

sweeping movement of the arm, he smite hard at Newton’s cheek. Smack. a

good blow. Newton recoil with an expression of astonishment.

“you—swine!” he say.



two other boy come run across the playground, and voice explain,

“new boy.... fight....”



but curiously enough the fight do not go on. Newton at a slightly

great distance continue to loom threateningly, but do no more than

loom. his cheek be very red. “I’ll break your jaw, cut at I like

that,” he say. “you swine!” he use foul and novel term expressive of

rage. he look at Probyn as if for approval, but Probyn offer none.

he continue to threaten, but he do not come within arm’s length again.



“hit he back, Newton,” several voice urge, but with no success.



“wait till I start on he,” say Newton.



“buck up, young Newton,” say Probyn suddenly, “and stop jaw. you

begin it. _I’m_ not go to help you. make a ring, you chap. it’s a

fair fight.”



Peter find himself face Newton in the centre of an interested circle.



Newton be walk crab fashion athwart the circle, sway with his

fist and elbow high. he be now act a dangerous intentness. “come

on,” he say terribly.



“hit he, Newton,” say the cadaverous boy. “don’t wait for he.”



“you start it, Newton,” Probyn insist. “and he’s hit you fair.”



a loud familiar sound, the clamorous ringing of a bell, strike across

the suspend drama. “that’s tea,” say Newton eagerly, drop his

fist. “it’s no good start on he now.”



“you’ll have to fight he later,” say Probyn. “now he’s hit you.”



“it’s up to you, Newton,” say the cadaverous boy, evidently follow

Probyn’s lead.



“Cavé. it’s noser,” say a voice.



there be a little pause.



“Toke!” cry Probyn.



“Toke, Simon Peter,” say the cadaverous boy informingly....



Peter find himself no long in focus. every one be move towards the

door whence Peter have descend to the playground, and at this door

there now stand a middle-aged man with a large nose and a sly

expression, survey the boy.



impel by gregarious instinct, Peter follow the crowd.



he do not like these hostile boy. he do not like this shabby-look

place. he be quite ready to believe that presently he would have to go

on fight Newton. he be not particularly afraid of Newton, but he

perceive that Probyn stand behind he. he detest Probyn already. he

be afraid of Probyn. Probyn be like a golliwog. he know by instinct

that Probyn be full of disagreeable possibility for he, and that it

would be very hard to get away from Probyn. and what do it all mean?

be he never go back to Limpsfield again?



the bell have have exactly the tone of the tea bell at Miss Murgatroyd’s

school. it might have be the same bell. and it have make his heart

homesick for the colour and brightness of the School of St. George and

the Venerable Bede, and for the friendly garden and familiar room of

Ingle-Nook. for the first time he realize that he have fall into this

school as an animal fall into a trap, that his world have change, that

home be very far away....



and what have they do to Joan?...



have he to live here always?...



it strike Mr. Noakley, the assistant master with the large nose, as he

watch the boy at tea, that the new boy have a face like a doll, but

really that face with its set, shining, expressionless eye be only the

mask, the very thin mask, that cover a violent disposition to

blubber....



well, no one be go to see Peter blub. no one be go to hear he

blub....



tonight perhaps in bed.



he have still to realize the publicity of a school dormitory....



he know he couldn’t box, but he have see something in Newton’s eye that

make he feel that Newton be not invincible. he would grip his fist in

a very knobby way and hit Newton as hard as he could in the face.

Oh!—_frightfully_ hard....



Peter be not eat very much. “bag I your slice of Toke,” say the

cadaverous boy.



“take the beastly stuff,” say Peter.



“little spoilt mammy coddle,” think old Nosey Noakley. “we ben’t good

enough for he.”





                                  § 7



so it be that Mr. Grimes, act for Lady Charlotte, set about the

rescue of Joan and Peter from, as she put it, “the freak, faddist and

Hill-top philosophy of the Surrey hill,” and their restoration to the

establish sobriety and decorum of english life. very naturally this

sudden action come as an astonishing blow to the two advanced aunt. at

nine o’clock that evening Miss Murgatroyd be call down to see Miss

Phyllis Stubland, who have ride over on her bicycle. “where be the

child?” ask Aunt Phyllis.



“you send for they,” say Miss Murgatroyd.



“send for they!”



“yes. I remember now. the young man say it be Lady Charlotte Sydenham.

don’t you know? she be go abroad tomorrow or the next day.”



“send for they!” Aunt Phyllis repeat....



two hour later Aunt Phyllis be tell the terrible news to Mary. Aunt

Phœbe be in London for the night to see Mr. Tree play _Richard II_, and

there be no mean of communicate with she until the morning. the

Ingle-Nook be much too Pre-Raphaelite to possess a telephone, and Aunt

Phœbe be sleep at the flat of a friend in Church Row, Hampstead.

next morning a telegram find she still in bed.



    “child kidnap by Lady Charlotte consult Sycamore Phyllis”



say the telegram.



“_no!_” cry Aunt Phœbe sharply.



then as the little servant-maid be on the point of close the door,

“tell Miss Jepson,” Aunt Phœbe command....



Miss Jepson find Aunt Phœbe out of bed and dress with a rapid

casualness. it be manifest that some great crisis have happen. “an

outrage upon all woman,” say Aunt Phœbe. “I have be outrage.”



“my dear!” say Miss Jepson.



“read that telegram!” cry Aunt Phœbe, point to a small ball of pink

paper in the corner of the room.



Miss Jepson go over to the corner with a perplexed expression, and

smooth out the telegram and read it.



“a _Bradshaw_ and a hansom!” Aunt Phœbe be demand as she move

rapidly about the room from one scatter garment to another. “no

breakfast. I can eat nothing. nothing. I be a tigress. a madden

tigress. madden. beyond endurance. oh! can you reach these button,

dear?”
