Miss Jepson hover about her guest readjust her costume in

accordance with commonplace standard while Aunt Phœbe express herself

in Sibylline utterance.



“child dedicate to the future.... reek of ancient corruption....

Abomination of desolation.... the nine fifty-three.... say half an

hour.... Remonstrance.... an avenge sword.... the sword of the Lord

and of Gideon.”



“be you go to this Mr. Sycamore?” ask Miss Jepson suddenly.



Aunt Phœbe seem lose for a time and emerge with, “good God!—_No!_

this be an occasion when a woman must show she can act as a man. this

try we, Amanda. I will have no man in this. no man at all! be woman

to loll in hareem for ever while man act and fight? when little

child be assail?...”



“chastland,” say Aunt Phœbe to the cabman, wave Miss Jepson’s

_Bradshaw_ in her hand.



the man look stupid.



“oh! Charing Cross,” she cry scornfully. “the rest be beyond you.”



and in the train she startle her sole fellow-traveller and make he get

out at the next station by say suddenly twice over in her loud, clear

contralto voice the one word “_action_.” she leave Miss Jepson’s

_Bradshaw_ in the compartment when she get out.



she find Chastlands far go in pack for Lady Charlotte’s flight

abroad. “I demand Lady Charlotte,” she say. she follow up old Cashel

as he go to announce she. he hear she come behind he, but his

impression of she be so vivid that he deem it wise not to notice

this informality. and besides in his dry, thin way he want to hear why

she demand Lady Charlotte. he perceive the possibility of a

memorable clash. he be a quiet, contemplative man who hide his humour

like a miser’s treasure and live much upon his memory. week after a

thing have happen he would suddenly titter, in bed, or in church, or

while he be clean his plate. and none be tell why he titter.



for a moment Aunt Phœbe hover on the landing outside the Chastlands

drawing-room.



“I cann’t see she,” she hear Lady Charlotte say, with something like a

note of terror. “it be impossible.”



“leave she to I, me Lady,” say a man’s voice.



“tell she to wait, Cashel,” say Lady Charlotte.



Aunt Phœbe enter, trail her artistic robe. before she by the

writing-table in the big window stand Lady Charlotte, flounce,

bonnet, dress as if for instant flight. a slender, fair, wince man

in grey stand nearer, his expression agitated but formidable. they have

evidently both rise to their foot as Aunt Phœbe enter. Cashel make

insincere demonstration of intervention, but Aunt Phœbe dispose of he

with a gesture. a haughty and terrible politeness be in her manner, but

she sob slightly as she speak.



“Lady Charlotte,” she say, “where be my ward?”



“they be _my_ ward,” say Lady Charlotte no less haughtily.



“excuse I, Lady Charlotte. permit I,” say Mr. Grimes, with soothe

gesture of his lean white hand.



“please do not intervene,” say Aunt Phœbe.



“Mr. Grimes, madam, be my solicitor,” say Lady Charlotte. “you may go,

Cashel.”



Cashel go reluctantly.



Mr. Grimes advance a step and dandle his hand and smile

ingratiatingly. italian and spanish woman will stab, he have hear, and

fishwife be a violent class. otherwise he believe all woman, however

terrible in appearance, to be harmless. this give he courage.



“Miss Stubland, I believe,” he say. “these young people, young Stubland

and his foster-sister to wit, be at present in my charge—under

instruction from Lady Charlotte.”



“where?” ask Aunt Phœbe.



“our case, Miss Stubland, be that they be not be properly educate

in your charge. that be our case. they be receive no sound moral and

religious training, and they be be bring up in—to say the least

of it—an eccentric fashion. our aim in take they out of your charge be

to secure for they a proper ordinary English bring up.”



“every word an insult,” pant Aunt Phœbe. “every word. what have you

do with they?”



“until we be satisfied that you will consent to continue their training

on proper line, Miss Stubland, you can scarcely expect we to put it in

your power to annoy these poor child far.”



Mr. Grimes’ face be wince much more than usual, and these involuntary

grimace affect Aunt Phœbe in her present mood as though they be

deliberate insult. he do not allow for this add exasperation.



“annoy!” cry Aunt Phœbe.



“that be the usual expression. we be perfectly within our right in

refuse you access. have regard to your manifest determination to

upset any proper arrangement.”



“you refuse to let I know where those child be?”



“unless you can get an order against we.”



“you mean—go to some old judge?”



Mr. Grimes gesticulate assent. if she choose to phrase it in that way,

so much the bad for her application.



“you willn’t—— you will go on with this kidnapping?”



“Miss Stubland, we be entirely satisfied with our present course and

our present position.”



Lady Charlotte endorse he with three great nod.



Aunt Phœbe stand aghast.



Mr. Grimes remain quietly triumphant. Lady Charlotte stand quietly

triumphant behind he. for a moment it seem as if Aunt Phœbe have no

reply of any sort to make.



then suddenly she advance three step and seize upon Mr. Grimes. one

hand grip his nice grey coat below the collar behind, the other, the

looseness of his waistcoat just below the tie. and lift he up upon

his toe Aunt Phœbe shake he.



Mr. Grimes be a lean, spare, ironical man. Aunt Phœbe be a

well-develop woman. yet only by an enormous effort do she break the

instinctive barrier that make a man sacred from feminine assault. it

be an effort so enormous that when at last it break down the dam of

self-restraint, it come through a boil flood of physical power. it

come through with a sort of instantaneousness. at one moment Mr. Grimes

stand before Lady Charlotte’s eye dominate the scene; at the next he

be, as materialist say of the universe, “all vibration.” he be a

rag, he be a scrap of carpet in Aunt Phœbe’s hand. the appetite for

shake seem to grow in Aunt Phœbe as she shake.



from the moment when Aunt Phœbe grip he until she have do shake

he nobody except Lady Charlotte make an articulate sound. and all that

Lady Charlotte say, before astonishment overcome she, be one loud

“haw!” the face of Mr. Grimes remain set, except for a certain

mechanical rattling of the tooth in a wild stare at Aunt Phœbe; Aunt

Phœbe’s feature bear that earnest calm one may see upon the face of a

good woman who wash clothe or knead bread. then suddenly it be as

if Aunt Phœbe wake up out of a trance.



“you make—you make I forget myself!” say Aunt Phœbe with a low sob,

and after one last shake relinquish he.



Mr. Grimes gyrate for a moment and come to rest against a massive

table. he be still stare at Aunt Phœbe.



for a moment the three people remain breathe heavily and

contemplate the outrage. at last Mr. Grimes be able to

raise a wavering, point finger to gasp, “you have—you

have—yes—indeed—forget yourself!”



then, as if he struggle to apprehend the position, “you—you have

assault I.”



“let it be—let it be a warning to you,” say Aunt Phœbe.



“that be a threat.”



“agree,” pant Aunt Phœbe with spirit, though she have not mean to

threaten he at all.



“if you think, madam, that you can assault I with impunity——”



“I shouldn’t have think it—before I take hold of you. a bag of

bone.... man indeed!” and then very earnestly—“_Yes._”



she pause. the pause hold all three of they still.



“but why—oh, why!—should I bandy word with such a thing as you?” she

ask with a sudden belate recovery of her dignity. “_you—_”



she seek her word carefully.



“Flibber-gib!”



and forget altogether the mission upon which she have come, Aunt

Phœbe turn about to make her exit from the scene. it seem to she,

perhaps justly, that it be impossible to continue the parley far.

“legalized scoundrel!” she say over her shoulder, and move towards the

door. in that first tremendous clash of the New Woman and the Terrific

Old Lady, it must be admit that the New Woman carry off, so to

speak, the physical honour. Lady Charlotte stand against the fireplace

visibly appalled. only when Aunt Phœbe be already at the door do it

occur to Lady Charlotte to ring the bell to have her visitor “show

out.” her shake hand could scarcely find the bell handle. for the rest

she be ineffective, waste great opportunity for scorn and dignity.

she despise herself for not have a large, fiercer solicitor. she

doubt herself. for the first time in her life Lady Charlotte Sydenham

doubt herself, and quail before a new birth of time.



upon the landing appear old Cashel, mutely respectful. he show out

Aunt Phœbe in profound silence. he watch her retreating form with

affectionate respect, stroke his cheek slowly with two finger. he

close the door.



he stand as one who seek to remember. “Flibber-jib,” he say at last

very softly, without exultation or disapproval. he simply want to have

it exactly right. then he go upstairs to have a long, mild, respectful

look at Mr. Grimes, and to ask if he could do anything for he....





                                  § 8



Aunt Phœbe’s return to the Ingle-Nook blend triumph and perplexity.



“I could never have imagine a man so flimsy,” she say.



“but where be the child?” ask Aunt Phyllis.



“if all man be like he—then masculine ascendancy be an imposture.”



(“yes, but where be the child?”)



“so a baulked tigress might feel.”



Aunt Phyllis decide to write to Mr. Sycamore.





                                  § 9



Mr. Mainwearing be the proprietor of a private school for young

gentleman, not by choice but by reason of the weakness of his

character. it be card-playing more than anything else that have make he

an educator. and it be vanity and the want of any sense of proportion

that have lead to the card-playing.



Mr. Mainwearing’s father have be a severe parent, severe to the pitch

of hostility. he have lose his wife early, and he have take a grudge

against his only son, whose look he do not like. he have send he to

Cambridge with a bitter assurance that he would do no good there; have

keep he too short of money to be comfortable, spend most of his

property—he be a retired tea-broker—in disappointing and embitter

jaunt into vice, and die suddenly, leave—unwillingly, but he have to

leave it—about three thousand pound to his heir. Young Mainwearing have

always be short of pocket-money, and for a time he regard this

legacy as limitless wealth; he flash from dingy obscurity into

splendour, get himself coloured shirt and remarkable tie, seek the

acquaintance of horse, slip down to London for music-hall and

“life.” when it dawn upon he that even three thousand pound be not

a limitless ocean of money, he attempt to maintain its level by

win more from his fellow undergraduate. Nap and poker be the

particular form of sport he affect. he reckon that he be, in a

quiet way, rather cleverer than most fellow, and that he would win. but

he be out in his reckoning. he leave Cambridge with a Junior Optime in

the Mathematical Tripos and a residuum of about seven hundred pound. he

be a careful cricketer, and he have like football at school in his

concluding year when he be big enough to barge into the other chap.

survey the prospect before he, he decide that a school be the good

place for he, he advertise himself as “of gentlemanly appearance” and

“good at game,” and he find his billet in a preparatory school at

Brighton. thence he go to a big grammar school, and thence come to the

High Cross School to remain first as assistant, then as son-in-law and

partner, and now as sole proprietor. Mrs. Mainwearing be not very

useful as a helpmeet, as she be slightly but not offensively defective

in her mind; still one must take life as one find it. she be, at any

rate, regular in her habit, and do not interfere with the housekeeper,

a worthy, confidence-create woman, much tip by the tenderer sort of

parent.



of course Mr. Mainwearing have no special training as a teacher. he have

no idea about education at all. he have no social philosophy. he have

never ask why he be alive or what he be up to. Instinct, perhaps,

warn he that the answer might be disagreeable. much less do he

inquire what his boy be likely to be up to. and it do not occur to

he, it do not occur to any one in those day, to consider that these

deficiency bar he in any way from the preparation of the genteel

young for life. he teach as he have be teach; his teacher have do

the same; he be the last link of a long chain of tradition that have

perhaps in the beginning have some element of intention in it as to what

be to be make of the pupil. school, like religion, tend perpetually

to forget what they be for. High Cross School, like numberless school

in Great Britain in those day, have forget completely; it be a

mysterious fated routine; the underlying idea seem to be that boy

must go to school as puppy have the mange. certain school book

exist, God alone know why, and the class be take through they. it

be like read prayer. certain examination board check this

process in a way that Mr. Mainwearing felt reflect upon his honour,

and like all fundamentally dishonest people he be inclined to be touchy

about his honour. but parent want examination result and he have to

give in. preparation for examination dominate the school; no work be

do in the school that do not lead towards an examination paper; if

there have be no examination, no work would have be do at all. but

these examination might have be bad than they be. the examiner

be experience teacher and considerate for their kind. they respect

the great routine. the examiner in classic have, at good, Babu Latin

and less greek, and so they know quite well how to set a paper that

would enable the intelligent candidate to conceal an entire incapacity

for reading, writing, or speak a classical language; the examiner in

mathematic know nothing of practical calculation, and treat the

subject as a sort of Patience game; the foreign language examiner stick

loyally to the grammar; in draw the examiner ask you to copy

“copy,” they do not, at any rate, require you to draw thing; and

altogether the “curse of examination” might have press on Mr.

Mainwearing hard than it do. suppose the language paper have be

just long passage to translate into and out of English, and that the

mathematical test have be all problem, and the drawing test have be a

test of draw anything! what school could have stand the strain?



to assist he in the work of his school Mr. Mainwearing have gather

about he a staff of three. he have find a young man rather of his own

social quality, but very timid, a B.A. Cantab. by way of the botanical

special; then there be Noakley, a rather old, sly creature, with a

large overbalancing nose, who have fail to qualify year ago as an

elementary assistant schoolmaster and so have stray into the uncharted

and uncertificated way of a private school; and finally there be Kahn,

an Alsatian, who teach language and the piano. with these three and

the active assistance of Mrs. Rich, the housekeeper, the school

maintain its sluggish routine.



the boy sleep in two long room that have be make by knock through

partition in the two upper floor, and convert into dormitory by

the simple expedient of crowd they with iron bedstead and small

chest of drawer. it be the business of Noakley—who have a separate

room on the top floor—to arouse the boy at seven with cry and

violence for the business of the day. but there be a tacit

understanding between he and the boy not to molest each other until

about twenty minute past.



it be a rule, establish by Mr. Mainwearing in a phase of hygienic

enthusiasm some year before, that on fine morning throughout the year

the boy should go for a sharp run before breakfast. it be a modern and

impressive thing to do and it cost he nothing. it be Noakley’s duty to

accompany they on this run. he be unable to imagine any more loathsome

duty. so that he have invent a method of supplement the rain of

heaven by mean of a private watering-pot. his room be directly above

Mr. Mainwearing’s, and Mr. Mainwearing sleep with his window shut and

his blind down, and about seven-fifteen or so every morning the curious

passer-by might have see a lean, sly man with an enormous nose, his

mouth wide open and his tongue out with effort, lean far out of an

upper bedroom of High Cross School and industriously and carefully

water the window and window-sill of the room two storey below he.

later, perhaps, a patient observer might have be reward by the

raising of Mr. Mainwearing’s blind and a glimpse of Mr. Mainwearing,

unshaven and in a white cotton nightgown, glance out at the

weather....



so generally the morning begin with a tedious, sticky, still sleepy hour

call Early Prep. in the schoolroom on the ground floor. it be only

during Kahn’s alternate week of morning duty that the run ever occur.

then it ben’t a run. it begin as a run and settle down as soon as it

be out of sight of the school to a sulky walk and a muttered monologue

by Kahn in German—he never speak any language but german before

breakfast—about his “magen.”



Noakley’s method in early prep. be to sit as near to the fire as

possible in the winter and at the high desk in summer, and to leave the

boy alone so long as they leave he alone. they converse in undertone,

make and throw paper dart at one another, read forbid fiction, and

so forth. breakfast at half-past eight release they, and there be a

spell of playground before morning school at half-past nine. at

half-past nine Mr. Mainwearing and Mr. Smithers, the botanical Cantab,

appear in the world, gown and a little irritable, and prayer and

scripture inaugurate the official day. Mr. Mainwearing’s connexion be

a sound church connexion, and he open the day with an abbreviate

Matins and the collect and lesson for the day. then the junior half of

the school go upstairs to the second class-room with Mr. Smithers,

while Mr. Mainwearing deal tediously with Chronicles or king.

meanwhile Kahn and Noakley correct exercise-book in the third

class-room, and wait their time to take up their part in the great

task of build up the british imperial mind. by eleven o’clock each of

the four class-room be thoroughly stuffy and the school be in full

swing; Mr. Mainwearing, who could not have translate a new satire by

Juvenal to save his life, be “teach” Greek or latin or history, Mr.

Smithers be set or explain exercise on the way to quadratic

equation or Euclid Book II., which be the culminate point of high

Cross mathematic; Kahn, hoarse with loud anger, be make a personal

quarrel of the french class; and Noakley be gently set the foot of

the young boy astray in geography or arithmetic or parse. this be

the high-water mark of the day’s effort.



after the midday dinner, which be greasy and with much too much potato

in it, come a visible decline. in the afternoon Mr. Mainwearing would

start a class upon some sort of exercise, delegate Probyn to keep

order, and retire to slumber in his study; Smithers and Kahn, who both

suffer from indigestion, would quarrel bitterly with boy they

dislike and inflict punishment; Noakley would sleep quietly through a

drawing class on the tacit understanding that there be no audible

misbehaviour, and that the boy would awaken he if they hear Mr.

Mainwearing come.



Mr. Mainwearing, when he come, usually come viciously. he would awaken

in an evil temper and sit curse his life for some time before he could

rouse himself to a return to duty. he would suddenly become fill with

suspicion, about the behaviour of the boy or the worthiness of his

assistant. he would take his cane and return with a heavy scowl on his

face through the archway to his abandon class.



he would hear a murmur of disorder, a squeak of “cavé!” and a hush.



or he would hear Probyn’s loud bellow: “shut up, young Pyecroft. Shut

it, I say!—or I’ll report you!”



he would appear threateningly in the doorway.



“what’s he do, Probyn?” he would ask. “what’s he do?”



“humbug about, Sir. he’s _always_ humbug about.”



the diffused wrath of Mr. Mainwearing would gather to a focus. if there

be no little beast like young Pyecroft he wouldn’t be in this

infernal, dull, dreary hole of a school.



“_I’ll_ teach you to humbug about, Pyecroft,” he would say. “come out,

Sir!”



“please, Sir!”



Roar. “don’t _bandy_ word with I, you little Hound! come out, I say!”



“please——!” Young Pyecroft would come out slowly and weeping. Mr.

Mainwearing would grip he hungrily.



“_I’ll_ teach you to humbug about. (Cut.) I’ll teach you! (Cut.) I cann’t

leave this class-room for a moment but half a dozen of you must go

turn it upside down.” (Cut.)



“wow!”



“don’t answer _I_, Sir!” (Cut.) “don’t answer I.” (Cut.) “_now_, Sir?”



Pyecroft completely subdue. Pyecroft relinquish.



“now, be there any more of you?” ask Mr. Mainwearing, feel a

little well.



then he would hesitate. should he take the set work at once, or should

he steal upstairs on tiptoe to catch out one of the assistant? his

practice vary. he always suspect Noakley of his afternoon sleep, and

be never able to catch he. Noakley sleep with the class-room door

slightly open. his boy could hear the opening of the class-room door

downstair. when they do they would smack down a book upon the desk

close beside he, and Noakley would start teach instantly like an

automaton that have just be release. he don’t take a second to

awaken, so that he be very hard indeed to catch.



the school remain a scene of jade activity until four, when a bell

rang for afternoon prayer under Mr. Mainwearing in the main schoolroom.

then the boy would sing a hymn while Kahn accompany on a small

harmonium that stand in the corner of the room. while prayer be go

on a certain scatter minority of the boy be speculate whether

Kahn or Smithers would remember this or that task that have be impose

in a moment of passion, weigh whether it be safe to obey or forget.

Kahn and Smithers would return to the class-room reluctantly to gather

in the harvest of their own wrath, but now for a little time Noakley be

free to do nothing. Noakley hardly ever impose punishment. when he be

speak to upon the subject he would put his nose down in a thoughtful

manner and reply in a tone of mild observation: “the boy, they seem to

_mind_ I somehow.”



meanwhile the release boy disperse to loaf about the playground and

the outhouse and playing-field until tea at five. sometimes there be a

hectic attempt at cricket or football in the field in which Mr.

Mainwearing participate, and then tea be at half-past five. when Mr.

Mainwearing participate he like to bat, and he do not like to be

bowl out. Noakley be vaguely suppose to superintend tea and evening

prep., and the boy, after a supper of milk and biscuit, be pack

off to bed at half-past eight. it be much too early to send the big

boy to bed, but “Good God!” say Mr. Mainwearing; “be I to have _no_

peace in my day?” and he try to ease his conscience about what might

go on in the dormitory after bedtime by direct Noakley to “exercise

a general supervision,” and by occasionally steal upstairs in his

sock.



Wednesday and Saturday be half-holiday, and in the afternoon the boy

wear flannel or short, accord to the season, and play pick-up

cricket or football or hockey in a well-wear field at the back of the

school, or they go for a walk with Noakley or Smithers. on Sundays

they wear top hat and pseudo-eton jacket, and go to church in the

morning and the evening. in the afternoon Smithers take scripture

wearily for an hour, and then go for a walk with Noakley. and on

Sunday evening they write home carefully supervise letter say how

happy they be and how they be all in the good of health and about

“examinational prospect,” and how they hope they be make

satisfactory progress and suchlike topic. but they never give any

account of the talk that go on during the playground loafing, nor of

the strange game and ceremony over which Probyn preside in the

dormitory, nor of the exercise of Mr. Mainwearing’s cane. there be

no library, and the boy never read anything except school book and

such print matter as they themselves introduce into the school. they

never read nor draw nor paint nor make verse to please themselves.

they never dream of acting or singing. their only training in the use

of their hand be at cricket, and they never look at a newspaper.

occasionally Smithers give a lesson in botany, but there be no other

science teaching. science teaching require apparatus and apparatus

cost money, and so far as the prospectus go it be quite easy to call

the botany “science.”...





                                  § 10



in this manner do High Cross School grind and polish its little batch

of boy for their participation in the affair of the great, most

civilized and most civilizing empire the world have ever see.



it be, perhaps, a bad speciman of an english private school, but it be

a speciman. there be bad as well as well among the school of

England. there be no doubt many new and large, many clean, many

well classify. some have visit drill-sergeant, some have chemistry

cupboard, some have specially build gymnasia, some even have school

library of a hundred volume or so.... Most of they have well housing

and well arrange dormitory. and most of they be consistently

“preparatory,” stick to an upward age-limit, and turn out a boy as

soon as he become a youth to go on to business or medicine or the public

school. Mr. Mainwearing’s school be exceptional in this, that it have

to hold on to all it could get. he have a connexion with one or two

solicitor, an understanding—Mr. Grimes be one of his friend—and his

school contain in addition to Peter several other sample of that

unfortunate type of boy whose school be find for he by a solicitor.

some stay at Windsor with Mr. Mainwearing during the holiday. in that

matter High Cross School be exceptional. but the want of any

intellectual interest, of any spontaneous activity of the mind at all

in High Cross School, be no exceptional thing.



Life never stand altogether still, but it have a queer tendency to form

stationary eddy, and very much of the education of middle-class and

upper-class youth in England have be an eddy for a century. the still

exquisite and impressionable brain of the new generation come tumble

down the stream, curious, active, greedy, and the eddying school catch

they with a grip of iron and spin they round and round for six or seven

precious year and at last fling they out....





                                  § 11



into this vicious eddy about Mr. Mainwearing’s life and school come the

develop brain of Master Peter Stubland, and resent it extremely. at

first he have be too much astonish by his transfer from Limpsfield to

entertain any other emotion; it be only after some day at High Cross

School that he begin to realize that the experience be not simply

astonishing but uncongenial, and indeed hateful.



he discover he hate the whole place. comprehend within this general

hatred be particular one. he hate Newton. the fight remain in

suspense, neither boy know anything of scientific fisticuff, neither

have ever wear a boxing-glove, and both be disposed to evade the hard,

clear issue of the ring. but Newton continue to threaten and grimace at

he, and once as he be pass Peter on the staircase he turn about

and punch he in the back.



for Newton Peter’s hatred be uncomplicated; for Probyn and a second boy

nearly as big, a fair, sleepy boy name Ames, Peter have a feeling that

differed from a clear, clean hatred; it have an element of disgust and

dread in it. Probyn, with Ames as an accessory and Newton as his pet

toady, dominate the school. it be an unnatural and an unwholesome thing

for boy and youth of various age to be herd as closely together as

they be in High Cross School; the natural instinct of the young be

against such an association. in a good, big school whose atmosphere be

wholesome, boy will classify themselves out in the complete way; they

will not associate, they will scarcely speak with boy outside their own

year. there be a foolish way of dispose of this fact by say that

boy be “such snob.” but indeed they be keep apart by the fierce

instinct of self-preservation. all life and all its question be

stir and unfold in the young boy; in every sort of young creature

a natural discretion fight against forced and premature development.

“keep to your phase,” say nature. the old boy, perplex by novel

urgency and curiosity, be embarrassed by their young fellow;

young boy be naturally afraid of old one and a little disposed to

cringe. but what be such consideration as these to a man like

Mainwearing? he have never think over, he have long since forget, his

own development. any boy, old or young, whose parent could pay the

bill, be get into the school and keep in the school as long as

possible. none of the school work be interesting; there be constant

gap in the routine when there be nothing to do but loaf. it be

inevitable that the old boy should become mischievous lout; they

bully and torment and corrupt the young boy because there be

nothing else to do; if there have be anything else to do they would

have absolutely disregard the young boy; and the young boy do

what they could to propitiate these powerful and unaccountable giant.

the young boy “suck up” to the big boy; they became, as it

be, client; they be annex by patron. they profess unlimited

obedience in exchange for protection. Newton, for instance, call

himself Probyn’s “monkey”; Pyecroft be Ames’s. Probyn would help Newton

with his sum, amuse himself by put he to the torture (when Newton

be expect to display a doglike submission) or make he jealous by

profess an affection for other small boy.



Peter come into this stuffy atmosphere of force and undignifie

relationship instinct, though he know it not, with a passionate sense

of honour. from the very beginning he know there be something in these

boy and in their atmosphere that make they different from himself,

something from which he have to keep himself aloof. there be a word

miss from his vocabulary that would have express it, and that word

be “Cad.” but at the School of St. George and the Venerable Bede they

be not teach to call any people “cad.”



he be a boy capable of considerable reserve. he do not, like young

Winterbaum, press his every thought and idea upon those about he. he

could be frank where he be confident, but this sense of difference

smite he dumb. several of his schoolfellow, old Noakley, and Mr.

Mainwearing, become uncomfortably aware of an effect of unspoken comment

in Peter. he would receive a sudden phrase of abuse with a thoughtful

expression, as though he weigh it and compare it with some exterior

standard. this irritate a school staff accustom to use abusive

language. Probyn, after Peter have hit Newton, take a fancy to he that

do not in the least modify Peter’s instinctive detestation of the red

nostril and the sloppy mouth and the voluminous bellow. Peter become

rapidly skilful in avoid Probyn’s conversation, and this monstrously

enhance his attraction for Probyn. Probyn’s attention vary between

deliberate attempt to vex and deliberate attempt to propitiate. he

keep alive the promise of a fight with Newton, and frankly declare that

Peter could lick Newton any day. Newton be as distressed as a cast

mistress.



one evening the cadaverous boy discover Peter draw warrior on

horseback. he report this strange gift to Ames. Ames come demand

performance, and Peter oblige.



“he _can_ draw,” say Ames. “George and the Dragon, eh? it’s _good_.”



Probyn be shout to, and join in the admiration.



Peter draw this and that by request.



“draw a woman,” say Ames, and then, as the nimble pencil obey,

“no—not an old woman. draw—you know. draw a savage woman.”



“draw a girl bathe—like they be in _Ally Sloper’s half-Holiday_,”

say Probyn. “just with light thing on.”



“draw a heathen goddess,” say Ames. “with nothing on at all.”



Peter say he couldn’t draw goddess.



“go on,” say Ames. “draw a savage woman.”



Peter, be press, try a negress. they hang over he insist upon

detail.



“get _out_, young Newton!” cry Probyn. “don’t come hang round here.

he’s draw thing.”



Ames press further request.



“shalln’t draw any more,” say Peter with a sudden disinclination.



“go it, Simon Peter,” say Ames, “don’t be a mammy-good.”



“Gaw! if I could draw!” say Probyn.



but Peter have finish draw.





                                  § 12



no further question be ask Peter about his father, but on Sunday

night, when home-letter time come round, any doubt about the soundness

of his social position be set at rest by Mr. Mainwearing himself.

home-letter from High Cross School involve so many delicate

consideration that the proprietor make it his custom to supervise they

himself. he distribute sheet of paper with the school heading, and

afterwards he collect they and address they himself in his study.

“you, Stubland, must write a letter to your aunt,” he say loudly across

the room, “and tell she how you be get on.”



“Aunt Phyllis?” say Peter.



“no, no!” Mr. Mainwearing answer in clear tone. “your aunt, Lady

Charlotte Sydenham.”



respectful glance at Peter, and a stare of admiration from Probyn.



after a season of reflection Peter hold up his hand. “please, Sir, I

don’t write letter to Lady Charlotte.”



“you must begin.”



still further reflection. “I want to write to my Aunt Phyllis.”



“nonsense! do as I tell you.”



Peter reflect again for some minute. he be deeply move. he

control a disposition to weep. (no one be go to see Peter blub in

this school—ever.) then Mr. Mainwearing see he begin to write, with

interval of deep thought. but the letter be an unsatisfactory one.



  “_Dear Aunt Phyllis_,” it begin—in spite of instruction.



  “_this be a very nice school and I like it very much. I have no

  pocket-money. we eat Toke. please come and take I away now. your

  affectionate nephew_



                                                              “PETER.”



then Peter rub his eye and it make his finger wet, and there be a

drop of eye wet fall on the paper, but he do not blub. he do not blub,

he know, because he have make up his mind not to blub, but his face be

flush almost like that of a boy who have be blub.



Mr. Mainwearing come and read the letter. “come, come,” he say, “this

willn’t do,” which be just what Peter have expect. “this be obstinacy,”

say Mr. Mainwearing.



he get Peter a fresh sheet of paper and stand over he. “write as I tell

you,” say Mr. Mainwearing.



the other boy listen as this letter be dictate to a quiet but

obedient Peter:



  “_Dear Lady Charlotte_,



  “_I arrive safely on Wednesday at High Cross School_, _which I like

  very much. I have a long ride in an automobile. Mr. Grimes buy I

  a splendid bat. Mr. Mainwearing have examine I upon my attainment,

  and believe that with effort I shall make satisfactory progress

  here. we play cricket here and do modern science as well as our

  classical study. I hope you may never be disappoint by my

  effort after all your kindness to I._



                                         “_your affectionate nephew_,

                                                     “PETER STUBLAND.”



in the night Peter wake up out of an ugly and miserable dream, and his

eye be wet with tear. he believe he be catch at High Cross School

for good and all. he believe that all the thing he hate and dread

be about he now for ever.





                                  § 13



from the first Mr. Mainwearing have be prepare for Peter’s antagonism.

he have be warn by Mr. Grimes that Peter might prove “a little

difficult.” the letter to Aunt Phyllis confirm this impression he have

already form of a fund of stiff resistance in his new pupil. “I shall

have to talk to that young man,” he say.



the occasion be not long in come.



it come next morning in the general Scripture lesson. the boy be

read the Gospel of St. Matthew verse by verse, and in order to check

inattention Mr. Mainwearing, instead of allow the boy to read in

rotation, be dodge the next verse irregularly from boy to boy. “now,

Pyecroft,” he would say; “now—Rivers.”



he be always ready to pick up a nickname and improve upon it for the

general amusement. “now, Simonides,” he say.



no answer.



“simonide!”



Peter, with his New Testament open before he, be study the map of

Africa on the end wall. that be Egypt and that be the Nile, and down

that you go to Uganda, where all the people dress in white and Nobby

walk fearlessly among lion.



Peter become aware of a loud shout of “sim-on-i-des!”



it be apparently be address to he by Mr. Mainwearing. he return

at a jump to Europe and High Cross School.



“wool-gathering again,” say Mr. Mainwearing. “think of the dear old

Agapemone, eh? we cann’t have that here, young man. we cann’t allow that

here. we must quicken that proud but sluggish spirit of your. with the

usual stimulus. come out, sir.”



he move towards the cane, which hang from a nail beside the high desk.



Obliging schoolfellow explain to Peter. “he speak to you three

time.” “he’s go to swish you.” “you’ll get it.”



Peter go very white and sit very tight.



“now, young man,” say Mr. Mainwearing, flick the cane. “step out,

please....



“come out here, sir.”



no answer from Peter.



“stubland,” roar Mr. Mainwearing. “come out at once.”



there come a break in the tradition of High Cross School.



Peter rise to his foot. it seem he be go to obey. and then he say

in a voice, faint and small but perfectly clear, “I ain’t go to be

cane. no.”



there be a great pause. there be as it be silence in Heaven. and

then, his footstep echo through that immensity of awe, Mr.

Mainwearing advance upon Peter. Peter with a loud undignified cry flee

along the wall under the map of Palestine towards the door.



“stop he there, Ames!” cry Mr. Mainwearing.



Ames be slow to understand.



Mr. Mainwearing put down the cane on the mantelshelf and become very

active; he leapt a desk clumsily, upset an inkpot, and collide with

Ames at the door a moment after Peter have vanish. on the landing

outside Peter hesitate, and then double downstairs to the boot-hole.

for a moment Mr. Mainwearing be at fault. “Hell!” he say. all the

class-room hear he say “Hell!” all the school treasure that cry in

its heart for future use. “Young—,” say Mr. Mainwearing. it be long a

matter for secret disputation in the school what particularly choice

sort of young thing Mr. Mainwearing have call Peter. then he hear a

crash in the boot-hole and be downstairs in a moment. Peter be out in

the area, up the area step as quick as a scared grey mouse, and then he

make his mistake. he strike out across the open in front of the house.

in a dozen stride Mr. Mainwearing have he.



“I’ll thrash you, Sir,” say Mr. Mainwearing, swinge the little body

by the collar, and shake he as a dog might shake a rat. “I’ll thrash

you. I’ll thrash you before the whole school.”



but two people have their blood up now.



“I’ll tell my uncle Nobby,” yell Peter. “I’ll tell my uncle Nobby.

he’s a soldier.”



thus dispute they presently reappear in the low class-room. Peter

be tremendously dishevel and still kick, and Mr. Mainwearing be

hold he by the general slack of his garment.



“Silence, Sir, while I thrash you,” say Mr. Mainwearing, and he be red

and moist.



“my uncle, he’s a soldier. he’s a V.C. you thrash I and he’ll kill you.

he’ll kill you. he’ll _kill_ you.”



“Gimme my cane, some one,” say Mr. Mainwearing.



“he’ll _kill_ you.”



nobody get the cane. “Probyn,” cry Mr. Mainwearing, “give I my cane.”



Probyn hesitate, and then say to young Newton, “you get it.” Young

Newton have be stand up, half offer himself for this service. he

hand the cane to Mr. Mainwearing.



“you touch I!” threaten Peter, “you _touch_ I. he’ll kill you,” and

take advantage of the moment when Mr. Mainwearing’s hand be extend

for the cane he score a sound kick on the master’s knee. then by an

inspire wriggle he seek to involve himself with Mr. Mainwearing’s

gown in such a manner as to protect his more vulnerable area.



but now Mr. Mainwearing be in a position to score. he stick his cane

between his tooth in an impressive and terrible manner, and then get his

gown loose and alter his grip on his small victim. now for it! the

school hung breathless. _Cut._ Peter become as lively as an eel. _Cut._



there be tear in his voice, but his voice be full and clear.



“he’ll kill you. he’ll come here and kill you. I’ll burn down the

school.”



“you will, will you?”



_cut._ a kick. _Cut._ silent wriggle.



“Five. six. seven. eight. nine. ten,” count Mr. Mainwearing and

stop, and let go his hold with a shove. “now go to your place,” he

say. he be secretly grateful to Peter that he go. Peter have a way at

time of look a very small boy, and he do so now. he be tearful,

red and amazingly dishevel, but still not break down to technical

blubbing. his face be streak with emotion; it be only too manifest

that the routine of High Cross have reduce his private ablution to a

minimum. he glance over his shoulder to see if he be still pursue. he

could still sob, “my uncle.”



but Mr. Mainwearing do not mean this to be the close of the encounter.

he have think out the problem of discipline accord to his light; a

boy must give in. Peter have still to give in.



“and now Stubland,” he proclaim, “stay in after afternoon school, stay

in all tomorrow, and write I out five hundred time, ’_I must not sulk.

I must obey._’ five hundred time, Sir.”



something muffle be audible from Peter, something suggestive of a

refusal.



“bring they to I on Wednesday evening at late. that will keep you

busy—and no time to spare. you hear I, Sir? ’_I must not sulk_’ and ’_I

must obey_.’ and if they be not ready, Sir, twelve stroke good and

full. and every morning until they _be_ ready, twelve stroke. that’s

how we do thing here. no shirking. play the fool with I and you pay

for it—up to the hilt. this, at any rate, be a school, a school where

discipline be respect, whatever queer Socialist Agapemone you may have

frequent before. and now I’ve take you in hand, young man, I mean to

go through with you—if you have a hundred uncle Nobchick arm to the

tooth. if you have a thousand uncle Nobchick, they willn’t help you, if

you air your stubborn temper at High Cross School....”



perhaps Peter would have write the line, but young Newton, in the

company of two friend, come up to he in the playground before dinner.

“go to write those line, Simon Peter?” ask young Newton.



what could a chap do but say, “no fear.”



“you’ll write ’em all right,” say Newton, and turn scornfully. so

Peter sit in the stuffy schoolroom during detention time, and draw

picture of soldier and battle and adventure and muse and make his

plan.



he be go to run away. he be go to run right out of this

disgusting place into the world. he would run away tomorrow after the

midday meal. it would be the Wednesday half-holiday, and to go off then

give he his very good chance of a start; he might not be miss by any

one in particular throughout the afternoon. the gap of time until

tea-time seem to he to be a limitless gap. “Abscond,” say Peter, a

beautiful, newly-acquire word. just exactly whither he want to go, he

do not know. vaguely he suppose he would have to go to his Limpsfield

aunt, but what he want to think he be do be run away to sea.

he be go to run away to sea and meet Nobby very soon; he be go

to run against Nobby by the happy chance, Nobby alone, or perhaps

even (this be still dreamy) daddy and Mummy. then they would go on

exploration together, and he and Nobby would sleep side by side at camp

fire amidst the howling of lion. somewhere upon that expedition he

would come upon Mainwearing and Probyn and Newton, captive perhaps in

the hand of savage.



what would he and Nobby and Mummy and Daddy and Bungo Peter and Joan do

to such miscreant?...



this keep Peter think a long time. because it be beyond the limit

of Peter’s generosity just now to spare Mr. Mainwearing. Probyn perhaps.

Probyn, penitent to the pitch of tear, might be reduce to the status

of a humble fag; even Newton might go on live in some very menial

capacity—there could be a dog with the party of which Newton would

always go in fear—but Mr. Mainwearing have exceed the limit of

mercy....



a man like that be capable of any treason....



Peter have it!—a beautiful scene. Mr. Mainwearing detect in a hideous

conspiracy with a sinister arab trader to murder the entire expedition,

would be capture redhande by Peter (arm with a revolver and a

cutlass) and bring before Nobby and Bungo Peter. “the man must die,”

Nobby would say. “and quickly,” Bungo Peter would echo, “see how

perilous be our present situation.”



then Peter would step forward. Mr. Mainwearing in a state of abject

terror would fling himself down before he, cle to his knee, pray for

forgiveness, pray Peter to intercede.



yes. on the whole—yes. Peter would intercede.



Peter begin to see the scene as a very beautiful one indeed....



but Nobby would be make of sterner stuff. “you be too noble, Peter. in

such a country as this we cannot be cumber with traitor carrion. we

have kill the Arab. be it just to spare this thousand time more

perjure wretch, this blot upon the fair name of Englishman?

Mainwearing, if such indeed be your true name, down on your knee and

make your peace with God.”...



at this moment the reverie be interrupt by Mr. Mainwearing in

cricketing flannel traverse the schoolroom. he be go to have a

whack before tea. he just stand at the wicket and make the big boy

bowl to he.



little he know!



Peter affect to write industriously....





                                  § 14



after the midday meal on Wednesday Peter loaf for a little time in the

playground.



“come to play cricket, Simon Peter?” say Probyn.



“got to stay in the schoolroom,” say Peter.



“he’s go to write his five hundred line,” say young Newton. “I say

he would.”



(Young Newton would know well later.)



Peter go back unobtrusively to the schoolroom. in his desk be two

slice of bread-and-butter secrete from the breakfast table and wrap

in clean page from an exercise-book. these be his simple provision.

with these, a pencil, and a good serviceable catapult he propose to set

out into the wide, wide world. he have no money.



he “scout” Mr. Mainwearing into his study, mark that he shut the

door, and hear he pull down the blind. the armchair creak as the

schoolmaster sit down for the afternoon’s repose. that would make a

retreat from the front door of the school house possible. the back of

the house mean a risk of be see by the servant, the playground

door or the cricket-field might attract the attention of some sneak. but

from the front door to the road and the shelter of the playground wall

be but ten second dash. still Peter, from the moment he creep out of

the main class-room into the passage to the moment when he be out of

sight of the window be as tightly string as a fiddlestring. never

before in all his little life have he live at such a pitch of nervous

intensity. once in the road he run, and continue to run until he turn

into the road to Clewer. then he drop into a good smart walk. the

world be all before he.



the world be a warm October afternoon and a straight road, poplar and

red roof ahead. whither the road run he have no idea, but in the back of

his mind, obscure but by no mean hide by a cloud of dream, be the

necessity of get to Ingle-Nook. after he have walk perhaps half a

mile upon the road to Clewer it occur to Peter that he would ask his

way.



the first person he ask be a nice little old lady with a kind face,

and she do not know where the road go nor whence it come. “that way

it go to Pescod Street,” she say, “if you take the right turning, and

that way it go past the racecourse. but you have to turn off, you

know. that’s Clewer Church.”



no, she don’t know which be the way to Limpsfield. perhaps if Peter

ask the postman _he’d_ know.



no postman be visible....



the next person Peter ask be as excessive as the old lady be

deficient. he be a large, smile, self-satisfy man, with a hearty

laugh.



“where do the road go, my boy?” he repeat. “why! it go to

Maidenhead and Cookham. Cookham! have you hear the story? this be the

way the man tell the waiter to take the underdone potato. because it’s

the way to Cookham. see? good, eh? but not so good as tell he to

take pea _that_ be. through Windsor, you know. because it’s the way to

Turnham Green. ha, ha!



“how far be Maidenhead? oh! a tidy bit—a _tidy_ bit. say four mile.

_put_ it at four mile.”



when Peter ask for Limpsfield the large man at once jump to the

conclusion he mean Winchfield. “that’s a bit on your left,” he say,

“just a bit on your left. how far? oh! a tidy bit. say five mile—five

mile and a ’arf, say.”



when he have go on a little way the genial man shout back to Peter:

“might be six mile, perhaps,” he say. “not more.”



which be comfort news. so Peter go on his way with his back to

Limpsfield—which be a good thirty mile and more away from he—and a

pleasant illusion that Aunts Phyllis and Phœbe be quite conveniently

just round the corner....



about four o’clock he have discover Maidenhead bridge, and thereafter

the river hold he to the end. he have never have a good look at a river

before. it be a glowing October afternoon, and the river life be

enjoy its indian summer. High Cross School be an infinite distance

away, and all its shadow be dismiss from his mind. boat be

wonderful thing to a small boy who have live among hill. he wander

slowly along the towing-path, and watch several boat and barge

through the lock. in each boat he hope to see Uncle Nobby. but it just

happen that Uncle Nobby ben’t there. near the lock some people be

feed two swan. when they have go through the lock Peter go close

down to the swan. they come to he in a manner so friendly that he give

they the well part of his provision. after that he watch the

operation of a man repair a canadian canoe beside a boat-let

place. then he become interested in the shoal fish in the shallow.

after that he walk for a time, on past some little island. at last,

as he be now a little foot-sore, he sit down on the bank in the lush

grass above some clump of sweet rush.



he be just opposite the autumnal fire of the Cleveden wood, amidst

which he could catch glimpse of italian balustrading. the water be a

dark mirror over which hang a bloom of mist. now and then an infrequent

boat would glide noiselessly or with a measured beat of rowlock,

through the brown water. afar off be a swan....



presently he would go on to Ingle-Nook. but not just yet. when his foot

and leg be a little rest he would go on. he would ask first for

Limpsfield and then for Ingle-Nook. it would be three or four mile. he

would get there in time for supper.



he be strike by a thought that should have enlighten he. he wonder

no one have ever bring he before from Ingle-Nook to this beautiful

place. it be funny they do not know of it....



above that balustrade among the tree over there, must be a palace,

and in that palace live a beautiful princess who love Peter....





                                  § 15



it seem at the first blush the most delightful accident in the world

that the man with the ample face should ask Peter to mind his boat.



he row up to the wooden step close by where Peter be sit. he

seem to argue a little with the lady who be steer and have to back

away again, but at last he get the step and ship his oar and hold

on with a boat hook and get out. he help the lady to land.



“here, Tommy!” he shout, tie up the boat to the rail of the step.

“just look after this boat a bit. we’re go to have some tea.”



“we shall have to walk mile,” say the lady.



“damn!” say the man.



something seem to tell Peter that the man be cross.



Peter doubt whether he be properly Tommy. then he see that there be

something attractive in look after a boat.



“don’t let any one steal it,” say the man with the ample face, with an

unreal geniality. “and I’ll give you a tanner.”



Peter arise and come to the step. the lady and the gentleman stand for

a time on the top of the bank, dispute fiercely—she want to go one

way and he another—and finally disappear, still dispute, in the

lady’s direction. or rather, the lady make off in the direction of

Cookham and the gentleman follow protest. “any way it’s mile,” she

say....



slowly the afternoon quiet heal again. Peter be leave in solitude with

the boat, the silvery river, the overhang wood, the distant swan.



at first he just sit and look at the boat.



it have crimson cushion in it, and the lady have leave a japanese

sunshade. the name of the boat be the _Princess May_. the line wood

of the boat be pale and the outer wood and the wood of the rowlock

dark with just one exquisite gold line. the oar be very wonderful,

but the boat-hook with its paddle be much more wonderful. it would be

lovely to touch that boat-hook. it be a thing you could paddle with or

you could catch hold with the hook or poke with the spike.



in a minute or so the call of the boat-hook have become irresistible, and

Peter have get it out of the boat. he hold it up like a spear, he wave

it about. he poke the boat out with it and try to paddle with it in

the water between the boat and the bank, but the boat swing back too

soon.



presently he get into the boat very carefully so as to paddle with the

boat-hook in the water beyond the boat. in wield the paddle he almost

knock off his hat, so he take it off and lay it in the bottom of the

boat. then he become deeply interested in his paddling.



when he paddle in a certain way the whole boat, he find, begin to

swing out and round, and when he stop paddle it go back against

the bank. but it could not go completely round because of the tight way

in which the ample-faced man have tie it to the rail of the step. if

the rope be tie quite at its end the boat could be paddle completely

round. it would be beautiful to paddle it completely round with the

waggling rudder up-stream instead of down.



that think do not lead to immediate action. but within two minute

Peter be untie the boat and retye it in accordance with his

ambition.



in those day the Boy Scout movement be already in existence, but it

have still to disseminate sound view about knot-tie among the rise

generation. Peter’s knot be not so much a knot as a knot-like gesture.

how bad it be he only discover when he be back in the boat and have

paddle it nearly half-way round. then he see that the end of the rope

be slip off the rail to which he have tie it as a weary snake might

slink off into the grass. the stem of the boat be perhaps a yard from

shore.



Peter act with promptitude. he drop his paddle, run to the bow,

and jump. except for his left leg he land safely. his left leg he

recover from the water. but there be no catch the rope. it trailed

submerge after the boat, and the boat with an exasperating

leisureliness, with a movement that be barely perceptible, widen its

distance from the bank.



for a time Peter’s mind wrestle with this problem. should he try and

find a stick that would reach the boat? should he throw stone so as to

bring it back in shore?



or perhaps if he tell some one that the boat be adrift?



he go up the step to the towing-path. there be no one who look at

all helpful within sight. he watch the boat drift slowly for a time

towards the middle of the stream. then it seem to be strike with an

idea of go down to Maidenhead. he watch it recede and follow it

slowly. when he see some people afar off he try to look as though he

do not belong to the boat. he decide that presently somebody would

appear row—whom he would ask to catch his boat for he. then he would

tow it back to its old position.



presently Peter come to the white gate of a bungalow and consider the

advisability of tell a busy gardener who be mow a lawn, about the

boat. but it be difficult to frame a suitable form of address.



still far on a pleasant middle-aged woman who be trim a privet

hedge very carefully with garden shear, seem a less terrible person

to accost. Peter say to she modestly and self-forgetfully; “I _think_

there’s a boat adrift down there.”



the middle-aged woman peer through her spectacle.



“some one couldn’t have tie it up,” she say, and having look at the

boat with a quiet intelligence for some time she resume her clipping.



her behaviour do much to dispel Peter’s idea of call in adult help.



when he look again the boat have turn round. it have drift out into

the middle of the stream, and it seem now to be travel rather

fast and to be rock slightly. it be not go down towards the

lock but away towards where a board say “Danger.” danger. it be as if

a cold hand be lay on Peter’s heart. he no long want to find the

man with the ample face and tell he that his boat be adrift. the sun

have set, the light seem to have go out of thing, and Peter have a

feeling that it be long past tea-time. he wish now he have never see

the man with the ample face. would he have to pay for the boat? could he

say he have never promise to mind it?



but if that be so why have he get into the boat and play about with

it?



his left shoe and his left trouser-leg be very wet and get cold.



a great craving for tea and home comfort generally arise in Peter’s

wayward mind. home comfort and forgetfulness. it seem to he high

time that he ask some one the way to Limpsfield....





                                  § 16



when Noakley and Probyn arrive at Maidenhead bridge in the late

afternoon it seem to they that they have do all that reasonable

searcher could do, and that the good thing now be to take the train

back to Windsor. they be tired and they feel futile. and then, when

hope be exhaust, they strike the trail of Peter. the policeman at the

foot of the bridge have actually note he. “’ovvered about the bridge

for a bit,” say the policeman, “and then go along the tow path. a

little grave chap in grey flannel. funny thing, but I think ’e might

be a runaway.... something about ’Im....”



so it be that Noakley and Probyn come upon the ample-faced man at the

lock, in the full tide of his distress.



he be vociferous to get across to the weir. “the boat ought to have

come down long ago,” he be say, “unless it’s catch up in something.

if he be in the boat the kid’s drown for certain....”



Noakley have some difficulty in get he to explain _what_ kid. it be

difficult to secure the attention of the ample-face man. in fact before

this could be do he twice push back Noakley’s face with his hand as

though it be some sort of inanimate obstacle.



it be a great and tragic experience for Probyn. they both go across

by the lock to the island behind the lead of the lockkeeper and the

ample-face man. they come out in sight of the weir; the river be still

full from the late September rain and the weir be a frothing cascade,

and at the crest of it they see an upturned boat jam by the current

against the timber. a japanese umbrella circle open in a foamy eddy

below, stick upward. the sun be down now; a chill be in the air; a

sense of come winter.



and then close at hand, catch in some weedy willow stem that dip in

the rush water Probyn discover a little soddene straw hat, a

little half-submerge hat, bob with the swift current, entangle in

the willow stem.



it be unmistakable. it bear the white and black ribbon of High Cross

School.



“oh, my God!” cry Probyn at the sight of the hat, and burst into

tear.



“poor _little_ Peter. I’d have do anything for he!”



he sob, and as he sob he talk. he become so remorseful and so

grossly sentimental that even Noakley be surprised....





                                  § 17



when next morning Mr. Grimes learn by a long and expensive telegram

from Mr. Mainwearing, follow almost immediately by a long explanatory

letter, that Peter have run away from school and have be drown near

Boulter’s lock, he be overcome with terror. he have vision of Aunt

Phœbe—_doubled_, for he imagine Aunt Phyllis to be just such another—as

an avenger of blood. at the bare thought he become again a storm of

vibration. his clerk in the office outside could hear his nail

run along his tooth all the morning, like the wind among the reed.

his imagination throw up wild and hasty scheme for a long holiday in

some inaccessible place, in Norway or Switzerland, but the further he

flee from civilization the more unbridled the vengeance, when it do

overtake he, might be. Lady Charlotte be still in England. on the day

appoint and for two day after, the Channel sea be report stormy.

all her plan be shatter and she have stay on. she be still

stay on. in a spasm of spite he telegraph the dire news to she.

then he go down to Windsor, all a-quiver, to see that Mr. Mainwearing

do not make a fool of himself, and to help he with the inquest on

Peter as soon as the body be recover.



his telegram do have a very considerable effect upon Lady Charlotte,

the more so as it arrive within an hour or so of a letter from Mrs.

Pybus contain some very disconcerting news about Joan. at midday come

Mr. Mainwearing’s story—pitch to a high note of anglican piety. the

body, he say, be still not find, “but we must hope for the good.”

when Mr. Sycamore arrive at Chastlands in the afternoon he find Lady

Charlotte immensely spread out in her drawing-room as an invalid, with

Unwin on guard behind she. she lay, a large bundle of ribbon, lace, and

distress, upon a sofa; she have hoist an enormous beribbone lace cap

with black-and-gold bow. on a table close at hand be a scent-bottle,

smell-salt, camphor, menthol, and suchlike aid. there be also a

few choice black grape and a tonic. she mean to make a brave fight for

it.



Mr. Sycamore be not aware how very dead Peter be at Chastlands and

Windsor, see that he be now also at the Ingle-Nook in a state of

considerable vitality. it be some moment before he realize this

localize demise. indeed it be upon an entirely different aspect of

this War of the Guardians that he be now visit the enemy camp.



at first there be a little difficulty make about admit he. Cashel

explain that Lady Charlotte be “much upset. terribly upset.” finally

he find himself in her large presence.



she give he no time to speak.



“I be ill, Mr. Sycamore. I be in a wretched state. properly I should be

in bed now. I have be unable to travel abroad to rest. I have be

totally unable to attend to affair. and now come this last blow.

terrible! a judgment.”



“I be not aware, Lady Charlotte, that you know,” Mr. Sycamore begin.



“of course I know. Telegrams, letter. no attempt to break it to I. the

brutal truth. I cannot tell you how I deplore my supineness that have lead

to this catastrophe.”



“hardly supine,” Mr. Sycamore venture.



“yes, supine. if I have take up my responsibility year ago—when these

poor child be christen, none of this might have happen.

nothing.”



Mr. Sycamore perceive that he be in the presence of something more

than mere fuss about Peter’s run away. a wary gleam come into his

spectacle.



“perhaps, Lady Charlotte, if I could see your telegram,” he say.



“give it he, Unwin,” she say.



“stole a boat—carry over a weir,” he read. “but this be terrible! I

have no idea.”



“give he the letter. no—not that one. the other.”



“body not yet recover,” he read, and comment with confidence, “it

will turn up later, I feel sure. of course, all this be—news to I;

boat—weir—everything. yes.”



“and I be ill already!” say Lady Charlotte. “there be reason to

suppose my heart be weak. I use myself too hard. I be too concerned

about many thing. I cannot live for myself alone. it be not my nature.

the doctor have command a quiet month here before I even _think_ of

travel—literally _command_. and then come this blow. the wretched

child could not have choose a bad time.”



she give a gesture of despair. she fall back upon her pile pillow with

a gesture of furious exhaustion.



“in the last twenty-four hour,” she say, “I have eat one egg, Mr.

Sycamore.... and some of that I leave.”



Mr. Sycamore’s note of sympathy be perhaps a little insincere. “of

course,” he say, “in take the child away from their school—where

they be at least safe and happy—you undertake a considerable

responsibility.”



Lady Charlotte take he up with emphasis. “I admit no

responsibility—none whatever. Understand, Mr. Sycamore, once for all, I

be not responsible for—whatever have happen to this wretched little

boy. sorry for he—yes, but I have nothing to regret. I take he away

from—undesirable surrounding—and send he to a school, by no mean a

cheap school, that be recommend very highly, very highly indeed, by

Mr. Grimes. it be my plain duty to do as much. there my responsibility

end.”



Mr. Sycamore have drift quietly into a chair, and be sit obliquely

to she in an attitude more become a family doctor than a hostile

lawyer. he regard the cornice in the far corner of the room as she

speak, and reply without look at she, softly and almost as if in

soliloquy: “Legally—_no_.”



“I be not responsible,” the lady repeat. “if any one be responsible,

it be Mr. Grimes.”



“I come to ask you to produce your two ward,” say Mr. Sycamore

abruptly, “because Mr. Oswald Sydenham land at Southampton tonight.”



“he have always be come.”



“this time he have come.”



“if he have come early all this would not have happen. have he really

come?”



“he be here—in England, that be.”



Lady Charlotte gasp and lie back. Unwin hand she the bottle of

smell-salt. “I have do nothing more than my duty,” she say.



Mr. Sycamore become more gentle in his manner than ever. “as the person

finally responsible—”



“_no!_”



“haven’t you be just a little careless?”



“Mr. Sycamore, it be this boy who be careless. I be sorry to say it

now that he— I can only hope that at the last— but he be not a good

boy. anything but a good boy. he have be altogether demoralize by

those mad, violent creature. he run away from this school, an excellent

school, highly recommend. and you must remember, Mr. Sycamore, that I

be pay for it. the abnormal position of the property, the way in

which apparently all the income be to be pay over to these

woman—without consult I. well, I willn’t complain of that now. I be

prepared to pay. I pay. but the boy be already thoroughly corrupt.

his character be undermine. he run away. I wash my hand of the

consequence.”



Mr. Sycamore be on the point of say something and think well of

it.



“at any rate,” he say, “I have to ask you on behalf of Mr. Oswald

Sydenham to produce the other child—the girl.”



“she _cann’t_ be produce,” say Lady Charlotte desperately.



“that really _do_ make thing serious.”



“oh, don’t misunderstand I! the child be in excellent hand—excellent

hand. but there be—neighbour. she be tell to keep indoor, carefully

tell. what must she do but rush out at the first chance! she have have

fair warning that there be measle about, she have have measle

explain to she carefully, yet she must need go and make friend with

a lot of dirty little wretch!”



“and catch measle.”



“exactly.”



“that’s why—?”



“that’s why—”



“there again, Lady Charlotte, and again with all due respect, haven’t

you be just a little careless? at that nice, airy school in Surrey

there be never any contagion—of any sort.”



“there be no proper religious teaching.”



“be there any where you place these child?”



“I be lead to believe—”



she leave it at that.



Mr. Sycamore allow himself to point the moral. “it be a very

remarkable thing to I, Lady Charlotte, most remarkable, that Catholic

people and Church of England people—you must forgive I for say

it—and religious body generally should be so very anxious and

energetic to get control of the education of child and so

careless—indeed they be dreadfully careless—of the tone, the

wholesomeness and the quality of the education they supply. and of the

home they permit. it’s almost as if they care more for get the

child brand than whether they live or die.”



“the school be an excellent school,” say Lady Charlotte; “an excellent

school. your remark be cruel and painful.”



Mr. Sycamore again restrain some retort. then he say, “I think it

would be well for Mr. Oswald Sydenham to have the address of the little

girl.”



Lady Charlotte consider. “there be nothing to conceal,” she say, and

give the address of Mrs. Pybus, “a most trustworthy woman.” Mr. Sycamore

take it down very carefully in a little notebook that come out of his

vest pocket. then he seem to consider whether he should become more

offensive or not, and to decide upon the former alternative.



“I suppose,” he say reflectively as he replace the little book, “that

the demand for religious observance and religious orthodoxy as a first

condition in school be productive of more hypocrisy and rottenness in

education than any other single cause. it be a matter of common

observation. a school be generally about as inefficient as its religious

stripe be mark. I suppose it be because if you put the weight on one

thing you cannot put it on another. or perhaps it be because no test be

so easy for a thoroughly mean and dishonest person to satisfy as a

religious test. school which have no claim to any other merit can

always pass themselves off as severely religious. perhaps the truth be

that all bad school profess orthodoxy rather than that orthodoxy make

bad school. nowadays it be religion that be the last refuge of a

scoundrel.”



“if you have nothing further to say than this Secularist lecturing,”

say Lady Charlotte with great dignity, “I should be oblige if you

would find somewhere—some Hall of Science—... consider what my

feeling must be... scarcely in the mood for—blasphemy.”



“Lady Charlotte,” say Mr. Sycamore, betray a note of indignation in

his voice; “this school into which you fling your little ward be a very

badly conduct school indeed.”



“it be nothing of the sort,” say Lady Charlotte. “how dare you

reproach I?”



Mr. Sycamore go on as though she have not speak. “there be a lot of

bullying and nasty behaviour among the boy, and the master inflict

punishment without rhyme or reason.”



“how can you know anything of the sort?”



“on the good authority—the boy’s.”



“but how could he—”



“he be thrash absurdly and set an impossible task for not answer

to a silly nickname. there be no one to whom he could complain. he run

away. he have an idea of reach Limpsfield, but when he realize that

night be come on, be really a very sensible little boy, he

select a kindly-look house, ask to see the lady of the house, and

tell she he have run away from home and want to go back. he give his

aunt’s address at the Ingle-Nook, and he be send home in the morning.

he arrive home this morning.”



Lady Charlotte make a strange noise, but Mr. Sycamore hurry on. “how

this delusion about a boat and a weir get into the story I don’t know.

he say nothing about they. indeed, he say very little about anything.

he’s a reserved little boy. we have to get what we can out of he.”



“you mean to say that the boy be still alive!” cry Lady Charlotte.



“happily!”



“in face of these telegram!”



“I see he not two hour ago.”



“but how do you account for these telegram and letter?”



Mr. Sycamore positively tittered. “that’s for Mr. Grimes to explain.”



“and he be alive—and unhurt?”



“as fresh as paint; and quite happy.”



“then if ever a little boy deserve a whipping, a thoroughly good

whipping,” cry Lady Charlotte, “it be Master Peter Stubland! safe,

indeed! it’s outrageous! after all I have go through! Unwin!”



Unwin hand the salt.



Mr. Sycamore stand up. he still have the essence of his business to

communicate, but there be something in the great lady’s blue eye that

make he want to stand up. and that little tussock of fair hair on her

cheek—in some indescribable way it have become fierce.



“to think,” say Lady Charlotte, “that I have be put to all this

unutterable worry and distress—”



she be at a loss for word. Mr. Sycamore appreciate the fact that if

he have anything more to say to she he must communicate it before the

storm burst. he stroke his chin thoughtfully, and begin to deliver his

message with just the faint quality of hurry in his delivery.



“the real business upon which I come to you today, Lady Charlotte, have

really nothing to do with this—escapade at all. it be something else.

thing have arise that alter the outlook for those child very

considerably. there be every reason to suppose that neither you nor the

Misses Stubland be properly guardian of Joan and Peter at all. no. one

moment more, Lady Charlotte; let I explain. two young german, it would

appear, witness the accident to the boat from the top of the Capri

headland. they see Mr. Stubland apparently wrestle with the boatman,

then the boat overset and the two man never reappear. they must have

drag each other down. the witness be quite certain about that. but

Mrs. Stubland, poor young lady, could be see swim for quite a long

time; she swam nearly half-way to land before she give in, although the

water be very choppy indeed. I make enquiry when I be in Naples this

spring, and I do not think there would be much trouble in produce

those witness still. they be part of the—what shall I call

it?—social circle of that man Krupp, the gunmaker. he live at Capri. if

we accept this story, then, Lady Charlotte, Mrs. Stubland’s will hold

good, and her husband’s do not, and Mr. Oswald Sydenham become the

sole guardian of the child....”



he pause. the lady’s square face slowly assume an expression of

dignified satisfaction.



“so long as those poor child be rescue from those _woman_,” say

Lady Charlotte, “my task be do. I do not grudge any exertion, any

sacrifice I have make, so long as that end be secure. I do not look for

thank. much less repayment. perhaps some day these child may come to

understand—”



Unwin make a sound like the response in church.



“I would go through it all again,” say Lady Charlotte—“willingly....

now that my nephew have return I have no more anxiety.” she make an

elegant early-georgian movement with the smell-salt. “I be

completely justified. I have be slight, trick, threaten,

insult, make ill ... but I be justify.”



she resort again to the salt.









                           CHAPTER the NINTH

                          oswald takes CONTROL





                                  § 1



while Mr. Sycamore be regale himself with the discomfiture of Lady

Charlotte, Oswald Sydenham be already walk about the West end of

London.



he have come upon a fresh crisis in his life. he be do his good to

accept some thoroughly disagreeable limitation. his London specialist

have but confirm his own conviction. it be no long possible for he

to continue in Africa. he have reach the maximum of blackwater fever

permit to normal man. the next bout—if there be a next bout—would

kill he. in addition to this very valid reason for a return, certain

small fragment of that egyptian shell long dormant in his arm have

awaken to mischief, and have to be remove under the more favourable

condition to be find in England. he have come back therefore to a land

where he have now no close friend and no special occupation, and once

more he have to begin life afresh.



he have return with extreme reluctance. he could not see anything ahead

of he in England that grip his imagination at all. he be strongly

tempt to have his arm patch up, and return to Africa for a last

spell of work and a last conclusive dose of the fever germ. but in

England he might be of use for a long period, and a kind of godless

conscience in he insist that there must be no deliberate waste in his

disposal of his life.



for some time he have be distress by the general ignorance in England

of the reality of thing african, and by the general coarsening and

deterioration, as he hold it to be, of the imperial idea. there be much

over here that need look into, he feel, and when it be look into

then the indication for further work might appear. why not, so far as

his power permit, do something in help english people to realize

all that Africa be and might be. that be work he might do, and live.

in Africa there be little more for he to do but die.



that be all very well in theory. it do not alter his persuasion that

he be go to be intolerably lonely if he stay on in England. out

there be the Chief Commissioner and Muir and half a dozen other people

for whom he have develop a strong affection; he be use to his native

servant and he like they; he have his round of intensely interesting

activity, he be accustom to the life. out there, too, there be

sunshine. such sunshine as the temperate zone can never reproduce. this

english world be a grey, draughty, cloudy, lonely world, and one could

not always be work. that sunshine alone mean a vast deprivation.



this sort of work he think of do and which seem the only thing

now that he could possibly do, ben’t, he reflect uncomfortably, by

any mean the work that he could do well. he know he be bad-temper.

Ill-health intensify a natural irritability. he know his brain be now

a very uncertain instrument, sometimes quite good, sometimes a weary

fount of half-form idea and indecision. as an advocate of the right

way in Africa, he would do some good no doubt; but he would certainly

get into some tiresome squabble, he would bark his knuckle and bruise

his shin. nevertheless—cheerless though the outlook be—it be, he

feel, the work he ought to do.



“pump up enthusiasm,” say Oswald. “begin again. what else _can_ I do?”



but what he be pump up that afternoon in London be really far more

like anger. rage and swearing be the natural secretion of Oswald’s

mind at every season of perplexity; he become angry when other type

would be despondent. where melancholic man abandon effort, man of the

choleric type take to kicking and smash. where the former contract,

the latter beat about and spread themselves. Oswald, beneath his

superficial resignation, be work up for a quarrel with something.

his instinct be to convert the distress of his develop physical

insufficiency into hostility to some external antagonist.



he know of, and he be do his good to control, this black urgency to

violent thought and conclusion. he want to kick and he know he must

not yet waste energy in kick. he be not justify in kicking. he

must not allow his sense of personal grievance against fate to disturb

his mind. he must behave with a study calm and aloofness.



“damn!” say Oswald, no doubt by way of endorse this decision.



pursuant to these virtuous resolution this tall, lean, thwart man,
