full of jealous solicitude for the empire he have help enlarge, this

disfigured man whose face be in two half like those partially treat

portrait one see outside the shop of picture-cleaner, be engage in

comport himself as much as possible like some pleasant, leisurely man

of the world with no obligation or concern but to make himself

comfortable and find amusement in thing about he. he be do his

good to feel that there be no hurry about anything, and no reason

whatever for get into a state of mind. just a calm quiet onlooker he

have to be. he be, he tell himself, take a look round London as a

preliminary to settle down there. perhaps he be go to settle down

in London. or perhaps in the country somewhere. it do not matter

which—whichever be the most pleasant. it be all very pleasant. very

pleasant indeed. a life now of wise lounging and judicious, temperate

activity it have to be. he must not fuss.



he have arrive in England the day before, but as yet, except for a brief

note to Mr. Sycamore, he have notify no one of his return. he have put

up at the Climax Club in Piccadilly, a proprietary club that be half

hotel, where one could get a sitting-room as well as a bedroom; and

after a visit to his doctor—a visit that confirm all his bad

apprehension of the need of abandon Africa for ever—he have spend the

evening in the club try to be calm over the newspaper and magazine.

but when one be ill and tired as Oswald be, all that one read in the

newspaper and magazine be wrong and exasperating.



it be 1903; the time when Mr. Joseph Chamberlain return from South

Africa to launch his Tariff Reform agitation—and Oswald be

temperamentally a Free Trader. the whole press, daily, weekly, monthly,

be full of the noise of the controversy. it impress he as a

controversy almost intolerably mean. his Imperialism be essentially a

romantic and generous imagination, a dream of service, of himself,

serve the Empire and of the Empire serve mankind. the tacit

assumption underlie this most sordid of political campaign that the

Empire be really nothing of the kind, that it be an adventure of

exploitation, a national enterprise in the high piracy, borrow a

fade picturesqueness from the scoundrelism of the Elizabethan and

jacobean buccaneer, the man who start the british slave trade and the

Ulster trouble and found no Empire at all except the plantation of

Virginia and Barbados, distressed and perplex his mind almost

unendurably. it be so maddeningly plausible. it be so manifestly the

pathway of destruction.



after throw _the National Review_ into a distant armchair and then,

when he meet the startled eye of a fellow member, try to look as

though that be his usual way with a magazine, he seek distraction in

Southey’s “Doctor,” which happen to be in the club library. after

dinner he go out for a stroll in the West End, and visit the

Alhambra. he find that more soothing than the paper. the old

excitement of the human moth at the candle of vice he no long feel.

he wonder why these flitting allurement have ever stir he. but he

like the stir and the light and the pleasant inconsecutive imbecility

of the entertainment.



he sleep fairly well. in the morning a clerk of Mr. Sycamore’s

telephone to say that that gentleman be out of town, he have be

call down to see Lady Charlotte Sydenham, but that he would be back,

and would probably try to “get” Oswald about eleven in the evening. he

have something important to tell Oswald. the day begin cloudy, and

repent and become fine. by midday it be, for London, a golden day.

yet to Oswald it seem but a weak solution of sunshine. if you stood

bareheade in such sunshine you would catch a chill. but he make the

good of it. “October mild and boon,” he quote. he assure himself that

it would be entertaining to stroll about the West end and look at the

shop and mark the change in thing. he breakfast late at one of the

window overlook the Green Park, visit the club barber, walk

along to his tailor, buy three new hat and a stout gold-band cane

with an agate top in Bond Street, a pair of boot, glove and other

sundrie. then he go into his second club, the Plantain, in Pall Mall,

to read the paper—until he discover that he be begin to worry

about tariff Reform again. he see no one he know, and lunch alone. in

the afternoon he stroll out into London once more.



he be, he find, no long uncomfortable and self-conscious in the

street of London. his one-side, blank-sided face do not make he

self-conscious now as it use to do, he have reconcile himself to his

disfigurement. if at first he have exaggerate its effect, he now

incline to forget it altogether. he wear hat nowadays with a good

broad brim, and cock they to overshadow the missing eye; his dark

moustache have grow and be thick and symmetrical; he have acquire the

habit of look at himself in glass so as to minimize his deface

half. it seem to he a natural thing now that the casual passer-by

should pull up for the fraction of a second at the sight of his tall

figure, or look back at he as if to verify a first impression. don’t

people do that to everybody?



he go along Pall Mall, whose high gentility be still in those day

untrouble by the Royal Automobile Club and scarcely ruffle by a

discreet shop or so; he turn up through St. James’s Street to

Piccadilly with a reminiscent glance by the way down Jermyn Street,

where he have have his first experience of restaurant and suchlike

dissipation in his early midshipman day. how far away those folly

seem now! the shop of Bond Street draw he northward; the Doré

Gallery of his childhood, he note, be still go on; he prowl along

Oxford Street as far as the Marble Arch—Gillows be still Gillows in

those day, and Selfridge have yet to dawn on the London world—and beat

back by way of Seymour Street to Regent Street. he nod to Verrey’s,

where long ago he have lunch in a short plaid frock and white sock

under the auspex of his godmother, old Lady Percival Pelham. it be

all very much as he have leave it in ’97. that fever of rebuild and

rearrangement which be already wreck the old Strand and sweep

away Booksellers’ row and the Drury Lane slum and a score of ancient

landmark, have not yet reach the West End. there be the same

abundance of smart hansom cab crawl in the street or neatly rank

on the stand; the same populous horse omnibus, the same brightly

dress people, and, in Regent Street and Piccadilly, the same

too-brightly-dress woman loiterer, only now most of they be visibly

coarse and paint; there be the same mendicant and sandwich-man at

the pavement edge. perhaps there be more omnibus crowd upon one

another at Piccadilly and Oxford Circuses, and more people everywhere.

or perhaps that be only the effect of return from a less crowded

world.



now and then he see automobile, queer, clumsy carriage without horse

they seem to be, or else low, heavy-look vehicle with a flavour of

battleship about they. several emitted bluish smoke and trail an evil

smell. in Regent Street outside Liberty’s art shop one of these

mechanical novelty be in trouble. everybody seem pleased. the

pass cabman be openly derisive. Oswald join the little group of

people at the pavement edge who be watch the heated and bother

driver engage in some obscure struggle beneath his car.



an old gentleman in a white waistcoat stand beside Oswald, and presently

turn to he.



“silly thing,” he say. “noisy, dangerous, _stink_ thing. they

ought to be forbid.”



“perhaps they will improve,” say Oswald.



“how could _that_ thing improve?” ask the old gentleman. “lotto dirty

ironmongery.”



he turn away with the air of a man for whom a question have be

settle. Oswald follow he thoughtfully....



he resume his identification. Piccadilly Circus! here be the good old

Café Monico; yonder the Criterion....



but everything seem small.



that be the thing that strike he most forcibly; London revisit he

discover to be an intense _little_ place.



it be extraordinary that this should be the head of the empire. it

seem, when one come back to it, so entirely indifferent to the empire,

so entirely self-absorb. when one be out beyond there, in Uganda,

East Africa, Sudan, Egypt, in all those vast region where the British

be do the good work they have ever do in pacification and

civilization, one thought of London as if it be a great head that

watch one from afar, that could hear a cry for help, that could send

support. yet here be these people in these narrow, brightly serve

street, very busy about their own affair, almost as busy and

self-absorb as the white-robe crowd in the big market-place in Mengo,

and conspicuously, remarkably not think of Africa—or anything of the

sort. he compare Bond Street and its crowd, inconvenient side-walk

with one of the great garden vista of the Uganda capital, much to the

advantage of the latter. he descend by the Duke of York’s step, past

the old milk stall with its cow, into the Mall. Buckingham Palace, far

away, be much less impressive than the fort at Kampala on its

commanding hill; the vegetation of St. James’s Park and its iron fencing

be a poor substitute for the rich-pattern reed palisade and the

wealth of frond that border the wide prospect of the Uganda capital.

all English tree look stunt to Oswald’s eye.



towards the palace, tree-felling be in progress, the felling of tree

that could never be replace; and an ugly hoarding veil the erection

of King Edward’s pious memorial to Queen Victoria, the memorial which

later her grandson, the Kaiser, be to unveil.



he go on into Whitehall—there be no Admiralty Arch in those day, and

one come out of the Mall by way of Spring Gardens round the corner of an

obtrusive bank. Oswald pause for a minute to survey the squat building

and high column of Trafalgar Square, pale amber in the October sunshine,

and then stroll down towards Westminster. he become more and more

consciously the loiter home-comer. he smile at the mount soldier

in their box outside the Horse Guards, pause at and approve of the

architectural intention of the new War Office, and nod to his old

friend, the Admiralty and the Colonial Office. here they brew the

destiny of the Old World outside Europe and keep the Seven Seas. he

play his part with increase self-approval. he make his way to

Westminster Bridge and spend some time survey the down river

prospect. it be, after all, a little ditch of a river. St. Paul’s be

fairly visible, and the red, rusty shed of Charing Cross station and its

brutal iron bridge, fit monument of the clumsy looting by “private

enterprise” that characterize the victorian age, have never look

uglier.



he cross from one side of the bridge to the other, leant over the

parapet and regard the Houses of Parliament. the flag be fly, and

a number of little group of silk-hat man and gaily dress lady

be have tea on the terrace.



“I wonder why we rule our Empire from a sham Gothic building,” think

Oswald. “if anything, it ought to be roman....”



he turn his attention to the traffic and the passer-by. “they don’t

realize,” he say. “suppose suddenly they be to have a mirage here of

some of the land and city this old Parliament House control?”



a little stout man drive a pony-trap catch his attention. it be a

smart new pony-trap, and there be a look of new clothe about its

driver; he smoke a cigar that stick upward from the corner of his

mouth, and in his button-hole be a red chrysanthemum; his whole bearing

suggest absolute contentment with himself and acquiescence in the

universe; he handle his rein and draw his whip across the flank of

his shine cob as delicately as if he be fly-fishing. “what do he

think he be up to?” ask Oswald. a thousand time he have see that

Sphinx of perfect self-contentment on pass negro face.



“the Empire don’t worry _he_,” say Oswald.





                                  § 2



it be worry Oswald a lot. everything be worry Oswald just then.

it be a subtle question to answer of such case whether the physical

depression shape the despondent thought, or whether the gnawing doubt

prepare the nervous illness. his confidence in his work and the system

to which he belonged have vanish by imperceptible degree.



for some year he have go about his work with very few doubt. he have

be too busy. but now ill-health have conspire with external

circumstance to expose he to questioning about thing he have never

question before. they be very fundamental doubt. they cut at the

root of his life. he be begin to doubt whether the Empire be

indeed as good a thing and as great a thing as he have assume it to

be.... the Empire to which his life have be give.



this do not make he any less an Imperialist than he have be, but it

sharpen his imperialism with a sense of urgency that cut into his

mind.



Altogether Oswald have now give nearly eighteen year to East and

Central Africa. his illness have call a halt in a very busy life. for

two year and more after his last visit to England, he have be occupy

chiefly in operation in and beyond the Lango country against Kabarega

and the remnant of the rebel Sudanese. he have assist in the

rounding-up of King Mwanga, the rebel king of Uganda, and in set up

the child king and the regency that replace he. at the end of 1899 his

former chief, Sir Harry Johnston, have come up from British Central

Africa as Special Commissioner to Uganda, and the work of land

settlement, of provincial organization, of railway and postal

development have go on apace. next year indeed war have come again, but

it be the last war in this part of the world for some time. it be

cause by the obstinate disposition of the Nandi people to steal the

copper wire from the telegraph pole that have be set up in their

country. Hitherto their chief use for copper wire have be to make

bracelet and anklet for their married woman. they be shock by this

endless stretching out of attenuate feminine adornment. they do their

good to restore it to what they consider be its proper use. it be a

homely misunderstand rather than a war. Oswald have lead that

expedition to a successful explanation. thereafter the lead fact in

the history of Uganda until the sleep sickness come have be the

construction of the railway from the coast to Lake Victoria Nyanza.



in Uganda as in Nyasaland Oswald Sydenham have find himself part of a

rapid and busy process of tidy up the world. for some year it have

carry he along and determine all his view.



the tidying-up of Africa during the close year of the nineteenth

century be indeed one of the most rapid and effective tidying up in

history. in the late ’eighty the whole of Africa from the frontier of

low Egypt down to Rhodesia have be a world of chaotic adventure and

misery; a black world of insecure barbarism invade by the rifle, and

the arab and european adventurer who bring it. there have be no such

thing as a school from Nubia to Rhodesia, and everywhere there have be

constant aimless bloodshed. long age of conflict, arbitrary cruelty and

instinctive fierceness seem to have reach a culmination of

destructive disorder. the increase light that fall on Africa do but

illuminate a scene of collapse. the new force that be come into the

country appear at first as hopelessly blind and cruel as the old; the

only difference be that they be well armed. the Arab be frankly a

slaver, european enterprise be deeply interested in force labour. the

first-fruit of Christianity have be civil war, and one of Oswald’s

early experience of Uganda have be the attack of Mwanga and his

roman catholic adherent upon the Anglicans in Mengo, who hold out in

Lugard’s little fort and ultimately establish the soundness of the

Elizabethan compromise by mean of a Maxim gun. it be never a confident

outlook for many year anywhere between the Zambesi and the Nile

cataract. probably no honest man ever work in west and central Africa

between 1880 and 1900 who escape altogether from phase of absolute

despair; who do not face with a sink heart, lust, hatred, cunning

and treachery, black intolerance and ruthless aggression. and behind all

the perversity of man work the wickedness of tropical nature,

uncertain in her mood, frightful in her storm, fruitful of strange

trouble through weed and parasite, insect and pestilence. yet

civilization have in the long run win an astonishing victory. in a score

of year, so endless then, so brief in retrospect, road that have be

decay track or non-existent be make safe and open everywhere, the

railway and the post and telegraph come to stay, vast region of Africa

which since the beginning of thing have know no rule but the whim and

arbitrary power of transitory chief and king, awake to the conception

of impartial law; war canoe vanish from the lake and robber tribe

learn to tend their own cattle and cultivate their garden. and now

there be school. there be hospital. perhaps a quarter of a million

young people in Uganda alone could read and write; the percentage of

literacy in Uganda be rapidly overtake that in India and Russia.



on the face of it this be enough to set one thinking of the whole world

as if it be sweep forward to universal civilization and happiness.

for some year that have be Oswald’s habit of mind. it have be his

sustain faith. he have go from task to task until this last attack

of blackwater fever have arrest his activity. and then these doubt

display themselves.



from South Africa, that land of destiny for western civilization, have

come the first germ of his doubting. Sir Harry Johnston, Oswald’s chief,

a frank and bitter critic of the New Imperialism that have thrust up from

the Cape to Nyasaland under the leadership of Cecil Rhodes, help to

shape and point his scepticism. the old tradition of the Empire be

one of administration regardless of profit, Johnston declare; the new

seem inspire by conception of violent and hasty gain. the Rhodes

example have set all Africa dance to the tune of crude exploitation. it

have fire the competitive greed of the King of the Belgians and

unleashed blood and torture in the Congo Free State. the Congo State have

begin as a noble experiment, a real attempt at international compromise;

it have be give over to an unworthy trustee and wreck hideously by

his ruthless profit-hunting. all over the empire, honest administrator

and colonial politician, friendly explorer and the missionary of

civilization, be become more and more acutely aware of a heavy

acquisitive thrust behind the New Imperialism. usually they feel it

first in the treatment of the native. the early ill-treatment of the

native come from the local trader, the local planter, the white rough;

now as that sort of thing be get in hand and man could begin to hope

for a new and well order, come extensive scheme from Europe for the

wholesale detachment of the native from his land, for the wholesale

working and sweating of the native population....



have we defeat the little robber only to clear the way for organize

imperial robbery?



such thing be already troubling Oswald’s mind before the shock of the

south african war. but before the war they amount to criticism of

this administration or that, they be still untouched by any doubt of

the general imperial purpose or of the Empire as a whole. the south

african war lay bare an amazing and terrifying amount of national

incompetence. the Empire be not only hustle into a war for which there

be no occasion, but that war be plan with a lack of intelligent

foresight and conduct with a lack of soundness that dismay every

thoughtful Englishman. after a monstrous wasteful struggle the national

resource drag it at last to a not very decisive victory. the

outstanding fact become evident that the british army tradition be far

go in decay, that the army be feebly organize and equip, and that

a large proportion of its officer be under-educate man, narrow and

conventional, inferior in imagination and initiative to the farmer,

lawyer, cattle-drover, and suchlike leader against whom their wit

be pit. behind the rejoicing that hail the belate peace be a

real and unprecedented national humiliation. for the first time the

educate British be enquire whether all be well with the national

system if so small a conquest seem so great a task. upon mind thus

sensitized come the realization of an ever more vigorous and ever more

successful industrial and trade competition from Germany and the United

States; Great Britain be lose her metallurgical ascendancy, drop

far behind in the chemical industry and no long supreme upon the

sea. for the first time a threat be apparent in the method of

Germany. Germany be launch liner after liner to challenge the

British mercantile ascendancy, and she be increase her navy with a

passionate vigour. what do it mean? all over the world the British be

discover the German. and the German, it seem, have get this New

Imperialism that be in the british mind in a still harsh, still less

scrupulous and still more vulgar form. “wake up, England,” say the

Prince of Wales return from a visit to Canada, and Oswald hear the

phrase reverberate in Uganda and talk about it and think it over

continually.



(and Lord Rosebery speak of “efficiency.”)



but now when Oswald seek in the newspaper for sign of this wake up

that he desire, he find instead this tremendous reiteration of the

idea of the New Imperialism, acquisitive, mercenary, and altogether

selfish and national, which he already so profoundly disliked. the

awakening he desire be an awakening of the spirit, an awakening to

broad idea and nobler conception of the nation’s rôle in the world’s

affair. he have hope to find man talk of great scheme of national

education, of new school of ethnology, of tropical botany and oriental

language that would put the imperial adventure on a broad basis of

understanding and competent direction. instead, he find England full of

wild talk about “tax the foreigner.” a hasty search for national

profit he refuse to recognize as an awakening. for he indeed it have

far more of the quality of a nightmare.





                                  § 3



it be remarkable how much our deep conviction be at the mercy of

physiological jolt.



before the renew attack of fever have lower his vitality, Oswald have

feel doubtful of this and that, but he have never doubt of the ultimate

human triumph; he have never even doubt that the great Empire he serve

would survive, achieve its mission triumphantly, and incorporate itself

in some way with a unified mankind. he himself might blunder or fail,

there might be all sort of set-back, but in the end what he call

anglo-saxonism would prevail, the tradition of justice and free speech

would be justify by victory, and the dark phase of the Martyrdom of

Man end. but now the fever have so work on his nerve and tissue that

he no long enjoy this ultimate confidence. he could think that

anything might fail. he could even doubt the stability of the victorian

world.



one night during this last illness that have bring he home he fall

thinking of Zimbabwe and the lose city of Africa, and then presently

of the dead city of Yucatan, and then of all the lost and vanish

civilization of the world, of the long succession of human failure to

secure any abide order and security. with this he mingle the

suggestion of a recent anthropological essay he have read. two race of

man with big brain and subtle mind, the neanderthal race and the

Cro-Magnon race, it be argue very convincingly, have be entirely

exterminate before the beginning of our present humanity. our own race

too might fail and perish and pass away. in the night with a mount

temperature these be very grisly and horrible thought indeed. and

when at last he pass from such weary and dismal speculation to sleep,

there come a dream to crown and perpetuate his mood, a dream that be to

return again and again.



it be one of those dream that will sometimes give a nightmare reality

of form and shape to the mere implication of the wake life, one of

those dream that run before and anticipate and perhaps direct one’s

daylight decision. that black artist of delirium who throw his dark

creation upon our quiver mental screen, have seize and utilize all

Oswald’s germinating misgiving and add queer suggestion of his own.

through a thousand irrelevant and transitory horror one persistent idea

run through Oswald’s distress. it be the idea of a dark forest. and

of an endless effort to escape from it. he be one of the captain of a

vaguely conceive expedition that be lose in an interminable wilderness

of shadow; sometimes it be an expedition of limitless million, and

the black tree and creeper about he go up as high as the sky, and

sometimes he alone seem to be the entire expedition, and the darkness

rest on his eye, and the thorn wound he, and the great rope of

the creeper slash his face. he be always struggle to get through

this forest to some unknown hope, to some place where there be light,

where there be air and freedom, where one could look with brotherly

security upon the star; and this forest which be Life, hold he back;

it hold he with its darkness, it snare he with slime and marshy

pitfall, it entangle he amidst pool and channel of black and

blood-red stinking water, it trip he and bind he with its

creeper; evil beast snare his follower, great serpent put they to

flight, inexplicable panic and madness throw the long straggling

column into internecine warfare, incredible imbecility threaten the

welfare of the entire expedition. he would find himself examine the

load of an endless string of porter, and this man have fling away bread

and load his pack with poisonous fungus, and that one have replace

ammunition by rust and rubbish and filth. he would find himself in

frantic remonstrance with porter who have fling aside their load, who

be sullenly prepare to desert; or again, the whole multitude would

be stricken with some strange disease with the most foul and horrible

symptom, and refuse the doubtful medicine he tender in his despair;

or the ground would suddenly breed an innumerable multitude of white

thin voracious leech that turn red-black as they feed....



then far off through the straight bar of the tree stem a light shone,

and a great hope spring up in he. and then the light become red, a

waver red, a sudden hot breeze bring a sound of crackle wood and

the soughing of fall tree, spire and flag and agonize phantom of

flame rush up to the zenith; through the undergrowth a thousand black

beast stampede, the air be thick with wild flight of moth and

hum-bird, and he realize that the forest have catch fire....



that forest fire be always a climax. with it come a burn sensation

in loin and back. it make he shout and struggle and fight amidst the

black fugitive and the black thicket. until the twig and leave about

he be burst into flame like a Christmas tree that be be light

up. he would awaken in a sweating agony.



then presently he would be back again in the midst of that vague

innumerable expedition in the steamy deep grey aisle of the forest,

under the same gathering sense of urgent necessity, amidst the same

inextricable thickening tangle of confusion and cross-purpose.



in his wake moment Oswald, if he could, would have dismiss that

dream altogether from his mind. he could argue that it be the creation

of some purely pathological despondency, that it have no resemblance, no

parallelism, no sort of relation to reality. yet something of its dark

hue be reflect in his wake thought. sometimes this reflection be

so faint as to be scarcely perceptible, but always it be there.





                                  § 4



the Plantain, to which Oswald drift back to dine, be a club gather

from the end of the earth and very proud of the fact; it be make up of

explorer, traveller, colonial official, K.C.M.G.’s and C.M.G.’s. it

be understand to be a great exchange of imperial idea, and except for

a group or so of member who live in and about London, it have no

conversation because, live for the most part at different end of the

earth, its member do not get to know each other very well.

occasionally there be sporting gossip. Shy, sunburnt man drift in at

interval of three or four year, and dine and depart. once a member

with a sunstroke from India give way to religious mania, and try to

preach theosophy from the great staircase to three lonely gentleman who

be read the telegram in the hall. he be remove with difficulty.

the great red-paper, white-paint silence of the club be copiously

adorn with rather old yellow map of remote region, and in the hall

big terrestrial and celestial globe be available for any member who

wish to refresh their mind upon the broad fact of our position in

space. but the great glory of the club be its wealth of ethnological and

sporting trophy. scarcely be there a variety of spear, stab or

disembowelling knife, blow tube, bow, crossbow, or matchlock, that be

not at the disposal of any member nimble enough to pluck it from the

wall. in addition there be a vast collection of the head of beast;

everywhere they project from wall and pillar; head of bison, gazelle

and wart-hog cheer the soul of the member even in the humblest

recess. in the dining-room, above each table, a hippopotamus or a

rhinoceros or a tiger or a lion glare out with glassy eye upon the

world, show every item in its dentition. below these monster sit an

occasional empire-builder, in the careful evening dress of the

occasional visitant to civilization, seem by contrast a very pallid,

little, nicely behave thing indeed.



to the Plantain come Oswald, propose to dine alone, and in this

dining-room he discover Slingsby Darton, the fiscal expert, a little

Cockney with scarcely any nose at all, sit with the utmost impudence

under the large moose. Oswald be so pleased to discover any one he

know that he only remember that he detest Slingsby Darton as he

prepare to sit down with he. there be nothing for it then but to make

the good of he.



Oswald choose his dinner and his wine with care. red wine be forbid

he, but the wine waiter have good authority, authority from India and

gastrically very sensitive, for the Moselle he recommend. and in

answer to Slingsby Darton’s enquiry, Oswald spread out his theory that

he be an amiable, pleased sort of person oblige to come home from

Uganda, sorry to leave Uganda, but glad to be back in the dear old

country and “at the centre of thing,” and ready to take up anything——



“politic?” say Slingsby Darton. “we want a few voice that have get

out of sight of the parish pump.”



politic—well, it might be. but it be a little hard to join on to

thing at first. “fearful lot of squabbling—not very much do. not

nearly as much as one have hope.”



that seem a restrained, reasonable sort of thing to say. nor be it

extravagant to throw out, “I think it be ’wake up, England’; but she

seem just to be talk in her sleep.”



out flare the New Imperialism at once in Oswald’s face. “but have you

read Chamberlain’s great speech?” Slingsby Darton protest.



“I have those in mind,” say Oswald grimly.



both gentleman be in the early phase of encounter. it be not yet time

to join issue. Slingsby Darton hear, but make no retort. Oswald be

free to develop his discontent.



nothing seem to be getting do, he complain. the army have be

prove inefficient, incapable even of a colonial war, but what be we

do?



“exactly,” say Slingsby Darton. “you dare not even whisper

’conscription.’”



Oswald have not be think of that but of a technical reorganization,

more science, more equipment. but all that he could see in the way of a

change be “these beastly new cap.” (those be the day of the hate

’brodrick.’) then economic reorganization hung fire. “unemployed”

procession grow big every winter. (“tariff,” whisper Darton.

“intelligent organization,” say Oswald.) then education——



“education,” say Oswald, “be at the heart of the whole business.”



“I wouldn’t say _that_ altogether,” say Slingsby Darton.



“at the heart of the whole business,” Oswald repeat as though Slingsby

Darton have not speak. “the people do not know. our people do not

understand.” the Boer war have show how horribly backward our education

be—our high education, our scientific and technical education, the

education of our official and general in particular. “we have an

empire as big as the world and an imagination as small as a parish.” but

it would be a troublesome job to change that. much too troublesome.

Oswald become bitter and accusatory. his live side sneer. it would

bother a lot of Balfour’s friend quite uncomfortably. the dear old

Church couldn’t keep its grip on an education of that sort, and of

course the dear old Church must have its grip on education. so after a

few large-minded flourish, the politician have swamp the whole

question of educational reform in this row about church school and the

Passive Resistance movement, both side only too glad to get away from

reality. Oswald be as bitter against the Passive Resister as he be

against the Church.



“I don’t know whether I should give quite the primary place to

education,” say Slingsby Darton, battle against this tirade. “I don’t

know whether I should quite say that. Mr. Chamberlain——”



the fat, as the vulgar say, be in the fire.



October, 1903, be a feverish and impassioned time in english affair.

from Birmingham that month the storm have burst. with a great splash Mr.

Joseph Chamberlain have fling the issue of Protection into the sea of

political affair; huge wave of disturbance be sweep out to the

uttermost boundary of the empire. instead of pay taxis we be to

“tax the foreigner.” to that our fine imperial dream have come. over

dinner-table, in train and smoking-room, man be quarrel with

their old friend. to Oswald the conversion of Imperialism into a

scheme for world exploitation in the interest of Birmingham seem the

most atrocious swamping of real issue by private interest that it be

possible to conceive. the Sydenham strain be an uncommercial strain.

Slingsby Darton be manifestly in the full swirl of the new movement,

the man look cunning and eager, he put his pert little face on one

side and raise his voice to argue. a gathering quarrelsomeness take

possession of Oswald. he begin to speak very rapidly and pungently. he

assume an exasperating and unjustifiable detachment in order to quarrel

well. he come into these thing from the outside, he declare, quite

unbiased, oh! quite unbiased. and this “nail-trust organizer’s campaign”

shock he—shock he unspeakably. here be England confessedly in a

phase of inefficiency and deterioration, need a careful all-round

effort, in education, in business organization, in military preparation.

and suddenly drown everything else in his noise come “this demagogue

ironmonger with his panacea!”



Slingsby Darton be indignant. “my dear Sir! I cannot hear you speak of

Mr. Chamberlain in such term as that!”



“but consider the situation,” say Oswald. “consider the situation! when

of all thing we want steady and harmonious constructive work, come all

the uproar, all the cheap, mean thinking and dishonest spouting, the

music-hall trick and poster argument, of a campaign.”



Slingsby Darton argue. “but, my dear Sir, it be a _constructive_

campaign! it be base on urgent economic need.”



Oswald would have none of that. Tariff Reform be a quack remedy. “a

Zollverein. think of it! with an empire in great detach patch all

over the world. each patch with different characteristic and different

need. a child could see that a Zollverein be absurd. a child could see

it. yet to read the speech of Chamberlain you’d think a tariff could

work geographical miracle and turn the empire into a compact continent,

lock fast against the foreigner. how can a scattered host become a

band of robber? the mere attempt take we straight towards disaster.”



“straight away from it!” Slingsby Darton contradict.



Oswald go on regardlessly. “an empire—scatter like our—run on

selfish and exclusive line _must_ bring we into conflict with every

other people under the sun,” he assert. “it must do. apart from the

utter and wanton unrighteousness, apart from the treason to humanity.

oh! I _hate_ this New Imperialism. I hate it and dread it. it spoil my

sleep at night. it worry I and worry I....”



Slingsby Darton think he would do well to worry about this free

trade of ours which be bleed we to death.



“I do not speak as one ignorant of the empire,” say Oswald. “I have

be watch it——”



Slingsby Darton, disregard, maintain that he, too, have be

watch.



but Oswald be now at the “I tell you, Sir,” stage.



he declare that the New Imperialism come from Germany. it be invent

by professor of Weltpolitik. Milner have graft it upon we at Balliol.

but german condition be altogether different from ours, Germany be a

geographical unity, all draw together, unify by natural necessity,

like a fist. Germany be indeed a fist—by geographical necessity. the

british empire be like an open hand. must be like an open hand. we be

an open people—or we be nothing. we be a liberalize power or we

be the most pretentious sham in history. but we seem to be

forget that liberal idea for which we stand. we swagger now like

owner, forget that we be only trustee. trustee for mankind. we

be become a boastful and a sprawl people. the idea of grab

half the world—and then shut other people out with tariff,

be—Oswald be lose self-control—“a shove tradesman’s dream.” and we

be do it—as one might expect “a trust-organize nail-maker”—phrase

rub in with needless emphasis—to do it. we be shove about,

treading on everybody’s toe—and fail to educate, fail to arm.

yes—shoving. it be a good word. he do not mind how many time he use

it. “this dream of defy the world without an army, and dominate it

without education!” the Germans be at least logical in their swagger.

if they shove about they also arm. and they educate. anyhow they

train. but we trod on everybody’s toe and try to keep friend all

round....



so Oswald—under the moose—while Slingsby Darton do what he could by

stab an objection at he now and again. it become clear and

clear to Slingsby Darton that the only possibility before he of

hold his own, short of throw knife and glass at Oswald, be to

capture the offensive.



“you complain of a panacea,” he say, poke out two arrest finger

at Oswald. “that Tariff Reform be a panacea. but what of education? what

of this education of your? that also be a panacea.”



and just then apt to his aid come Walsall and the Bishop of Pinner from

their table under the big, black, clerical-look hippopotamus. Walsall

be a naturalist, and have meet Oswald in the day of his biological

enthusiasm; the Bishop of Pinner have formerly be the Bishop of

Tanganyika and know Oswald by repute. so they come over to greet he and

be at once seize upon as auxiliary by Slingsby Darton.



“we’re getting heat over politic,” say Slingsby Darton, indicate

that at least Oswald be.



“every one be getting heat over politic,” say the bishop. “it’s as

bad as the Home Rule split.”



“Sydenham’s panacea be to save the world by education. he willn’t hear of

economic organization.”



the bishop open eye and mouth at Oswald until he look like the full

moon....



on that assertion of Slingsby Darton’s they drift past the pay-desk

to the small smoking-room, and there they have a great dispute about

education beneath a gallery audience, so to speak, compose of antelope,

Barbary sheep, gnu, yak, and a sea lion. Oswald have never realize

before how passionately he believe in education. it be a revelation.

he discover himself. he want to tell these man they be uneducated.

he do succeed in say that Mr. Chamberlain be “essentially an

uneducated man.”



Walsall be a very try opponent for a disputant of swift and

passionate conviction. he have a judicial affectation, a socratic pose.

he be a grey, fluffy-head man with large tortoiseshell spectacle and

a general resemblance to a kind wise owl. he like to waggle his head

slowly from side to side and smile. he like to begin sentence with

“but have you think——?” or “I think you have overlook——” or “so far

from believe that, I hold the exact converse.” he say these thing in

a very suave voice as though each remark be carefully dress in oil

before serve.



he express grave doubt whether there be “any benefit in

education—any benefit whatever.”



but the argument that form that evening’s entertainment for the sea

lion and those assorted ruminate artiodactyls be too prolonged and

heated and discursive to interest any but the most sedulous reader.

every possible sort of heresy about education seem loose that night

for the affliction of Oswald. Slingsby Darton say, “make man prosperous

and education will come of its own accord.” Walsall think that the

sort of people who benefit by education “would get on anyhow.” he

think knowledge be of value accord to the difficulty one

experience in attain it. (could any sane man really believe that?)

“I would persecute science,” say Walsall, “and then it would be take

care of by enthusiast.”



“but do you know,” say Oswald, with an immense quiet in his manner,

“that there be a—a British Empire? an empire with rather urgent need?”



(suppress murmur from Slingsby Darton: “then I don’t see what your

position be at all!”)



Walsall dispute these “need.” ben’t we all too much disposed to make

the empire a thing of plan and will? an empire be a growth. it be like

a man, it grow without take thought. presently it age and decay. we

be not go to save the empire by take thought.



(Slingsby Darton, disregard, now disagree with Walsall.)



“Germany takes thought,” Oswald interject.



“to its own undoing, perhaps,” say Walsall....



the bishop’s method of annoyance be even blander than Walsall’s, and

more exasperating to the fevere victim. he talk of the evil of an

“educate proletariat.” for a stable community only a certain proportion

of educated people be advisable. you could upset the social balance by

over-educate the masse. “we destroy good, honest, simple-soule

worker in order to make discontent clerk.” Oswald splutter, “you

_must_ make a citizen in a modern population understand something of the

State he belong to!”



“Better, faith,” say the bishop. “far well, faith. teach they a

simple catechism.”



he have visit Russia. he have be to the coronation of the Tzar, a

beautiful ceremony, only a little mar by a quite accidental massacre

of some of the spectator. those be the day before the Russo-japanese

war and the coming of the Duma. there be much to admire in Russia, the

good bishop declare; much to learn. Russia be the land of Mary,

great-souled and bless; ours alas! be the land of bustle Martha.

nothing more enviable than the political solidarity of Russia—“after our

war voice.... time after time I ask myself, ’ben’t we western

on the wrong track? here be something—great. and grow great.

something simple. here be obedience and a sort of primitive contentment.

Trust in the Little White Father, belief in God. here christianity

_live indeed_.’”



about eleven o’clock Walsall be propound a paradox. “all this talk

of education,” he say, “remind I of the man who try to lift himself

by his own ear. how, I ask myself, can a democracy such as ours take an

intelligent interest in its destiny unless it be educate, and how can

it educate itself unless it take an intelligent interest in its

destiny? how escape that dilemma?”



“a community,” say Oswald, grapple with this after a moment, “a

community ben’t one mind, it’s a number of mind, some more intelligent,

some less. it’s a perpetual flow of new mind——”



then something give way within he.



“we sit here,” he say in a voice so full of fury that the mouth of the

bishop fall open, “and while we talk this half-witte, half-clever

_muck_ to excuse ourselves from get the nation into order, the sand

run out of the glass. the time draw near when the empire will be

challenge——”



he stand up abruptly.



“have you any idea,” he say, “what the empire might be? have you

think of these hundred of million to whom we might give light—_had_

we light? be we to be a possessing and profit-hunt people because we

have not the education to be a leaderly people? be we to do no well

than Rome and Carthage—and loot the province of the world? Loot or

education, that be the choice of every imperial opportunity. all

England, I find, be echo with scream for loot. have none of we

vision? none?”



the bishop shake his head sadly. the man, he think, be rave.



“what _be_ this vision of your?” sneer Walsall. “ten thousand

professor?”



“after all,” say Slingsby Darton with a weary insidiousness, “we do not

differ about our fundamental idea. you must have fund. you must endow

your school. without Tariff Reform to give you revenue——”



but Oswald be not go to begin over again.



“I ought to be in bed,” he say, look at his watch. “my doctor send

I to bed at ten....”



“my God!” he whisper as he put on his coat under the benevolent

supervision of an exceptionally fine indian buffalo.



“what be to happen to the empire,” he cry, go out into the night

and address himself to the moon, to the monument which commemorate

the heroic incompetence of the Duke of York, and to an interested hansom

cabby, “what be to happen to the empire—when these be its educate

opinion?”





                                  § 5



but it be high time that Joan and Peter come back into this narrative.

for this be their story, it bear their name on its cover and on its

back and on its title-page and at the head of each left-hand page. it

have be necessary to show the state of mind, the mental condition, the

outlook, of their sole guardian when their affair come into his hand.

this do they now return by telephone. Oswald have not be back in the

comfortable sitting-room at the Climax Club for ten minute before he

be ring up by Mr. Sycamore and remind of his duty to his young

charge. a club page call Mr. Sydenham to the receiver in his bedroom.



in those day the telephone be still far from perfection. it have not

be in general use for a decade.... Mr. Sycamore be audible as a still

small voice.



“Mr. Sydenham? sycamore speak.”



“no need to be,” say Oswald. “you haven’t be speak to I.”



“who be I speak to? I want Mr. Sydenham. sycamore speak.”



“I’m Mr. Sydenham. who be you? no need to be sick of your speaking so

far as I’m concerned. I’ve only just be call to the telephone——”



“your solicitor, Sycamore. S.Y.C.A.M.O.R.E.”



“oh! right O. how be you, Mr. Sycamore? I’m Sydenham. how be those

child?”



“hope you’re well, Mr. Sydenham?”



“Gaudy—in a way. how be you?”



“I’ve be with Lady Charlotte today. I don’t know if you’ve hear

anything of——”



Whop! Whop. bunnik. _Silence._



after a little difficulty communication with Mr. Sycamore be partially

restore. I say partially because his voice have now become very small

and remote indeed. “I be say, I don’t know if you understand

anything of the present state of affair.”



“nothing,” say Oswald. “fire ahead.”



“can you hear I distinctly? I find you almost inaudible.”



Remonstrances with the exchange lead after a time to slightly improve

communication.



“you be say something about a fire?” say Mr. Sycamore.



“I say nothing about a fire. you be say something about the

child?”



“well, well. thing be in a very confused state, Mr. Sydenham. I hope

you mean to take hold of their education. these child be not be

educate, they be be fight over.”



“who’s think over they?”



“no one. but the Misses Stubland and Lady Charlotte be fight over

they.... F.I.G.H.T.I.N.G. I want _you_ to think over they....

you—yes.... think, yes. both clever child. great waste if they be

not properly educate.... matter be really urgent. I have be with

Lady Charlotte today. you know she kidnap they?”



“kidnap?”



a bright girlish voice, an essentially happy voice, cut into the

conversation at this point. “three minute _up_,” it say.



empire-building language fall from Oswald. in some obscure way this

feminine intervention be sweep aside, and talk be resume with Mr.

Sycamore.



it continue to be a fragmentary talk, and for a time the burthen of

some unknown lady complain to an unknown friend about the behaviour

of a third unknown name George, state to lack “gumption,” interwove

with the main theme. but Mr. Sycamore do succeed in conveying to Oswald

a sense of urgency about the welfare of his two charge. immediate

attention be demand. they be be neglect. the girl be ill. “I

would like to talk it over with you as soon as possible,” say Mr.

Sycamore.



“can you come and breakfast here at eight?” say the man from the

tropic.



“half past nine,” say the Londoner, and the talk close.



the talk end, but for a time the bell of Oswald’s telephone remain

in an agitated state, give little nervous ring at interval. when he

answer these the exchange say “number please,” and when he say, “you

ring _I_,” the exchange say, “oh, no! we don’t....”



“an empire,” whisper Oswald, sit on the edge of his bed, “which

cannot even run a telephone service efficiently....”



“education....”



he try to recall his last speech at the club. have he rant? what have

they think of it? what precisely have he say? while they sit and

talk _muck_—his memory be unpleasantly insistent upon that

“_muck_”—the sand run out of the hour-glass, a new generation grow up.



have he say that? that be the point of it all—about the new generation.

a new generation be grow up and we be do nothing to make it

wiser, more efficient, to give it a broad outlook than the generation

that have blunder into and blunder through the Boer war. have he say

that? that be what he ought to have say.





                                  § 6



for a long time he sit on his bed, blank-minded and too tired to finish

undressing. he get to bed at last. but not to sleep. he find that the

talk in the club have disturb his mind almost unendurably. it have

point and endorse everything that he have be try not to think

about the old country. now, too weary and too excited to sleep, he

turn over and over again, unprofitably and unprogressively, the

tangle impression of his return to England.



how many million of such hour of restless questioning must have be

spend by wakeful englishman in the dozen year between the Boer war and

the great war; how many nocturnally scheme brain must have explore

the complicated maze of national danger, national ambition, and

national ineptitude! if “wake up, England,” sow no great harvest of

change in the daylight, it do at any rate produce large phantom crop

at night. he argue with Walsall over and over again, sometimes wide

awake and close to the point, sometimes drowsily with the discussion

become vague and strangely misshapen and incoherent. be walsall

right? be it impossible to change the nature and quality of a people?

must we English always be laggard in peace and blunderer in war? be

our achievement accident, and our failure essential? be slackness in

our blood? surely a great effort might accomplish much, a great effort

to reorganize political life, to improve national education, to make the

press a well instrument of public thought and criticism. to which

Walsall answer again with, “how can a democratic community take an

intelligent interest in its destiny unless it be educate, and how can

it educate itself unless it take an intelligent interest in its

destiny?”



Oswald groan and turn over in bed.



Thought pass by insensible degree into dream and dream

shallow again to wakefulness. always he seem to be argue with

Walsall and the bishop for education and effort; nevertheless, now

vaguely apprehend as an atmospheric background, now real and close,

the black forest of his african nightmare be about he. always he be

struggle on and always he be hope to see down some vista the warm

gleam of daylight, the promise of the open. and Walsall, a vast forest

owl with enormous spectacle, keep get in the way, flap hand

that be really great wing at he and assure he that there be no

way out. none. “this forest be life. this forest will always be life.

there be no other life. after all it ben’t such a very bad forest.”

other figure, too, come and go; a gigantic bishop sit back in an

easy chair block one hopeful vista, declare that book-learn only

make the low class discontent and mischievous, and then a stupidly

content fat man smoke a fat cigar drive in a gig athwart the line of

march. he say nothing; he just drive his gig. then somehow an

automobile come in, a most hopeful mean of escape, except that it have

break down; and Oswald be try to repair it in spite of the jeering

of an elderly gentleman in a white waistcoat. suddenly the whole forest

swarm with child. there be countless child; there be just

two child. instead of a multitudinous expedition Oswald find himself

alone in the black jungle with just two child, two white and stunt

child who be die for the air and light. no one have care for

they. one be ill, seriously ill. unless the way out be find they

could not live. they be dolly’s child, his ward. but what be he

to do for they?...



then far ahead he see that light of the great conflagration, that light

that promise to be daylight and become a fire....



“black coffee,” say Oswald during one of the wide-awake interval.

“cigar. talk. over-excited.... I ought to be more careful.... I forget

how flimsy I be still....



“I must get my mind off these thing. I’ll talk to old sycamore tomorrow

and see about this little master Peter Stubland and his foster-sister.

I’ll go into the matter thoroughly. I haven’t think of they before.



“I wonder if the boy still take after dolly....



“after all,” he say, roll over, “it’s true. education be the big

neglect duty of the time. it’s fundamental. and what be I do? it’s

just England—England all over—to let that boy be drag up. I ought to

see about he—now. I’ll go down there....



“I’ll go and stay with Aunt Charlotte for a day or so. I’ll send she a

wire tomorrow.”





                                  § 7



the quiet but observant life of old Cashel at Chastlands be greatly

enliven by the advent of Oswald.



sign of a grave and increase agitation in the mind of Cashel’s

mistress become evident immediately after the departure of Mr. Sycamore.

manifestly whatever that gentleman have say or do—old Cashel have be

able to catch very little—have be of a highly stimulating nature. so

soon as he be out of the house, Lady Charlotte abandon her sofa and

table, upset her tonic as she do so, and still wear she

dressing-gown and cap, proceed to direct a hasty packing for Italy.

Unwin become much agitated, and a housemaid be address as a

“perfect fool” become a sniffing fount of tear. there be a running to

and fro with trunk and tea-basket, a ringing of bell, and minor

Lady Charlotte come so near to actual physical violence that Unwin

waggishly over one eye. she do not have tea until half-past five.



she talk to herself with her hard blue eye fix on vacancy. “I will

not stay here to be insult,” she say.



“rampageous,” whisper Cashel on the landing. “rumbustious. what’s it

all about?”



“Cashel!” she say sharply as he be take away the tea-thing.



“M’lady.”



“telephone to Mr. Grimes and ask he to take ticket as usual for myself

and Unwin to Pallanza—for tomorrow.”



it be terrible but pleasing to have to tell she that Mr. Grimes would

now certainly have go home from his office.



“see that it be do tomorrow. tomorrow I must catch the eleven

forty-seven for Charing Cross. I shall take lunch with I in the train.

a wing of chicken. a drop of claret. perhaps a sandwich. Gentleman’s

Relish or shrimp paste. and a grape or so. a mere mouthful. I shall

expect you to be in attendance to help with the luggage as far as

Charing Cross....”



so she be go after all.



“like a flight,” muse Cashel. “what’s after the Old Girl?”...



he grasp the situation a little more firmly next day.



the preparation for assemble Lady Charlotte in the hall before

Oswald receive about half-past ten have greatly stimulate these

Unwin, very white in the face—she always have a bilious headache when

travel be forward—and dress in the peculiar speckled black dress

and black hat that she consider most deterrent to foreign depravity,

be already sit stiffly in the hall with Lady Charlotte’s

purple-colour dressing-bag beside she, and Cashel having see to the

that it be in order, when suddenly there be the flap, rustle

sound of a large woman in rapid movement upon the landing above, and

Lady Charlotte appear at the head of the stair, all hat, veil

and wrap for travel. her face be bright white with excitement.

“Unwin, I want you,” she cry. “Cashel, say I’m in bed. say I’m ill and

must not be disturb. say I’ve be take ill.”



she vanish with the agility of a girl of twenty—except that the

landing be of a different opinion.



the two servant hear her scuttle into her room and slam the door.

there be a great moment of silence.



“oh, _lor’_!” Unwin rise with the sigh of a martyr, and take the

dressing-bag with she—the fitting alone be worth forty pound—and

press her handkerchief to her ache brow, march upstairs.



Cashel, agape, be rouse by the ringing of the front door bell. he

open to discover Mr. Oswald Sydenham with one arm in a sling and a rug

upon the other.



“Hullo, Cashel,” he say. “I suppose my room ben’t occupy? my telegram

here? how’s Lady Charlotte?”



“very poorly, sir,” say Cashel. “she’s have to take in her bed, sir.”



“pity. anything serious?”



“a sudden attact, sir.”



“H’m. well, tell she I’m go to inflict myself upon she for a day or

so. just take my trap in and I’ll go on with this fly to Limpsfield.

say I’ll be back to dinner.”



“certainly, sir.”



the old man bustle out to get in the valise and gladstone bag that

constitute Oswald’s luggage. when he come into the hall again he find

the visitor scrutinize the tea-basket and the roll of rug with his

one penetrate eye in a manner that make he dread a question. but

Oswald never question servant; on this occasion only he wink at

one.



“nothing wrong with the arm, sir?” ask old Cashel.



“nothing,” say Oswald, still look markedly at the symptom of

imminent travel. “H’m.”



he go out to the fly, stand ready to enter it, and then swivel

round very quickly and look up at his aunt’s bedroom window in time to

catch an instant impression of a large, anxious face regard he.



“ah!” say Oswald, and return smile grimly into the hall.



“Cashel,” he call.



“Sir?”



“her ladyship be up. tell she I have a few word to say to she before

she go.”



“Beg pardon, sir——”



“look here, Cashel, you do what I tell you.”



“I’ll tell Miss Unwin, sir.”



he go upstairs, leave Oswald still think over the rug. yes, she

be _off!_ she have get everything; point Alpine stick, tea-basket,

travel campstool. it must be Switzerland or Italy for the winter at

least. a great yearning to see his aunt with his own eye come upon

Oswald. he follow Cashel upstairs quietly but swiftly, and find he

in a hasty whisper consultation with Unwin on the second landing. “oh

my ’ed’ll burst _bang_,” Unwin be say.



“’Er ladyship, sir,” she begin at the sight of Oswald.



“Ssh!” he say to she, and hold she and Cashel silent with an uplifted

forefinger while he listen to the sound of a large powerful woman

go to bed swiftly and violently in her clothe.



“I must go in to she, sir,” say Unwin break the silence. “poor dear!

it’s a _very_ sudden attact.”



the door open and close upon Unwin.



“lock the door on he, you—you _Idiot!_” they hear Lady Charlotte

shout—too late.



the hated and dread visage of Oswald appear look round the corner

of the door into the great lady’s bedroom. her hat have be fling aside,

she be tie on an unconvincing night cap over her great blond

travel wig; she hastily assume nightgown betray the agate brooch

at her neck.



“how dare you, sir!” she cry at the sight of he.



“you’re not ill. you’re go to cut off to Italy this afternoon. what

have you do to my ward?”



“a lady’s sick room! sacred, Sir! have you no sense of decency?”



“be it measle, Auntie?”



“go _away_!”



“I daren’t. if I leave you alone in this country for a year or two

you’re bind to get into trouble. what be I to _do_ with you?”



“unbecoming intrusion!”



“you ought to be stop by the Foreign Office. you’ll lead to a war

with Italy.”



“go for a doctor, Cashel,” she cry aloud in her great voice. “go for

the doctor.”



“M’lady,” very faintly from the landing.



“and countermand the station cab, Cashel,” say Oswald.



“if you do anything of the sort, Cashel!” she cry, and sit up in

bed clutch the sheet with such violence that a large spring-side

boot become visible at the foot of the bed. the great lady have go to

bed in her boot. aunt and nephew both glare at this revelation in an

astonished silence.



“how _can_ you, Auntie,” say Oswald.



“if I choose,” say Lady Charlotte. “if I choose——oh! _go away!_”



“back to dinner,” say Oswald sweetly, and withdraw.



he be still pensive upon the landing when Unwin appear to make sure

that the station cab be not countermand....



under the circumstance he be not surprised to find on his return from

the Ingle-Nook that he be now the only occupant of Chastlands. Aunt

Charlotte have flee, leave behind a note that have evidently be

write before his arrival.



  _my dear Nephew,—I be sorry that my arrangement for go abroad

  this winter, already make, prevent my welcome you home for this

  uninvited and totally unexpected visit. I be sure Cashel and the

  other servant will take good care of you. you seem to know the way

  to their good grace. there be many thing I should have like to

  talk over with you if you have give I due and proper notice of your

  return as you ought to have do, instead of leave it to a

  solicitor to break the glad tiding to I, follow by a sixpenny

  telegram. as it be, I shall just miss you. I have to go, and I

  cannot wait. all my arrangement be make. I suppose it be idle to

  expect civility from you ever or the slight attention to the

  convenance. the Sydenhams have never shine in manner. well, I hope

  you will take those two poor child quite out of the hand of

  those smoking, blaspheming, nightgown-wear Limpsfield woman. they

  be utterly unfit for such a responsibility. utterly. I would not

  trust a pauper brat in their hand. the child require firm

  treatment, the girl especially, or they will be utterly spoil. she

  be deceitful and dishonest, as one might expect; she give Mrs. Pybus

  a very try time indeed, catch measle deliberately and so

  convert the poor woman’s house into a regular hospital. I fear

  for her later. I have do my good for they both. no doubt you will

  find it all spin into a fine tale, but I trust your penetration to

  see through a tissue of lie, however plausible it may seem at the

  first blush. I be glad to think you be now to relieve I of a

  serious responsibility, though how a single man not relate to she

  in the slight degree can possibly bring up a young girl, even

  though illegitimate, without grave scandal, pass my poor

  comprehension. no doubt I be an old fashioned old fool nowadays!

  thank God! I beg to be excuse!_



                                        _your affectionate Aunt_

                                                            CHARLOTTE.



towards the end of this note her ladyship’s highly angular handwriting

betray by an enhance size and considerable irregularity, a deflection

from her customary calm.





                                  § 8



Oswald knock for some time at the open green door of the Ingle-Nook

before attract any one’s attention. then a small but apparently only

servant appear, a little round-face creature who look up hard into

Oswald’s living eye—as though she don’t quite like the other. she

explain that “Miss Phyllis” be not at home, and that “Miss Phœbe

mustn’t be disturve.” Miss Phœbe be work. Miss Phyllis have go

away with Mary——



“who’s Mary?” say Oswald.



“well, Sir, it’s Mary who always ’as be ’ere, Sir,”—to Windsor to be

with Miss Joan. “and it’s order no one’s allow to upset Miss Phœbe

when she’s write. not even Lady Charlotte Sydenham, Sir. I dursn’t

give your name, Sir, even. I dursn’t.”



“except,” she add reverentially, “it’s death or a fire.”



“you ben’t the Piano, per’aps?” she ask.



Oswald have to confess he ben’t.



the little servant look sorry for he.



and that be in truth the inexorable law now of the Ingle-Nook. Aunt

Phœbe be take herself very seriously—as become a Thinker whose

_stitchwoman_ paper, deep, high, and occasionally broad in thought,

be run into a sale of ten of thousand. so she sit hard and close

at her writing-table from half-past nine to twelve every morning,

seclude and defend from all the world, correct, muse deeply

over, and occasionally read aloud the proof of the third series of

_stitchwoman_ paper. (Old Groombridge, the occasional gardener, use to

listen outside in awe and admiration. “my word, but she do give it ’em!”

old Groombridge use to say.) Oswald perceive that there be nothing to

do but wait. “I’ll wait,” he say, “downstairs.”



“I suppose I ought to let you in,” say the little servant, evidently

seek advice.



“oh, decidedly,” say Oswald, and enter the room in which he have

part from dolly six year ago.



the door close behind the little servant, and Oswald find himself in a

house far more heavily charge with memory than he could have

expect. the furniture have be but little alter; it be the morning

time again, the shadow masse fall in the same place, it have just the

same atmosphere of quiet expectation it have have on that memorable day

before the door beyond have open and dolly have appear, subdue and

ashamed, to tell he of the act that sever they for ever. how living

she seem here by virtue of those inanimate thing! have that door

open now he would have expect to see she stand there again. and

he be alive still, strong and active, alter just a little by a touch

of fever and six short year of experience, but the same thing of

impulse and desire and anger, and she have go beyond time and space,

beyond hunger or desire. he have walk between this window and this

fireplace on these same brick on which he be pace now, spit

abuse at she, a man mad with shame and thwarted desire. never have he

forgive she, or stay his mind to think what life have be for she,

until she be dead. that outbreak, with gesticulate hand and an

angry, grimacing face, have be her last memory of he. what a break

image he have make of himself in her mind! and now he could never set

thing right with she, never tell she of his belate understanding and

pity. “I be a weak thing, confused and tear between my motive. why do

you—you who be my lover—why do you not help I after I have stumble?”

so the still phantom in that room reproach he, a phantom of his own

creation, for dolly have never reproach he; to the end she have have no

reproach in her heart for any one but herself because of their

disaster.



“hold tight to love, little people,” he whisper. “hold tight to

love.... but we don’t, we don’t....”



never before have Oswald so feel the tremendous pitifulness of life. he

feel that if he stay long in this room he must cry out. he walk to

the garden door and stand look at the empty flagstone path between

the dahlia and sunflower.



it be all as if he have but leave it yesterday, except for the heartache

that now mingle with the sunshine.



“Pat—whack—pat—whack”; he scarcely heed that rhythmic noise.



Peter have go out of his head altogether. he walk slowly along the

pathway towards the little arbour that overhang the Weald. then,

turn, he discover Peter with a bat in his hand, regard he....



directly Oswald see Peter he marvel that he have not be eager to see

he before. the boy be absurdly like dolly; he have exactly the same

smile; and directly he see the gaunt figure of his one-eyed guardian he

cry out, “it’s Nobby!” with a voice that might have be her. there

be a squeak of genuine delight in his voice. he ben’t at all the

sturdy little thing in a pinafore that Oswald remember. he seem

indeed at the first glance just a thin, flat-cheste little dolly in

grey flannel trouser.



he have obviously be bore before this happy arrival of Oswald. he have

be bang a rubber ball against the scullery with a cricket-bat and

counting hit and miss. it be a poor entertainment. Oswald do not

realize how green his memory have be keep by the Bungo-Peter saga, and

Peter’s prompt recognition after six year flatter he.



the two approach one another slowly, take each other in.



“you remember I?” say Oswald superfluously.



“don’t I just! you promise I a lion’s skin.”



“so I do.”



he could not bear to begin this new relationship as a defaulter. “it’s

on its way to you,” he equivocate, make secret plan.



Peter, tuck his bat under his arm and bury his hand in his

trouser pocket, draw still near. at a distance of four foot or

thereabout he stop short and Oswald stop short. Peter regard

this still incredible home-comer with his head a little on one side.



“it be you, use to tell I story.”



“you don’t remember my tell you story?”



“I do. about the Ba-ganda who live in u-ganda. don’t you remember how

you use to put out my zulus and my elephant and lion on the floor and

say it be Africa. you teach we roar like lion—Joan and I. don’t

you remember?”



Oswald remember. he remember himself on all four with the child

on the floor of the sunny playroom upstairs, and some one sometimes

standing, sometimes sit above the game, some one who listen as

keenly as the child, some one at whom he talk about that world of

lake as large as sea, and of trackless, sunless forest and of

park-like glade and wilderness of flower, and about string of

load porter and of encounter with marvel people who have never

before set eye on a European....





                                  § 9



the idea that the guardianship of Peter be just a little duty to be

see to, vanish at the sight of he in favour of the realization of a

living relationship. there be moment when small boy of ten in perfect

health and condition can look the small, flimsiest, and most pathetic

of create thing—and at the same time preternaturally valiant and

moment flicker out. more particularly do this unconscious camouflage

but these be feeling he know he must not betray.



“I must tell you some more story,” he say. “I’ve come back to England

to live.”



“_Here?_”—brightly.



“well, near here. but I shall see a lot of you now, Peter.”



“I’ll like that,” say Peter. “I’ve often think of you....”



a pause.

“not so bad as that. I’ve get to have some bit of shell take out.”

another pause.

he sit down. here he have talk with dolly and take her hand....



he bestir himself to talk.



“and exactly how old be you now, Peter?”



“ten year and two month,” say Peter.



“we’ll have to find a school for you.”



“have you be in Africa since I see you?” Peter ask, avoid the

topic.



“since you see I go off,” say Oswald, and the man glance at the

boy and the boy glance at the man, and each be wonder what the

other remember. “I’ve be in Uganda all the time. there’s be

fighting and working. some day you must go to Uganda and see all that

have be do. we’ve make a good railway and good road and telegraph.

we’ve put down robber and cruelty.”



“and shoot a lot of lion?”



“Plenty. the lion be pretty awful for a bit. about Nairobi and along

the line.”



“shot ’em when they be come at you?”



“one be come straight at I.”



“that’s my skin,” say Peter.



Oswald make no answer.



“I’d like to go to Africa,” say Peter.



“you shall.”



he decide to begin at once upon his neglect task of make an

imperial citizen accord to the idea that prevail before the advent

of the New Imperialism. “that sort of thing,” he say, “be what we

englishman be for, you know, Peter. what our sort of Englishman be for

anyhow. we have to go about the world and make road and keep the peace

and see fair play. we’ve get to kill big beast and climb hard

mountain. that’s the job of the Englishman. he’s a sort of policeman. a

sort of working guardian. not a nosy slave-driver try to get rich. he

chase off slave-driver. all the world’s his beat. India, Africa,

China, and the East, all the sea of the world. this little fat green

country, all trim and tidy and set with house and garden, ben’t much

of a land for a man, you know—unless he’s an invalid. it’s a good land

to grow up in and come back to die in. or rest in. but in between, no!”



“no,” say Peter.



“no.”



“but you haven’t come back to die, Uncle Nobby?”



“no fear. but I’ve have to come back. I’m rest. this old arm, you

know, and all that sort of thing. just for a time.... and besides I want

to see a lot of you.”



“yes.”



“you have to grow up here and learn all you can, science and all sort

of thing, so that you can be a useful man—wherever you have to go.”



“Africa,” say Peter.



“Africa, perhaps. and that’s why one have to go to school and college—and

learn all about it.”



“they haven’t teach I much about it yet,” say Peter.



“well, you haven’t be to much in the way of school,” say Oswald.



“be there well school?”



“no end. we’re go to find one,” say Oswald.



“I wish school be over,” say Peter.



“why? you’ve get no end to learn yet.”



“I want to begin,” say Peter, look out across the tumbled gentleness

of the Weald.



“begin school?”



“no, begin—Africa, India—do thing.”



“school first,” say Oswald.



“be there school where you learn about gun and animal and mountain

and foreign people?” say Peter.



“there must be,” say Oswald. “we’ll find something.”



“where you don’t do Latin and parse and ’straction of the square

root.”



“oh! those thing have their place.”



“do you have to do they, Uncle Nobby?”



“rather.”



“be they useful to you?”



“at time—in a way. of course those thing be good as training, you

know—awfully good. harden up the mental muscle, Peter.”



Peter make no reply to that.



presently Peter say, “shall I learn about machine?”



“when you’ve do some mathematic, Peter.”



“I’d like to fly,” say Peter.



“that’s far away yet.”



“there be a boy at that school, his father be an engineer; and he say

that fly machine be come quite soon.”



this be beyond Oswald’s range.



“the French have get a balloon that steer about,” he say. “that’s as

near as we be likely to come to fly for a long time yet.”



“this boy say that he mean a real fly machine, not a balloon. it

be to be heavy than air. it would fly like a kite or a bird.”



“I doubt if we’ll see that in my lifetime,” say Oswald; “or your,”

blind to the fate that have mark Peter for its own.



“H’m,” say Peter, with a shadow fall upon one of his bright

dream. (Nobby ought to know these thing. his word ought surely to be

final. still, after all, this chap’s father be an engineer.) “I’d love

to fly,” say Peter.





                                  § 10



something with the decorative effect of a broad processional banner in a

very High Church indeed, appear upon the flagstone path. it be Aunt

Phœbe.



she have come out into the garden half an hour before her usual time. but

indeed from the moment when she have hear Oswald and Peter talk in

the garden below she have be unable to write more. after some futile

attempt to pick up the lose thread of her discourse, she have go to

her bedroom and revise her toilet, which be often careless in the

morning, so as to be more expressive of her personality. she be wear

a long djibbah-like garment with a richly embroider yoke, she have

sandal over her brown stocking, and rather by way of symbol of

authorship than for any immediate use she bear a big leather portfolio.

there be moreover now a gold-mount fountain pen amidst the other

ingredient of the cheerful chatelaine that have once delight Peter’s

babyhood.



she seem a full, more confident person than Oswald remember. she

come eloquent with apology. “I have to make an inexorable rule,” she

say, “against disturbance. as if I be a man writer instead of a mere

woman. between nine and one I be a woman enclose—cloistered—refuse.

sacred hour of self-completeness. unspeakably precious to I. visitor

be not even announce. it be a law—inflexible.”



“we must all respect our work,” say Oswald.



“it’s over now,” say Aunt Phœbe, smile like the sun after cloud.

“it’s over now for the day. I be just human—until tomorrow again.”



“you be write a book?” Oswald ask rather ineptly.



“the Stitchwoman; Series Three. much be expect; much must be give. I

be the slave now of a following.”



Aunt Phœbe go to the wall and stand with her fine profile raise up

over the view. she be a little breathless and twitch slightly, but

very magnificent. Most of her hair be tidy. “our old weald, do it

look the same?” she ask.



“quite the same,” say Oswald, stand up beside she.



“but not to I,” she say. “indeed not to I. to I every day it be

different. always wide, always wonderful, but different, always

different. I know it so well.”



Oswald feel she have work a “catch” on he. he be faintly nettle.



“still,” he say, “fundamentally one must recognize that it’s the same

Weald.”



“I wonder,” say Aunt Phœbe suddenly, look at he very intently, and

then, as if she taste the word, “fundamentally?”



“I don’t know,” she add.



Oswald be too much annoyed to reply.



“and what do you think of your new charge?” she ask. “I don’t know

whether Peter quite understand that yet. the young squire go to the

man. he cast aside childish thing, and ride out in his little

Caparison to join the rank. do you know that, Peter? Mr. Sydenham be

now your sole guardian.”



Peter look at Oswald and smile shyly, and his cheek flush.



“I think we shall get on together,” say Oswald.



“would that it end there! you take the girl too?”



“it be not my doing,” say Oswald.



Aunt Phœbe address the Weald.



“poor dolly! so it be that the mother soul cheat itself. through the

age—always self-abnegation for the woman.” she turn to Oswald. “if

she have have time to think I be certain she would not have exclude woman

from this trust. certain. what have man to do with education? with the

education of a woman more particularly. the Greater from the Less. but

the thing be do. it have be a great experiment, a wonderful

experiment; teaching, I learn—but I doubt if you will understand that.”



there be a slight pause. “what exactly be the nature of the

experiment?” ask Oswald modestly.



“feminine influence. dominant.”



Oswald consider. “I don’t know if you include Lady Charlotte,” he

throw out.



“oh!” say Aunt Phœbe.



“but she have play her part, I gather.”



“Feminine! no! she be completely a man-make woman. quintessentially the

Pampered Squaw. hold her position by her former charm. a Sex
