Residuum. Relict. this last outrage. an incident—merely. her course of

action be dictate for she. a man. a mere solicitor. one Grimes. the

flimsiest creature! an aspen leaf—but Male. male.”



stern thought keep Aunt Phœbe silent for a time. then she remark very

quietly, “I shake he. I shake he _well_.”



“I hope still to have the benefit of your advice,” say Oswald gravely.



“Nay,” she say. but she be pleased. “a shy comment, perhaps. but the

difference will be essential. don’t expect I to guide you as you would

wish to be guide. that phase be over between man and woman. we hand the

child over—since the law will have it so. take they!”



and then address the Weald, Aunt Phœbe, in vibrate accent, utter

a word that be to be the keynote of a decade of feminine activity.



“the vote,” say Aunt Phœbe, get a wonderful emotional buzz into her

voice. “the vo-o-o-o-o-te.”





                                  § 11



so it be that Oswald find himself fully invest with his

responsibility.



there be a terrifying suggestion in Aunt Phœbe’s manner that he would

presently have to clap Peter’s hat on, make up a small bundle of Peter’s

possession, and fare forth with he into the wide world, pick up the

convalescent at Windsor on the way, but that be a misapprehension of

Aunt Phœbe’s intention. and, after all, it be Peter’s house and garden

if it come to that. for a time at least thing could go on as they be.

but the task of direction be now fully his. whether these two young

people be properly educate or not, whether they too become slacker

and inadequate or worthy citizen of this great empire, rest now

entirely in his hand.



“they must have the good,” he say....



the good be not immediately apparent.



from Chastlands and his two room at the Climax Club Oswald conduct

his opening research for the educational good, and whenever he be at

chastland he come over nearly every day to the Ingle-Nook on his

bicycle. it be a well-remember road. Scarcely be there a turn in it

that do not recall some thought of the former time when he have ride

over daily for a sight of dolly; he would leave his bicycle in a clump

of gorse by the high road that be surely an outgrown fragment of the

old bush in which he have be willnot to leave it six year before; he

would walk down the same rusty path, and his heart would quicken as it

use to quicken at the thought of see dolly. but presently Peter

begin to oust dolly from his thought. sometimes Peter would be stand

wait for he by the high road. sometimes Peter, mount on a little

outgrown bicycle, would meet he on the purple common half way.



a man and a boy of ten be perhaps well company than a man and a boy

of fifteen. there’s so much less egotism between they. at any rate Peter

and Oswald talk of education and travel and politic and philosophy

with unembarrassed freedom. Oswald, like most childless people, have have

no suspicion of what the grey matter of a bright little boy’s brain can

hold. he be amazed at Peter’s view and curiosity. it be Oswald’s

instinct never to talk “down” to man, woman or child. he have never

think about it, but if you have question he he would have tell you

that that be the sort of thing one don’t do. and this instinct give

he a wide range of available companionship. Peter have never conceive

such good company as Oswald. you could listen to Oswald for hour. they

discourse upon every topic out of dreamland. and sometimes they come

very close even to that dreamland where Bungo Peter adventure

immortally. Oswald would feel a transfiguring presence, a touch of

fantasy and half suspect their glorious companion.



much of their talk be a kind of story-telling.



“how should we go to the Congo Forest?” Peter would ask. “would one go

by Nairobi?”



“no, that’s the other way. we’d have to go——”



and forthwith Nobby and Peter be get their stuff together and

count how many porter they would need....



“one day perhaps we’d come upon a place ’feste with crocodile,” Peter

would say.



“we would. you would be push rather ahead of the party with your

gun, look for anything there might be—push through tall reed far

above your head,” Oswald would oblige.



“you’d be with I,” insist Peter....



it be really story-telling....



it be Peter’s habit in those day when he be alone to meditate on

paper. he would cover sheet after sheet with rapidly draw scene of

adventure. one day Oswald find himself figure in one of these dream

picture. he and Peter be lead an army in battle. “Capture of Ten

War Elephants” be the legend thereon. but he realize how clearly the

small boy see he. nothing be spare of the darken, browless side of

his face with its asymmetrical glass eye, the figure of he be very

long and lean and bent, with its arm still in its old sling; and it be

draw manifestly with the utmost confidence and admiration and love....



Peter’s hostility to school be remove very slowly. the lesson at

High Cross have scar he badly, and about Miss Mills clung

association of the utmost dreariness. still it be Oswald’s instinct to

consult the young man on his destiny.



“there’s a lot you don’t know yet,” say Oswald.



“cann’t I read it out of book?” ask Peter.



“you cann’t read everything out of book,” say Oswald. “there’s thing

you ought to see and handle. and thing you can only learn by do.”



Oswald want Peter to plan his own school.



Peter consider. “I’d like lesson about the inside of animal, and

about the people in foreign country—and how engine work—and all that

sort of thing.”



“then we must find a school for you where they teach all that sort of

thing,” say Oswald, as though it be merely a question of order

good from the Civil Service Stores....



he have much to learn yet about education.





                                  § 12



but Oswald be still only face to face with the half of his

responsibility.



one morning he find Peter at the schoolroom table very busy cut big

letter out of white paper. beside he be a long strip of Turkey twill

from the dressing-up box that the Ingle-Nook have plagiarize from the

Sheldricks. “I’m get ready for Joan,” say Peter. “I’m go to put

’welcome’ on this for over the garden gate. and there’s to be a

triumphal arch.”



Hitherto Peter have scarcely betray any interest in Joan at all, now he

seem able to think of no one else, and Oswald find himself reduce

abruptly from the position of centre of Peter’s universe to a mere

helper in the decoration. but he be begin to understand the small

boy by this time, and he take the withdrawal of the limelight

philosophically.



when Aunt Phyllis and Joan arrive they find the flagged path from the

“welcome” gate festoon with chain of coloured paper (buy with

Peter’s own pocket-money and make by he and Oswald, with some slight

assistance and much moral support from Aunt Phœbe in the evening) to the

door. the triumphal arch have be achieve rather in the gothic style by

put the movable Badminton net post into a sort of trousere of

assorted oriental cloth from the dressing-up chest, and cross two

head of giant Heracleum between they. Peter stand at the door in the

white satin suit his innocent vanity love—among other rôle it have

serve for Bassanio, Prince Hal, and Antony (over the body of

Cæsar)—with a face of extraordinary solemnity. behind he stand Uncle

Nobby.



Joan ben’t quite the Joan that Peter expect. she be still wan from

her illness and she have grow several inch. she be as tall as he. and

she be white-faced, so that her hair seem blacker than ever, and her

eye be big and lustrous. she came walk slowly down the path with

her eye wide open. there be a difference, he feel, in her movement as

she come forward, though he could not have say what it be; there be

more grace in Joan now and less vigour. but it be the same Joan’s voice

that cry, “oh, Petah! it’s lovely!” she stand before he for a moment

and then throw her arm about he. she hug he and kiss he, and

Uncle Nobby know that it be the smear of High Cross School that make

he wriggle out of her embrace and not return her kiss.



but immediately he take she by the hand.



“it’s well in the playroom, Joan,” he say.



“all right, Joan, go on with he,” say Oswald, and come forward to meet

Aunt Phyllis. Aunt Phœbe be on the staircase a little aloof from these

thing, as become a woman of intellect, and behind Aunt Phyllis come

Mary, and behind Mary come the Limpsfield cabman with Aunt Phyllis’s

trunk upon his shoulder, and demolish the triumphal arch. but Peter

do not learn of that disaster until later, and then he do not mind; it

have serve its purpose.



the playroom (it be the old nursery rechristene) be indeed well. it

be all glorious with paper chain of green and white festoon from

corner to corner. on the floor to the right under the window be every

toy soldier that Peter possess draw up in review array—a gorgeous new

scot Grey band in the front that Oswald have give he. but that be

nothing. the big armchair have be draw out into the middle of the

room, and on it be _Peter’s own lion-skin_. and a piece of red

stair-carpet have be put for Joan to go up to the throne upon. and

beside the throne be a little table, and on the table be a tinsel robe

from Clarkson’s and a wonderful gilt crown and a sceptre. Oswald have

bring they along that morning.



“the crown be for _you_, Joan!” say Peter. “the sceptre be buy for

_you_.”



little white-faced Joan stand stockishly with the crown in one hand and

the sceptre in the other. “put the crown on, Joan,” say Peter. “it’s

your. it’s a rest’ration ceremony.”



but she don’t put it on.



“it’s lovely—and it’s lovely,” whisper Joan in a sort of rapture, and

stare about she incredulously with her big dark eye. it be home

again—_home_, and Mrs. Pybus have pass like an evil dream in the night.

she have never really believe it possible before that Mrs. Pybus could

pass away. even while Aunt Phyllis and Mary have be nurse she, Mrs.

Pybus have hover in the background like something more enduring,

wait for they to pass away as inexplicably as they have come. Joan have

hear the whine voice upon the stair every day and always while she

be ill, and once Mrs. Pybus have come and stand by her bedside and

remark like one who maintain an argument, “she’ll be ’appy enough

’ere when she’s well again.”



_no more Mrs. Pybus!_ no more whining scolding. no more unexpected

slap and have to go to bed supperless. no more measle and uneasy

misery in a bed with grey sheet. no more dark dreadful saying that

lurk in the mind like jungle beast. she be home, home with Peter,

out of that darkness....



and yet—outside be the darkness still....



“Joan,” say Peter, try to rouse she. “there’s a cake like a birthday

for tea....”



when Oswald come in she be still hold the gilt crown in her hand.



she let Peter take it from she and put it on her head, still stare

incredulously about she. she take the sceptre limply. Peter be almost

gentle with this strange, stare Joan.





                                  § 13



for some day Oswald regard Joan as a grave and thoughtful child. she

seem to be what country people call “old-fashioned.” she might have

be a changeling. he do not hear her laugh once. and she follow

Peter about as if she be his shadow.



then one day as he cycle over from Chastlands he hear a strange tumult

proceeding from a little field on Master’s farm, a marvellous mixture of

familiar and unfamiliar sound, an uproar, wonderful as though a

tinker’s van have meet a school treat and the twain have get drunk

together. the source of this row be hide from he by a little

coppice, and he dismount and go through the wood to investigate.

Joan and Peter have discover a disused cowshe with a sloping roof of

corrugate iron, and they have also happen upon an abandon kettle and

two or three tin can. they be now engage in hurl these latter

object on to the resonant roof, down which they roll thunderously

only to be immediately return. Joan be no long a slip of pensive

dignity, Peter be no long a marvel of intellectual curiosity. they

be both shriek their maximum. Oswald have never before suspect

Joan of an exceptionally full voice, nor Peter of so vast a wealth of

gurgle laughter. “keep the Pot-A-boilin’” yell Joan. “keep the

Pot-a-boilin’.”



“Hoo!” cry Peter. “Hoo! go it, Joan. wow!”



and then, to crown the glory, _the kettle burst_. it come into two

piece. that be too perfect! the two child stagger back. each

seize a half of the kettle and kick it deliberately. then they roll

away and fall on their stomach amidst the grass, kick their leg in

the air.



but the spirit of rowdyism grow with what it feed upon.



“oh, let’s do something _reely_ awful!” cry Joan. “let’s do something

_reely_ awful, Petah!”



Peter’s leg become still and stiff with interrogation.



“oh, Petah!” say Joan. “if I could only smash a window. frow a brick

frough a real window, a Big Glass Window. just one Glass Window.”



“_where’s_ a window?” say Peter, evidently in a highly receptive

condition.



from which pitch of depravity Oswald rouse he by a prod in the

back....





                                  § 14



but after that Joan change rapidly. colour creep back into her skin,

and a faintly rollick quality into her bearing. she become short

again and visibly sturdy, and her hair frizz more and stick out

more. her laugh and her comment upon the world become an increasingly

frequent embroidery upon the quiet of the Ingle-Nook. she seem to have

a delusion that Peter be just within earshot, but only just.



Oswald wonder how far her recent experience have vanish from her

mind. he think they might have do so altogether until one day Joan

take he into her confidence quite startlingly. he be smoke in the

little arbour, and she come and stand beside he so noiselessly that he

do not know she be there until she speak. she be hold her hand

behind she, and she be regard the South Downs with a pensive frown.

she be pay he the most beautiful compliment. she have come to

consult he.



“Mrs. Pybus say,” she remark, “that every one who don’t believe

there’s a God go straight to Hell....



“I don’t believe there’s a God,” say Joan, “and Peter _know_ there

ben’t.”



for a moment Oswald be a little take aback by this simple theology.

then he say, “D’you think Peter’s look everywhere, Joan?”



then he see the real point at issue. “one thing you may be sure about,

Joan,” he say, “and that be that there ben’t a Hell. which be rather a

pity in its way, because it would be nice to think of this Mrs. Pybus of

your go there. but there’s no Hell at all. there’s nothing more

dreadful than the dreadful thing _in_ life. there’s no need to worry

about Hell.”



that he think be fairly conclusive. but Joan remain pensive, with

her eye still on the distant hill. then she ask one of those

unanswerable child’s question that be all implication, imputation,

assumption, misunderstanding, and elision.



“but if there ben’t a Hell,” say Joan, “what do God do?”





                                  § 15



it be after Joan have drift away again from these theological

investigation that Oswald, after sit some time in silence, say

aloud and with intense conviction, “I love these child.”



he be no long a stranger in England; he have a living anchorage. he

look out over the autumnal glory of the Weald, dream intention.

these child must be educate. they must be educate splendidly.

Oswald want to see Peter serve the empire. the boy would have

pluck—he have already the lovely brain—and a sense of fun. and Joan?

Oswald be, perhaps, not quite so keen in those day upon educate

Joan. that be to come later....



after all, the empire, indeed the whole world of mankind, be make up of

Joans and Peters. what the empire be, what mankind become, be nothing

but the sum of what we have make of the Joans and Peters.









                           CHAPTER the TENTH

                      a searching of schoolmaster





                                  § 1



so it be that a systematic intention take hold of the life of Joan and

Peter. they have be snatch apart adventurously and disastrously out

of the hand of an aimless and impulsive modernism and drag off into

dusty and decay corner of the anglican system. now they be to be

rescue by this Empire worshipper, this disfigured and suffer

educational fanatic, and teach——?



what be there in Oswald’s mind? his intention be still sentimental

and cloudy, but they be begin to assume a firm and definite form.

just as the Uganda child be be make into civilized man and woman

accord to the light and mean of the Protectorate government, so

these two child have to be make fit ruler and servant of the

great empire in the world. they have to know all that a rule race

should know, they have to think and act as befit a lead people. all

this seem to he the simple and obvious necessity of the case. but he

be a sick man, fatigue much more readily than most man, give to mood

of bitter irritability; he have little knowledge of how he might set

about this task, he do not know what help be available and what be

impossible. he make enquiry and some be very absurd enquiry; he

seek advice and talk to all sort of people; and meanwhile Joan and

Peter spend a very sunny and pleasant November run wild about

Limpsfield—until one day Oswald note as much and pack they off for

the rest of the term to Miss Murgatroyd again. the School of St. George

and the Venerable Bede be concentrate upon a Christmas production of

_Alice in Wonderland_. there could not be very much bad teaching anyhow,

and there would be plenty of fun.



how be one to learn where one’s child may be educate?



this story have its comic aspect: Oswald go first to the Education

Department!



he think that if one have two rather clever and hopeful child upon

whom one be prepared to lavish time and money, an Imperial Education

Department would be able to tell an anxious guardian what school

exist for they and the respective claim and merit and

inter-relationship of such school. but he find that the government

which publish a six-inch map of the British Isles on which even the

mean outhouse be mark, have no information for the enquire parent

or guardian at all in this matter of school. an educational map have

still to become a part of the equipment of the civilized state. as it

be inconceivable that party capital could be make out of the production

of such a map, it be likely to remain a desideratum in Great Britain

for many year to come.



in an interview that remain dignified on one side at least until the

last, Oswald be refer to the advertisement column of _the Times_

and the religious and educational paper, and to—“a class of educational

_agent_,” say the official with extreme detachment. “usually, of

course, people _hear_ of school.”



so it be that England still refer back to the happy day of the

eighteenth century when our world be small enough for everybody to know

and trust and consult everybody, and tell in a safe and confidential

manner everything that matter.



“oh, my God!” groan Oswald suddenly, give way to his internal

enemy. “my God! here be two child, brilliant child—with plenty

of money to be spend on they! don’t the Empire care a twopenny damn

what become of they?”



“there be an Association of Private Schoolmasters, I believe,” say the

official, stare at he; “but I don’t know if it’s any good.”





                                  § 2



Joan be rehearse a special dance in costume and Peter be

word-perfect as the White Knight long before Oswald have find even a

hopeful school for either of they. he clung for some time to the

delusion that there must exist somewhere a school that would exactly

meet Peter’s natural and reasonable demand for an establishment where

one would learn about “gun and animal, mountain, machine and foreign

people,” that would give lesson about “the inside of animal” and “how

engine work” and “all that sort of thing.” the man want a school keep

by Leonardo da Vinci. when he find a curriculum singularly bare of

these vital matter, he begin to ask question.



his question presently develop into a very tiresome and try

catechism for Schoolmasters. he do not allow for the fact that most

private schoolmaster in England be rather overworked and rather

under-exercise man with considerable financial worry. indeed, he make

allowance for no one. he want to get on with the education of Joan

and Peter—and more particularly of Peter.



his Catechism vary considerably in detail, but always it run upon the

line of the following question.



“what sort of boy be you try to make?”



“how will he differ from an uneducated boy?”



“I don’t mean in manner, I mean how will he differ in imagination?”



“yes—I say—imagination.”



“don’t you _know_ that education be build up an imagination? I

think everybody know that.”



“then what _be_ education do?”



here usually the Catechized would become troublesome and the catechist

short and rude. the Catechism would be not so much continue as resume

after incivility and a silence.



“what sort of curriculum be my ward to go through?”



“why be he to _do_ Latin?”



“why be he to _do_ greek?”



“be he go to read or write or speak these language?”



“then what be the strange and peculiar benefit of they?”



“what will my ward know about Africa when you have do with he?”



“what will he know about India? be there any indian boy here?”



“what will he know about Garibaldi and Italy? about engineering? about

Darwin?”



“will he be able to write good English?”



“do your boy do much german? russian? spanish or Hindustani?”



“will he know anything about the way the Royal Exchange affect the

empire? but why shouldn’t he understand the elementary fact of finance

and currency? why shouldn’t every citizen understand what a pound

sterling really mean? all our everyday life depend on that. what do

you teach about Socialism? nothing! do you say nothing? but he may be a

member of Parliament some day. anyhow he’ll be a voter.”



“but if you cann’t teach he everything why not leave out these damned

classic of your?”...



the record of an irritable man seek the impossible be not to be dwelt

upon too closely. during his search for the boy’ school that have yet to

exist, Oswald give way to some unhappy impulse; he make himself

distressing and exasperate to quite a number of people. from the first

his attitude to scholastic agent be hostile and uncharitable. his

appearance make they nervous and defensive from the outset, more

particularly the fierce cocking of his hat and the red intensity of his

eye. he come in like an accusation rather than an application.



“and tell I, be these all the school there be?” he would ask,

sit with various print and copygraphed paper in his hand.



“all we can recommend,” the genteel young man in charge would say.



“all you be _pay_ to recommend?” Oswald would ask.



“they be the good school available,” the genteel young man would

fence.



“bah!” Oswald would say.



a bad opening....



from the ruffle scholastic agent Oswald would go on in a mood that be

bind to ruffle the hopeful school proprietor. indeed some of these

interview become heated so soon and so extravagantly that there be a

complete failure to state even the most elementary fact of the case.

Lurid misunderstanding blaze. Uganda get perplexingly into the

dispute. from one admirable establishment in Eastbourne Oswald retreat

with its principal call after he from his dignified portico, “I

wouldn’t take the little nigger at any price.”



when his doctor see he after this last encounter he tell he; “you be

not get on as well as you ought to do. you be run about too

much. you ought to be rest completely.”



so Oswald take a week’s rest from school visit before he try again.





                                  § 3



if it have not be for the sense of Joan and Peter grow visibly day

by day, Oswald might perhaps have display more of the patience of the

explorer. but his be rather the urgency of a thirsty traveller who

look for water than the deliberation of a trigonometrical survey. in a

little while he master the obvious fact that preparatory school be

condition by the school for which they prepare. he find a school at

Margate, White Court, which differ rather in quality, and particularly

in the quality of its proprietor, than in the nature of its arrangement

from the other school he have be visit, and to this he commit

Peter. assist by Aunt Phyllis he find an education for Joan in

Highmorton School, ten mile away; he settle himself in a furnished

house at Margate to be near they both; and having thus gain a

breathing time, he devote himself to a completer study of the

perplexing chaos of upper-class education in England. what be it “up

to”? he have his own clear conviction of what it ought to be up to, but

the more he see of exist condition, the more hopelessly it seem to

be up to either entirely different thing or else, in a spirit of

intellectual sabotage, up to nothing at all. from the preparatory

school he go on to the great public school, and from the public

school he go to the university. he bring to the quest all the

unsympathetic detachment of an alien observer and all the angry passion

of an anxious patriot. with some suggestion from Matthew Arnold.



“Indolence.” “Insincerity.” these two word become more and more

frequent in his thought as he go from one great institution to

another. occasionally the headmaster he talk to have more than a

suspicion of his unspoken comment. “their imagination be dead within

they,” say Oswald. “if only they could see the empire! if only they

could forget their little pride and dignity and affectation in the

vision of mankind!”



his impression of headmaster be for the most part take against a

background of white-flannel boy in playing-field or grey-flannel

boy in walled court-yard. Eton give he its river effect and a

bright, unforgetable boatman in a coat of wonderful blue; Harrow

display its view and insist upon its hill. physically he like

almost all the school he see, except Winchester, which he visit on a

rainy day. almost always there be fine architectural effect; now

there be a nucleus of Gothic, now it be time-wear Tudor red brick, now

well-proportion grey Georgian. Most of these establishment have the

dignity of age, but Caxton be wealthily new. Caxton be a nest of new

building of honey-colour stone; it be grow energetically but

tidily; it wave its hand to a busy wilderness of rock and plant and

say, “our botanical garden,” to a piece of field and say “our museum

group.” but it have science laboratory with big apparatus, and the

machinery for a small engineering factory. Oswald with an experienced

eye approve of its biological equipment. all these great school be

visibly full of life and activity. at time Oswald be so impressed by

this life and activity that he feel ashamed of his enquiry; it seem

ungracious not to suppose that all be go well here, that almost any

of these school be good enough and that almost any casual or

sentimental consideration, Sydenham family tradition or the like,

should suffice to determine which be to have the moulding of Peter. but

he have set his heart now on get to the very essential of this

problem; he be resolve to be blind by no fair appearance, and

though these school look as firmly rooted and stoutly prosperous as

british oak and as naturally grow as they, though they have an air of

discharge a function as necessary as the beating of a heart and as

inevitably, he still keep his grip on the idea that they be artificial

thing of man’s contrive, and still press his question: what be

you try to do? what be you do? how be you do it? how do you

fit in to the imperial scheme of thing?



so challenge these various high and headmaster have most of they the

air of man invite to talk of thing that be easy to understand than

to say. they be not at all pompous about their explanation; from

first to last Oswald never discover the pompous schoolmaster of legend

and history; without exception they seem anxious to get out of their

gown and pose as intelligent layman; but they be not intelligent

layman, they do not explain, they do not explain, they wave hand and

smile. they “hope” they be “turn out clean english gentleman.”

they don’t train their man specially to any end at all. the aim be to

develop a general intelligence, a general goodwill.



“in relation to the empire and its destiny?” say Oswald.



“I should hardly fix it so definitely as that,” say Overtone of

Hillborough.



“but don’t you set before these youngster some general aim in life to

which they be all to contribute?”



“we rather leave the sort of contribution to they,” say Overtone.



“but you must put something before they of where they be, where they

be to come in, what they belong to?” say Oswald.



“that lie in the world about they,” say Overtone. “king and country—we

don’t need to preach such thing.”



“but what the King signify—if he signify anything at all—and the aim

of the country,” urge Oswald. “and the empire! the empire—our reality.

this greatness of our beyond the sea.”



“we don’t stress it,” say Overtone. “english boy be apt to be

suspicious and ironical. have you read that delightful account of the

patriotic lecture in _stalky and co_? oh, you _should_.”



a common evasiveness characterize all these headmaster when Oswald

demand the particular of Peter’s curriculum. he want to know just

the subject Peter would study and which be to be make the most

important, and then when these question be answer he would demand:

“and why do you teach this? what be the particular benefit of that to

the boy or the empire? how do this other fit into your scheme of a

clear-minded man?” but it be difficult to get even the first question

answer plainly. from the very outset he find himself entangle in

that longstanding controversy upon the educational value of latin and

Greek. his circumstance and his disposition alike dispose he to be

sceptical of the value of these shibboleth of the british academic

world. their share in the time-table be enormous. excellent gentleman

who fail to impress he as either strong-minded or exact, seek to

convince he of the pricelessness of Latin in strengthen and

discipline the mind; hink of Carchester, the distinguished greek

scholar, slip into his hand at part a pamphlet assert that only

greek study would make a man write English beautifully and precisely.

unhappily for his argument Hinks have write his pamphlet neither

beautifully nor precisely. Lippick, irregularly bald and with neglect

tooth, a man needlessly unpleasing to the eye, descant upon the greek

spirit, and its blend of wisdom and sensuous beauty. he quote euripide

at Oswald and breathe an antique air in his face—although he know that

Oswald know practically no Greek.



“well,” say Oswald, “but compare this,” and give he back three good

minute of Swahili.



“but what do it mean? it’s gibberish to I. a certain melody perhaps.”



“in English,” Oswald grin, “you would lose it all. it be a passage

of—oh! quite fantastic beauty.”...



no argument, no apologetic, stay the deepening of Oswald’s

conviction that education in the public school of Great Britain be not

a forward-go process but a habit and tradition, that these classical

schoolmaster be say “nothing like the classic” in exactly the

same spirit that the cobbler say “nothing like leather,” because it be

the stuff they have in stock. these subject be for the most part be

slackly, tediously, and altogether badly teach to boy who find no

element of interest in they, the boy be as a class acquire a

distaste and contempt for learn thus present, and a subtle, wide

demoralization ensue. they find a justification for crib and every

possible device for shirk work in the utter remoteness and

uselessness of these main subject; the extravagant interest they take

in school game be very largely a direct consequence of their intense

boredom in school hour.



such be the impression form by Oswald. to his eye these great

school, architecturally so fine, so happy in their out-of-door aspect,

so pleasant socially, become more and more visibly whirlpool into which

the live curiosity and happy energy of the nation’s youth be draw

and catch, and fatigue, thwart, and waste. they be beautiful

shelter of intellectual laziness—from which Peter must if possible be

save.



but how to save he? there be, Oswald discover, no save he

completely. Oswald have a profound hostility to solitary education. he

know that except through accidental circumstance of the rare sort, a

private tutor must necessarily be a poor thing. a man who be cheap

enough to devote all his time to the education of one boy can have very

little that be worth impart. and education be socialization.

Education be the process of make the unsocial individual a citizen....



Oswald’s decision upon Caxton in the end, be by no mean a certificate

of perfection for Caxton. but Caxton have a good if lopsided Modern Side,

with big, businesslike chemical and physical laboratory, a quite

honest and living-look biological and geological museum, and a

pleasant and active layman as headmaster. the mathematical teaching

instead of be a drill in examination solution be carry on in

connexion with work in the physical and engineering laboratory. it be

true that the “Modern side” of Caxton teach no history of any sort,

ignore logic and philosophy, and, in the severity of its modernity,

exclude even that amount of Latin which be need for a complete

mastery of English; nevertheless it do manifestly interest its boy

enough to put game into a secondary place. at Caxton one do not see

boy play game as old lady in hydropath play patience,

desperately and excessively and with a forced enthusiasm, because they

have nothing well to do. even the Caxton school magazine do not give

much more than two-third of its space to game. so to Caxton Peter

go, when Mr. Mackinder of White Court have do his duty by he.





                                  § 4



Mr. Henderson, the creator of Caxton, be of the large sized variety of

schoolmaster, rather round-shoulder and with a slightly persecute

bearing towards parent; his mind seem busy with many

thing—building, extension, governor, chapel. Oswald walk with he

through a field that be visibly become a botanical garden, towards

the school playing-field. once the schoolmaster stop, his mind

distress by a sudden intrusive doubt whether the exactly right place

have be choose for what he call a “biological pond.” he have to ask

various question of a gardener and give certain direction. but he be

listen to Oswald, nevertheless.



Oswald discourse upon the training of what he call “the fortunate

Elite.” “we cann’t properly educate the whole of our community yet,

perhaps,” he say, “but at least these expensive boy of ours ought to

be give everything we can possibly give they. it’s to they and their

class the Empire will look. naturally. we ought to turn out boy who

know where they be in the world, what the empire be and what it aim to

do, who understand something of their responsibility to Asia and

Africa and have a philosophy of life and duty....”



“More of that sort of thing be do,” say Mr. Henderson, “than

outsider suppose. master talk to boy. lend they book.”



“in an incidental sort of way,” say Oswald. “but three-quarter of the

boy you miss.... even here, it seem, you must still have your

classical side. you must still keep on with Latin and Greek, with

course that will never reach through the dull grind to the stale old

culture beyond. why not drop all that? why not be modern outright, and

leave Eton and Harrow and Winchester and Westminster to go the old way?

why not teach modern history and modern philosophy in plain English

here? why not question the world we see, instead of the world of those

dead levantine? why not be a modern school altogether?”



the headmaster seem to consider that idea. but there be the grave

of practical objection.



“we’d get no scholarship,” he consider. “our boy would stop at a

dead end. they’d get no appointment. they’d be dreadfully

handicap....



“we’re not a complete system,” say Mr. Henderson. “no. we’re only part

of a big circle. we’ve get to take what the parent send on to we and

we’ve get to send they on to college or the profession or what not.

it’s only part of a process here—only part of a process.”...



just as the ultimate excuse of the private schoolmaster have be that

they could do no more than prepare along the line dictate for they by

the public school, so the public school wave Oswald on to the

university. thus he come presently with his question to the university,

to Oxford and Cambridge, for it be clear these set the pattern of all

the rest in England. he come to Oxford and Cambridge as he come to the

public school, it must be remember, with a fresh mind, for the navy

have snatch he straight out of his preparatory school away from the

ordinary routine of an english education at the tender age of thirteen.





                                  § 5



Oswald’s investigation of Oxford and Cambridge begin even before Peter

have enter School House at Caxton. as early as the spring of 1906, the

scarred face under the soft feel hat be to be see project from one

of those brown-colour hansom cab that use to ply in Cambridge. his

bag be on the top and he be go to the University Arms to instal

himself and have “a good look round the damned place.” at time there

still hang about Oswald a faint flavour of the midshipman on leave in a

foreign town.



he spend three day watch undergraduate, he prowl about the

street, and with his face a little on one side, bring his red-brown

eye to bear on the book in bookshop window and the display of sock

and tie and handkerchief in the outfitter. in those year the

chromatic sock be just dawn upon the adolescent mind, it have still

to achieve the iridescent glory of its crowning year. but Oswald

find it symptomatic; _ex pede Herculem_. he be to be see survey

the back, and stand about among the bookstall in the Market Place.

he paddle a canadian canoe to Byron’s pool, and watch a cheerful

group dispose of a huge tea in the garden of the inn close at hand. they

seem to joke for his benefit, neat rather than merry jesting. so that

be Cambridge, be it? then he go on by a tedious crosscountry journey

to the slack horror of one of the Oxford hotel, and make a similar

preliminary survey of the land here that he propose to prospect. there

seem to be more rubbish and more remainder in the Oxford second-hand

bookshop and less comfort in the hotel; the place be more

self-consciously picturesque, there be less of Diana and more of Venus

about its beauty, a rather blowsy Gothic Venus with a bad tooth or so.

so it impress Oswald. the glamour of Oxford, sunrise upon Magdalen

tower, Oriel, Pater, and so forth, be lose upon Oswald’s toughen

mind; he have spend his susceptible adolescence on a battleship, and the

sunblaze of Africa have give he a taste for colour like a taste for raw

rye whiskey....



he walk about the perfect garden of St. Giles’ College and beat at the

head of Blepp, the senior tutor, whose acquaintance he have make in the

Athenaeum, with his stock question. the garden of St. Giles’ college be

as delicate as fine linen in lavender; its turf be suppose to make

american visitor regret the ancestral trip in the _mayflow_ very

bitterly; Blepp have fancy that in a way it answer Oswald. but Oswald

turn his glass eye and his ugly side to the garden, it might just as

well have not be there, and keep to his questioning; “what be we

make of our boy here? what be they go to make of the empire? what

be you teach they? what be you not teach they? how be you

work they? and why? why? what’s the idea of it all? suppose presently

when this fine October in history end, that the weather of the world

break up; what will you have ready for the storm?”



Blepp feel the ungraciousness of such behaviour acutely. it be like

suddenly ask the host of some great beautiful dinner-party whether he

earn his income honestly. like shout it up the table at he. but

Oswald be almost as comfortable a guest for a don to entertain as a

spur in one’s trouser pocket. Blepp do his good to temper the occasion

by an elaborate sweet reasonableness.



“don’t you think there’s something in our atmosphere?” he begin.



“I don’t like your atmosphere. the Oxford shop seem grubby little

shop. the street be narrow and badly light.”



“I ben’t think of the shop.”



“it’s where the youngster buy their stuff, their furniture, and as far

as I can see, most of their idea.”



“you’ll be in sympathy with the american lady who complain the other

day about our want of bathroom,” Blepp sneer.



“well, _why not_?” say Oswald outrageously.



Blepp shrug his shoulder and look for sympathy at the twisted

brick chimney of St. Giles’.



Oswald become jerkily eloquent. “we’ve get an empire sprawl all over

the world. we’re a people at grip with all mankind. and in a few year

these few thousand man here and at Cambridge and a few thousand in the

other university, have practically to be the mind of the empire. think

of the problem that press upon we as an empire. all the nation sharpen

themselves now like knife. be we make the mentality to solve the

irish riddle here? be we prepare any outlook for India here? what be

you do here to get ready for such task as these?”



“how can I show you the reality that go on beneath the surface?” say

Blepp. “you don’t see what be brew today, the talk that go on in

the man’s room, the mutual polishing of mind. look not at our formal

life but our informal life. consider one college, consider for example

Balliol. think of the Jowett influence, the Milner group—not blind to

the empire there, be we? even that fellow Belloc. a saucy rogue, but

good rich stuff. all out of just one college. these be thing one

cannot put in a syllabus. these be thing that defeat statistic.”



“but that be no reason why you should put chaff and dry bone into the

syllabus,” say Oswald....



“this place,” say Oswald, and wave his arm at the great serenity of

St. Giles’, “it have the air of a cathedral close. it might be a

beautiful place of retirement for sad and weary old man. it seem a

thousand mile from machinery, from great town and the work of the

world.”



“would you have we teach in a foundry?”



“I’d have you teach something about the storm that seem to I to be

gather in the world of labour. these youngster here be go to be

the statesman, the writer and teacher, the lawyer, the high

official, the big employer, of tomorrow. but all that world of

industry they have to control seem as far off here as if it be on

another planet. you’re not talk about it, you’re not think about

it. you’re teach about the Gracchi and the greek fig trade. you’re

magnify that pompous bore Cicero and minimizing—old Salisbury for

example—who be a far more important figure in history—a great man in

a great world.”



“with all respect to his memory,” say Blepp, “but _good Lord_!”



“much great. your classic put out your perspective. dozen of live

statesman be great than Cicero. of course our modern be great. if

only because of the greatness of our horizon. Oxford and Cambridge

ought to be the learning and think part of the whole empire, twin

hemisphere in the imperial brain. but when I think of the size of the

imperial body, its hundred of nation, its thousand of city, its

tribe, its vast extension round and about the world, the immense

problem of it, and then of the size and quality of _this_, I’m remind

of the Atlantosaurus. you’ve hear of the beast? its brain be small

than the ganglia of its rump. no doubt its brain think itself quite up

to its job. it ben’t. something eat up the Atlantosaurus. these two

place, this place, ought to be big enough, and bigly conceive enough,

to irradiate our whole world with idea. all the empire. they ought to

dominate the mind of hundred of million of man. and they dominate

nothing. leave India and Africa out of it. they do not even dominate

England. think only of your labour at home, of that huge blind Titan,

whom you willn’t understand, which don’t understand you——”



“there again,” interrupt Blepp sharply, “you be simply ignorant of

what be go on here. because Oxford have a certain traditional beauty

and a decent respect for the past, because it don’t pose and assert

itself rawly, you be offend. you do not realize how active we can be,

how up-to-date we be. it wouldn’t make we more modern in spirit if we

live in enamel bathroom and lecture in corrugated iron shed. that

ben’t modernity. that’s your mistake. in respect to this very question

of labour, we _have_ get our labour contact. have you never hear of

Ruskin College? found here by an American of the most modern type, one

Vrooman.” he repeat the name “Vrooman,” not as though he love it but

as though he think it ought to appeal to Oswald. “I think he come from

Chicago.” surely a teutonic name from Chicago be modern enough to

satisfy any one! “it be a college of real working-man, of the Trade

Union leader type, the actual horny-handed article, who come up here—I

suppose because they don’t agree with your idea that we deal only in the

swathing of mummy. they at any rate think that we have something to

tell the modern world, something worth their learning. perhaps they know

their need well than you do.”



Oswald be momentarily abash. he express a desire to visit this

Ruskin College.



Blepp explain he be not himself connect with the college. “not

quite my line,” say Blepp parenthetically; but he could arrange for a

visit under proper guidance, and presently under the wing of a don of

radical tendency Oswald go.



it seem to he the most touching and illuminate thing in Oxford. it

remind he of _Jude the Obscure_.



Ruskin College be shelter over some stable in a back street, and

it display a small group of oldish young man, for the most part

with north-country accent, engage in live under austere

circumstance—they pay scarcely anything and do all the

housework—and do their good to get hold of the precious treasure

of knowledge and understanding they be persuade Oxford possess.

they have come up on their saving by virtue of extraordinary

sacrifice. graduation in any of the Oxford school be manifestly

impossible to they, if only on account of the greek bar; the

university have no use for these respectful pilgrim and no intention

of encourage more of they, and the “principal,” Mr. Dennis Hird,

in the tooth of much opposition, be vamp a sort of course for

they with the aid of a few liberal-minded junior don who deliver

a lecture when their proper engagement permit. there be a vague

suggestion of perplexity in the conversation of the two student

with whom Oswald talk. this tepid drip of disconnected instruction

ben’t what they have expect, but then, what have they expect?

Vrooman, the idealist who have set the thing go, have return to

America leave much to be explain. Oswald dine with Blepp at St.

Osyth’s that night, and speak over the port in the common room of

these work man who be “dun Oxford for wisdom.”



Jarlow, the wit of the college, who have be entertain the company

with the last half-dozen Spoonerisms he have invent, be at once

remind of a little poem he have make, and he recite it. it be

suppose to be by one of these same Ruskin College man, and his artless

rhyming of “Socrates” and “fate” and “side” and “euripide,” combine

with a sort of modest pretentiousness of thought and intention, be very

laughable indeed. everybody laugh merrily except Oswald.



“that’s quite one of your good, Jarlow,” say Blepp.



but Oxford have be rub Oswald’s fur backwards that day. the common

room become aware of he sit up stiffly and regard Jarlow with an

evil expression.



“why the Devil,” say Oswald, address himself pointedly and

querulously to Jarlow, “shouldn’t a work-man say ’So-_crates_?’ we

all say ’Paris.’ these man do Oxford too much honour.”





                                  § 6



perhaps there be a sort of necessity in the educational stagnation of

England during those crucial year before the Great War. all the

influential and important people of the country be have a thoroughly

good time, and if there be a grow quarrel between worker and

employer no one see any reason in that for stick a goad into the

teacher. the disposition of the mass of man be always on the side of

custom against innovation. the clear-headed effort of yesterday tend

always to become the unintelligent routine of tomorrow. so long as we

get along we go along. in the less exacting day of good Queen Victoria

the educational process of Great Britain have serve well enough; they

still go on because the necessity for a more thorough, coherent, and

lucid education have still to be make glaringly manifest. few people

understand the discontent of a Ray Lankester, the fretfulness of a

Kipling. foresight die when the imagination slumber. only catastrophe

can convince the mass of people of the possibility of catastrophe. the

system have the inertia of a spinning top. the most thoroughly and

completely mis-taught of one generation become the mis-teacher of the

next. “learn, obey, create nothing, initiate nothing, have no

troublesome doubt,” run the rule of scholarly discretion. “prize-boy,

scholar, fellow, don, pedagogue; prize-boy, scholar, fellow, don”—so

spin the circle of the school. into that relentless circle the bright,

curious little Peters, who want to know about the inside of animal

and the way of machine and what be happen, be draw; the little

Joans, too, be be draw. the good escape complete deadening, they

find a use for themselves, but life usually keep they too busy and use

they too hard for they ever to return to teach in college or school of

the reality they have experience. and so as Joan and Peter grow up,

Oswald become more and more tolerant of a certain rabble rout of inky

outsider who, without authority and dignity, be at least put

live idea of social function and relationship in the way of

adolescent inquiry.



it become manifest to Oswald that the real work of high education, the

discussion of God, of the state and of sex, of all the great issue in

life, while it be be elaborately evade in the formal education of

the country, be to a certain extent be do, thinly,

unsatisfactorily, pervertedly even by the talk of boy and girl among

themselves, by the casual suggestion of tutor, friend, and chance

acquaintance, and more particularly by a number of irresponsible

journalist and literary man. for example though the high education of

the country afford no comprehensive view of social inter-relationship

at all, the propaganda of the socialist do give a scheme—Oswald

think it be a mistaken and wrong-head scheme—of economic

interdependence. if the school show nothing to their child of the

Empire but a few tiresome map, Kipling’s story, for all his Jingo

violence, do at least breathe something of its live spirit. as Joan

and Peter grow up they ferret out and bring to their guardian’s

knowledge a school of irresponsible contemporary teacher, Shaw, Wells

and the other Fabian Society pamphleteer, the Belloc-Chesterton group,

Cunninghame Graham, Edward Carpenter, Orage of _the New Age_,

Galsworthy, Cannan; the suffragette, and the like. if the formal

teacher lack boldness these strange self-appoint instructor seem

to be nothing if not bold. _the Freewoman_, which die to rise again as

_the New Freewoman_, exist it seem chiefly to mention everything

that a young lady should never dream of mention. Aunt Phœbe’s

monthly, _wayleave_, in its green and purple cover, make a gallant

effort to outdo that valiant weekly. Aunt Phœbe be a bright and

irresponsible assistant in the education of Oswald’s ward. she sow

the house with strange book whenever she come to stay with they. Oswald

find Joan read Oscar Wilde when she be seventeen. he do not

interrupt her reading, for he could not imagine how to set about the

interruption. later on he discover a most extraordinary volume by

Havelock Ellis lie in the library, an impossible volume. he read in it

a little and then put it down. afterwards he could not believe that book

exist. he think he must have dream about it, or dream the content

into it. it seem incredible that Aunt Phœbe——!... he be never quite

sure. when he go to look for it again it have vanish, and he do not

like to ask for it.



More and more do this outside supplement of education in England press

upon Oswald’s reluctant attention. Most of these irregular he dislike

by nature and tradition. none of they have the dignity and restraint of

the great Victorians, the corinthian elegance of Ruskin, the Teutonic

hammer-blow of Carlyle. Shaw he understand be a lean, red-haired

Pantaloon, terribly garrulous and vain; Belloc and Chesterton thrust a

shameless obesity upon the public attention; the social origin of most

of the crew be appalling, Bennett be a solicitor’s clerk from the

pottery, Wells a counter-jumper, Orage come from Leeds. Oswald have

see a picture of Wells by Max that confirm his bad suspicion about

these people; a heavy bang of hair assist a cascade moustache to veil

a pasty face that be broad rather than long and with a sly, conceite

expression; the creature still wear a long and crumpled frock coat,

acquire no doubt during his commercial phase, and rub together two

large, clammy, white, misshapen hand. except for Cunninghame Graham

there be not a gentleman, as Oswald understand the word, among they

all. but these writer get hold of the intelligent young because they

do at least write freely where the university teacher fear to tread.

they write, he think, without any decent restraint. they season even

wholesome suggestion with a flavour of scandalous excitement. it

remain an open question in his mind whether they do more good by

make young people think or more harm by make they think wrong.

progressive don he find maintain the former opinion. with that

support Oswald be able to follow his natural disposition and leave the

reading of his two ward unrestraine.



and they read—and think, to such purpose as will be presently tell.





                                  § 7



but here Justice demand an interlude.



before we go on to tell of how Joan and Peter grow up to adolescence in

these school that Oswald—assist by Aunt Phyllis in the case of

Joan—find for they, Mr. Mackinder must have his say, and make the

Apology of the Schoolmaster. he make it to Oswald when first Oswald

visit he and choose his school out of all the other preparatory

school, to be Peter’s. he appear as a little brown man with a

hedgehog’s nose and much of the hedgehog’s indignant note in his voice.

he come, shy and hostile, into the drawing-room in which Oswald await

he. it be, by the by, the most drawing-room-like drawing-room that

Oswald have ever be in; it be as if some one have say to a furniture

dealer, “People expect I to have a drawing-room. please let I have

exactly the sort of drawing-room that people expect.” it display a

grand piano towards the french window, a large standard lamp with an

enormous shade, a pale silk sofa, an Ottoman, a big fern in an ornate

pot, and water-colour of venetian lagoon. in the midst of it all stand

Mr. Mackinder, in a highly contract state, mutely radiate an

interrogative “well?”



“I’m look for a school for my nephew,” say Oswald.



“you want he here?”



“well— do you mind if first of all I see something of the school?”



“we’re always open to investigation,” say Mr. Mackinder, bitterly.



“I want to do the very good I can for this boy. I feel very strongly

that it’s my duty to he and the country to turn he out—as well as a

boy can be turn out.”



Mr. Mackinder nod his head and continue to listen.



this be something new in private schoolmaster. for the most part they

have open themselves out to Oswald, like sunflower, like the receptive

throat of nestling. they have embrace and silence he by the wealth

of their assurance.



“I have two little ward,” he say. “a boy and a girl. I want to make

all I can of they. they ought to belong to the Elite. the strength of a

country—of an empire—depend ultimately almost entirely on its Elite.

this empire ben’t overwhelm with intelligence and most of the talk we

hear about the tradition of statesmanship——”



Mr. Mackinder make a short snort noise through his nose that seem

to indicate his opinion of contemporary statesmanship.



“you see I take this schooling business very solemnly. these upper-class

school, I say, these school for the son of prosperous people and

scholarship winner, be really Elite-make machine. they really

make—or fail to make—the empire. that make I go about ask

schoolmaster a string of question. some of they don’t like my

question. perhaps they be too elementary. I ask: what be this

education of yours up to? what be the design of the whole? what be this

preparation of yours for? this be call a Preparatory School. you lay

the foundation. what be the design of the building for which these

foundation be lay?”



he pause, determine to make Mr. Mackinder say something before he

discourse far.



“it ben’t so simple as that,” be wring from Mr. Mackinder. “suppose we

just walk round the school. suppose we just see the sort of place it be

and what we be do here. then perhaps you’ll be able to see well

what we contribute—in the way of make a citizen.”



the inspection be an unusually satisfactory one. White Court be one of

the few private school Oswald have see that have be build expressly

for its purpose. its class room be well light and well arrange, its

little science museum seem good and well arrange and well provide

with diagram; its gymnasium be businesslike; its wall blackboard

unusually abundant and generously use, and everything be tidy.

nevertheless the Catechism for Schoolmasters be not spare. “now,” say

Oswald, “now for the curriculum?”



“we live in the same world with most other english school,” Mr.

Mackinder sulk. “this be a preparatory school.”



“what be call english subject?”



“yes.”



“how do you teach geography?”



“with book and map.”



Oswald speak of lantern slide and museum visit. the cinema have yet to

become an educational possibility.



“I do what I can,” say Mr. Mackinder; “I’m not a millionaire.”



“do you _do_ classic?”



“we do Latin. clever boy do a little greek. in preparation for the

public school.”



“Grammar of course?...”



“what else?...”



“french, german, Latin, Greek, bit of mathematic, botany, geography,

bit of history, book-keeping, music lesson, some water-colour

painting; it’s very mixed,” say Oswald.



“it’s miscellaneous.”



Mr. Mackinder rouse himself to a word of defence: “the boy don’t

specialize.”



“but this be a diet of scrap,” say Oswald, revive one of the most

controversial topic of the catechism. “nothing can be do thoroughly.”



“we be necessarily elementary.”



“it’s rather like the White Knight in _Alice in Wonderland_ pack his

luggage for nowhere.”



“we have to teach what be require of we,” say Mr. Mackinder.



“but what _be_ education up to?” ask Oswald.



as Mr. Mackinder offer no answer to that riddle, Oswald go on. “what

be Education in England up to, anyhow? in Uganda we know what we be

do. there be an idea in it. the old native tradition be break

up. we teach they to count and reckon english fashion, to read and

write, we give they book and the christian element, so that they could

join on to our civilization and play a part in the great world that be

break up their little world. we don’t teach they anything that

don’t serve mind or soul or body. we see the end of what we be do.

but half this school teaching of yours be like teach in a dream. you

don’t teach the boy what he want to know and need to know. you spend

half his time on calculation he have no use for, mere formal

calculation, and on this dead language stuff——! it’s like try to

graft mummy steak on live flesh. it’s like boil fossil for soup.”



Mr. Mackinder say nothing.



“and damn it!” say Oswald petulantly; “your school be about as good a

school as I’ve see or be likely to see....



“I have an idea,” he go on, “of just get the very good out of those

two youngster—the boy especially—of make every hour of his school

work a gift of so much power or skill or subtlety, of open the world

to he like a magic book.... the boy’s tug at the magic cover....”



he stop short.



“there be no such school,” say Mr. Mackinder compactly. “this be as

good a school as you will find.”



and there he leave the matter for the time. but in the evening he dine

with Oswald at his hotel, and it may be that iced champagne have

something to do with a certain relaxation from his afternoon restraint.

Oswald have already arrange about Peter, but he want the little man to

talk more. so he set he an example. he talk of his own life. he

represent it as a life of disappointment and futility. “I envy you

your life of steadfast usefulness.” he speak of his truncated naval

career and his disfigurement. of the year of uncertainty that have

follow. he talk of the ambition and achievement of other man, of

the large hope and ambition of youth.



“I too,” say Mr. Mackinder, warm for a moment, and then leave his

sentence unfinishe. Oswald continue to generalize....



“all life, I suppose, be disappointment—be anyhow largely

disappointment,” say Mr. Mackinder presently.



“we get something do.”



“five per cent., ten per cent., of what we mean to do.”



the schoolmaster reflect. Oswald refill his glass for he.



“to begin with I think, none of these other fellow really know how to

run a school. I will, I say, make a nest of Young Paragons. I will take

a bunch of boy and get the good out of they, the good possible; watch

they, study they, foster they, make a sort of boy so that the White

Court brand shall be look for and recognize....”



he sip his faintly seethe wine and put down the glass.



“five per cent.,” he say; “ten per cent., perhaps.” he touch his lip

with his dinner-napkin. “I have turn out some creditable boy.”



“do you make any experiment in the subject you teach?”



“at first. but one of the thing we discover in life as we grow past the

first flush of beginning, be just how severely we be condition. we

be condition. we seem to be free. and we be in a net. you have

criticize my curriculum today pretty severely, Mr. Sydenham. much that

you say be absolutely right. it be wasteful, discursive, ineffective.

yes.... but in my place I doubt if you could have make it much other

than it be....



“one or two thing I do. latin grammar here be teach on line strictly

parallel with the English and french and German—that be to say, we teach

language comparatively. it be troublesome to arrange, but it make a

difference mentally. and I take a class in Formal Logic; English

teaching be imperfect, expression be slovenly, without that. the boy

write english verse. the mathematical teaching too, be as modern as the

examine board will let it be. small thing, perhaps. but you do not

know the obstacle.



“Mr. Sydenham, your talk today have remind I of all the magnificent

thing I set out to do at White Court, when I sink my capital in

build White Court six and twenty year ago. when I find that I

couldn’t control the choice of subject, when I find that in that

matter I be rule by the sort of school and college the boy have to

go on to and by the preposterous examination they would have to pass,

then I tell myself, ’at least I can cultivate their character and

develop something like a soul in they, instead of crush out

individuality and imagination as most school do....’



“well, I think I have a house of clean-minded and cheerful and willing

boy, and I think they all tell the truth....”



“I don’t know what I’m to do with the religious teaching of these two

youngster of mine,” say Oswald abruptly. “practically, they’re

godless.”



Mr. Mackinder do not speak for a little while. then he say, “it be

almost unavoidable, under existing condition, that the religious

teaching in a school should be—formal and orthodox.



“for my own part—I’m liberal,” say Mr. Mackinder, and add, “very

liberal. let I tell you, Mr. Sydenham, exactly how I see thing.”



he pause for a moment as if he collect his view.



“if a little boy have grow up in a home, in the sort of home which one

might describe as God-fear, if he have not only hear of God but see

God as a live influence upon the people about he, then—then, I admit,

you have something real. he will believe in God. he will know God.

God—simply because of the faith about he—will be a knowable reality.

God be a faith. in man. such a boy’s world will fall into shape about

the idea of God. he will take God as a matter of course. such a boy can

be religious from childhood—yes.... but there be very few such home.”



“Less, probably, than there use to be?”



Mr. Mackinder disavow an answer by a gesture of hand and shoulder.

he go on, frown slightly as he talk. he want to say exactly

what he think. “for all other boy, Mr. Sydenham, God, for all

practical purpose, do not exist. their world have be make without

he; they do not think in term of he; and if he be to come into their

life at all he must come in from the outside—a discovery, like a mighty

rush wind. by what be call Conversion. at adolescence. until that

happen you must build the soul on pride, on honour, on the decent

instinct. it be all you have. and the less they hear about God the

well. they will not understand. it will be a canot to they—a kind of

indelicacy. the two great thing in the world have be the most

vulgarize. God and sex.... if I have my own way I would have no

religious service for my boy at all.”



“instead of which?”



Mr. Mackinder pause impressively before reply.



“the local curate be prepare two of my eld boy for Confirmation at

the present time.”



he gaze gloomily at the tablecloth. “if one could do as one like!” he

say. “if only one could do as one like!”



but now Oswald be realize for the first time the eternal tragedy of

the teacher, that sower of unseen harvest, that reaper of thistle and

the wind, that serf of custom, that subjugate rebel, that feeble,

persistent antagonist of the triumphant thing that rule he. and behind

that immediate tragedy Oswald be now apprehend for the first time

something more universally tragic, an incessantly recur story of

high hope and a grey end; the story of boy and girl, clean and

sweet-minded, grow up into life, and of the victory of world inertia,

of custom drift and the tarnishing year.



Mr. Mackinder speak of his own youth. quite early in life have come

physical humiliation, the realization that his slender and delicate

physique debar he from most active occupation, and his resolve to

be of use in some field where his weak and undersized body would be at

no great disadvantage. “I make up my mind that teaching should be my

religion,” he say.



he tell of the difficulty he have encounter in his attempt to get

any pedagogic science or training. “this be the most difficult

profession in the world,” he say, “and the most important. yet it be

not study; it have no establish practice; it be not endow.

building be endow and institution, but not teacher.” and in Great

Britain, in the school of the class that will own and rule the

country, ninety-nine per cent. of the work be do by unskilled

workman, by low-grade, genteel woman and young man. in America the

teacher be nearly all woman. “how can we expect to raise a nation

nearly as good as we might do under such a handicap?” he have read and

learn what he could about teaching; he have serve for small salary in

school that seem live and efficient; finally he have build his own

school with his own money. he have have the dire difficulty in get

a staff together. “what can one expect?” he say. “we pay they hardly

well than shop assistant—less than bank clerk. you see the relative

importance of thing in the british mind.” what hope or pride be there

to inspire an assistant schoolmaster to do good work?



“I think I could make a school different from all other school, and I

find I have to make a school like most other fairly good school. I have

to work for what the parent require of I, and the idea of the

parent have be shape by their school. I have never dream of the

immensity of the resistance these would offer to constructive change. in

this world there be incessant change, but most of they be landslide

or epidemic.... I try to get away from stereotype examination. I

couldn’t. I try to get away from formal soul-destroy religion. I

couldn’t. I try to get a staff of real assistant. I couldn’t. I have

to take what come. I have to be what be require of I....



“one work against time always. over against the Parents. it be not only

the boy one must educate, but the parent—let alone one’s self. the

parent demand impossible thing. I have be ask for Greek and for

book-keeping by double-entry by the same parent. I have—I have to leave

the matter—as if I think such thing be possible. after all, the

Parent be master. one cann’t run a school without boy.”



“you’d get _some_ boy,” say Oswald.



“not enough. I’m up against time. the school have to pay.”



“cann’t you hold out for a time? run the school on a handful of oatmeal?”



“it’s run it on an overdraft I don’t fancy. you’re not a married

man, Mr. Sydenham, with son to consider.”



“no,” say Oswald shortly. “but I have these ward. and, after all,

there’s not only today but tomorrow. if the world be go wrong for

want of education——. if you don’t give it your son will suffer.”



“tomorrow, perhaps. but today come first. I’m up against time. oh, I’m

up against time.”



he sit with his hand hold out supine on the table before he.



“I start my school twenty-seven year ago next Hilary. and it seem

like yesterday. when I start it I mean it to be something memorable

in school.... I jump into it. I think I should swim about.... it

be like jump into the rapid of Niagara. I be seize, I be rush

along.... ai! ai!...”



“time’s against we all,” say Oswald. “I suppose the next glacial age

will overtake we long before we’re ready to fight out our destiny.”



“if you want to feel the generation rush to waste,” say Mr.

Mackinder, “like rapid—like rapid—you must put your heart and life

into a private school.”









                          CHAPTER the ELEVENTH

                              ADOLESCENCE





                                  § 1



“the generation rush to waste like rapid—like rapid....”



ten year later Oswald find himself repeat the word of the little

private schoolmaster.



he be in the grave perplexity. Joan be now nineteen and a half and

Peter almost of age, and they have have a violent quarrel. they would not

live in the same house together any long, they declare. Peter have

go back overnight to Cambridge on his motor bicycle; Joan’s be out of

order—an embitter addition to her distress—and she have cycle on her

push bicycle over the hill that morning to Bishop’s Stortford to catch

the Cambridge train. and Oswald be leave to think over the situation and

all that have lead to it.



he sit alone in the May sunshine in the little arbour that overlook

his rose garden at Pelham Ford, try to grasp all that have happen to

these stormy young people since he have so boldly take the care of their

life into his hand. he find himself try to retrace the phase of

their upbringing, and his thought go wide and far over the problem of

human training. suddenly he have discover his charge adult. Joan have

stand before he, amazingly grow up—a woman, young, beautiful,

indignant.



who could have foretell ten year ago that Joan would have be

declare with tear in her voice but much stiffness in her manner, that

she have “stand enough” from Peter, and call he “weak.”



“he insult all my friend, Nobby,” she have say, “and as for his——.

he’s like that puppy we have who dig up rotten bone we have never

suspect, all over the garden.



“oh! _his woman be horrible_!” Joan have cry....





                                  § 2



Oswald’s choice of a permanent home at Pelham Ford have be largely

determine by the educational requirement of Joan and Peter. while

Peter have be at White Court and Joan at Highmorton School twelve mile

away, Oswald have occupy a not very well furnish “furnish house” at

Margate. when Peter, after an inquisition by Oswald into English Public

Schools, have be award at last as a sort of prize, with reservation,

to Caxton, Oswald—convince now by his doctor and his own disagreeable

experience that he must live in England for the rest of his life if he

be to hope for any comfort or activity—decide to set up a permanent

home with a garden and building that would be helpful through day of

dullness in some position reasonably accessible from London, Caxton and

Margate, and later on from Cambridge, to which they be both

predestine. after some search he find the house he need in the

pretty little valley of the Rash, that run north-eastward from Ware.

the Stubland aunt still remain as tenant of the Ingle-Nook, and make

it a sort of alternative home for the youngster.



the country to the north and east of Ware be a country of miniature

gorge with frequent water-splash. the stream widen and cross the

road in a broad, pebbly shallow of ripple just at the end of Pelham

Ford, there be a causeway with a white handrail for bicycle and foot

passenger beside the ford, and beyond it be an inn and the post office

and such thatch, whitewash home as constitute the village. then

beyond come a row of big tree and the high red wall and iron gate of

this house Oswald have take. the church of Pelham Ford be a little

humped, spireless build up the hill to the left. the stream brawl

along for a time beside the road. through the gate of the house one

look across a lawn bar by the shadow of big tree, at a blaze

flower-garden that go up a series of terrace to the little red tile

summerhouse that command the view of the valley. the house be to the

right and near the road, a square comfortable eighteenth-century

red-brick house with ivy on its shadowed side and fig tree and rise

tree towards the sun. it have a classical portico, and a grave but

friendly expression.



the Margate house have be a camp, but this be furnish with some

deliberation. Oswald have leave a miscellany of possession behind he in

Uganda which Muir have pack and send on after he when it be settle

that there could be no return to Africa. the hall befit the home of a

member of the Plantain Club; African spoil adorn it, three lion’

head, a white rhinoceros head, elephant’ foot, spear, gourd, tusk;

in the midst a large table take the visitor’s hat and stick, and bear a

large box for the post. out of this hall open a little close study

Oswald rarely use except when Joan and Peter and their friend be at

home and a passage lead to a sunny, golden-brown library possess three

large southward window on the garden, a room it have please he greatly

to furnish, and in which he do most of his writing. it have a parquet

floor and oriental rug like sunlit flower-bed. across the hall,

opposite the study, be a sort of sittingroom-livingroom which be give

over to Joan and Peter. it have be call the Schoolroom in the day

when their holiday visit have be mitigate by the presence of some

temporary governess or tutor, and now that those discipline day be

over their two develop personality still jostle in the one

apartment. a large pleasant drawing-room and a dining-room complete the

tale of room on the ground floor.



in this room across the hall there be much that would have repay

research on the part of Oswald. the room be a joint room only when Joan

and Peter be without guest in the house. whenever there be guest,

whether they be woman or man, Joan turn out and the room become a

refuge or rendezvous for Peter. it be therefore rather Peter’s than

Joan’s. here as in most thing it be Peter’s habit to prevail over

Joan. but she have her right; she have have a voice in the room’s

decoration, a share in its disorder. the upper bookshelf to the right

of the fireplace be hers and the wall next to that. against this stand

her bureau, locked and secure, over and against Peter’s bureau. Oswald

have give they these writing desk three christmas ago. but the mess

on the table under the window be Peter’s, and Peter have more than his

fair share of the wall. the stuff bird and animal and a row of

scull be the result of a “mooseum” phase of Peter’s when he be

fourteen. the water-colour picture be Peter’s. the hearthrug be the

lion-skin that Peter still believe have be bring for he from

Nairobi by Oswald.



Peter could caricature, and his good effort be frame here; his style

be a deliberate compliment to the incomparable Max. he have be very

successful twice in bring out the latent fierceness of Joan; one not

ungraceful effort be call “the Scalp Dance,” the other, less pleasing

to its subject, represent Joan in full face with her hand behind she

back and her foot apart, “tell the Whole Troof.” Joan, alas! have no

correspond skill for a retort, but she have frame an enlargement of a

happy snap-shot of Peter on the garden wall. she have stand below and

hold her camera up so that Peter’s boot and leg be immense and his

head dwindle to nothing in perspective. so see, he become an

embodiment of masculine brutality. the legend be, “the Camera can

detect what our eye cannot.”



one corner of this room be occupy by a pianola piano and a large

untidy collection of classical music roll; right and leave of the

fireplace the bookshelf bear an assortment of such literature as

appeal in those day to animate youth, classic of every period from

Plato to Shaw, and such modern as Compton Mackenzie, Masefield, Gilbert

Cannan and Ezra Pound. back number of _the Freewoman_, _the New Age_,

_the New Statesman_, and _the Poetry Review_ mingle on the low

shelf. there be a neat row of philosophical textbook in the Joan

section; Joan for no particular reason be take the moral science

tripos; and a microscope stand on Peter’s table, for he be

biological....





                                  § 3



Oswald’s domestic arrangement have at first be a grave perplexity. in

Uganda he have keep house very well with a Swahili over-man and a number

of “boy”; in Margate this sort of service be difficult to obtain, and

the holiday need of the child seem to demand a feminine influence

of the governess-companion type, a “lady.” a succession of refined

feminine personality have intersect these year of Oswald’s life.

they be all lady by birth and profession, they all wear collar

support by whalebone about their neck, and they all develop and

betray a tenderness for Oswald that lead to a series of flight to the
