Climax Club and firm but generous dismissal. Oswald’s idea of

matrimony be crude and commonplace; he could imagine himself marry

no one but a buxom young woman of three-and-twenty, and he could not

imagine any buxom young woman of three-and-twenty take a healthy

interest in a man over forty with only half a face and fit of fever and

fretfulness. when these lady one after another throw out their gentle

intimation he have the ingratitude to ascribe their courage to a sense

of his own depreciate matrimonial value. this cause just enough

indignation to nerve he to the act of dismissal. but on each occasion

he spend the good part of a morning and make serious inroad upon the

club notepaper before the letter of dismissal be frame, and he always

fall back upon the stock lie that he be go abroad to a Kur-Ort and

be go to lock up the house. on each occasion the house be lock up

for three or four week, and Oswald live a nomadic existence until a

fresh lady could be find. finally God send he Mrs. Moxton.



she come in at Margate during an interregnum while Aunt Phyllis be in

control. Aunt Phyllis after a reflective interview pass she on to

Oswald. she be more like Britannia than one could have imagine

possible; her face be perhaps a little long and calm and her pink

chin rather more numerous.



“I understand,” she say, seat herself against Oswald’s desk, “that

you be in need of some one to take charge of your household.”



“do you—hear?” begin Oswald.



“it’s the talk of Margate,” she say calmly.



“so I understand that you be prepared to be the lady——”



“I be _not_ a lady,” say Mrs. Moxton with a faint asperity.



“I beg your pardon,” say Oswald.



“I be a housekeeper,” she say, as who should say: “at least give I

credit for that.” “I have have experience with a single gentleman.”



there seem to be an idea in it.



“I be housekeeper to the late Mr. Justice Benlees for some year, until

he die, and then unhappily, be in receipt of a small pension from

he, I take to keep a boarding-house. Winnipeg House. on the Marine

Parade. a most unpleasant and anxious experience.” her note of

indignation return, and the clear pink of her complexion deepen by a

shade. “a torrent of Common People.”



“exactly,” say Oswald. “I have see they walk about the town.

beastly new yellow boot. and fast, squeaky little girl in those new

floppy white hat. you think you could dispose of the boarding-house?”



Mrs. Moxton compress her chin slightly in assent.



“it’s a saleable concern?”



“there be those,” say Mrs. Moxton with a faint sense of the marvel of

God’s universe in her voice, “who would be glad of it.”



he rest his face on his hand and regard her profile very earnestly

with his one red-brown eye—from the beginning to the end of the

interview Mrs. Moxton never once look straight at he. he perceive

that she be incapable of tenderness, dissimulation, or any personal

relationship, a woman in profile, a woman with a pride in her work, a

woman to be trust.



“you’ll _do_,” he say.



“of course, Sir, you will take up my reference first. they be a

little—old, but I think you will find they satisfactory.”



“I have no doubt about your reference, Mrs. Moxton, but they shall be

take up nevertheless, duly and in order.”



“thank you, Sir,” say Mrs. Moxton, give he a three-quarter face, and

almost look at he in her pleasure.



and thereafter Mrs. Moxton rule the household of Oswald accord to

the law and habit of the late Mr. Justice Benlees, who have evidently

be a very wise, comfortable, and intelligent man. when she come on

from the uncongenial furniture at Margate to the comfort and beauty of

Pelham Ford she betray a certain approval by expand an inch or so

in every direction and let out two new chin, but otherwise she make

no remark. she radiate decorum and a faint smell of lavender. she have,

it seem, always possess a black-water-silk dress and a gold chain.

even Lady Charlotte approve of she.



for some year Mrs. Moxton enable Oswald to disregard the social

difficulty that be suppose to surround feminine adolescence. Joan

and Peter get along very well with Pelham Ford as their home, and no

other feminine control except an occasional visit from the Stubland

aunt. then Aunt Charlotte become tiresome because Joan be grow up.

“how can the gal grow up properly,” she ask, “even consider what

she be, in a house in which there ben’t a lady at the head?”



Oswald reflect upon the problem. he summon Mrs. Moxton to his

presence.



“Mrs. Moxton,” he say, “when Miss Joan be here, I’ve be think,

don’t you think she ought to be, so to speak, mistress of the place?”



“I have be wonder when you would make the change, Mr. Sydenham,”

say Mrs. Moxton. “I shall be very pleased to take my order from Miss

Joan.”



and after that Mrs. Moxton use to come to Joan whenever Joan be at

Pelham Ford, and tell she what order she have to give for the day. and

when Joan have visitor, Mrs. Moxton tell Joan just exactly what

arrangement Joan be to order Mrs. Moxton to make. in all thing that

matter Mrs. Moxton rule Joan with an obedience of iron. her curtsey,

slow, deliberate and firm, insist that Joan be a lady—and have get to

be one. she take to call Joan “Ma’am.” Joan have to live up to it, and

do. visitor increase after the young people be at Cambridge. junior

don from Newnham and Girton would come and chaperon their hostess, and

Peter treat Oswald to a variety of sample of the young male

generation. some of the sample Oswald like more than other. and he

conceal very carefully from Aunt Charlotte how mix these young

gathering be, how light be the Cambridge standard of chaperonage,

and how very junior be some of the junior don from the woman’s

college.





                                  § 4



when child be small we elder in charge be apt to suppose they

altogether plastic. there be resistance, it be true, but these express

themselves at first only in tantrum, in apparently quite meaningless

outbreak; we impose our phrase and value so completely, that such

spasmodic opposition seem to signify nothing. we impose our name for

thing, our classification with their thousand implication, our

interpretation. the child be imitative and obedient by instinct, its

personality for the most part latent, warily hide. that be “hand,” we

dictate, that be “hat,” that be “pussy cat,” that be “pretty, pretty,”

that be “good,” that be “nasty,” that be “ugly—Ugh!” that again be

“fearsome; run away!” there be no discussion. if we know our parental

business we be able to establish all sort of habit, readiness,

disposition in these entirely plastic day. “time for Peter to go to

bed,” utter with gusto, become the signal for an interesting ritual

upon which he embark with dignity. until some idiot visitor remark

loudly, “don’t he _hate_ go to bed? I always _hate_ go to bed.”

Whereupon in that matter the seed of reflection and dissent be sow in

the little mind.



and so with most other matter. for a few year of advantage the new

mind be clay and we have it to ourselves, and then, still clay, it

become perceptibly resistant, perceptibly disposed to recover some

former shape we have give it or to take an outline of its own. it

discover we be not divine and that even Dadda cannot recall the

sunset. it be not only that other mind be come in to modify and

contradict our decision. we contradict ourselves and it note the

contradiction. and old Nature begin to take an increase share in the

accumulate personality. apart from what we give and those other give,

thing bubble up inside it, desire, imagination, creative dream. by

imperceptible degree the grow mind slip away from we. a little

while ago it seem like some open vessel into which we could pour

whatever we choose; now suddenly it be closed and lock, hide a

fermentation.



perhaps thing have always be more or less so between elder and

young, but in the old day of slow change what father and mother have

to tell the child, priest and master re-echoed, law and institution

confirm, the practice of every one, good or evil, endorse in black or

white. but from the break-up of the catholic culture in England onward

there have be an unceasing conflict between more and more divergent

story about life, and in the last half century that clash have

enormously intensify. what begin as a war of ideal become at last a

chaos. adolescence be once either an obedience or a rebellion; at the

opening of the twentieth century it have become an interrogation and an

experiment. one hear very much of the right of the parent to bring up

child in his own religion, his own idea, but no one ever bother to

explain how that right be to be preserve. in Ireland one find near

Dublin educational establishment surround by ten-foot wall top

with broken glass, protect a catholic atmosphere for a few precious

and privileged specimen of the Erse nation. Mr. James Joyce in his

_portrait of the Artist as a Young Man_, have bottle a speciman of that

catholic atmosphere for the astonishment of posterity. the rest of the

youth of the change world lie open to every wind of suggestion that

blew. the parent or guardian find himself a mere competitor for the

attention and conviction of his charge.





                                  § 5



through childhood and boyhood and girlhood, Peter’s sex and seniority

alike have conspire to give he a leadership over Joan. his seem the

rich, lively mind, he tell most of the story and initiate most of

the game; Joan be the follower. that masculine ascendancy last until

Peter be leave Caxton; in spite of various emancipate force at

Highmorton. then in less than a year Joan take possession of herself.



Reserve be a necessary grace in all young brother and sister. Peter

spread his reverie as a peacock spread its tail, but Joan keep she

dream discreetly private. all youth live much in reverie; thereby the

strong mind anticipate and rehearse themselves for life in a thousand

imagination, the weak one escape from it. against that early

predominance of Peter, Joan maintain her self-respect by extensive

secret supplement of the Bungo-Peter saga. for example she be

Bungo-Peter’s “Dearest Belovèd.” Peter never suspect how Bungo-Peter

and she cuddle up together at the camp fire and be very close and

warm every night, until she go off to sleep....



when she be about fourteen Joan’s imagination pass out of the phase

of myth and saga into the world of romance. the real world draw close

to she. Bungo-Peter vanish; Nobby shrink down to a real Uncle Nobby.

her childish reverie have disregard possibility; now the story have to

be plausible; it have to join on to Highmorton and the Ingle-Nook and

Pelham Ford; its heroine have to be conceivable as the real Joan. and

with the coming of reality, come mood. there be time when she feel

dull, and the world look on she with a grey and stupid face, and other

time to compensate she for these dull phase, season of unwonted

exaltation. it be as if her be sometimes draw itself together in

order presently to leap and extend itself.



in these new phase of expansion she have the most perfect conviction

that life, and particularly her life, be wonderful and beautiful and

destine to be more and more so. she begin to experience a strange, new

happiness in mere existence, a happiness that come with an effect of

revelation. it be hard to convey the peculiar delight that invade she

during these phase. it be almost as if the earth have just be create

for she and give to she as a present. there be moment when the world

be a crystal globe of loveliness about she, moment of ecstatic

realization of a universal beauty. the slight thing would suffice to

release this sunshine in her soul. she would discover the intensest

delight in little, hitherto disregard detail, in the colour of a leaf

hold up to the light, or the rhythm of ripple on a pond or the touch

of a bird’s feather. there be moment when she want to kiss the

sunset, and time when she would clamber over the end wall of the garden

at Pelham Ford in order to lie hidden and still, with every sense awake,

in the big clump of bracken in the corner by the wood beyond. the smell

of crushed bracken delight she intensely. she want to be a nymph

then and not a girl in clothe. and shine summer stream and lake

rouse in she a passionate desire to swim, to abandon herself wholly to

the comprehensive sweet silvery caress of the water.



in the day of the Saga story, the time of the story have always be

now—and never; but in the drama of adolescence the time of all Joan’s

reverie be tomorrow; what she dream of now be thing that be to

be real experience in quite a little time, when she have grow just a

year or so old, when she be a little tall, when she have leave

school, when she be really as beautiful as she hope to be.



the world about she by example and precept, by play and story and

poem and history, be supply she with a rich confusion of material

for these anticipatory sketch. one main history emerge in she

fifteenth year. it go on for many month. Joan of Arc be in the

making of it, and Jane Shore, and Nell Gwyn. at first she be the Lady

Joan, and then she become just Joan Stubland, but always she be the

king’s mistress.



from the very beginning Joan have find something splendid and attractive

in the word “mistress.” it have come to she first in a history lesson,

and then more brightly clothe in a costume novel. but it be a very

glorious and noble kind of mistress that Joan have in view. her idea of

the authority and duty of a mistress be vague; but she know that a

mistress rule by beauty. that she rule Joan never doubt—or why

should she be call mistress? and she prevail over queen, so french

history have instruct she. she make war and peace. Joan of Arc be

inextricably mix in with the vision. she be a beautiful girl, and she

tell the king of France what to do. at need she lead army. what else

but a mistress could you call she? “mistress of France,” magnificent

phrase! of such idea be Joan Stubland weave. the king perhaps would do

injustice, or neglect a meritorious case. then Joan Stubland would

appear, watchful and dignified. “no,” she would say. “that must not be.

I be the king’s mistress.”



and she wear a kind of light armour. without skirt. never with skirt.

Joan at fourteen already see long skirt ahead of she, and hate they as

a man might hate a swamp that he must presently cross knee-deep.



where the king go Joan go. but he be not the current king, nor his

destine successor. she have study these monarch in the illustrate

paper—and in the news. she do not think much of they. they stand down

out of Joan’s dream in favour of a young autocrat. after all, be

there not also a young prince, her contemporary, who would some day be

king? but in her imagination he be not like his publish portrait;

instead—and this be curious—he be rather like Peter. he be as much

like Peter as any one. this be all of Peter that ever get into her

reverie, for there be a curious bar in her mind to Peter be think

of either as her lover or as any one not her lover. something obscure in

her composition bar any such direct imagination about Peter.



so, contrawise to all establish morality and to everything to which

her properly constitute teacher be try to shape she, a chance

phrase in a history book fill the imagination of Joan with this dream

of a different sort of woman’s life altogether. in which one go side

by side with a man in a manly way, share his power, be dear and

beautiful to he. compare with such a lot who would be one of these

wife? who would stay at home and—as a consequence apparently of the

religious ceremony of matrimony—have baby?



the king’s mistress story be Joan’s dominant reverie, but it be not

her only one. it be, so to speak, her serial; it be always “to be

continue in our next.” but her busy mind, whenever her attention be

not fully occupy, be continually spin romance; beside the serial

story there be endless incidental one. almost always they be love

story. they be violent and adventurous in substance, full of chase,

fight, and confrontation, but Joan do not stint herself of kiss

and embrace. there be time when she like tremendously to think of

herself kiss. Most little girl of thirteen or fourteen be think

with the keen interest and curiosity about this lover business and

its mystery, and Joan be no exception. she be deeply interested to

find she be almost as old as Juliet. inspire by Shakespeare, Joan

think quite a lot about balcony and ladder—and Romeo. some of her

school contemporary jest about these thing and be very arch and

sly. but she be as shy of talk about love as she be prone to love

reverie. she talk of flower and poetry and music and scenery and

beautiful thing as though they be thing in themselves, but in her

heart she be convince that all the loveliness that shine upon she in

the world be only so much intimation of the come loveliness of love.



the outward and visible disposition of Highmorton School be all against

the spirit of such dream. the disposition of Highmorton be towards a

scorn of male. what Joan know surely to be lovely, Highmorton denounce

as “soppy.” “Soppy” be a terrible word in boy’ school and girl’

school alike, a flail for all romance. but in the girl’ school it be

use more particularly against tender thought of man. Highmorton teach

the revolt of woman from the love of man—in favour of the love of woman.

the school resound always with the achievement of the one important

sex, hitherto hold back by man-make law from demonstrate an all-round

superiority. the staff at Highmorton have all a common hardness of

demeanour; they be without exception suffragette, and most of they

militant suffragette. they play hockey with great violence, and let

the eld girl hear they say “damn!” the one who have any beauty

aspire to sub-virile effect; they impress small adorer as if they

be sexless angel. there be Miss Oriana Frobisher (science) with the

glorious wave in her golden hair and the flash of lightning in her

glass. she have do great feat with love, it be say; she have

refuse a professor of botany and a fabulously rich widower, and the

mathematical master be “go” on she. there be Miss Kellaway, dark and

pensive, know to her worshipper as “queen of the Night,” fragile, and

yet a swift and nimble forward. Aunt Phœbe also have become a leading

militant, and Aunt Phyllis, who waver on the verge of militancy,

continue the Highmorton teaching in the holiday. “absolute equality

between the sex,” be their demand; their moderate demand, see what

man be. Joan would have be more than human not to take the colour of

so universal a teaching. and yet in her reverie there be always one

man exempt from that doom of general masculine inferiority. she have no

use for a dream lover—unless he be die of consumption or, Tristram

fashion, of love-cause wound—who could not out-run, out-fence,

out-wrestle and out-think she, or for a situation of asserted equality

which could not dissolve into caress devotion.





                                  § 6



and of these preoccupation with the empire and the duty and destiny

of the empire and the collective affair of mankind, which to Oswald

be the very gist and purpose of education, Highmorton teach Joan

practically nothing. Miss Jevons, the Head, would speak now and then of

“loyalty to the crown” in a rather distant way—Miss Murgatroyd have be

willnot to do the same thing—and for the rest leave politic alone. except

that there be one thing, one supreme thing, the vote. when first little

Joan hear of the Vote at Limpsfield she be inclined to think it be a

flatten red round thing rather like the Venerable Bede at the top of

the flagstaff. she learn little well at Highmorton. she gather

that woman be go to “get the vote” and then they be to vote. they

be go to vote somehow against the man and it would make the world

well, but there be very little more to it than that. the idea

remain strictly personal, strictly dramatic. wicked man like Mr.

Asquith who oppose the vote be to be cast down; one of the dazzle

Pankhurst family, or perhaps Miss Oriana Frobisher, be to take his

place. profound scepticism about this vote—in her heart of heart she

call it the “old vote,” be hide by Joan from the general

observation of the school. she have only the slight attack of that

common schoolgirl affliction, schoolmistress love; she never idolize

Miss Jevons or Miss Frobisher or Miss Kellaway. their enthusiasm for the

vote, therefore, prevent her.



later on it be to be different. she be to find in the vote a symbol of

personal freedom—and an excellent excuse for undergraduate misbehaviour.



it be true Highmorton School present a certain amount of history and

geography to Joan’s mind, but in no way as a process in which she be

concerned. she grow up to believe that in England we be out of

history, out of geography, eternally bless in a constitution that we

could not well, under a crown which be henceforth for ever, so to

speak, the centre of an everlasting social tea-party, and that party

“politic” in Parliament and the great vote struggle have take the place

of such real convulsion of human fortune as occur in other country

and other time. war, famine, pestilence; the world have do with they.

nation, king and people, politic, be for Joan throughout all her

schoolday no more than scenery for her unending private personal

romance.



but because much have be tell here of Joan’s reverie it be not to be

imagine that she be addict to brood. it be only when her mind

be unoccupied that the internal story-teller get to work. usually Joan

be pretty actively occupy. the Highmorton ideal of breezy activity

take hold of her very early; one keep “on the Go.” in school she like

her work, even though her unworshipping disposition get she at time at

loggerhead with her teacher; there be so much more in the lesson

than there have be at Miss Murgatroyd’s. out of school she become

rather a disorderly influence. at first she miss Peter dreadfully.

then she begin to imitate Peter for the benefit of one or two small

associate with less initiative than her own. then she become

authentically Peter-like. she try a mild saga of her own in those

junior day, and teach her friend to act a part in it as Peter have

teach she to be a companion of the great Bungo. she develop the same

sort of disposition to go up ladder, climb over wall, try the front

of cliff, go through open door and try closed one, that use to make

Peter such agreeable company. once or twice she and a friend or so even

get lose by the mistress in charge of a school walk, and come home by a

different way through the outskirt of broadstair. but that lead to an

awe-inspire “fuss.” moreover, it take Joan some year to grasp the

idea that the physical correction of one’s friend be not ladylike. when

it come to other girl she perceive that Peter’s way with a girl be

really a very good way—well than either hauteur or pinch. hold

down, for instance, or the wrist wrench.



all the time that she be at Highmorton Joan find no friend as good as

Peter. Tel Wymark, with the freckle, become important about Joan’s

fifteenth birthday as a good giggle associate, a person to sit with in

the back seat of lecture and debate and tickle to death with dry

comment on the forward proceeding. to turn on Tel quietly and slowly

and do a gargoyle face at she be usually enough to set she off—or even

to pull a straight face and sit as if you be about to gargoyle. Tel’s

own humour be by no means negligible, and she have a store of Limericks,

the first Limericks Joan have encounter. Joan herself rarely giggle;

on a few occasion she laugh loudly, but for most comic occasion she

laughter be internal, and so this disintegration of Tel by merriment

become a fascinating occupation. it be no doubt the contrast of her

dark restraint that subject she to the passionate affection of Adela

Murchison.



that affair begin a year or so before the friendship with Tel. Adela be

an abundant white-flesh creature rather more than a year old than

Joan. she come back from the Easter holiday, stage strike, with she

head full of Rosalind. she have see Miss Lillah McCarthy as Rosalind in

_as you like it_, and have fall violently in love with she. she go

over the play with Joan, and Joan be much fascinated by the Rosalind

masquerade; in such guise Joan Stubland might well have meet her king for

the first time. then Adela and Joan let their imagination loose and

play at Shakespearian love-making. they would get together upon walk

and steal apart whenever an opportunity offer. Adela want to kiss a

great deal, and once when she kiss Joan she whisper, “it’s not

rosalind I love, not Lillah or any one else; just Joan.” Joan kiss she

in return. and then something twist over in Joan’s mind that drive she

to austerity; suddenly she would have no more of this kissing, she

herself could not have explain why or wherefore. it be the queer

recoil. “we’re be too soppy,” she say to Adela, but that do not in

the least express it. Adela become a protest and urgent lover; she

write Joan note, she try to make scene, she demand be there any

one else?



“no,” say Joan. “but I don’t like all this rot.”



“you do!” say Adela with ready tear shine in her pretty eye.



“and I don’t now,” say Joan....



Joan herself be puzzle, but she have no material in her mind by which

she could test and analyse this revulsion. she hide a dark secret from

all the world, she hide it almost from herself, that once before, in the

previous summer holiday, one afternoon while she be stay with her

aunt at the Ingle-Nook, she have walk over by the Cuspard house on the

way to Miss Murgatroyd’s. and she have meet young Cuspard, grow tall and

quaintly good-look, in white flannel. they have stop to talk and

sit down on a tree together, and suddenly he have kiss she. “you’re

lovely, Joan,” he say. it be an incredible thing to remember, it be a

memory so astounding as to be obscure, but she know as a fact that she

have kiss he again and have like this kissing, and then have have just

this same feeling of terror, of enormity, as though something vast

clutched at she. it be fantastically disagreeable, not like a real

disagreeable thing, but like a dream disagreeable thing. she resolve

that in fact it have not happen, she bar it back out of the current

of her thought, and it shadow her life for day.





                                  § 7



the modern world tell the young a score of conflict story—more or

less distinctly—about every essential thing. while man like Oswald dream

of a culture tell the young plainly what they be suppose to be for,

what this or that or the other be for, the current method of instruction

about God and state and sex alike be a wrangle that never join issue.

for every youth and maiden who be not strictly seclude or very stupid,

adolescence be a period of distressful perplexity, of hidden hypothesis,

misunderstood hint, check urgency, and wild stampede of the

imagination. Joan’s opening mind be like some ill-defend country

across which army march. come the School of St. George and the

Venerable Bede, lead by Miss Murgatroyd and applaud by Aunt Phœbe,

bare its head and foot and knee, cast aside corset, appeal to

nature and simplicity, profess fearlessness, and tell the young a

great deal less than it have the air of tell they. come Highmorton, a

brace wind after that relax atmosphere.



but Limpsfield have at least a certain honesty in its limited initiation;

Highmorton be comparatively an imposture. with an effect of go right

on beyond all establish thing to something fine and new,

Highmorton be really restore prudery in a brutalize form. it be no

more vigorous to ban a topic by call it “soppy” and wave a muddy

hockey-stick at it in a threatening manner, than it be to ban it by

call it “improper” and primly cut it dead. there the topic

remain.



a third influence have make a contributory grab at Joan; Aunt Charlotte

Sydenham’s raid on the child’s education be on behalf of all that

be then most orthodox. Hers be indeed the essential english culture of

the early victorian age; a culture that so far as sex go be pure

suppression—temper by the broad hint and titter chatter of

servant and base people....



stick away, shut in, in Joan’s memory, shut in and disregard as bee

will wax up and disregard the decay body of some foul intruder, be

certain passage with Mrs. Pybus. they carry an impression at once

vague and enormous, of a fascinating unclean horror. they be

inseparably mix up with strange incredulous thought of hell that be

implant during the same period. such scenery as they need be

supply by the dusty, fade furnishing of the little house in Windsor,

they have the same faintly disagreeable dusty smell of a home only

cleanse by stray wipe with a duster and spiritless sweep with

tea-leave.



that period have be a dark patch upon the sunlit fabric of Joan’s life.

over it all brood this Mrs. Pybus, frankly dirty while “do” her

house in the morning, then insincerely tidy in the afternoon. she talk

continually to, at, and round about Joan. she be always talk. she

be an untimely widow prone to brood upon the unpleasant but enormously

importunate fact that married life have thrust upon she. she have an

irresistible desire to communicate her experience with an air of

wisdom. she have a certain conceit of wisdom. she have no sense of the

respect due to the ignorance of childhood. like many woman of her class

and type she be too egotistical to allow for childhood.



never before have Joan hear of disease. now she hear of all the

disease of these two profoundly clinical family, the Pybuses and the

Unwins. the Pybus family specialize in cancer, “chumor” and morbid

growth generally; one, but he be rather remote and legendary, have have

an “insec’ in ’be ’ed”; the distinction of the Unwins on the other hand

be in difficult parturition. all this stuff be pour out in a

whine monologue in Joan’s presence as Mrs. Pybus busy herself in the

slatternly detail of her housework.



“two case of cancer I’ve see through from the very first pang,” Mrs.

Pybus would begin, and then piously, “God grant I never see a third.”



“whatever you do, Joan, one thing I say never do—good though Pybus be

and kind. never marry no one with internal cancer, ’owever ’ard you may

be drive. indigestion, rheumatism, even a wooden leg rather. well a

man that drink. I say it and I know. it don’t make it any easy,

Joan, to sit and see they suffer.



“you’ve get your trouble yet to come, young lady. I don’t expec’ you

understand ’arf what I’m tell you. but you will some day. I sometimes

think if I ’adn’t be kep’ in ignorance thing might have be well

for I—all I bin call upon to go through.” that be the style of

thing. it be like pour drainage over a rosebud. First Joan listen

with curiosity, then with horror. then unavailingly, always overpower

by a grotesque fascination, she try not to listen. monstrous fragment

get through to her cower attention. here be thing for a little

girl to carry off in her memory, material as she sicken for measle

for the most terrifying and abominable of dream.



“there’s poor lady that have to be reg’lar cut open....



“I ’ad a dreadful time when I marry Pybus. often I say to ’Im

afterwards, you cann’t complain of _I_, Pybus. the thing one live

through!...



“’be sister’s ’usband don’t ’ave no mercy on ’er....



“don’t you go outside this gate, Joan—ever. if one of these ’ere Tramps

should get hold of you.... I’ve ’eard of a little girl....”



if a congenial gossip should happen to drop in Joan would be tell to sit

by the window and look at the “nice picture book”—it be always that one

old volume of _the Illustrated London News_—while a talk go on that

insist on be hear, now drop to harsh whisper, now rise

louder after the assurance of Mrs. Pybus:



“Lord! _she_ willn’t understand a word you’re say.”



if by chance Mrs. Pybus and her friend drift for a time from personal

or consanguineous experience then they deal with crime. difficulty

in the disposal of the body fascinate these lady even more than the

pleasing detail of the act. and they prefer murder of woman by man.

it seem more natural to they....



the world change again. through the tossing distress of the measle

Aunt Phyllis reappear, and then come a journey and the Ingle-Nook and

dear Petah! and Nobby. she be back in a world where Mrs. Pybus could

not exist, where the thing of which Mrs. Pybus talk could not happen.

yet there be this in Joan’s mind, unformulated, there be a passionate

stress against its formulation, that all the other thing she think

about love and beauty be poetry and dream, but this alone of all

the voice that have speak over and about she, tell of something real.

in the unknown beyond to which one get if one press on, be something

of that sort, something monstrous, painful and dingy....



reality!



Wax it over, little dream bee; cover it up; don’t think of it! back to

reverie! be a king’s mistress, clothe in armour, who sometimes grant a

kiss.





                                  § 8



it be in the nature of Mrs. Pybus to misconceive thing. she never

grasp the true relationship of Joan and Peter; Mr. Grimes have indeed

be deliberately vague upon that point in the interest of the Sydenham

family, the use of the Stubland surname for Joan have help he; and so

there drop into Joan’s ear a suggestion that be at the time merely

perplexing but which become gradually an establish fact in her mind.



“ow! don’t you know?” say Mrs. Pybus to her friend. “ow, no! she’s——”

(her voice sink to a whisper.)



for a time what they say be so confidential as to convey nothing to

Joan but a sense of mystery. “ow ’be mother ever stand ’er in the ’ouse

pass my belief,” say Mrs. Pybus, come up to the audible again.

“why! I’d ’ave _kill_ ’er. but lady and gentleman don’t seem to ’ave

no natural affection—not wot I call affection. there she be bring

into the ’ouse and treat just as if she be the little chap’s sister.”



“she’d be——?” say the friend, try to grasp it.



“’arf sister,” say Mrs. Pybus. “of a sort. neither ’ere nor there, so

to speak. not in the eye of the law. and there they be—leastway they

be until Lady Charlotte Sydenham interfere.”



the friend nod her head rapidly to indicate intelligent appreciation.



“it ben’t like be _reely_ brother and sister,” say Mrs. Pybus,

contemplate possibility. “it’s neither one thing nor another. and

all wrop up in mystery as you might say. why, oo know? they might go

fall in love with each other.”



“_’Orrible!_” say Mrs. Pybus’s friend.



“it ’ad to be put a stop to,” say Mrs. Pybus.



Confirmatory nodding, with a stern eye for the little figure that sit in

a corner and pretend to be interested in the fade exploit of

vanish royalty, record in that old volume of _the Illustrated

London News_....



that conversation sink down into the deep of Joan’s memory and remain

there, obscure but exercise a dim influence upon her relation with

Peter. one phrase send up a bubble every now and then into her conscious

thought: “half-sister.” it be year after that she begin to piece

together the hidden riddle of her birth. Mummy and Daddy be away; that

have serve as well for she as for Peter far beyond the Limpsfield day.

it ben’t until child be in their teen that these thing interest

they keenly. it ben’t a thing to talk about, she know, but it be a

thing to puzzle over. who be really her father? who be her mother? if

she be Peter’s half-sister, then either his father be not her or his

mother....



when people be all manifestly in a plot to keep one in the dark one

do not ask question.





                                  § 9



after the first violent rupture that Mr. Grimes have organize, Joan and

Peter part and meet again in a series of separation and resumption.

they go off to totally dissimilar atmosphere, Joan to the bracing and

roughen air of Highmorton and Peter first to the brightness of White

Court and then to the vigorous work and play of Caxton; and each time

they return for the holiday to Margate or Limpsfield or Pelham Ford

change, novel, and yet profoundly familiar. always at first when

holiday bring they together again they be shy with each other and

intensely egotistical, anxious to show off their new trick and make the

most of whatever small triumph school life have give they. then in a

day or so they would be at their ease together like a joint that have

be dislocate and have slip into place again. Cambridge at last

bring they near together, and end this series of dislocation.

after much grave weighing of the situation by Miss Fairchild, the

principal of Newton Hall, Peter, when Joan come up, be give the status

of a full brother.



they grow irregularly, and that make some quaint variation of

relationship. Peter, soon after he go to Caxton, fall to expand

enormously. he develop a chest, his limb become great thing. there

be a summer bite into Joan’s memory when he regard she as nothing

more than a “leetle teeny female tick,” and descant on the minuteness

of her soul and body. but he have lose some of his lightness, if none of

his dexterity and balance, as a climber, and Joan get her consolation

among the light branch of various tree they explore. next

Christmas Joan herself have do some serious growing, and the gap be

not so wide. but it be only after her first term at Newnham that Joan

pass from the subservience of a junior to the confidence of a senior.

she do it at a bound. she meet he one day in the narrow way between

Sidney Street and Petty Cury. her hair be up and her eye be steady;

most of her leg have vanish, and she have clothe like a real woman. we

do not foregather even with foster brother in the street of Cambridge,

but a pass hail be beyond the reach of discipline. “Hullo, Petah!”

she say, “what a gawky great thing you’re get!”



Peter, a man in his second year, be so take aback he have no adequate

reply.



“you’ve grow too,” he say, “if it come to that”;—a flavourless reply.

and there be admiration in his eye.



an encounter for subsequent regret. he think over it afterwards. the

cheek of she! it make his blood boil.



“so long, Petah,” say Joan, carry it off to the end....



they be stern than brother and sister with each other. there be

never go to be anything “soppy” between they. at fourteen, when Peter

pass into the red indian phase of a boy’s development, when there can

be no more “blubbing,” no more shirk, he carry Joan with he. she

respond magnificently to the idea of pluck. spartan ideal rule they

both. and a dark taciturnity. Joan would have die with shame if Peter

have penetrate the secret romance of Joan Stubland, and the day of

Peter’s saga be over for ever. when Peter be fifteen he be consume

by a craving for a gun, and Oswald give he one. “but kill,” say

Oswald. “if you let anything get away wounded——”



Peter take Joan out into the wood at the back. he miss a pigeon, and

then he get one.



“pick it up, Joan,” he say, very calmly and grandly.



Joan be white to the lip, but she pick up the bloodstained bird in

silence. these thing have to happen.



then out of a heap of leave in front dart a rabbit. Lop, lop, lop,

go its little white scut. _Bang!_ and over it roll, but it ben’t

instantly kill. Horror come upon Joan. she be near; she run to the

wretched animal, which be lie on its side and kick automatically,

and stand over it. its eye be bright and wide with terror. “oh, how

be I to _kill_ it?” she cry, with agony in her voice; “what be I to

do-o?” she wring her hand. she feel she be go to piece, give

herself away, fail utterly. Peter would despise she and jeer at she.

but the poor little beast! the poor beast! there be a limit to pride.

she catch it up. “Petah!” she cry quite pitifully, on the verge of a

whimper.



Peter have come up to she. he don’t look contemptuous. he be

white-lipped too. she have never see he look scared before. he snatch

the rabbit from she and kill it by one, two, three—she count—quick

blow—she don’t see. but she have meet his eye, and they be as

distressed as her. just for a moment.



then he be a fifth form boy again. he examine his victim with an

affectation of calm. “too far back,” he say. “bad shot. mustn’t do that

again.”...



the rabbit be quite still and limp now, dangle from Peter’s hand, its

eye have glaze, blood drip and clot at its muzzle, but its

rhythmic desperate kicking be still beat in Joan’s brain.



be this to go on? could she go on?



Peter’s gun and the pigeon be lie some yard away. he regard they

and then look down at the rabbit he hold.



“now I know I can shoot,” he say, and leave the sentence unfinishe.



“bring the pigeon, Joan,” he say, end an indecision, and pick up

his gun and lead the way back towards the house....



“we get a pigeon and a rabbit,” Joan babble at tea to Oswald. “next

time, Petah’s go to let I have the gun.”



our tone be altogether sport.



but there be no next time. there be many unspoken thing between Joan

and Peter, and this be to be one of they. for all the rest of their

live neither Joan nor Peter go shoot again. Men Peter be destine

to slay—but no more beast. necessity never compel they, and it would

have demand an urgent necessity before they would have face the risk

of see another little furry creature twist and wriggle and of mark

how a bright eye glaze over. but they be both very bitterly ashamed

of this distressing weakness. they leave far shoot for “tomorrow,”

and it remain always tomorrow. they say nothing about their real

feeling in the matter, and Peter clean and oil his new gun very

carefully and hang it up conspicuously over the mantelshelf of their

common room, ready to be take down at any time—when animal cease to

betray feel.





                                  § 10



Joan and Peter detest each other’s friend from the beginning. the

quarrel that culminate in that amazing speech of Joan’s, have be

smoulder between they for a good seven year. it go right back to

the day when they be still boy and girl.



to begin with, after their first separation they have have no particular

friend; they have have acquaintance and habit of association, but the

mind still lack the continuity necessary for friendship and Euclid

until the early teen. the first rift come with Adela Murchison. Joan

bring she for the summer holiday when Peter have be just a year at

Caxton.



that be the first summer at Pelham Ford. Aunt Phyllis be with they,

but Aunt Phœbe be in great labour with her first and only novel, a

fantasia on the theme of feminine genius, “these be my child, or

Mary on the Cross.” (it be afterwards greatly censor. boot, the

druggist librarian, would have none of it.) she stay alone, therefore,

at the Ingle-Nook, writing, revising, despairing, tear up and

begin again, recite her more powerful passage to the scarlet but

listening ear of Groombridge and the little maid, and go more and

more unkempt, unhooked, and unbuttoned. Oswald, instead of resort to

the Climax Club as he be apt to do when Aunt Phœbe be imminent, abode

happily in his new home.



Adela be a month or so old than Peter and, what annoy he to begin

with, rather more fully grow. she be, as she only too manifestly

perceive, a woman of the world in comparison with both of her host.

she be still deeply in love with Joan, but by no mean indifferent to

this dark boy who look at she with so much of Joan’s cool detachment.



Joan’s romantic dream be Joan’s inmost secret, Adela’s romantic

intention be an efflorescence. she be already hoist the signal

for masculine surrender. she never fail to have a blue ribbon astray

somewhere to mark and help the blueness of her large blue eye. she

insist upon the flaxen wave over her ear, and secretly assist they

to kink. she have a high colour. she have no rouge yet in her possession

but there be rouge in her soul, and she would rub her cheek with her

hand before she come into a room. she discover to Joan the incredible

fact that Oswald be also a man.



with her arm round Joan’s waist or over her shoulder she would look back

at he across the lawn.



“I say,” she say, “he’d be _frightfully_ good-look—if it ben’t for

_that_.”



and one day, “I wonder if Mr. Sydenham’s ever be in love.”



she lie in wait for Oswald’s eye. she go after he to ask he

unimportant thing.



once or twice little thing happen, the slight thing, but it might

have seem to Joan that Oswald be disposed to flirt with Adela. but

that be surely impossible....



the first effect of the young woman upon Peter be a considerable but

indeterminate excitement. it be neither pleasurable nor unpleasurable,

but it hang over the giddy verge of be unpleasant. it make he want

to be very large, handsome and impressive. it also make he acutely

ashamed of want to be very large, handsome and impressive. it turn

he from a simple boy into a conflict of motive. he want to extort

admiration from Adela. also he want to despise she utterly. these

impulse work out to no coherent system of remark and gesture, and

he become awkward and tongue-tie.



Adela want to be show all over the house and garden. she put her arm

about Joan in a manner Peter think offensive. then she throw back her

hair at he over her shoulder and say, shoot a glance at he, “you

come too.”



Cheek!



still, she be a guest, and so a fellow have to follow with his hand in

his pocket and watch his own private and particular Joan be order

about and—what be somehow so much more exasperating—_pawed_ about.



at what seem to be the early opportunity Peter excuse himself, and

go off to the outhouse in which he have his tool and chemical and

thing. he decide he would rig up everything ready to make Sulphuretted

Hydrogen—although he know quite well that this be neither a large,

handsome, nor impressive thing to do. and then he would wait for they to

come along, and set the odour going.



but neither of the girl come near his Glory Hole, and he be not go

to invite they. he just hover there unvisite, wait with his

preparation and whistle soft melancholy tune. finally he make a lot

of the gas, simply because he have get the stuff ready, and stank himself

out of his Glory Hole into society again.



at supper, which have become a sort of dinner that night, Adela insist

on talk like a rather languid, smart woman of the world to Nobby.

Nobby take she quite seriously. it be perfectly sicken.



“D’you hunt much?” say Adela.



“not in England,” say Nobby. “there’s too many hedge for I. I’ve a

sailor’s seat.”



“all my people hunt,” say Adela. “it’s rather a bore, don’t you think,

Mr. Sydenham?”



talk like that!



two day pass, during which Peter be either be bored to death in

the company of Adela and Joan or also bore to death keeping aloof from

they. he cycle to Ware with they, and Adela’s cycle have a change speed

arrangement with a high gear of eighty-five that make it difficult to

keep ahead of she. Beast!



and on the second evening she introduce a new card game, Demon

Patience, a scrambling sort of game in which you pile on ace in the

middle and cry “stop!” as soon as your stack be out. it be one of

those game, one of those inferior game, at which boy in their teen

be not nearly as quick as girl, Peter discover. but presently Joan

begin to pull ahead and beat Adela and Peter. the two girl begin to

play against each other as if his poor little spurt don’t amount to

anything. they certainly don’t amount to very much.



Adela begin to play with a sprawl eagerness. her colour deepen; her

manner deteriorate. she be torment between ambition and admiration.

when Joan have run she out for the third time, she cry, “oh, Joan, you

wonderful darling!”



and clutch and kiss she!...



all the other thing might have be bearable if it have not be for

this perpetual confabulating with Joan, this go off to whisper with

Joan, this putting of arm round Joan’s neck, this whispering that be

almost kiss Joan’s ear. one couldn’t have a moment with Joan. one

couldn’t use Joan for the slight thing. it would have be well if

one haven’t have a Joan.



on the mill-pond there be a boat that Joan and Peter be allow to

use. on the morning of the fifth day Joan find Peter hang about in

the hall.



“Joan.”



“yes?”



“come and muck about in Baker’s boat.”



“if Adela——”



“oh, _leave_ Adela! we don’t want she. she’d stash it all up.”



“but she’s a visitor!”



“pretty rotten visitor! what do you bring she here for? she’s rotten.”



“she’s not. she’s all right. you’re be horrid rude to she. every

chance you get. I like she.”



“silly tick, she be!”



“she’s tall than you be, anyhow.”



“Nyar Nyar Nyar Nyar,” say Peter in a singularly ineffective mockery of

Adela’s manner. Adela appear, descend the staircase. Peter turn

away.



“Peter want to go in the boat on the mill-pond,” say Joan, as if with

calculated wickedness.



“oh! I _love_ boat!” say Adela.



“what be a chap to do but go?”



but under a thin mask of playfulness Peter splash they both a

lot—especially Adela. and in the evening he refuse to play at Demon

Patience and go and sit by himself to draw. he try various design.

he be rather good at draw Mr. Henderson, and he do several study

of he. then the girl, who find Demon Patience slow with only two

player, come and sit beside he. he be inspire to begin an ugly

caricature of Adela.



he begin at the eye.



Joan know he well than Adela. she see what be come. down come she

little brown paw on the paper. “no, you don’t, Petah,” she say.



Peter look into her face, hot against his, and there be a red light

in his eye.



“Leago, Joan,” he say.



a struggle begin in which Adela take no share.



the Sydenham blood be hot blood, and though it don’t like hurt

rabbit it can be pretty rough with its first cousin. but Joan be

still grip the crumple half of the offend sheet when Aunt

Phyllis, summon by a scared Adela, come in. the two be on the

hearthrug, panting, and Joan’s tooth be deep in Peter’s wrist; they

part and rise somewhat abash. “my _dear_!” cry Aunt Phyllis.



“we be play,” say Joan, flushed and breathless, but honourably

tearless.



“yes,” say Peter, hold his wrist tight. “we be play.”



“romping,” say Aunt Phyllis. “ben’t you a little rough? Adela, you

know, ben’t use to your style....”



after that, Peter shun further social intercourse. he affect a

great concentration upon experimental chemistry and photography, and

bicycle in lonely pride to Waltham Cross, Baldock, and Dunmow. he give

himself up to the road of Hertfordshire. when at last Adela depart it

make no difference in his aloofness. Joan be henceforth as nothing to

he; she be just a tick, a silly little female tick, an associate of

thing that go “Nyar Nyar Nyar.” he hate she. at least, he would have

hate she if there be anything that a self-respect Caxtonian could

hate in a being so utterly contemptible. (yet at the bottom of his heart

he love and respect she for bite his wrist so hard.)



Deprived of Adela, Joan become very lonely and forlorn. after some day

there be sign of relent on the part of Peter, and then come his

visitor, Wilmington, a boy who have go with he from White Court to

Caxton, and after that there be no need of Joan. with a grim resolution

Peter shut Joan out from all their pursuit. she be annihilate.



the boy do experimental chemistry together, make the most disgusting

stink, blow up a small earthwork by mean of a mine, and stain their

hand bright yellow; they have long bicycle ride together, they do

“splorjum” in the wood, they “muck about” with Baker’s boat. joan by

no effort could come into existence again. once or twice as Peter be

go off with Wilmington, Peter would glance back and feel a gleam of

compunction at the little figure that watch he go. but she have her

Adelas. she and Adela write letter to each other. she could go and

write to her beastly Adela now....



“cann’t Joan come?” say Wilmington.



“she’s only a tick,” say Peter.



“she’s not a bad sort of tick,” say Wilmington.



(what business be it of his?)



Joan fall back on Nobby, and go for walk with he in the afternoon.



then come a complication. towards the end Wilmington get quite soppy on

Joan. it show.



Aunt Phyllis suggest charade for the evening hour after dinner.

Wilmington and Peter play against each other, and either of they take

out any people he want to act with he. Aunt Phyllis be a grave and

dignified actress and Nobby could do well than you might have

expect. Peter do Salome. (sal—owe—I; do sal volatile for Sal.) he

sit as Herod, crown and scornful with the false black beard, and Joan

dance and afterwards bring the football in on a plate. Aunt Phyllis

do pseudo-oriental music. but when Wilmington see Joan dance he know

what it be to be in love. he sit glower passion. for a time he

remain frozen rigid, and then break into wild hand-clapping. his ear

be bright red, and Aunt Phyllis look at he curiously. it be with

difficulty that his clouded mind could devise a charade that would give

he a call upon Joan. but he think at last of Milton. (mill-tun.)



“I want you,” he say.



“willn’t aunty do?”



“no, _you_. it’s get to be a girl.”



he hold the door open for she, and stumble go out of the room. he

be more breathless and jerky than ever outside. Joan hear his

exposition with an unfriendly expression.



“and what be I to do then?” she ask....



“and then?...”



they do “Mill” and “tun” pretty badly. Came Wilmington’s last precious

moment with she. he break off in his description of Milton blind and

Joan as the amanuensis daughter. “Joan,” he whisper, go hoarse with

emotion. “Joan, you’re lovely. I’d die for you.”



a light of evil triumph come into Joan’s eye.



“ugly thing!” say Joan, “what do you come here for? you’ve spoil my

holiday. let _go_ of my hand!... let’s go in and do our tableau.”



and afterwards when Wilmington meet Joan in the passage she treat he

to a grimace that be only too manifestly intend to represent his own

expression of melancholy but undying devotion. in the presence of other

she be coolly polite to he.



Peter read his friend like a book, but refrain from injurious comment,

and Wilmington depart in a state of grave nervous disarray.



a day pass. there be not much leave now of the precious holiday. come

a glow September morning.



“Joe-un,” whoop Peter in the garden—in just the old note.



“pee-tah!” answer Joan, full-voiced as ever, distant but draw

nearer.



“come and muck about in Baker’s boat.”



“right-o, Petah!” say Joan, and approach with a slightly prance

gait.





                                  § 11



grow out of his red indian phase Peter move up into the Lower Sixth

and become a regular cynical man of the world with an air of know

more than a thing or two. he be, in fact, learn a vast number of

thing that be outside the book; and rearrange many of his early

shock and impression by the help of a confusing and increase mixture

of half-light. the chaotic disrespect of the young go out of his

manner in his allusion to school affair, he no long speak of various

master as “Buzzy,” “Snooks,” and “the Croker,” and a curious

respectability have invade his demeanour. the Head have have he in to tea

and tennis. the handle of the prefect’s birch be perhaps not more than

a year now from his grip, if he bear himself gravely. he reproach Joan

on various small occasion for “thunder bad form,” and when

Wilmington come, a much more wary and well-look Wilmington with his

heart no long on his sleeve, the conversation become, so to speak,

political. they talk at the dinner-table of the behaviour of so-and-so

and this-and-that at “high” and at “bottom” and on “the Corso”; they

discuss various case of “side” and “cheek,” and the permanent effect

of these upon the standing and reputation of the youth concern; they

be earnest to search out and know utterly why Best do not get his

colour and whether it be just to “super” old Rawdon. they discuss

the question of superannuation with Oswald very gravely. “don’t you

think,” say Oswald, “if a school take a boy on, it ought to see he

through?”



“but if he don’t work, sir?” say Wilmington.



“a school oughtn’t to produce that lassitude,” say Oswald.



“a chap ought to _use_ a school,” say Peter.



that be a new point of view to Oswald and Joan.



afterwards come Troop, a large boy than either Peter or Wilmington, a

prefect, a youth almost incredibly manly in his manner, and join on to

these discussion. say Oswald, “there ought not to be such a thing as

superannuation. a man ought not to be let drift to the point of

unteachable incapacity. and then throw away. some master ought to have

shepherd he in for special treatment.”



“they don’t look after we to that extent, sir,” say Troop.



“don’t they teach you? or fail to teach you?”



“it’s the school teach we,” say Peter, as though it have just occur

to he.



“still, the master be there,” say Oswald, smile.



“the master be there,” Troop acquiesce. “but the life of the school

be the tradition. and a big chap like Rawdon hang about, too big to

lick and too stupid for responsibility—— it break thing up, sir.”



Oswald be very much interested in this prefect’s view of the school

life. behind his blank mask he engender question; his one eye watch

troop and go from Troop to Peter. this manliness in the teach

surprise he tremendously. Peter be acquire it rapidly, but Troop

seem to embody it. Oswald himself have be a man early enough and have

lead a hard life of mutual criticism and exasperation with his fellow,

but that have be in a work reality, the navy; this, he reflect,

be a case of cock crow inside the egg. these boy be live in a

premature autonomous state, an aristocratic republic with the Head as a

sort of constitutional monarch. there be one questionable consequence

at least. they be acquire political habit before they have acquire

wide horizon. be the political habit of a school where all the boy

be of one race and creed and class, suitable for the problem of a

world’s affair?



Troop, under Oswald’s insidious leading, display his idea modestly

but frankly, and they be the idea of a large child. Troop be a

good-look, thoroughly healthy youth, full of his grave

responsibility towards the school and incline to claim a liberal

attitude. he be very great upon his duty to “make the fellow live

decently and behave decently.” he be lure into a story of how one

youth with a tendency to long hair have be partly win and partly drive

to a more seemly coiffure; how he have deal with a game shirker, and

how a fellow have be detect lend socialist pamphlets—“not to his

friend, sir, I shouldn’t mind that so much, but push they upon any

one”—and restrain. “seditious sort of stuff, sir, I believe. no, I do

not _read_ it, sir.” Troop be for cold bath under all circumstance,

for no smoking under sixteen and five foot six, and for a simple and

unquestione loyalty to any one who come along and profess to be in

authority over he. when he mention the king his voice drop

worshipfully. upon the just use of the birch troop be conscientiously

prolix. there be prefect, he say, who “savage” the fellow. other

swish without judgment. troop put conscience into each whack.



Troop’s liberalism interested Oswald more than anything else about

he. he be proud to profess himself no mere traditionalist; he want

Caxton to “broaden down from precedent to precedent.” indeed he have

ambition to be remember as a reformer. he hope, he say, to leave

the school “well than he find it”—the modern note surely. his idea

of a great and memorable improvement be to let the Upper Fifth

fellow into the Corso after morning service on Sunday. he do not

think it would make they impertinent; rather it would increase their

self-respect. he be also inclined to a reorganization of the

afternoon fag “to stop so much bawl down the corridor.” there

ought to be a bell—an electric bell—in each prefect’s study. no doubt

that be a bit revolutionary—Troop almost smirk. “it’s all very well

for school like Eton or Winchester to stick to the old custom, sir,

but we be suppose to be an up-to-date school. don’t you think, sir?”

the egg be everything to this young cockerel; the world outside be

naught. Oswald lead he on from one solemn puerility to another, and as

the big boy talk in his stout man-of-the-world voice, the red eye

rove from he to Peter and from Peter back to Troop. until presently

it realize that Peter be watch it as narrowly. “what do Peter

really think of this stuff?” think Oswald. “what do Nobby really

think of this stuff?” query Peter.



“I suppose, some day, you’ll leave Caxton,” say Oswald.



“I shall be very sorry to, sir,” say Troop sincerely.



“have you think at all——”



“not yet, sir. at least——”



“troop’s people,” Peter intervene, “be Army people.”



“I see,” say Oswald.



Joan listen enviously to all this prefectorial conversation. at

Highmorton that sort of boss and influencing be do by the junior

staff....



Oswald do his good to lure Troop from his administrative preoccupation

into general topic. but apparently some one whom Troop respect have

warn he against general topic. Oswald lug and push the talk

towards religion, Aunt Phyllis help, but they come up against a stone

wall. “my people be Church of England,” say Troop, intimate thereby

that his opinion be bank with the proper authority. it be not

for he to state they. and in regard to politic, “all my people be

conservative.” one evening Oswald show he a portfolio of drawing

from various indian temple, and suggest something of the complex

symbolism of the figure. Troop think it be “rather unhealthy.”

but—turn from these monstrosity—he have hope for India. “my cousin

tell I, sir, that cricket and polo be spread very rapidly there.”

“Polo,” say Oswald, “be an indian game. they have play it for

century. it come from Persia originally.” but Troop be unable to

imagine Indians ride horse; he have the common british delusion that

the horse and the ship be both invent in our island and that all

foreign people be necessarily amateur at such thing. “I think they

ride elephant,” say Troop with quiet conviction....



Troop be not only a great experience for Oswald, he also exercise the

always active mind of Joan very considerably.



Peter, it seem, haven’t even mention she beforehand.



“Hullo!” say Troop at the sight of she. “got a sister?”



“foster-sister,” say Peter, minimize the thing. “Joan, this be

Troop.”



Joan regard he critically. “can he play D.P.?”



“not one of my game,” say Troop, who be chary of all game not

usually play.



“it’s a game like Snap,” say Peter with an air of casual contempt, and

earn a bright scowl.



for a day or so Troop and Joan keep aloof, watch one another. then

she catch he out rather neatly twice at single wicket cricket; he have

a weakness for give catch to point and she have observe it.

“catch!” he cry approvingly. also she snick and slip and at last

slog boldly at his patronize under-arm bowling. “here’s a Twister,”

he say, like an uncle speak to a child.



Joan smack it into the cedar. “_Twister!_” quoth Joan, run.



after that he take formal notice of she, betray a disposition to

address she as “Kid.” (Ralph Connor be at that time add his quota to

the great british tradition. it be true he write in American about

cowboy—but a refined cowboy be the full realization of an English

gentleman’s pre-war ideal—and Ralph Connor’s cowboy be essentially

refine. Thence come the “kid,” anyhow.) but Joan take umbrage at the

“kid.” and she dislike Troop’s manner and influence with Peter. and the

way Peter stand it. she do not understand what a very, very great be

a prefect be in an english public school, she do not know of Troop’s

superbness at rugger, it seem to she that it be bad manner to behave

as though a visit to Pelham Ford be an act of princely condescension.

she be even disposed to diagnose troop’s largeness, very unjustly, as

fat. so she pull up Troop venomously with “my name’s not Kit, it’s

Joan. J.O.A.N.”



“sorry!” say Troop. and be of that insensitive class whose passion

be only to be rouse by a smacking, he begin to take still more notice

of she. she be, he perceive, a lively kid. he feel a strong desire to

reprove and influence she. he have no suspicion that what he really

want to do be to interest Joan in himself.



Joan’s tennis be incurably tricky. troop’s idea of tennis be to play

very hard and very swiftly close over the net, but without cunne.

Peter and Wilmington follow his lead. but Joan force victory upon an

unwilling partner by do unexpected thing.



Troop declare he do not mind be defeat, but that he be shock

by the spirit of Joan’s play. it ben’t “sporting.”



“those short return ben’t do, kid,” he say.



“I do they,” say Joan. “ancient.”



Peter and Wilmington be visibly shocked, but Troop show no

resentment at the gross familiarity.



“but if every one do they!” he reason.



“I could take they,” say Joan. “any one could take they who know how.”



the dispute seem likely to die down into unverifiable assertion.



“Peter can take they,” say Joan. “he drop they back. but he ben’t

do it today.”



Peter reflect. troop would never understand, but there be something

reasonable in Joan’s line. “I’ll see to Joan,” he say abruptly, and

come towards the middle of the net.



the game continue on unorthodox but brilliant line. “I don’t call this

tennis,” say Troop.



“if you serve to she left,” say Peter.



“but she’s a girl!” protest Troop. “_serve!_”



he make the concession that be proper to a lady, and Joan score the

point after a brief rally with Peter. “Game,” say Joan.



Troop declare he do not care to play again. it would put he off

tennis. “take I as a partner,” say Joan. “no—I don’t think so,

thank,” say Troop coldly.



every one become thoughtful and drift towards the net. Oswald

approach from the pergola, consider the problem.



“I’ve be think about that sort of thing for year,” he remark,

stroll towards they.



“well, sir, ben’t you with I?” ask Troop.



“no. I’m for Joan—and Peter.”



“but that sort of trick play——”



“no. the way to play a game be to get all over the game and to be equal

to anything in it. if there be a stroke or anything that spoil the game

it ought to be bar by the rule. apart from that, a game ought to be

work out to its last possibility. thing oughtn’t to be bar in the

interest of a few conventional swipe. this cut down of a game to

just a few type of stroke——”



Peter look apprehensive.



“it’s laziness,” say Oswald.



Troop be too puzzled to be offend. “but you have to work tremendously

hard, sir, at the proper game.”



“not mentally,” say Oswald. “there’s too much good form in all our

game. it’s just a way of cut down a game to a formality.”



“but, for instance, sir, would you bowl grounder at cricket?”



“if I think the batsman have be too lazy to learn what to do with

they. why not?”



“if you look at it like _that_, sir!” say Troop and have no more to say.

but he go away marvelling. Oswald be a V.C. yet he look at game

like—like an American, he play to win; it be enough to perplex any

one....



“must confess I don’t see it,” say Troop when Oswald have go....



when at last Troop and Wilmington depart Oswald go with they to the

station—the luggage be send on in the cart—and walk back over the

plough ridge and up the lane with Peter. for a time they keep silence,

but Troop be in both their mind.



“he’s a good sort,” say Peter.



“Admirable—in some way.”



“I think,” say Peter, “you don’t like he. you keep on pull his

leg.”



so Peter have see.



“well, he don’t exercise his brain very much,” say Oswald.



“stop short at his neck,” say Peter. “Exercise, I mean.”



“you and Troop be singularly unlike each other,” say Oswald.



“oh, that’s exactly it. I cann’t make out why I like he. if nothing else

attract I, that would.”



“do he know why he like you?”



“haven’t the ghost of an idea. it worry he at time. make he want to

try and get all over I.”



“do he—at all?”



“lot,” say Peter. “I fag at the blessed Cadet Corps simply because I

like he. at rugger he’s rather a god, you know. and he’s a clean chap.”



“he’s clean.”



“oh, he’s clean. it’s catch,” say Peter, and seem to reflect. “and

in a sort of way lately old Troop’s take to swat. it’s pathetic.”

then with a shade of anxiety, “I don’t think for a moment he twig you

be pull his leg.”



Oswald come to the thing that be really trouble he. “allow for

his class,” say Oswald, “that young man be grow up to an outlook

upon the world about as broad and high as the outlook of a bricklayer’s

labourer.”



Peter reflect impartially, and Oswald note incidentally what a good

profile the boy be develop.



“a clean, serious bricklayer’s labourer,” say Peter, weigh his

“but he may go into Parliament, or have to handle a big business,” say

“Army for troop,” say Peter, “via a university commission.”



“even army have to be handle intelligently nowadays,” say Oswald.



“he’ll go into the cavalry,” say Peter, make one of those tremendous

jump in thought that be characteristic of himself and Joan.





                                  § 12



a day or so after troop’s departure Peter waylay Oswald in the garden.

“I wish you’d come to the Glory Hole and look at an old rat I dissect

the dissection be a good piece of work, the stomach clean out and the

the next morning Oswald stop short in the middle of his shaving,

“I _think_ it’s all right.



“no nonsense about it anyhow.



“but what a tortuous, untraceable business the coming of knowledge be!

                                  § 13



Joan follow a year after Peter to Cambridge. she enter at Newton

come from Highmorton to Newnham be like emerge from some narrow,

Oswald have be at great pain to find out what be the good education

clear tracing of every traceable consequence of the “period” in current

the world with a brain as big as a pin’s head. as Joan and Peter grow

perhaps the problem of make the teacher of youth an inspiring figure

into the Cambridge lecture room and laboratory go Joan and Peter,

he try to find comfort in comparison.

                                  § 14
