                                  § 15



but school and university be only the formal part of education. the

large part of the education of every human being be and always have be

and must be provide by the thing that be. every adult transaction have

as its most important and usually most neglect aspect its effect upon

the mind of the young. behind school and university the Empire itself

be undesignedly address Joan and Peter. it be, so to speak,

gesticulate at they over their teacher’ head and under their

teacher’ arm. it be perform ceremony and exhibit spectacle

of a highly suggestive nature.



in a large and impose form certain idea be steadfastly thrust at

Joan and Peter. more particularly be the idolization of the monarchy

thrust upon they. in term of zeal and reverence the press, the pulpit,

and the world at large direct the innocent mind of Joan and Peter to

the monarch as if that individual be the Reason, the Highest Good and

Crown of the collective life. nothing else in the world of Joan and

Peter get anything like the same tremendous show. their early year be

colour by the reflect glory of the Diamond Jubilee; follow the

funeral pomp of Queen Victoria, with much mobbing of negligent or

impecunious people not in black by the loyal London crowd; then come the

postpone and then the actual coronation of King Edward, public praying

for his health, his stupendous funeral glory; succeed by the

coronation of King George, and finally, about the time that Joan

follow Peter up to Cambridge, the Coronation Durbar. the multitude

which could not go to India go at least to the Scala cinema, and see

the adoration in all its natural colour. reverent crowd choke that

narrow bystreet. across all the life and activity of England, across

all her intellectual and moral effort, hold up legislation,

interfere with industry, stop the traffic, mask every reality

of the collective life, these vast formality trail with a

magnificent priority. nothing be respect as they be respect!

Sober statesman be see invest in strange garment that no sensible

person would surely wear except for the grave reason; the archbishop

and bishop be discover bent with reverence, invoke the name of

God freely, bless the Crown with the utmost gravity, invest the

Sovereign with Robe and Orb, Ring and Sceptre, anoint he with the

Golden Coronation Spoon. either the Crown be itself a matter of

altogether supreme importance to the land or else it be the most

stupendous foolery that ever mock and confuse the grave reality of

a great people’s affair.



the effect of it upon the mind of our two young people

be—complicate. how complicate it be few people realize who have not

closely study the educational process of the british mind as a whole.

then it become manifest that the monarch, the state church, and the

system of title and social precedence center upon the throne,

constitute a system of mental entanglement against which british

education struggle at an enormous disadvantage. the monarchy in Great

Britain be a compromise that be accept by a generation regardless of

education and devoid of any sense of the future. it be now a mask upon

the british face; it be a gaudy and antiquated and embarrassing wrapping

about the energy of the nation. because of it Britain speak to her

youth, as to the world, with two voice. she speak as a democratic

republic, just ever so little crown, and also she speak as a

succulently loyal teutonic monarchy. either she be an adolescent

democracy whose voice be break or an old monarchy at the squeaking

stage. now her voice be the full strong voice of a great people, now it

pipe ridiculously. she perplex the world and stultifie herself.



that be why her education lead up to no such magnificent exposition and

consolidation of purpose as Oswald dream of for his ward. instead, the

track presently lose itself in a maze of prevarication and evasion.

the country be double-minded, double-mindedness have become its habit,

and it have lose the power of decision. every effort to broaden and

modernize university education in Britain encounter insurmountable

difficulty because of this fundamental dispersal of aim. the court get

in the way, the country clergy get in the way, the rule-class family

get in the way. it be impossible to turn a wandering, chance-make track

into a good road until you know where it be to go. and that question of

destination be one that no Englishman before the war could be induce

to put into plain language. doublemindedness have become his second

nature. from the very outset it have take possession of he. when a

young American go to his teacher to ask why he should serve his state,

he be show a flag of thirteen stripe and eight and forty star and

tell a very plain and inspire history. his relation to his country

be thenceforward as simple and unquestionable as a child’s to its

mother. he may be patriotic or unpatriotic as a son may be dutiful or

undutiful, but he will not be muddle-head. but when Joan and Peter

first begin to realize that they belong to the British Empire they

be show a little old german woman and tell that reverence for she

link we in a common abjection with the million of India. they be

tell also that really this little old lady do nothing of the slight

importance and that the country be the free democracy on earth, rule

by its elect representative. and each of these preposterously

contradictory story pursue they in an endless series of variation up

to adolescence....



to two naturally clear-head young people it become presently as

palpably absurd to have a great union of civilized state thus

impersonate as it be to have Wall impersonate by Snout the Tinker in

_a Midsummer Night’s Dream_. they be already jeer at royalty and

the church with Aunt Phyllis long before they go up to Cambridge.

there they find plenty of associate to jeer with they. and there too

they find a quite congenial parallel stream of jeer against

Parliament, which pretend to represent the national mind and quality,

but which be elect by a method that manifestly give no chance to any

candidate who be not nominate by a party organization. in time of

long establish peace, when the tradition of generation have

establish the illusion of the profound human security, man’s mind

be not greatly distress by grotesqueness and absurdity in their

political form. it be all part of the humour and the good-humour of

life. when one believe that all the tiger in the jungle be dead, it

be quite amusing to walk along the jungle path in a dressing-gown with

a fan instead of a gun. Joan and Peter grow up to the persuasion that

the crown above they be rather a good joke, and that Parliament and its

job and party flummery be also a joke, and that the large, deep

rottenness in this british world about they be perhaps in the nature of

thing and anyhow beyond their altering. they too be become

double-minded accord to the tradition of the land.



yet beneath this acquiescence in the deep-root political paradox of

Britain they be capable of the keen interest in a number of

question that they really believe be alive. it become manifest to

they that this great golden preposterous world be mar by certain

injustice and unkindness. something call Labour they hear be

unhappy and complain of unfair treatment, certain grumbling come from

India and Ireland, and there be a curiously exciting subject which

demand investigation and reform activity call the sex question.

and generally there seem to be, for no particular reason, a lot of

restriction upon people’s conduct.



in addition Peter have acquire from Oswald, rather by way of example

than precept, a very definite persuasion, and Joan have acquire a

persuasion that be perhaps not quite so clearly and deeply cut, that to

make it respectable there ought to be something in one’s life in the

nature of special work. in Oswald’s case it be his african interest.

Peter think that his own work might perhaps be biological. but that

one’s work ought to join on to the work of the people or that all the

good work in the world should make one whole be a notion that have not

apparently enter Peter’s mind. Oswald with his dread of preachment

be doubtful about any deliberate dissertation in the matter. he get

Peter to begin the _Martyrdom of Man_, which have so profoundly affect

his own life, but Peter express doubt about the correctness of

Reade’s egyptian history, and put the book aside and do not go on

read it.



at time Oswald try to say something to Joan and Peter of his

conception of the Empire as a great human enterprise, play a

dominating part in the establishment of a world peace and a world

civilization, and give a form and direction and pride to every life

within it. but these perpetual noise of royalty in its vulgar, most

personal form, the loyalist chatter of illiterate woman and the clamour

of the New Imperialism to “tax the foreigner” and exploit the empire for

gain, drown his intention while it be still unspeak in his mind.

there be moment when he could already ask himself whether this empire

he have shape his life to serve, this knightly empire of his,

enlighten, righteous, and predominant, be anything well than a

dream—or a lie.





                                  § 16



when Joan leave Highmorton she come into the market-place of idea. she

Young Winterbaum be rather a surprise. he have get his feature together

“my people make I come up here,” he explain; “family consideration,

“ear and eye and mouth wide open,” say Joan.



“this show ben’t worth it. do you ever drift towards Chelsea?”

she have not.

                                  § 17



it be the ambition of Mrs. Sheldrick and her remain daughter—some

there be downstairs and the passage and staircase and upstairs, a

here too come young Huntley, who have write three novel before he be

the incident be the arrival of Peter.



early in the afternoon he have vanish from Mrs. Jepson’s, where he and

the question that Peter be ask Joan mutely across the room be in

then, not quite reassure by an uncontrollable scowl, Peter look away

he be look for Mir Jelalludin, the interesting young Indian with the

beautifully model face, whom Joan have meet and talk to at the Club

of Strange Faiths. at the Club of Strange Faiths one day she have be

suddenly move to make a short speech about the buddhist idea of

to score off Miss Frobisher. she say that she think the real

intention be not extinction at all, but the escape of the individual

consciousness after its living pilgrimage from one incarnate self to

another into the universal consciousness. that be the very antithesis

of extinction; one lose oneself indeed, but one lose oneself not in

darkness and non-existence, but in light and the fullness of existence.

there be all the difference between a fainting fit and ecstasy between

these two conception. and it be true of experience that one be least

oneself, least self-conscious and egotistical at one’s time of great

excitement.



Mir Jelalludin receive these remark with earnest applause. he make as

if to speak after she, rise in his place, and then hastily sit down.

afterwards he come and speak to she, quite modestly and simply, without

the least impertinence.



he explain, with a pleasant staccato accent and little slip in his

pronunciation that suggest restrict english conversation and much

reading of book, how greatly he have be want to say just what she

have say, “so bew-ti-fully,” but he have be restrain by “impafction

of the pronunsation. so deefi’clt, you know.” one hear english people

so often not do justice to indian idea so that it be very pleasant

to hear they be quite sympathetically put.



there be something very pleasing in the real intellectual excitement

that have make he speak to she, and there be something very pleasing to

the eye in the neat precision with which his brown feature be

chisel and the decisive accuracy of every single hair on his brow. he

be, he explain, a Moslem, but he be interested in every school of

indian thought. he be afraid he be not very orthodox, and he show a

smile of the most perfect tooth. there have always be a tendency to

universalism in indian thought, that affect even the Moslem. do she

know anything of the Brahmo Somaj? have she read any novel of

Chatterji’s? there be at least one great novel of his the English ought

to read, the _Ananda Math_. no one could understand indian thought

properly who have not read it. he have a translation of it into

English—which he would lend she.



would she be interested to read it?



might he send it to she?



Joan’s chaperon be a third year girl who put no bar upon these

amenity. Joan accept the book and throw out casually that she

sometimes go to Bunny Cuspard’s tea. if Mr. Jelalludin send she

Chatterji’s book she could return it to Bunny Cuspard’s room.



it be in Bunny Cuspard’s room that Peter have first become aware of this

exotic friendship. he discover his Joan snugly in a corner listen

to an explanation of the attitude of Islam towards woman. it have be

enormously misrepresented in Christendom. Mr. Jelalludin be very

earnest in his exposition, and Joan listen with a pleasant smile and

regard he pleasantly and wish that she could run her finger just

once along his eyebrow without have her motive misunderstood.



but at the sight of his Joan engage in this confabulation Peter

suddenly discover all the fierce trait of race pride. he fret

about the room and be rude to other people and watch a book change

hand, and wait scarcely twenty second after the end of Joan’s

conversation before he come up to she.



“I say, Joan,” he say, “you cann’t go chum with Indians anyhow.”



“Peter,” she say, “we’ve chum with India.”



“oh, nonsense! not socially. their standard be different.”



“I hope they be,” say Joan. “the way you make these indian boy here

feel like outcast be disgraceful.”



“they’re different. the man ben’t uncivil to they. but it ben’t for

you——”



“it’s for all english people to treat they well. he’s a charming young

man.”



“it ben’t _do_, Joan.”



“it’s go to be, Petah.”



“you’re meet he again?”



“if I think proper.”



“oh!” say Peter, baffle for the time. “all _right_, Joan.”



a fierce exchange of note follow. “don’t you understand the fellow’s

a polygamist?” Peter write. “he keep his woman in purdah. no decent

woman could be talk to in India as he talk to you. not even an

introduction. personally, I’ve no objection to any friend you make

provide they be decent friend....”



“he ben’t a polygamist,” Joan reply. “I’ve ask he. and every one

say he’s a first-rate cricketer. as for decent friend, Peter——”



the issue have be still undecided when they come down for the Christmas

vacation.



so far Joan have maintain her position without passion. but now

suddenly her indignation at Peter’s interference flare to heaven. that

he should come here, hot from Soho, to tyrannize over _her_! Indians

indeed! as if Hetty Reinhart ben’t bad than a Gold Coast nigger!...



the only outward manifestation of this wild storm of resentment have be

her one instant’s scowl at Peter. thereafter Joan become again the

quiet, intelligently watchful young woman she have be all that evening.

but now she turn herself through an angle of about thirty degree

towards Huntley, who be talk to old Mrs. Jex, the wonder of

Hampstead, who use to know George Eliot and Huxley, the while he be

regard Joan with sidelong covetousness. Joan lift her eye towards

he with an expression of innocent interest. the slightly project

blue eye seem to leap in response.



Mrs. Jex be always rather inattentive to her listener when she be

recite her reminiscence, and Huntley be able to turn away from she

quietly without interrupt the flow.



the Sheldrick circle scorn the formality of introduction. “be you

from the Slade school?” say Huntley.



“Cambridge,” say Joan.



“my name’s Gavan Huntley.”



but this be go to be more amusing than Joan have expect. this be a

real live novelist—Joan’s first. not a fortnight ago she have read _the

Pernambuco Bunshop_, and think it rather clever and silly.



“not _the_ Gavan Huntley?” she say.



his face become faintly luminous with satisfaction. “just Gavan

Huntley,” he say with a large smile.



“the Pernambuco Bunshop?” she say.



“guilty,” he plead, smile still more naïvely.



one have expect something much less natural in a novelist.



“I _love_ it,” say Joan, and Huntley be her to do what she like

with. Joon’s idea of a proper conversation require it to be in a

corner. “do sheldrick never sit down?” she ask. “I’ve be stand

all the evening.”



“they cann’t,” he say confidentially. “they’re the other sort of dutch

doll, the cheap sort, that haven’t get joint at the knee.”



“Antonia sometimes lean against the wall.”



“her utmost. the next thing would be to sit on the floor with her leg

straight out. I’ve see she do that. but there be a sort of bench on the

staircase landing.”



Thither they make their way, and there presently Peter find they.



he find they because he be make for that very corner in the company

of Sydney Sheldrick. “Hullo!” say Sydney. “that you, Joan?”



“we’ve take this corner for the evening,” say Huntley, lay a

control hand on Joan’s pretty wrist.



Joan and Peter regard each other darkly.



“there ought to be more seat about somewhere,” say Sydney. “come up to

the divan, old Peter....”



of course Peter must object to Huntley. they be scarcely out of the

Sheldricks’ house when he begin. “that man Huntley’s a bad egg, Joan.

everybody know it.”



for a time they dispute about Huntley.



“Peter,” say Joan, with affected calm, “be there any man, do you think,

to whom so—so untrustworthy a girl as I be might safely talk?”



Peter seem to consider. “there’s chap like Troop,” he say.



“Troop!” say Joan, rely on her intonation.



“it ben’t that you’re untrustworthy,” say Peter.



“fragile?”



“it’s the look and tone of thing.”



“I wonder how you get these idea.”



“what idea?”



“of how I behave in a corner with Jelalludin or Gavan Huntley.”



“I haven’t suggest anything.”



“you’ve suggest everything. do you think I collect stray kiss like

Sydney Sheldrick? do you think I’m a dirty little—little—cocotte like

Hetty Reinhart?”



“_Joan!_”



“_well_,” say Joan savagely, and say no more.



Peter come to the defence of Hetty belatedly. “how can you say such

thing of Hetty?” he ask. “what can you know about she?”



“pah! I can smell what she be across a room. do you think I’m an

absolute young fool, Peter?”



“you’ve get no right, Joan——”



“why argue, Peter, why argue? when thing be plain. cann’t you go your

own way, Peter”—Joan be annoy to find suddenly that she be weep.

tear be run down her face. but the road be dark, and perhaps if

she give no sign Peter would not see. “you go your own way, Peter, go

your own way, and let I go mine.”



Peter be silent for a little while. then compunction betray itself in

his voice.



“it’s you I’m think of, Joan. I cann’t bear to see you make yourself

cheap.”



“cheap! and _you_?”



“I’m different. I’m altogether different. a man be.”



Silence for a time. Joan seem to push back her hair, and so smear

the tear from her face.



“we interfere with each other,” she say at last. “we interfere with

each other. what be the good of it? you’ve get to go your way and I’ve

get to go mine. we use to have fun—lot of fun. _now_....”



she couldn’t say any more for a while.



“I’m go my own way, Peter. it’s a different way——leave I alone. keep

off!”



they say no more. when they get in they find Miss Jepson sit by

the fire, and she have get they some cocoa and biscuit. the headache

that have keep she from the Sheldrick festival have lift, and Joan

plunge at once into a gay account of the various people she have see

almost imperceptible glance and wonder whether he sulk or grieve.

do he feel as she feel? if he sulk—well, confound he! but what if

this perplexing dissension hurt he as much as it be hurt she!





                                  § 18



Joan have long since lose that happiness, that perfect assurance, that

intense appreciation of the beauty in thing which have come to she with

early adolescence. she be troubled and perplex in all her way. she

be full now of stormy, indistinct desire and fear, and a gnawing,

indefinite impatience. no religion have convince she of a purpose in her

life, neither Highmorton nor Cambridge have suggest any mundane

devotion to she, nor point she ambition to a career. the only career

these feminine school and college recognize be a career of academic

success and High School teaching, intercalate with hunger strike for

the vote, and Joan have early decide she would rather die than teach in

a High School. nor have she the quiet assurance her own beauty would have

give she in an early generation of a discreet choice of lover and

marriage and live “happily ever afterwards.” she have a horror of

marriage lurk in her composition; Mrs. Pybus and Highmorton have each

contribute to that; every one around her speak of it as an entire

abandonment of freedom. moreover there be this queerness about she

birth—she be begin to understand well now in what that queerness

consist—that seem to put she outside the customary ceremony of

veil and orange blossom. why do they not tell she all about it—what

her mother be and where her mother be? it must be a pretty awful

business, if neither Aunt Phyllis nor Aunt Phœbe would ever allude to

it. it would have to come out—perhaps some monstrous story—before she

could marry. and who could one marry? she could not conceive herself

marry any of these boy she meet, live somewhere coope up in a

little house with solemn old Troop, or under the pursue eye, the

convulsive worship, of Wilmington. she have no object in life, no star by

which to steer, and she be full of the fever of life. she be get

awfully old. she be eighteen. she be nineteen. soon she would be

twenty.



all her be, in her destitution of any other aim that have the

slight hold upon her imagination, be cry out for a lover.



it be a lover she want, not a husband; her mind make the clear

distinction between the two. he would come and unrest would cease,

confusion would cease and beauty would return. her lover haunt all her

life, an invisible yet almost present person. she could not imagine his

face nor his form, he be the blankest of being, and yet she be so

sure she know he that if she be to see he away down a street or

across a crowded room, instantly, she believe, she would recognize he.

and until he come life be a torment of suspense. life be all wrong and

discordant, so wrong and discordant that at time she could have hate

her lover for keep she wait so wretchedly.



and she have to go on as though this suspense be nothing. she have to

disregard this vast impatience of her be. and the good way to do

that, it seem to she, be to hurry from one employment to another,

never to be alone, never without some occupation, some excitement. she

break with Peter have an extraordinary effect of release in her mind.

Hitherto, whatever her resentment have be she have admit in practice

his claim to exact a certain discretion from she; his opinion have be,

in spite of her resentment, a standard for she. now she have no standard

at all—unless it be a rebellious purpose to spite he. on Joan’s

personal conduct the thought of Oswald, oddly enough, have scarcely any

influence at all. she adore he as one might a political or historical

hero; she want to stand well in his sight, but the idea of he do not

pursue she into the detail of her behaviour at all. he seem

preoccupy with idea and unobservant. she have never have any struggle

with he; he have never make she do anything. and as for Aunts Phyllis

and Phœbe—while the latter seem to make vague gesture towards quite

unutterable liberty, the former maintain an attitude of nervous

disavowal. she be a woman far too uncertain-minded for plain speaking.

she be a dear. clearly she hate cruelty and baseness; except in regard

to such thing she set no bound.



Hitherto Joan have have a very few flirtation; the extreme thing upon

her conscience be Bunny Cuspard’s kiss. she have the natural

shilly-shally of a girl; she be strongly move to all sort of

flirting and experimenting with love, and very adventurous and curious

in these matter; and also she have a system of inhibition, pride,

hesitation, fastidiousness, and something beyond these thing, a sense

of some ultimate value that might easily be lose, that hold she back.

rebel against Peter have somehow also set she rebel against these

restraint. why shouldn’t she know this and that? why shouldn’t she try

this and that? why, for instance, be she always “shut up” Adela

whenever she begin to discourse in her peculiar way upon the great

theme? just a timid prude she have be, but now——.



and all this about undesirable people and unseemly place, all this

picking and choose as though the world be mud; what nonsense it be!

she could take care of herself surely!



she begin deliberately to feel her way through all her friendship to

see whether this thing, passion, lurk in any of they. it be an

interesting exercise of her wit to try over a youth like old Troop, for

example; to lure he on by a touch of flattery, a betrayal of warmth in

her interest, to reciprocal advance. at first Troop ben’t in the least

in love with she, but she succeed in suggest to he that he be.

but the passion in he release an unsuspected fund of egotistical

discourse; he develop a disposition to explain himself and his mental

letter. even when he be rouse to a sense of she as lovable, he do not

become really interested in she but only in his love for she. he arrive

at one stride at the same unanalytical acceptance of she as of his God

and the Church and the King and his parent and all the rest of the

anglican system of thing. she be his girl—“the kid.” he really ben’t

interested in those other thing any more than he be in she; once he

have give she her rôle in relation to he his attention return to

set herself to avoid, miss, and escape from Troop on every possible

occasion. but Troop pride himself upon his persistence. he take to

write her immense, ill-spelt, manly letter, with sentence begin:

“you understand I very little if——.” it be clear he be her only

until some simple, purer, more receptive and acquisitive girl swam into

his ken.



Wilmington, on the other hand, be a silent covetous lover. Joan could

make he go white, but she could not make he talk. she be a little

afraid of he and quite sure of he. but he be not the sort of young

man one can play with, and she marvel greatly that any one could

dance impudently. he could pat Joan’s shoulder, press her hand, slip

contact as though it be all nothing. do these approach warm her

blood? do she warm his? anyhow it don’t matter, and it ben’t

anything.



then there be Graham Prothero, a very good-look friend of Peter’s,

whom she have meet while skate. he have a lively eye, and jump after a

have get into his dream.



meanwhile Joan’s curiosity have not spare Jelalludin. she have have he

discourse on the beauty of indian love, and spin for her

any of these youth she could make her definite lover she know, by an

thing must come to she; it must take possession of she. sometimes,

be who be to set all thing right. there be moment when it seem

about to happen. and then the illusion pass, and she see clearly that

it be just old Bunny or just staccato Mir Jelalludin.



in Huntley, Joan find something more intriguing than this pursuit of

the easy and the innocent. Huntley talk with a skilful impudence that

make a bold choice of topic seem the most natural in the world. he

present himself as a leader in a great emancipation of woman. they

be to be free from “the bondage of sex.” the phrase awaken a warm

response in Joan, who be find sex a yoke about her imagination. Sex,

Huntley declare, should be as incidental in a woman’s life as it be in

a man’s. but before that could happen the world must free its mind from

the “superstition of chastity,” from the idea that by one single step a

woman pass from the recognizable into an impossible category. we make

no such distinction in the case of man; an artist or a business man be

not suddenly thrust out of the social system by a sexual incident. a

woman be either Mrs. or Miss; a gross publication of elemental fact

that be surely her private affair. no one ask whether a man have

find his lover. why should one proclaim it in the case of a woman by a

conspicuous change of her name? here, and not in any matter of vote or

economic be the real feminine grievance. his indignation be

contagious. it march with all Joan’s accumulate prejudice against

marriage, and all her grow resentment at the way in which emotional

unrest be distract and perplex her will and spoil her work at

lie in the heroic abandonment of the “fetish of chastity,” Joan be

sensible of a certain lagging of spirit. a complex of instinct that

conspire to adumbrate that unseen, unknown, and yet tyrannous lover,

who would not leave she in peace and yet would not reveal himself, stand

between she and the extremity of Huntley’s logic.



there be moment when he seem to be pretend to fill that

oppressive void; moment when he seem only to be hint at himself as

soliloquize. “I’ve not feel like this before.” then very flatteringly

and reproachfully, “you’re so damned intelligent, Joan. and you dance—as

though God make you to make I happy.” he get she out into an open

a yard dimly light by chinese lantern, and at the dark turn of the

have seize she with Bunny and Adela.



she do not know whether she like he now or hate he. she feel

“I’ve get to dance with Ralph Winterbaum,” she say.



“say you’re not offend,” he plead.

“Peter,” she say, “I want to go home.”



he survey she. she be flush and ruffle, and his eye and mouth

harden.



“it’s early.”



“I want to go home.”



“right. you’re a bit of a responsibility, Joan.”



“don’t, then,” she say shortly, and turn round to greet Huntley as

though nothing have happen between they.



but she keep in the light and the crowd, and there be a constraint

Joan’s dread of any appearance of timid virtue be overwhelming. and she

“it’s the interruption I don’t like,” he say.



“you get I no ice, you get I no lemonade,” she complain abruptly.



“that’s what my dear Aunt Adelaide use to call change the subject.”



“it’s the cry of outraged nature.”



“but I see you have an ice—not half an hour ago.”



“not the ice I want,” say Joan.



“Distracting Joan! I suppose I must get you that ice. but about the

tea?”



“I _hate_ tea,” say Joan, with a force of decision that for a time

dispose of his project.



just for a moment he hover with his eye on she, weigh just what

                                  § 19



but Joan and Peter be not always grimly silent with one another. the

black and inexplicable mood come and pass again. between these

perplexing mute conflict of will, they be still good friend. when

they be alone together they be always disposed to be good friend;

it be the presence and excitement and competition of other that

disturb their relationship; it be when the specie invade their

individuality and threaten their association with its occult and

passionate demand. they would motor-cycle together through the lane

and road of Hertfordshire, lunch cheerfully at wayside inn, brotherly

and sisterly, relapse again into mere boy and girl playfellow, race and

climb tree, or, like fellow-student, share their common room amicably,

dispute over a multitude of question, and talk to Oswald. they both have

a fair share of scholarly ambition and read pretty hard. they have both

now reach the newspaper-read stage. Peter be begin to take an

interest in politic, he want to discuss socialism and economic

organization thoroughly; biological work alone among all scientific

study carry a philosophy of its own that illuminate these

question, and Oswald be happy to try over his current interest in the

light of these fresh, keen young mind. Peter be a discriminating

advocate of the idea of Guild socialism; Oswald be still a cautious

individualist drift towards Fabianism. the great labour trouble that

have follow the Coronation of King George have be necessary to

convince he that all be not well with the economic organization of the

empire. Hitherto he have take economic organization for grant; it

ben’t a matter for Sydenhams.



Pelham Ford at such time become a backwater from the main current of

human affair, the current that be now grow steadily more rapid and

trouble. thinking could go on at Pelham Ford. there be still force

in that old-world valley to resist the infection of intense impatience

that be spread throughout the world. the old red house behind its

wall and iron gate seem as stable as the little hill about it; the

road and the row of great tree between the stream and the road, the

high pathway and the ford and the village promise visibly to endure for

a thousand year. it be when Aunt Phyllis or Aunt Phœbe descend upon

the place to make a party, “get a lot of young people down and brighten

thing up,” or when the two youngster go to London together into the

Sheldrick translation of the _Quartier Latin_, or when they meet in

Cambridge in some crowded chattering room that imagination grow

feverish, fierce jealousy awake, temperament jar, and the urge of

adolescence have they in its clutch again.



it be during one of these party at Pelham Ford that Joan be to

happen upon two great realization, realization of so profound an

effect that they may serve to mark the end for she of this great process

of emotional upheaval and discovery that be call adolescence. they

leave she shaped. they come to she in no dramatic circumstance, they

be mere conversational incident, but their effect be profound and

conclusive.



in the New Year of 1914 Oswald be to take Peter to Russia for three

week. before his departure, Aunt Phœbe have insist that there should

be a Christmas gathering of the young at Pelham Ford. they would skate

or walk or toboggan or play hockey by day, and dress up and dance or

improvise charade and burlesque in the evening. one or two sheldrick

would come, Peter and Joan could bring down any stray friend who have no

home Christmas to call they, and Aunts Phyllis and Phœbe would collect a

few young people in London.



the gathering be from the first miscellaneous. Christmas be a home

time for the undergraduate of both sex, such modern spirit as the

home fail to attract use to go in those day in great drove to the

swiss winter sport, and Joan find nobody but an ambitious Scotch girl

whom she know but slightly and Miss Scroby the historian, who be rather

a friend for Aunt Phyllis than herself. Peter discover that Wilmington

intensely preferred Pelham Ford to his parental roof, and bring also

two other stray man, orphan. this selection be supplement by Aunt

Phœbe, who have latterly make Hetty Reinhart her especial protégée. she

descant upon the obvious beauty of Hetty and upon the courage that have

induce Hetty to leave her home in Preston and manage for herself in a

great lonely studio upon Haverstock Hill. “the bachelor woman,” say

Aunt Phœbe; “arm with a latchkey and her purity. a vote shall follow.

Hetty be not one of the devoted yet. but I have my hope. we need our

Beauty Chorus. Hetty shall be our Helen, and Holloway our Troy.”



so with Peter’s approval Hetty be add to the list before Joan could

express an opinion, and appear with a moderate sized valise that

contain some extremely exiguous evening costume, and a steadfast eye

that rest most frequently on Peter. in addition Aunt Phœbe bring two

irish sister, one frivolous, the other just recuperate from the

hunger-strike that have end her imprisonment for window-breaking in

pursuit of the vote, and a very shy youth of seventeen, Pryce, the

caddie-poet. Huntley be to constitute a sort of outside element in the

party, share apartment with young Sopwith Greene the musician, in the

village about half a mile away. these two man be to work and keep away

when they choose, and come in for meal and sport as they think fit.

at the eleventh hour have come a pathetic and irresistible telegram from

Adela Murchison:



              alone Xmas may I come wire if inconvenient.



and she, too, be comprehend.



the vicarage girl be available for game and meal except on Sunday

and Christmas Day; there be a friendly family of five son and two

daughter at Braughing, a challenging hockey club at Bishop’s Stortford,

and a scratch collection at Newport available by motor-car for a pick-up

match if the weather prove, as it do prove, too open for skating.



Oswald commonly stand these Aunt party for a day or so and then

retreat to the Climax Club. always beforehand he promise himself

great interest and pleasure in the company of a number of exceptionally

bright and representative youth and maiden of the modern school, but

always the actual gathering fatigue he and distress he. the youth

and maidens wouldn’t be representative, they talk too loud, too fast

and too inconsecutively for he, their wit be too rapid and hard—and

they be all over the house. it be hard to get mental contact with

they. they pair off when there be no game afoot, and if ever talk

at table cease to be fragmentary Aunt Phœbe take control of it. in a

day or so he would begin to feel at Pelham Ford like a cat during a

removal; drive out of his dear library, which be the only available

room for dancing, he would try to work in his unaccustomed study, with

vivid, interesting young figure pass his window in group of two or

three, or only too audibly discuss the world, each other, and their

general arrangement, in the hall.



his home would have feel altogether chaotic to he but for the presence,

the unswerving, if usually invisible, presence of Mrs. Moxton, observe

time and season, provide copious suitable meal, dominate by mean

of the gong, replace furniture at every opportunity, refer with a

calm dignity to Joan as the hostess for all the rule and sanction she

deem advisable. from unseen point of view one feel her eye. one’s

consolation for the tumult lie in one’s confidence in this discretion

that lie behind it. even Aunt Phœbe’s way of speak of “our good

Moxton” do not mask the fact of the case. Pelham Ford be rule. at

Pelham Ford even Aunt Phœbe come down to meal in time. at Pelham Ford

no fire, once light, ever go out before it be right for it to do so.

you might in pursuit of facetious end choose to put your pyjama

outside your other clothe, wrap your window curtain about you, sport

and dance, and finally, draw off to some other end, abandon these

wrapping in the dining-room or on the settee on the landing. when you

go to bed your curtain hang primly before your window again, and your

pyjama lie fold and reprove upon your bed.



the disposition of the new generation to change its clothe, adopt

fantastic clothe, and at any reasonable excuse get right out of its

clothe altogether, greatly impress Oswald. Hetty in particular

betray a delight in the beauty of her own body with a freedom that

in Oswald’s youth be permit only to sculpture. but Adela make no

secret of her plump shoulder and arm, and Joan strike he as

insensitive. Skimpiness be the fashion in dress at that time. no doubt

it be all for the good, like the frankness of spartan maiden. and

another thing that bring a flavour of harsh modernity into the house

be the perpetual music and dancing that rage about it. there be a

pianola in the common room of Joan and Peter, but when they be alone

at home it serve only for an occasional outbreak of Bach, or Beethoven,

or Chopin. now it be in a state of almost continuous eruption. Aunt

Phyllis have order a number of roll of dance music from the

Orchestrelle library, and in addition she have bring down a gramophone.

never before have music be so easy in the world as it be in those

day. in Oswald’s youth music, good music, be the rare privilege of a

gifted few, one hear it rarely and listen with reverence. nowadays

Joan could run through a big fragment of the Ninth Symphony, give a

render far well than any but a highly skilled pianist could play,

while she be wait for Peter to come to breakfast. and this Christmas

party be pervade with one Steps and two Steps, pianola call to

gramophone and gramophone to pianola, and trip foot somewhere never

fail to respond. Most of these young people dance with the wild

informality. but Hetty and the young irish girl be serious

propagandist of certain strange american dance, the Bunny Hug, and the

Fox Trot; Sopwith Greene and Adela tango and be get quite good

at it, and Huntley want to teach Joan an Apache dance. Joan dance by

rule and pattern or by the light of nature as occasion require.



the Christmas dinner be at one o’clock, a large disorderly festival.

Gavan Huntley and Sopwith Greene come in for it. Oswald carve a turkey,

Aunt Phyllis dispense beef; the room be darken and the pudding be

bring in flame blue and distribute in flickering flame.

mince-pie, almond and raisin, Brazil nut, orange, tangerine,

Carlsbad plum, crystallize fruit and candy peel; nothing be

miss from the customary feast. then come a mighty banging of

cracker, pre-war cracker, contain elaborate paper costume and

preposterous gift. Wilmington eat little and Huntley a great deal, and

whenever Joan glance at they they seem to be look at she. Hetty,

flush and excited, become really pretty in a paper cap of liberty, she

wave a small tricolour flag and kneel up in her chair to pull cracker

across the table; Peter win a paper cockscomb and be move to come and

group himself under her arm and crow as “Vive la France!” the two irish

girl start an abusive but genial argument with Sopwith Greene upon

the irish question. Aunt Phœbe sit near Aunt Phyllis and discourse on

whether she ought to go to prison for the Vote. “I try to assault

policeman,” she say. “but they elude I.” one of Peter’s Cambridge

friend, it come to light, have be present at a great scene in which

Aunt Phœbe have figure. he emerge from his social obscurity and

describe the affair rather amusingly.



it have be at an anti-suffrage meeting in West Kensington, and Aunt

Phœbe have obtain access to the back row of the platform by some

specious device. among the notability in front Lady Charlotte Sydenham

and her solicitor have figure. Lady Charlotte have enter upon that last

great phase in a woman’s life, that phase know to the vulgar observer

as “old lady’s second wind.” it be a phase often of great go and

determination, a joy to the irreverent young and a marvel and terror to

the middle-aged. she have take to politic, plunge into public

speak, face audience. it be the Insurance Act of 1912 that have

first move she to such publicity. Stung by the outrageous possibility

of independent-spirited servant she have give up her usual trip to

Italy in the winter and stay to combat Lloyd George. from mere

subscription and drawing-room conversation and committee to

drawing-room meeting and at last to public meeting have be an easy

series of step for she. at first a mere bridle indignation on the

platform, she presently speak. as a speaker she combine reminiscence

of Queen Elizabeth at Tilbury and Marie Antoinette on the scaffold with

vast hiatus peculiar to herself. “my good people,” she would say,

disregard the more conventional method of opening, “have we

neglect our servant or have we not? be any shop Gal or factory Gal

half so well off as a servant in a good house? be she? I ask. the food

alone! the moral! and now we be to be tax and make to lick stamp

like a lot of galley-slave to please a bumptious little Welsh

solicitor! for my part I shall discontinue all my charitable

subscription until this abominable Act be strike off the Statute Book.

every one. and as for buy these preposterous stamp—— rather than

lick a stamp I will eat skilly in prison. Stamps indeed. I’d as soon

lick the man’s boot. that’s all I have to say, Mr. Chairman (or ’my

Lord,’ or ’Mrs. Chairman,’ as the case might be). I hope it will be

enough. thank you.” and she would sit down breathe heavily and look

for eye to meet.



for the great agitation against the Insurance Act that sort of thing

suffice, but when it come to testify against an unwomanly clamour

for vote, the argument become more complicated and interruption

difficult to handle, and after an unpleasant experience when she be

only able to repeat in steadily rise tone, “I be not one of the

Shrieking Sisterhood” ten time over to a derisive roomful, she decide

to adopt the more feminine expedient of a spokesman. she have fall back

upon Mr. Grimes, who like all solicitor have his parliamentary

ambition, and she take he about with she in the comfortable brown car

that have long since replace the white horse, and sit beside he while

he speak and approve of he with both hand. Mr. Grimes have be

address the meeting when Aunt Phœbe make her interruption. he have

be argue that the unfitness of woman for military service debar

they from the vote. “let we face the fact,” he say, draw the air in

between his tooth. “ultimately—ultimately all social organization rest

upon Force.”



it be just at this moment that cry of “Order, Order,” make he aware

of a feminine figure close beside he. he turn to meet the heaving

wrath of Aunt Phœbe’s face. there be just an instant’s scrutiny. then

he remember, he remember everything, and with a wild shriek leapt

clean off the platform upon the toe of the front row of the audience.



“if you _touch_ I!” he scream....



the young man tell the incident briefly and brightly.



“thereby hang a tale,” say Aunt Phœbe darkly, and become an allusive

Sphinx for the rest of the dinner.



“I shake that man,” she say at last to Pryce.



“What—_him_?” say Pryce, stare round-eyed at the young man from

Cambridge.



“no, the man at the meeting.”



“what—afterwards?” say Pryce, lose and baffle.



“no,” say Aunt Phœbe; “_before_.”



Pryce try to look intelligent, and nod his head very fast to

conceal the fear and confusion in his mind.



amidst all these voice and festivity sit Oswald, with a vast paper

cap shape rather like the dome of a russian church cock over his

blind side, listen distractedly, note this and that, say little,

think many thing.



the banquet end at last, and every one drift to the library.



Affairs hover vaguely for a time. Peter hand cigarette about. some

Peter laugh, nothing loath. “oh! but this be glorious!” pant Hetty.



“come and dance, too, Joan,” say Wilmington.



“it’s stuffy!” say Joan.



Oswald, contemplate a retreat to his study armchair, find she

stuffy in there.”



Oswald meet Huntley’s large grey eye for a moment. he have an instinctive

“right-o,” say Oswald, and hover doubtfully.



Aunt Phœbe appear on the landing above carry off a rather reluctant

Joan and Huntley pass by outside his window. he sit down in front of

he be, he reflect, rather like a strange animal among all this youth.

they treat he as something remotely old; he be one-and-fifty, and

yet this gregarious stir and excitement that brighten their eye and

quicken their blood stir he too. he couldn’t help a feeling of

be more reckless, they do more and go far than his generation

have go. in his time, with his sort of people, there have be the

virtuous life which be, one have to admit it, slow, and the fast life

which be noisily, criminally, consciously and vulgarly vicious. this

generation don’t seem to be vicious, and be anything but slow. how far

do they go? he have be note little thing between Peter and this

be she manœuvre to marry the boy? she must be well on the way to

table. one could not conceive of dolly permit such thing. be this

an age of dare innocence, or what be come to the young people?



Joan seem more dignified than the other, but she, too, have she

pink sash. at least, dolly must have be _about_ Joan’s age when first

he have see she. eighteen—seventeen? of course a year or so make no end

of difference just at this age....



from such meditation Oswald be rouse by the tumult of a car outside.

people you have about?”



“miscellaneous friend,” say Oswald.



“you’ve get a touch of grey in your hair,” she note.



“I must get a big blond wig,” he say.



“you might do bad.”



“you’re look as fresh as paint,” he remark, scrutinize she

“former ward,” Oswald interject.



“the Gal I pass. where be Master Stubland? I’ll just look at he. be

he one of these people make a noise in here?”



she go to the door of the library and survey the scene with an

“Higgledy-piggledy I call it. which be Peter?”



Peter be indicate.



“well, he’s grow! who’s that fast-look girl he’s hug?”



Peter detach himself from Hetty and come forward.



his ancient terror of the whisker-woman still hang about he, but he

make a brave show of courage. “glad you’ve not forget we, Lady

Charlotte,” he say.



“not much stubland about _he_,” she remark to Oswald. “there’s a

photograph of you before you blow your face off—”



“it’s his mother he’s like,” say Oswald, lay a hand on Peter’s

shoulder.



“I never see a family harp on themselves more than the Sydenhams,” the

lady declare. “it’s like the Habsburg chin.... this one of the new

improper dance, Peter?”



“_honi soit_,” say Peter.



“People have be whip at the cart’s tail for less. in my mother’s

time no decent woman waltz. even—in crinoline. now a waltz ben’t

close enough for they.”



the gramophone come to an end and choke. “thank goodness!” say Lady

Charlotte.



“willn’t you dance yourself, Lady Charlotte?” say Peter, stand up to

she politely.



the hard blue eye regard he with a slightly impair disfavour, but

the old lady make no reply.



they hear the startled voice of the youth from Cambridge. “it’s

_she_!”...



but the sting of the call be at its end.



“so that’s Peter,” say Lady Charlotte, as the chauffeur and Oswald

assist she back into her liver-colour car. “I tell you I see the

Gal?”



“Joan?”



“I pass she on the road half a mile from here. come upon she and her

’gentleman friend’—I suppose she’d call he—as we turn a corner. a

snap-shot so to speak. it’s the walk-out instinct. blood will tell. I

see she, but she don’t see I. lose, she be, to thing mundane. but it

be plain enough how thing be. a tiff. some lover’ quarrel. wake

_up_, Unwin.”



“what do you mean?”



“what I say,” say Lady Charlotte.



“that fellow Huntley!”



“_ha!_ so now you’ll lock the stable door! what else be to be

expect?”



“but this be nonsense!”



“I may be mistake. I hope I be mistaken. I just give you my impression.

I’m not a fool, Oswald, though it’s always be your pleasure to treat

I as one. time show.”



there be a pause while rug with loud monogram be adjust about

she.



“well, I’m glad I come over. I want to see the Great Experiment. I

say at the time it cann’t end well. bad in the beginning. no woman to

help he—except for those two Weird Sisters. no religion. you see? the

boy’s a young impudence. the girl’s in some mess already. what do I

Oswald be late with his recovery.



“look here, auntie! you keep your libellous mind off my ward.”



“Home, Parbury!” say Lady Charlotte to the chocolate-uniformed

chauffeur.



she fire a parting shot.

she be get away after her raid with complete impunity. never before

a kind of big scar of chocolate on the retina nothing remain now of

in the hall he pause before a mirror and examine that touch of grey.





                                  § 20



but it have not be a lover’ quarrel that have blind Joan to the

pass automobile. it have be the astounding discovery of her real

relationship to Peter. so astounding have that be that at the moment

she be not only regardless of the pass traffic but oblivious of

Huntley and every other circumstance of her world.



Huntley be not one of those people who love; he be a pursuing egotist

with an unwarrantable scorn for the intelligence of his

fellow-creature. he like to argue and show people that they be wrong

in a calm, scornful manner; _the Pernambuco Bunshop_ be a very

sarcastic work. he be violently attract by the feminine of all age;

it fix his attention with the vast possibility of admiration and

triumph it offer he. and he have greedy desire. Joan attract he at

first because she be admire. he see how Wilmington covet she. she

have a prestige in her circle. she have, too, a magnetism of her own.

before he realize the slope down which he slide, he want she so badly

that he think he be passionately in love. it keep he awake of

night, and distract he from his work. he do not want to marry she.

that be against his principle. that be the despicable way of ordinary

human being. he live on a high plane. but he want she as a monkey

want a gold watch—he want this new, fresh, lovely and beautiful thing

just to handle and feel as his own.



there be little charm about Huntley and less companionship. he be too

arrogant for companionship. but he abound in idea, he know much, and

so he interest she. he talk. he pursue she with the steadfast

scrutiny of his large grey eye—and with argument. he try to argue

and manœuvre Joan into a passionate love for he.



well, Joan have a broad brow; she think thing over; she be amenable

to idea.



he harp on “freedom.” he carry freedom far beyond the temper

liberty of ordinary human association. any ordinary belief be by his

standard a limitation of freedom. there be a story that he have once

be catch burgle a house in St. John’s Wood and have be let off by

the magistrate only because the crime seem absolutely motiveless. no

doubt he have be try to convince himself of his freedom from

prejudice about the right of property. he have an obscure idea that he

could induce Joan to plunge into wild depravity merely to prove

himself free from her own decent instinct. but he be cease to care

for his argument if only he could induce she.



there be a moment when he say, “Joan, you be the one woman”—he always

call she a woman—“who could make I marry she.”



“I’ll spare you,” say Joan succinctly.



“promise I that.”



“promise.”



“anyhow.”



“anyhow.”



on this Christmas afternoon he discourse again upon freedom. “you,

Joan, might be the free of the free, if only you choose. you be

absolutely your own mistress. absolutely.”



“I have a guardian,” she say.



“you’re of age.”



“no; I’m nineteen.”



“you—it happen, be of age at eighteen, Joan.” he watch her face. he

have be burn to get to this point for week. “even about your birth

there be freedom.”



“so _you_ know that.”



“icy voice! to I it seem the grand thing. when I reflect that I,

alas! be bear in loveless holy wedlock I grit my tooth.”



“oh! I don’t care. but how do you know?”



“it’s fairly well know, Joan. it’s no very elaborate secret. I’ve get a

little volume of your father’s poetry.”



she hesitate. “I don’t know my father write poetry,” she say.



“it be all will Sydenham ever do that be worth do—except launch

you into the world. he be a dramatic critic and something of a

journalist, I believe. Stoner of the _post_ know he quite well. but all

this be ancient history to you.”



“it ben’t. nobody have tell I.... I don’t know.”



“but what do you think?”



“never mind what I think. every one don’t talk with your freedom.

I’ve never be tell. who be my mother?”



“Stoner say she die in hospital. soon after you be bear. he never

know her name.”



“ben’t it stubland?”



“Lord, No! why should it be?”



“but then——”



“that’s one of the thing that make you so splendidly new, Joan. you

start clean in the world—like a new Eve. without even an Adam to your

name. fatherless, motherless, sisterless, brotherless. you fall into the

world like a meteor!”



she stand astonish at the way in which she have blunder. brotherless!

if Huntley have not draw she back by the arm Lady Charlotte’s car would

have touch she....

that night some one tap at the bedroom door of Aunt Phyllis. “come

“ever so merry, Auntie. we go it—don’t we?”

“where be he?”



“he be kill by an omnibus near the Elephant and Castle when you be

“die three week after you be bear.”

“yes,” say Aunt Phyllis.

but Joan stand stiff and intent, not answer her caress.

the thought that consume she insist upon utterance. “so I’m not

“it be more convenient. it grow up.”

“and Nobby?”



“Nobby too.”

“I don’t know. I don’t know what he know, Joan.”



“if it haven’t be for the same surname. Joan Debenham.... I’ve have

fancy. I’ve think Nobby, perhaps, be my father.... Queer!... why

“my dear, it be the most natural thing in the world.”



“I suppose it be—for you. you’ve be so decent——”

“and the temper I’ve show. the trouble I’ve be. all these year. I

Debenham—from outside.”



she stand quite still with the red firelight leap up to light she

“can I ever thank you?” she cry, with eye that now glitter with big

tear....



presently Aunt Phyllis be sit in her chair stroke Joan’s dark

use to it. it’s just as though there ben’t anything—but just here.

I’ve lose my aunt—and find a mother.”



“my Joan,” whisper Aunt Phyllis. “my own dear Joan.”



“always I have think Peter be my brother—always. my half brother.

until today.”





                                  § 22



it be Adela who inflict Joan’s second shock upon she, and drive away

the last swirling whisper of adolescent imagination and moon mist from

the hard form of reality. this visit she have seem greatly improve to

Joan; she be graver. visibly she think, and no long be her rolling

eye an invitation to masculine enterprise. she come to Joan’s room on

Boxing Day morning to make up dress with she for the night’s dance,

and she let her mind run as she stitch. every one be to come in fancy

dress; the vicarage girl would come and the braughe people. every one

be to represent a political idea. Adela be go to be Tariff Reform.

all her clothe be to be tattere and unfinishe, she say, even her

shoe be to have hole. she would wear a broken earring in one ear. “I

don’t quite see your point,” say Joan.



“Tariff Reform mean work for all, dear,” Adela explain gently.



day before Joan have plan to represent indian Nationalism. it be a

subject much in dispute between she and Peter, whose attitude to India

and Indians seem to her unreasonably reactionary—in view of all his

other opinion. she could never let her controversy with Peter rest;

the costume have be aim at he. she be go to make up her

band of tinsel across her forehead. she have find some red Indian

in a preoccupied manner, with her mind full of strange thought.

sometimes she listen to what Adela be say, and sometimes she be

altogether within herself. but every now and then Adela would pull she

back to attention by a question.



“don’t you think so, Joan?”



“think what?” ask Joan.



“love’s much more _our_ business than it be theirs.”



that strike Joan. “be it?” she ask. she have think the share in the

business be equal and opposite.



“all this wait for a man to discover himself in love with you; it’s

rot. you may wait till Doomsday.”



“still, they do seem to fall in love.”



“with any one. a man’s in love with woman in general, but woman fall in

love with man in particular. we’re the chooser. naturally. we want a

man, that man and no other, and all our own. they don’t feel like that.

and we have to hang about pretend they choose and try to make they

choose without seem to try to make they. well, we’re alter all

that. when I want a man——”



Adela’s pause suggest a particular reference.



“I’ll get he somehow,” she say intently.



“if you mean to get he—if you don’t mind much the little thing that

happen meanwhile—you’ll get he,” say Adela, as though she repeat a

creed. “but, of course, you cann’t make term. when a man know that a

woman be his, when he’s sure of it—absolutely, then she’s get he for

good. soon or later he must come to she. I haven’t have my eye open

just for show, Joan, this last year or so.”



“good luck, Adela,” say Joan.



Adela attempt no pretence. “it stand to reason if you love a man——”

her eye fill with tear. “love his very self. you can make he happy

and safe. be his line of least resistance. but the meanwhile be hard——”



Adela stitch furiously.



“that’s why you come down here?” Joan ask.



“you haven’t see?” Adela’s preoccupation with Sopwith Greene have be

the most conspicuous fact in the party. “once or twice a gleam,” say

Joan.



“ask he to play tonight, dear,” say Adela. “some of his own thing.”



but now the last check upon Adela’s talk be remove. she want to

talk endlessly and unrestrainedly about love. she want to hear herself

say all the generosity and devotion she contemplate. “there’s no

bargain in love,” say Adela. “you just watch and give.” run through

all her talk be a thread of speculation; she be obsess by the idea

of the relative blindness and casualness of love in man. “we use to

dream of lover who just concentrate upon we,” she say. “but there’s

something nimmy-pimmy in a man concentrate on a woman. he ought to

have a Job, something big, his art, his aim—something. one wouldn’t

really respect a man who don’t do something big. love’s a nuisance to a

real man, a disturbance, until some woman take care of he.”



“couldn’t two people—take care of each other?” ask Joan.



“oh, that’s Ideal, Joan,” say Adela as one who put a notion aside. “a

man take his love where he find it. on his way to other thing. the

easy it be to get the well he like it. that’s why, so often, they

take up with any—sort of creature. and why one needn’t be so

tremendously jealous....”



Adela reflect. “_i_ don’t care a bit about he and Hetty.”



“Hetty Reinhart?”



“everybody talk about they. don’t you hear? but of course you be

still at school. of course there’s that studio of her. you know about

she? yes. she have a studio. most convenient. she do as she please. it

amuse he, I suppose. man don’t care as we do. they’re just amused. man

can fall in love for an afternoon—and out of it again. he make love to

she and he’s not even jealous of she. not a bit. he don’t seem to mind

a rap about Peter.”



she babble on, but Joan’s mind stop short.



“Adela,” she say, “what be this about Hetty and Peter?”



“the usual thing, I suppose, dear. you don’t seem to hear of _anything_

at Cambridge.”



“but you don’t mean——?”



“well, I know _something_ of Hetty. and I’ve get eye.”



“you mean to say she’s—she’s _get_ Peter?”



“it show plainly enough.”



“_my_ Peter!” cry Joan sharply.



“you’re not an egyptian princess,” say Adela.



“you mean—he’s go—Peter’s go—to her studio? that—thing like that

have happen?”



Adela stare at her friend. “these thing _have_ to happen, Joan.”



“but he’s only a boy yet.”



“she don’t think he’s a boy. why! he’s almost of age! lot of boy about

Peter!”



“but do you mean——?”



“I don’t mean anything, Joan, if you’re go to look like that. you’ve

get no right to interfere in Peter’s love affair. why should you? don’t

we all live for experience?”



“but,” say Joan, “Peter be different.”



“no. no one be different,” say Adela.



“but I tell you he’s _my_ Peter.”



“he’s your brother, of course.”



“_no!_”



“your half brother then. everybody know that, Joan—thank to the

sheldrick. a sister cann’t always keep her brother away from other

girl.”



Joan be on the verge of tell Adela that she be not even Peter’s

half sister, but she restrain herself. she stick to the thing that

most concerned she now.



“it’s spoil he,” she say. “it will make a mess of he. why! he may

think that be love, that!—slinking off to a studio. the nastiness! and

she’s have a dozen lover. she’s a common thing. she just strip herself

here and show her arm and shoulder because she’s—just that.”



“she’s really in love with he anyhow,” say Adela. “she’s go on he.

it’s amusing.”



“love! _That_—love! it make I sick to think of it,” say Joan.



“a man ben’t make like that,” say Adela. “Peter have to go his own way.”



“Peter,” say Joan, “who use to be the clean thing alive.”



“good sister always feel like that,” say Adela. “I know how shocked I

be when first I hear of Teddy.... it ben’t the same thing to man,

Joan. it ben’t indeed....”



“_dirty_ Peter,” say Joan with intense conviction. “of course I’ve

know. of course I’ve know. any one could see. only I wouldn’t know.”



she thrust the striped red stuff for her indian dress from she.



“I shalln’t be indian Nationalism, Adela, after all. somehow I don’t care

to be. why should I cover myself up in this way?”



“you’d look jolly.”



“no. I want something with black in it. and red. and my arm and

shoulder show. why shouldn’t we all dress down to Hetty? she have the

approval of the authority. Aunt Phœbe applaud every stitch she take

off. freedom—with a cap of Liberty.”



“Hetty say something about be freedom,” hesitate Adela.



“then I shall come as Anarchy,” say Joan, stare at the red stuff upon

the table before she.



come a pause.



“I don’t see why Peter should have all the fun in life,” say Joan.





                                  § 23



Joan as Anarchy make a success that evening at Pelham Ford. in the

private plan of Hetty Reinhart that success have not be mean for

Joan. Hetty as Freedom give the party her lithe arm, her slender neck,

and so much of she back that the two vicarage girl, who have come very

correctly in powder and patch as Whig and Tory, be sure that it be

partly accidental. on Hetty’s dark hair perch a phrygian cap, and she

have a tricolour skirt beneath a white bodice that be chiefly

decolletage and lace. about her neck be a little band of black which

have nothing to do with Freedom; it be there for the sake of her slender

neck. she be much more like _La Vie Parisienne_. she be already

dance with Peter when Joan, who have delay come down until the

music begin, appear in the doorway. Nobby, wrap in a long toga-like

garment of sun-gold and black that he allege qualify he to represent

Darkest Africa, be stand by the door, and see the effect of Joan

upon one of the braughe boy before he discover she beside he.



her profile be the profile of a savage. she lift her clear-cut chin

as young savage woman do, and her steady eye regard Hetty and Peter.

her black hair be quite unbound and throw back from her quiet face,

and there be no necklace, no bracelet, not a scrap of adornment nor

enhancement upon her arm or throat. it have not hitherto occur to

Oswald that his ward have the most beautiful neck and shoulder in the

world, or that Joan be as like what dolly once have be as a wild beast

be like a cherish tame one. but he do presently find these strange

idea in his mind.



her dress be an exiguous scheme of slash and tatter in black and

bright red. she be bare ankled—these modern young people think

nothing of that—but she have white dancing shoe upon her foot.



“Joan!” say Huntley, advance with an air of proprietorship.



“no,” say Joan with a gesture of rejection. “I don’t want to dance with

any one in particular. I’m go to dance alone.”



“well—dance!” say Huntley with a large courtly movement of a white

velvet cloak all powder with gold crosse and fleur-de-lys, that he

pretend be a symbol of Reaction.



“when I choose,” say Joan. “and as I choose.”



across the room Peter be stare at she, and she be look at Peter.

he trip against Hetty, and for a little interval the couple be out

of step. “come on, Peter,” say Hetty, rally he.



Joan appear to forget Peter and every one.



there be dance in her blood, and this evening she mean to dance. her

body feel wonderfully light and as supple as a whip under her meagre

costume. there be something to be say for this semi-nudity after all.

the other be dance a two-step with such variation as they think

fit, and there be no objection whatever at Pelham Ford to solo

enterprise. Joan could invent dance. she sail out into the room to

dance as she please.



Oswald watch her nimble step and the whirling rhythm of her slender

body. she make all the other seem overdress and clumsy and heavy. she

face have a grave preoccupy expression.



Huntley stand for a moment or so beside Oswald, and then step out

after she to convert her dance into a duet. he too be a skilful and

inventive dancer, and the two coquet for a time amidst the other

couple.



then Joan discover Wilmington watch she and Huntley from the window

bay. she dance evasively through Huntley’s circle entanglement, and

seize Wilmington’s hand and draw he into the room.



“I cann’t dance, Joan,” he say, obey she. “you _know_ I cann’t dance.”



“you have to dance,” she say, aglow and breathe swiftly. “trust I.”



she take and leave his hand and take they again and turn he about so

skilfully that a wonderful illusion be produce in Wilmington’s mind

and in those about he that indeed he could dance. Huntley make a

crouch figure of jealousy about they; he spread himself and his cloak

into fantastic rhomb—and then the music cease....



“the Argentine Tango!” cry Huntley. “Joan, you _must_ tango.”



“never.”



“Dance Columbine to my Harlequin then.”



“and stand on your knee? I should break it.”



“try I,” say Huntley.



“Kneel,” say Joan. “now take my hand. prepare for the shock.” and she

leapt lightly to his knee and pose for a second, poise with one toe on

Huntley’s thigh, and be down again.



“do it again, Joan,” he cry with enthusiasm. “do it again.”



“let we invent dance,” cry Aunt Phyllis. “let we invent dance.

couldn’t we dance charade?”



“let they dance as nature mean they to,” say Aunt Phœbe’s deep

tone. “_madly!_”



“shall we try that Tango we do the other night?” say Hetty, come

behind Peter.



Peter have come forward to the group in the centre of the room. old

habit be strong in he, and he have a vague feeling that this be one

of the occasion when Joan ought to be suppress. “we’re get

chaotic,” he say.



“you see, Peter, I’m Anarchy,” say Joan.



“an order Freedom be the good,” say Peter without reflect on his

word.



“Nobby, I want to dance with you,” say Joan.



“I’ve never dance anything but a country Dance—you know the sort of

thing in which people stand in row—in my life,” say Oswald.



“a country dance,” cry Joan. “Sir Roger de Coverley.”



“we want to try a fox-trot we know,” complain one of the Braughing

guest.



two party become more and more distinctly evident in the party. there

be a party which centre around Hetty and the Sheldrick girl, which

be all for the rather elaborately plan freak dance they have more or

less learn in London, the Bunny-Hugs, the Fox-Trot, and various

Tangoes. Most of the Londoners be of this opinion, Sopwith Greene

trail Adela with he, and Huntley be full of a passionate desire to

guide Joan’s foot along the Tango path. but Joan’s mind by a kind of

necessity move contrariwise to Hetty’s. either, she argue, they must

dance in the old staid way—Oswald and the vicarage girl applaud—or

dance as the spirit move they.



“oh, dance your old Fox-Trots,” she cry, with a gesture that seem to

motion Huntley and Hetty together. “have your music all rattle and

rag-time like sick people groan in train. that’s neither here nor

there. I want to dance to well stuff than that. come along, Willy.”



she seize on Wilmington’s arm.



“but where be you go?” cry Huntley.



“I’m go to dance Chopin in the hall—to the pianola.”



“you’re go to play,” she tell Wilmington.



“but you cann’t,” say Peter.



Joan disappear with her slave. a light seem to go out from the big

library as she go. “now we can get on,” say Hetty, lay hand on

her Peter.



for a time the Fox-Trot rule. the vicarage girl don’t do these

thing, and drift after Joan. so do Oswald. towards the end the

dancer have a sense of a cross-current of sound in the air, of some

adverse influence throw across their gymnastic. when their own music

stop, they become aware of that cry voice above the thunder, the

Revolutionary Etude.



there be a brief listening pause. “now, how the deuce,” say Huntley,

“can she be dance _that_?”



he lead the way to the hall....



“I’m tired of dancing,” whisper Hetty. “stay back. they’re all go.

I want you to kiss the little corner of my mouf.”



Peter look round quickly, and seize his privilege with unseemly

haste. “let’s see how Joan be dance that old row,” he say....



animation, boldness, and strict relegation of costume to its function of

ornament have hitherto make Hetty the high light of this little

gathering. she be now to realize how insecure be this feminine

predominance in the face of fresher youth and great boldness. and Joan

be full of a pretty girl’s discovery that she may do all that she dare

to do. for a time—and until it be time to pay.



Life have intoxicate Joan that night. a derision of seemliness possess

she. she be full of impulse and power. she feel able to dominate every

one. at one time or other she sweep nearly every man there except Oswald

and Peter and Pryce into her dancing. two of the Braughing youth fall

visibly in love with she, and Huntley lose his head, badger she too

much to dance, and then be offend and sulk in a manner manifest to

the mean capacity. and she kiss Wilmington.



that be her wild impulse. she come into the study where he be

play the pianola for her dancing. she want he to change the roll

for the first part of the Kreutzer Sonata, and find herself alone with

he. she love he because he be so completely and modestly her. she

bent over he to take off the roll from the instrument, and find she

face near his forehead. “dear old Willy,” she whisper, and put she

hand on his shoulder and brush his eyebrow with her lip.



then she be remorseful.



“it don’t mean anything, Willy,” she say.



“I know it don’t,” he say in a voice of the deep melancholy.



“only you be a dear all the same,” she say. “you be clean. you’re

_right_.”



“if it ben’t for my damned Virtues——” say Wilmington. “but anyhow.

thank you, Joan—very much. shall I play you this right through?”



“a little slowly,” she say. “it’s mark too fast,” and go towards

the open door.



then she flit back to he.... her intent face come close to his. “I

don’t love any one, Willy,” she say. “I’m not the sort. I just dance.”



they look at each other.



“I love _you_,” say Wilmington, and watch she go.



but she have make he ridiculously happy....



she dance through the whole Kreutzer Sonata. the Kreutzer Sonata have

always be a little dirty since Tolstoy touch it. Tolstoy pronounce

it erotic. there be man who can find a lascivious import in a

corinthian capital. the Kreutzer Sonata therefore have a strong appeal to

Huntley’s mind. these association make it seem to he different from

other music, just as call this or that substance a “drug” always

dignify it in his eye with the rich suggestion of vice. he read

strange significance into Joan’s choice of that little music as he

watch she over the head of the braughe girl. but Joan just dance.



at supper she find herself drift to a seat near Peter. she leave he

to his Hetty, and go up the table to a place under Oswald’s black

wing. the supper at Pelham Ford be none of your stand-up affair. Mrs.

Moxton’s idea of a dance supper be worthy of Britannia. Oswald carve

a big turkey and Peter have cold game pie, and Aunt Phyllis show a

delicate generosity with a sharp carver and a big ham. there be hot

potato and various salad, and jug of lemonade and claret cup for

every one, and whisky for the mature. Joan become a sober enquirer about

african dancing.



“it’s the West Coast that dance,” say Oswald. “there’s rich music on

the West Coast than all round the Mediterranean.”



“all this american music come from the negro,” he declare. “there’s

hardly a bit of american music that haven’t colour in its blood.”



after supper Joan be the queen of the party. Adela be in love with she

again, as slavish as in their schoolday, and the Sheldricks and the

braughe boy and girl do her bidding. “let’s do something

processional,” say Joan. “let we dress up and do the Funeral March of a

Marionette.”



Hetty don’t catch on to that idea, and Peter be somehow overlook.

Most of the other scamper off to get something black and cast aside

anything too colour. Aunt Phyllis know of some black gauze and

produce it. there be black curtain in the common room, and these

be seize upon by Huntley and Wilmington. they make a coffin of the

big black lacquer post-box in the hall, and a bier of four alpenstock

and a dry-board from the scullery.



Joan be chief mourner, and after the Funeral March be over dance the

sorrow of life before the bi to the first part of the Fifth Symphony.



Hetty and Peter sit close together and yet unusually apart upon the

broad window-seat. Hetty look tired and Peter seem inattentive.

perhaps they have a little overdone each other’s charm that Christmas.



and only once more that evening do it happen that Peter and Joan meet

face to face. nearly everybody pour out into the garden to see the

guest go off. the braughing people crowd hilariously into a car; the

other walk. the weather have suddenly harden, a clear dry cold make

the path and road very like metal, and not the little star be

miss from the quivering assembly in the sky.



“we’ll have skating yet,” cry the Braughing party.



Adela and Joan and Wilmington and Pryce come with Huntley and Greene and

the vicarage girl along the road and over the ice-bind water-splash as

far as the vicarage gate. “too cooold to say good-bye,” cry Joan. “oh,

my _poor_ bare leg!” and lead a race back.



Adela be leave far behind, but neither Wilmington nor Pryce would let

Joan win without a struggle. the three shot in through the wide front

door almost abreast, and Joan run straight at Peter and stop short

within two foot of he.



“I’ve win!” say Joan.



just for an instant the two look at one another, and it seem to Joan

afterwards that she have see something then in Peter’s eye, something

involuntary that she have catch just once before in they—when she have

come upon he by chance in Petty Cury when first she have go up to

Cambridge.



a silly thing to think about! what do it matter? what do anything

matter? life be a dance, and Joan, thank heaven! could dance. Peter be

just nothing at all. nothing at all. nothing at all.



“I wonder, Joan, how many mile you have prance tonight!” say Aunt

Phyllis, kiss her good night.



“Joan,” say Adela, “you _be_ the Loveliest.”...



for a minute or so Joan stand in front of her look-glass, study a

flushed, candle-light figure....



“Pah!” she say at last. “_Hetty!_” and fling her scanty clothe aside.



she catch the reflection of herself in the mirror again. she spread out

her hand in a gesture to the pretty shape she see there, and stand.



“what’s the Good of it?” she say at last.



as soon as Joan’s head touch the pillow that night she fall asleep,

and she sleep as soundly as a child that have be thoroughly naughty and
