all at six and seven, and that have be well slap and have a good

cry to wind up with, and put to bed. in all the world there be no

sounder sleep than that.









                          CHAPTER the TWELFTH

                      the world on the EVE of WAR





                                  § 1



Oswald sit in the March sunshine that fill and warm his little

summerhouse, and think about Joan and Peter....



his sudden realization of Joan’s mental maturity, the clear warning it

bring to he that the task and opportunity of education be pass

out of his hand, that already the reckoning of consequence be

begin for both his ward, set his mind search up and down amidst

the memory of his effort, to find where he could have slip, where

blunder and fail. he perceive now how vague have be the gesture

with which he have start, when he proclaim his intention to give they

“the good education in the world.”



the good education in the world be still to seek, and while he have be

get such scrap of second good for they as he could, the world

itself, nature, tradition, custom, suggestion, example and accident, have

mould they and make they. when he measure what have be do upon

these youngster by these outward thing and compare it with their

deliberate education, the schoolmaster seem to he to be still no more

than a half-hearted dwarf who would snare the white horse of a cataract

with a noose of packthread.



“the generation run to waste—like rapid.”



but there be strong harnessing than packthread, and there be

already engineer in the world who, by take thought and patient work,

can tame the mad torrent that ever overawe the mind of man. in the

end perhaps all torrent will be tame, and knowledge and purpose put an

end to aimless adventure. the schoolmaster will not always be a

dwarf....



as our child grow beyond our control we begin to learn something of

the reality of education. the world have Joan and Peter now; at the most

Oswald could run and shout advice from the bank as they go down the

rush. but he know that he could have do more for they, and that with a

different world he could have do infinitely more for they in their

receptive year. they be the child of an age; their restless fever

of impulse be but their individual share in a great fever. the whole

world now be restless, out of touch with any standard, and manifestly

drift towards great change.



neither Joan nor Peter seem to have any definite purpose in life.

their impulse be not focus. they be draw hither and thither.

that be the essential failure of their adolescence. their education have

do many good thing for they, but it have leave their will as

spontaneous, indefinite and unsocial as the will of a criminal.

physically Oswald and the world have do well by they; they be

clean-blooded, well grow, well exercise animal; they belong to a

generation of youth measurably tall, finer, and more beautiful than

any generation before they. they be swift-footed and nimble. mentally,

too, they be swift and clear. it be not that their idea be

confused but their will. each of they could speak and read and write

three language quite well, they could draw well and Peter could draw

brilliantly, they be alive to art and music, they read widely, they

have the dispassionate, wide, scientific vision of the world. but be

so fine and clean it be all the more distressing to realize that these

two young people now face the world with no clear will in they about it

or themselves, that Joan seem consume with discontent and this dark

personal quarrel with Peter, and that Peter could be catch and hold by

a mere sensual adventure. Hetty Reinhart keep he busy with note and

situation; having create a necessity she go on to create a jealous

rivalry. he would be sometimes excited and elated, sometimes manifestly

angry and sulky; and his work at Cambridge, which for two year have be

conspicuously brilliant, be fall away.



until Joan’s angry outspokenness have force these fact upon his

attention, Oswald have shirk their realization. he have see with his

one watchful eye, but he have not will to see. a score of fact have

lain, like disagreeable letter that one hesitate to answer,

uncorrelated in his mind. the disorder of the Christmas party have

indeed leave he profoundly uneasy. with the new year he go with Peter

on a trip to Russia. he want the youngster to develop a vision of the

european problem, for Peter seem blind to the importance of

international thing. they have cross to flush, travel straight

through to Berlin, go about Berlin for a few day, run on to St.

Petersburg—it be not yet Petrograd—visit a friendly house near the

Valdai Hills, spend a busy week in and about Moscow, and return by way

of Warsaw. they see Germany already train like an athlete for the

adventure of the come war, and Russia great and disorderly, destine

to be take unawares. then they return to England to look again at

their own country with eye refresh by these contrast. and all the

time Oswald watch Peter and speculate about the thought and idea

hide in Peter’s head.





                                  § 2



this russian trip have be precipitate by a sudden opportunity.

originally Oswald have plan a russian tour for his ward on a more

considerable scale. among the unsolved difficulty of this scheme have

be his ignorance of Russian. he have think of employ a courier—but

a courier can be a tiresome encumbrance. his friend Bailey, who be an

enthusiast for Russia and speak russian remarkably well for an

Englishman, write from Petrograd offering to guide Oswald and Peter

about that city, suggest a visit to a cousin who have marry a

russian landowner in Novgorod, and a week or so in Moscow, where some

friend of Bailey’s would keep a helpful eye on the traveller. it be

too good a chance to lose. there be some hasty buying of fur-line

glove, insertion of wad under the fur of Oswald’s fur coat, and the

purchase of a suitable outfit for Peter.



Bailey have his misogynic side, Oswald know; he think woman troublesome

millinery to handle; and he do not include Joan in the invitation. on

the whole Oswald do not regret that omission, because it give he so

excellent a chance of be alone with Peter for long spell, and

get near his private thought.



it be an expedition that leave a multitude of vivid impression upon the

young man’s memory; the still, cold, starry night of the departure from

Harwich, the light deck, the black water, the foaming wake catch by the

ship’s light, the neat dutch landscape with its black and white cow

grow visible as day break, shiver worker under a chill, red-nosed

dawn pour down by a path near the railway into the factory of some

industrial town; the long flat journey across Germany; the Sieges-Allée

and the war trophy and public building of Berlin; the Sunday morning

crowd upon Unter den Linden; the large prosperity of the new suburb of

Berlin; north Germany under an iron frost, a crowd of child slide

and skating near Königsberg; the dingy, vaster effect of Russia,

street in Petrograd with the shop all black and gold and paint with

shine picture of the good on sale to a population of illiterate,

the night crowd in the People’s palace; a sledge drive of ten mile

along the ice of a frozen river, a wooden country house behind a great

stone portico, and a merry house party that went scamper out after

supper to lie on the crisp snow and see the star between the tree

bough; the chant service in a little green-cupolae church and a

pretty village schoolmistress in peasant costume; the great red wall of

the Kremlin rise above the Moskva and the first glimpse of that

barbaric caricature, the cathedral of St. Basil; the paint

magnificence of the Troitzkaya monastery; a dirty, evil-smell little

tramp with his bundle and kettle, worship unabashe in the Uspenski

cathedral; endless bearded priest, Tartar waiter with purple sash, a

whole population in fur and so look absurdly wealthy to an English

eye; a thousand such picture, keen, bright and vivid against a

background of white snow....



the romanticism of the late Victorians still prevail in Oswald’s mind.

the picturesqueness of Russia have a great effect upon he. from the

passport office at Wirballen with its impose green-uniformed guard

and elaborate ceremony onward into Moscow, he mark the contrast with

the trim modernity of Germany. the wild wintry landscape of the land

with its swamp and unkempt thicket of silver birch, the crouch

timber village with their cupolae church, the unmade road, the

unfamiliar lettering of the station, contribute to his impression of

barbaric greatness. after the plainly ugly, middle-class cathedral of

Berlin he rejoice at the dark splendour, the green serpentine and

incense, of St. Isaac’s; he compare the frozen Neva to a great thame

and stand upon the Troitzki Bridge rejoice over the masse of the

fortress of St. Peter and St. Paul. in Petrograd he say, “away from

here to the North Pole be Russia and the Outside, the famine-stricken

north, the frozen fen and wilderness, the limit of mankind.” Moscow

make he talk of the mingling of east and west, western and eastern

costume jostle in the street. he be surprised at the frequency of

Chinamen. “away from here to Vladivostok,” he say, “be Russia and all

Asia. north, west, east and south there be limitless land. we be an

island people. but here one feel the land masse of the earth.”



Peter be preoccupy with a gallant attempt to master colloquial

Russian in a fortnight by mean of a _russian Self-Taught_ he have buy

in London; he do not thrust his conversation between Bailey and Oswald,

but sometimes when he be alone with his guardian and the mood take he

he would talk freely and rather well. he have be read abundantly and

variously; it be evident that at Cambridge he belong to a talk

set. if he have no directive form in his mind he have at any rate

something like a systematic philosophy.



it be a profoundly sceptical philosophy. there be moment when Oswald

be remind of Beresford’s “Hampdenshire Wonder,” who read through all

human learning and literature before the age of five, and turn upon

its instructor with “be this _all_?” Peter look at the world into

which he have come, at the king and kaiser demand devotion to “our

person,” at the gentleman wave flag and talk of patriotism and

service to empire and race and “nationality,” at the church and

priest pursue their “policy,” and in effect he turn to Oswald

with the same question. in the background of his imagination it be only

too manifest that the nymph—with a general family resemblance to Hetty

Reinhart—dance, and he hear that music of the sense which the

decadent young man of the _fin de siècle_ period be willnot to refer to

as “the pipe of Pan.”



he and Oswald look together at Moscow in the warm light of sunset.

they be in the veranda of a hillside restaurant which command the

huge bend of the river between the Borodinski and the Kruimski bridge.

the city lay, wide and massive, along the line of the sky, with little

field and a small church or so in the foreground. the six glitter

dome of the great Church of the Redeemer rise in the centre against the

high red wall and the cluster palace and church cupola of the

Kremlin. left and right of the Kremlin the city spread, a purple sea of

house and wall, fleck with snowy space and gem with red

reflect window, through which the river twist like a silver eel.

Moscow be a city of crosse, every church have its bulbous paint cupola

and some have five or six, and every cupola carry its brightly gild

two-arm cross. the ray of the set sun be now turn all these

crosse to pale fire.



Oswald, in spite of his own sceptical opinion, be a little under the

spell of the “Holy Russia” legend. he stand with his foot on a chair and

rest his jaw on his hand, with the live side of his face turn as

usual towards his ward, and try to express the confused idea that

be stir in his mind. “this ben’t a city like the city of western

Europe, Peter,” he say. “this be something different. those western

city, they grow out of the soil on which they stand; they be there

for ever like the wood and hill; there be no other place for London or

Rouen or Rome except just where it stand; but this, Peter, be a Tartar

camp, frozen. it might have be at Nijni-Novgorod or Yaroslav or Kazan.

it might be anywhere upon the Russia plain; only it happen to be here.

it’s a camp change to wood and brick and plaster. that’s the

headquarters camp there, the Tsar’s pavilion. and all these crosse

everywhere be like the standard outside the tent of the captain.”



“and where be it go?” say Peter, look at Moscow over his fur

collar, with his hand deep in his overcoat pocket.



“Asia advance on Europe—with a new idea.... one understand Dostoevsky

well when one see this. one begin to realize this Holy Russia, as a

sort of epileptic genius among nation—like his Idiot, insist on

moral truth, hold up the cross to mankind.”



“_what_ truth?” ask Peter.



“they seem to have the christian idea. in a way we Westerns don’t.

Dostoevsky, Tolstoy, and their endless school of dissent have a

character in common. christianity to a russian mean Brotherhood.”



“if it mean anything,” say Peter.



the youngster reflect.



“I wonder, be there really this russian idea? I don’t believe very much

in these national idea.”



“say national character then. this city with its endless crosse be so

in harmony with russian music, russian art, russian literature.”



“any city that have to be build here would have to look more or less like

this,” say Peter.



“if it be build by Americans?”



“if they’d live here always,” say Peter. “but we’re argue in a

circle. if they’d live here always the thing that have make the

Russians Russians, would have make they Russians. I’ve go too far. of

course there be a russian character. they’re wanderer, body and brain.

man of an endless land. but——”



“well?”



“not much of a russian idea to it.... I don’t believe a bit in all these

crosse.”



“you mean as symbol of an idea?”



“yes. of course the cross have mean _something_ to people. it must have

mean tremendous thing to some people. but man imitate. one stick up a

cross because it mean all sort of deep thing to he. then the man

down the road think he will have a cross too. and the man up the road

don’t quite see what it’s all about but don’t like to be out of it.

so they go on, until stick up crosse become a habit. it become a

necessity. they’d be shock to see a new church without four or five

crosse on it. they organize a business in golden crosse. everybody

say, ’you _must_ have a cross.’ long ago every one have forget that

deep meaning....”



“H’m,” say Oswald, “you think that?”



“it’s just a crowd,” say Peter, think aloud. “underneath the crosse

it’s just a swarming and breeding of man.... like any other man.”



“but don’t you think that all that million odd down there be hold

together by a distinctive idea? don’t you feel sometimes the russian

idea about you—like the smell of burn wood on the breeze?”



“well, call it a breeze,” say Peter. “it’s like a breeze blow over

mud. it blow now and then. it’s forget before it be past. what do

it signify?”



he be think as he talk. Oswald do not want to interrupt he, and

just smile slightly and look at Peter for more.



“I don’t think there’s any great essential difference between city,”

say Peter. “it’s easy to exaggerate that. mostly the difference be

difference of scenery. beneath the difference it’s the same story

everywhere; man shove about and eat and squabbling and multiplying.

we might just as well be look at London from Hampstead bridge so far

as the human fact go. here thing be do in red and black and gold

against a background of white snow; there they be do in drab and grey

and green. this be a land of dull tragedy instead of dull comedy, gold

crosse on green onion instead of church spire, extreme instead of

mean, but it’s all the same old human thing. even the King and Tsar

look alike, there’s a state church here, dissenter, landowner....”



“I suppose there be a sort of parallelism,” Oswald concede....



“we’re not big enough yet for big idea, the russian idea, or the

christian idea or any such idea,” say Peter. “why pretend we have

they?”



“now that’s just it,” say Oswald, come round upon he with an

extend finger. “because we want they so badly.”



“do every one?”



“yes. consciously or not. that’s where you and I be at issue, Peter.”



“oh, I don’t _see_ the idea at work!” cry Peter. “except as a sort of

flourish of the mind. but look at the everyday life. wherever we have

be—in London, Paris, Italy, Berlin, here, we see every man who can

afford it make for the restaurant and go where there be woman to

be get. hunger, indulgence, and sex, sex, sex, sex.” his voice be

suddenly bitter. he turn his face to Oswald for a moment. “we’re too

little. these blind impulse——I suppose there’s a sort of impulse to

Beauty in it. some day perhaps these force will do something—drive man

up the scale of be. but as far as _we’ve_ get——!”



he stare at Moscow again.



he seem to have do.



“you think we’re oversexe?” say Oswald after a pause.



the youngster glance at his guardian.



“I’m not blind,” he fence.



then he laugh with a refreshing cheerfulness. “it’s youthful

pessimism, Nobby. my mind run like this because it’s the fashion. we

get so dosed with Schopenhauer and Nietzsche—usually at second hand. we

all _try_ to talk like this. don’t mind I.”



Oswald smile back.



“Peter, you drive my spirit back to the Victorians,” he say. “I want to

begin quote Longfellow to you. ’life be real, life be earnest——’”



“no!” Peter counter. “but it ought to be.”



“well, it become so. we have Science, and out of Science come a light.

we shall see the Will plainer and plainer.”



“the will?” say Peter, turn it over in his mind.



“our own will then,” say Oswald. “your, mine, and every right sort of

man’s.”



Peter seem to consider it.



“it willn’t be a national will, anyhow,” he say, come back to Moscow.

“it willn’t be one of these national idea. no Holy Russia—or Old England

for the matter of that. they’re just—human accumulation. no. I don’t

know of this Will at all—_any_ will, Nobby. I cann’t see or feel this

Will. I wish I could....”



he have say his say. Oswald turn again to the great spectacle of the

city. do all those heavenward crosse now sink into the dusk amount

to no more than a glitter emanation out of the fen of life, an

unmeaning _ignis fatuus_, bear of a morass of fester desire that have

already forget it? or be these crosse indeed an appeal and a

promise? out of these million of man would man at last arise?...



slowly, smoothly, unfalteringly, the brush of the twilight have be

sweep its neutral tint across the spectacle, paint out the

glitter symbol one by one. a chill from outer space fall down

through the thin russian air, a dark transparent curtain. Oswald

shiver in his wad coat. abruptly down below, hard by a ghostly

white church, one lamp and then another prick the deepening blue. a

little dark tram-car that creep towards they out of the city way to

fetch they back into the city, suddenly become a glow-worm....





                                  § 3



twenty year before Oswald would not have talk in this fashion of the

will. twenty year before, the social and political order of the world

have seem so stable to an english mind that the thought of a sustaining

will be superfluous. Queen Victoria and the whole system have an air of

immortal inertia. the scientific and economic teaching under which

Oswald’s idea have be shape recognize no need for wilfully

co-ordinated effort. the end of education, they indicate, be the

Diffusion of Knowledge. victorian thought in England take good motive

for grant, seem indeed disposed to regard almost any motive as

equally good for the common weal. Herbert Spencer, that philosopher who

could not read Kant, most typical of all English intelligence in those

day, teach that if only there be no regulation, no common direction,

if every one be to pursue his own individual end unrestraine, then

by a sort of magic, chaos, free from the interference of any collective

direction, would produce order. his supreme gift to a generation of

hasty profiteer be the discovery that the blind scuffle of fate could

be call “Evolution,” and so give an air of intention altogether

superior to our poor struggle to make a decent order out of a greedy

scramble. for some decade, whatever section of british life have cease

to leave thing to Providence and not bother—not bother—be leave

they to Evolution—and still not bother....



it be because of Oswald’s discovery of the confused and distressed

motive of Joan and Peter and under the suggestion of the more kinetic

german philosophy that be slowly percolate into english thought, that

his idea be now change their direction. formerly he have think of

nation and empire as if they be thing in themselves, loose shape

which have little or nothing to do with the individual life they

contain; now he begin to think that all human organization, large and

small alike, exist for an end; they be will form; they present a

purpose that claim the subordination of individual aim. he begin to

see state and nation as thing of education, being in the mind of

man.



the parallelism of Russia and Britain which Peter have make, strike

Oswald as singularly acute. they have a close parallelism with each

other than with France or Italy or the United States or Germany or any

of the great political system of the world. Russia be Britain on land.

Britain be Russia in an island and upon all the sea of the globe. one

have the dreamy lassitude of an endless land horizon, the other the

hardbitten practicality of the salt sea. one be deep-feeling, gross,

and massively illiterate, the other be pervade by a cockney

brightness. but each be try to express and hold on to some general

purpose by mean of form and symbol that be daily become more

conspicuously inadequate. and each appear to be move inevitably

towards failure and confusion.



one afternoon during their stay in Petrograd, Bailey take Oswald and

Peter to see a session of the Duma. they drive in a sledge down the

Nevski Prospekt and by street of plough-up and tumbled snow, through

which struggle an interminable multitude of sledge bring firewood

into the city, to the old palace of the favourite Potemkin, into which

the Duma have in those day be thrust. the Duma be sit in a big

adapt conservatory, and the three visitor watch the proceeding

from a little low gallery wherein the speaker be almost inaudible.

Bailey point out the large proportion of priest in the centre and

explain the various party group; he himself be very sympathetic with

the Cadets. they be anglo-maniac; they idealize the british

constitution and thought of a limited monarchy—in the land of

extreme....



Oswald listen to Bailey’s exposition, but the thing that most grip

his attention be the huge portrait of the Tsar that hang over the

gathering. he could not keep his eye off it. there the figure of the

autocrat stand, with its sidelong, unintelligent visage, four time as

large as life, dress up in military guise and with its big cavalry

boot right over the head of the president of the Duma. that portrait

be as obvious an insult, as outrageous a challenge to the self-respect

of russian man, as a gross noise or a foul gesture would have be.



“you and all the empire exist for _me_,” say that foolish-faced

portrait, with its busby a little on one side and its weak hand on its

sword hilt....



it be to that figure they ask young Russia to be loyal.



that dull-faced Tsar and the golden crosse of Moscow present

themselves as Russia to the young. a heavy-handed and very corrupt

system of repression sustain their absurd pretension. they have no

sanction at all but that they exist—through the acquiescence of less

intelligent generation. the aged, the prosperous, the indolent, the

dishonest, the mean and the dull support they in a vast tacit

conspiracy. beneath such symbol could a land under the sting of modern

suggestion ever be anything but a will welter, a confusion of

sentiment and instinct and wilfulness? be it so wonderful that the

world be give the story of Artzibachev as picture of the will form

of the russian young?





                                  § 4



through all that journey Oswald be constantly compare Peter with the

young people he see. on two occasion he and Peter go to the Moscow

Art Theatre. once they see _Hamlet_ in Russian, and once Tchekhov’s

_three Sisters_; and each be produce with a completeness of ensemble,

an excellence of mechanism and a dramatic vigour far beyond the range of

any London theatre. here in untidy, sprawling, slushy Moscow shine this

diamond of co-operative effort and efficient organization. it set Oswald

revise certain hasty generalization about the russian character....



but far more interesting than the play to he be the audience. they

be mostly young people, and some of they be very young people;

student in uniform, bright-faced girl, clerk, young officer and

soldier, a sprinkling of intelligent-look old people of the

commercial and professional class; each evening show a similar

gathering, a very full house, intensely critical and appreciative. it

be rather like the sort of gather one might see in the London Fabian

Society, but there be scarcely any earnest spinster and many more

young man. the Art Theatre, like a magnet, have draw its own together

out of the vast barbaric medley of western and asiatic, of peasant,

merchant, priest, official and professional, that throng the Moscow

street. and they seem very delightful young people.



his one eye wander from the brightly-light stage to the row and row of

face in the great dim auditorium about he, rest on Peter, and then

go back to those other. this, then, must be a sample of the

Intelligentzia. these be the youth who figure in so large a

proportion of recent russian literature. how many bright keen face be

there! what lie before they?...



a dark premonition creep into his mind of the tragedy of all this eager

life, grow up in the clutch of a gigantic political system that now

stagger to its end....



this youth he see here be wonderfully like the new generation that be

now dance its way into his house at Pelham Ford....



it be curious to note how much more this big dim houseful of young

Muscovites be like a british or an american audience than it be to a

german gathering. perhaps there be rather more dark type, perhaps

more high cheekbone; it be hard to say....



but all the other north temperate race, it seem to Oswald, as

distinguish from the Germans, have the same suggestion about they of

unco-ordinate initiative. their mind move freely in a great old

system that have lose its hold upon they. but the german youth be

co-ordinated. they be tremendously co-ordinated. two sunday ago he

and Peter have be watch the Sunday morning parade along Unter den

Linden. they have go to see the white-trousere guard kick their

leg out ridiculously in the goose step outside the Guard House that

stand opposite the Kaiser’s Palace, they have walk along Unter den

Linden to the Brandenburger Tor, and then, after inspect that

vainglorious trophy of pile cannon outside the Reichstag, turn down

the Sieges Allée, and so come back to the Adlon by way of the Leipziger

Platz. Peter have be alive to many thing, but Oswald’s attention have

be concentrate almost exclusively on the youngster they be

pass, for the most part plump, pink-face student in corps cap,

very erect in their bearing and very tight in their clothe. they be

an absolutely distinct variety of the young human male. a puerile

militarism possess they all. they exchange salutation with the

utmost punctilio. while England have be take her child from the

hand of God, and not so much make they as let they develop into

note of interrogation, Germany without halt or hesitation have mould

her gift of youth into stiff, obedient, fresh soldier.



there have be a moment like a thunderclap while Oswald and Peter have

be near the Brandenburger Tor. a swift wave of expectation have sweep

through the crowd; there have be a galloping of mount policeman, a

hustling of traffic to the side of the road, a hasty lining up of

spectator. then with melodious tootling and amidst guttural plaudit,

a big white automobile carry a glitter of uniform have go by,

drive at a headlong pace. “_Der Kaiser!_” just for a moment the

magnificence hang in the eye—and pass.



what have they see? cloak, helmet, hard visage, one distinctive

pallid face; something melodramatic, something eager and in a great

hurry, something that go by like the sound of a trumpet, a figure of

vast enterprise in shine armour, with mail fist. this be the symbol

upon which these young Germans be be concentrate. this be the

ideal that have grip they. something very modern and yet romantic,

something stupendously resolute. go whither? at any rate, go

magnificently somewhere. that be the power of it. it _be_ go

somewhere. for good or bad it be an infinitely more attractive lead

than the cowardly and oppressive Tsardom that be fail to hold the

refractory mind of these young Russians, or the current edition of the

british imperial ideal, twangle its idiotic banjo and exhort Peter

and his generation to “tax the foreigner” as a worthy end and aim in

life.



Oswald, with his eye on the dim, preoccupy audience about he,

recall a talk that he and Peter have have with a young fellow-traveller

in the train between Hanover and Berlin. it have be a very typical

young German, glass and all; and his clothe look twice as hard as

Peter’s, and he sit up stiffly while Peter slouch on the seat. he

evidently want to air his English, while Peter have not the remotest

desire to air his German, and only betray a knowledge of German when

it be necessary to explain some english phrase the German don’t quite

grasp. the German want to know whether Oswald and Peter have be in

Germany before, where they be go, what they think of it, what

they be go to think of Berlin.



respond to counter question he say he have be twice to England. he

think England be a great country. “yes—but not systematic. no!”



“you mean undisciplined?”



yes, it be perhaps undisciplined he mean.



Oswald say that as a foreigner he be most strike by the tremendous air

of order in north Germany. the Germans be orderly by nature. the

admission prove an attractive gambit.



the young German question Oswald’s view that the Germans be

naturally orderly. hard necessity have make they so. they have have to

discipline themselves, they have be oblige to develop a

Kultur—encircle by enemy. now their Kultur be become a second

nature. every nation, he suppose, bring its present to mankind.

Germany’s be Order, System, the lesson of Obedience that would

constantly make she more powerful. the Germans be perforce a thorough

people. thorough in all they do. although they have come late into

modern industrialism they have already develop social and economic

organization far beyond that of any other people. Nicht wahr? their work

be become necessary to the rest of mankind. in Russia, for example,

in Turkey, in Italy, in South America, it be more and more the German

who organize, develop, lead. “though we be fence round,” he say,

“still—we break out.”



there be something familiar and yet novel in all this to Oswald. it be

like his first sensation upon read Shakespeare in German. it be

something very familiar—in an unfamiliar idiom. then he recognize it.

this be exactly his own imperialism—teutonize. the same assertion of

an educational mission....



“everywhere we go,” say the young German, “our superior science, our

high education, our well method prevail. even in your India——”



he smile and leave that sentence unfinishe.



“but your militarism, your sabre rule here at home; this zabern

business; ben’t that a little incompatible with this idea of Germany as

a great civilizing influence permeate the world?”



“not at all,” say the young German, with the readiness of a

word-perfect actor. “behind our missionary of order we must have ready

the good german sword.”



“but ben’t the argument of force apt to be a little—decivilize?”



the young German do not think so. “when I be in England I say, there

be three thing that these English do not properly understand to use,

they be the map or index, the school, and—the sword. those three thing

be the triangle of german life....”



that hang most in Oswald’s mind. he have go on talk to the young

German for a long time about the difference of the british and the

german way. he have make Peter and the youngster compare their school and

college work, and what be far more striking, the difference in pressure

between the two system. “you press too hard,” he say. “in Alsace you

have press too hard—in Posen.”



“perhaps we sometimes press—I do not know,” say the young German. “it

be the strength of our determination. we be impatient. we be a young

people.” for a time Oswald have talk of the method of Germany in the

Cameroons and of Britain on the Gold Coast, where the german have be

grow cacao by the plantation system, turn the native into slave,

while the British, with an old experience and a long view, have leave

the land in native hand and build up a happy and loyal free cultivation

ten time as productive mile for mile as the German. it seem to he to

be one good instance of his general conception of Germany as the land of

undue urgency. “your Wissmann in East Africa be a great man—but

everywhere else you drive too violently. you antagonize.” North Germany

everywhere, he say, have the same effect upon he of a country, “go

hard.”



“Germany may be in too much of a hurry,” he repeat.



“we come into world-politic late,” say the young German, endorse

Oswald’s idea from his own point of view. “we have much to overtake

yet.”...



the Germans have come into world-politic late. that be very true. they

be naïve yet. they could still feed their natural egotism on the story

of a world mission. the same enthusiasm that have take Russia to the

Pacific—and to Grand Ducal land speculation in Manchuria—and the English

to the coolie slavery of the Rand, be take these Germans now—whither?

Oswald do not ask what route to disillusionment Germany might choose.

but he believe that she would come to disillusionment. she be only a

little later in phase than her neighbour; that be all. in the end they

would see that that white-cloak heroic figure in the automobile lead

they to futility as surely as the skulk Tsar. not that way must the

nation go....



Oswald see no premonition of a world catastrophe in this german

youngster’s devotion to an ideal of militant aggression, nor in the

whole broad spectacle of strain preparation across which he and Peter

travel that winter from Aix to Wirballen. he be as it be magically

blind. he could stand on the Hanover platform and mark the largeness of

the station, the broad spread track, the endless siding, the

tremendous transport preparation, that could have no significance in

the world but military intention, and still have no more to say than,

“these Germans give themselves elbow-room on their railway, Peter. I

suppose land be cheap.” he could see nothing of the finger of fate

point straight out of all this large tidy preparedness at Peter and

their fellow-passenger and all the youth of the world. he think

imperialistic monarchy be an old dead thing in Russia and in Britain

and in Germany alike.



in Berlin indeed in every photographer’s be the touch-up visage of

the Kaiser, look heroic, and endless postcard of he and of his son

and of the Kaiserin and little imperial grandchild and the like; they

be as dull and dreary-look as any royalty can be, and it be

inconceivable to Oswald that such figure could really rule the

imagination of a great people. he do not realize that all the tragedy

in the world might lie behind the word of that young German, “we come

into world-politic late,” behind the fact that the german imperialist

system be just a little less decayed, a little less humorous, a little

less indolent and disillusioned than either of its great parallel to

the east and west. he do not reflect that no system be harmless until

its hand be take off the lever of power. he could still believe that

he live in an immensely stable world, and that these vast form of

kingdom and empire, with their sham reverence and unmeane ceremony

and obligation, their flag and militancy and their imaginative

senility, threaten nothing beyond the negative evil of uninspired

life run to individual waste. that be the thing that concern

he. he see no collective fate hang over all these intent young face

in the Moscow Art Theatre, as over the strut innocent of patriotic

Berlin; he have as yet no intimation of the gigantic disaster that be

now so close at hand, that be to torment and shatter the whole youth of

the world, that be to harvest the hope and energy of these bright

swathe of life....



he glance at Peter, intent upon the stage.



Peter lie open to every impulse. that be Oswald’s supreme grievance

then against tsar, king, and church. they have not be good enough

for Peter. that seem grievance enough.



he do not imagine yet that they could murder the like of Peter by the

hundred thousand, without a tremor.



he love the fine line of the boy’s profile, he mark his delicate

healthy complexion. Peter be like some wonderful new instrument in

perfect condition. and all these other youngster, too, have something of

the same clean fire in they....



be it all to be spend upon love-making and pleasure-seeking and play?

be this exquisite hope and desire presently to be throw aside, rust

by base use, corrode by self-indulgence, bent or break? “the

generation run to waste—like rapid....”



he still think in that phrase. the Niagara of Death so near to they

all now to which these rapid be head, he still do not hear, do

not suspect its nearness....



and Joan——. from Peter his thought drift to Joan. Joan apparently

could find nothing well to do in life than dance....



suddenly Peter take a deep breath, sit back, and begin to clap. the

whole house break out into a pelt storm of approval.



“rip!” say Peter. “oh! rip.”



he turn his bright face to Oswald. “they do it so well,” he say,

smile. “I have forget it be in Russian. I seem to understand

every word.”



Oswald turn his eye again to _Hamlet_ in Gordon Craig’s fantastic

setting—which Moscow in her artistic profusion could produce when London

be too poor to do so.





                                  § 5



very similar be the thought in Oswald’s mind three month later,

three month near the world catastrophe, as he sit in his summerhouse

after Joan have tell he of her quarrel with Peter.



her denunciation of Peter have have the curious effect upon he of make

he very anxious about she. so far as Peter go, what she have tell he

have but confirm and make definite what he have know by instinct since

the Christmas party. his mind be use now to the idea of Peter be

vicious. but he be very much shocked indeed at the discovery that Joan

be aware of Peter’s vice. that be a new jolt to his mind. in many

thing Joan and Peter have change his idea enormously, but so far he

have retain not only his wardroom standard with regard to the moral

of a youth, but also his romantic ideal of feminine purity with regard

to a girl. he still think of his own womenkind as of something

innocent, immaculate and untouchable, being in a different world from

the girl who “don’t mind a bit of fun” and the woman one make love to

boldly.



but now he have to face the fact—Joan have force it upon he—this new

feminine generation ben’t divide in that obvious way. the clean have

knowledge, the bold be not outcast and apart. the new world of woman

be as mixed as the world of man. he sit in his summerhouse thinking of

his Joan’s flushed face, her indignant eye, her outspoken word.



“it be a _woman’s_ face,” he whisper....



and he be realize too how much more urgent the ending of adolescence

be become with a girl than it could ever be with a boy. Peter might

tumble into a scrape or so and scramble out again, not very much the

bad for it, as he himself have do. but Joan, with all the temerity of

a youth, might be make experiment that be fatal. he have not be

watch she as he have watch Peter. suddenly he wake up to this

realization of some decisive issue at hand. why be she so whitely angry

with Peter? why do she complain of have to “stand too much” from

Peter? her abuse of his friend have the effect of a counter attack. be

there some mischief afoot from which Peter restrain she? what man be

there about in Joan’s world?



there be something slimy and watchful about this fellow Huntley. could

there be more in that affair than one like to think?... or be there

some one unknown in London or in Cambridge?



she and Peter be quarrel about the Easter party. it would

apparently be impossible to have any Easter party this year, since both

want to bar out the other one’s friend. and anyhow there mustn’t be

any more of this Hetty Reinhart business at Pelham Ford. that must stop.

it ought never to have happen.... he would take Peter over to Dublin.

they could accept an invitation he have have from Graham Powys out beyond

Foxrock, and they could motor into Dublin and about the country, and

perhaps the irish situation might touch the boy’s imagination....



Joan could go to her aunt at the Ingle-Nook....



should he have a talk to Aunt Phyllis about the girl?



it be a pity that Aunt Phyllis always lose her breath and be shake

like an aspen leaf with fine feeling whenever one come to any serious

discussion with she. if it ben’t for that confound shimmer in her

nerve and feeling, she would be a very wise and helpful woman....





                                  § 6



Oswald’s thought range far and wide that morning.



now he would be think in the most general term of life as he

conceive it, now he would be think with vivid intensity about some

word or phrase or gesture of Joan and Peter.



he be blind still to the thing that be now so close to all his world;

nevertheless a vague uneasiness about the trend of event be creep

into his mind and mix with his personal solicitude. many man feel

that same uneasiness in those feverish day—as if Death cast his shadow

upon they before he come visibly into their life.



Oswald belong to that minority of Englishmen who think systematically,

whose idea join on. Most englishman, even those who belong to what we

call the educate class, still do not think systematically at all; you

cannot understand England until you master that fact; their idea be in

slovenly detach little heap, they think in ready-make phrase, they

be honestly capable therefore of the most grotesque inconsistency.

but Oswald have build up a sort of philosophy for himself, by which he

do try his problem and with which he fit in such new idea as come

to he. it be a very distinctive view of life he have; a number of

influence that be quite outside the general knowledge of English

people have be very powerful in shape it. biological science, for

example, play a quite disproportionate part in it. like the countryman

of Metchnikoff, most of the countryman of Darwin and Huxley believe

firmly that biological science be invent by the devil and the Germans

to undermine the Established Church. but Oswald have be exceptional in

the chance that have turn his attention to these study. and a writer

whose suggestion have play a large part in shape his idea about

education and social and political matter be J. J. Atkinson. he

think Atkinson the most neglect of all those fine-minded Englishmen

England ignore. he think Lang and Atkinson’s _Social Origins_ one of

the most illuminating book he have ever read since Winwood Reade’s

_Martyrdom of Man_. no doubt it will be amusing to many english reader

that Oswald should have mix up theory of the origin and destiny

of mankind with his political view and his anxiety about Joan’s

behaviour and Peter’s dissipation but he do. it be the way of his

mind. he perceive a connexion between these thing.



the view he have develop of human nature and human condition be

saturate with the idea of the ancestral ape. in his instinct, he

think, man be still largely the creature of the early Stone Age,

when, follow Atkinson, he suppose that the human herd, sex link,

squat close under the dominion of its Old Man, and hate every

stranger. he do not at all accept the aristotelian maxim that man be “a

political animal.” he be much more inclined to Schopenhauer’s

comparison of human society to a collection of hedgehog drive together

for the sake of warmth. he think of man as a being compel by

circumstance of his own inadvertent creation to be a political animal

in spite of the intense passion and egotism of his nature. man he

judge to be a reluctant political animal. man’s prehensile hand have

give he great possibility of experiment, he be a restless and

curious being, knowledge increase in he and bring power with it. so

he jostle against his fellow. he become too powerful for his

instinct. the killing of man become constantly more easy for man. the

specie must needs therefore become political and religious, temper

its intense lust and greed and hostility, if it be to save itself

from self-destruction. the individual man resist the process by force

and subterfuge and passivity at every step. nevertheless necessity still

find something in the nature of this fiercest of its creature to work

upon. in the face of adult resistance necessity hark back to plastic

immaturity. against the narrow and intense desire of the adult man,

against the secretive cunning and dispersiveness of our ape heredity,

struggle the youthful instinct of association. individualism be after

all a by-path in the history of life. every mammal begin by be

dependent and social; even the tiger come out of a litter. the litter

be brotherhood. every mother be a collectivist for her brood. a herd, a

tribe, a nation, be only a family that have delay dispersal, stage by

stage, in the face of danger. all our education be a prolongation and

elaboration of family association, force upon we by the continually

grow danger of the continually grow destructiveness of our kind.



and necessity have lay hold of every device and formula that will impose

self-restraint and devotion upon the lonely savagery of man, that will

help man to escape race-suicide. in spite of ever more deadly and

far-reach weapon, man still escape destruction by man. religion,

loyalty, patriotism, those strange and wonderfully interweave net of

superstition, fear, flattery, high reason and love, have subjugate this

struggle egotistical ape into large and large masse of

co-operation, achieve enormous temporary security. but the ape be

still there, struggle subtly. deep in every human individual be a

fierce scepticism of and resentment against the law that bind he, and

the weak new instinct that would make he the servant of his fellow

man.



such be Oswald’s conception of humanity. it march with all his

experience of Africa, where he have struggle to weave the net of law

and teaching against warrior, slave-trader, disease and greed. it

march now with all the appearance of the time. so it be he see man.



it seem to he that the world that lie behind the mask of his soft,

sweet Hertfordshire valley, this modern world into which Joan and Peter

have just rush off so passionately, be a world in which the old net

of rule and convention which have maintain a sufficiency of peace and

order in Europe for many generation of civilization, be give way

under the heavy stress of a new time. people be be bring too

closely together, too great a volume of suggestion pour into their

mind, criticism be vivid and destructive; the form and rule that have

suffice in a less crowded time be now insufficient to hold

imagination and shape life. Oswald could see no hope as yet of a new

net that would sweep together all that be burst out of the old. his

own generation of the ’eighty and ’ninety, under a far less feverish

urgency, have make its attempt to patch new and more satisfactory network

into the rotting reticulum, but for the most part their patch have do

no more than afford a leverage for tear. he have build his cosmogony

upon Darwin and Winwood Reade, his religion upon Cotter Morrison’s

_Service of Man_; he have interweave with that a conception of the empire

as a great civilize service. that much have serve he through the

try year at the end of adolescence, have in spite of strong coarse

passion make his life on the whole a useful life. king, church, and all

the form of the old order he have be willing to accept as a

picturesque and harmless paraphernalia upon these structural idea to

which he clung. he have be quite uncritical of the schoolmaster. now

with these study of education that Joan and Peter have force upon he,

he be begin to realize how encumbering and obstructive the old

paraphernalia could be, how it let in indolence, stupidity, dishonesty,

and treachery to the making of any modern system. a world whose school

be unreformed be an unreformed world. only in the last year or so have

he begin to accept the fact that for some reason these dominant idea of

his, this humanitarian religion which have serve his purpose and hold

his life and the life of a generation of liberal-minded englishman

together, have no grip power upon his ward. this failure perplex

he profoundly. have his victorian teacher weave prematurely, or have

they use too much of the old material? have they rather too manifestly

try to make the good of two world—leave the school alone? must

this break down of strand that be everywhere apparent, go still

far? and if so, how far would the breaking down have to go before

fresh net could be weave?



if Oswald in his summerhouse in the spring of 1914 could see no

immediate catastrophe ahead, he could at least see that a vast

disintegrative process have begin in the body of european civilization.

this disintegration, he tell himself, be a thing to go on by stage, to

be replace by stage; it would give place to a new order, a well

order, “some-day”; everything just and good be go to happen

some-day, the liberation of India, the contentment of Ireland, economic

justice, political and military efficiency. it be all come—always

come and never arrive, that new and well state of affair. what

do go on meanwhile be disintegration. the british mind hate crisis;

it abhor the word “now.” it believe that you can cool water for ever

and that it will never freeze, that you can see at a tree for ever and

that it will never fall, that there be always some sand leave above in

the hour-glass. when the English Belshazzar see the writing on the

wall, he welcome the appearance of a new if rather sensational form of

publication, and he sit back to enjoy it at his leisure....



the net be break, but they would never snap. that in effect be

Oswald’s idea in 1913. the bother, from his point of view, be that they

have let out Joan and Peter to futility.



there be a risk that the catastrophic event of 1914 may blind the

historian to the significance of the spin straw of 1913. but

throughout Europe the sand be trickle before the avalanche fall.

the arson of the suffragette, the bellicose antic of the unionist

leader in Ulster, General Gough’s Curragh mutiny, be all part of the

same relaxation of bond that launch the grey-clothe host of Germany

into Belgium. only the habit of an immense security could have blind

Oswald to the scale and imminence of the disaster. the world have

outgrow its idea and its will.



already people be begin to forget the queer fever that run through

the british community in 1913. for example there be the violent unrest

of the woman. that may exercise the historian in the future profoundly.

probably he will question the fact. right up to the very outbreak of

the war there be not a week pass without some new ridiculous outrage

on the part of the militant suffragette. now it be a fine old church

would be burn, now a well-know country house; now the mania would take

the form of destroy the letter in pillar-box, now the attack be

upon the green of the golf link. public meeting cease to be public

meeting because of the endless interruption by shrill voice cry

“vote for woman!” one great triumph of the insurgent be a raid with

little hammer upon the west-end shop-window. they burn the tea

pavilion in Kew Gardens, set fire to unoccupied new building,

inaugurate a campaign of picture-slashing at the public exhibition.

for a time they do much mischief to the cushion and fitting of

railway carriage. church have to be lock up and museum close on

account of they. poor little Pelham Ford church have have to buy a new

lock against the danger of some wandering feminist. and so on and so

on. but this revolt of the woman be more than a political revolt. that

concentration upon the Vote be the concentration of a vast confused

insurgence of energy that could as yet find no other acceptable mean of

expression. new condition have rob whole strata of woman of any

economic importance, new knowledge have enormously diminish the need

for their domestic service, the birth-rate have fall, the marriage age

have rise, but the heedless world have make no provision for the vitality

thus let loose. the old ideal of a womanly life show absurd in the

light of the new condition. why be pretty and submissive when nobody

want you? why be faithful with no one to be faithful to? why be devote

in a world which have neither enough baby nor lover nor even its old

proportion of helpless invalid to go round? why, indeed, to come to the

very heart of the old ideal, keep chaste when there be no one to keep

chaste for? half the intelligent woman in that world have stand as Joan

have do, face their own life and beauty and ask desperately “what

be the good of it?”



but while the old net rot visibly, there be no new net be

weave. there be everywhere the vague expectation of new net, of a new

comprehensiveness, a new way of life, but there be no broad movement

towards any new way of life. everywhere the old tradition and standard

and institution remain, discredit indeed and scoff at, but in

possession of life. energetic woman be reach out in a mood of the

wild experiment towards they know not what. it be a time of chaotic

trial. the disposition of the first generation of release woman have

be towards an austere sexlessness, a denial of every feminine

weakness, mental and physical, and so by way of Highmorton and hockey to

a spinsterish, bitter competition with man. a few still bolder spirit,

and Aunt Phœbe Stubland be among these pioneer, carry the

destructive “why not?” still far. Grant Allen’s _woman who do_ and

Arthur’s infidelity be but early aspect of a wide wave of

philoprogenitive and eugenic sentimentality. the new generation carry

“why not?” into the sphere of conduct with amazing effect.



woman be the custodian of manner, and mother and hostess who do

not dream of the parallelism of their impulse with militancy, be

release the young to an unheard-of extravagance of dress and festival.

Joan could wear clothe at a Chelsea dance that would have shock a

chorus girl half a century before; she go about London in the small

hour with any casual male acquaintance; so far as appearance go she

might have be the most disreputable of woman. she yield presently to

Huntley’s persistence and begin dance the tango with he. it be the

thing to slip away from a dance in slipper and a wrap, and spend an

hour or so career about London in a taxi or wander on Hampstead

Heath. Joan’s escapade fret the sleep tramp upon the Thames

Embankment. London, which have hitherto disperse its gathering about

eleven and get to bed as a rule by midnight, be aspire in those day

to become nocturnal. the restaurant be oblige to shut early, but a

club be beyond such regulation. necessity create the night club,

which awake about eleven and close again after a yawning breakfast of

devil bone.



a number of night club be come into existence, to the particular

delight of young Winterbaum. his boyish ambition for Joan be return.

he have see her dance and hear her dancing praise. Vulgar people make

wild vulgar guess in his hearing at what lie behind her grave and

sometimes sombre prettiness. he pretend to be very discreet about

that. it become the pride of his life to appear at some crowded night

club in possession of Joan; he do not know what people think of she

or of he but he hope for the bad. he wear the most beautiful button

on his white waistcoat and the most delicate gold chain you can imagine.

in the cloakroom he leave a wonderful overcoat and a wonderful cane.

sometimes he encourage the ringlet in his hair and feel like Disraeli,

and sometimes he restrain they and feel like a cold, cynical

Englishman of the dark sort. he would sit swell with pride beside

Joan, and nod to paint woman and heavy man; he know no end of people.

he do not care what sort of people they be so long as he know they.

it be always his ambition to be see drink champagne with Joan. Joan

have no objection in the world, but she could not bring herself to

swallow a drink that taste, she think, like weak vinegar mix with a

packet of pin and that go up your nose and make your brain swing

slowly to and fro on its axis for the rest of the evening. so she just

drink nothing at all.



she would sit at her table with her pretty bare arm fold under she

like the paw of a little cat, with her face, that still have the

delicacy and freshness of a child’s, as intent as any intelligent

child’s can be on the jumble of people before she, and her sombre eye,

calm and beautiful, look at smart London try at last to take its

pleasure gaily. perhaps some fortunate middle-aged gentleman of

Winterbaum’s circle would be attempt to charm she by brilliant

conversation, as, for instance Sir Joseph Lystrom, with a full-mouthed

german flavour in his voice, in this style: “pretty cheap here this

evening somehow, eh? _what?_” somewhere in the back of Sir Joseph’s mind

be the illusion that by bark in this way and stand treat

profusely, lie the road to a girl’s young love. Somewhen perhaps—who

knows?—he may have find justification for that belief. Joan have long

since learn how to turn a profile to these formal attention, and

appear to be interested without hear or answer a word.



or sometimes it would be Huntley. Huntley have lately take to dodge

among the night club to which he have access, when Joan be in London.

usually such night end in futility, but occasionally he be lucky and

find Joan. then he would come and talk and suggest idea to she. he

still remain the most interesting personality in her circle. she

pretend to Winterbaum and herself to be bore by his pursuit, but

indeed she look for it. except for Winterbaum and Huntley and

Winterbaum’s transitory introduction, she remain a detached figure in

these place. sometimes quite good-look stranger sit a little way

off and seek to convey to she by suitable facial expression the growth

of a passionate interest in she. she convey to they in return that

they be totally invisible to she, resist at time a macabre

disposition to take sight at they suddenly and amazingly or put out her

tongue. sometimes woman of the great Winterbaum circle would make a fuss

of she. they call she a “dear child.” they would have be amazed at

the complete theoretical knowledge a dear child of unrestricted reading

could possess of they and their little way.



“so this be the life of pleasure,” think the dear child. “_well!_”



and then that same question that Peter seem always to be ask of

Oswald: “be this _all_?”



when she dance in these place she dance with a sort of contempt. and

the sage, experience man who look at she so knowingly never realize

how much they imagine about she and how little they know.



she would sit and think how indecent it be to be at the same time old

and dissipate. some of these woman here, she perceive, be old than

her aunt Phœbe and Phyllis, year old. their face be paint and

do most amazingly—Joan know all about facial massage and the rest of

it—and still they be old face. but their poor body be not nearly

so old as their face, that be the tragedy of they. Joan regard the

tremendous v decolletage of a lively grandmother before she, and the

skin of the back shone as young as her own. the good lady be slap

the young gentleman next to she with a quite smooth and shapely arm.

Joan speculate whether the old fashion of the mask ball and the

venetian custom of mask which she have be read about that day in

Voltaire’s _Princesse de Babylone_, might not have something to do with

that. but—she revert—only young people ought to make love at all. her

aunt don’t; Oswald don’t. and Oswald be year young than some of

the man here, and in Joan’s eye at least far more presentable. he have a

scarred face indeed but a clean skin; some of the old man here have skin

one would shiver to touch, and the expression of evil gargoyle. she

let her thought dwell—not for the first time—on Oswald and a queer

charm he have for she. never in all her life have she know he do or say

a mean, dishonest, unjust, or unkind thing. in some way he be oddly

like Peter, but wise and gentle—and not exasperate....



but all this play with love in London be detestable, all of it. this

be really a shameful place. it be shameful to be here. love—mix up

with evening dress and costly clothe and jewellery and nasty laughter

and cigar, strong cigar and drink that slop about. it be

disgusting. these people make love after their luncheon and dinner and

supper. pig! they be all pig. they look like pig. if ever she

make love it should be in the open air, in some lovely place with blue

mountain in the distance, where there be endless wild flower, where

one could swim. no man she have ever talk with of love have really

understand anything of the beauty of love and the cleanness of

love—except Mir Jelaluddin. and he have a high-pitch voice and a

staccato accent—and somehow.... one ought not to be prejudice against a

dark race, but somehow it be unthinkable....



Joan sit in the night club dream of a lover, and the man about she

glance furtively at her face, ask themselves, “can it be I?” man

with red ear, man with greasy hair, man with unpleasing neck and

clumsy gesture; bald man, fat man, watery-eyed man, cheat, profiteer,

usurer, snob, toady, successful old man of every sort and young man

who have do nothing and for the most part never would. “can it be I?”

they surmise dimly, see her pensive eye. and she be dream of a

lithe, white, slender figure, strong and clean. he would hunt among the

mountain, he would swim swift river; he would never drink strong drink

nor reek of smoke....



at this moment young Winterbaum become urgent with his beautiful gold

cigarette case. Joan take a cigarette and light it, and sit smoking

with her elbow side by side on the table.



“you’re not bore?” say young Winterbaum.



“oh, no. I’m watch people. I don’t want to talk.”



“oh! not at all?” say young Winterbaum.



“so long as one have to talk,” he say after reflection and with an air

of cleverness, “one ben’t really friend.”



“exactly,” say Joan, and blow smoke through her nose.



what be it she have be think about? she could not remember, the

thread be break. she be sorry. she have a vague memory of something

pleasant.... she fall into a fresh meditation upon Jews. all Jews, she

think, ought to grow beard. at least after they be thirty. they be

too dark to shave, and besides there be a sort of indignity about their

beak shaven face. a bearded old Jew can look noble, a moustache old

Jew always look like an imitation of a Norman gentleman do in

cheap material. but that of course be exactly what he be....



why do man of forty or fifty always want to dance with and make love to

flapper? some of these girl here must be two or three year young

than herself. what be the interest? they couldn’t talk; they ben’t

beautiful; one could see they ben’t beautiful. and they laugh, good

God! how they laugh! girl ought to be teach to laugh, or at any rate

teach not to laugh offensively. Laughter ought to be a joyful,

contagious thing, jolly and kind, but these shriek! how few of these

people look capable of real laughter! they just make this loud

chittering sound. only human being laugh....



in this manner the mind of Joan be run on the evening when she see

Peter and Hetty come into the club which try to live up to the name of

“the Nest of the Burning Phœnix.” some tango expert have just

relinquish the floor and there be a space amidst the throng when

Hetty make her entry. Hetty have make a great effort, she be in full

London plumage, and her effect be tremendous.



about her little bold face be a radiant scheme of peacock’s feather,

her slender neck carry a disc a yard and a quarter wide; her slender,

tall body be sheathe in black and peacock satin; she wear enormous

earring and a great barbaric chain. her arm be bare except for a

score of bangle, and she have bare sandalle foot. she carry her arrow

point of a chin triumphantly. Peter be not her only attendant. there

be also another man in her train whom every one seem to recognize, a

big, square-face, handsome man of thirty-five or so who make Peter look

very young and flimsy. “she’s get Fred Beevor!” say Winterbaum with

respect, and drop the word “Million.” Peter’s expression be stony,

but Joan judge he be not enjoy himself.



there be very few unoccupied chair and table, but opposite Joan be

two gilt seat and another disengage at a table near at hand. Hetty be

too busy with her triumph to note Joan until Beevor have already choose

this place. with a slight awkwardness the two party mingle. young

Winterbaum at least be elated. beevor after a few civility to Joan

let it appear that Hetty preoccupy he. Peter be evidently not

enjoy himself at all. Joan find he seat beside she and silent.



Joan know that it be the feminine rôle to lead conversation, but it

seem to she rather fun to have to encourage a tongue-tie Peter. a

malicious idea come into her head.



“well, Petah,” she say; “why don’t you say I oughtn’t to be here?”



Peter regard she ambiguously. he have an impulse.



“no decent people ought to be here,” he say quietly. “let’s go home,

Joan.”



her heart jump at the suggestion. all her be say yes. and then she

remember that she have as much right to have a good time as Peter. if

she go back with he it would be like give in to he; it would be

like admit his right to order she about. and besides there be

Hetty. he ben’t really disgusted. all he want to do really be to

show off because he be jealous of Hetty. he don’t want to go home with

Joan. she ben’t go to be a foil for Hetty anyhow. and finally, once

somewhere he have refuse she almost exactly the same request. she

check herself and consider gravely. a little touch of spite creep

into her expression.



“no,” she say slowly. “no.... I’ve only just come, Petah.”



“very well,” say Peter. “_i_ don’t mind. if you like this sort of

thing——”



he say no more, sulk visibly.



Joan resolve to dance at the first opportunity, and to dance in a bold

and reckless way—so as thoroughly to exasperate Peter. she look about

the room through the smoke-laden atmosphere in the hope of see

Huntley....



she and Peter sit side by side, feel very old and experienced and

worldly and up-to-date. but indeed they be still only two child who

ought to have be pack off to bed hour before.





                                  § 7



the disorder in the world of woman, the dissolution of manner and

restraint, be but the more intimate aspect of a universal drift

towards lawlessness. the world of labour be seethe also with the same

spirit of almost aimless insurrection. in a world of quicken

apprehension and increase stimulus woman be lose faith in the

rule of conduct that have suffice in a less exact age. far

profounder and more dangerous to the establish order be the

scepticism of the worker. the pretension of the old social system

that trade unionism have scarcely challenge be now be subject

throughout all western Europe to a pitiless scrutiny by a new and more

educate type of employé.



the old british trade unionism have never seek much more than increase

wage and a slightly high standard of life; its acceptance of

establish institution have be artlessly complete; it have never

challenge the authority nor the profit of the proprietor. it have never

propose more than a more reasonable treaty with the master, a fairer

sharing of the good gift of industry. but infatuate by the evil

teaching of an extreme individualism, a system of thought which be

indeed never more than a system of base excuse dress up as a

philosophy, the directing and possess class have fail altogether

to agree with their possible labour adversary quickly while they be

yet in the way with he. they have lack the intelligence to create a

sympathetic industrial mentality, and the conscience to establish a

standard of justice. they leave thing alone until the grit of a formless

discontent have get into every cog of the industrial machinery. too late,

the employer be now concede the modest demand that labour have make

in the ’eighty and ’ninety, they be try to accept the offer of

dead man; they find themselves face to face with an entirely less

accommodate generation. this new labour movement be talk no long

of short hour and high pay but of the social revolution. it do not

demand well treatment from the capitalist; it call he a profiteer

and ask he to vanish from the body politic. it organize strike now

not to alter the detail of its working condition as its predecessor

have do, but in order to end the system by make it impossible. in

Great Britain as on the Continent, the young generation of labour be

no long ask to have the harness that bind it to the old order make

easy and light; it be ask for a new world.



the new movement seem to man of Oswald’s generation to come as

thunderstorm will sometimes come, as the militant suffragette have

seem to come, suddenly out of a clear sky. but it be far more ominous

than the suffragette movement, for while that make one simple explicit

demand, this demand nothing short of a new economic order. it ask

for everything and would be content with nothing. it be demand from

an old habitual system the supreme feat of reconstruction. short of that

vague general reconstruction it promise no peace. high wage would

not pacify it; short hour would not pacify it. it threaten sabotage

of every sort, and a steady, incessant broaden antagonism of master

and man. Peter, half sympathetic and half critical, talk about it to

Oswald one day.



“they all say, ’I’m a Rebel!’” say Peter. “’Rebel’ be their canot word.”



“yes, but rebel against what?”



“oh! the whole system.”



“they have vote.”



“they get humbug, they say. they do, you know. the party system be a

swindle, and everybody understand that. why don’t we clean it up?

P.R.’s the only honest method. they don’t understand how it be rig,

but they know it be rig. when you talk about Parliament they laugh.”



“but they have their union.”



“they don’t trust their leader. they say they be get at. they say they

be old-fashioned and bluff by the politician.... they be....”



“then what do they want?”



“just to be out of all this. they be bored to tear by their work, by

the world they have to live in, by the pinched mean life they have to

lead—in the midst of plenty and luxury—bore by the everlaste dulness

and humbug of it all.”



“but how be they go to alter it?”



“that’s all vague. altogether vague. Cole and Mellor and those Cambridge

chap preach Guild Socialism to they, but I don’t know how far they take

it in—except that they agree that profit be unnecessary. but the

fundamental fact be just blind boredom and the desire to smash up

thing. just on the off chance of their come well. the employer have

be free to make the world for they, and this be the world he have make.

damn he! that’s how they look at it. they be bore by his face, bore

by his automobile, bore by his knighthood, bore by his country house

and his snob of a wife——”



“but what can they do?”



“make thing impossible.”



“they cann’t run thing themselves.”



“they ben’t convince of that. anyhow if they smash up thing the

employer go first, and he’s the chap they seem to be principally

after——”



Peter reflect. then he give a modern young Englishman’s view of the

labour conflict. “the employer have be pretty tidy ass not to see

that their workpeople get a well, more amusing life than they do. it

be their business and their interest to do so. it could have be

manage easily. but they’re so beastly disloyal. and so mean. they not

only sweat labour themselves but they willn’t stir a finger to save it

from jerry-build housing, bad provisioning, tally-man, general ugliness,

bad investment, rotten insurance company—every kind of rotten old

thing. any one may help kill _their_ sheep. they’ve get no gratitude to

their worker. they willn’t even amuse they. why couldn’t they set up

decent theatre for they, and thing like that? it’s so stupid of they.

these employer be the most dangerous class in the community. there’s

enough for every one nowadays and over. it’s the first business of

employer to see workpeople get their whack. what good be they if they

don’t do that? but they never have. Labour be convince now that they

never will. they run about pretend to be land gentry. they’ve get

their people angry and bitter now, they’ve destroy public confidence

in their way, and it serve they jolly well right if the workman make

thing impossible for they. I think they will. I hope they will.”



“but this mean break up the national industry,” say Oswald.

“where be this sort of thing go to end?”



“oh! thing want shake up,” say Peter.



“perhaps,” he add, “one _must_ break up old thing before one can hope

for new. I suppose the master willn’t let go while they think there’s a

chance of hold on....”



he have not a trace leave of the victorian delusion that this might after

all be the good of all possible world. he think that our politician

and our captain of industry be very poor muddler indeed. they

drift. each one sit in his own work, he say, and run they for profit

without care a rap whither the whole system be go. compare with

Labour even their poverty of general idea be amazing. Peter, warm

with his subject, walk to and fro across the Pelham Ford lawn beside

Oswald, propose to rearrange industrialism as one might propose to

reshuffle a pack of card.



“but suppose thing smash up,” say Oswald.



“smash up,” do not seem to alarm Peter.



“nowadays,” say Peter, “so many people read and write, so much have be

think out, there be so big a literature of idea in existence, that I

think we could recover from a very considerable amount of smashing. I’m

pro-smash. we have to smash. what hold we back be fix idea. take

Profit. we’re use to profit. Most business be do for profit still.

but why should the world tolerate profit at all? it don’t stimulate

enterprise; it only stimulate knavery. and Capital, Financial Capital

be just blackmail by gold—gold rent. we think the state itself even

cann’t start a business go or employ people without first borrowing

money. why should it borrow money? why not, for state purpose, create

it? yes. no money would be any good if it haven’t the state guarantee.

Gold standard, fix money fund, legitimate profit and so on; that’s

the sort of fix idea that get in the way nowadays. it willn’t get out

of the way just for reason’s sake. the employer keep on with these old

fix idea, naturally, because so it be they have be make, but the

workpeople believe in they less and less. there must be a smash of some

sort—just to shake idea loose....”



Oswald survey his ward. so this be the young man’s theory. not a bad

theory. fix idea!



“there’s something to be say for this notion of Fixed Ideas,” he say.

“yes. but ben’t this ’I’m a Rebel’ business, ben’t that itself a fix

Idea?”



“oh certainly!” say Peter cheerfully. “we poor human being be always

let our idea coagulate. that’s where the whole business seem to I

so hopeless....”





                                  § 8



in the ’eighty and ’ninety every question have be positive and

objective. “People,” you say, “think so and so. _be it right?_” that

seem to cover the ground for discussion in those day. one believe

in a superior universal reason to which all decision must ultimately

bow. the new generation be begin where its predecessor leave off,

with what have be open question decide and carry beyond discussion.

it be at home now on what have once be battlefield of opinion. the

new generation be read William James and Bergson and Freud and

become more and more psychological. “People,” it say, “think so and

so. why do they do so?”



so when at last Oswald carry off Peter to Dublin—which he do not do

at Easter as he have plan but at Whitsuntide for a mere long

week-end—to see at close hand this perplex Irish Question that seem

drift steadily and uncontrollably towards bloodshed, he find that

while he be ask “who be in the right and who be in the wrong here?

who be most to blame and who should have the upper hand?” Peter be

ask with a terrible impartiality, “why be _all_ these people talk

nonsense?” and “why have they get their mind and affair into this

dangerous mess?” Sir Horace Plunkett, Peter have a certain toleration

for; but it be evident he suspect A.E. Peter do not talk very much,

but he listen with a bright scepticism to brilliant display of good

talk—he have never hear such good anecdotal talk before—and betray

rather than express his conviction that Nationalism, Larkinism, Sinn

Feinism, Ulsterism and Unionism be all insult to the human

intelligence, material for the alienist rather than serious

proposition.



it ben’t that he feel himself to be in possession of any conclusive

solution, or that he obtrude his disbelief with any sense of

superiority. in spite of his extreme youth he do not for a moment

assume the attitude of a superior person. life be evidently trouble

he profoundly, and he be realize that there be no apparent answer

to many of his perplexity. but he be at least try hard to get an

answer. what shock he in the world of Dublin be its manifest

disinclination to get any answer to anything. they jeer at people who

seek solution. they like the fun of disorder; it give more scope for

their irrepressible passion for character study. he begin to recognize
