one particular phrase as the keynote of Dublin’s animation: “hev ye

hurrd the let?”



on the Sunday afternoon of their stay in Dublin, Powys motor they

through the city by way of Donnybrook and so on round the bay to Howth

to see the view from Howth Head. Powys drive with a stray guest beside

he. behind, Peter impart impression to Oswald.



“I don’t like these high wall,” he say. “I’ve never see such a lot of

high wall.... it’s just as if they all shut themselves in from one

another.”



“Fixed Ideas, Peter?”



“they _be_ rather like Fixed Ideas. I suppose high wall be fun to

climb over and throw thing over. but—it’s uncivilized.”



“everybody,” grumble Peter, “be give to fix idea, but the Irish

have ’em for choice. all this rot about Ireland a nation and about the

Harp, which ben’t properly their symbol, and the dear old Green Flag

which ben’t properly their colour!... they cann’t believe in that stuff

nowadays.... but _can_ they? in our big world? and about be a Black

Protestant and pretend Catholics be poison, or the other way round.

what be Protestants and Catholics now?... old dead squabble.... Dead

as Druids.... keep up all that bickering stuff, when a child of eight

ought to know nowadays that the Christian God start out to be a

universal, charitable God.... if Christ come to Dublin the Catholics and

Protestants would have a free fight to settle which be to crucify

Him....”



“it’s the way with they,” say Oswald. “we’ve get to respect irish

opinion.”



“it don’t respect itself. everywhere else in the world, wherever we

have be, there’s be at least something like the germ of an idea of a

new life. but here! when you get over here you realize for the first

time that England be after all a live country try to get on to

something—compare with this merry-go-round.... it’s exactly like a

merry-go-round churn away. it’s the atmosphere of a country fair. an

Irishman haven’t any idea of a future at all, so far as I can see—except

that perhaps his grandchild will tell story of what a fine fellow

he be....”



the automobile halt for a moment at cross road, and the finger-post

be in Erse character.



“look at _that!_” say Peter with genuine exasperation. “and hardly a

Dubliner know fifty word of the language! it’s foolery. if we be

irish I suppose we should smother London with black-letter. we should go

on pretend that we, too, be still Catholics and Protestants. the

pseudo-Protestants would hang Smithfield with black on account of the

martyr, and the pseudo-Catholics would come and throw the meat about on

Fridays. Chesterton and Belloc would love it anyhow.”...



Oswald be not sure of the extent of Peter’s audience. “the

susceptibility of a proud people, Peter,” he whisper, with his eye

on the back of their host.



“bother their susceptibility. much they care for _our_

susceptibility. the bad insult you can offer a grow-up man be to

humour he,” say Peter. “what’s the good of pretend to be

sympathetic with all this wear of the Green. it’s like our White Rose

League. let ’em do it by all mean if they want to, but don’t let’s

pretend we think it romantic and beautiful and all the rest of it. it’s

just pose and dress up, and it’s a nuisance, Nobby. all Dublin be

pose and dress up and play at rebellion, and so be all Ulster.

the Volunteers of the eighteenth century all over again. it’s like

historical charade. and they’ve point loaded gun at each other. only

idiot point load gun. why cann’t we English get out of it all, and

leave they to pose and dress up and then tell anecdote and anecdote

and anecdote about it until they be sick of it? if ever they be sick

of it. let they have their Civil War if they want it; let they keep on

with Civil Wars for ever; what have it get to do with we?”



“you’re a Home ruler then,” say Oswald.



“I don’t see that we English do any good here at all. what be we here

for anyhow? the Castle’s just another Fixed Idea, something we haven’t

the mental vigour to clear away. nobody do any good here. we’re not

give they new idea, we’re not unify they, we’re not let

Ireland out into the world—which be what she want—we’re not do

anything but just hold on.”



“what’s that?” say Powys suddenly over his shoulder.



“Peter’s declare for Home Rule,” say Oswald.



“after his glimpse of the slum of Dublin?”



“it’s out of malice. he want to leave Irishmen to Irishmen.”



“Ulster say _no_!” say Powys. “tell he to talk to Ulster,” and

resume a conversation he have interrupt with the man beside he.



at the corner where Nassau Street run into Grafton Street they be

hold up for some lengthy minute by a long procession that be trail

past Trinity College and down Grafton Street. it have several band, and

in the forefront of it go National Volunteers in green uniform,

obviously for the most part old soldier; they be follow by man with

green badge, and then a straggle of Larkinites and various Friendly

Societies with their band and banner, and then by a long dribble of

child and then some workgirl, and then a miscellany of people who

have apparently fall in as the procession pass because they have

nothing else to do. as a procession it be tedious rather than

impressive. the warm afternoon—it be the last day in May—have take the

good feeling out of the walker. few talk, still few smile. the

common expression be a long-visage discontent, a gloomy hostile stare

at the car and police cordon, an aimless disagreeableness. they be

all be very stern and resolute about they do not quite know what.

they mean to show that Dublin could be as stern and resolute as

Belfast. between the part of the procession be lengthy gap. it be a

sunshiny, dusty afternoon, and the leg of the processionist be dusty

to the knee, their brow moist, and their lip dry. there be an

unhurrie air about they of go nowhere in particular. it be evident

that many of their banner be heavy. “what’s it all about?” ask

Oswald.



“Lord know,” say Powys impatiently. “it’s just a demonstration.”



“be that all? why don’t we cut across now and get on?”



“there’s more come. don’t you hear another band?”



“but the police could hold it up for a minute and let all these tramcar

and automobile across.”



“there’d be a fight,” say Powys. “they daren’t.”...



“and I suppose this sort of thing be go on in the north too?” ask

Oswald after a pause.



“oh! everywhere,” say Powys. “Orange or Green. but they’ve get more

gun up north.”



“these people don’t really want Ireland a Nation and all the rest of

it,” say Peter.



“_oh?_” say Powys, stare at he.



“well, look at they,” say Peter. “you can see by their face. they’re

just bored to death. I suppose most people _be_ bored to death in

Ireland. there’s nothing do. England just hold they up, I suppose.

and it’s an island—rather off the main line. there’s nothing to get

people’s mind off these endless, dreary old quarrel. it’s all they

have. but they’re bore by it....”



“and that’s why we talk nothing but anecdote, Peter, eh?” Powys

grin.



“well, you _do_ talk a lot of anecdote,” say Peter, who haven’t realize

the sharpness of his host’s hearing.



“oh! we do. I don’t complain of your see it. it ben’t your discovery.

have you read or hear the true word that be ever say of

Ireland—by that man Shaw? in _John Bull’s Other Island_.... that

laugh scene about the pig. ’nowhere else could such a scene cause a

burst of happiness among the people.’ that’s the very gut of thing

here; eh?”



“it’s his good play,” say Oswald, avoid too complete an assent.



“it get there,” Powys admit, “anyhow. the way all they fool come

into the shanty and snigger.”...



the last dreg of the procession pass reluctantly out of the way. it

fade down Grafton Street into a dust cloud and a confusion of band

noise. the policeman prepare to release the congested traffic. Peter

lean out to count the number of tram and automobile that have be

hold up. he be still count when the automobile turn the corner.



they shake Dublin off and spin cheerfully through the sunshine along the

coast road to Howth. it be a sparkling bright afternoon, and the road

be cheerful with the prim happiness of many couple of irish lover.

but that afternoon peace be the mask wear by one particular day. if the

near future could have cast a phantom they would have see along this

road a few week ahead of they the gun-runner of Howth march to the

first foolish bloodshed in Dublin street....



they see Howth Castle, make up now by Lutyens to look as it ought to

have look and never have look in the past. the friend Powys have

bring want to talk to some of the castle people, and while these two

stay behind Oswald and Peter go on, between high hedge of clip

beech and up a steep, winding path amidst great bush of rhododendron

in full flower to the grey rock and heather of the crest. they stand in

the midst of one of the most beautiful view in the world. Northward

they look over Ireland’s Eye at Lambay and the blue Mourne mountain

far away; eastward be the lush green of Meath, southward be the long

beach of the bay sweep round by Dublin to Dalkey, back by more blue

mountain that run out eastward to the Sugar Loaf. below their foot the

pale castle cluster amidst its rich greenery, and to the east, the

level blue sea sustain one single sunlit sail. it be rare that the

sense of beauty flood Peter, as so often it flood Joan, but this

time he be transport.



“but this be altogether beautiful,” he say, like one who be take by

surprise.



and then as if to himself: “how beautiful life might be! how splendid

life might be!”



Oswald be stand on a ledge below Peter, and with his back to he. he

wait through a little interval to see if Peter would say any more.

then he prick he with “only it ben’t.”



“no,” say Peter, with the sunlight go out of his voice. “it ben’t.”



he go on talk after a moment’s reflection.



“it’s as if we be hypnotize and couldn’t get away from mean thing,

beastly suspicion, and stale quarrel. I suppose we be still half

ape. I suppose our brain set too easily and rapidly. I suppose it’s

easy to quarrel yet and still hard to understand. we take to jealousy

and bitterness as duckling take to water. think of that stale, dusty

procession away there!”



Oswald’s old dream vision of the dark forest come back to his mind. “be

there no way out, Peter?” he say.



“if some great idea would take hold of the world!” say Peter....



“there have be some great idea,” say Oswald....



“if it would take hold of one’s life,” Peter finish his thought....



“there have be Christianity,” say Oswald.



“Christianity!” Peter point at the distant mist that be Dublin. “sour

Protestants,” he say, “and dirty priest set simple people by the

ear.”



“but that ben’t true christianity.”



“there ben’t true christianity,” say Peter compactly....



“well, there’s love of country then,” say Oswald.



“that Dublin corporation be the most patriotic and nationalist in the

world. fierce about it. and it’s get complete control there. it’s green

in grain. no English need apply.... from the point of view of

administration that town be a muck heap—for patriotic crowing. look at

their dirty, ill-paved street. look at their filthy slum! see how they

let their bless nation’s child fester and die!”



“there be big idea than patriotism. there be idea of empire, the

Pax Britannica.”



“Carson smuggling gun.”



“well, be there nothing? do _you_ know of nothing?”



Oswald turn on his ward for the reply.



“there’s a sort of idea, I suppose.”



“but what idea?”



“there’s an idea in our mind.”



“but what be it, Peter?”



“call it Civilization,” Peter try.



“I believe,” he go on, weigh his word carefully, “as you believe

really, in the Republic of Mankind, in universal work for a common

end—for freedom, welfare, and beauty. haven’t you teach I that?”



“_have_ I teach you that?”



“it seem to I to be the commonsense aim for all humanity. you’re awake

to it. you’ve awaken I to it and I believe in it. but most of this

world be still deep in its old Fixed idea, walk in its sleep. and it

willn’t wake up. it willn’t wake up.... what can we _do_? we’ve get to a

sort of idea, it’s true. but here be these Irish, for example,

naturally witty and quick than you or I, hypnotize by Orange and

Green, by Protestant and Catholic, by all these stale thing—drift

towards murder. it’s murder be come here. you can smell the bloodshed

come on the air—and we cann’t do a thing to prevent it. not a thing.

the silliest bloodshed it will be. the silly bloodshe the world have

ever see. we cann’t do a thing to wake they up....



“we’re _in_ it,” say Peter in conclusion. “we cann’t even save

ourselves.”



“I’ve be want to get at your political idea for a long time,” say

Oswald. “you really think, Peter, there might be a big world

civilization, a world republic, do you call it?—without a single slum

hide in it anywhere, with the whole of mankind busy and happy, the

race live in peace, each accord to its aptitude, a world go

on—go on steady and swift to still well thing.”



“how can one believe anything else? don’t you?”



“but how do we get there, Peter?”



“oh, how do we get there?” echo Peter. “how do we get there?”



he dance a couple of step with vexation.



“I don’t _know_, Nobby,” he cry. “I don’t know. I cann’t find the way.

I’m make a mess of my life. I’m not get on with my work. you

_know_ I’m not.... either we’re mad or this world be. here’s all these

people in Ireland let a solemn humbug of a second-rate lawyer with a

heavy chin and a lumpish mind muddle they into a civil war—and _that’s_

reality! that’s life! the solemn League and Covenant—copy out of old

history book! that’s be serious! and over there in England, across

the sea, muddle and muck and nonsense indescribable. oh! and we’re _in_

it!”



“but ben’t there big movement afoot, Peter, social reform, the labour

movement, the emancipation of woman, big change like that?”



“only big discontent.”



“but don’t discontent make the change?”



“it’s just boredom that’s get they. it ben’t any disposition to _make_.

Labour be bored, woman be bored, all Ireland be bore. I suppose Russia

be bore and Germany be get bore. she be bore all the world with

her soldiering. how bored they must be in India too—by we! the day bore

its way round the earth now—like a mole. out of sight of the star. but

boring people don’t mean make a new world. it just mean bore on

to decay. it just mean one sort of foolish old fix idea rub and

saw against another, until something break down.... oh! I want to

get out of all this. I don’t _like_ this world of ours. I want to get

into a world awake. I’m young and I’m greedy. I’ve only get one life to

live, Nobby.... I want to spend it where something be be make. make

for good and all. where clever man can do something more than sit

overlong at meal and tell spiteful funny story. where there’s

something well to do than play about with one’s brain and viscera!...”





                                  § 9



in the day when Peter be bear the anglican system hold the Empire with

apparently invincible feeling of security and self-approval; it

possess the land, the church, the army, the foreign office, the court.

such people as Arthur and Dolly be of no more account than a stray

foreign gipsy by the wayside. when Peter come of age the anglican system

still hold on to army, foreign office, court, land, and church, but now

it be haunt by a sense of an impalpable yet gigantic antagonism that

might at any time materialize against it. it have an instinctive

perception of the near possibility of a new world in which its base

pride could have no adequate satisfaction, in which its authority would

be flout, its poor learning despise, and its precedent disregard.

the curious student of the history of England in the decade before the

Great War will find the clue to what must otherwise seem a hopeless

tangle in the steady, disingenuous, mischievous antagonism of the old

anglican system to every kind of change that might bring near the

dread process of modernization. education, and particularly

university, reform be block, the most necessary social legislation

fight against with incoherent passion, the light, most reasonable

taxation of land or inheritance resist.



wherever the old system could find ally it snatch at they and seek

to incorporate they with itself. it have long since take over the New

Imperialism with its tariff scheme and its spirit of financial

adventure. it have sneer aloof when the new democracy of the elementary

school seek to read and think; it have let any casual adventurer to

supply that read; but now the creator of _answer_ and _comic cut_

rule the _Times_ and sit in the House of Lords. it be a little

doubtful still whether he be of the new order or the old, whether he

be not himself an instalment of revolution, whether the Tories have

buy he or whether he have buy they, but at any rate he do for a

time seem to be serve the end of reaction.



to two source of strength the Anglicans clung with desperate

resolution, India and Ulster. from India the mass of english people be

shut and bar off as completely as any foreigner could have be.

India be the preserve of the “rule class.” to India the good

anglican, smite by doubt, chill by some disrespectful comment or

distress by some item of progress achieve, could turn, leave all

thought of new and unpleasant thing behind he; there in what he love

to believe be the “unchanging East” he could recover that sense of

walk freely and authoritatively upon an abundance of inferior people

which be so necessary to his nature, and which be be so seriously

impair at home. the institution of caste realize his secret ideal.

from India he and his womankind could return refresh, to the struggle

with Liberalism and all the power of democratic irreverence in England.

and Ulster be a still more precious stronghold for this narrow culture.

from the fastness of Ulster they could provoke the restless temperament

of the Irish to a thousand petty exasperation of the English, and for

Ulster, “loyal Ulster,” they could appeal to the generous partisanship

of the English against their native liberalism. More and more do it

become evident that Ulster be the keystone of the whole anglican

ascendancy; to that they owe their grip upon british politic, upon

army, navy, and education; they trade—nay! they exist—upon the open

irish sore. with Ireland heal and content England would be lose to

they. England would democratize, would americanize. the Anglicans would

vanish out of british life as completely as the kindre Tories vanish

out of America at the close of the eighteenth century. and when at last,

after year of confused bickering, a Home Rule Bill become law, and

peace between the two nation in Ireland seem possible, the Anglicans

step at once from legal obstruction to open treason and revolt. the

arming of Ulster to resist the decision of Parliament be incite from

Great Britain, it be support enthusiastically by the whole of the

Unionist party in Great Britain, its headquarters be in the west end

of London, and the refusal of General Gough to carry out the

precautionary occupation of Ulster be hail with wild joy in every

Tory home. it be not a genuine popular movement, it be an artificial

movement for which the landowne church people of Ireland and England

be chiefly responsible. it be assist by tremendous exertion on the

part of the London yellow press. when Sir Edward Carson go about

Ulster in that warm June of 1914, review armed man, promise “more

mauser,” and pour out inflammatory speech, he be manifestly

prepare bloodshed. the old Tory system have reach a point where it

have to kill man or go.



and it do not mean to go; it mean to kill. it mean to murder man.



if youth and the new idea be to go on with the world, the price be

blood.



Ulster be a little country; altogether the dispute do not affect many

thousand of man, but except for the difference in scale there be

indeed hardly any difference at all between this scramble towards civil

conflict in Ireland and the rush, swift and noiseless, that be now

carry central Europe towards immeasurable bloodshed. to kill and

mutilate and waste five human being in a petty riot be in its essence

no less vile a crime than to kill and mutilate and waste twenty

million. while the British Tories count their thousand, the Kaiser

and his general staff reckon in million; while the british

“loyalist” be smuggle a few disuse machine-gun from Germany,

Krupp’s factory be turn out great gun by the hundred. but the

evil thing be the same evil thing; a system narrow and outworn, full of

a vague fear of human reason and the common sense of mankind, full of

pride and greed and the insolent desire to trample upon man, a great

system of false assumption and fix idea, oppress by a thirsty

necessity for reassurance, be seek the refreshment of loud

self-assertion and prepare to drink blood. the militarist system that

centre upon Potsdam have clamber to a point where it have to kill man

or go. the Balkans be the Ulster of Europe. if once this balkan

trouble settle down, an age of peace might dawn for Europe, and how

would Junkerdom fare then, and where would Frau Bertha sell her good?

how would the War Lord justify his glory to the social democrat?...



but Oswald, like most englishman, be not attend very closely to

affair upon the continent. he be preoccupy with the unreason of

Ulster.



recently he have have a curious interview with Lady Charlotte Sydenham,

and her white excited face and blaze blue eye insist now upon

play the part of mask to the Ulster spirit in his thought. she have

have to call he in because she have run short of ready money through

over-subscription to various scheme for arm the northern patriot.

she have sit at her writing-desk with her cap a little over one eye, as

though it be a military cap, and the tuft of reddish hair upon she

cheek more like bristle than ever, and he have walk about the room

contrive disagreeable thing to say to she after his willnot. he be

disinclined to let she have more money, he confess; she ought to have

have more sense, he say, than to write off big cheque, cheque beyond

her mean, in support of this seditious mischief. if she ask these

people who have take her money, probably they would let she have some

back to go on with.



this enrage she nicely, as he have mean it to do. she scold at he. a

nice Sydenham he be, to see his King insult and his country tear

apart. he who have once wear the Queen’s uniform. thank God! she herself

be a Parminter and belong to a sounder strain!



“it’s you who be insult the king,” Oswald interpolate, “try to

defy his act in Parliament.”



“_oh!_” cry Lady Charlotte, bang the desk with her freckle fist.

“oh! parliament! I’d shoot ’em down! first that vile Budget, then the

attack on the Lords.”



“they pass the Parliament Act,” say Oswald.



“to save themselves from be swamp in a horde of working-man

peer—sit there in their cap with their dirty boot on the

cushion. Lord Keir Hardie! you’ll want Lord Chimneysweep and Viscount

Cats-meatman next.... then come that abominable Insurance Act—one thing

bad than another! set class against class and give they idea!

then we give up South Africa to the Boers again! what do we fight for?

don’t we buy the country with our blood? why, my poor cousin Rupert

Parminter be a prisoner in Pretoria for a whole year—thirteen weary

month! for nothing! and now Ireland be to be hand over to priest and

rebel. to _Irishmen_! and I—I be not to lift a finger, not a finger, to

save my king and my Country and my God—when they be all go straight

to the Devil!”



“H’m,” say Oswald, rustle the counterfoil in his hand. “but you

_have_ be lift your finger, you know!”



“if I could give more——”



“you _have_ give more.”



“I’d give it.”



“willn’t grime make a friendly advance? but I suppose you’re up to the

neck with Grimes.... I wonder what interest that little swindler charge

you.”



the old lady could not meet the mild scrutiny of his eye. “you come here

and grin and mock while your country be be hand over to a gang of

God-know-whos!” she say, stare at her inkpot.



“to whom probably it belong as much as it do to I,” say Oswald.



“thank God the army be sound,” say Aunt Charlotte. “thank God this

don’t end with your Parliaments! mark my word, Oswald! on the day

they raise their Home Rule flag in Ireland there will be man shoot

down—man shoot down. a grim lesson.”



“some perhaps kill by your own particular cheque,” say Oswald. “who

know?”



“I hope so,” say Lady Charlotte, with a quiver of deep passion in her

voice. “I hope so sincerely. if I could think I have cause the death of

one of those traitor.... if it could be Lloyd George!”...



but that be too much apparently even for Lady Charlotte to hope for.



Oswald, when he have come to she, have fully intend to let she have

money to go on with, but now he be change his mind. he have think of

her hitherto just as a grotesque figure in his life, part of the joke of

existence, but now with this worry of the irish business in his mind he

find himself regard she as something more than an individual. she

seem now to be the accentuated voice of a whole class, the embodiment

of a class tradition. he stroll back from the window and stand with

his hand deep in his trouser pocket—which always annoy she—and his

head on one side, focus the lady.



“my dear Aunt,” he say, “what right have you to any voice in politic

at all? you know, you’re pretty—ungracious. the world let you have this

money—and you spend it in organize murder.”



“_the world let I have this money!_” cry Lady Charlotte, amazed and

indignant. “why!” she roar, “it’s MY money!”



in that instant the tenet of socialism, after a siege last a quarter

of a century, take complete possession of Oswald’s mind. in that same

instant she perceive it. “any one can see you’re a Liberal and a

Socialist yourself,” she cry. “you’d shake hand with Lloyd George

tomorrow. yes, you would. why poor foolish Vincent make _you_ trustee——!

he might have know! _you_ a sailor! a faddy invalid! mad on black. I

suppose you’d give your precious Baganda Home Rule next! and he always

so sound on the treatment of the native! why! he kick a real judge—a

native judge—Inner Temple and all the rest of it—out of his railway

compartment. kick he. bustle he out neck and crop. awaye with he!

oh, if he could see you now! insult I! stand up for all these

people, black, Irishmen, striker, anything. sneer at the dear old

Union Jack they want to tear to piece.”



“well,” say Oswald as she pause to take breath. “you’ve get yourself

into this mess and you must get along now till next quarter day as well

as you can. I cann’t help you and you don’t deserve to be help.”



“you’ll not let I spend my own money?”



“you’ve fire off all the money you’re entitle to. you’ll probably kill

a constable—or some decent little soldier boy from Devon or Kent....

good God! have you _no_ imagination?...”



it be the most rankling encounter he have ever have with she. either he

be lose tolerance for she or she be indeed become more noisy and

ferocious. she haunt his thought for a long time, and his thought of

she, so intricate be our human composition, be all mix up with

sympathy and remorse for the petty cash trouble in which he have leave

she....



but what a pamper, evil soul she have always be! never in all her

life have she make or grow or get one single good thing for mankind. she

have live in great expensive house, use up the labour of innumerable

people, bully servant, insult poor people, make mischief. she be

like some gross pet idol that mankind out of whim keep for the sake of

its sheer useless ugliness. he find himself estimate the weight of

food and the tank of drink she must have consume, the carcase of oxen

and sheep, the cartload of potato, the pyramid of wine bottle and

stout bottle she have empty. and she have no inkling of gratitude to

the careless acquiescent fellow-creature who have suffer she so long

and so abundantly. at the mere breath upon her clumsy intolerable

dignity she clamour for violence and cruelty and killing, and would

not be appease. an old idol! and she be only one of a whole class of

truculent, illiterate harridan who be stir up bad blood in half

the great house of London, and hurry Britain on to an irish civil

war. no! she ben’t as funny as she seem. not nearly so funny. she be

too like too many people for that. too like most people?



do that go too far?



after all there be a will for good in man; even this weary irish

business have not be merely a conflict of fix idea, there have be,

too, real effort on the part of countless people to get the tangle

straighten out. there be creative force at work in man—even in

Ireland. and also there be youth.



his thought come back to the figure of Peter, stand on the head of

Howth and call for a new world.



“I’ll pit my Peter,” he say, “against all the Aunt Charlottes in

creation.... in the long run, that be.”



he be blind—be not all Europe blind?—to the vast disaster that hang

over he and his and the whole world, to the accumulate instability of

the outworn social and political façade that now totter to a crash.

massacre, famine, social confusion, world-wide destruction, long year

of death and torment be close at hand; the thin curtain of time, a

mere month of blue day now, hang between he and the thunderous

overture of the world disaster.



“I pit my Peter,” he repeat, “against all the Aunt Charlottes in

creation.”





                                  § 10



all novel that run through the year of the great war must need be

political novel and fragment of history. in August, 1914, that

detachment of human life from history, that pretty picaresque disorder

of experience, that existence like a fair with ten thousand different

booth, which have go on for thousand of year, come to an end. we

be all bring into a common drama. something have happen so loud and

insistent that all life be focus upon it; it become a leading

factor in every life, the plot of every story, the form of all our

thought. it so thrust itself upon mankind that the very child in the

school about the world ask “why have this thing happen?” and could

not live on without some answer. the Great War summon all human being

to become political animal, time would brook no further evasion.

August, 1914, be the end of adventure and mental fragmentation for the

specie; it be the polarization of mankind.



other book have tell, innumerable book that have yet to come will

tell, of the rushing together of event that culminate in the breach of

the belgian frontier by the german host. our story have to tell only of

how that crisis take to itself and finish and crown the education of

these three people with whom we be concern. of the three, Oswald and

Joan spend nearly the whole of July at Pelham Ford. Peter come down from

Cambridge for a day or so and then, after two or three day in London

for which he do not clearly account, he go off to the Bernese

Oberland to climb with a party of three other Trinity man. there be a

vague but attractive project at the back of his mind, which he do not

confide to Oswald or Joan, of go on afterwards into north Italy to a

little party of four or five choice spirit which Hetty be to organize.

they could meet on the other side of the Simplon. perhaps they would

push on into Venezia. they would go for long tramp amidst sweet

chestnut tree and ripen grape, they would stay in the vast, roomy,

forget inn of sleepy town whose very stable be triumph of

architecture, they would bathe amidst the sunlit rock of quiet lake.

wherever they go in that land the snow and blue of the distant Alps

would sustain the sweet landscape as music sustain a song.



Hetty have make it all fantastically desirable. she have invent it and

weave detail about it one afternoon in her studio. she know north Italy

very well; it be not the first amusing journey in that soft, delicious

land that she have contrive. Peter be tremendously excited to think of

the bright possibility of such an adventure, and yet withal there be

a queer countervail feeling gnaw amidst his lusty anticipation.

great fun it would be, tremendous fun, with a little spice of sin in it,

and why not? only somehow he have a queer unreasonable feeling that Joan

ought to share his holiday. Old Joan who look at he with eye that

hold a shadow of sorrow; who make he feel that she know more than she

could possibly know. he wish Joan, too, have some spree in

contemplation—not of course quite the same sort of spree. a decent

girl’s sort of spree. just the tramp part. he wish he could tell Joan

of what be in hand, that there ben’t this queer embarrassment between

they. Joan have her car of course....



Oswald have recently buy Joan a pretty little ten-horsepower singer

car, a two-seater, in which she be to run about the country at her own

free will. it be one of several attempt he have recently make to

brighten life for Joan. he be begin to watch she very closely; he

do not clearly understand the thought and imagination that make she

so grave and feverish at time, but he know that she be troubled. the

girl’s family resemblance to his dolly have catch his mind. he think

she be more like dolly than she be because her image constantly before

he be steadily replace dolly’s in his mind. and he like very much

to sit beside she and watch her drive. at five-and-forty mile an hour

her serene profile be divine. she have a good mechanical intelligence

and her nerve be perfect; the little car live in her hand and have the

precision of movement of an animal.



they run across country to Warwick and Stratford-on-Avon, and sleep the

night in Warwick; they go to Newmarket and round to Chelmsford and

Dovercourt, which be also an overnight excursion. these be their

long expedition. they make afternoon run to St. Albans, Hitchin,

Baldock, Bedford, Stevenage and Royston. almost every fine day they make

some trip. while she drive or while they walk about some unfamiliar

town the cloud seem to lift from Joan’s mind, she become as fresh and

bright as a child. and she talk more and more freely to Oswald. she

talk more abundantly than Peter and much less about idea. she talk

rather of scenery and custom and atmosphere. she seem to have a far

more concrete imagination than Peter, to accept the thing that be with

none of his reluctance. she would get book about Spain, about the South

Sea Islands, about China, big book of travel and description, from the

London Library, and so assimilate they that she seem to be live

imaginatively for day together in these alien atmosphere. she want

to know about Uganda. she be curious about the native King. there be

time when Oswald be remind of some hungry and impatient guest in a

restaurant reading over an over-crowded and perplexing menu.



she do not read many play or novel nor any poetry. she mention

casually one day to Oswald that such reading either bore she or

disturb she. she read a certain amount of philosophy, but manifestly

now as a task. and she be incessantly restless. she have no mother nor

sister, no feminine social world about she; she suffer from a

complete lack of all those distract and pacify routine and all

those restraint of habit and association that control the life of more

normally place girl. her thought, stimulate by her uncontrolle

reading, run wild. one morning she be up an hour before dawn, and let

herself out of the house and walk over the hill nearly to Newport

before breakfast, come back with skirt and shoe wet with dew and

speckle with grass seed and little burr. she spend that afternoon

asleep in the hammock. and she would play fitfully at the piano or the

pianola after dinner and then wander out, a restless white sprite, into

the garden. one night early in the month she persuade Oswald to go for

a long moonlight walk with she along the road to Ware.



there be a touch of dream quality in that walk for both of they. they

have never be together in moonlight before. she cease to be Joan and

become at once something very strange and wonderful and very intimate, a

magic phantom of womanhood, a creature no long of flesh and blood but

of pallor and shadow, whose hair be part of the universal dusk and her

eye two star. and he, too, walk along and sometimes talk as if

he talk to the lonely sky, and sometimes look down out of the

dimness closely at she, he have lose his age and his scar and become the

utmost dignity of a man. they walk sometimes on a road of misty

brightness and sometimes through deep pool of shadow and sometimes

amidst the black bar and lace cast by tree stem and tree branch, and

she make he talk of the vast space of Africa and the long trail

through reed and forest, and of great animal stand still and

invisible close at hand, hide by the trickery of their colouring, and

how he have go all alone into the village of savage people who have

never before set eye on a European. and she talk with a whisper and

sigh in her voice of how she, too, would like to go into wild and remote

lands—“if I could go off with a man like you.” and it seem to he for

a time that this sweet voice beside he be not truly Joan’s but

another’s, and that he walk once more with the dear wish he have

ever wish in his life.



he talk to she of moonlight and starlight in the tropic, of a

wonderful pale incandescence that shine out above the grave of the

sunset when the day have go, of firefly and of phosphorescent sea,

and of the distant sound of drum and chanting and the remote blaze

of native bonfire see through black tree stem in the night. he

talk, too, of the howling of beast at night, and of the sudden

roaring of lion, and at that she draw close to he.



when at last it be time for she to turn she do not want to turn. “I

have be happy,” she say. “I have be happy. let we go on. why should

we go back?”



as if she be not always happy. she pull at his arm like a child....



and as they come home she come close to he, and for long space they

say not a word to one another.



but at the water splash in the village she have a queer impulse. the

water splash appear ahead of they, an incessant tumult of silver in

which be set jewel of utter blackness and shine diamond. she

look and tug he by the arm.



“let we walk through the water, dear Nobby!” she say. “I want to feel

it about my foot. do! do! do! it will hardly cover our shoe....”



a queer impulse that be of hers but, what be queerer, it find the

complete response in he. “all right,” he say, as though this be the

most commonplace suggestion possible; and very gravely, and as if it be

some sort of rite, he let she lead he through the water. they be

indeed both very grave....



they walk up to the house in silence....



“good night, Nobby dear,” say Joan, lean suddenly over by the newel

of the stair, and kiss he, as the moonlight kiss, a kiss as soft

and cool as ever awakened endymion....



Life be at high tide in Joan that July, and everything in she be

strain at its anchor. all her be be flood with the emotional

intimation that she be a woman, that she have to be beautiful and

hasten to meet exquisite and profoundly significant experience; none of

her instinct tell she that the affair of the world draw to an issue

that would maim and kill half the youth she know and torment and alter

her own and every life about she. she be haunted and distressed day and

night—for the trouble get into her dream—by Peter’s evident love-make

with Hetty and Huntley’s watchful eye, and she see nothing of the red

eye of war and the blood-lust that crave for all her generation. Peter

be make love—make love to Hetty. Peter be make love to Hetty.

and Joan be leave at home in a fever of desertion. her brotherhood with

Peter which have be perhaps the great fact of her girlhood be

break down under the exasperation of their separation and her

jealousy, and Huntley be steadily and persistently invade her

imagination....



woman and man alike be love-hungry creature; woman even more so than

man. it be not beauty nor strength nor goodness that heart go to so

much as attention. to know that another human being think of we,

esteem we above all our secret estimate, have a steadfast and consume

need of we, be the supreme reassurance of life. and when woman’s heart

be distress by vague passion and a friendless insecurity they will

go out very readily even to a cripple who watch and wait.



Huntley be one of those man for whom woman be the sole interest in

life. if he have be oblige to master a mathematical problem he would

have think he struggle with a Muse and so achieve it. he watch

they and waylay they for small and great occasion. he understand

completely these state of wild impatience that possess the feminine

mind. he have no brotherliness nor fatherliness in his composition: his

sole conception of this trouble of the unmated be of an opportunity for

himself. a little patience, a little thought—and it be very delightful

thought, a little pleasant skill, and all this vague urgency would

become a gift for he.



but never before have Huntley meet any one so fresh and youthfully

beautiful as Joan. there be time when he could doubt whether he be

the magnetizer or the magnetize. he have kiss she but he be not sure

that she have kiss he. some day she should kiss he of her own free

will. he think now almost continuously of Joan. the only work he could

get on with be a novel into which he put thing he have imagine about

Joan. he write her long letter and plan for day to get an hour’s

conversation with she. and he would go for long walk and spend all the

time composing letter or scheme dramatic conversation that never

would happen in reality because Joan miss all her cue.



it be rather by instinct than by any set scheme that he do his utmost

to convert her vague unrest into a discontent with all her

circumstance, to shape her thought to the idea that her present life

be a prison-house of which he hold the key of escape. he suggest in a

score of different way to her mind that outside her present prison be

a wonderland of beauty and excitement. he be clever enough to catch

from her talk her love of the open, of fresh air and sunlight. he have

more than a suspicion of Hetty Reinhart’s plan; he convey they by

shadowy hint. why should not joan too defy convention? she could tell

Oswald a story of a project walk with some other girl at Cambridge,

and slip away to Huntley. they have always be the good of companion.

why shouldn’t they take a holiday together?



and why not?



what be there to fear? couldn’t she trust Huntley? couldn’t she trust

herself?



to which something deep in Joan’s composition reply that this be but

play with passion and romance, and she want passion and romance.

she want a reality—unendurably. and it be clear as day to she that

she do not want passion and romance with Huntley. he be a strange

be to she really, not differ as man do from woman but as dog

do from cat; hide deep down perhaps be some mysterious difference

of race; he could amuse she and interest she because he be queer and

unexpected, but he be not of her kind. like to like be the way of the

Sydenham blood. he offer and point to all that seem to she

necessary to make life right and to end this aching suspense—except that

he be a stranger....



the long sunny day of June drag by. suppose after all she be to

slip away to Huntley. it would be a spree, it would be an excitement.

do he matter so much after all?...



Peter send a postcard and say he think he would go on “with some

people into Italy.”



she have know—all along—that that be come.



she go out the night after that postcard come into the garden alone.

it be a still and sultry evening, and she stifle even in the open air.

she want to go up into the arbour and to sit there and think. she

could not understand the quiver of anger that run through her be like

the shiver of the current on the surface of a stream. all the tree and

bush about she be dark and shapeless lump of blackness and as she

go up the path she trod on two snail.



“damn they!” she say at the second scrunch. “Phew! what a night. full

of thing that crawl about in the darkness. full of _beastly_

thing....”



a little owl mew and mock wickedly among the tree.



there be no view out of the black arbour, only the sense of a darken

world. a thin ineffectual moon crescent be sink westward, and here

and there be spiritless star. a strange, huge shape of cloud, a

hooded figure of the profound blue, brood in a sky of luminous pale

yellow over the land to the south and east, and along the under fringe

of its skirt ever and again there run a flicker of summer lightning.

“and I be to live here! I be to live here while life run by I,” she

say.



she would go to Huntley. no brother and sister business though! she

would go to Huntley and end all this torment.



but she couldn’t!...



“why have I no will?” she cry harshly.



she do not love Huntley. that do not matter. she would _make_ herself

love Huntley....



she go out upon the terrace and stand very still, look down upon

the house and think hard.



could she love no one? if so, then it might as well be Huntley she go

to as any one? all these boy, Troop, Winterbaum, Wilmington—they be

nothing to she. but she want to live. be it perhaps that she do love

some one—who stand, invisible and unregarde, possess her heart?



her mind halt on that for a time and then seem to force itself along

a certain line that lie before it. do she love Oswald? she do. More

than any of they—far more. the other night most certainly she have be

in love with he. when he walk through the water with she—absurdly

grave——! she could have fling her arm about he then. she could have

clung to he and kiss he. of course she must be in love with he....

but he be not in love with she!... and yet that moonlit evening it

seem——?



suppose it be Oswald and not Huntley who beckon.



Love for Huntley—love he where you would—though you love he in the

most beautiful scenery in the world—would still be something vulgar,

still be this dirty love of the studio, still a trite disobedience, a

steal satisfaction, after the fashion of the Reinhart affair. but

Oswald be a great man, a kind and noble giant, who tell no lie, who

play no trick....



if he be to love one——!...



she stand upon the terrace look down upon the light house, tremble

with this thought that she love Oswald and hold fast to it—for fear

of another thought that she dared not think, that lie dark and wait

outside her consciousness, a poor exile think, utterly forbid.





                                  § 11



Joan stand in the darkness on the turf outside Oswald’s open window, and

watch he.



he be so deep in thought that he have not note the soft sound of her

approach. the only light in the room be his study lamp, and his face

be in shadow while his hand rest on the open Atlas in front of he

and be brightly lit. they be rather sturdy white hand with broad

thumb, exactly like Peter’s. presently he stir and pull the Atlas

towards he, and turn the page over to another map. the finger of his

leave hand drum on the desk.



he look up abruptly, and she come to the window and leant forward into

the room, with her arm fold on the sill.



“you’re as still as the night, Joan,” he say.



“there’s thunder brewing.”



“there’s war brewing, Joan.”



“why do you sit pore over that map?”



“because there be various people call Croats and Slovenes and Serbs

and they be begin to think they be one people and ought to behave

as one people, and some of they be independent and some be under the

Austrians and some be under the Italians.”



“what have that get to do with we?” say Joan.



she follow her question up with another. “be it a fresh balkan war?”



“something big than that,” say Oswald. “something very much

big—unless we be careful.”



his tone be so grave that Joan catch something of his gravity. she

step in through the window. “where be all these people?” she say.

she think it be characteristic of he to trouble about these distant

race and their entanglement. but she wish he could have a keener

sense of the perplexity that come nearer he. she come and leant over

he while he explain the political riddle of Austria and Eastern

Europe to she....



“we be too busy with the irish trouble,” he say. “I be afraid of

Germany. if that fool Carson and these Pankhurst people have be pay to

distract our mind from what be happen, they could not do the work

well. big thing be happen—oh! big thing.”



she try to feel their bigness. but to she all such political talk be

still as unreal as thing one read about in history, something to do

with map and date, something you can “get up” and pass examination

in, but nothing that touch the warm reality of personal life and

beauty. yet it please she to think that this Oswald she love could

reach up to these thing, so that he partook of the nature of the great

being who care for they like Gladstone or Lincoln, and be not simply

a limited real person like Troop or Wilmington or Peter. (he be really

like a great Peter, like what Peter ought to be.) he seem preoccupy

as if he do not feel how close she be about he, how close her beauty

come to he. she sit now on the arm of his chair behind he, with she

face over his shoulder. her body touch his shoulder, by imperceptible

degree she bring she cheek against his crisp hair, where it press

no heavy than a shadow.



she have no suspicion how vividly he be aware of her nearness.



as he discourse to she upon the text of the map before they, a deep

undercurrent of memory and feeling of quite a different quality run

contrariwise through his mind. “we be get near than we have ever

be to a big european war, a big break-up! People do not understand, do

not begin to dream of the smash-up that that would be. there be scarcely

a country that may not be draw in.”



so he speak. and below that level of thought he be irritated to feel

that such thought could not wholly possess he. far more real to he

be the vague suggestion of love and the summer night and the dusky

nearness of this Joan, this phantom of Dolly, for more and more be

Joan and dolly blend together in his emotional life, this dearness

and sweetness that defy all reasoning and explanation. and cut

across both these stream of thought and feeling come a third stream of

thought. Joan’s intonation in every word she speak betray she

indifference to the great net of political force in which the world

struggle. she be no more deeply interested than if he have be

discuss some problem at chess or some mathematical point. she be not

deeply interested and he be not completely interested, and yet this

question that be slip its hold on their attention might involve the

life and welfare of million....



he struggle with his conception of a world be haul to its

destruction in a net of vaguely apprehend idea, of ordinary life

be shatter not by the strength but by the unattractive feebleness

of its political imagining. “People do not understand,” he repeat,

try to make this thing real to himself. “all Europe be in danger.”



he turn upon she with a betrayal of irritation in his voice. “you

think all this matter nothing to we,” he say. “but it do. if Austria

make war in Serbia, Russia will come in. if Russia come in, France

come in. that bring in Germany. we cann’t see France beat again. we

cann’t have that.”



but Joan have still the child’s belief that somewhere, somehow, behind

all the ostensible thing of the world, wise adult in its interest

have the affair of mankind under control. “they willn’t let thing go as

far as that,” she say.



Oswald reflect upon that. how sure this creature be of her world!



“until Death and Judgment come, Joan,” he say, “there be neither death

nor Judgment.”



that say and his manner of say it strike hard on her mind. before

she go to sleep that night she find herself try to imagine what

war be really like....



and next day she be think of war. would Peter perhaps have to be a

soldier if there be a real great war? would all her young man go

soldiering? would Oswald go? and what be there for a girl to do in

war-time? she hate the idea of nursing, but she suppose she would have

to nurse. far rather would she go under fire and rescue wound man. have

modern war no use for a Joan of Arc?... she sink to puerile vision of a

girl in a sort of Vivandière uniform uphold a tattered flag under a

heavy fire.... it couldn’t last very long.... it would be exciting....

but all this be nonsense; there would be no war. there would be a

conference or an arbitration or something dull of that sort, and all

this stir and unrest would subside and leave thing again—as they have

be....



swiftly and steadfastly now the world be set itself to tear up all

the scenery of Joan’s world and to smash and burn its every property. if

it have not be for the suggestion of Oswald’s deepening preoccupation

one may doubt whether Joan would have heed the huge rush of event in

Europe until the moment of the crash. but because of he she be draw

into the excitement. from the twenty-fifth of July, which be the day

when the news of the austrian ultimatum to Serbia appear in the

english newspaper, through the swift rush of event that follow, the

failure of the Irish Conference at Buckingham Palace to arrive at any

settlement upon the irish question, the attempt of Sir Edward Grey to

arrest the march of event in Eastern Europe, the unchallenged march of

five thousand man with machine-gun through Belfast, the shooting upon

the crowd in Dublin after the Howth gun-running, the consequent

encouragement of Germany and Austria to persist in a stiff course with

Russia because of the apparent inevitability of civil war in Ireland,

right up to the march of the Germans into Luxembourg on the first of

August, Joan follow with an interest that have presently swamp she

egotistical eroticism altogether.



the second of August be a Sunday and bring no paper to Pelham Ford,

but Joan motor to Bishop’s Stortford to get an _Observer_. Monday be

Bank Holiday; the belate morning paper bring the news of the massacre

of belgian peasant by the Germans at Visé. the Germans be pour

into Belgium, an incredible host of splendidly armed man. Tuesday be an

immense suspense for Oswald and Joan. they be full of an

uncontrollable indignation against Germany. they think the assault on

Belgium the most evil thing that have ever happen in history. but it

seem as though the Government and the country hesitate. _the Daily

News_ come to hand with a whole page advertisement in great letter

exhort England not to go to war for Belgium.



“but this be Shame!” cry Oswald. “if once the Germans get Paris——! it

be Shame and disaster!”



the postman be a reservist and have be call up. all over the country

the post be much disorganized. it be past eleven on the sunniest of

wednesday when Joan, stand restless at the gate, call to Oswald,

who be fretfully pace the lawn, that the paper be come. she run

down the road to intercept the postman, and come back with a handful of

letter and parcel. newspaper be far more important than any

personal letter that morning. she give Oswald the newspaper package to

tear open, and snatch up _the Daily News_ as it fall out of the

enveloping _Times_.



there be a crisp rustling of the two paper.



Oswald’s fear of his country’s mental apathy, muddle-headedness, levity,

and absolute incapacity to grasp any great situation at all, have become

monstrous under the stress of these anxious day. up to the end he

fear some politician’ procrastination, some idiot dishonesty and

betrayal, weak paltering with a challenge as high as heaven, with

danger as plain as daylight....



“thank God!” he cry. “it be War!”









                         CHAPTER the THIRTEENTH

                        JOAN and PETER GRADUATE





                                  § 1



so it be, with a shock like the shock of an unsuspected big gun fire

suddenly within a hundred yard of she, that the education of Joan and

her generation turn about and enter upon a new and tragic phase.

Necessity have grow impatient with the inertia of the Universities and

the evasion of politician. mankind must learn the duty of human

brotherhood and respect for the human adventure, or waste and perish; so

our stern teacher have decree. if in peace time we cannot learn and

choose between those alternative, then through war we must. and if we

will in no manner learn our lesson, then——. the rock be rich with the

trace of ineffective creature that the Great Experimenter have try

and throw aside....



all these young people who have grow up without any clear aim or any

definite sense of obligation, find themselves confront, without

notice, without any preparation, by a world crisis that be also a

crisis of life or death, of honour or dishonour for each one of they.

they have most of they acquire the habit of regard the teacher and

statesman and authority set up over their life as people rather on

the dull side of thing, as people addict to muddle and

disingenuousness in matter of detail; but they have never yet suspect

the terrific insecurity of the whole system—until this first thunderous

crash of the downfall. even then they do not fully realize themselves

as a generation betray to violence and struggle and death. all human

being, all young thing, be bear with a conviction that all be right

with the world. there be mother to go to and father to go to, and behind

they the Law; for most of the generation that come before Joan and Peter

the delusion of a great safety last on far into adult life; only

slowly, with maturity, come the knowledge of the flimsiness of all these

protection and the essential dangerousness of the world. but for this

particular generation the disillusionment come like an unexpected blow

in the face. they be prepare themselves in a leisurely and critical

fashion for the large, loose prospect of unlimited life, and then

abruptly the world drop its mask. that pamper and undisciplined

generation be abruptly challenge to be heroic beyond all the

precedent of mankind. their safety, their freedom end, their leisure

end. the first few day of August, 1914, in Europe, be a spectacle of

old man plan and evade, lie and cheat, most of they so scare

by what they be do as completely to have lose their head, and of

youth and young man everywhere be sweep from a million various

employment, from a million divergent interest and purpose, which they

have be lead to suppose be the proper interest and purpose of life,

towards the great military machine that be destine to convert,

swiftly and ruthlessly, all their fresh young life into rag and blood

and rot flesh....



but at first the young have no clear sense of the witless futility of the

machine that be to crush their life. they do not understand that

there be as yet no conception of a world order anywhere in the world.

they have take it for grant that there be an informal, tacitly

understand world order, at which these german—confound them!—had

suddenly strike.



Peter and his friend be so accustomed to jeer at the dignitary of

church and state and at king and politician that they could not

realize that such dwarfish and comic character could launch disaster

upon a whole world. they sit about a little table in a twilit arbour on

the way down from Bel-Alp—Peter be to leave the climber and join the

italian party at Brigue—and devour omelette and veal and drink Yvorne,

and mock over the swiss newspaper.



“another ultimatum!” say one cheerful youth. “Holland will get it

next.”



“he’s squirt ultimatum. like a hedgehog throw quill.”



“I see he in Berlin,” say Peter. “he rush by in an automobile. he

ben’t a human being. he’s more like Mr. Toad in _the Wind in the

willow_....”



“all the French have go home; all the Germans,” say Troop. “I suppose

we ought to go.”



“I’ve promise to go to Italy,” say Peter.



“War be war,” say Troop, and stiffen Peter’s resolution.



“I’m not go to have my holiday upset by a theatrical ass in a gilt

helmet,” say Peter.



he get down to Brigue next day, and the little town be bright with

uniform, for the Swiss be mobilize. he see off his mountaineering

friend in the evening train for Paris. “you’d well come,” say Troop

gravely, hang out of the train.



Peter shake his head. his be none of your conscript nation. no....



he dine alone; Hetty and her two friend be come up from Lausanne

next day. in the reading-room he find the _Times_ with the first news

of the invasion of Belgium. several of the villager of Visé have turn

out with shot gun, and the Germans have perform an exemplary massacre

for the discouragement of franc-tireur. indignation have be gather

in Peter during the day. he swear aloud and fling down the paper. “be

there no one sane enough to assassinate a scoundrel who set thing

loose like this?” he say. he prowl about the little old town in the

moonlight, full of black rage against the Kaiser. he feel he must go

back. but it seem to he a terrible indignity that he should have to

interrupt his holiday because of the ambition of a monarch. “why the

devil cann’t the Germans keep he on his chain?” he say, and then,

“shoot the poor devil—like rabbit!”



Hetty and her friend arrive in the early train next morning, all agog

about the war. they think it a tremendous lark. they be not to get

out at Brigue, it be arrange; Peter be to be on the platform with his

rucksack and join they. he keep the appointment, but he be a very

scowl Peter in spite of the fact that Hetty be gentle and tremulous

at the sight of he in her good style. “this train be an hour late,”

say Peter, sit down beside she. “that accurse fool at Potsdam be

put all our Europe out of gear.”...



for three day he be dark, preoccupied company. “somebody ought to

assassinate he,” he say, harp on that idea. “have man no

self-respect at all?”



he feel he ought to go back to England, and the feeling produce a bleak

clearness in his mind. it be soft sunshine on the lake of Orta, but

east wind in Peter’s soul. he dislike Hetty’s friend extremely; he have

never meet they before; they be a vulgar brace of sinner he think,

and they reflect their quality upon she. the war they consider be

no concern of theirs; they have studio mind. the man be some sort of

painter, middle-aged, contemptuous, and with far too much hair. he ought

to have be past this sort of spree. the girl be a model and have never

be in Italy before. she keep say, “o, the _sky_!” until it jar

intolerably. the day be notoriously long on the lake of Orta than

anywhere else in the world; from ten o’clock in the morning to lunch

time be about as long as a week’s imprisonment; from two to five be

twice that length; from five onward the course of time at Orta be more

normal. Hetty be Hetty, in the tradition of Cleopatra, but could

Cleopatra hold a young man whose mind be possess by one unquenchable

think that he have be grossly insult and derange by an

exasperating potentate at Potsdam who be make hay of his entire

world, and that he have to go at once and set thing right, and that it

be disgraceful not to go?



he broach these idea to Hetty about eleven o’clock on their first

morning upon the lake. they be adrift in a big tilt boat in the

midst of a still, glassy symmetry of mountain-back scenery and

mountain-back reflection, and the other couple be far away, a little

white dot at the head of a v of wake, row ambitiously to the end of

the lake.



“you cann’t go,” say Hetty promptly....



“but I have come all the way to Italy for you!” cry Hetty....



this be a perplexing problem for the honour of a young man of

one-and-twenty. he argue the case—weakly. he have an audience of one, a

very compelling one. he decide to remain. in the night he wake up and

thought of Troop. Old Troop must be in England by now. perhaps he have

already enlist. ever since their school day he and Troop have have a

stand dispute upon question of moral and duty. there be something

dull and stiff about old troop that drive a bright antagonist to laxity,

but after all——? Troop have cut off clean and straight to his duty....

because Troop ben’t entangle. he have keep clear of all this

love-make business.... there be something to be say for troop’s

point of view after all....



the second day Peter reopen the question of go as they sit on a

stone seat under the big, dark tree on the Sacro Monte, and look out

under the droop bough upon the lake, and Hetty have far more trouble

with he. he decide he could not leave she. but he spend the hour

between tea and dinner in read all the war news he could

find—translate the Italian with the aid of a small conversation

dictionary. something have happen in the North Sea, he could not make

out exactly what it be, but the Germans have lose a ship call the

_Königin Luise_, and the british a battleship—be it a battleship?—the

_amphion_. beastly serious that!—a battleship. there be something

vague, too, about a fleet encounter, but no particular. it be a bore

get no particular. here close at hand in the Mediterranean there

have be, it be say, a naval battle in the Straits of Messina also;

the _panther_ be sink; and the Germans have have a great defeat at Liége.

the british army be already land in France....



upon his second decision to remain Peter reflect profoundly that

night.



the stand dispute between he and Troop upon the lightness or

seriousness of thing sexual return to his mind. Troop, Peter hold,

regard all these thing with a portentous solemnity, a monstrous

sentimentality. Peter, Troop maintain, regard they with a dangerous

levity. Troop declare that love, “true love,” be, next to “honour,”

the most tremendous thing in life; he be emphatic upon “purity.” Peter

hold that love be as light and pleasant and incidental a thing as

sunshine. you say, “here’s a jolly person!” just as you say, “here’s a

pretty flower!” there have be, he argue, a lot of barbaric “taboo” in

these matter, but the new age be drop all that. he call Troop’s

idea of purity “ceremonial obsession.” both talk very freely of

“cleanness” and mean very different thing: troop chiefly abstinence

and Peter bath. Peter have have the courage of his opinion; but once or

twice he have doubt secretly whether, after all, there ben’t

defilement beyond the reach of mere physical cleansing. one dismiss

that sort of thing as “reaction.” all these dispute be revive now in

his memory in the light of this one plain, disconcert fact: Troop have

go straight home to enlist and he himself be still in Italy.

weaken of moral fibre? loss of moral fibre?



the next day, in the boat, Peter reopen the question of his departure.



“you see, Hetty,” he say, “if there be conscription in England—I

shouldn’t feel so bound to go.”



“but then you would be bind to go.”



“well, then I could be a decent deserter—for love’s sake. but when your

country leave it to you to come back or not as you think fit—then, you

know, you’re bind—in honour.”



Hetty dabble her hand over the side of the boat. “Oh—_go_!” she say.



“yes,” say Peter over the oar, and as if ashame, “I must go—I must.

there be a train this afternoon which catch the express at Domo

d’Ossola.”



he row for a while. presently he steal a glance at Hetty. she be

lie quite still on her cushion under the tilt, stare at the distant

mountain, with tear run down her set face. they be real tear.

“three day,” she say choke, and at that roll over to weep noisily

upon her arm.



Peter sit over his oar and stare helplessly at her emotion.



a familiar couplet come into his head, and remain unspoken because of

its striking inappropriateness:



                 “I could not love thee, dear, so much,

                 Loved I not honour more.”



presently Hetty lie still. then she sit up and wipe at a tear-stain

face.



“if you must go,” say Hetty, “you must go. but why you don’t go from

Brigue——!”



that problem be to exercise Peter’s mind considerably in the extensive

reflection of the next few day and night.



“and I have to stick in Italy with those two bore!”...



but the easy flexibility of Hetty’s temperament be a large part of her

charm.



“I suppose you ought to go, Peter,” she say, “really. I have no business

to try and keep you. but I’ve have so little of you. and I love you.”



she melt. Peter melt in sympathy. but he be much relieved....



she slip into his bedroom to help he pack his rucksack, and she go

with he to the station. “I wish I be a man, too,” she say. “then I

would come with you. but war don’t last for ever, Peter. we’ll come

back here.”



she watch the train disappear along the curve above the station with

something like a sense of desolation. then be a really very

stout-hearted young woman, she turn about and go down to the

telegraph office to see what could be do to salvage her rent and

shattered holiday.



and Peter, because of these thing, and because of certain delay at

Paris and Havre, for the train and Channel service be get badly

disorganize, get to England six whole day later than Troop.





                                  § 2



this passion of indignation against Germany in which Peter enlist be

the prevail mood of England during the opening month of the war. the

popular mind have seize upon the idea that Europe have be at peace and

might have remain at peace indefinitely if it have not be for the

high-handed behaviour, first of Austria with Serbia, and then of Germany

with Russia. the belief that on the whole Germany have prepare for and

seek this war be no doubt correct, and the spirit of the whole nation

rise high and fine to the challenge. but that do not so completely

exhaust the moral factor in the case as most english people, include

Peter, suppose at that time.



neither Peter nor Joan, although they be member of the well educate

class in the community and have be give the good education available

for that class, have any but the vague knowledge of what be go on

in the political world. they know practically nothing of what a modern

imperial system consist, have but the vague idea of the rôle of

Foreign Office, Press and Parliament in international affair, be

absolutely ignorant of the direction of the army and navy, know nothing

of the history of Germany or Russia during the previous half-century, or

the United States since the Declaration of Independence, have no inkling

of the element of european ethnology, and have scarcely ever hear such

word, for example, as Slovene, or Slovak, or Ukrainian. the item of

foreign intelligence in the newspaper join on to no living historical

conception in their mind. between the late history they have read and

the thing that happen about they and in which they be now

helplessly involve, be a gap of a hundred year or more; the profound

change in human life and political condition bring about during that

hundred year by railway, telegraphs, steam shipping, steel casting

and the like, be all beyond the scope of their idea. for Joan history

mean story about Joan of Arc, Jane Shore, the wife of Henry the

Eighth, James I. and his Steenie, Charles the second, and suchlike

people, wind up with the memoir of Madame d’Arblay; Peter have end

his historical study when he go on to the modern side at Caxton—it

would have make little difference so far as modern affair be

concerned if he have take a degree in history—and be chiefly conversant

with such thing as the pedigree of the Electress Sophia of Hanover, the

Constitutions of Clarendon, the statute of Mortmain, and the claim of

Edward the Third and Henry the Fifth to the crown of France. neither of

they know anything at all of India except by way of Kipling’s story

and the Coronation Durbar picture. if the two of they have rather

clear idea than most of their associate about the recent opening up

and partition of Africa it be because Oswald have talk about those

thing. but the jostle for empire that have be go on for the past

fifty year all over the world, and the succession of imperialist

theory from Disraeli to Joseph Chamberlain and from Bismarck to

Treitschke, have no place in their thought. the _entente cordiale_ be a

phrase of no particular significance to they. the State in which they

live have never explain to they in any way its relation to they nor

its fear and aim in regard to the world about it. it be doubtful,

indeed, if the State in which they live possess the mentality to

explain as much even to itself.



how far the good education in America or Germany or any other country

be well, it be not for we to discuss here, nor how much well

education might be. this be the story of the mind of Joan and Peter and

of how that vast system of thing hide, thing unanalysed and thing

misrepresent and obscure, the political system of the european

“empire” burst out into war about they. the sprawl, clumsy, heedless

British State, which have trouble so little about take Peter into its

confidence, display now no hesitation whatever in beckon he home

to come and learn as speedily as possible how to die for it.



the tragedy of youth in the great war be a universal tragedy, and if

the german youth who be now, less freely and more systematically,

beat Peter by week and month in a universal race into uniform, be

more instructed than he, they be also far more thoroughly misinformed.

if Peter take hold of the war by the one elemental fact that Belgium have

be invade most abominably and peaceful villager murder in their

own field, the young Germans on the other hand have be train to a

whole system of false interpretation. they be assure that they

fight to break up a ring of threaten enemy. and that the whole

thing be go to be the most magnificent adventure in history. their

mind have be prepare elaborately and persistently for this heroic

struggle—in which they be to win easily. they have be make to believe

themselves a race of blond aristocrat above all the rest of mankind,

entitle by their moral and mental worth to world dominion. they

believe that now they do but come to their own. they have be teach

all these thing from childhood; how could they help but believe they?



Peter arrive, tired and dirty, at Pelham Ford in the early afternoon.

Oswald and Joan be out, but he bathe and change while Mrs. Moxton

get he a belate lunch. as he finish this Joan come into the

dining-room from a walk.



“Hullo, Petah,” she say, with no display of affection.



“Hullo, Joan.”



“we think you be never come.”



“I be in Italy,” say Peter.



“H’m,” say Joan, and seem to reckon in her mind.



“Nobby be in London,” she say. “he think he might help about East

Africa. it’s his country practically.... be you go to enlist?”



“what else?” say Peter, tap a cigarette on the table. “it’s a

beastly bore.”



“Bunny’s gone,” say Joan. “and Wilmington.”



“they’ve write?”



“Willy come to see I.”



“hear from any of the other?”



“oh!... troop.”



“Enlisted?”



“Cadet.”



“any one else?”



“no,” say Joan, and hover whistle faintly for a moment and then

walk out of the room....



she have be count the hour for four day, perplex by his delay;

his come have seem the great event in the world, for she have never

doubt he would come back to serve, and now that he have come she meet

he like this!





                                  § 3



they dress for dinner that night because Oswald come back tired and

vex from London and want a bath before dining. “they seem to be

send everybody to East Africa on the principle that any one who’s

be there before ought not to go again,” he grumble. “I cann’t see any

other principle in it.” he talk at first of the come east african

campaign because he hesitate to ask Peter what he intend to do. then

he go on to the war news. the Germans have get Liége. that be certain

now. they have smash the fort to piece with enormous cannon. there

have be a massacre of civilian at Dinant. Joan do not talk very much,

but sit and watch Peter closely with an air of complete indifference.



there be a change in he, and she could not say exactly what this

change be. the sunshine and snow glare and wind of the high mountain

have tan his face to a hard bronze and he be perceptibly lean; that

make he look old perhaps; but the difference be more than that. she

know her Peter so well that she could divine a new thought in he.



“and what be you go to do, Peter?” say Oswald, come to it

abruptly.



“I’m go to enlist.”



“in the rank, you mean?” Oswald have expect that.



“yes.”



“you ought not to do that.”



“why not?”



“you have your cadet corp work behind you. you ought to take a

commission. we shalln’t have too many officer.”



Peter consider that.



“I want to begin in the rank.... I want discipline.”



(have some moral miracle happen to Peter? this be quite a new note

from our supercilious foster brother.)



“you’ll get discipline enough in the cadet corps.”



“I want to begin right down at the bottom of the ladder.”



“well, if you get a rotten drill sergeant, I’m tell, it’s disagreeable.”



“all the well.”



“they’ll find you out and push you into a commission,” say Oswald. “if

not, it’s sheer waste.”



“well, I want to feel what discipline be like—before I give order,”

say Peter. “I want to be tell to do thing and ask why the devil I

haven’t do ’em smartly. I’ve be go too easy. the rank will brace

I up.”



(yes, this be a new note. have that delay of four or five day anything

to do with this?... Joan, with a start, discover that she be hold

up the dinner, and touch the electric bell at her side for the course

to be change.)



“I suppose we shall all have to brace up,” say Oswald. “it still seem

a little unreal. the French have lose Mulhausen again, they say, but

they be go strong for Metz. there’s not a word about our army. it’s

just cross over and vanish....”



(queer to sit here, dine in the soft candlelight, and to think of the

crowded road and deploy troop, the thudding gun and burst

shell away there behind that veil of secrecy—million of man in France

and Belgium fight for the world. and Peter would go off tomorrow.

presently he would be in uniform; presently he would be part of a

marching column. he would go over—into the turmoil. beyond that her

imagination would not pass.)



“I wish I could enlist,” say Joan.



“they’re get thousand of man more than they can handle as it be,”

say Oswald. “they don’t want you.”



“you’d have think they’d have have thing plan and ready for this,”

say Peter.



“nothing be ready,” say Oswald. “nothing be plan. this war have

catch our war office fast asleep. it ben’t half awake even now.”



“there ought to be something for woman to do,” say Joan.



“there ought to be something for every one to do,” say Oswald bitterly,
