“but there ben’t. this country ben’t a state; it’s a crowd adrift. do

you notice, Peter, as you come through London, the endless multitude of

people just stand about? I’ve never see London like that before.

People not walk about their business, but just stand.”...



Peter tell of thing he have see on his way home. “the French be in a

scowling state. all France scowl at you, and Havre be pack with

bargain in tour car—just leave about—by rich people come home....”



so the talk drift. and all the time Joan watch Peter as acutely and

as unsuspectedly as a mother might watch a grow-up son. tomorrow

morning he would go off and join up. but it ben’t that which make he

grave. new experience always elated Peter. and he wouldn’t be afraid;

not he.... she have be let into the view of three other young man who

have go to war already; Troop have write, correctly and consciously

heroic, “_some of the chap seem to be get a lot of emotion into

it_,” say Troop. “_it’s nothing out of the way that I can see. one just

fall into the line of one’s uncle and cousin._”



Wilmington have say: “I just want to see you, Joan. I’m tell I’ll be

most useful as a gunner because of my mathematic. when it come to

go over, you willn’t forget to think of I, Joan?”



Joan answer truthfully. “I’ll think of you a lot, Billy.”



“there’s nothing in life like you, Joan,” say Wilmington in his white

expressionless way. “well, I suppose I’d well be go.”



but Bunny have discourse upon fear. “_I’ve enlist_,” he write,

“_chiefly because I’m afraid of go Pacifist right out—out of funk.

but it’s hell, Joan. I’m afraid in my bone. I hate bang, and they say

the row of modern artillery be terrific. I’ve never see a dead body, a

human dead body, I mean, ever. have you? I would go round a quarter of a

mile out of my way any time to dodge a butcher’s shop. I be sick when I

find Peter dissect a rabbit. you know, sick, à la Manche. no

metaphor. I shall run away, I know I shall run away. but we’ve get to

stop these beastly Germans anyhow. it ben’t kill the Germans I shall

mind—I’m fierce on Germans, Joan; but see the chap on stretcher or

lie about with all sort of horrible injury._”



Sheets of that sort of thing, write in an unusually bad

handwriting—apparently rather to comfort himself than to sustain Joan.



well, it ben’t Peter’s way to think beforehand of be “on stretcher

or lie about,” but Bunny’s scribbling have get the stretcher into

Joan’s thought. and it make her wish somehow that Peter, instead of

be unusually grave and choose to be a ranker, be take this job

with his usual easy confidence and go straight and gaily for a

commission.



after dinner they all sit out in garden chair, outside the library

window, and have their coffee and smoke. Joan get her chair and draw it

close to Peter’s. two hundred mile away and less be battle and

slaughter, perhaps creep near to they, the roaring of great gun,

the rattle of rifle fire, the hoarse shout of man attack, and a

gathering harvest of limp figure “on stretcher and lie about”; but

that evening at Pelham Ford be a globe of golden serenity. not a leaf

stir, and only the little squeak and rustling of small creature

that run and flit in the dusk ruffle the quiet air.



Oswald make Peter talk of his climbing. “my only mountain be

Kilimanjaro,” he say. “no great thing so far as actual climbing go.”

Peter have begin with the Dolomites, have go over to Adelboden, and then

work round by the Concordia Hut to Bel Alp. “be it very beautiful?”

ask Joan softly under his elbow.



“you could have do it all. I wish you have come,” say Peter.



there be a pause.



“and Italy?” say Joan, still more softly.



“where do you go in Italy, Peter?” say Oswald, pick up her

question.



Peter give a travel-book description of Orta and the Isle of San Giulio.



Joan sit as still and watchful as a little cat watch for a mouse.

(something have put Peter out in Italy.)



“it’s off the main line,” say Peter. “the London and Paris paper don’t

arrive, and one have to fall back on the _Corriere della Sera_.”



“very good paper too,” say Oswald.



“News don’t seem so real in a language you don’t understand.”



he be excuse himself. so he be ashamed to that extent. that be what

be bother he. one might have know he wouldn’t care for—those other

thing....



late that night Joan sit in her room thinking. presently she unlock

her writing-desk and take out and re-read a letter. it be from Huntley

in Cornwall, and it be very tender and passionate. “_the world have go

mad, dearest_,” it run; “_but we need not go mad. the full moon be

slip by. I lie out on the sand last night pray for you to come,

try to will you to come. oh—when be you come?_”...



and much more to the same effect....



Joan’s face harden. “Po’try,” she say. she take a sharpen pencil

from the glass tray upon her writing-table and regard it. the pencil

be finely point—too finely point. she break off the top with the

utmost care and test the blunt point on her blot-paper to see if

it be broad enough for her purpose. then she scrawl her reply across

his letter—in five word: “_you ought to enlist. joan_,” and address

an envelope obliquely in the same uncivil script.



after which she select sundry other letter and a snap-shot give a

not unfavourable view of Huntley from her desk, and having scrutinize

the latter for an interval, tear they all carefully into little bit and

drop they into her wastepaper basket. she stand regard these

fragment for some time. “I might have go to he,” she whisper at

last, and turn away.



she blow out her candle, hesitate by her bedside, and walk to the

open window to watch the moon rise.



she sit upon her window-sill like a Joan of marble for a long time. then

she produce one of those dark saying with which she be willnot to wrap

rather than express her profounder thought.



“Queer how suddenly one discover at last what one have know all

along.... Queer....



“well, I know anyhow.”...



she stand up at last and yawn. “but I don’t like war,” say Joan.

“stretcher! or lie about! groan. in the darkness. boy one have

dance with. oh! beastly. _beastly!_”



she forget her intention of undressing, put her foot on the sill, and

rest chin on fist and elbow on knee, scowl out at the garden as

though she see thing that she do not like there.





                                  § 4



so it be that Joan see the beginning of the great winnowing of mankind,

and Peter come home in search of his duty.



within the first month of the war nearly every one of the man in Joan’s

world have be spin into the vortex; hers be so largely a world of

young or unattached people, with no deep root in business or employment

to hold they back. even Oswald at last, in spite of many rebuff, find

a use for himself in connection with a corps of african labourer behind

the front, and contrive after a steady pressure of many month towards

the danger zone, to get himself wound while he be talk to some of

his dear Masai at an ammunition dump. a Hun raider drop a bomb, and

some fly splinter of wood cut he deeply and extensively. the

splinter be vicious splinter; there be complication; and he find

himself back at Pelham Ford before the end of 1916, age by ten year.

the Woman’s Legion capture Joan from the date of its formation, and

presently have she drive a car for the new Ministry of Munitions, which

come into existence in the middle of 1915.



her career as a chauffeuse be a brilliant one. she live, after the

free manner of the Legion, with Miss Jepson at Hampstead; she go down

every morning to her work, she drive she good and her good continually

improve, so that she become distinguished among her fellow. the

Ministry grow aware of she and proud of she. a time arrive when

important official quarrel to secure she for their journey. eminent

foreign visitor invariably find themselves behind she.



“but she drive like a man,” they would say, a little breathlessly,

after some marvellously skid corner.



“all our girl drive like this,” the Ministry of Munitions would remark,

carelessly, loyally, but untruthfully.



Joan’s habitual wear become khaki; she have puttee and stout boot and

little brass lettering upon her shoulder and sleeve, and the only

distinctive touch she permit herself be the fur of her overcoat

collar and a certain foppery about her gauntlet....



extraordinary and profound change of mood and relationship occur in

the british mind during those first two year of the war, and reflect

themselves upon the mind of Joan and Peter. to begin with, and for

nearly a year, there be a quality of spectacularity about the war for

the British. they feel it to be an immense process and a vitally

significant process; they read, they talk, they think of little

else; but it be not yet feel to be an intimate process. the habit of

detachment be too deeply ingrained. great Britain be an island of

onlooker. to begin with the war seem like something tremendous and

arresting go on in an arena. “business as usual,” say the business

man, put up the price of anything the country seem to need. there

be a profound conviction that british life and the british community

be eternal thing; they might play a part—a considerable part—in these

foreign affair; they might even have to struggle, but it be

inconceivable that they should change or end. September and October in

1914 see an immense wave of volunteer enthusiasm—enthusiasm for the most

part thwart and waste by the unpreparedness of the authority for

anything of the sort, but it be the enthusiasm of an audience eager to

go on the stage; it be not the enthusiasm of performer in the arena

and unable to quit the arena, fight for life or death. to secure any

sort of official work be to step out of the undistinguished throng. in

uniform one feel dressed up and part of the pageant. young soldier be

self-conscious in those early day, and incline to pose at the ordinary

citizen. the ordinary citizen want to pat young soldier on the back

and stand they drink out of his free largesse. they be “in it,” he

feel, and he at most be a patron of the affair.



that spectacularity give way to a sense of necessary participation only

very slowly indeed. the change begin as the fresh, bright confidence

that the Battle of the Marne have beget give place to a deepening

realization of the difficulty on the road to any effective victory.

the persuasion spread from mind to mind that if Great Britain be to

fight this war as she have live through sixty year of peace, the

gentleman amateur among the nation, she would lose this war. the change

of spirit that produce its first mark result in the creation of the

Ministry of Munitions with a new note of quite unofficial hustle, and

lead on through a series of inevitable step to the adoption of

conscription, mark a real turning about of the british mind, the close

of a period of chaotic freedom almost unprecedented in the history of

community. it be the rediscovery of the State as the necessary form

into which the individual life must fit.



to the philosophical historian of the future the effort of govern

and lead people in Great Britain to get will together, to explain

necessity, to supplement the frightful gap in the education of every

class by hastily improvise organization, by speech, press-campaign,

poster, circular, cinema show, parade and proclamation; hasty,

fitful, ill-conduct and sometimes dishonestly conduct appeal though

they be, will be far more interesting than any story of battle and

campaign. they remind one of a hand scramble in the dark for

something long neglect and now find to be vitally important; they be

like voice call in a dark confusion. they be England seek to

comprehend herself and her situation after the slumber of two century.

but to people like Joan and Peter, who be not philosophical

historian, the process go on, not as a process, but as an apparently

quite disconnected succession of event. imperceptibly their thought

change and be socialize. Joan herself have no suspicion of the

difference in orientation between the Joan who stand at her bedroom

window in August, 1914, the most perfect spectator of life, stare out

at the darkness of the garden, dumbly resent the call that England

be make upon the free life of all her friend, and the Joan of 1917,

in khaki and a fur-collared coat, who sling a great car with a swift,

unerre confidence through the London traffic and out to Woolwich or

Hendon or Waltham or Aldershot or Chelmsford or what not, keen and

observant of the work her passenger discuss, a conscious part now of

a great and grow understanding and criticism and will, of a

rediscovered unity, which be England—awakening.



Youth grow wise very fast in those tremendous year. from the simple and

spectacular acceptance of every obvious appearance, the young mind

pass very rapidly to a critical and intricate examination. in the

first blaze of indignation against Germany, in the first enthusiasm,

there be a disposition to trust and confide in every one in a position

of authority and responsibility. the War Office be suppose—against

every possibility—to be plan wisely and act rapidly; the wisdom

of the Admiralty be take for grant, the politician now could have

no end in view but victory. it be assume that Sir Edward Carson could

become patriotic, Lord Curzon self-forgetful, Mr. Asquith energetic, and

Mr. Lloyd George straightforward. it be indeed a phase of extravagant

idealism. throughout the opening week of the war there be an

appearance, there be more than an appearance, of a common purpose and a

mutual confidence. the swift response of the Irish to the call of the

time, the generous loyalty of India, be like intimation of a new age.

the whole Empire be uplifted; a flush of unwonted splendour suffuse

british affair.



then the light fade again. there be no depth of understanding to

sustain it; habit be in the long run a more powerful thing than even the

supreme need. in a little time all the inglorious characteristic of

Britain at peace, the double-mindedness, the slackness, be reappear

through the glow of warlike emotion. fifty year of undereducation be

not to be atone for in a week of crisis. the man in power be just the

same man. the inefficient be still inefficient; the individualist

still self-seeking. the party politician forget their good resolution,

and revert to their familiar intrigue and manœuvre. Redmond and

Ireland learn a bitter lesson of the value of generosity in the face of

such ignorant and implacable antagonist as the Carsonites. Britain, it

become manifest, have neither the greatness of education nor yet the

simplicity of will to make war brilliantly or to sustain herself

splendidly. at every point devoted and able people find themselves

baffle by the dull inertia of the old system. and the clear flame of

enthusiasm that blaze out from the youth of the country at the first

call of the war be colour more and more by disillusionment as that

general bickering which be british public life revive again, and a

gather tale of waste, failure, and needless suffering mock the

reasonable expectation of a swift and glorious victory.



the change in the thought and attitude of the youth of Britain be to be

find express very vividly in the war poetry of the successive year.

such glow young hero as Julian Grenfell and Rupert Brooke shine

with a faith undimmed; they fight consciously, confident of the nearness

of victory; they sing and die in what they believe to be a splendid

cause and for a splendid end. an early death in the great war be not an

unmitigated misfortune. three year later the young soldier’s mind find

a voice in such poetry as that of young Siegfried Sassoon, who come home

from the war with medal and honour only to denounce the war in verse

of the extreme bitterness. his song be no long of picturesque

nobility and death in a glorious cause; it be a cry of anger at the

old man who have lead the world to destruction; of anger against the

dull, ignorant man who can neither make war nor end war; the man who

have lose the freshness and simplicity but none of the greed and egotism

of youth. Germany be no long the villain of the piece. youth turn

upon age, upon law and institution, upon the whole elaborate

rottenness of the european system, say: “_what be this to which you

have bring we? what have you do with our life?_”



no story of these year can ever be true that do not pass under a

shadow. of the little group of youth and man who have figure in this

story thus far, there be scarcely one who be not either kill

outright or crippled or in some way injure in the Great War—except

only Huntley. Huntley develop a deepening conscience against warfare

as the war go on, and suffer nothing bad than some unpleasant

half-hour with Tribunals and the fatigue of agricultural labour.

Death, which have first come to Joan as a tragic end to certain

“kittay,” be now the familiar associate of her every friend. her

confidence in the safety of the world, in the wisdom of human law and

institution, in the worth and dignity of empire and monarchs, and the

collective sanity of mankind be withdraw as a veil be withdraw, from

the harsh reality of life.



Wilmington, with his humourless intensity, be one of the first to bring

home to she this disillusionment and tragedy of the youth of the world.

he like pure mathematic; it be a subject in which he feel

comfortable. he have work well in the first part of the mathematical

tripos, and he be work hard in the second part when the war break

out. he fluctuate for some day between an utter repudiation of all war

and an immediate enlistment, and it be probably the light and colour of

Joan in his mind that make Wilmington a warrior. War be a business of

kill, he decide, and what he have to do be to apply himself and his

mathematic to gunnery as efficiently as possible, learn as rapidly

as might be all that be useful about shell, gun and explosive, and

so get to the killing of Germans thoroughly, expeditiously, and

abundantly. he be a particularly joyless young officer, white-faced and

intent, with an appearance of scorn that presently develop from

appearance into reality, for most of his colleague. he be work as

hard and as well as he could. at first with incredulity and then with

disgust he realize that the ordinary british officer be not do so.

they sing song, they rag, they leave thing to chance, they think

blunder funny, they condone silliness and injustice in the power

above. he would not sing nor rag nor drink. he work to the verge of

exhaustion. but this exemplary conduct, oddly enough, do not make he

unpopular either with the junior officer or with his senior. the

former tolerate he and rather admire he; the latter put work upon

he and seek to promote he.



in quite a little while as it seem—for in those day, while each day

seem long and laborious and heavy, yet the week and month pass

swiftly—he be a captain in France, and before the end of 1915 he write

to say that his major have leave he practically in command of his battery

for three week. he have be twice slightly wound by that time, but he

get little leisure because he be willing and indispensable.



he write to Joan very regularly. he be a motherless youth, and Joan be

not only his great passion but his friend and confidante. his interest

in his work overflow into his letter; they be more and more about

gunnery and the art of war, which become at last, it would seem, a

serious rival to Joan in his affection. he describe ill, but he would

send her reasoned statement of unanswerable view. he could not

understand why consideration that be so plain as to be almost

obvious, be be universally disregard by the Heads and the War

Office. he appeal to Joan to read what he have to say, and tell he

whether he or the world be mad. when he come back on leave in the

spring of 1916, she be astonish to find that he be still visibly as

deeply in love with she as ever. the fact of it be he have word for his

gunnery and military science, but he have no word, and that be the

essence of his misfortune, for his love for Joan.



but the burthen of his story be bitter disillusionment at the levity

with which his country could carry on a war that must need determine

the whole future of mankind. he would write out proposition of this

sort: “it be manifest that success in warfare depend upon certain

primary factor, of which generalship be one. no country resolute to win

a war will spare any effort to find the _good man_, and make they its

general and leader irrespective of every other consideration. no

honourable patriot will permit general to be appoint by any mean

except the _good selective method_, and no one who care for his

country will obstruct (1) the _promotion_, (2) _try over_, and (3)

_prompt removal_, if they fail to satisfy the most exact test, of

all possible man. and next consider what sort of man will be the good

commander. they must be _fresh-minded young man_. all the great

general of the world, the supreme case, the Alexanders, Napoleons, and

so on, have show their quality before thirty even in the day when

strategy and tactic do not change very greatly from year to year, and

now when the material and expedient of war make warfare practically a

_new thing_ every few year, the need for fresh young commander be far

more urgent than ever it have be. but the british army be at present

command by oldish man who be manifestly of not more than mediocre

intelligence, and who have no knowledge of this new sort of war that have

arise. it be a war of gun and infantry—with aeroplane come in more

and more—and most of the high position be hold by cavalry officer;

the artillery be invariably command by man unused to the handling of

such heavy gun as we be use, who stick far behind our forward

position and decline any practical experience of our difficulty. they

put we in the wrong position, they move we about absurdly; young

officer have have to work out most of the problem of gun-pit and so

forth for themselves—against resistance and mere stupid interference

from above. the Heads have no idea of the kind of work we do or of the

kind of work we could do. they be bad than amateur; they be

unteachable fossil. but why be this so? if the country be serious about

the war, why do it permit it? if the government be serious about the

war, why do it permit it? if the War Office be serious about the war,

why do it permit it? if G.H.Q. be serious about the war, why do it

permit it? what be wrong? there be a hitch here I don’t understand. be I

over-serious, and be all this war really some sort of gross, grim joke,

Joan? do I take life too seriously?



“Joan, in this last push this battery do its little job _right_; we cut

all the wire opposite we and blow out every blessed stake. we make a

nice tidy clean up. it be quite easy to do, give hard work. if I

haven’t do it I ought either to have be shoot for neglect or dismiss

for incapacity. but on our left it ben’t do. well, there be at

least a hundred poor devil of our infantryman on that wire, a hundred

mother’ son, hang like rag on it or crumple up below. I see they.

it make I sick. and I see the chap who be chiefly responsible for

that, Major Clutterwell, a little bit screw, be the life and soul

of a little party in Hazebrouck three day after! he ought to have be

the life and soul of a hari kari party, but either he be too big a cad

or too big a fool—or both. the way they shy away our infantryman over

here be damnable. they be the fine man in the world, I’m convince;

they will go at anything, and the red tab send they into impossible

job, fail to back they up—always they fail to back they up; they

neglect they, Joan; they neglect they even when they be fight and

die! there be man here, colonel, staff officer, I would like to

beat about the head with an iron bar....”



this be an unusually eloquent passage. frequently his letter be

mainly diagram to show for example how we crowd battery to brass

away at right angle to the trench when we ought to enfilade they, or

some such point. sometimes he be try to establish profound truth

about the proper function of field gun and howitzer. for a time he

be gnaw a bitter grievance. “I be tell to shell a line I couldn’t

reach. the contours wouldn’t allow of it. you can do a lot with a shell,

but you cannot make it hop slightly and go round a corner. there be a

definite limit to the height to which a gun will lob a shell. I try to

explain these elementary limitation of gunfire through the telephone,

and I be tell I should be put under arrest if I do not obey order. I

ben’t up against a commander, I ben’t up against an intelligence; I

be up against a silly old man in a temper. so I put over a barrage

about fifty yard beyond the path—the near possible. every one be

perfectly satisfied—the Boche include. thus it be that the young

officer be subdue to the medium he work in.”



at time Wilmington would embark on a series of proposition to

demonstrate with mathematical certitude that if the man and material

waste at Loos have be use in the Dardanelles, the war would have be

decide by the end of 1915. but the topic to which his mind recur

time after time be the topic of efficient leadership. “modern war

demand continuity of idea, continuity of will, and continuous

progressive adaptation of mean and method,” he write—in two separate

letter. in the second of these he have get on to a fresh notion.

“Education in England be a loafer education; it do not point to an

end; it do not drive through; it do not produce _mind that can hold

out_ through a long effort. the young officer come out here with the

good intention in the world, but one’s everyday life be shape not by

our intention but our habit. their habit of mind be loaf habit.

they learn to loaf at school. Caxton, I be now convince, be one of the

good school in England; but even at Caxton we do not fully acquire the

_habit of steadfast haste_ which modern life demand. everything that

get do out here be do by a spurt. with the idea behind it of

presently do nothing. the ordinary state of everybody above the

non-commission rank be loaf. at the present moment my major be

shoot pheasant; the battery to the left of we be curse because

they have to shift—it hold up their scheme for a hunt. just as though

artillery work ben’t the most intense sport in the world—especially now

that we be go to have kite balloon and do really scientific

observe. even the conscientious man of the Kitchener-Byng school don’t

really seem to I to _get on_; they work like Trojans at establish and

routine stuff but they don’t keep up inquiry. they be human, all too

human. man be a sedentary animal, and the schoolmaster exist to prevent

his sit down comfortably.” this from Wilmington without a suspicion

of jesting. “this human weakness for just live can only be correct

in school. the more I scheme about increase efficiency out here, the

more I realize that it cann’t be do here, that one have to go right back

to the school and begin with _a more continuous urge_. when this war be

over I shall try to be a schoolmaster. I shall hate it most of the time,

but then I hate most thing....”



but Wilmington never become a schoolmaster. he get a battery of six-inch

gun just before the Somme push in 1916, and he go forward with they

into position he choose and build up very carefully, only to be shift

against his wish almost at once to a new and, he believe, an

altogether inferior position. he be blow to nothingness by a german

shell while he be construct a gun pit.





                                  § 6



Wilmington be not the first of Joan’s little company to be kill. Joan

have the gift of friendship. she be rare among girl in that respect.

she be less of an artist in egotism than most of her contemporary;

there be even time when she could be self-forgetful to the pitch of

untidiness. two other among that handful of young soldier who be

kill outright and who have be her friend, write to she with some

regularity right up to the time of their death, and find a comfort in

do so. they write to she at first upon neat notepaper adorn with

regimental crest, but their later letter as they work their slow

passage towards the place of death be pencil on thin paper. she

keep they all. she feel she could have be a good sister to many

brother.



one of these two who die early be Winterbaum. she do not hear from

this young man of the world for some week after the declaration of war.

then come a large photograph of himself in cavalry uniform, and a manly,

worldly letter strongly reminiscent of Kipling and anticipatory of

Gilbert Frankau. “there be something splendid about this life after

all,” he write. “it’s good to be without one’s little luxury for a

space, democratically undistinguished among one’s fellow. it’s good to

harden up until nothing seem able to bruise one any more. I bathe

yesterday, without water, Joan—just a dry towel, and that not over

clean—be all that be available. after this be all over I shall have

such an appetite for luxury—I shall be fierce, Joan.”



those early day be still day of unrestricted plenty, and the

disposition of the british world be to pet and indulge everything in

khaki. Young Winterbaum wear his spur and the most beautiful

riding-breech to night club and great feast in the more

distinguished restaurant. he take his car about with he, his neat

little black-and-white car, fit with ivory fopperie. he try hard

to take it with he to France. from France his scribble letter become

more and more heroic in tone. “Poor David have be do in,” he say. “I

be now only three from the Contango peerage. Heaven send I get no

nearer! no feudal dignity for I. I would give three gild chamber

at any time for one reasonably large and well-light studio. and—I have a

kind of affection for my cousin.”



his prayer be answer. he get no nearer to the Contango peerage. the

power above he decide that a little place call Loos be of such

strategic value to the british army as to be worth the life of a great

number of young man, and pay in our generous british fashion even more

than the estimate. Winterbaum be part of the price. no particular of

his death ever come to Joan and Peter. the attack begin brightly, and

then die away. there be a failure to bring up reserve and grasp

opportunity. Winterbaum vanish out of life in the muddle—one of

thousand. he be the first of the little company of Joan’s friend to

be kill.



Bunny Cuspard spread a less self-conscious, more western, and altogether

more complicated psychology before Joan’s eye. like Wilmington he have

falter at the outset of the war between enlistment and extreme

pacifism, but unlike Wilmington he have never reconcile himself to his

decision. Bunny be out of sympathy with the fierceness of mankind; he

want a kindly, prosperous, rather funny world where there be nothing

more cruel than gossip; that be the world he be fit for. he

repeat in his own person and quality the tragedy of Anatole France. he

want to assure the world and himself that at heart everything be

quite right and magnificent fun, to laugh gaily at everything, see

through its bristling hostility into the depth of genial absurdity

beneath.



and so often he could find no genial absurdity.



he have always pretend that discover novel sort of cake for his

tea or new step for dance be the really serious business of life.

one of his holiday amusement have be “Little war,” which he play

with toy soldier and little model house and miniature wood of twig

and hill of boarding in a big room at his Limpsfield home. he would

have vacation party for day to carry out these war, and he and his

guest conduct they with a tremendous seriousness. he have elaborate

his miniature battle scenery more and more, make graveyard, church,

inn, wall, fence—even stick absurd notice and advertisement upon

the wall, and write epitaph upon his friend in the graveyard. he

have love the burlesque of it. he have feel that it bring history into

a proper proportion to humour. but one of the drawback have always be

that as the player lie upon the floor to move their soldier and gun

about they crush down his dear little toy house and wood....



his mind still fight desperately to see the war as a miniature.



he get to a laugh ever and again by a great effort, but some of the

thing that haunt his imagination would not under any circumstance

dissolve in laughter. thing that other people seem to hear only to

dismiss remain to suppurate in his mind. one or two of the thing that

be most oppressive to he he never tell Joan. but she have a glimpse

now and then of what be there, through the crack in his laughter.



he have hear a man tell a horrible story of the opening bombardment

of Ypres by the Germans. the core of the story be a bricke tunnel near

the old fortification of the town, whither a crowd of refugee have flee

from the bombardment, and into which a number of injured people have be

carry. a shell explode near the exit and imprison all those people

in a half-light without any provision or help. there be not even

drinking-water for the wounded. a ruptured drain pour a foul trickle

across the slimy floor on which the wounded and exhausted lay. now quite

near and now at a distance the shell be still burst, and through

that thudding and uproar, above all the crouch and murmuring

distress of that pit of misery sound the low, clear, querulous voice

of a little girl who be talk as she die, talk endlessly of how

she suffer, of how her sister could not come to help she, of she

desire to be take away; a little, scolding, indignant spirit she be,

with a very clear explicit sense of the vast impropriety of everything

about she.



“why do not some one come?”



“be tranquil,” an old woman’s voice remonstrate time after time. “help

will come.”



but for most of the people in the tunnel help never come. through a

slow, unhurrye night of indescribable pain and discomfort, in hunger,

darkness, and an evil stench, their life ebb away one by one....



that dark, dreadful, stink place, quiver to the incessant thunder

of gun, sink through twilight into night, light by flash and distant

flame, and pass through an eternity of misery to a cold, starve

dawn, thread by the child’s shrill voice, take a pitiless grip upon

Bunny’s imagination. he could neither mitigate it nor forget it.



how could one laugh at the Kaiser with this rankle in his mind? he

could not fit it into any merry scheme of thing, and he could not bear

any scheme that be not merry; and not to be able to fit dreadful thing

into a scheme that do at last prevail over they be, for such a mind

as Bunny’s, to begin to drift from sanity.



the second story that mutely reinforce the shrill indictment of that

little belgian girl be a description he have hear of some poor devil

be shoot for cowardice at dawn. a perplexed, stupid youth of two- or

three-and-twenty, with little golden hair that gleam on a pallid

cheek, be lead out to a heap of empty ammunition box in a desolate and

mutilate landscape of mud and splinter tree under a leaden sky, and

set down on a box to die. it be as if Bunny have see that live body

with his own eye, the body that jump presently to the impact of the

bullet and lurch forward, and how the officer in command—who have be

himself but a little child in a garden a dozen year or more ago—come up

to the pitiful prostrate form and put his revolver to the head behind

the ear that would never hear again and behind the eye that stare and

glaze, and pull the trigger “to make sure.”



Bunny could feel that revolver behind his own ear. it feel as a dental

instrument feel in the mouth.



“oh, my God!” cry Bunny; “oh, my God!” start up from his sack of

straw on the floor in his billet in the middle of the night.



“oh! _shut_ it!” say the man who be try to sleep beside he.



“sorry!” say Bunny.



“you keep it for the Germans, mate.”



“oh! oh! if I could kill this damned Kaiser with ten thousand torment!”

whisper Bunny, quiet down....



these be not the only story that torment Bunny’s mind, but they

be the chief one. other come in and go again—story of the

suffering of wounded man, of almost incredible brutality do to

woman and child and helpless people, and of a hundred chance

reasonless horror; they come in with an effect of support and

confirmation to these two principal figure—the shrill little girl

make her bitter complaint against God and the world which have promise

to take care of she, and have scare she horribly and tear her limb and

thrust she, thirsty and agonize, into a stink drain to die; and the

poor puzzle lout, catch and condemn, who have to die so dingily and

submissively because his heart have fail he. against the grim

instance of their sombre and squalid fate the soul of Bunny battle

whenever, by night or day, think overtake he in his essential and

characteristic resolve to see life as “fun”—as “great fun.”



these two fellow-sufferer in life take possession of his imagination

because of their intense kindre with himself. so far as he get his

riddle clear it be something after this fashion: “why, if the world be

like this, why be we in it? what be I do in this nightmare? why be

there little girl and simple lout—and I?”



the day draw near when he would have to go to the front. he write

shamelessly to Joan of his dread of that experience.



“_it’s the mud and dirtiness and ugliness_,” he say. “_I be a domestic

cat, Joan—an indoor cat...._



“_I’ve get a Pacifist temperament...._



“_all the same, Joan, the Germans start this war. if we don’t beat

they, they will start other. they be intolerable brute—the Junkers,

anyhow. until we get they down they will go on kick mankind in the

stomach. it be their idea of dignified behaviour. but we be cast our

youth before swine.... why ben’t there more assassin in the world? why

cann’t we kill they by machinery—painlessly and cleanly? we ought to be

clever than they be._”



there be extraordinarily little personal fear in Bunny. he be not

nearly so afraid of the thing that would happen to he as of the thing

that would happen about he. he hate the smashing even of inanimate

thing; a break-down chair or a roofless shed be painful to he.

whenever he think of the trench he think of treading and slip

in the dark on a torn and still live body....



he stick stoutly to his reasoning that England have to fight and that he

have to fight; but hide from Joan, hide from every live soul, he

keep a secret resolve. it be, he know, an entirely illogical and

treasonable resolve, and yet he find it profoundly comforting. he would

never fire his rifle so that it would hurt any one even by chance, and

he would never use his bayonet. he would go over the top with the good

of they, and carry his weapon and shout.



if it come to close fighting he would go for a man with his hand and

try to disarm he.



but this resolve be never put to the test. the Easter newspaper of

1916 arrive with flaming headline about an insurrection in Dublin and

the seizure of the Post Office by the rebel. oddly enough, this do not

shock Bunny at all. it produce none of the effect of horror and

brutality that the german invasion of Belgium have make upon his mind. it

impress he as a “rag”; as the sort of rag that they get up to at

Cambridge during season of excitement. he be delight by the seizure

of the Post Office, by the appearance of a revolutionary flag and the

issue of republican stamp. it be as good as “Little Wars”; it be

“Little Revolutions.” he don’t like the way they have shoot a policeman

outside Trinity College, but perhaps that report ben’t true. the whole

affair have restore that flavour of adventure and burlesque that he have

so sadly miss from the world since the war begin.



he have always idealize the irish character as the pleasantest

combination of facetiousness and generosity. when he find himself part

of a draft crossing to Dublin with his back to the grim war front, his

spirit rise. he could forget that nightmare for a time. he be go to

a land of wit and laughter which have rebel for a lark. he feel sure

that the joke would end happily and that he would be shake hand with

congenial spirit still wear Sinn Fein badge before a fortnight be

out. perhaps he would come upon Mrs. O’Grady or Patrick Lynch, whom he

have be accustom to meet at the Sheldricks’. he have hear they be

in it. and when the whole business have end brightly and cheerfully

then all those clever and witty people would grow grave and helpful, and

come back with he to join in that temporarily neglect task of

fight on the western front against an iron brutality that threaten

to overwhelm the world.



he be still in this cheerful vein two day later as he be cross St.

Stephen’s Green. his quaint, amiable face be smile pleasantly and he

be march with a native ungainliness that no drill-sergeant could

ever overcome, when something hit he very hard in the middle of the

body.



he know immediately that he have be shoot.



he be not dismay or shock by this, but tremendously interested.



all other feeling be swamp in his surprise at a curious

contradiction. he have feel hit behind, he be convince he have be hit

behind, but what be queer about it be that he be spin round as

though he have be hit in front. it give he a preposterous drunken

feeling. his head be quite clear, but he be altogether incapable of

control these spin leg of his, which be go round backward.

his facile sense of humour be arouse. it be really quite funny to be

spin backwards in this way. it be like a new step in dancing. his

hilarity increase. it be like the mad dancing they have ever have at

Hampstead or Chelsea. the “backwards step.” he laugh. he have to laugh;

something be tickle his rib and throat. his whole be laugh. he

laugh a laugh that become a rush of hot blood from his mouth....



the soul of Bunny, for all I know, laugh for ever among the star; but

it be a dead young man who finish those fantastic gyration.



he pause and sway and drop like an empty sack, and lie still in

St. Stephen’s Green, the modest contribution of one happy Sinn Fein

sniper to the Peace of Mankind.



perhaps Bunny be well out of a life where there can be little room for

Bunnyism for many year to come, and lucky to leave it laugh. and as

an offset to his loss we have to count the pleasant excitement of

Ireland in get well back into the limelight of the world’s affair,

and the bright and glowing gathering of the armed young hero who get

away, recount their deed to one another simultaneously in some

secure place, with all the rich, tumultuous volubility of the Keltic

habit.



“do ye see that red-haired fella I get in the square, boy?... ah, ye

should have see that fella I get in the square.”





                                  § 7



but not all the world of Joan be at war. the Sheldrick circle, for

example, after some wide fluctuation during which Sydney almost become

a nurse and bab nearly enlist into the Women’s Legion, take a marked

list under the influence of one of the son-in-law towards pacifism.

Antonia, who have take two german prize at school, be speedily

provoke by the general denunciation of “Kultur” into a distinctly

pro-german attitude. the Sheldrick circle settle down on the whole as a

pro-german circle, with a poor opinion of President Wilson, a marked

hostility to Belgians, and a disposition to think the hardship of

drown by U-boat much exaggerated.



the Sheldricks be like seedling that begin flourish and then damp

off. from amusing schoolfellow they have change into irritate and

disappointing friend. energy leak out of they at adolescence. they

seem to possess the vitality for positive conviction no long, they

display an instinctive hostility to any wave of popular feeling that

threaten to swamp their weak but still obstinate individuality.

their general attitude towards life be one of protest

refractoriness. whatever it be that people believe or do, you be

give to understand by undertone and abstinence that the sheldrick

know well, and for the most exquisite reason don’t. all their

friend be protester and rebel and seceder, or incomprehensible

poet, or inexplicable artist. and from the first the war be

altogether too big and strong for they. confront by such question as

whether fifty year of belligerent preparation, culminate in the most

cruel and wanton invasion of a peaceful country it be possible to

imagine, be to be resist by mankind or condone, the sheldrick fall

back upon the counter statement that Sir Edward Grey, be a landowner,

be necessarily just as bad as a German Junker, or that the government

of Russia be an unsatisfactory one.



in a few month it be perfectly clear to the Sheldricks that they would

have nothing to do with the war at all. they be go to ignore it.

Sydney just go on quietly do her little statuette that nobody

would buy, little portrait bust of her sister and suchlike thing; now

and then her mother contrive to get she a commission. bab keep on

try to get a part in somebody’s play; Antonia continue to produce

djibbah in chocolate and grocer’s blue and similar tint. one see the

sister drift about London in costume still trailingly

Pre-Raphaelite when all the rest of womankind be cut its skirt

short and short, their face rather pained in expression and

deliberately serene, ignore the hope and fear about they, the stir,

the huge effort, the universal participation. it be not their affair,

thank you. they be not go to wade through this horrid war; they

be go round.



every time Joan go to see they, either they have become more

phantomlike and incredible, or she have become coarser and more real.

would they ever get round? she ask herself; and what would they be

like when at last they attempt, if ever they attempt, to rejoin the

main stream of human interest again?



they keep up their Saturday evening, but their gathering become

thin and less and less credible as the war go on. the first wave of

military excitement carry off most of the sightly young man, and

presently the more capable and enterprise of the woman vanish one

after another to nurse, to join the Women’s Legion, to become substitute

clerk and release man to volunteer, to work in canteen and so forth.

there be, however, a certain coming and go of ambiguous adventurer,

who in those early day go almost unchallenged between London and

Belgium on ambulance work, on mysterious mission and with no mission

at all. belgian refugee drift in and, when they find a lack of

sympathy for their simple thirst for the destruction of Germans under

all possible circumstance, out again. then Ireland call her own, and

Patrick Lynch go off to die a martyr’s death with arm in his hand

after three day of the most exhilarating mixed shooting in the street

of Dublin. Antonia discover passionate memory as soon as he be

dead, and nobody be allow to mention the name of Bunny in the

Sheldrick circle for fear of spoil the emotional atmosphere. Hetty

Reinhart, after some fluctuation, go khaki, flit from one ministry

to another in various sort of clerical capacity, take such

opportunity as offer of entertain young officer lonely in our

great capital, and be no more see in Hampstead. what be leave of this

little group in the Hampstead _Quartier Latin_ draw together into a band

of resistance to the creep approach of compulsory service.



Huntley’s lofty scorn of the war have intensify steadily; the harsh

disappointment of Joan’s patriotism have sting he to great effort of

self-justification, and he become one of the most strenuous writer in

the extreme Pacifist press. not an act or effort of the Allies, he

insist, that be not utterly vile in purpose and doom to accelerate

our defeat. not an act of the enemy’s that be not completely think

out, wisely calculate, and plan to give the world peace and freedom

on the most reasonable term. he be particularly active in prepare

handbill and pamphlet of instruction for lifelong conscientious

objector to war service who have not hitherto think about the subject.

community of view bring he very close in feeling to both Babs and

Sydney Sheldrick. there be much talk of a play he be to write which

be to demonstrate the absurdity of Englishmen fight Germans just

because Germans insist upon fight Englishmen, and which be also to

bring out the peculiarly charming Babsiness of Babs. he study she

thoroughly and psychologically and physiologically and intensively and

extensively.



by a great effort of self-control he abstain from send his writing

to Joan. once however they be near meeting. on one of Joan’s rare

call Babs tell she that he be come to discuss the question whether

he should go to prison and hunger-strike, or consent to take up work of

national importance. bab be very full of the case for each

alternative. she be doubtful which course involve the great moral

courage. moral courage, it be evident, be be carry to giddy

height by Huntley. it would be pure hypocrisy, he feel, to ignore the

vital value of his writing, and while he could go on with these quite

comfortably while work as a farm hand, with a little judicious

payment to the farmer, their production would become impossible in

prison. he must crucify himself upon the cross of harsh judgment, he

feel, and take the former course. he want to make his view exactly

clear to every one to avoid misunderstanding.



Joan hesitate whether she should stay and insult he or go, and choose

the seemly course.





                                  § 8



Joan be already drive a car for the Ministry of Munitions before

Peter get himself transfer from the rank of the infantry to the

Royal Flying Corps. Peter’s career as an infantryman never take he

near to the western front than Liss Forest. then he perceive the

error of his way and decide to get a commission in the Royal Flying

Corps. in those day the Flying Corps be still a limited and

inaccessible force with a huge waiting list, and it need a

considerable exertion of influence to secure a footing in that select

band.... but at last a day come when Peter, rather self-conscious in his

new leather coat and cap, walk out from the mess past a group of

chat young pilot towards the aeroplane in which he be to have his

first experience of flight.



he have a sense of be scrutinize, but indeed hardly any one upon the

aerodrome note he. this sense of an audience make he deliberately

casual in his bearing. he salute his pilot in a manner decidedly

offhand. he clamber up through strut and wire to the front seat as if

he be a clerk ascend the morning omnibus, and strap himself in as

if it hardly matter whether he be strap in or not.



“Contact, sir,” say the mechanic. “contact,” come the pilot’s voice

from behind. the engine roar, a gale sweep backwards, and Peter

vibrate like an aspen leaf.



the wheel be clear, the mechanic jump aside, and Peter be

career across the grass in a series of light leap, and then his

progress become smooth. he do not perceive at first the reason for

this sudden steadying of the machine. he find himself tilt upward.

he be off the ground. he have be off the ground for some second. he

look over the side and see the grass fifty foot below, and the black

shadow of the aeroplane, as if it flee before they, rush at a hedge,

double up at the hedge, and start again in the next field. and up

he go.



Peter stare at field, hedge, tree, shed and roadway grow small

below he. he note cow in plan and an automobile in plan, in a lane,

go it seem very slowly indeed. it be a stagnant world below in

comparison with his own forward sweep. his initial nervousness and

self-consciousness have pass away. he be enormously interested and

delighted. he be try to remember when it be that Nobby have say: “I

doubt if we’ll see that in my lifetime—or your.” it be somewhen long

ago at Limpsfield. quite early....



and then abruptly Peter be clutch the side with his thick-gloved

hand; the aeroplane be come round in a close curve and banking

steeply, very steeply. for a moment it seem as though there be

nothing at all between he and England below. if he fall out——!



he look over his shoulder and meet the hard regard of a pair of

steel-blue eye.



he remember that after all he be under observation. this be no mere

civilian’s joy ride. he affect a concentration upon the scenery. the

aeroplane swing slowly back again to the level, and his hand leave the

side....



they be go up very rapidly now. the world seem to be roll in

at the edge of a great circle that grow constantly large. away to the

left be broad space of brown sand, and grey ripple and smooth

shine water channel, and beyond, the sapphire sea; beneath and to the

right be field, house, village, wood, and a distant range of hill

that seem to be come near. the scale be change and everything

be become maplike. cow be little dot now and man scarcely

visible.... and then suddenly all the scenery seem to be rush

upward before Peter’s eye and he have a feeling like the feeling one have

in a lift when it start—a down-borne feeling. he affect indifference,

and give the pilot his whistle profile. down they sweep, fast than a

luge on the swift ice run, until one could see the ditch in the

shadow beneath the hedge and cow be plainly cow again, and then

once more they be heel over and curve round. but Peter have be

ready for that this time; he have be tell himself over and over

again that he be strap in. he betray no surprise. he be get

more and more exhilarated.



and then they be climb again and soar straight out towards the

sea. up go this roar dragonfly in which Peter be sit, at a

hundred and twenty mile or so per hour, leave the dwindle land

behind.



up they go and up, until the world seem nearly all sea and the coast

be far away; they mount at last above a little white cloud puff and

then above a haze of cloud, and when Peter look down he see at a vast

distance below, through a clear gap in that filmy cloud fabric, three

ship small than any toy. of the man he could distinguish nothing.

how sweet the cold clear air have become!



and high above the world, in the lonely sky above the cloud fleece, the

pilot see fit to spring a surprise upon Peter.



he be not of the genial and considerate order of teacher; he believe

in weed out dud as swiftly as possible. he have an open mind as to

whether this rather over-intelligent-look beginner might not, under

certain circumstance, squeal. so he just try he and, without a note

of preparation, loop the loop with he.



the propeller that span before the eye of Peter dip. Peter bow in

accord with it. it dip more and more steeply, until the machine be

almost nose down, until Peter be look at the sea and the land as one

sit and look at a wall. he be tilt down and down until he be face

downward. and then as abruptly he be tilt up; it be like be in a

swing; the note of the engine alter as if a hand sweep up a scale of

note; the sea and the land seem to fall away below he as though he

leave they for ever, and the blue sky sweep down across his field of

vision like a curtain: he be, so to speak, on his back now with his

leg in the air, look straight at the sky, at nothing but sky, and

expect to recover. for a vast second he wait for the swing to end.

this be surely the end of the swing....



only—most amazingly—he don’t recover! he want to say, “ouch!” he be

immensely surprised—too surprised to be frightened. he go over

backwards—in an instant—and the sea and the land reappear above the

sky and also come down like curtain, too, and then behold! the

aeroplane be drive down and the world be in its place again far

below.



“the Loop!” whisper Peter, a little daze, and glance back at his

pilot and smile. this be no perambulator excursion. “the loop—first

trip!”



the blue eye seem a little less hard, the weather-red face be

smile faintly.



then grip by an irresistible power, Peter find himself go down,

down, down almost vertically. the pilot have apparently stop the

engine....



Peter watch the majestic expansion of the landscape as they fall. they

have come back over the land. far away he could see the aerodrome like a

scatter collection of little toy hut, and grow big and big

every instant. he sit quite still, for it be all right—it must be all

right. but now they be get very near the ground, and it be still

rush up to meet they, and pour outwardly as it rise. a cat now

would be visible....



it _be_ all right. the engine pick up with a roar like a score of

lion, and the pilot level out a hundred foot above the tree....



then presently they be drop to the aerodrome again; down until the

hedge be plain and the graze cattle close and distinct; and then,

with a sense of infinite regret, Peter perceive that they be back on

the turf again and that the flight be over. they dance lightly over

the turf. their rush slow down. they taxi gently up to the hangar

and the engine shudder and, with a pathetic drop to silence,

stop....



a little stiffly, Peter unbuckle himself and stretch and set himself

to clamber to the ground.



his weather-bite senior nod to he and smile faintly....



Peter walk towards the mess. it be wonderful—and intensely

disappointing in that it be so soon over. there be still great piece

of the afternoon leave....





                                  § 9



the aerodrome be short of machine and instructor, and he have to wait

a couple of week before he could get into the air a second time.



he work sedulously to gather knowledge during that waiting interval,

and his first real lesson find he a very alert and ready pupil. this

time the dual control be at his disposal, and for a straight or so the

pilot leave thing to he altogether. come half a dozen other lesson,

and then Peter find himself sit alone in a machine outside the

great shed, watch closely by a knot of friendly rival, and, for the

first time on his own account, conduct that duologue he have hear now

so often on other lip. “switch off.”... “suck in.” “contact!”



he start across the ground. his first sensation border on panic.

Hitherto the machine he have fly in have be just machine; now this

one, this one be an animal; it start out across the aerodrome like a

demented ostrich, swerve wildly and try to turn round. always

before this, the other man have do the taxi business on the ground. it

have never occur to Peter that it involve any difficulty. Peter’s

heart nearly fail he in that opening twenty second; he be convince

he be go to be kill; and then he determine to get up at any cost.

at any rate he wouldn’t smash on the ground. he let out the accelerator,

touch his control, and behold he be up—he be up! instantly the

machine cease to resemble a flounder ostrich, and become a steady

and dignified carinate, sway only slightly from wing to wing. up he

go over the hedge, over the tree, beyond, above the familiar field

of cow. the moment of panic pass, and Peter be himself again.



he have get right outside the aerodrome and he have to bank and bring she

round. already he have do that successfully a number of time with an

instructor to take care of he. he do it successfully now. his

confidence grow. back he buzz and drone, a hundred foot over the

aerodrome. he make three complete circuit, rise outside the aerodrome

and come down, make a good landing. he be instantly smite with the

intense regret that he have not make eight or nine circuit. it be a

mere hop. any man of spirit would have go on. there be four hour of

daylight yet. he might have go up; he might have try a spiral....

_damn!_



but the blue eye of the master approve he.



“couldn’t have make a well landing, Stubland,” say the master. “try

again tomorrow. follow it up close. short and frequent dose. that’s the

way.”



Peter have make another stage on his way to France.



come other solo flight, and flight on different type of machine, and

then a day of glory and disobedience when, three thousand foot above the

chimney of a decent farmhouse, Peter loop the loop twice. he have

learn by that time what it be to side-slip, and what air pocket can

do to the unwary. he have learn the bitter consequence of come down

with the engine go strong. he have have a smash through that all too

common mistake, but not a bad smash; a few strut and wire of the left

wing be all that have go. a hedge and a willow tree have stop he.

he have have a forced landing in a field of cabbage through engine

stoppage, and half an hour in a snowstorm when he have have doubt in an

upward eddy whether he might not be fly upside down. that have be a

nasty experience—his bad. he have several time take his hand off the

control and let the old bus look after herself, so badly be the

snowflake spin about in his mind. he dream a lot about fly, and

few of his dream be pleasant dream. and then this fantastic old

world of our, which have so suddenly divert his education to these

thing, and teach he to fly with a haste and intensity it have never

put into any teaching before, decide that he be ripe for the air war,

and pack he off to France....





                                  § 10



now, see that Joan have at last discover that she be in love with

Peter, it would be pleasantly symmetrical to record that Peter have also

discover by this time that he be in love with Joan.



but as a matter of fact he have discover nothing of the sort. he have

be amazed and humiliate by his three day of hesitation and

procrastination at Orta; the delay be altogether out of keep with

his private picture of himself; and he discover that he be not in

love with any one and that he do not intend again to be lure into any

dangerous pretence that he be. he have do with Hetty, he be

convince; he do not mean to see she any more, and he lead a life of

exasperate Puritanism for some month, refuse to answer the

occasionally very skilful and perplexing letter, with amusing and

provocative illustration, that she write he.



the idea of “relax moral fibre” obsess he, and our genial Peter

for a time abandon both smoking and alcohol, and be only deter

from further abstinence by their impracticability. the ordinary

infantry mess, for example, cater ill and resentfully for

vegetarian.... Peter’s day in the rank be day of strained

austerity. he be a terribly efficient recruit, a fierce soldier, a

wonderful influence on slacker, stripe gravitate towards he, and a

prophetic corporal see sergeant-major write on his forehead.

occasionally, when his imagination get loose or after a letter from

Hetty, he would indulge privately in fit of violent rage, find great

relief in the smashing of light object and foul and outrageous

language. he find what he consider a convenient privacy for this

idiosyncrasy in a disused cowshe near the camp, and only realize that

he have an audience when a fellow recruit ask anxiously, “and how’s

Miss Blurry ’Etty?” Whereupon Peter discover a well outlet for

pentup nervous energy in a square fight.



Joan see hardly anything of he during those early and brutal day, but

she think about he mightily. she share Oswald’s opinion that he

ben’t in his right place, and she write to he frequently. he answer

perhaps half her letter. his answer strike she as be rather pose.

the strain show through they. Peter be try very hard not to be

Peter. “I’m get down to elemental,” be one of his experiment in

the statement of his moral struggle.



then quite abruptly come his decision to get into the Royal Flying

Corps.



neither Oswald nor Joan venture any comment on this, because both of

they have a feeling that Peter have, in a sense, climb down by this

decision to go up....



in the Royal Flying Corps Peter’s rather hastily conceive theory of

moral fibre come into an uncongenial atmosphere. the Royal Flying Corps

be amazingly young, swift, and confident, and “moral fibre” base on

abstinence and cold self-control be not at all what it be after. the

Royal Flying Corps be much more inclined to scrap with soda-water

syphon and rag to the tune of a gramophone. it be a body that have have

to improvise a tradition of conduct in three or four swift year, and

its tradition be still unstable. mainly it be the tradition of the

game and sport side of a public school, roughly adapt to the new

need of the service; it be an essentially boyish tradition, even man

old enough to have go through the university be in a minority in

it, and Peter at one-and-twenty be one of the more elderly class of

recruit. and necessarily the tradition of the corps still vary widely

with the dominant personality and favourite hero of each aerodrome

and mess and squadron. it be a crowd of plastic boy, leave amazingly to

chance lead. their senior have no light for they, and they pick up

such hint as they could from Kipling and the music-hall, from

overheard conversation, and one another.



be it not an incredible world in which old man make war and untutored

young man have to find out how to fight they; in which tradition and the

past be mere entanglement about the foot of the young? the fly

service take the very flower of the youth of the belligerent nation;

they take the young man who be most manifestly fit to be

politician, statesman, leader of man, master in industry, and maker

of the new age; the boy of nerve, pluck, imagination, invention, and

decision. and there be not a sign of any realization on the part of any

one of the belligerent state of the fact that a large proportion of

this most select and valuable mass of youth be destine to go on live

after the war and be go to matter tremendously and be the backbone

of the race after the war. they let all these boy specialize as jockeys

specialize. the old man and ruler want these youngster to fight and

die for they; that any future lie beyond the war be too much for these

scared and unteachable ancient to apprehend. the short way to immediate

efficiency be to back the tradition of recklessness and gallantry, and

so the short way be take; if the brave lad be keep bold and

reckless by woman, wine and song, then by all mean, say their elder,

let they have these help. “a short life and a merry one,” say the

British Empire to these lad of eighteen and nineteen encouragingly. “a

short life and a merry one,” say the Empire to its future.



if the story of the air force be a glorious and not a shameful thing it

be because of the endure hope of the world—the incessant gallantry of

youth. these boy take up their great and cardinal task with the

unquenchable hopefulness of boyhood and with the impudence and humour of

their race. they bring in the irreverence and the spartanism of their

year. they make a language for themselves, an atrocious slang of

facetious misnomer; everything one do be a “stunt”; everything one

use be a “gadget”; the machine be “’bus” and “camel” and “pup”;

the old man be perpetually plead in vain for more dignity in the

official report. and these youngster work out their moral problem

accord to their own generous and yet puerile idea. they argue the

question of drink. could a man fly well or bad if he be “squiffy”?

do funk come to the thoughtful? and be ever a man gallant without

gallantry? after the death of Lord Kitchener there survive no man in

Britain of the quality to speak plainly and authoritatively and honestly

about chastity and drink to the young soldier. the State have no mind in

these matter. in most matter indeed the State have no mind; it be a

little old silly state. and the light side of the feminine temperament

flame up into shameless acquiescence in the heroic presence of the

fly man. youth instinctively set towards romantic adventure, and

the scale of chance for a considerable number of the fly man swing

between mésalliance and Messalina.



the code and the atmosphere vary from mess to mess and from squadron

to squadron; young man be by nature and necessity hero-worshipper and

imitative. Peter’s line fall among pleasant man of the “irresponsible”

school. the two good flyer he know, include he of the hard blue eye

who have first instruct he, be man of a physique that defy drink

and dissipation. vigour could smoke, drink, and dance in London, catch

the last train back with three second to spare, and be fly with an

unshaken nerve by half-past six in the morning; Vincent would only

perform stunt when he be “tight,” and then he seem capable of take

any risk with impunity. he could be funny with an aeroplane then a

thousand foot up in the air. he could make it behave as though it be

drunk, as though it be artful; he could make it mope or wag its tail.

man go out to watch he. the mess be decorate with picture from _La

Vie Parisienne_, and the art and literature of the group be Revue. now

see that Peter’s sole reason for his puritanism be the preservation

of efficiency, this combination of a fast life and a fine record in the

air be very disconcerting to he.



if he have be naturally and easily a first-class fly man he might

have stick to his line of high austerity, but he be not. he fly well,

but he have to fly with care; like many other airman, he always feel a

shadow of funk before go up, on two or three bad morning it be on

his conscience that he have delay for ten minute or so, and he be

more and more inclined to think that he would fly well if he fly with

a less acute sense of possibility. it be the start and the uneventful

flying that irk he most; hitherto every crisis have find he cool and

able. but the slap-dash style, combine with the exquisite accuracy of

these rake, Vigours and Vincent, fill he with envious admiration.



in the mess Peter meet chiefly youth of his own age or a year or so

young; he soon become a master of slang; his style of wit win its way

among they. he cease to write of “get down to elemental” to Joan,

and he cease to think of all other girl and woman as invention of the

devil. only they must be keep in their place. as Vigours and Vincent

keep they. just as one keep drink in its place. one must not, for

example, lose train on account of they....



through these month Joan maintain a strained watch upon the

development and fluctuation of Peter. he write—variously; sometimes

offhand duty note and sometimes long and brotherly letter—incurably

brotherly. every now and then she have glimpse of he when he come to

London on leave. manifestly he like her company and trust she—as

though she be a man. it be exasperate. she dress for Peter as she

have never dress for any one, and he would take she out to dine at the

_rendezvous_ or the _Petit Riche_ and sit beside she and glance at

common scrap of feminine humanity, at dirty little ogle bare-throate

girl in patch-up raiment and with harsh and scream voice, as

though they be the most delicious of forbid fruit. and he seem

to dislike be alone with she. if she drop her hand to touch his on

the table, he would draw his away.



be the invisible barrier between they invincible?



for a time during his infantry phase he have show a warm affection. in

his early day in the fly corps it seem that he draw still close

to she. then her quick, close watch upon he detect a difference. Joan

be get to be a very shrewd observer nowadays, and she feel a subtle

there have be some sort of spree in London with two or three other wild

spirit, and there have be “girl” in the party. such girl! he never

acute sense of smell detect a flavour of face powder or cheap scent

about Peter when he come along one day, half an hour late, to take she

to the Ambassadors. she be bad company that night for he.



for a time Joan be bad company for any one.



she be bad when she realize that Hetty be somehow reinstate in

Joan. not alone. as he speak, he glance at Joan and meet her eye.



it be astonishing that Miss Jepson never hear the loud shout of

“Hetty” that seem to fill the room.



it be just after this realization that an elderly but still gallant

colonel, go on an expedition for the War Office with various other

technical authority to suppress some disturbing invention that the

Ministry of Munitions be press in a troublesome manner, decide to

common error that a girl who wear khaki and drive a car demand less

respect from old gentleman and be altogether more playful than the

victorian good woman. possibly he be lure on to his own destruction.



when he descend at the Ministry, he look pinch and aged. he be

shake to the pitch of confidence. “my word,” he whisper. “that girl

drive like the devil. but she’s a vixen ... snap your head off....

don’t know whether this sort of thing be good for woman in the long run.



“robs ’em of Charm,” he say.





                                  § 11



it be just in this phase of wrath and darkness that Wilmington come

eye.



the last evening they have together be at the Criterion. they dine in

and then Wilmington decide to make himself interesting at any cost upon

this last occasion.



“Joan,” he say, knock out a half-consume cigarette upon the edge of

his plate.



“Billy?” say Joan, wake up.



“Queer, Joan, that you don’t love I when I love you so much.”



“I’d trust you to the end of the earth, Billy.”



“I know. but you don’t love I.”



“I think of you as much as I do of any one.”



“no. Except—_one_.”



“Billy,” say Joan weakly, “you’re the straight man on earth.”

“you’ve love Peter since you be six year old. it ben’t as

though—you’d treat I badly. I cann’t grumble that you’ve have no room

for I. he’s always be there.”



Joan, after an interval, decide to be frank.

“it’s not much good pretend I don’t hate Peter. I do. if I could kill

his face be a white misery. Joan glance round at the table about she,

“yes?”



“it’s queer.... I feel mean.... as though it ben’t right.... but look

Joan perceive he have something very important to say. she sit watch

“you don’t understand this Peter business, Joan. I—I do. mostly when I’m

not actually plan out or carry out the destruction of Germans, I

much to think about. and I think I see thing you don’t see. I know I

this last be to the waiter, who be make the customary warning about

“sorry,” say Wilmington to Joan, and leant forward over his fold arm

“it’s like this, Joan. Peter ben’t where we be. I—I’m very definite and

he’s be spoil. he’s so damned easy and good-looking. he be get hold

Wilmington flush for a moment. “I’m a chaste man, Joan. it’s a rare

natural way. the natural way be love of soul and body. he’s be

artificially heat ... the young man get make miscellaneous.... lot of

the girl even be miscellaneous....”



he consider the word. “miscellaneous? Promiscuous, I mean.... it

you’re fastidious. he’s neither. he take the thing that offer. to

grave people sex be a sacrament, something—so solemn and beautiful——”



the tear stand in his eye. “if I go on,” he say.... “I cann’t go

for a time he say no more, and pull his unconsumed cigarette to

“all this be—rather true,” say Joan. “but——!”



“what do it lead up to?”



“yes.”



“it mean Peter’s the ordinary male animal. under modern condition.

Lazy. Affectionate and all that, but not a scrap of emotion or love—yet

anyhow. not what you and I know as love. you may dress it up as you

but he’d hate the idea of any one else—make love to you.... it’s an

idea—— it’s outside of his conception of you.... he’ll never think of it

for himself.”



Joan sit quite still. after what seem a long silence she look up at

he.



Wilmington be watch her face. he see she understand his drift.



“you could cut she out like _that_,” say Wilmington, with a gesture

that gain an accidental emphasis by knock his glass off the table

the broken glass supply an incident, a distraction, with the waiter,

“that’s all I have to say,” say Wilmington when that be all settle.

the face of gratitude that look at he fade to a mask.



“you’re think of Peter already,” say Wilmington, watch her face.



it be true. she start, detect.



he speculate cheerlessly.



“you’ll marry I some day perhaps. when Peter’s throw you over.... it’s

man of my sort who get thing like that....”



he stand up and reach for her cloak. she, too, stand up.



then, as if to reassure she, he say: “I shall get kill, Joan. so we

live.... I always have take everything too seriously. always.... I

shall kill a lot of Germans yet, but one day they will get I. and Peter

will be up there in the air, like a cheerful midge—with all the Archies

miss he....”





                                  § 12



this conversation be a cardinal event in Joan’s life. Wilmington’s

suggestion raise out of the grave of forgetfulness and incorporate

with themselves a conversation she have have long ago with Adela—one

Christmas at Pelham Ford when Adela have be in love with Sopwith

Greene. Adela too have maintain that it be the business of a woman to

choose her man and not wait to be choose, and that it be the woman who

have to make love. “a man’s in love with woman in general,” have be

use a queer phrase, “it’s for a woman to find her own man and keep he

and take care of he.” man have to do their own work; they couldn’t think

about love as woman be oblige by nature to think about love. “love’s

just a trouble to a real man, like a mosquito singing in his ear, until

some woman take care of he.”



all those idea come back now to Joan’s mind, and she do she good to

consider they and judge they as generalization. but indeed she judge

make, and still she argue with herself. be it beneath her dignity to
