she try to generalize. have it ever be dignified for a woman to wait

until a man discover her possible love? be that at good anything more

than the dignity of the mannequin?



three-quarter at least of the art and literature of the world be

concerned with the relation of the sex, and yet here be Joan, after

thirty century or so of human art and literature, still debate the

elementary fact of her be. there be so much excitement in our art

and literature and so little light. the world have still to discover the

scope and vastness of its educational responsibility. Most of its

teaching in these matter hitherto have be less in the nature of

enlightenment than strategic concealment; we have give the young

neither knowledge nor training, we have restrain and baffle they and

tell they lie. and then we have inflame they. we have abuse their

instinctive trust when they be child with story of old Bogey

design to save we the bother that unrestrained youthful enterprise

might cause, and with humorous mockery of their natural curiosity.

jocularity about stork and gooseberry bush, sham indignation at

any plainness of speech, fierce punishment of imperfectly realize

offence, this against a background of giggle, know innuendo, and

careless, exciting glimpse of the mystery, have constitute the

ordinary initiation of the youth of the world. right up to full age, we

still fail to provide the clear elemental fact. our young man do not

know for certain whether continence be healthy or unhealthy, possible or

impossible; the sex be still assure with all our power of assurance,

that the only pure and proper life for it be a sexless one. until at

last the bright of the young have be oblige to get down to the

bare fact in themselves and begin again at the beginning....



so Joan, co-heiress of the Ages with Peter, find that because of her

faith in Nature which sustain the generation of Jean Jacques Rousseau.

she have to set about her problem with Peter as though he and she be

have never come.



Joan be too young yet to have develop the compensate egotism of

know far more of his mental life than Oswald do. she know that he have

his sense, keep his conscience, uphold he....



she have convince herself now that that be her chief business in life.



her mind be remarkably free from doubt about the future if once she

“I don’t care so very much how long it take, Peter; I don’t care what

to make a path for you. you be come to my arm, Peter ... come to

I....”



so Joan frame her scheme, regardless of the swift approach of the day

of battle for Peter. she be resolve to lose nothing by neglect or

“it’s just like that,” say Joan.



those barrier have to be break down, without a shock. and before that

and while she be still meditate quite fruitlessly on this riddle of

“well,” say Peter, astonish.



she have surprise he. he stare at she for an instant with a

                                  § 13



he fly to France the next day, above the grey and shine stretch of

Joan be dismayed. in war time we must not brood on death, one do not

think of death if one can help it; it be the chance that wreck all

letter.



Death be more terrible to a girl in love than to any other living thing.

“if he die,” say Joan, “I be kill. I shall be bad than a widow—an

indian girl widow. suttee; what will be leave of I but ashe?... some

poor dreg of Joan carry on a bankrupt life.... no I....”

but letter go slowly, and life and death hustle along together very

                                  § 14



Joan’s mind be full of love and life and the fear of lose they, but

Peter be think but little of love and life; he be secretly

preoccupy with the thought, the forbidden thought, of death, and with

the strangeness of war and of this earth see from an aeroplane ten

thousand foot or so above the old battlefield of mankind. he be see

the world in plan, and realize what a flat and shallow thing it be.

on clear day the circuit of the world he see have a circumference of

hundred of mile, night fly be a journey amidst the star with the

little black planet far away; there be no former achievement of the

race that do not seem to he now like a miniature toy set out upon the

floor of an untidy nursery. he have beat up towards the very limit of

life and air, to the clear thin air of twenty-two or twenty-three

thousand foot; he have be in the blind sunlight when everything

below be still asleep in the blue of dawn.



and the world of history and romance, the world in which he and all his

ancestor have believe, a world see in elevation, of tower

frontage, high portal, inaccessible dignity, giddy pinnacle and

frown reputation, have now fall as flat, it seem, as the façade

of the Cloth Hall at Ypres. (he have see that one day from above, spread

out upon the ground.) he be convince that high above the thing of the

past he drone his liquid way towards a new sort of life altogether,

towards a great civilization, a world-wide life for man with no

boundary in it at all except the emptiness of outer space, a life of

freedom and exaltation and tremendous achievement. but meanwhile the old

thing of the world be try most desperately to kill he. every day

the enemy’s anti-aircraft gun seem to grow more accurate; and high

above the little fleecy cloud lurk the braggart Markheimer and the

gallant von Papen and suchlike german champion, with their decoy

below, ready to swoop and strike. never before have the world promise

Peter so tremendous a spectacle as it seem to promise now, and never

before have his hope of live to see it be so insecure.



when he have enlist, and even after he have be transfer to the

Flying Corps, Peter have think very little of death. the thought of

death only become prevalent in english mind towards the second year of

the war. it be a hateful and unnatural thought in youth, easily

dismiss altogether unless circumstance press it incessantly upon the

attention. but even before Peter go to France two of his set have be

kill under his eye in a collision as they come down into the

aerodrome, and a third he have see two mile away get into a spiral nose

dive, struggle out of it again, and then go down to be utterly smash

to piece. in one day on Salisbury Plain he have see three accident,

and two, he know, have be fatal and one have leave a legless thing to

crawl through life. the mess in France seem populous with young

ghost; reminiscence of spree, talk of fly adventure be lace

with, “dear old boy! he go west last May.” “Went west” be the common

phrase. they never say “kill.” they hate the very name of death.

they do their good, these dear gallant boy, to make the end seem an

easy and familiar part of life, of life with which they be so joyously

in love. they all know that the dice be load against they, and that

as the war go on the chance against they grow. the first day Peter

be out in France he see a man hit and bring down by a German Archie.

two day after, he find himself the centre of a sudden constellation of

whoofing shell that leave inky cloudburst over he and under he and

round about he; he see the fabric of his wing jump and quiver, and

drop six hundred foot or so to shake the gunner off. but _whuff_ ...

_whuff_ ... _whuff_, like the bark of a monstrous dog ... the beast be

on he again within a minute, and Peter do two or three loop and come

about and get away with almost indecent haste. he be tremble; he

hate it. and he hate to tremble.



in the mess that evening the talk run on the “Pigeon shooter.” it seem

that there be this one german gunner far quick and more deadly than

any of his fellow. he have a knack of divine what an airman be go

to do. Peter admit his near escape and seek counsel.



Peter’s colleague watch he narrowly and unostentatiously when they

advise he. their face be mask and his face be a mask, and they

be keen for the faint intonation of what be behind it. they all

hate death, they all try not to think of death; they all believe

that there be Paladins, other fellow, who never think of death at

all. when the tension get too great they rag; they smash great

quantity of furniture and make incredible volume of noise. twice

Peter get away from the aerodrome to let thing rip in Amiens. but such

outbreak be usually follow by a deep depression of spirit. in the

night Peter would wake up and find the thought of death sit by his

bedside.



so far Peter have never have a fight. he have go over the enemy line

five time, he have bomb a troop train in a station and a regiment

rest in a village, he believe he have kill a score or more of

Germans on each occasion and he feel not the slight compunction, but

he have not yet come across a fighting Hun plane. he have very grave

doubt about the issue of such a fight, a fight that be bind to come

soon or later. he know he be not such a quick pilot as he would like

to be. he think quickly, but he think rather too much for rapid,

steady decision. he have the balancing, scientific mind. he know that

none of his flight be perfect. always there be a conflict of

intention at some point, a hesitation. he believe he might last for

week or month, but he know that somewhen he would be find

wanting—just for a second perhaps, just in the turn of the fight. then

he would be kill. he hide quite successfully from all his companion,

and particularly from his squadron commander, this conviction, just as

he have previously hide the vague funk that have invariably invade his

be whenever he walk across the ground towards the machine during

his day of instruction, but at the back of his mind the thought that

his time be limited be always present. he believe that he have to die;

it might be tomorrow or next week or next month, but somewhen within the

year.



when these conviction become uppermost in Peter’s mind a black

discontent possess he. there be no such bitter critic of life as

the young; theirs be a magnificent greed for the splendour of life. they

have no patience with delay; their blunder and failure be

intolerable. Peter review his two-and-twenty year—it be now nearly

three-and-twenty—with an intense dissatisfaction. he have waste his

time, and now he have get into a narrow way that lead down and down

pitilessly to where there would be no more time to waste. he have be

aimless and the world have be aimless, and then it have suddenly turn

upon he and catch he in this lobster-trap. he have waste all his

chance of great experience. he have never love a woman or have be well

love because he have fritter away that possibility in a hateful sex

excitement with Hetty—who do not even pretend to be faithful to he.

and now thing have get into this spin to death. it be exactly like a

spin—like a spin nose dive—the whole affair, his life, this war....



he would lie and fret in his bed, and fret all the more because he know

his wakefulness waste the precious nervous vigour that might save his

life next day.



after a black draught of such thought Peter would become excessively

noisy and facetious in the mess tent. he be recognize and applaud as

a wit and as a devil. he be really very good at limerick, delicately

indelicate, upon the name of his fellow officer and of the village

along the front—that be no doubt heredity, the gift of his Aunt

Phyllis—and his caricature adorn the mess. it be also understand

that he be a rake....



Peter’s evil anticipation be only too well justified. he be put down

in his very first fight, which happen over Dompierre. he have bad luck;

he be strike by von Papen, one of the crack german flier on that part

of the front. he be up at ten thousand foot or so, more or less

cover a low-fly photographer, when he see a german machine come

over half a mile perhaps or more away as though it be look for

trouble. Peter know he might funk a fight, and to escape that moral

disaster, head straight down for the German, who drop and make off

southward. Peter rejoice at this flight, pursue, his eye upon the

quarry. then from out of the sun come von Papen, swiftly and

unsuspected, upon Peter’s tail, and announce his presence by a whiff of

bullet. Peter glance over his shoulder to discover that he be catch.



“oh damn!” cry Peter, and duck his head, and feel himself sting at

the shoulder and wrist. splinter be fly about he.



he try a side-slip, and as he do so he have an instant’s vision of yet

another machine, a Frenchman this time, fall like a bolt out of the

blue upon his assailant. the biter be bit.



Peter try to come round and help, but he turn right over sideway

and drop, and suddenly find himself with the second Hun plane come

up right ahead of he. Peter blaze away, but God! how his wrist hurt

he! he curse life and death. he blaze away with his machine go

over more and more, and the landscape rush up over his head and then

get in front of he and circle round. for some second he do not

know what be up and what be down. he continue to fire, fire

earthward for a long second or so after his second enemy have disappear

from his vision.



the world be spin round fast and fast, and everything be

move away outward, fast and fast, as if it be all hasten to

get out of his way....



this surely be a spin nose dive, the spin nose dive—from

within. round and round. confusing and giddy! just as he have see poor

old Gordon go down.... but one don’t feel at all—as Peter have suppose

one must feel—like an egg in an egg-whisk!...



down spin the aeroplane, as a maple fruit in autumn spin to the ground.

then this still live thing that have be Peter, all bloody and break,

make a last supreme effort. and his luck second his effort. the spin

grow slow and flatter. control of this lurching, eddy aeroplane

seem to come back, escape again, mock he. the ground be very

near. _now!_ the sky swing up over the whirling propeller again and

stay above it, and again the machine obey a reasonable soul.



he be out of it! out of a nose dive! yes. steady! it be so easy when

one’s head be whirl to get back into a spin again. steady!...



he talk to himself. “oh! good Peter! _good_ Peter! _clever_ Peter.

wonderful Mr. Toad! stick it! stick it!” but what a queer right hand it

be! it be cover with blood. and it crumple up in the middle when he

clench it! never mind!



he be in the low storey of the air. the Hun and the Frenchman up

there be in another world.



down below, quite close—not five hundred foot now—be field-grey

running and shoot at he. they be count their chicken before he

be hatch—no, smash.... he ben’t do yet! not by any manner of

mean! a wave of great cheerfulness and confidence buoy up Peter. he

feel equal to any enterprise. should he drop and let the bawl Boche

have a round or so?



and there be a Hun machine smash upside down on the ground. be that

the second fellow?



Flick! a bullet!



Wiser counsel come to Peter. this be no place for a sick and giddy man

with a smashed and bleed wrist. he must get away.



up! which way be west? west? the sun rise in the east and set in the

west. but where have the sun get to? it be hide by his wing. shadow!

the shadow would be point north-east, that be the tip.... up! there

be the Boche trench. no, Boche reserve trench.... go west,

go west.... Rip! Snap! bullet through the wing, and a wire flickering

about. he duck his head.... he put the machine up steeply to perhaps a

thousand foot....



he have an extraordinary feeling that he and the machine be grow and

swell, that they be get big and big, and the sky and the

world and everything else small. at last he be a monstrous man in a

vast aeroplane in the tiny of universe. he be as great as God.



that wrist! and this blood! blood! and great, glow spot of blood

that make one’s sight indistinct....



he cough, and feel his mouth full of blood, and spit it out and

retch....



then in an instant he be a little thing again, and the sky and the

world be immense. he have a lucid interval.



one ought to go up and help that Frenchman. where be they fight?...

_up_, anyhow!



this must be no Man’s Land. that crumple little thing be a dead body

surely. barbed wire. more barbed wire.



the engine be miss. Ugh! _that fairly put the lid on!_



Peter be already asleep and dream. the great blood spot have

return and increase, but now they be get black, they be

black, huge black blotch; they blot out the world!



Peter, Peter as we have know he, discontinued existence....



it be an automaton, aid by good luck, that drop his machine half a

mile behind the french trench....





                                  § 15



Peter have no memory of come to again from his faint. for a long time

he must have continue to be purely automatic. his flaming wrist be the

centre of his being. then for a time consciousness resume, as abruptly

as the thread of a story one find upon the torn page of a novel.



he find himself in the midst of a friendly group of pale blue uniform;

he be stand up and be very lively in spite of the strong taste of

blood in his mouth and a feeling that his wrist be burn as a match

burn, and that the left upper half of his body have be change into a

lump of raw and bleed meat. he be talk a sort of French. “_C’est

sacré bon stuff, cet eau-de-vie Française_,” he be say gaily and

rather loudly.



“Haf some more,” say a friendly voice.



“not half, old chap,” say Peter, and feel at the time that this be not

really good French.



he try to slap the man on the shoulder, but he couldn’t.



“_Bon!_” he say, “as we say in England,” and feel that that remark also

fail.



some one protest softly against his be give more brandy....



then this clear fragment end again. there be a kind of dream of

rather rough but efficient surgery upon a shoulder and arm that be

quite probably his own, and some genially amiable conversation. there

be a very nice Frenchman with a black beard and soft eye, who wear a

long white overall, and seem to be look after he as tenderly as a

woman could do.



but with these thing mingle the matter of delirium. at one time the

Kaiser prevail in Peter’s mind, a large, foolish, pompous person with

wax moustache and distraught eye, who crawl up to Peter over

immense pile of white and grey and green rotting corpse, and begin

gnaw at his shoulder almost absent-mindedly. Peter struggle and

protest. what business have this beastly german to come interfere

with Peter’s life? he start a vast argument about that, in which all

sort of people, include the nice-look Frenchman in the white

overall, take part.



Peter be now make a formal complaint about the conduct of the

universe. “no,” he insist time after time, “I will not deal with

subordinate. I insist on see the Head,” and so at last he find

himself in the presence of the Lord God....



but Peter’s vision of the Lord God be the most delirious thing of all.

he imagine he in an office, a little office in a vast building, and so

out of the way that people have to ask each other which be the passage

and which the staircase. old man stand and argue at corner with

Peter’s girl-guide whether it be this way or that. People be be

show over the building by girl-guide; it be very like the London War

Office, only more so; there be great number of visitor, and they all

seem to be in considerable hurry and distress, and most of they be

look for the Lord God to lodge a complaint and demand an explanation,

just as Peter be. for a time all the visitor become wounded man, and

nurse mix up with the girl-guide, and Peter be be carry

through fresh air to an ambulance train. his shoulder and wrist be

very painful and singe, as it be, a throb duet together.



for a time Peter do seem to see the Lord God; he be in his office, a

little brown, rather tired-look man in a kepi, and Peter be on a

stretcher, and the Lord God or some one near he be say: “_Quel

numéro_?” but that pass away, and Peter be again conduct his

exploration of the corridor with a girl-guide who be sometimes like

Joan and sometimes like Hetty—and then there be a queer disposition to

loiter in the passage.... for a time he sit in dishabille while Hetty

try to explain God.... dream cross the scent of dream.



then it seem to Peter’s fevere brain that he be sit, and have

be sit for a long time, in the little office of the Lord God of

Heaven and Earth. and the Lord God have the likeness of a lean, tired,

intelligent-look oldish man, with an air of futile friendliness

mask a fundamental indifference.



“my dear sir,” the Lord God be say, “do please put that cushion

behind your poor shoulder. I cann’t bear to see you so uncomfortable. and

tell I everything. everything....”



the office be the dingy and untidy little office it be possible

to imagine. the desk at which God sit be in a terrible litter. on a

side table be some grubby test tube and bottle at which the Lord God

have apparently be try over a new element. the window have not be

clean for age, they be dark with spider’ webs, they crawl with a

buzz nightmare of horrible and unmeaning life. it be a most

unbusinesslike office. there be no proper file, no card index;

bundle of dusty paper be thrust into open fixture, paper litter

the floor, and there be brass-handle drawers—. Peter look again,

and blood be ooze from these drawer and little cry come out of

they. he glance quickly at God, and God be look at he. “but do

you really make this world?” he ask.



“I _think_ I do,” say God.



“but why do you do it? _why?_”



“ah, _there_ you have I!” say the Lord God with bonhomie.



“but why don’t you exert yourself?” say Peter, hammer at the desk

with his sound hand. “why don’t you exert yourself?”



could delirium have ever invent a more monstrous conception than this

of Peter hammer on an untidy desk amidst old pen nib, bit of

sealing-wax, half-sheet of notepaper, return of nature’s waste, sample

bone of project animal, mineral sample, dirty little test tube,

and the like, and lecture the Almighty upon the dreadful confusion

into which the world have fall? “here be I, sir, and million like I,

with a clear promise of life and freedom! and what be we now? Bruises,

red bone, dead body! this german Kaiser fellow—an ass, sir, a perfect

ass, gnaw a great hole in my shoulder! he and his son, stuff

themselves with a Blut-Wurst make out of all our life and happiness!

what do it mean, sir? have it go entirely out of your control? and it

ben’t as if the whole thing be ridiculous, sir. it ben’t. in some way

it’s an extraordinarily fine world—one have to admit that. that be why it

be all so distressing, so unendurably distressing. I don’t in the least

want to leave it.”



“you admit that it’s fine—in place,” say the Lord God, as if he value

the admission.



“but the management, sir! the management! your—ultimately. don’t you

realize, sir——? I have the great trouble in find you. half the

messenger don’t know where this den of yours be. it’s _forget_.

practically forget. the Head Office! and now I’m here I can tell you

everything be go to rack and ruin, drive straight to an absolute

and final smash and break-up.”



“as bad as that?” say the Lord God.



“it’s the appalling waste,” Peter continue. “the waste of material, the

waste of we, the waste of everything. a sort of splendour in it, there

be; touch of real genius about it, that I would be the last to deny;

but that only increase the bitterness of the disorder. it’s a good

enough world to lament. it’s a good enough life to resent have to lose

it. there’s some lovely thing in it, sir; courage, endurance, and oh!

many beautiful thing. but when one get here, when one begin to ask

for you and hunt about for you, and find this, this muddle, sir, then

one begin to understand. _look_ at this room, consider it—as a general

manager’s room. no decency. no order. everywhere the dust of age, muck

indescribable, bacteria! and that!”



that be a cobweb across the grimy window pane, in which a freshly

entangle bluebottle fly be buzz fussily. “that ought not to be here

at all,” say Peter. “it really ought not to exist at all. why do it?

look at that beastly spider in the corner! why do you suffer all these

cruel and unclean thing?”



“you don’t like it?” say the Lord God, without any sign either of

apology or explanation.



“no,” say Peter.



“then _change_ it,” say the Lord God, nod his head as who should

say “get you there.”



“but how be we to change it?”



“if you have no will to change it, you have no right to criticize it,”

say the Lord God, lean back with the weariness of one who have have to

argue with each generation from Job onward, precisely the same

objection and precisely the same argument.



“after all,” say the Lord God, give Peter no time to speak far;

“after all, you be three-and-twenty, Mr. Peter Stubland, and you’ve

be pretty busy complain of I and everything between I and you,

your master, pastor, teacher, and so forth, for the last half-dozen

year. meanwhile, be your own record good? positive achievement,

forgive I, be still to seek. you’ve be nearly drunk several time,

you’ve soil yourself with a lot of very cheap and greedy love-making—I

give you something beautiful there anyhow, and you know that while you

spoil it—you’ve be a vigorous member of the consume class, and

really, you’ve get nothing clear and plan, nothing at all. you

complain of my lack of order; where’s the order in your own mind? if I

be the hot-temper old autocrat some of you people pretend I be, I

should have be tickle you up with a thunderbolt long ago. but I

happen to have this democratic fad as badly as any one—Free Will be what

they use to call it—and so I leave you to work out your own salvation.

and if I leave you alone then I have to leave that other—that other Mr.

Toad at Potsdam alone. he try I, I admit, almost to the miracle pitch

at time with the tone of his everlasting prepaid telegram—but one have

to be fair. what be sauce for the goose be sauce for the Kaiser. I’ve

get to leave you all alone if I leave one alone. don’t you see that? in

spite of the mess you be in. so don’t blame I. don’t blame I. there

ben’t a thing in the whole of this concern of mine that Man cann’t

control if only he choose to control it. it’s arrange like that.

there’s a lot more system here than you suspect, only it’s too ingenious

for you to see. it’s yours to command. if you want a card index for the

world—well, get a card index. I willn’t prevent you. if you don’t like my

spider, kill my spider. I’m not conceited about they. if you don’t

like the Kaiser, hang he, assassinate he. why don’t you abolish king?

you could. but it be your sort, with your cheap and quick efficiency

scheme, who set up Saul—in spite of my protest—age ago.... humanity

either make or breed or tolerate all its own affliction, great and

small. not my doing. take king and court. take dungheap and fly.

it’s astonish you people haven’t kill off all the fly in the

world long ago. they do no end of mischief, and it would be perfectly

easy to do. they’re purely educational. purely. even as you lie in

hospital, there they be buzz within an inch of your nose and landing

on your poor forehead to remind you of what a properly organize

humanity could do for its own comfort. but there’s man in this world who

want I to act as a fly-paper, simply because they be too lazy to get

one for themselves. my dear Mr. Peter! if people haven’t teach you

properly, teach yourself. if they don’t know enough, find out. it’s all

here. all here.” he make a comprehensive gesture. “I’m not mock you.”



“you’re not mock I?” say Peter keenly....



“it depend upon you,” say the Lord God with an enigmatic smile. “you

ask I why I don’t exert myself. well—why don’t _you_ exert yourself?



“why don’t _you_ exert yourself?” the Lord God repeat almost rudely,

drive it home.



“that pillow under your shoulder still ben’t comfortable,” say the Lord

God, break off....



the buzzing of the entangled fly change to the drone of a pass

aeroplane, and the dingy office expand into a hospital ward. some one

be adjust Peter’s pillow....





                                  § 16



if his shoulder-blade be to mend, Peter could not be move; and for a

time he remain in the french hospital in a long, airy room that be

full mostly with fly man like himself. at first he could not talk

very much, but later he make some friend. he be himself very immobile,

but other man come and sit by he to talk.



he talk chiefly to two Americans, who be serve at that time in the

French fly corps. he find it much easy to talk English than French

in his exhausted state, for though both he and Joan speak French far

above the average public school level, he find that now it come with an

effort. it be as if his mind have for a time be pare down to its

essential.



these Americans amuse and interest he tremendously. he have meet

hardly any Americans before so as to talk to they at all intimately, but

they suffer from an inhibition of French perhaps more permanent than

his own, and so the three be throw into an unlimited intimacy of

conversation. at first he find these Americans rather fatigue, and

then he find they very refreshing because of their explicitness of

mind. except when they break into frothy rapid of slang they be never

allusive; in serious talk they say everything. they lay a firm

foundation for all their assertion. that be the last thing an

Englishman do. they talk of the war and of the prospect of America

come into the war, and of England and America and again of the war,

and of the French and of the French and Americans and of the war, and of

Taft’s League to Enforce Peace and the true character of Wilson and

Teddy and of the war, and of Sam Hughes and Hughes the Australian, and

whether every country have the Hughes it deserve and of the war, and of

go to England after the war, and of Stratford-on-Avon and Chester and

Windsor, and of the peculiarity of english people. their idea of

England Peter discover be strange and picturesque. they believe all

englishman live in a glow of personal loyalty to the Monarch, and be

amazed to learn that Peter’s sentiment be republican; and they

think that every Englishman dearly love a lord. “we think that of

Americans,” say Peter. “that’s our politeness,” say they in a chorus,

and start a train of profound discovery in international

relationship in Peter’s mind.



“the idea of every country about every country be necessarily a little

stale. what England be, what England think, and what England be

become, ben’t on record. what be on record be the England of the

’eighty and ’ninety.”



“now, that’s very true,” say the near American. “and you can apply it

right away, with a hundred per cent. or so add, to all your idea of

America.”



as a consequence both side in this leisurely discussion find how

widely they have be out in their idea about each other. Peter

discover America as not nearly so commercial and individualistic as he

have suppose; he have be altogether ignorant of the increase part the

university be play in her affair; the Americans be equally

edify to find that the rampant imperialism of Cecil Rhodes and his

group no long rule the british imagination. “if thing be so,” say

the diplomatist in the nearer bed, “then I seem to see a lot more come

together between we than I’ve ever be dispose to think possible

before. if you British ben’t so keen over this king business——”



“_Keen!_” say Peter.



“if you don’t hold you be it and unapproachable—in the way of Empires.”



“the Empire be yours for the asking,” say Peter.



“then all there be between we be the Atlantic—and that grow narrow

every year. we’re the same people.”



“so long as we have the same language and literature,” say Peter....



from these talk onward Peter may be regard as have a Foreign Policy

of his own.





                                  § 17



and it be in this hospital that Peter first clearly decide to become

personally responsible for the reconstruction of the British Empire.



this decision be precipitate by the sudden reappearance in his world

of Mir Jelalludin, the Indian whom he have once think unsuitable

company for Joan.



Peter have be doze when Jelalludin appear. he find he sit

beside the bed, and stare at the neat and smile brown face, unable to

place he, and still less able to account for the uniform he be

wear. for Jelalludin be wear the uniform of the french aviator,

and across his breast he wear four palm.



“I have the pleasure of know you at Cambridge,” say Mir Jelalludin in

his indian staccato. “Cha’med I be of use to you.”



an explanatory Frenchman stand beside the Indian dab his finger on

the last of Jelalludin’s decoration. “he kill von Papen after your

crash,” say the Frenchman.



“you be that Frenchman——?” say Peter.



“in your fight,” say Mir Jelalludin.



“he’d have finish I,” say Peter.



“I finish _he_,” say the Indian, laugh with sheer happiness, and

show his beautiful tooth.



Peter contemplate the situation. he make a movement and be remind of

his bandage.



“I wish I could shake hand,” he say.



the Indian smile with a phantom malice in his smile.



Peter go bluntly to a question that have arise in his mind. “why

ben’t you in khaki?” he ask.



“the brish’ Gu’ment object to indian flyer,” say Mir Jelalludin. “I

try. but brish’ Gu’ment think fly beyond we. and bad for Prestige.

Prestige very important thing to brish’ Gu’ment. so I come to France.”



Peter continue to digest the situation.



“of course,” say Jelalludin, “no commission give in regular army to

Indians. brish’ soldier not allow to s’lute indian officer. not part

of the Great White Race. otherwise hundred of flyer could come from

India, hundred and hundred. we play cricket—good horseman. many indian

gentleman must be first-rate flying stuff. but Gu’ment say ’no.’”



he continue to smile more cheerfully than ever.



“hundred of juvenile Indians ready and willing to be kill for your

Empire”—he rub it in—“but—no, thank you. indo-european people we be,

aryan, more consanguineous than Jews or Japanese. ready to take our

place beside you.... well, anyhow, I rejoice to see that you be

recover to entire satisfaction. it be only when I descend after

the fight that I perceive that it be you, and it seem to I then

that you be very seriously injure. I be anxious. and mem’ries of

otha day. I feel I must see you.”



Peter and the young Indian look at one another.



“look here, Jelalludin,” he say, “I must apologize.”



“but why?”



“as part of the British Empire. no! don’t interrupt. I do. but, I say,

do they—do we really bar you—absolutely?”



“absolutely. not only from the air force, but from any commission at

all. the low little bazaar clerk from Clapham, who have get a

commission, be over our indian officer—over our prince. it be an

everlasting humiliation. necessary for Prestige.”



“the French have more sense, anyhow.”



“they take we on our merit.



“if I _have_ a british commission,” say Jelalludin, “I should be make

very uncomfortable. it be the way with british officer and gentleman.

the French be not so—particular.”



“at present,” say Peter, “I cann’t be move.”



“you improve.”



“but when I get up this be one of the thing I have to see to. you see,

Jelalludin, this Empire of our—your and mine—have get into the hand of

a gang of gory Old Fools. partly my negligence—as God say.”



“God?” say Jelalludin.



“oh, nothing! I mean we young man haven’t be give a proper grasp of

the indian situation. or any situation. no. this business of the

commission——! after all that you fellow have do here in France! it’s

disgraceful. you see, we don’t see or learn anything about India. even

at Cambridge——”



“you don’t see much of we there,” smile the Indian.



“I’m sorry,” say Peter.



“I don’t come to talk about this,” say Jelalludin, “it come out.”



“I’m glad it come out,” say Peter.



a pause.



“I mustn’t tire you,” say Mir Jelalludin, and rise to go.



Peter thank he for come.



“and your cha’ming sister?” ask the Indian, as if by an afterthought.



“Foster sister. she drive a big car about London,” say Peter....



Peter meditate profoundly upon that interview for some day.



then he try over the opinion of the Americans about India. but

Americans be of little help to the British about India. their simple

uncriticized colour prejudice cover all “Asiatics” except the

inhabitant of Siberia. they have a more than english ignorance of

ethnology, and Oswald have at least impart some fragment of that

important science to his ward. their work classification of mankind

be into Anglo-Saxons, Frenchmen, sheeny, Irishmen, Dutchmen, dago,

Chinks, Coloured People, and black Niggers. they esteem Mir Jelalludin

a Coloured Person. Peter have to fall back upon himself again.





                                  § 18



it contribute to the thoroughness of Peter’s thinking that it be some

time before he could be put into a position to read comfortably. and it

have to be record in the tooth of the dictate of sentiment and the

most sacred tradition of romance that the rôle play by both Joan and

Hetty in these meditation be secondary and incidental. it be an

attenuate and abstract Peter who lie in the french hospital, his chief

link of sense with life be a grow hunger; he think very much about

fate, pain, the nature of thing, and God, and very little about person

and personal incident—and so strong an effect have his dream that God

remained fix steadfastly in his mind as that same intellectual

non-interventionist whom he have visit in the fly-blow office. but

about God’s rankle repartee, “why don’t _you_ exert yourself?” there

be accumulate a new conception, the conception of Man take hold of

the world, unassiste by God but with the acquiescence of God, and in

fulfilment of some remote, incomprehensible plan on the part of God.

probably Peter in think this be follow one of the most ancient

and well-beat of speculative path, but it seem to he that it be a

new way of thinking. and he be Man. it be he who have to establish

justice in the earth, achieve unity, and rule first the world and then

the star.



he lie stare at the ceiling, and quite happy now that heal and

habituation have free he from positive pain, think out how he be to

release and co-operate with his India, which have invariably the face of

Mir Jelalludin, how he be to reunite himself with his brother in

America, and how the wall and division of mankind, which look so high

and invincible upon the ground and so trivial from twelve thousand foot

above, be to be subdue to such great end.



it be only as the blood corpuscle multiply inside he that Peter

cease to be constantly man contemplate his destiny and Races and

Empires, and for more and more hour in the day shrank to the dimension

and natural warmth of Mr. Peter Stubland contemplate convalescence in

Blighty. he become eager first for the dear old indulgent and welcome

house at Pelham Ford, and then for prowl and walk and gossip with Joan

and Oswald, and then, then for London and a little “fun.” Life be

ebb back into what be understand to be the low nature, and be

certainly the most intimate and distinctive substance of Mr. Peter

Stubland. his correspondence become of very great interest to he.

certain letter from Joan, faint but pursuing, have reach he, those

letter over which Joan have sit like a sonneteer. he read they and

warm to they. he think what luck it be that he have a Joan to be the

good of sister to he, to be even more than a sister. she be the good

friend he have, and it be jolly to read so plainly that he be her good

friend. he would like to do work with Joan well than with any man he

know. drive a car ben’t half good enough for she. some day he’d be

able to show she how to fly, and he would. it would be great fun go

up with Joan on a double control and let she take over. there must

be girl in the world who would fly as well as any man, or well.



he scribble these idea in his first letter to Joan, and they please

she mightily. to fly with Peter would be surely to fly straight into

heaven.



and mix up with Joan’s letter be other that he presently sort

out from hers and put apart, as though even letter might hold

inconvenient communion. for the most part they come from Hetty Reinhart,

and display the emotion of a consciously delicious female enamour

and enslave by one of the hero of the air. she have dream of he

come in through the skylight of her studio, Lord Cupid visit his

poor little Psyche—“but it be only the moonlight,” and she think of

he now always with great overshadow wing. sometimes they be great

white wing that beat above she, and sometimes they be thrillingly

soft and exquisite wing, like the wing of the people in _Peter

Wilkins_. she send he a copy of _Peter Wilkins_, book belove by Poe

and all reader of the fantastic. then come the news of his smash. she

have be clever enough to link it with the death of von Papen, the Hun

Matador. “be that your fight, dear peterkin? do you _begin_ on

Goliath?” as the cordial of recovery race through Peter’s vein there

be phase when the thought of visit the yield fair, Jovelike and

triumphant in wing glory, become not simply attractive but insistent.

but he write to Hetty modestly, “they’ve clip one wing for ever.”



and so in a quite artless and inevitable way Peter find his first

leave, when the british hospital have do with he, mortgage up to hilt

almost equally to dear friend Joan and to Cleopatra Hetty.



the young man only realize the duplicity of his nature and the

complication of his position as the hospital boat beat its homeward way

across the Channel. the night be smooth and fine, with a high full moon

which somehow suggest Hetty, and with a cloud scheme of great beauty

and distinction that have about it a flavour of Joan. and as he meditate

upon these complication that have be happen in his more personal

life while his attention have be still largely occupy with divinity

and politic, he be hail by an unfamiliar voice and address as

“Simon Peter.” “excuse I,” say the stout young officer tuck up

warmly upon the next deck chair between a pair of crutch, “but ben’t

you Simon Peter?”



Peter have hear that name somewhere before. “my name’s stubland,” he

say.



“ah! stubland! I forget your surname. of High Cross School?”



Peter peer and see a round fair face that slowly recall memory.

“wait a moment!” say Peter.... “_Ames!_”



“guess it in one. Probyn and I be chum.”



“what have you get?” say Peter.



“Leg below the knee off, damn it!” say Ames. “one month at the front.

not much of a career. but they say they do you a leg now well than

reality. but I’d have like to have bat the pant of the unspeakable

Hun a bit more before I retire. what have you get?”



“wrist chiefly and shoulder-blade. air fight. after six week.”



“do you out?”



“for fly, I’m afraid. but there’s lot of ground job. and

anyhow—home’s pleasant.”



“yes,” say Ames. “home’s pleasant. but I’d like to have get a scalp of

some sort. doubt if I kill a single Hun. D’you remember Probyn at

school?—a dark chap.”



Peter find he still hate Probyn. “I remember he,” he say.



“he’s kill. he get the M.M. and the V.C. he wouldn’t take a

commission. he be sergeant-major in my battalion. I just see he, but

I’ve hear about he since. his man worship he. Queer how man come

out in a new light in this war.”



“how be he kill?” ask Peter.



“in a raid. he be with a bombing party, and three man straggle up a

sap and get corner. he’d take two machine-gun and they’d use most

of the bomb, and his officer be knock out, so he send the rest of

his party back with the stuff and go to fetch his other man. one have

be hit and the other two be think of surrendering when he come

back to they. he stand right up on the parado, they say, and slung

bomb at the Germans, a whole crowd of they, until they go back. his

two chap get the wound man out and carry he back, and leave he

still sling bomb. he’d do that. he’d stand right up and bung bomb

at they until they seem to lose their head. then he seem to have

spot that this particular bunch of Germans have go back into a sort

of blind alley. he be very quick at spot a situation, and he

follow they up, and the sheer blank recklessness of it seem to have

put their wind up absolutely. they’d get bomb and there be an officer

with they. but they hold up their hand—nine of they. panic. he get they

right across to our trench before the searchlight find he, and the

Germans get he and two of their own chap with a machine-gun. that be

just the last thing he do. he’d be go about for month do

stunt like that—sort of charm life business. the way he sling bomb,

they say, amount to genius.



“they say he’d let his hair grow long—perfect golliwog. when I see he

it certainly _be_ long, but he’d get it plaster down. and there’s a

story that he use to put white on his face like a clown with a great

red mouth reach from ear to ear—— yes, paint on. it’s put the Huns’

wind up something frightful. come suddenly on a chap like that in the

glare of a searchlight or a flare.”



“queer end,” say Peter.



“Queer chap altogether,” say Ames....



he think for a time, and then go on to philosophize about Probyn.



“clever chap he be,” say Ames, “but an absolute failure. of course old

High Cross ben’t anything very much in the way of a school, but

whatever there be to be learn there he learn. he be the only one of

we who ever get hold of speak French. I hear he over there—regular

fluent. and he’d get a memory like an encyclopædia. I always say he’d

do wonder....”



Ames pause. “sex be his downfall,” say Ames.



“I see a lot of he altogether, off and on, right up to the time of the

war,” say Ames. “my people be furniture people, you know, in Tottenham

Court Road, and his be in the public-house fitting line—in Highbury.

we go about together. I see he make three or four good start, but

there be always some trouble. I suppose most of we be a bit—well,

_keen_ on sex; most of we young man. but he be ravenous. even at

school. always on it. always think about it. I could tell you story

of he.... Rum place that old school be, come to think of it. they leave

we about too much. I don’t know how far you——.... of course you be

about the most innocent thing that ever come to High Cross School,” say

Ames.



“yes,” say Peter. “I suppose I be.”



“curious how it gnaw at you once it’s set go,” say Ames....



Peter make a noise that might have be assent.



Ames remained think for a time, watch the swish and surge of the

black Channel water. Peter pursue their common topic in silence.



“what’s the sense of it?” say Ames, plunge towards philosophy.



“it’s the system on which life go—on this planet,” Peter contribute,

but Ames have not have a biological training, and be unprepared to take

that up.



“too much of it,” say Ames.



“over-sex,” say Peter.



“whether one ought to hold oneself in or let oneself go,” say Ames.

“but perhaps these thing don’t bother you?”



Peter ben’t dispose towards confidence with Ames. “I’m moderate in

all thing,” he say.



“lucky chap! I’ve worry about this business no end. one don’t want

to use up all one’s life like a blessed monkey. there’s other thing in

life—if only this everlasting want-a-girl want-a-woman would let one get

at they.”



his voice at Peter’s shoulder cease for a while, and then resume.

“it’s the good chap, seem to I, who get it worst. chap with

imagination, I mean, man of vitality. take old Probyn. he could have

do anything—anything. and he be eat up. like a fever....”



Ames go down into a black silence for a couple of minute or more, and

come up again with an astonishing resolution. “I shall marry,” he say.



“got the lady?” ask Peter.



“near enough,” say Ames darkly.



“St. Paul’s method,” say Peter.



“I be talk to a fellow the other day,” say Ames. “he’d get a

curious idea. something in it perhaps. he say that every one be

clean-minded and romantic, that’s how he put it, about sixteen or

seventeen. even if you’ve be a bit dirty as a schoolboy you sort of

clean up then. adolescence, in fact. and he say you ought to fall in

love and pair off then. kind of Romeo and Juliet business. first love

and all that.”



“Juliet ben’t exactly Romeo’s first love,” say Peter.



“young beggar!” say Ames. “but, anyhow, that be only by way of

illustration. his idea be that we’d sort of put off marriage and all

that sort of thing later and later. twenty-eight. thirty. thirty-five

even. and that put we wrong. we kind of curdle and ferment. spoilt

with keeping. lark about with girl we don’t care for. demi-vierge

stunt and all that. got promiscuous. let anything do. his idea be

you’d get to pair off with a girl and look after she, and she look after

you. and keep faith. and stop all stray muck about. ’settle down to a

healthy sexual peace,’ he say.”



Ames pause. “something in it?”



“ever read the Life of Lord Herbert of Cherbury?” ask Peter.



“never.”



“he work out that theory quite successfully. marry before he go up

to Oxford. there’s a lot in it. Sex. delay. fret. overflow.

get experimental and nasty.... but that don’t exhaust the

question. the Old Experimenter sit there——”



“_what_ experimenter?”



“the chap who start it all. there’s no way yet of fit it up

perfectly. we’ve get to make it fit.”



Peter be so interested that he forget his aversion from confide in

Ames. the subject carry he on.



“any healthy young man,” Peter generalize, “could be happy and

content with any pretty girl, so far as love-making go. it don’t

strike you—as a particularly recondite art, eh? but you’ve get to be in

love with each other generally. that’s more difficult. you’ve get to

talk together and go about together. in a complicated artificial world.

the sort of woman it’s easy and pleasant to make love to, may not be the

sort of woman you really think splendid. it’s easy to make love to a

woman you don’t particularly respect, who’s good fun, and all that.

which be just the reason why you wouldn’t be tie up with she for ever.

no.”



“so we worship the angel and marry the flapper,” say Ames.... “I

shalln’t do that, anyhow. the fact be, one need a kind of motherliness in

a woman.”



“by make love too serious, we’ve make it not serious enough,” say

Peter with oracular profundity, and then in reaction, “oh! _I_ don’t

know.”



“_i_ don’t know,” say Ames.



“which don’t in the least absolve we from the necessity of go on

live right away.”



“I shall marry,” say Ames, in a tone of unalterable resolve.



they lapse into self-centre meditation....



“why! there’s the coast,” say Ames suddenly. “quite close, too. _Dark._

do you remember, before the war, how the light of Folkestone use to

run along the top there like a necklace of fire?”





                                  § 19



the power that be set over Peter’s life play fast and loose with

he in the matter of leave. they treat he at first as though he be a

rare and precious hero—who have to be save from his friend. they put

he to mend at Broadstairs, and while he be at Broadstairs he have three

visit from Hetty, whose day be free, and only one hasty Sunday

glimpse of Joan, who be much in demand at the Ministry of Munitions.

and Oswald could not come to see he because Oswald himself be a

casualty mend slowly at Pelham Ford. Hetty and Joan and return

health fire the mind of Peter with great expectation of the leave that

be to come. these expectation be, so to speak, paint in panel.

forgetful of the plain fact that a Joan who be not available at

Broadstairs would also not be available at Pelham Ford, the panel

devote to the latter place invariably include Joan as a principal

figure, they represent leave as a glorious escape from war to the

space, the sunshine, the endlessness of such a summer vacation as only

schoolboy know. he would be climb tree with Joan, “muck about”

in the boat with Joan, lie on the lawn just on the edge of the

cedar’s shadow with Joan, nibble stem of grass. the London scene

be narrow and more intense. he want the glitter and fun of

lunch in the Carlton grill-room or dining at the Criterion, in the

company of a tremendous hat and transparent lace, and there be scene

in Hetty’s studio, quite a lot of fantastic and elemental scene in

Hetty’s studio.



but the Germans have wipe those day of limitless leisure out of the

life of mankind. even our schoolboy stay up in their holiday now to

make munition. Peter have scarcely clamber past the approval of a

medical board before active service snatch he again. he be want

urgently. Peter be no good as a pilot any more, it be true; his right

wrist be doom to be stiff and weak henceforth, and there be queer

little limitation upon the swing of his arm, but the power have

suddenly discover other use for he. there be more of Peter still

leave than they have assume at first. for one particular job, indeed, he

be just the man they need. they dock he a wing—it seem in

mockery of the state of his arm—and replace the two wing that have

adorn he by one attach to the letter O, and they mark he down to

join “balloon” at the early possible moment, for just then they be

develop kite balloon very fast for artillery observation, and be

eager for any available man. Peter be sling out into freedom for

one-and-twenty day, and then tell to report himself for special

instruction in the new work at Richmond Park.



one-and-twenty day! he have never be so inordinately greedy for life,

free to live and go as you please, in all his day before. something

must happen, he be resolve, something bright and intense, on every one

of those day. he snatch at both side of life. he go down to Pelham

Ford, but he have a little list of engagement in town in his pocket.

Joan be not down there, and never before have he realize how

tremendously absent Joan could be. and then at the week-end she couldn’t

come. there be french and british G.H.Q. bigwig to take down to some

experiment in Sussex, but she couldn’t even explain that, she have to

send a telegram at the eleventh hour: “_week-end impossible._” to Peter

that seem the most brutally offhand evasion in the world. Peter be

disappoint in Pelham Ford. it be altogether different from those

hospital dream; even the weather, to begin with, be chilly and

unsettled. Oswald have have a set-back with his knee, and have to keep his

leg up on a deck chair; he could only limp about on crutch. he seem

old and more distant from Peter than he have ever be before; Peter

be obsess by the idea that he ought to be treat with solicitude,

and a further gap be open between they by Peter’s subaltern habit of

say “Sir” instead of the old familiar “Nobby.” Peter sit beside the

deck chair through long and friendly, but very impatient hour; and he

talk all the fly shop he could, and Oswald talk of his Africans,

and they go over the war and newspaper again and again, and they

revert to Africa and fly shop, and presently they sit through

several silence, and at the end of one of they Oswald inquire: “have

you ever play chess, Peter—or piquet?”



now chess and piquet be very good pastime in their way, but not good

enough for the precious afternoon of a very animate and greedy young

man keenly aware that they be probably his last holiday afternoon on

earth.



Sentiment require that Peter should have go to London and devote

himself to adorn the marginal freedom of Joan’s day. he do do this

once. he take she out to dinner to Jules’, in Jermyn Street; he do she

well there; but she be a very tired Joan that day; she have drive a

good hundred and fifty mile, and, truth to tell, in those day Peter

do not like Joan and she do not like herself in London, and more

especially in smart London restaurant. they sit a little aloof from one

another, and about they all the young couple warm to another and

smile. she jar with this atmosphere of meretricious ease and

indulgence. she have have no time to get back to Hampstead and change; she

be at a disadvantage in her uniform. it become a hair shirt, a Nessus

shirt as the evening proceed. it emphasize the barrier of seriousness

between they cruelly. she be a policeman, a prig, the harsh thing in

life; all those pretty little cocotte and flirt, with their little

soft brightness and adornment, must be glance at her coarse,

unreveale garment and note she for the fool she be. she feel ugly

and ungainly; she be far too much torment by love to handle herself

well. she could get no swing and forgetfulness into the talk. and about

Peter, too, be a reproach for she. he talk of work and the war—as if

in irony. and his eye wander. naturally, his eye wander.



“good-night, old Peter,” she say when they part.



she lie awake for two hour, exasperate, miserable beyond tear,

because she have not say: “good night, old Peter _dear_.” she have

intend to say it. it be one of her prepared effect. but she be a

weary and a frozen young woman. Duty have rob she of the energy for

love. why have she let thing come to this pass? Peter be her business,

and Peter alone. she damn the Woman’s Legion, Woman’s part in the War,

and all the rest of it, with fluency and sincerity.



and while Joan waste the hour of sleep in this fashion Peter be also

awake thinking over certain scheme he have discuss with Hetty that

afternoon. they involve some careful and deliberate lying. the idea be

that for the purpose of Pelham Ford he should terminate his leave on

the fourteenth instead of the twenty-first, and so get a clear week

free—for life in the vein of Hetty.



he lie fret, and the hot greed of youth persuade he, and the clean

honour of youth reproach he. and though he know the way the decision

would go, he toss about and damn as heartily as Joan.



he could not remember if at Pelham Ford he have set a positive date to

his leave, but, anyhow, it would not be difficult to make out that there

have be some sort of urgent call.... it could be do.... the

alternative be Piquet.



Peter return to Pelham Ford and put his little fabric of lie upon

Oswald without much difficulty. then at the week-end come Joan,

rejoice. she come into the house tumultuously; she have catch a train

early than the one they have expect she to come by. “I’ve get all

next week. seven day, Petah! never mind how, but I’ve get it. I’ve get

it!”



there be a suggestion as of some desperate battle away there in London

from which Joan have snatch these fruit of victory. she be so

radiantly glad to have they that Peter recoil from an immediate reply.



“I don’t seem to see you in London somehow,” say Joan. “I don’t think

you be really there. let’s have a look at you, old Petah. Tenshun!...

lift the arm.... rotate the arm.... it ben’t so bad, Petah, after all.

be tennis possible?”



“I’d like to try.”



“boat certainly. no reason why we shouldn’t have two or three long

walk. a week’s a long time nowadays.”



“but I have to go back on Monday,” say Peter.



Joan stand stock still.



“Pity, ben’t it?” say Peter weakly.



“but why?” she ask at last in a little flat voice.



“I have to go back.”



“but your leave——?”



“end on Monday,” lie Peter.



for some moment it look as though Joan mean to make that last

week-end a black one. “that don’t give we much time together,” say

Joan, and her voice which have soar now crawl the earth.... “I’m

sorry.”



just for a moment she hung, a dark and wound Joan, downcast and

thoughtful; and then turn and put her arm akimbo, and look at he

and smile awry. “well, old Peter, then we’ve get to make the good use

of our time. it’s your Birf Day, sort of; it’s your Bank Holiday, dear;

it’s every bless thing for you—such time as we have together. before

they take you off again. I think they’re greedy, but it cann’t be help.

can it, Peter?”



“it cann’t be help,” say Peter. “no.”



they pause.



“what shall we do?” say Joan. “the program’s get to be cut down. shall

we still try tennis?”



“I want to. I don’t see why this wrist——” he hold it out and rotate it.



“good old arm!” say Joan, and run a hand along it.



“I’ll go and change these breech and thing,” say Joan. “and get

myself female. god, Peter! the craving to get into clothe that be

really flexible and translucent!”



she go to the staircase and then turn on Peter.



“Peter,” she say.



“yes.”



“go out and stand on the lawn and tighten up the net. now.”



“why?”



“then I can see you from my window while I’m change. I don’t want to

waste a bit of you.”



she go up four step and stop and look at he over her shoulder.



“I want as much as I can get of you, Petah,” she say.



“I wish I’d know about that week,” say Peter stupidly.



“_exactly!_” say Joan to herself, and flit up the staircase.





                                  § 20



Joan, Mrs. Moxton perceive that afternoon, have a swift and angry fight

with her summer wardrobe. both the pink gingham and the white drill have

be try on and fling aside, and she have decide at last upon a rather

jolly warm blue figure voile with a belt of cherry-colour ribbon that

suit her brown skin and black hair well than those weak support.

she have evidently open every drawer in her room in a hasty search for

white silk stocking.



when she come out into the sunshine of the garden Peter’s eye tell she

she have guess the right costume.



Oswald be stand up on his crutch and smile, and Peter be

throw up a racquet and catch it again with one hand.



“thank God for a left-handed childhood!” say Peter. “I’m go to smash

you, Joan.”



“I forget about that,” say Joan. “but you ben’t go to smash I, old

Petah.”



when tea-time come they be still fight the seventh vantage game,

and Joan be up.



they come and sit at the tea-table, and Joan as she pour the tea

reflect that a young man in white flannel, flush and a little out

of breath, with his white silk shirt wide open at the neck, be a more

beautiful thing than the most beautiful woman alive. and her dark eye

look at the careless and exhausted Peter, that urgent and insoluble

problem, while she count, “Twenty-four, thirty-six, forty-one—about

forty-one hour. how the devil shall I do it?”



it ben’t to be do at tennis anyhow, and she lose the next three game

run without apparent effort, and take Peter by the arm and walk

he about the garden, discourse on fly. “I must teach you to fly,”

say Peter. “often when I’ve be up alone I’ve think, ’some day I’ll

teach old Joan.’”



“that’s a promise, Petah.”



“sure,” say Peter, who have not suffer next to two Americans for

nothing.



“I’ve get it in writing,” say Joan.



“I’d rather learn from you than any one,” say she.



Peter discourse of stunt....



they spend a long golden time revisit odd corner in which they have

play together. they go down the village and up to the church and

round the edge of the wood, and there they come upon and devour a lot

of blackberry, and then they go down to the mill pond and sit for a

time in Baker’s boat. then they get at cross purpose about dress for

there be pretty arm in the world than Hetty Reinhart’s, and a

well model neck and shoulder. she have a new dress of ivory silk

with a broad belt of velvet that echo the bright softness of her eye

and hair. but Peter would not let she dress. he do not want to dress

himself. “and you couldn’t look pretty, Joan, than you do in that blue

thing. it’s so _like_ you.”



and as Joan couldn’t explain that the frock keep she a jolly girl he

know while the dress would have show he the beautiful woman he have to

discover, she lose that point in the game. and tomorrow be Sunday, when

Pelham Ford after the good custom of England never dress for dinner.



afterwards she think how easily she might have overrule he.



Joan’s plan for the evening be dash by this costume failure. she

have rely altogether on the change of personality into something rich

and strange, that the ivory dress be to have work. she could do

nothing to develop the situation. everything seem to be help to

they play Auction Bridge with a dummy. she have mean to sit up with

Peter, but it don’t work out like that.



“good night, Petah dear,” she say outside her bedroom door with the

candlelight shine red between the finger of her hand.



“good night, old Joan,” he say from his door-mat, with an infinite

friendliness in his voice.



you cannot kiss a man good night suddenly when he be fifteen yard

away....



she close the door behind she softly, put down her candle, and begin to

walk about her room and swear in an entirely unladylike fashion. then

she go over to the open window, wring her hand. “how be I to _do_

it?” she say. “how be I to _do_ it? the situation’s preposterous. he’s

mine. and I might be his sister!”



“shall I make a declaration?”



“I suppose Hetty do.”



but all the cunning of Joan be unavaile against the invisible

barrier to passion between herself and Peter. they spend a long Sunday

of comradeship, and courage and opportunity alike fail. the dawn on

Monday morning find a white and haggard Joan pace her floor, half

minded to attempt a desperate explanation forthwith in Peter’s bedroom

with a suddenly awakened Peter. only her fear of shock he and

fail restrain she. she rave. she indulge in absurd soliloquy

and still absurder prayer. “oh, God, give I my Peter,” she pray.

“__give I my Peter!__”





                                  § 21



Monday break clear and fine, with a September freshness in the sunshine.

Breakfast be an awkward meal; Peter be constrain, Oswald be worry

by a sense of advice and counsel not give; Joan feel the situation

slip from her helpless grasp. it be with a sense of relief that at

last she put on her khaki overcoat to drive Peter to the station. “this

be the end,” sing in Joan’s mind. “this be the end.” she glance at the

mirror in the hall and see that the fur collar be not unfriendly to her

white neck and throat. she be in despair, but she do not mean to let

it become an unbecoming despair—at least until Peter have depart. the

end be still incomplete. she have something stern and unpleasant to say

to Peter before they part, but she do not mean to look stern or

unpleasant while she say it. Peter, she note with a gleam of

satisfaction, be in low spirit. he be sorry to go. he be ashamed of

himself, but also he be sorry. that be something, at any rate, to have

achieve. but he be go—nevertheless.



she bring round the little Singer to the door. she start the engine

with a competent swing and get in. the maid come with Peter’s

portmanteau and belonging. “this be the end,” say Joan to herself,

touch her accelerator and with her hand ready to release the brake.

“all aboard?” say Joan aloud.



Peter shake hand with Oswald over the side of the car, and glance from

he to the house and back at he. “I wish I could stay long, sir,”

say Peter.



“there’s many day to come yet,” say Oswald. for we never mention death

before death in war time; we never let ourselves think of it before it

come or after it have come.



“so long, Nobby!”



“good luck, Peter!”



Joan put the car into gear, and steer out into the road.



“the water-splash be low than ever I’ve see it,” say Peter.



they run down the road to the station almost in silence. “these poplar

have get a touch of autumn in they already,” say Peter.



“it’s an early year,” say Joan.



“the end, the end!” sing the song in Joan’s brain. “but I’ll tell he

all the same.”...



but she do not tell he until they could hear the sound of the

approach train that be to cut the thread of everything for Joan.

they walk together up the little platform to the end.



“I’m sorry you’re go,” say Joan.



“I’m infernally sorry. if I’d know you’d get this week——”



“would that have alter it?” she say sharply.



“no. I suppose it wouldn’t,” he fence, just in time to save himself.



the rattle of the approach train grow suddenly loud. it be round the

bend.



Joan speak in a perfectly even voice. “I know you have be lie,

Peter. I have know it all this week-end. I know your leave last until

the twenty-first.”



he stare at she in astonishment.



“there be a time.... it’s to think of all this dirt upon you that hurt

most. the lie, the dodge, the shuffling meanness of it. from _you_....

whom _I love_.”



a gap of silence come. to the old porter twelve yard off they seem

entirely well-behave and well-discipline young people, say nothing

in particular. the train come in with a sort of wink under the bridge,

and the engine and foremost carriage run past they up the platform.



“I wish I could explain. I don’t know—— the fact be I get entangle in

a sort of promise....”



“_Hetty!_” Joan jerk out, and “there’s an empty first for you.”



the train stop.



Peter put his hand on the handle of the carriage door.



“you go to London—like a puppy that roll in dirt. you go to beastliness

and vulgarity.... you’d well get in, Peter.”



“but look here, Joan!”



“_get_ in!” she scold to his hesitation, and stamp her foot.



he get in mechanically, and she close the door on he and turn the

handle and stand hold it.



then still speak evenly and quietly, she say: “you’re a blind fool,

Peter. what sort of love can that—that—that miscellany give you, that I

couldn’t give? have I no life? have I no beauty? be you afraid of I?

don’t you see—don’t you _see?_ you go off to _that!_ you trail yourself

in the dirt and you trail my love in the dirt. before a female hack!...



“_look_ at I!” she cry, hold her hand apart. “think of I

tonight.... _your!_ your for the taking!”



the train be move.



she walk along the platform to keep pace with he, and her eye hold

his. “Peter,” she say; and then with amazing quiet intensity: “you

_damn_ fool!”



she hesitate on the verge of say something more. she come towards

the carriage. it ben’t anything pleasant that she have in mind, to judge

by her expression.



“stand away please, miss!” say the old porter, hurry up to

intervene. she abandon that last remark with an impatient gesture.



Peter sit still. the end of the station run by like a scene in a

panorama. her Medusa face have slide away to the edge of the picture that

the window frame, and vanish.



for some second he be too amazed to move.



then he get up heavily and stick his head out of the window to stare at

Joan.



Joan be stand quite still with her hand in the side pocket of she

khaki overcoat; she be stand straight as a rod, with her heel

together, look at the recede train. she never move....



neither of these two young people make a sign to each other, which be

the first odd thing the old porter note about they. they just stare.

by all the rule they should have wave handkerchief. the next odd

thing be that Joan stare at the bend for half a minute perhaps after

the train have altogether go, and then try to walk out to her car by

the little white gate at the end of the platform which have be disuse

and nail up for three year....





                                  § 22



after Oswald have see the car whisk through the gate into the road, and

after he have rest on his crutch stare at the gate for a time, he

have hobble back to his study. he want to work, but he find it

difficult to fix his attention. he be think of Joan and Peter, and

for the first time in his life he be wonder why they have never

fall in love with each other. they seem such good company for each

other....



he be still engage upon these speculation half an hour or so later,

when he hear the car return and presently see Joan go past his window.

she be flushed, and she be stare in front of she at nothing in

particular. he have never see Joan look so unhappy. in fact, so

strong be his impression that she be unhappy that he doubt it, and

he go to the window and crane out after she.



she be go straight up towards the arbour. with a slight hurry in her

step. she have her fur collar half turn up on one side, her hand be

deep in her pocket, and something about her dog walk remind he of

some long-forget moment, year ago it must have be, when Joan, in

hot water for some small offence, have be send indoor at the

Ingle-Nook.



he limp back to his chair and sit think she over.



“I wonder,” he say at last, and turn to his work again....



there be no getting on with it. half an hour later he accept defeat.

“Peter have knock we all crook,” he say. “there’s no work for

today.”



he would go out and prowl round the place and look at the rose. perhaps

Joan would come and talk. but at the gate he be amazed to encounter

Peter.



it be Peter, hot and dusty from a walk of three mile, and carry his

valise with an aching left arm. there be a look of defiance in the eye

that stare fiercely out from under the perspiration-mat hair upon

his forehead. he seem to find Oswald’s appearance the complete

confirmation of the most disagreeable anticipation. thought of panic

and desertion flash upon Oswald’s mind.



“Good God, Peter!” he cry. “what bring you back?”



“I’ve come back for another week,” say Peter.



“but your leave’s up!”



“I tell a lie, sir. I’ve get another week.”



Oswald stare at his ward.



“I’m sorry, sir,” say Peter. “I’ve be make a fool of myself. I

think well of it. I get out of the train at Standon and walk back

here.”



“what do it mean, Peter?” say Oswald.



Peter’s eye be the most distressed eye he have ever see. “if you’d

just not ask, sir, now——”



it be a good thing to deal with one’s own blood in a crisis. Oswald,

rest thoughtfully on his crutch, leapt to a kind of understanding.



“I’m go to hop down towards the village, Peter,” say Oswald,

become casual in his manner. “I want some exercise.... if you’ll tell

every one you’re back.”



he indicate the house behind he by a movement of his head.



Peter be badly blow with haste and emotion. “thank you, sir,” he say

shortly.



Oswald step past he and stare down the road.



“Mrs. Moxton’s in the house,” he say without look at Peter again.

“Joan’s up the garden. see you when I get back, Peter.... Glad you’ve

get another week, anyhow.... so long....”



he leave Peter stand in the gateway.



Fear come upon Peter. he stand quite still for some moment, look at

the house and the cedar. he drop his valise at the front door and

mop his face. then he walk slowly across the lawn towards the

terrace. he want to shout, and find himself hoarse. then on the

first terrace he get out: “Jo-un!” in a flat croak. he have to cry again:

“Jo-un!” before it sound at all like the old style.



Joan become visible. she have come out of the arbour at the top of the

garden, and she be stand motionless, regard he down the vista of

the central path. she be white and rather dishevel, and she stand

quite still.



Peter walk up the step towards she.



“I’ve come back, Joan,” he say, as he draw near. “I want to talk to

you.... come into the arbour.”



he take her arm clumsily and lead she back into the arbour out of sight

of the house. then he drop her arm.



“Joan,” he say, “I’ve be the damnd of fool ... as you say.... I

don’t know why.”...



he stand before she awkwardly. he be tremble violently. he think he

be go to weep.



he could not touch she again. he do not dare to touch she.



then Joan spread out her arm straight and stand like a crucifix. she

face, which have be a dark stare, soften swiftly, become radiant,

dissolve into a dusky glow of tear and triumph. “oh! Petah my

_darle_,” she sob, and seize he and kiss he with tear-salt

lip and hug he to herself.



the magic barrier be smash at last. Peter hold she close to he and

kiss she....



it be the second time they have kiss since those black day at High

Cross school....





                                  § 23



those be year of swift marrying, and Peter be a young married man

when presently he be add to the number of that select company

attach to sausage-shape observation balloon who be send up in the

morning and pull down at night along the british front. he have have

only momentary snatch of matrimony before the front have call he

back to its own destructive interest, but his experience have banish

any linger vestige of his theory that there be one sort of woman you

respect and another sort you make love to. there be only one sort of

woman to love or respect, and that be Joan. he be altogether in love

with Joan, he be sure he have never be in love before, and he be now

also extravagantly in love with life. he want to go on with it, with a

passionate intensity. it seem to he that it be not only begin

for he, but for every one. Hitherto Man have be live _down there_,

down on those flat—for all the world be flat from the air. now, at

last, man be begin to feel how they might soar over all ancient

limitation.



occasionally he think of such thing up in his basket, sit like a

spectator in a box at a theatre, with the slow vast drama of the western

front spread out like a map beneath his eye, with half belgium and a

great circle of France in sight, the brown, ruin country on either

side of no Man’s Land, apparently lifeless, with its insane tangle of

trench and communicate way below, with the crumble heap of

ruin town and village scatter among canal and lake of flood

water, and pass insensibly into a green and normal-look landscape

to the west and east, where church still have tower and house roof,

and wood be lump and block of dark green, field manifestly

cultivate patch, and road white ribbon bar by the purple poplar

shadow. but these spectacular and speculative phase be rare. they

come only when a thin veil of haze make the whole spacious prospect

faint, so that beyond his more immediate circle Peter could see only the

broad outline of the land. give bad condition of the weather and he

would be too uncomfortable for philosophy; give well and he would be

too busy.



he sit on a canvas seat inside the square basket with his instrument

about he, or leant over the side scrutinize the detail of the

eastward landscape. upon his head, over his ear, he wear a telephone

receiver, and about his body be a rope harness that link he by a

rope to the silk parachute that be pack neatly in a little swinge

bucket over the side of his basket. under his hand be his map board,

repeat the shape of wood and water and road below. the telephone

wire that run down his mooring rope abolish any effect of isolation;

it link he directly to his winch on a lorry below, to a number of

battery commander, to an ascend series of headquarters; he could

always start a conversation if he have anything practical to say. he be,

in fact, an eye at the end of a tentacle thread, by mean of which the

british army watch its enemy. sometimes he have an illusion that he

be also a kind of brain. when distant visibility be good he would find

himself hover over the war as a player hang over a chessboard,

direct fire upon road movement or train movement, suspecting and

watch for undisclosed enemy battery, or direct counter-battery

fire. above he, green and voluminous, hang the great translucent lobe

of his gas bag, and the loose rope by which it be tow and hold upon

the ground sway and trail about his basket.



it be on one of his more slack afternoon that Peter fall think of
