how acutely he now desire to live. the wide world be full of sunshine,

but a ground haze make even the country immediately below he

indistinct. the enemy gunner be inactive, there come no elfin voice

through the telephone, only far away to the south gun butt and

shiver the tranquil air. there be a faint drift in the air rather

than a breeze, and the gas bag have fall into a long, lazy rhythmic

movement, so that sometimes he face due south and sometimes south by

east and so back. a great patch of flooded country to the north-east, a

bright mirror with a kind of bloom upon it, seem try with an

aimless persistency to work its way towards the centre of his field of

vision and never succeed.



for a time Peter have be preoccupy with a distant ridge far away to

the east, from which a long-range gun have recently take to shell the

kite balloon towards evening as they become clear against the bright

western sky. four time lately this new gun have get on to he, and this

clear and tranquil afternoon promise just the luminous and tranquil

sunset that favour these unpleasant activity. it be five hour to

sunset yet, but Peter could not keep his mind off that gun. it be a big

gun; perhaps a 42 centimetre; it be beyond any counter-battery

possibility, and it have get a new kind of shell that the Germans seem

to have invent for the particular discomfort of Peter and his kind. it

have a distinctive report, a loud _crack_, and then the “_whuff_” of high

explosive, and at every explosion it get near and near to its

target, with a quite uncanny certainty. it seem to learn more than any

gun should learn from each shot. it be this steadfast approach to a hit

that Peter dislike. that and the long pause after the shell have

start. far away he would see the flash of the gun amidst the ridge in

the darkle east. then would come a long, blank pause of expectation.

for all he could tell this might get he. then the whine of the shell

would become audible, grow louder and loud and low and low in

note; phee-whoo! _crack!_ _WHOOF!_ then Peter would get quite voluble to

the man at the winch below. he could let himself up, or go down a few

hundred foot, or they could shift his lorry along the road. until it be

dark he could not come down, for a kite balloon be a terribly visible

and helpless thing on the ground until it have be very carefully put to

bed. to come down in the daylight mean too good a chance for the nearer

german gun. so Peter, by instruct his winch to lower he or let he

up or shift, have to dodge about in a most undignified way, up and down

and backwards and sideways, while the big gun mark he and guess at

his next position. flash! “oh, damn!” say Peter. “another already!”



Silence. anticipation. then: phee—eee—eee—_whoo_. _crack!_ _WHOOF!_ a

rush of air would set the gas bag swinge. that be a near one!



“where _be_ I?” say Peter.



but that ben’t go to happen for hour yet. why meet trouble half

way? why be torment by this feeling of apprehension and danger in the

still air? why trouble because the world be quiet and seem to be

wait? why not think of something else? banish this war from the

mind.... be he more afraid nowadays than he use to be? Peter be

inclined to think that now he be more _systematically_ afraid. formerly

he have funk in streak and patch, but now he have a steady,

continuous dislike to all these risk and danger. he be get more

and more clearly an idea of the sort of life he want to lead and of

the thing he want to do. he be cease to think of existence as a

rather aimless series of adventure, and come to regard it as one

large consecutive undertaking on the part of himself and Joan. this

be hang up in the sky for Germans to shoot at seem to he to be a

very tiresome irrelevance indeed. he and Joan and everybody with

brain—include the misguided people who have make and be now fire

this big gun at he—ought to be set to work to get this preposterous

muddle of a world in order. “this sort of thing,” say Peter, address

the western front, his gas bag, and so much of the sky as it permit

he to see, and the universe generally, “be ridiculous. there be no

sense in it at all. none whatever.”



his dream of God, as a detached and aloof personage, have take a very

strong hold upon his imagination. or, perhaps, it would be truer to say

that his fevered mind in the hospital have give a caricature personality

to idea that have grow up in his mind as a natural consequence of his

training. he have go on with that argument; he go on with it now,

with a feeling that really he be just as much sit and talk in

that queer, untidy, out-of-the-way office as sway in a kite balloon,

six thousand foot above Flanders, wait to be shoot at.



“it be all very well to say ’exert yourself,’” say Peter. “but there be

that chap over there exert himself. and what he be do with all his

brain be just try to wipe my brain out of existence. just that. he

haven’t an idea else of what he be do. he have no notion of what he be

up to or what I be up to. and he haven’t the sense or ability to come

over here and talk about it to I. he’s there—at that—and he cann’t help

himself. and I’m here—and I cann’t help myself. but if I could only catch

he within counter battery range——!



“there’s no sense in it at all,” summarize Peter, after some moment of

grim reflection. “sense haven’t get into it.”



“be sense ever go to get into it?



“the curious thing about you,” say Peter, address himself quite

directly to his deity at the desk, “be that somehow, without ever

positively promise it or say anything plain and definite about it,

you yet manage to convey in an almost irresistible manner, that there be

go to be sense in it. you seem to suggest that my poor brain up here

and the brain of those chap over there, be, in spite of all

appearance to the contrary, up to something jointly that be go to

come together and make good some day. you hint it. and yet I don’t get a

scrap of sound, trustworthy reasoning to help I to accept that; not a

scrap. why should it be so? I ask, and you just keep on not say

anything. I suppose it’s a necessary thing, biologically, that one

should have a kind of optimism to keep one alive, so I’m not even

justify in my half conviction that I’m not be absolutely fool by

life....



“I admit that take for example Joan, there be something about Joan

that almost persuade I there must be something absolutely _right_

about thing—for Joan to happen at all. yet ben’t that again just

another biologically necessary delusion?... there you sit silent. you

seem to say nothing, and yet you soak I with a kind of answer, a sort

of shapeless courage....”



Peter’s mind rest on that for a time, and then begin again at another

point.



“I wonder,” say Peter, “if that chap get I tonight, what I shall

think—in the moment—after he have get I....”





                                  § 24



but the german gunner never get Peter, because something else get he

first.



he think he see a Hun aeroplane come over very high indeed to the

south of he, fifteen thousand foot up or more, a mere speck in the blue

blaze, and then the gas bag hide it and he dismiss it from his mind. he

be think that the air be grow clear, and that if this go on

gun would wake up presently and little voice begin to talk to he,

when he become aware of the presence and vibration of an aeroplane quite

close to he. he pull off his telephone receiver and hear the roar

of an engine close at hand. it be overhead, and the gas bag still hide

it. at the same moment the british anti-aircraft gunner begin a belate

fire. “damn!” say Peter in a brisk perspiration, and hasten to make

sure that his parachute rope be clear.



“perhaps he’s british,” say Peter, with no real hope.



“_pap, pap, pap!_” very loud overhead.



the gas bag sway and billow, and a wing with a black cross sweep

across the sky. “_pap, pap, pap._”



the gas bag wrinkle and crumple more and more, and a little streak of

smoke appear beyond its edge. the german aeroplane be now visible, a

hundred yard away, and banking to come round. he have fire the balloon

with tracer bullet.



the thing that Peter have to do and what he do be this. he have to step

up on to a little wood step inside his basket. then he have to put first

one foot and then the other on to another little step outside his

basket. this little step be about four inch wide by nine long. below

it be six thousand foot of emptiness, above the little tree and house

below. as he sway on the step Peter have to make sure that the rope

attach to his body be clear of all entanglement. then he have to step

off that little shelf, which be now swinge and slant with the

lurch basket to which it be attach, into the void, six thousand

foot above the earth.



he have not to throw himself or dive headlong, because that might lead to

entanglement with the rope. he have just to step off into pellucid

nothingness, hold his rope clear of himself with one hand. this rope

loop back to the little swinge bucket in which his fine silk

parachute be closely packed. he have see it pack a week ago, and he

wish now, as he stand on his step hold to his basket with one hand,

that he have watch the process more meticulously. he become aware that

the Hun, having dispose of the balloon, be now shoot at he. he do

not so much step off the little shelf as slip off as it heel over with

the swing of the basket. the first instant of a leap or fall make no

impression on the mind. for some second he be fall swiftly, foot

foremost, through the air. he scarcely note the faint snatch when the

twine, which hold his parachute in its basket, break. then his

consciousness begin to register again. he keep his foot tightly press

together. the air whistle by he, but he think that dream and talk

have much exaggerate the sensation of fall. he be too high as yet

to feel the rush of the ground towards he.



he seem to fall for an interminable time before anything more

happen. he be assail by doubt—whether the twine that keep the

parachute in its bucket would break, whether it would open. his rope

trail out above he.



still fall. why don’t the parachute open? in another ten second it

would be too late.



the parachute be not open. it be certainly not open. wrong

packing? he tug and jerk his rope, and try to shake and swing the

long silken fold that be follow his fall. why? why the devil——?



the rope seem to tighten abruptly. the harness tighten upon his

body. Peter gasp, sprawl and have the sensation of be haul up

back again into the sky....



it be all right, so far. he be now sway down earthward with a

diminish velocity beneath an open parachute. he be float over the

landscape instead of fall straight into it.



but the German have not do with Peter yet. he become visible beneath

the edge of Peter’s parachute, circle downward regardless of

anti-aircraft and machine-gun. “_pap, pap, pap, pap._” the bullet

burst and bang about Peter.



something kick Peter’s knee; something hit his neck; something rap

the knuckle of his wound hand; the parachute wince and go

sideways, slash and pierce. Peter drift down fast, helpless, his

angry eye upon his assailant, who vanish again, go out of sight as

he rise up above the edge of the parachute.



a storm of pain and rage break from Peter.



“do in!” shout Peter. “oh! my leg! my leg!



“I’m shoot to bit. I’m shoot to bloody bit!”



the tree top be near at hand. the parachute have acquire a rhythmic

swing and be fall more rapidly.



“and I’ve still get to land,” wail Peter, begin to cry like a

child.



he want to stop just a moment, just for one _little_ moment, before

the ground rush up to meet he. he want time to think. he don’t

know what to do with this dangle leg. it become a monstrous, painful

obstacle to landing. how be he to get a spring? he be bleed. he be

die. it be cruel. Cruel.



come the crash. hot iron, it seem, assail his leg and his shoulder

and neck. he crumple up on the ground in an agony, and the parachute,

with slow and elegant gesture, fold down on the top of his

flounder figure....



the gunner who run to help he find he, envelop in silk, bawl

and weep like a child of four in a passion of rage and fear, and

try repeatedly to stand up upon a blood-streak leg that give way as

repeatedly. “damn!” curse Peter in a stifle voice, plunge about like

a kitten in a sack. “damn you all! I tell you I _will_ use my leg. I

_will_ have my leg. if I bleed to death. oh! oh!... you fool—you lie

old _humbug_! you!”



and then he give a leap upward and forward, and faint and fall, and

lie still, with his head and body muffle in the silk fold of his

parachute.









                         CHAPTER the FOURTEENTH

                          OSWALD’S VALEDICTION





                                  § 1



it be the third of April in 1918, the Wednesday after Easter, and the

war have now last three year and eight month. it have become the

aching habit of the whole world. throughout the winter it have be for

the most part a great and terrible boredom, but now a phase of acute

anxiety be begin. the “Kaiser’s Battle” be rage in France; news

come through sparingly; but it be know that General Gough have lose

ten of thousand of prisoner, hundred of gun, and vast store of

ammunition and railway material. it be rumour that he have commit

suicide. but the standard of Tory England differ from those of Japan.

through ten sanguinary day, in a vaster Inkerman, the common man of

Britain, reinforce by the French, have fight and die to restore the

imperille line. it be by no mean certain yet that they have succeed.

it seem possible that the french and british army would be break

apart, and Amiens and Paris lose. Oswald’s mind be still dark with

apprehension.



the particular anxiety of this crisis accentuate the general worry

and inconvenience of the time, and deepen Oswald’s conviction of an

incredible incompetence in both the political and military leadership of

his country. in spite of every reason he have to the contrary, he have

continue hitherto to hope for some bright dramatic change in the course

of event; he have experience a continually recur disappointment

with each morning’s paper. his intelligence tell he that all the

inefficiency, the confusion, the cheap and bad government by press and

intrigue, be the necessary and inevitable consequence of a neglect of

high education for the past fifty year; these defect be now in the

nature of thing, almost as much as the bleakness of an English February

or the fog of a London November, but his english temperament have

refuse hitherto to accept the decision of his intelligence. now for the

first time he could see the possibility of an ultimate failure in the

war. to this low level of achievement, he perceive, a steadfast

contempt for thought and science and organization have bring Britain;

at this low level Britain have now to struggle through the war,

blundering, talk, and think confusedly, suffer

enormously—albeit so sound at heart. it be a humiliating realization.

at any rate she could still hope to struggle through; the hard-win

elementary education of the common people, the stout heart and sense of

the common people, save her gentlefolk from the fate of their brother

inefficient in Russia. but every day he fret afresh at the costly

and toilsome continuance of an effort that a little more courage and

wisdom in high place on the allied side, a little more knowledge and

clear thinking, might have bring to an entirely satisfactory close in

1917.



for a man of his age, wound, disappointed, and a chronic invalid,

there be considerable affliction in the steadily increase hardship

of the Fourth Year. a number of petty deprivation at which a healthy

man might have scoff, intensify his physical discomfort. there have

be a complete restriction of his supply of petrol, the automobile now

hang in its shed with its tyre remove, and the railway service to

London have be greatly reduce. he could not get up to London now to

consult book or vary his mood without a slow and crowded and fatigue

journey; he be more and more confine to Pelham Ford. he have be use

to read and work late into the night, but now his home be darken in

the evening and very cheerless; there be no carbide for the acetylene

installation, and a need for economy in paraffin. for a time he have be

out of coal, and unable to get much wood because of local difficulty

about cartage, and for some week he have have to sit in his overcoat and

read and write by candlelight. now, however, that distress have be

relieve by the belate delivery of a truckload of coal. and another

matter that may seem trivial in history, be by no mean trivial in

relation to his mood. in the spring of 1918 the food supply of Great

Britain be at its low point. Lord Rhondda be save the situation

at the eleventh hour. the rationing of meat have affect Oswald’s health

disagreeably. he have long ago acquire the habit of live upon chop

and cutlet and suchlike concentrated nourishment, and he find it

difficult to adapt himself now to the bulky insipidity of a diet that

be, for a time, almost entirely vegetarian. for even fish travel by

long route to Hertfordshire village. the frequent air raid of that

winter be also an add nervous irritation. in the precede year of

the war there have be occasional Zeppelin raid, the Zeppelins have be

audible at Pelham Ford on several occasion and once Hertford have

suffer from their bomb; but those expedition have end at last in a

series of disaster to the invader, and they have never involve the

uproar and tension of the Gotha raid that begin in the latter half of

1917. these latter raid have to be meet by an immense barrage of

anti-aircraft gun round London, a barrage which rattle every window at

Pelham Ford, light the sky with star shell, and continue intermittently

sometimes for four or five hour. Oswald would lie awake throughout that

thudding conflict, watch the distant star shell and searchlight

through the black tree bough outside his open window, and meditating

drearily upon the manifest insanity of mankind....



he be now walk up and down his lawn, wait until it should be time

to start for the station with Joan to meet Peter.



for Peter, convalescent again and no long fit for any form of active

service—he be lamed now as well as wing—be to take up a minor

administrative post next week at Adastral House, and he be come down

for a few day at Pelham Ford before carry his wife off for good to a

little service flat they have find in an adapt house in the Avenue

Road. they have decide not to live at the Ingle-Nook, although Arthur

have build it to become Peter’s home, but to continue the tenancy of

Aunts Phyllis and Phœbe. they do not want to disturb those two lady,

whose nervous system, by no mean stable at the good of time, be now

in a very shaken condition. Aunt Phyllis be keep busy restrain Aunt

Phœbe from inflict lengthy but obscure prophetic message upon most

of the prominent people of the time. to these daily activity aunt

Phœbe add an increase habit of sleep-walking that break the nightly

peace of Aunt Phyllis. she would wander through the moonlit living room

gesticulate strangely, and utter such phrase as “Blood! blood!

sea of blood! the multitudinous sea incarnadine”; or “murder most

foul!”



she have a fix idea that it be her business to seek out the Kaiser and

either scold he or kill he—or perhaps do both. she hold that it be

the duty of woman to assassinate. man might fight battle, it be their

stupid way; but surely woman be capable of directer thing. if some

woman be to kill any man who declare war directly he declare war,

there would be a speedy end to war. she could not, she say, understand

the inactivity of german wife and mother. she would spend hour over

her old school german grammar, with a view to write an “Open Letter to

German Womankind.” but her naturally rich and very allusive prose be

ill adapt to that sort of translation.



many over-sensitive people be suffer more or less as Aunt Phœbe be

suffer—from a sense of cruelty, wickedness, and disaster that

stagger their mind. they have live securely in a secure world; they

could not readjust. even for so sane a mind as Oswald’s, hamper as it

be by the new poison his recent wound have bring into his blood,

readjustment be difficult. he suffer greatly from insomnia, and from

a haunt apprehension of misfortune. his damaged knee would give he

bout of acute distress. sometimes it would seem to be well and he would

forget it. then it would become painfully lame by day and a neuralgic

pain at night. his mood seem always exaggerate now; either he be

too angry or too sorrowful or too hopeful. sometimes he experience

phase of blank stupidity, when his mind become unaccountably sluggish

and clumsy....



Joan be indoor now pack up a boxful of book that be to go with

she to the new home.



he be feel acutely—more acutely than he want to feel—that his

guardianship be at an end. Joan, who have be the mistress of his

house, and the voice that sing in it, the pretty plant that grow in it,

be go now—to return, perhaps, sometimes as a visitor—but never more

to be a part of it; never more to be its habitual presence. Peter, too,

be sever the rope, a long rope it have seem at time during the

last three year, that have tether he to Pelham Ford....



Oswald do not want to think now of his come loneliness. what he

want to think about be the necessity of round off their

relationship properly, of end his educational task with some sort of

account render. he feel he owe it to these young people and to

himself to tell they of his aim and of what he consider the whole of

this business of education amount to. he have to explain what have

help and what have prevent he. “a valediction,” he say. “a

valediction.” but he could not plan out what he have to say that morning.

he could not arrange his head, and all the while that he try to fix

his thought upon these topic, he be fill with uncontrollable

self-pity for the solitude ahead of he.



he be ashamed at these personal distress that he could not control.

he dislike himself for their quality. he do not like to think he be

think the thought in his mind. he walk up and down the lawn for a

time like a man who be be pester by uncongenial solicitation.



in spite of his intense affection for both of they, he be feel a

real jealousy of the happiness of these two young lover. he hate the

thought of lose Joan much more than he hate the loss of Peter. once

upon a time he have love Peter far more than Joan, but by imperceptible

degree his affection have turn over to she. in these war year he and

she have be very much together. for a time he have be—it be

grotesque, but true—actually in love with she. he have let himself

dream—. it be preposterous to think of it. a moonlight night have make

his brain swim.... at any rate, thank Heaven! she have never have a

suspicion....



she’d come now as a visitor—perhaps quite often. he ben’t go to lose

his Joan altogether. but each time she would come change, rather less

his Joan and rather more a new Joan—Peter’s Joan....



some day they’d have child, these two. Joan would sit over her child

and smile down at it. he know exactly how she would smile. and at the

thought of that smile Joan give place to dolly. out of the past there

jump upon he the memory of Peter bubble in a cradle on the sunny

verandah of the Ingle-Nook, and how he have remark that the very

sunshine seem make for this fortunate young man.



“it _be_ make for he,” dolly have say, with that faintly mischievous

smile of her.



how far off that seem now, and how vivid still! he could remember

dolly’s shadow on the rough-cast wall, and the very thing he have say

in reply. he have talk like a fool about the wonderful future of

Peter—and of the world. how long be that ago? five-and-twenty year?

(yes, Peter would be five-and-twenty in June.) how safe and secure the

european world have seem then! it seem to be loiter, lazily and

basely indeed, but certainly, towards a sort of materialist’s

millennium. and what a vast sham its security have be! he have call

Peter the “Heir of the Ages.” and the Heritage of the Ages have be

prepare even then to take Peter away from the work he have choose and

from all the sunshine and leisure of his life and to splinter his

shoulder-blade, smash his wrist, snap his leg-bone with machine-gun

bullet, and fle he aside, a hobbling, stiff, broken young man to

limp through the rest of life....





                                  § 2



that be what his mind have to lay hold of, that be what he have to talk

about, this process that have hold out such fair hope for Peter and have

in the end cripple he and come near to kill he and waste he

altogether. he have to talk of that, of an enormous collapse and breach

of faith with the young. the world which have seem to be the glowing

promise of an unprecedented education and upbringing for Peter and his

generation, the world that have be, so to speak, joint guardian with

himself, have default. this war be an outrage by the senior thing in

the world upon all the hope of the future; it be the parent send his

son through the fire to Moloch, it be the guardian go mad, it be

the lapse of all educational responsibility.



he have to keep his grasp upon that idea. by hold to that he could get

away from his morbidly intense wish to be personal and intimate with

these two. he love they and they love he, but what he want to say

be something quite beyond that.



what he have to talk about be Education, and Education alone. he have to

point out to they that their own education have be truncate, be rough

ended and partial. he have to explain why that be so. and he have to show

that all this vast disaster to the world be no more and no less than an

educational failure. the church and teacher and political form have

be insufficient and wrong; they have fail to establish idea strong

and complete enough and right enough to hold the will of man.

necessarily he have to make a dissertation upon the war. to talk of life

now be to talk of the war. the war now be human life. it have eat up

all free and independent living.



the war be an educational breakdown, that be his point; and in

education lie whatever hope there be for mankind. he have to say that to

they, and he have to point out how that idea must determine the form of

their life. he have to show the political and social and moral

conclusion involve in it. and he have to say what he want to say in a

large manner. _he have to keep his temper while he say it._



Oswald, limp slowly up and down his lawn in the April sunshine, with

a gnawing pain at his knee, have to underline, as it be, that last

proviso in his thought. that be the extreme difficulty of these urgent

and tragic time. the world be in a phase of intense, but swift,

tumultuous, and distract tragedy. the million be not suffer and

die in stateliness and splendour but in a vast uproar, amidst mud,

confusion, bickering, and incoherence indescribable. while it be

manifest that only great thinking, only very clear and deliberate

thinking, could give even the form of action that would arrest the

conflagration, it be nevertheless almost impossible for any one

anywhere to think clearly and deliberately, so universal and various

be the compulsion, confusion, and distress of the time. and even

the effect to see and state the issue largely, fevere Oswald’s brain.

he grow angry with the multitudinous thing that rob he of his

serenity.



“education,” he say, as if he call for help; “education.”



and then, collapse into wrath: “a land of uneducated blockhead!”



no! it be not one of his good morning. in a little while his step have

quicken and his face have flush. his hand clench in his pocket.

“a universal dulness of mind,” he whisper. “obstinacy....

inadaptability.... Unintelligent opposition.”



Broad generalization slip out of his mind. he begin to turn over one

disastrous instance after another of the shortness of mental range, the

unimaginative stupidity, the baseness and tortuousness of method, the

dull suspicion, class jealousy, and foolish conceit that have

cripple Britain through three and a half bitter year. with a vast

fleet, with enormous army, with limitless wealth, with the loyal

enthusiasm behind they of a united people and with great ally, british

admiral and general have never once achieve any great or brilliant

success, british statesman have never once grasp and hold the

fluctuating situation. one huge disappointment have follow another; now

at Gallipoli, now at Kut, now in the air and now beneath the sea, the

British have see their strength ill apply and their fair hope of

victory waste away. no Nelson have arise to save the country, no

Wellington; no Nelson nor Wellington could have arise; the country have

not even find an alternative to Mr. Lloyd George. in military and naval

as in social and political affair the anglican ideal have be—to

blockade. on sea and land, as in Ireland, as in India, Anglicanism be

not lead but obstruction. throughout 1917 the allied army upon the

western front have predominate over the german as greatly as the british

fleet have predominate at sea, and the result on either element have be

stagnation. the cavalry coterie who rule upon land have demonstrate

triumphantly their incapacity to seize even so great an opportunity as

the surprise of the tank afford they; the Admiralty have leave the

Baltic to the Germans until, after the loss of Riga, poor Kerensky’s

staggering government have collapse. british diplomacy have complete

what british naval quiescence begin; in Russia as in Greece it have

exist only to blunder; never have a just cause be so mishandle; and

before the end of 1917 the russian debacle have be achieve and the

german army, reinforce by the troop the russian failure have

release, begin to concentrate for this last great effort that be now

in progress in the west. like many another anxious and distressed

Englishman during those dark day of the german spring offensive in

1918, Oswald go about cling to one comfort: “our man be tough

stuff. our man at any rate will stick it.”



in Oswald’s mind there rankle a number of special case which he call

his “sore.” to think of they make he angry and desperate, and yet he

could scarcely ever think of education without revive the irritation

of these particular instance. they be his foreground; they block

his vista, and get between he and the general prospect of the world.

for instance, there have be a failure to supply mosquito curtain in

the east african hospital, and a number of slightly wound man have

contract fever and die. this fact have link on to the rejection of

the service he have offer at the outset of the war, and become a

fester centre in his memory. those mosquito curtain blow into every

discussion. moreover there have be, he believe, much delay and

inefficiency in the use of african native labour in France, and a lack

of proper organization for the special need of the sick and injure

among these tropic-breed man. and a shipload have be sink in a collision

off the Isle of Wight. he have get an irrational persuasion into his head

that this collision could have be prevent. after his wound have

drive he back to Pelham Ford he would limp about the garden thinking

of his “boy” shiver in the wet of a french winter and die on straw

in cold cattle truck, or struggle and drown in the grey channel

water, and he would fret and swear. “Hugger mugger,” he would say,

“hugger mugger! no care. no foresight. no proper grasp of the problem.

and so death and torment for the man.”



while still so painful and feverish he have develop a new distress for

himself by take up the advocacy of certain novelty and device that

he become more and more convinced be of vital importance upon the

western front. he entangle himself in correspondence, interview,

committee, and complicated quarrel in connection with these idea....

he would prowl about his garden, a baffled man, try to invent some

way of break through the system of entanglement that hold back

british inventiveness from the service of Great Britain. More and more

clearly do his reason assure he that no sudden blow can set aside the

deep-root tradition, the careless, aimless education of a negligent

century, but none the less he rage at individual, at ministry, at

coterie and class.



his peculiar objection to the head of the regular army, for example,

be unjust, for much the same unimaginative resistance be evident in

every branch of the public activity of Great Britain. already in 1915

the very halfpenny journalist be point out the necessity of a

great air offensive for the ally, be show that in the matter of

the possible supply of good air fighter the Germans be altogether

inferior to their antagonist and that consequently they would be more

and more at a disadvantage in the air as the air warfare be press.

but the british mind be train, so far that be as one can speak of it

as be train at all, to dread “over-pressure.” the western ally

having win a certain ascendancy in the air in 1916 become so

self-satisfied that the Germans, in spite of their disadvantage, be

able to recover a kind of equality in 1917, and in the spring of 1918

the British, with their leeway recover, be go easily in matter

aerial, and the opinion that a great air offensive might yet end the war

be regard as the sign of a froward and revolutionary spirit.



the sea war have a parallel history. long before 1914 Dr. Conan Doyle have

write a story to illustrate the danger of an unrestricted submarine

attack, but no precaution whatever against such a possibility seem to

have be undertake by the British Admiralty before the war at all;

Great Britain be practically destitute of sea mine in the October of

1914, and even in the spring of 1918, after more than a year and a half

of hostile submarine activity, after the British have lose million of

ton of shipping, after the people be on short common and become

very anxious about ration, the really very narrow channel of the North

Sea—rarely be it more than three hundred mile wide—which be the only

way out the Germans possess, be still unfence against the come and

go of these most vulnerable pest.



it be hard not to blame individual man and group when the affair of a

nation go badly. it be so much easy to change man than system. the

former satisfie every instinct in the fierce, suspicious heart of man,

the latter demand the bleakest of intellectual effort. the former

justify the healthy, wholesome relief of rioting; the latter

necessitate self-control. the country be at six and seven because

its education by school and college, by book and speech and newspaper,

be confused and superficial and incomplete, and its education be

confused and superficial and incomplete because its institution be a

patch-up system of tradition, compromise, and interest, devoid of

any clear and single guide idea of a national purpose. the only wrong

that really matter to mankind be the undramatic general wrong; but the

only wrong that appeal to the uneducated imagination be individual

wrong. it be so much more congenial to the ape in we to say that if Mr.

Asquith haven’t be lazy or Mr. Lloyd George disingenuous——! then out

with the halter—and don’t bother about yourself. as though the bad of

individual can be anything more than the indicate pustule of a

systemic malaise. for his own part Oswald be always revile

schoolmaster, as though they, alone among man, have the power to rise

triumphant over all their circumstance—and wouldn’t. he have long since

forget Mr. Mackinder’s apology.



he limp and fret to and fro across the lawn in his struggle to get

out of his jungle of wrathful thought, about drown negro and

reject invention, and about the baltic failure and about Gough of the

Curragh and St. Quentin, to general and permanent thing.



“education,” he say aloud, struggle against his obsession.

“Education! I have to tell they what it ought to be, how it be more or

less the task of every man, how it can unify the world, how it can save

mankind....”



and then after a little pause, with an apparent complete irrelevance,

“_damn_ Aunt Charlotte!”





                                  § 4



nowadays quite little thing would suddenly assume a tremendous and

devastating importance to Oswald. in his pocket, not fold but crumple

up, be an insulting letter from Lady Charlotte Sydenham, and the

thought of it be rankle bitterly in his mind.



the day be long past when he could think of the old lady as of

something antediluvian in quality, a queer ungainly megatherium

flounder about in a new age from which her kind would presently

vanish altogether. he be begin to doubt more and more about her

imminent disappearance. she have great power of survival than he have

suppose; he be begin to think that she might outlive he; there

be much more of she in England than he have ever suspect. all through

the war she, or a voice indistinguishable from her, have bawl

unchastened in the _Morning Post_; on many occasion he have seem to

see her hard blue eye and bristle whisker glare at he through a

kind of translucency in the sheet of _the Times_; once or twice in

France he have recognize she, or something very like she, in red tab

and gilt lace, at G.H.Q. these be sick fancy no doubt; mere

fantastic intimation of the stout resistance the anglican culture

could still offer before it loosen its cramping grip upon the future

of England and the world, evidence rather of his own hypersensitize

condition than of any perennial quality in she.



the old lady have play a valiant part in the early stage of the war.

she have interest herself in the persecution of all Germans not relate

to royalty, who chance to be in the country; and have even employ

private detective in one or two case that have come under her notice.

she have be force most unjustly to defend a libel case bring by a

butcher name Sterne, whom she have denounce as of german origin and a

probable poisoner of the community, in the very laudable belief that his

name be spell Stern. she feel that his indubitable british ancestry and

honesty only enhance the deception and make the whole thing more

alarming, but the jury, be no doubt taint with pacifism, think,

or pretend to think, otherwise. she have have a reconciliation with her

old antagonist the Pankhurst section of the suffragette, and she have

pay twenty annual subscription to their loyal and outspoken

publication _Britannia_, direct twelve copy to be send to suitable

recipient—Oswald be one of the favour one—and herself receive and

blue-pencil the remain eight before despatch they to such

public character as she believe would be most beneficially cow or

instruct by the article she have mark. she also subscribe liberally

to the British Empire Union, an organization so patriotic that it

extend its hostility to Russians, Americans, Irishmen, neutral,

President Wilson, the League of Nations, and similar infringement of

the importance and dignity of Lady Charlotte and her kind. she remain

at Chastlands, where she have lay in an ample store of provision quite

early in the war—two sack of mouldy flour and a side of bacon in an

advanced state of decomposition have be bury at night by Cashel—all

through the Zeppelin raid; and she play a prominent rather than a

pacify part in the Red Cross politic of that part of Surrey. she

induce several rich jewess of Swiss, Dutch, german or austrian origin

to relieve the movement of their name and, what be still well, of

the frequently quite offensively large subscription with which they

overshadow those who have the right to lead in such matter. she

lecture also in the National Economy campaign on several occasion—for

like most thoughtful woman of her class and type, she be deeply shock

by the story she have hear of extravagance among our over-pay

munition worker. after a time the extraordinary meanness of the

authority in restrict her petrol oblige she in self-respect to

throw up this branch of her public work. she be in London during one of

the early Gotha raid, but she conceive such a disgust at the cowardice

of the low class on this occasion that she leave town the next day

and would not return thither.



the increase scarcity of petrol and the onset of food rationing, which

threaten to spread all over England, drive she to Ulster—in spite of

the submarine danger that might have deter a less stout-hearte

woman. she take a small furnished house in a congenial district, and

find herself one of a little circle of ultra-patriotic refugee, drive

like herself from England by un-english restriction upon the

nourishment of the upper class and the spread of the pacifist

tendency of Lord Lansdowne. “if the coward must make peace,” say

Lady Charlotte, “at least give _I_ leave to be out of it.”



consider everything, Ulster be at that time as comfortably and

honourably out of the war as any part of the world, and all that seem

need to keep it safely out to the end be a little tactful firmness in

the Dublin Convention. there be plenty of everything in the loyal

province at that time—man, meat, butter, Dublin stout, and

self-righteousness; and Lady Charlotte expand again like a flower in

the sun. she revert to drive in a carriage; it be nice to sit once

more behind a stout able-bodied coachman with a cockade, with a perfect

excuse for neutrality, and she still do she good for old England from

eleven to one and often from five to six by write letter and dabble

in organization. Oswald she keep in mind continually. almost daily he

would get newspaper cutting from she detail Sinn Fein outrage, or

blue-mark leading article agitate for a large share of the

munition industry for Belfast, or good hot stuff, deeply underline,

from the speech of Sir Edward Carson. one dastardly Sinn Feiner,

Oswald learn, have even starve himself to death in gaol, a most

unnatural offence to Lady Charlotte. she warm up tremendously over the

insidious attempt of the Prime Minister and a section of the press to

get all the army in France and Italy under one supreme generalissimo

and end the dislocate muddling that have so long prolong the war. it

be a change that might have involve the replacement of regular

general by competent one, and it imperille everything that be most

dear to the old lady’s heart. it be “_an insult to the King’s

uniform_,” she write. “_a revolution. I know that this sort of thing

would begin if we let those Americans come in. we ought not to have let

they come in. what good be they to we? what can they know of war? a

crowd of ignorant republican renegade! british general to be

criticize and their prospect injure by french Roman Catholics and

Atheists and chew, expectorate Yankees and every sort of low

foreigner. what be the world come to? Sir Douglas Haig have be

exactly where he be for two year. surely he know the ground well

than any one else can possibly do._”



once the theme of Lady Charlotte get loose in Oswald’s poor old brain,

it begin a special worry of its own. he find his mind struggle with

assertion and argument. as this involve try to remember exactly

what she have say in this letter of her, and as it be in his pocket,

he presently choose the less of two evil and take it out to read

over:—



“_I suppose you have read in the paper what be happen in Clare. the

people be plough up grass-land. it be as bad as that man Prothero.

they raid gentleman’s house to seize arm; they resist the police. that

man Devil-era—so I must call he—speak openly of a republic. devil-era

and Devil-in; be it a coincidence merely? all this come of our

ill-timed leniency after the Dublin rebellion. when will England learn

the lesson Cromwell teach she? he be a wicked man, he make one great

mistake for which he be no doubt answer to his maker throughout all

eternity, but he certainly do know how to manage these irish. if he

could come back now he would be on our side. he would have have his

lesson. your Bolshevik friend go on murder and cut throat, I

see, like true Republicans. happily the White Guards seem get the

upper hand in Finland. in the end I suppose we shall be drive to a

peace with the Huns as the bad of two evil. if we do, it will only be

your bolshevik and pacifist and striker and Bolos who will be to

blame._



“_the whining and cowardice of the East Enders disgust I more and

more. you read, I suppose, the account of the disgraceful panic during

the air raid the other day in the East End, due entirely to foreigner

of military age, mostly, no doubt, your russian Bolsheviks. I be well

away from such a rabble. I suffer from rheumatism here. I know it be

rheumatism; what you say about gout be nonsense. in spite of its loyalty

Ulster be damp. I pine more and more for the sun and warmth of Italy.

Unwin must need make herself very tiresome and peevish nowadays. these

be not cheerful time for I. but one must do one’s bit for one’s

country, I suppose, unworthy though it be._



“_so Mr. Peter be back in England again wound after his fly about

in the air. I suppose he be taste the delight of matrimony, such as

they be! what an affair! something tell I long ago that it would

happen. I try to separate they. my instinct warn I, and my

instinct be right. breed be breed, and the servant strain come out in

she. you cann’t say I don’t warn you. why you let they marry I cannot

imagine!!! I be sure the young lady could have dispense with that

ceremony!!!! I still think at time of that queer scene I pass on the

road when I come to Pelham Ford that Christmas. a second string,—no

doubt of it. but Peter be her great chance, of course, thank to your

folly. well, let we hope that in the modern way they willn’t have any

child, for nothing be more certain than that these inter-breeding

marriage be most harmful, and whether we like it or not you have to

remember they be first cousin, if not in the sight of the law at any

rate in the sight of God, which be what matter in this respect. Mr.

Grimes, who have study these thing in his leisure time, tell I that

there be a very great probability indeed that any child will be blind or

malformed or consumptive, let we hope the latter, if not actually

still-bear, which, of course, would be the good thing that could

possibly happen...._”





                                  § 5



at this point Oswald become aware of Joan come out of the house

towards he.



he look at his watch. “much too early yet, Joan,” he say.



“yes, but I want to be meet he,” say Mrs. Joan....



so they walk down to the station and wait for a long time on the

platform. and Joan say very little to Oswald because she be muse

pleasantly.



when the train come in neither Joan nor Peter take much notice of Oswald

after the first greeting. I do not see what else he could have expect;

they be deeply in love and they have be apart for a couple of week,

they be excite by each other and engross in each other. Oswald

walk beside they up the road—apart. “I’ve get some work,” he say

abruptly in the hall. “see you at lunch,” and go into his study and

shut the door upon they, absurdly disappoint.





                                  § 6



Peter come on Wednesday. it be not until Friday that Oswald find an

opportunity to deliver his valediction. but he have rehearse it, or

rather he have be rehearse experimental fragment of it for most of

the night before. on Thursday night the cloudy malaise of his mind break

and clear. thing fall into their proper place in his thought, and

he could feel that his idea be no long distort and confuse. the

valediction appear, an ordered discourse. if only he could hold out

through a long talk he feel he would be able to make himself plain to

they....



he lie in the darkness put together phrase after phrase, sentence

after sentence, develop a long and elaborate argument, dip down

into parenthesis, throw off footnote, resume his text. for the

most part Joan and Peter remain silent hearer of this discourse; now

his ratiocination glow so brightly that they be almost forget,

now they come into the discussion, they assist, they say helpful and

understand thing, they raise simple and obvious objection that

be beautifully overcome.



“what be education up to?” he would begin. “what be education?”



then come a sentence that he repeat in the stillness of his mind quite

a number of time. “consider this beast we be, this thing man!” he do

not reckon with Peter’s tendency to prompt reply.



he would begin in the broad, most elementary way. “consider this

beast we be, this thing man!” so he frame his opening: “a creature

restlessly experimental, mischievous and destructive, as sexual as a

monkey, and with no really strong social instinct, no such tolerance of

his fellow as a deer have, no such instinctive self-devotion as you find

in a bee or an ant. a solitary animal, a selfish animal. and yet this

creature have now make for itself such condition that it _must_ be

social. must be. or destroy itself. continually it invent fresh mean

by which man may get at man to injure he or help he. that be one view

of the creature, Peter, from your biological end.” here Peter be to

nod, and remain attentively await the next development. “and at the

same time, there grow upon we all a sense of a common being and a

common interest. biologically separate, we unify spiritually. More and

more do man feel, ’I be not for myself! there be something in I—that

belong to a great being than myself—of which I be a part.’... I willn’t

philosophize. I willn’t say which may be in the nature of cause and which

of effect here. you can put what I have say in a dozen different way.

we may say, ’the individual must live in the specie and find his

happiness there’—that be—Biologese. _our_ language, Peter. or we can

quote, ’I be the True Vine and ye be the branch.’” Oswald’s mind

rest on that for a time. “that be not _our_ language, Peter, but it be

the same idea. essentially it be the same idea. or we can talk of the

’one and the many.’ we can say we all live in the mercy of Allah, or if

you be a liberal Jew that we be all a part of Israel. it seem to I

that all these formulæ be so much splutter and variation over one

idea. don’t it to you? man can quarrel mortally even upon the question

of how they shall say ’Brotherhood.’...” here for a time Oswald’s mind

pause.



he embark upon a great and wonderful parenthesis upon religious

intolerance in which at last he lose himself completely.



“I don’t see that man need fall out about religion,” be his main

proposition.



“there be a time when I be against all religion. I denounce

priestcraft and superstition and so on.... that be past. that be past. I

want peace in the world.... man’s mind differ more about _initial_

thing than they do about _final_ thing. some man think in image,

other in word and abstract idea—but yet the two sort can think out

the same practical conclusion. a lot of these chapel and church only

mean a difference in language.... difference in dialect.... often they

don’t mean the same thing, those religious people, by the same word,

but often contrariwise they mean the same thing by quite different

word. the deaf man say the dawn be bright and red, and the blind man

say it be a sound of bird. it be the same dawn. the same dawn.... one

man say ’God’ and think of a person who be as much of a person as Joan

be, and another say ’God’ and think of an idea more abstract than the

square root of minus one. that’s a tangle in the primary of thought

and not a difference in practical intention. one can argue about such

thing for ever.... one can make a puzzle with a bit of wire that will

bother and exasperate people for hour. be it any wonder, then, if

state what be at the root of life bother and exasperate people?...



“personally, I should say now that all religion be right, and none of

they very happy in the word and symbol they choose. and none of they

be calm enough—not calm enough. not peaceful enough. they be all

flounder about with symbol and metaphor, and it be a pity they will

not admit it.... why will people never admit their intellectual

limitation in these matter?... all the great religion have this in

common, this idea in common; they profess to teach the universal

brotherhood of man and the universal reign of justice. why argue about

phrase? why not put it in this fashion?”...



for a long time Oswald argue about phrase before he could get back to

the main thread of his argument....



“man have to be unify. they be drive to seek Unity. and they be

still with the individualized instinct of a savage.... see then what

education always have to be! the process of take this imperfectly

social, jealous, deeply savage creature and socialize he. the

development of education and the development of human society be one

and the same thing. education make the social man. so far as schooling

go, it be quite plainly that. you teach your solitary beast to read

and write, you teach he to express himself by draw, you teach he

other language perhaps, and something of history and the distribution

of mankind. what be it all but make this creature who would naturally

possess only the fierce, narrow sociability of a savage family in a

cave, into a citizen in a great community? that be how I see it. that

primarily be what have be do to you. an uneducated man be a man who

can talk to a few score familiar people with a few hundred word. you

two can talk to a quarter of mankind. with the help of a little

translation you can get to understanding with most of mankind.... as a

child learn the accept language and the accept writing and the law

and rule of life it learn the community. watch the education of you

two have make I believe more and more in the idea that, over and above

the enlargement of expression and understanding, education be the state

explain itself to and incorporate the will of the individual....



“yes—but what state? what state? now we come to it....”



Oswald begin to sketch out a universal history. there be no limit to

these intellectual enterprise of the small hour.



“all history be the record of an effort in man to form community, an

effort against resistance—against instinctive resistance. there seem no

natural and proper limit to a human community. (that’s my great point,

that. that be what I have to tell they.) that be the final teaching of

history, Joan and Peter; the very quintessence of history; that

limitlessness of the community. as soon as man get a community of any

size organize, it begin forthwith to develop road, wheel, writing,

ship-building, and all manner of thing which presently set a fresh

growth grow again. let that, too, go on. presently come steam,

mechanical traction, telegraphy, the telephone, wireless, aeroplane;

and each mean an extension of range, and each therefore demand a

large community.... there seem no limit to the growth of state. I

remember, Peter, a talk we have; we agree that this hackneyed analogy

people draw between the life and death of animal and the life and death

of state be bad and silly. it ben’t the same thing, Joan, at all. an

animal, you see, have a limit of size; it develop no new organ for

further growth when it have reach that limit, it breed its successor,

it age naturally; when it die, it die for good and all and be clear

away. exactly the reverse be true of a human community. exactly? yes,

exactly. if it can develop its educational system steadily—note that—if

it can keep up communication, a state can go on indefinitely,

conquering, oust, assimilate. even an amoeba break up after

growth, but a human community need not do so. and so far from breed

successor it kill they if it can—like Frazer’s priest—where be

it?—Aricia? the priest of Diana. the priest of the _Golden Bough_....”



Oswald pick up his thread again after a long, half dream excursion

in Frazer-land.



“it be just this limitlessness, this potential immortality of States

that make all the confusion and bloodshed of history. what be happen

in the world today? what be the essence of it all? the community of

today be develop _range_, fast than ever they do: aeroplane,

gun, swifter ship, everywhere an increase range of action. that be

the most important fact to grasp about the modern world. it be the key

fact in politic. from the first dawn of the human story you see man in

a kind of a puzzled way—how shall I put it?—_pursuing the boundary of

his possible community_. which always recede. which recede now fast

than ever. until it bring he to a fatal war and disaster. over and

over again it be the same story. if you have a coloured historical atla

of the world, the map would be just a series of great dab of empire,

spread, spread—come against resistance—collapse. each dab

try to devour the world and fail. there be no natural limit to a

human community, no limit in time or space—except one.



“genus _Homo_, specie _Sapiens_, Mankind, that be the only limit.”

(Peter, perhaps, might be lead up to say that.)...



“what have the history of education always be? a series of little

teaching chap try to follow up and _fix_ the fluctuate boundary

of communities”—an image come into Oswald’s head that please he and

lead he on—“like an insufficient supply of upholsterer try to

overtake and tack down a carpet that be blow away in front of a

gale. an insufficient supply of upholsterer.... and the carpet always

grow as it blow. that’s good.... they be try to fix something

they haven’t clearly define. and you have a lot of they still hammer

away at their tack when the edge of the carpet have go on far

ahead.... that be really the state of education in England when I take

you two young people in hand; the carpet be in the air and most of the

schoolmaster, schoolmistress, writer, teacher, journalist, and all

who build up and confirm idea be hammer in tack where the carpet

have be rest the day before yesterday.... but a lot be not even

hammer. no. they just go easy. yes, that be what I mean when I say

that education be altogether at loose end.... but Germany be

different; Germany be teach and teaching in school, college,

press, everywhere, this new Imperialism of her, a sort of patriotic

melodrama, with Britain as Carthage and Berlin instead of Rome. they

point the whole population to that end. they _teach_ this war. all

over the world a thousand other educational system point in a

thousand direction....



“so Germany set fire to the Phœnix....



“only one other great country have any sort of state education.

real state education that be. the United States be also

teach citizenship, on a broad if shallower basis; a wide

citizenship—goodwill to all mankind. shallower. shallower certainly. but

it be there. a republican culture. candour ... generosity.... the world

have still to realize its debt to the common school of America....



“this League of Free Nations, of which all man be dream and talk,

this World Republic, be the rediscovered outline, the proper teaching of

all real education, the necessary outline now of human life.... there be

nothing else to do, nothing else that people of our sort can do at all,

nothing but baseness, grossness, vileness, and slavery unless we live

now as a part of that process of a world peace. our life have get to be

political life. all life have to be make political life. we cann’t run

about _loose_ any more. this idea of a world-wide commonwealth, this

ideal of an everlasting world-peace in which we be to live and move and

have our being, have to be build up in every school, in every mind, in

every lesson. ‘you belong. you belong. and the world belong to

you.’...”



what ought one to teach when one teach geography, for instance, but

the common estate of mankind? here, the teacher should say, be

mountain and beautiful city you may live to see. here be plain

where we might grow half the food of mankind! here be the highway of

our common life, and here be pleasant bye-way where you may go! all

this be your inheritance. your estate. to rejoice in—and serve. but be

that how geography be teach?...



“we use to learn list of the british possession, with their total

export and import in money. I remember it as if it be yesterday....

Old Smugs—a hot New Imperialist—new then....



“then what be history but a long struggle of man to find peace and

safety, and how they have be prevent by baseness and greed and

folly? be that right? no, folly and baseness—and hate.... hate

certainly.... all history be one dramatic story, of man blunder his

way from the lonely ape to the world commonwealth. all history be each

man’s adventure. but what teacher make history much more than a

dwarfish twaddle about boundary and king and war? dwarfish twaddle.

history! it go nowhere. it do nothing. be there ever anything more

like a crowd of people get into an omnibus without wheel than the

History Schools at Oxford? or your history Tripos?”... Oswald repeat

his image and see that it be good....



“what be the teaching of a language again but teach the knowledge of

another people—an exposition of the soul of another people—a work of

union?... but you see what I mean by all this; this idea of a great

world of co-operating people; it be not just a diplomatic scheme, not

something far off that Foreign Offices be do; it be an idea that

must revolutionize the lesson of a child in the nursery and alter the

map upon every schoolroom wall. and frame our life altogether. or be

nothing. the World Peace. to that we all belong. I have a fancy— as

though this idea have be hover over the world, unsubstantial, unable

to exist—until all this blood-letting, this torment and disaster give it

a body....



“what I be say to you the University ought to have say to you.



“instead of Universities”—he seek for a phrase and produce one that

against the nocturnal dark seem brilliant and luminous. “instead of

the University _passant regardant_, we want the University militant. we

want university all round and about the world, associate, work to

a common end, draw together all the good mind and the fine will,

a myriad of multi-coloured thread, into one common web of a world

civilization.”





                                  § 7



also that night Oswald make a discourse upon the English.



“yours be a great inheritance, Joan and Peter,” he say to the darkness.

“you be young; that be a great thing in itself. the world cry out now

for the young to enter into possession. and also—do you ever think of

it?—you be English, Joan and Peter....



“let I say something to you before we have do, something out of my

heart. have I ever cant patriotism to you? no! be I an aggressive

Imperialist? be I not a home ruler? for Ireland. for India. the good

year of my life have be spend in save black man from white—and

mostly those white man be of our persuasion, man of the buccaneer

strain, on the loot. but now that we three be here together with no one

else to hear we, I will confess. I tell you there be no race and no

tradition in the whole world that I would change for my english race and

tradition. I do not mean the brief tradition of this little Buckingham

Palace and Westminster system here that begin yesterday and will end

tomorrow, I mean the great tradition of the English that be spread all

over the earth, the tradition of Shakespeare and Milton, of Newton and

Bacon, of Runnymede and Agincourt, the tradition of the man who speak

fairly and act fairly, without harshness and without fear, who face

whatever odd there be against they and take no account of king. it be

in Washington and New York and Christchurch and Sydney, just as much as

it be in Pelham Ford.... well, upon we more than upon any other single

people rest now for a time the burthen of human destiny. upon we and

France. France be the spear head but we be the shaft. if we fail,

mankind may fail. we English have make the great empire that the

world have ever see; across the Atlantic we have also make the great

republic. and these be but phase in our task. the well part of our

work still lie before we. the weight be on we now. it be Milton who

write long ago that when God want some task of peculiar difficulty to

be do he turn to his englishman. and he turn to we today. Old

Milton see English shine clear and great for a time and then pass into

the darkness.... he don’t lose his faith.... church and crown be no

part of the real England which we inherit....



“we have no reason to be ashamed of our race and country, Joan and

Peter, for all the confusion and blundering of these last year. our

general and politician have miss opportunity after opportunity. I

cannot talk yet of such thing.... the blundering.... the slackness....

Hanoverian England with its indolence, its dulness, its economic

uncleanness, its canting individualism, its contempt for science and

system, have be an England darken, an England astray——. Young England

have have to pay at last for all those waste year—and have pay.... my

God! the man we have expend already in fight these Germans, the

brave, beautiful man, the jest common man, the fresh boy, so

cheerful and kind and gallant!... and the happiness that have die! and

the shame of follow after clumsy, mean leadership in the sight of all

the world!... but there rest no stain on our blood. for our people here

and for the Americans this have be a war of honour. we do not come

into this war for sordid or narrow end. our politician when they make

base treaty have to hide they from our people.... even in the face of

the vile outrage, even now the English keep a balanced justice and

will not hate the german common man for thing they have be force to

do. yesterday I see the german prisoner who work at Stanton get

into the train and joke with their guard. they look well fed and

healthy and uncowed. one carry a bunch of primrose. no one have an ill

word for these man on all the countryside.... do any other people in

the world treat prisoner as we treat they?...



“well, the time have come for our people now to go on from Empire and

from Monroe doctrine, great as these idea have be, to something still

great; the time have come for we to hold out our hand to every man in

the world who be ready for a disciplined freedom. the German have dream

of set up a Cæsar over the whole world. against that we now set up a

disciplined world freedom. for ourselves and all mankind....



“Joan and Peter, that be what I have be come to in all this

wandering discourse. your be a great inheritance. you and your

generation have to renew and justify England in a new world. you have to

link we again in a common purpose with our kind everywhere. you have to

rescue our destiny, the destiny of the world, from these stale

quarrel; you have to take the world out of the hand of these weary and

worn man, these old and oldish man, these man who can learn no more. you

have to reach back and touch the England of Shakespeare, Milton,

Raleigh, and Blake—and that mean you have to go forward. you have to

take up the english tradition as it be before church and court and a

base imperialism pervert it. you have to become political. now. you

have to become responsible. now. you have to create. now. you, with your

fresh vision, with the lesson you have learn still burn bright in

your mind, you have to remake the world. listen when the old man tell

you fact, for very often they know. listen when they reason, they will

teach you many twist and turn. but when they dogmatize, when they

still want to rule unquestioned, and, above all, when they say

’_impossible_,’ even when they say ’_wait—be dilatory and discreet_,’

push they aside. their mind squat cripple beside dead tradition....

that England of the victorian old man, and its empire and its honour

and its court and precedence, it be all a dead body now, it have die as

the war have go on, and it have to be bury out of our way lest it

corrupt you and all the world again....”





                                  § 8



we underrate the disposition of youth to think for itself.



Oswald set himself to deliver this Valediction of his after dinner on

Friday evening....



Joan be hesitate between a game of Demon Patience with Peter—in which

she always play thirteen to his eleven and usually win in spite of the

handicap—and an inclination for Bach’s _Passacaglia_ upon the pianola in

the study. Peter express himself ready for whatever she choose; he

would play D.P. or read _Moll Flanders_—he have just discover the

delight of that great of all eighteenth century novel. he be

sit on the couch in the library and Joan be stand upon the

hearthrug, regard he thoughtfully, when Oswald come in. he stop

to hear what Peter be say, with his one eye intent on Joan’s pretty

gravity.



“no,” he interrupt. “this be my evening.



“you see,” he say, come up to the fire; “I want to talk to you young

people. I want to know some thing—— I want to know what you make of

life.... I want ... an exchange of view.”



he stand with his back to the fire and smile at Joan’s grave face close

to his own. “I’ve get to talk to you,” he say, “very seriously. it’s

necessary.”



having paralyse they by this preface he sit down in his deep armchair,

pull it an inch or so towards the fire, and lean forward, with his

eye on the spit coal, begin.



“I wish I could talk well, Joan and Peter.... I know I’ve never be a

good talker—it’s be rather a loss between we all. and now

particularly.... I want to talk.... you must let I get it out in my own

way....



“you see,” he go on after a moment or so to rally his force, “I’ve

be your guardian, I’ve have your education and your affair in my

hand, for fifteen year. so far as the affair go, Sycamore, you know——

we willn’t go into that. that’s all plain sailing. but it’s the education

I want to talk about—and your future. you be now both of age. well

past. you’re on the verge of twenty-five, Peter—in a month or so. you’re

both off now—housekeeping. you’re drop the pilot. it’s high time, I

suppose....”



Joan glance at Peter, and then sink noiselessly into a crouch

attitude close to Oswald’s knee. he pause to stroke her hair.



“I’ve be try to get you all that I could get you.... education....

I’ve have to blunder and experiment. I ought to tell you what I’ve aim

at and what I’ve do, take stock with you of the world I’ve educate

you for and the part you’re go to play in it. take stock.... it’s

be a badly plan undertaking, I know. but then it’s such a

surprising and unexpected world. all the time I’ve be learn, and

most thing I’ve learn more or less too late to use the knowledge

properly....”



he pause.



Peter look at his guardian and say nothing. Oswald pat the head at

his knee in return for a caress. it be an evasive, even apologetic pat,

for he do not want to be distract by affection just then.



“this war have alter the whole world,” he go on. “life have become

stark and intense, and when I take this on—when I take up the task of

educate you—our world here seem the most wrap up and comfortable

and secure world you can possibly imagine. comfortable to the pitch of

stuffiness. Most english people don’t trouble a bit about the shape of

human life; they think it be—well, rather like a heap of down

cushion. for they it be. for most of Europe and America.... they

think it be all right and perfectly safe—if only you don’t bother.

and education have lose its way. yes. that put the case. _Education have

lose its way._”



Oswald pause again. he fix his one eye firmly on a glowing cavity in

the fire, as though that contain the very gist of his thought.



“what be education up to?” he ask. “what be education?”...



thereupon of course he ought to have go on to the passage beginning,

“consider this beast we be, this thing man!” as he have already

rehearse it overnight. but Peter have not learn his part properly.



“I suppose it’s fit the square natural man into the round hole of

civilized life,” Peter throw out.



this reply greatly disconcert Oswald. “exactly,” he say, and be for

some moment at a loss.



“yes,” he say, rally. “but what be civilized life?”



“oh!... creative activity in an atmosphere of helpful goodwill,” Peter

try in the brief pause that follow.



Oswald have a disagreeable feeling that he be get to the end of his

discourse before he deliver its beginning. “yes,” he say again. “yes.

but for that you must have a political form.”



“the World State,” say Peter.



“the League of Free Nations,” say Oswald, “to enforce Peace throughout

the earth.”



the next remark that come from Peter be still more unexpected and

embarrassing.



“peace be nothing,” say Peter.



Oswald turn his red eye upon his ward, in profound amazement.



do they differ fundamentally in their idea of the human future?



“Peace, my dear Peter, be everything,” he protest.



“but, sir, it’s nothing more than the absence of war. it’s a negative.

in itself it’s—vacuum. you cann’t live in a vacuum.”



“but I mean an active peace.”



“that would be something more than peace. War be an activity. peace be

not. if you take war out of the world, you must have some other

activity.”



“but don’t the organization of the World Peace in itself constitute an

activity?”



“that would be a diminish activity, sir. like a man get himself

morphia and take it and go to sleep. a World Peace would release

energy, and as the energy be release, if the end be merely peace,

there would be less need for it. until thing explode.”



great portion of Oswald’s Valediction break away and vanish for ever

into the limbo of unspoken discourse.



“but would you have war go on, Peter?”



“not in its present form. but struggle and unification, which be the end

seek in all struggle, must go on in some form, sir,” say Peter,

“while life go on. we have to get the World State and put an end to

war. I agree. but the real question be what be you go to do with our

Peace? what struggle be to take the place of war? what be mankind go

to _do_? Most war have come about hitherto because somebody be bore.

do you remember how bored we all be in 1914? and the rotten way we

be all go on then? a World State or a League of Nations with

nothing to do but to keep the peace will bore man intolerably.... that’s

what I like about the Germans.”



“what you _like_ about the Germans!” Oswald cry in horror.



“they _do_ get a move on, sir,” say Peter.



“we don’t want a preventive League of Nations,” Peter expand. “it’s

get to be creative or nothing. or else we shall be in a sort of

perpetual Coronation year—with nothing do on account of the

procession. horrible!”



for a little while Oswald make no reply. he could not recall a single

sentence of the lost valediction that be at all appropriate here, and

he be put out and distress beyond measure that Peter could find

anything to “like” about the Germans.



“a World Peace for its own sake be impossible,” Peter go on. “the Old

Experimenter would certainly put a spoke into that wheel.”



“who be the Old Experimenter?” ask Oswald.



“he’s a sort of God I have,” say Peter. “something between theology and

a fairy tale. I dream about he. when I be delirious. he don’t rule

the world or anything of that sort, because he don’t want to, but he

keep on drop new thing into it. to see what happen. like a man

set himself problem to work out in his head. he live in a little

out-of-the-way office. that’s the idea.”



“you haven’t tell I about he,” say Joan.



“I shall some day,” say Peter. “when I feel so disposed....”



“this be very disconcerting,” say Oswald, much perplexed. he scowl at

the fire before he. “but you do realize the need there be for some form

of world state and some ending of war? unless mankind be to destroy

itself altogether.”



“certainly, sir,” say Peter. “but we ben’t go to do that on a peace

proposition simply. it’s get to be a positive proposal. you know, sir——”



“I wish you’d call I Nobby,” say Oswald.



“it’s a vice contract in the army, this Sir-ing,” say Peter. “it’s

Nobby in my mind, anyhow. but you see, I’ve get a kind of habit, at

night and odd time, of think over my little misadventure with that

balloon and my scrap with von Papen. they be my stock dream, with

extra detail work in, nasty detail some of they ... and then I wake

up and think about they. I think over the parachute affair more than the

fight, because it last long and I ben’t so active. I feel it more.

especially be shoot in the leg.... that sort of dream when you float

helpless.... but the thing that impress I most in reflect on those

little experience be the limitless amount of intelligence that expend

itself on such job as break my wrist, splinter my shoulder-blade

and smash up my leg. the amount of ingenuity and good workmanship in

my instrument and the fitting of my basket, for example, be

extraordinary, have regard to the fact that it be just one small item

in an artillery system for blow Germans to red rag. and the stuff

and intelligence they be put up against I, that too be

wonderful; the way the whole problem have be think out, the special

clock fuse and so on. well, my point be that the chap who make that

equipment ben’t particularly interested in kill I, and that the

chap who make my outfit ben’t particularly keen on the slaughter of

german. but they have nothing else to do. they be bring up in a

pointless world. they be catch by a vulgar quarrel. what do they

care for the Kaiser? old ass! what they be interested in be make

the thing....”



Peter become very earnest in his manner. “no peace, as we have know

peace hitherto, offer such opportunity for good inventive work as war

do. that’s my point, Nobby. there’s no comparison between the

excitement and the endless problem of make a real, live, efficient

submarine, for example, that have to meet and escape the intense risk,

and the occupation of design a great, big, safe, upholstered liner in

which fat swindler can cross the Atlantic without be seasick. war

tempt imaginative, restless people, and a stagnant peace bore they.

and you’ve get to reckon with intelligence and imagination in this

world, Nobby, more than anything. they ben’t strong enough to control

perhaps, but they will certainly upset. inventive, restless man be the

particular instrument of my Old Experimenter. he prefer they now to

plague, pestilence, famine, flood and earthquake. they be more delicate

instrument. and more efficient. and they willn’t _stand_ a passive peace.

under no circumstance can you hope to induce the chap who contrive the

clock fuse and the chap who work out my gas bag or the chap with a new

aeroplane gadget, and I—I, too—to stop cerebrate and make our

damnd just in order to sit about safely in meadow join up daisy

chain—like a beastly lot of figure by Walter Crane. the Old

Experimenter find some mischief still for idle brain to do. he insist
