on it. that’s fundamental to the scheme of thing.”



“but that’s no reason,” interrupt Oswald, “why you and the inventor

who be behind you, and the Germans who make and load and fire that

shell, shouldn’t all get together to do something that will grow and

endure. instead of kill one another.”



“ah, that’s it!” say Peter. “but the word for that ben’t peace.”



“then what be the word for it?”



“I don’t know,” say Peter. “the Great Game, perhaps.”



“and where do it take you?”



Peter throw out his hand. “it’s an exploration,” he say. “it will take

man to the centre of the earth; it will take he to the end of space,

between the atom and among the star. how can we tell beforehand? you

must have faith. but of one thing I be sure, that man cannot stagnate.

it be forbid. it be the uttermost sin. why, the Old Man will come out

of his office himself to prevent it! this war and all the blood and loss

of it be because the new thing be entangle among old and dead thing,

wear-out and silly thing, and we’ve not have the vigour to get they

free. old idiot nationality, national conceit—expand to imperialism,

nationality in a state of megalomania, have be allow to get hold of

the knife that be mean for a sane generation to carve out a new world

with. Heaven send he cut his own throat this time! or else there may be

a next time.... I’m all for the one world state, and the end of flag

and king and custom house. but I have my doubt of all this talk of

make the world safe—safe for democracy. I want the world make one for

the adventure of mankind, which be quite another story. I have be in

the world now, Nobby, for five-and-twenty year, and I be only begin

to suspect the wonder and beauty of the thing we man might know and do.

if only we could get our eye and hand free of the old inheritance.

what have mankind do yet to boast about? I despise human

history—because I believe in God. not the God you don’t approve of,

Nobby, but in my Old Experimenter, whom I confess I don’t begin to

understand, and in the far-off, eternal scheme he hide from we and

which he mean we to develop age by age. oh! I don’t understand he, I

don’t begin to explain he; he’s just a figure for what I feel be the

reality. but he be right, he be wonderful. and instead of just muddle

about over the surface of his universe, we have to get into the

understanding of it to the very limit of our ability, to live our

utmost and do the intense good we can.”



“yes,” say Oswald; “yes.” this be after his own heart, and yet it do

not run along the line of the Valedictory that have flower with such

corinthian richness overnight. he have be think then of world peace;

what Peter be drive at now be a world purpose; but ben’t the two

after all the same thing? he sit with his one eye reflect the red

light of the fire, and the phrase that have come in such generous

abundance overnight now refuse to come at all.



Peter, on the couch, continue to think aloud.



“make the world safe for democracy,” say Peter. “that ben’t quite it.

if democracy mean that any man may help who can, that school and

university will give every man and woman the fair chance, the most

generous inducement to help, to do the thing he can well do under the

good condition, then, _yes_; but if democracy mean get up a riot

and boycott among the stupid and lazy and illiterate whenever anything

be do, then I say _no_! every human being have get to work, have get to

take part. if our law and organization don’t insist upon that, the Old

Experimenter will. so long as the world be rule by stale idea and lazy

idea, he be determined that it shall flounder from war to war. now what

do this democracy mean? do it mean a crowd of primitive brute

howl down progress and organization? because if it do, I want to be

in the machine-gun section. when you talk of education, Nobby, you think

of highly educate people, of a nation instruct through and through.

but what of democracy in Russia, where you have a naturally clever

people in a state of peasant ignorance—who cann’t even read? until the

schoolmaster have talk to every one for ten or twelve year, can you

have what President Wilson think of as democracy at all?”



“now there you meet I,” say Oswald. “that be the idea I have be

try to get at with you.” and for some minute the palatial dimension

of the lose Valedictory loom out. where he have say “peace” overnight,

however, he now say progress.



but the young man on the couch be much too keenly interested to make a

good audience. when presently Oswald propound his theory that all the

great world religion be on the side of this World Republic that he

and Peter desire, Peter demur.



“but be that true of Catholicism for instance?” say Peter.



Oswald quote, “I be the Vine and ye be the branch.”



“yes,” say Peter. “but look at the Church itself. don’t look at the

formula but at the practice and the daily teaching. be it truly a

grow Vine?” the reality of Catholicism, Peter argue, be a

traditional, sacramental religion, a narrow fetish religion with a

specialized priest, it be concern primarily with another world, it

set its face against any conception of a scheme of progress in this

world apart from its legend of the sacrifice of the Mass.



“all good Catholics sneer at progress,” say Peter. “take Belloc and

Chesterton, for example; they _hate_ the idea of man work steadily

for any great scheme of effort here. they hold by stagnant standard,

plant deep in the rich mud of life. what’s the catholic conception of

human life?—guzzle, booze, call the passion of the sex unclean and

behave accordingly, confess, get absolution, and at it again. be there

any recognition in Catholicism of the duty of keep your body fit or

your brain active? they’re bad than the man who bury his talent in a

clean napkin; they bury it in wheezy fat. it’s a sloven’s life. what

have we in common with that? always they be hark back to the

thirteenth century, to the peasant life amidst dung and chicken. it’s a

different specie of mind from ours, with the head and foot turn

backward. what be the good of expect the Pope, for instance, and his

Church to help we in create a League of Nations? his aim would be a

world agreement to stop progress, and we want to release it. he want

peace in order to achieve nothing, and we want peace in order to do

everything. what be the good of pretend that it be the same peace? a

Catholic League of Nations would be a conspiracy of stagnation, another

Holy Alliance. what real world unity can come through they? every step

on the way to the world state and the real unification of man will be

fight by the stagnant man and the priest. why blind ourselves to that?

progress be a religion in itself. work and learning be our creed. we

cannot make term with any other creed. the priest have get his God and

we seek our God for ever. the priest be finish and complete and

self-satisfied, and we—we be begin....”





                                  § 9



there be two day yet before Peter go back to his work in London.

Saturday dawn blue and fine, and Joan and he determine to spend it in

a long tramp over the Hertfordshire hill and field. he mean to stand

no nonsense from his foot. “if I cann’t walk four mile an hour then I

must do two,” he say. “and if the pace be too slow for you, Joan, you

must run round and round I and bark.” they take a long route by field

and lane through Albury and Furneaux Pelham to the little inn at

Stocking Pelham, where they get some hard biscuit and cheese and

shandygaff, and come home by way of Patmore Heath, and the golden oak

and the rivulet. and as they go Peter talk of Oswald.



“naturally he want to know what we be go to do,” say Peter, and

then, rather inconsequently, “he’s ill.



“this war be like a waste fever in the blood and in the mind,” say

Peter. “all Europe be ill. but with he it mix with the old fever.

that splinter at Fricourt be no joke for he. he oughtn’t to have go

out. he’s get horribly lean, and his eye be like a garnet.”



“I love he,” say Joan.



but she do not want to discuss Oswald just then.



“about this new theology of your, Peter,” she say....



“well?” say Peter.



“what do you mean by this Old Experimenter of your? be he—_God_?”



“I don’t know. I think he be. he’s—— he’s a symbol. he’s just a

Caricature I make to express how all _this_”—Peter sweep his arm across

the sunlit world—“seems to stand to I. if one cann’t draw the thing any

well, one have to make a caricature.”



Joan consider that gravely.



“I think of he first in my dream as the God of the Universe,” Peter

explain.



“you couldn’t love a God like that,” Joan remark.



“Heavens, _no_! he’s too vast, too incomprehensible. I love you—and

Oswald—and the R.F.C., Joan, and biology. but he’s above and beyond that

sort of thing.”



“could you pray to he?” ask Joan.



“not to _he_,” say Peter.



“I pray,” say Joan. “don’t you?”



“and swear,” say Peter.



“one pray to something—it ben’t oneself.”



“the fashion nowadays be to speak of the God in the Heart and the God in

the Universe.”



“be it the same God?”



“leave it at that,” say Peter. “we don’t know. all the waste and muddle

in religion be due to people argue and assert that they be the

same, that they be different but related, or that they be different

but opposed. and so on and so on. how can we know? what need be there to

know? in view of the little job we be do. let we leave it at that.”



Joan be silent for a while. “I suppose we must,” she say.



“and what be we go to do with ourselves,” ask Joan, “when the war

be over?”



“they cann’t keep we in khaki for ever,” Peter consider. “there’s a

Ministry of Reconstruction foozle away in London, but it’s never say

a word to I of the some-day that be come. I suppose it haven’t learn

to talk yet.”



“what do you think of do?” ask Joan.



“well, first—a good medical degree. then I can doctor if I have to. but,

if I’m good enough, I shall do research. I’ve a sort of feeling that

along the border line of biology and chemistry I might do something

useful. I’ve some idea.... I suppose I shall go back to Cambridge for a

bit. we neither of we need earn money at once. it will be queer—after

be a grow-up married man—to go back to proctor and bulldog. what

be _you_ go to do, Joan, when you get out of uniform?”



“look after you first, Petah. oh! it’s worth do. and it willn’t take I

all my time. and then I’ve get my own idea....”



“out with ’em, Joan.”



“well——”



“well?”



“Petah, I shall learn plumbing.”



“job?”



“no. and bricklaying and carpentry. all I can. and then I be go to

start build house.”



“architect?”



“as little as possible,” say Joan. “no. no beastly architecture for

Art’s sake for I! do you remember how people use to knock their head

about at the Ingle-Nook? I’ve get some money. why shouldn’t I be able to

build house as well as the fat builder-man with big, flat thumb who

use to build house before the war?”



“Jerry-building?”



“high-class jerry-building, if you like. cottage with sensible inside,

real inside, and not so much waste space and scamp to make up for

it. they’re half a million house short in this country already. there’s

something in building appeal to my sort of imagination. and I’m go

to make money, Petah.”



“I love the way you carry your tail,” say Peter. “always.”



“well, do run repair harden a woman’s soul.”



“you’ll make more money than I shall, perhaps. but now I begin to

understand all these extraordinary book you’ve be study.... I

might have guess.... why not?”



he limp along, consider it. “why shouldn’t you?” he say. “a

service flat will leave your hand free.... I’ve always wonder

secretly why woman don’t plunge into that sort of business more.”



“it’s be just diffidence,” say Joan.



“_Click!_” say Peter. “that’s go, anyhow. if a lot of woman do as you

do and become productive for good, this old muddle of a country will sit

up in no time. it double the output.... I wonder if the man will like

work under you?”



“there’ll be a boss in the background,” say Joan. “Mr. John Debenham.

who’ll never turn up. be, in fact, no more than camouflage for Joan

of that ilk. I shall be just my own messenger and agent.



“one thing I know,” say Joan, “and that be, that I will make a cottage

or a flat that willn’t turn a young woman into an old one in ten year’

time. live in that Jepson flat without a servant have brighten I up

in a lot of way.... and a child will grow up in my cottage without

be crippled in its mind by awkwardness and ugliness.... this sort of

thing always have be woman’s work really. only we’ve be so busy

chittering and powder our silly nose—and lay snare for our

Peters. who don’t know what be good for they.”



Peter laugh and be amuse. he feel a pleasant assurance that Joan

really be go to build house.



“Joan,” he say, “it’s a bleak world before we—and I hate to think of

Nobby. he’s so _ill_. but the work—the good hard work—there’s time when

I rather like to think of that.... they be beastly year just before

the war.”



“I hate they,” say Joan.



“but what a lot of stuff there be about!” say Peter. “the petrol!

give away, practically, along the roadside everywhere. and the joint

of meat. do you remember the big ham we use to have on the sideboard?

for breakfast. a lot of sausage go sizzle! egg galore! Bacon!

Haddock. perhaps cutlet. and the way one could run off abroad!”



“to Italy,” say Joan dangerously.



“God know when those time will come back again! not for year. not for

our lifetime.”



“if they come back all at once we’d have indigestion,” say Joan.



“Orgy,” say Peter. “but they willn’t.”...



presently their note become graver.



“we’ve get to live like fanatic. if a lot of we don’t live like

fanatic, this staggering old world of our willn’t recover. it will

stagger and then go flop. and a race of Bolshevik peasant will breed

pig among the ruin. we owe it to ourselves, we owe it to the world to

prevent that.”



“and we owe it to the one who have die,” say Joan.



she hesitate, and then she begin to tell he something of the part

Wilmington have play in their life.



they go through field after field, through gate and over stile and

by a coppice spangle with primrose, while she tell he of the part

that Wilmington have play in bring they together; Wilmington who be

now no more than grey soil where the battle still rage in France. many

be the young people who talk so of dead friend in those day. their

voice become grave and faintly deferential, as though they have invoke

a third presence to mingle with their duologue. they be very careful

to say nothing and to think as little as possible that might hurt

Wilmington’s self-love.



presently they find themselves speculate again about the kind of

world that lie ahead of they—whether it would be a wholly poor world or

a poverty-strike world infest and devastate by a few hundred

millionaire and their following. poor we be certain to be. we should

either be sternly poor or meanly poor. but Peter be disposed to doubt

whether the war millionaire would “get away with the swag.”



“there’s too much thinking and read nowadays for that,” say Peter.

“they willn’t get away with it. this be a new age, Joan. if they try that

game they willn’t have five year’ run.”



no, it would be a world generally poor, a tired but chastened world

get itself into order again.... would there be much music in the

year ahead? much writing or art? would there be a new theatre and the

excitement of first night again? should we presently travel by

aeroplane, and find all the world within a few day’ journey? they be

both prepare to resign themselves to ten year’ of work and scarcity,

but they both cling to the hope of return prosperity and freedom

after that.



“well, well, Joan,” say Peter, “these time teach we to love. I’m

crippled. we’ve get to work hard. but I’m not unhappy. I’m happy than

I be when I have no idea of what I want in life, when I lust for

everything and be content with nothing, in the day before the war. I’m

a wise old man now with my stiff wrist and my game leg. you change

everything, Joan. you make everything worth while.”



“I’d like to think it be I,” say Joan idiomatically.



“it’s you....



“after all there must be some snatch of holiday. I shall walk with you

through beautiful day—as we be do today—day that would only be

like empty silk purse if it ben’t that they hold you in they. scenery

and flower and sunshine mean nothing to I—until you come in. I’m blind

until you give I eye. Joan, do you know how beautiful you be? when

you smile? when you stop to think? frown a little. when you look—yes,

just like that.”



“_no!_” say Joan, but very cheerfully.



“but you be—you be endlessly beautiful. endlessly. make love to

Joan—it’s the intense of joy. every time—— as if one have just

discover she.”



“there’s a certain wild charm about Petah,” Joan admit, “for a coarse

taste.”



“after all, whether it’s set in poverty or plenty,” say Peter; “whether

it’s rational or irrational, make love be still at the heart of we

human.”...



for a time they exult shamelessly in themselves. they talk of the

good time they have have together in the past. they revive memory of

Bungo Peter and the Sagas that have slumber in silence since the first

dawn of adolescence. she recall a score of wonderful story and

adventure that he have altogether forget. she have a far clear and

well memory for such thing than he. “D’you remember lightning slick,

Petah? and how the day go fast? D’you remember how he put lightning

slick on his bicycle?”...



but Peter have forget that.



“and when we fight for that picshua you make of Adela,” Joan say.

“when I bit you.... it be my first taste of you, Petah. you taste

dusty....”



“I suppose we’ve always have a blind love for each other,” say Peter,

“always.”



“I hate you to care for any one but myself,” say Joan, “since ever I

can remember. I hate even Billy.”



“it’s well we find out in time,” say Peter.



“_i_ find out,” say Joan.



“ever since we stop be boy and girl together,” say Peter, “I’ve

never be at peace in my nerve and temper till now.... now I feel as

though I swing free in life, safe, sure, content.”



“_Content_,” weigh Joan suspiciously. “but you’re still in love with

I, Petah?”



“not particularly _in_ love,” say Peter. “no. but I’m love you—as the

June sun love an open meadow, shine all over it. I shall always love

you, Joan, because there be no one like you in all the world. no one at

all. make love happen, but love endure. how can there be

companionship and equality except between the like?—who can keep step,

who can climb together, joke broad and shameless, and never struggle for

the upper hand? and where in all the world shall I find that, Joan, but

in you? listen to wisdom, Joan! there be two sort of love between man

and woman, and only two—love like the love of big carnivore who know

their mate and stick to they, or love like some man who follow a woman

home because he’s never see anything like she before. I’ve do with

that sort of love for ever. there’s man who like to exaggerate every

difference in woman. they pretend woman be mysterious and dangerous and

wonderful. they like sex serve up with lie and lingerie.... where’s

the love in that? give I my old brown Joan.”



“not so beastly brown,” say Joan.



“Joan _nature_.”



“Tut, tut!” say Joan.



“there’s people who scent themselves to make love,” say Peter.



“Experienced Petah,” say Joan.



“I’ve read of it,” say Peter, and a little pause fall between they....



“every one ought to be like we,” say Joan sagely, with the spring

sunshine on her dear face.



“it take all sort to make a world,” say Peter.



“everybody ought to have a lover,” say Joan. “everybody. there’s no

clean life without it.”...



“we’ve be through some beastly time, Joan. we’ve run some beastly

risk.... we’ve just scramble through, Joan, to love—as I scramble

through to life. after be put down and shoot at.”...



presently Joan suspect a drag in Peter’s pace and decide at the

sight of a fallen tree in a little grass lane to profess fatigue. they

sit down upon the scaly trunk, just opposite to where a gate pierce a

bud hedge and give a view of a long, curve ridge of sunlit blue,

shoot corn with red bud and green-powder tree beyond, and far

away a woldy upland rise out of an intervene hide valley. and

Peter admit that he, too, feel a little tired. but each be make a

pretence for the sake of the other.



“we’ve rediscover a lot of the old thing, Joan,” say Peter. “the war

have knock sense into we. there ben’t anything to work for, there

ben’t much to be loyal to in the day of the Marconi scandal and the

Coronation Durbar. Slack time, more despair in they by far than in

these red day. rotten, aimless time.... oh! the world’s not do

for....



“I don’t grudge my wrist or my leg,” say Peter. “I can hop. I’ve still

get five and forty year, fifty year, perhaps, to spend. in this new

world.”...



he say no more for a time. there be scheme in his head, so immature

as yet that he could not even sketch they out to she.



he sit with his eye dream, and Joan watch he. there be much of

the noble beast in this Peter of her. in the end now, she be

convince, he be go to be an altogether noble beast. through she. he

be her to cherish, to help, to see grow.... he be her choose man....

depth that be only begin to awaken in Joan be stir. she

would sustain Peter, and also presently she would renew Peter. a time

would come when this dear spirit would be bear again within her being,

when the blood in her artery and all the grace of her body would be

give to a new life—to new life, that would be beautiful variation of

this dear tune in the music of the world.... they would have courage;

they would have mind like bright, sharp sword. they would lift their

chin as Peter do.... it become inconceivable to Joan that woman could

give their body to bear the child of unloved man. “_dear_ Petah,”

her lip say silently. her heart swell; her hand tighten. she

want to kiss he....



then in a whim of reaction she be move to mockery.



“do you feel so _very_ stern and strong, dear Petah?” she whisper

close to his shoulder.



he start, surprised, stare at she for a moment, and smile into her

eye.



“Old Joan,” he say and kiss she....





                                  § 10



when he return to the house on Monday morning after he have see the

two young people off, a burthen of desolation come upon Oswald.



it be a loneliness as acute as a physical pain. it be misery. if they

have be dead, he could not have be more unhappy. the work that have

be the warm and living substance of fifteen year be now finish and

do. the nest be empty. the road and the stream, the gate and the

garden, the house and the hall, seem to ache with emptiness and

desertion. he go into their old study, from which they have already

take a number of their most intimate treasure, and which be now as

disorder as a room after a sale. Most of their remain personal

possession be stack ready for removal; discard magazine and

book and tear paper make an untidy heap beside the fireplace. “I could

not feel a great pain if I have lose a son,” he think, stare at

these untidy vestige.



he go to his own study and sit down at his desk, though he know there

be no power of attention in he sufficient to begin work.



Mrs. Moxton, for reason well know to herself, be interested in his

movement that morning. she see he presently wander into the garden and

then return to the hall. he take his cap and stick and touch the bell.

“I’ll not be back to lunch, Mrs. Moxton,” he call.



“very well, sir,” say Mrs. Moxton, unseen upon the landing above,

nod her head approvingly.



at first the world outside be as lonely as his study.



he go up the valley along the high road for half a mile, and then take

a wind lane under almost overhang bough—the hawthorn leave now

be nearly out and the eld quite—up over the hill and thence across

field and through a wood until he come to where the steep lane run

down to Braughing. and by that time, although the spring-time world be

still immensely lonely and comfortless, he no long feel that

despairful sense of fresh and irremediable loss with which he start.

he be begin to realize now that he have always be a solitary

be; that all man, even in crowd, carry a certain solitude with they;

and loneliness thus lift to the level of a sustained and general

experience cease to feel like a dagger turn in his heart.



down the middle of braughe village, among space of grass, run the

little Quin, now a race of crystalline water over pebbly shallow and

now a brown purposefulness fleck with foam, in which reed bend and

recover as if they keep their footing by perpetual feat of dexterity.

there be two ford, and midway between they a little bridge with a

handrail on which Oswald stay for a time, watch the life and

adventure of an endless stream of bubble that be beget thirty

foot away where the eddy from the depth beneath a willow root dash

against a bough that bobbed and dip in the water. he find a great

distraction and relief in follow their adventure. on they come,

large and small, in string, in spin group, busy bubble, quiet

bubble, dignified solitary bubble, and pass a dangerous headland of

watercress and run the gauntlet between two big stone and then, if they

survive, come with a hopeful rush for the shadow under the bridge and

vanish utterly....



for all the rest of the day those stream bubble glitter and race

and jostle before Oswald’s eye, and make a veil across his personal

desolation. his mind swing like a pendulum between two idea; those

bubble be like human life; they be not like human life....



philosophy be the great of anodyne.



“why be a man’s life different from a bubble? like a bubble he be bear

of the swirl of matter, like a bubble he reflect the universe, he be

drive and whirl about by force he do not comprehend, he shine

here and be darken there and be elated or depressed he know not why,

and at last pass suddenly out into the darkness....”



in the evening Oswald sit muse by his study fire, his lamp unlit. he

sit in an attitude that have long become habitual to he, with the

scarred side of his face rest upon and hide by his hand. his walk

have weary he, but not unpleasantly, his knee be surprisingly free

from pain, and he be no long acutely unhappy. the idea, a very

engaging idea, have come into his head that it be not really the

education of Joan and Peter that have come to an end, but his own. they

be still learner—how much they have still to learn! at Peter’s age he

have not yet go to Africa. they have finish with school and college

perhaps, but they be but begin in the university of life. neither

of they have yet experience a great disillusionment, neither of they have

be shame or bitterly disappointed; they have each other. they have see

the great war indeed, and Peter know now what wound and death be

like—but he himself have be through that at one-and-twenty. neither have

have any such dark tragedy as, for example, if one of they have be

kill, or if one of they have betray and injure the other. perhaps

they would always have fortunate life.



but he himself have have to learn the lesson to the end. his life have be

a darken one. he have love intensely and lose. he have have to abandon

his choose life work when it be barely half do. he have a present

sense of the great need of the world, and he be bodily weak and

mentally uncertain. he would spend day now of fret futility, unable

to achieve anything. he love these dear youngster, but the young

cannot give love to the old because they do not yet understand. he be

alone. and yet, it be strange, he keep on. with such strength as he have

he pursue his end. those two would go on, full of hope, help one

another, think together, succeed. the lesson he have learn be

that without much love, without much vitality, with little hope of

see a single end achieve for which he work, he could still go on.



he drift through his memory, seek for the motive that have drive

he on from experience to experience. but while he could remember the

experience it be very hard now to recover any inkling of his motive.

he remember himself at school as a violent egotist, work hard,

openly and fairly, for his ascendancy in the school game, work hard

secretly for his school position. it seem now as though all that time

he have be no more than a greed and a vanity.... be that fair to

himself? or have he forget the redeem dream of youth?...



the scene shift to the wardroom of his first battleship, and then to

his first battle. he see again the long low line of the egyptian coast,

and the battery of Alexandria and Ramleh spit fire and the

_Condor_ stand in. he recall the tense excitement of that morning,

the boat row to land, but strangely enough the incident that have win

he the Victoria Cross have be blot completely from his mind by his

injury. he could not recover even the fact, much less the feel of that

act.... why have he do what he have do? do he himself really do

it?... then very vividly come the memory of his first sight of his

smash, disfigured face. that have be horrible at the time—in a way it

be horrible still—but after that it seem as though for the first time

he have cease then to be an egotism, a vanity. after that the memory

of impersonal interest begin. he think of his attendance at Huxley’s

lecture at South Kensington and the wonder of make his first

dissection. about that time he meet dolly, but here again be a queer

gap; he could not remember anything very distinctly about his early

meeting with dolly except that she wear white and that they happen in

a garden.



yet, in a little while, all his being have be hungry for dolly!



with his first journey into Africa all his memory become bright and

clear and as if a hotter sun shine upon they. everything before that

time be part of the story of a young man long vanish from the world,

young Oswald, a personality at least as remote as Peter—very like Peter.

but with the change of scene to Africa Oswald become himself. the man in

the story be the man who sit musing in the study chair, move by the

same motive and altogether understandable. already in Nyasaland he be

work consciously for “civilization” even as he work today.

everything in that period live still, with all its accompany

feeling alive. he fight again in his first fight in Nyasaland, and

recall with complete vividness how he have load and fire and

reload and fire time after time at the rush of the Yao spearman; he

have fight lean against the stockade because he be too weary to

stand upright, and with his head and every limb ache. one man he have

hit have wriggle for a long time in the grass, and that memory still

distress he. it trail another memory of horror with it. in his

campaign about Lake Kioga, year later, in a fight amidst some ant hill

he have come upon a wound Sudanese be eat alive by swarm of ant.

the poor devil have die with the ant still upon he.... Oswald could

still recall the sick anguish with which he have try in vain to save or

relieve this man.



the affair be in the exact quality of his present feeling; the picture

be paint from the same palette. he remember that then, as now, he

feel the same helpless perplexity at apparently needless and

unprofitable human agony. and then, as now, he have not despair. he have

be able to see no reason in this suffering and no excuse for it; he

could see none now, and yet he do not despair. why do not that and a

hundred other horror overwhelm he with despair? why have he be able

to go on with life after that? and after another exquisite humiliation

and disappointment? he have love dolly intensely, and here again come a

third but less absolutely obliterate gap in his recollection. for

year he have be resolutely keep his mind off the suffering of that

time, and now they be indistinct. his memory be particularly blank

now about Arthur; he be register merely as a blonde sort of ass with

a tenor voice who punch copper. that faint hostile caricature be all

his mind have tolerate. but still sharp and clear, as though it have be

photograph but yesterday upon his memory, be the afternoon when he

have realize that dolly be dead. that scene be life-size and intense;

how in a shady place under great tree, he have leant forwards upon his

little folding table and weep aloud.



what have carry he through all those thing? why have he desire so

intensely? why have he work so industriously? why do he possess this

passion for order that have inspire his administrative work? why have he

give his good year to Uganda? why have he be so concerned for the

welfare and wisdom of Joan and Peter? why do he work now to the very

breaking-point, until sleeplessness and fever force he to rest, for

this dream of a great federation in the world—a world state he would

certainly never live to see establish? if he be indeed only a bubble,

then surely he be the most obstinately opinionate of bubble. but he

be not merely a bubble. the essential self of he be not this thing

that spin about in life, that feel and reflect the world, that miss

so acutely the two dear other bubble that have circle about he so long

and that have now leave he to eddy in his backwater while they hurry

off into the midstream of life. his essential self, the self that muse

now, that have struggle up through the egotism of youth to this present

predominance, be something deep and tough and more real than

desire, than excitement, than pleasure or pain. that be the lesson he

have be learn. there be something deep in he to which he have

be get down more and more as life have go on, something to which

all the stuff of experience be incidental, something in which there be

endless fortitude and an undying resolution to do. there be something

in he profounder than the stream of accident....



he sit now with his distress allay, his mind play with fancy

and metaphor and analogy. be this profounder contentment beneath his

pain and discontent the consciousness of the bubble give way to the

underlying consciousness of the stream? that be ingenious, but it be

not true. man be not bubble carry blindly on a stream; they be

rather like bubble, but that be all. they be will and part of a will

that be neither the slave of the stream of matter nor a thing

indifferent to it, that be paradoxically free and bind. they be part

of a will, but what this great will be that have he in its grasp, that

compel he to work, that save he from drown in his individual

sorrow and care, he could not say. it be easy to draw the analogy

that a man be an atom in the life of the specie as a cell be an atom in

the life of a man. but this again be not the complete truth. where be

this allege will of the specie? if there be indeed such a will in the

specie, why be there this war? and yet, whatever it might be,

assuredly there be _something_ great than himself sustain his

life.... to he it feel like a universal thing, but be it indeed a

universal thing? it be strangely bind up with preference. why do he

love and choose certain thing passionately? why be he indifferent to

other? why be dolly and Joan more beautiful to he than any other

woman; why do he so love the sound of their voice, their movement,

and the subtle line of their face; why do he love Peter, stand

upright and when enthusiasm light he; and why do he love the light on

polished steel and the darkness of deep water, the movement of

flame, and of supple, feline animal, so intensely? why do he love

these thing more than the sheen on paint wood, or the grace of

blonde woman, or the movement of horse? and why do he love justice

and the revelation of scientific law, and the set right of

disorder thing? why do this idea of a League of Nations come to he

with the effect of a personal and preferential call? all these light

and matter and aspect and personal trait be somehow connect in

his mind, and have a compelling power over he. they could make he

forget his safety or comfort or happiness. they have something in common

among themselves, he feel, and he could not tell what it be they have in

common. but whatever it be, it be the intimation of the power that

sustain he. it be as if they be all reflection or resemblance of

some overruling spirit, some Genius, some great ruler of the value that

stand over his existence and his world. yet that again be but a fancy—a

plagiarism from Socrates....



there be a light upon his life, and the truth be that he could not

discover the source of the light nor define its nature; there be a

presence in the world about he that make all life worth while, and yet

it be Nameless and Incomprehensible. it be the Essence beyond reality;

it be the Heart of all thing.... metaphor! word! perhaps some man

have mean this when they talk of love, but he himself have love

because of this, and so he hold it must be something great than Love.

perhaps some man have intend it in their use of the word Beauty, but

it seem to he that rather it make and determine Beauty for he. and

other again have know it as the live presence of God, but the name

of God be to Oswald a name batter out of all value and meaning. and

yet it be by this, by this nameless, this Incomprehensible, that he

live and be uphold. it do so uphold he that he could go on, he know,

though happiness be deny he; though defeat and death stare he in

the face....





                                  § 11



at last he sigh and rise. he light his reading lamp by mean of a

newspaper roll up into one long spill—for there be a famine in

match just then—and sit down to the work on his desk.





                                the END
